sections.title: Historical Foundations and the Logic of Regulated Proteolysis

subsections.title: The pre-ubiquitin problem
subsections.body:
Before the 1980s, intracellular protein degradation was

thought to be predominantly lysosomal. This view was inconsistent with key observations: many
cytosolic and nuclear proteins turn over with very different half-lives even though they share the
same cytoplasm; degradation is often strongly ATP-dependent, whereas lysosomal hydrolases are
not; and substrate-specific degradation signals (such as PEST sequences, N-end rule residues, or
phospho-degrons) did not fit a nonselective bulk-hydrolysis model. Etlinger and Goldberg (1977)
showed that reticulocyte lysates, which have very few lysosomes, still degrade abnormal proteins
in an ATP-dependent manner, implying an extralysosomal ATP-dependent proteolytic system.

subsections.title: Discovery of ubiquitin and the conjugation cascade
subsections.body: Aaron Ciechanover, Avram Hershko, and Irwin Rose at Fox Chase Cancer Center (1978 to 1983) fractionated the reticulocyte lysate and identified a small heat-stable polypeptide, initially called ATP-dependent proteolysis factor 1 (APF1), which they showed to be ubiquitin (originally isolated by Goldstein and colleagues in 1975 as a ubiquitously expressed polypeptide of unknown function). In a landmark series of papers (Ciechanover et al. 1980, PNAS; Hershko et al. 1983, JBC; Hershko and Ciechanover, Annu Rev Biochem 1992 and 1998), they demonstrated that ubiquitin is covalently attached to substrates through an ATP-dependent three-enzyme cascade (E1, E2, E3), and that polyubiquitinated proteins are recognized and degraded by a large ATP-dependent protease, which was later purified by Rechsteiner, DeMartino, Tanaka, and others as the 26S proteasome. Hershko, Ciechanover, and Rose shared the 2004 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for “the discovery of ubiquitin-mediated protein degradation.” Key facts: • UPS degrades the majority of short-lived regulatory proteins in eukaryotes (estimated >80% of cellular proteins pass through the proteasome at some point). • Ubiquitin itself is one of the most highly conserved eukaryotic proteins; yeast and human ubiquitin differ at only 3 of 76 residues. • Hershko, Ciechanover, and Rose received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2004; the foundational biochemistry was largely complete before the genome era.

subsections.title: Why regulated proteolysis matters
subsections.body:
Unlike phosphorylation, ubiquitination leading to proteasomal degradation is essentially irreversible at the level of the substrate: once the protein is

destroyed, the signal is zeroed until new protein is synthesized. This makes the UPS the principal
mechanism for unidirectional transitions, such as cell cycle commitments (cyclin destruction by
APC/C and SCF), termination of signaling (IκBα degradation freeing NF-κB), and developmental
transitions (degradation of pluripotency factors). Because even a small mismatch between synthesis and degradation rates creates large changes in steady-state protein abundance, the UPS is the
principal rheostat of the proteome.

subsections.title: Primary and tertiary structure Ubiquitin is a 76 amino acid, ~8.5 kDa globular polypeptide
subsections.body:
Its structure was solved by crystallography in 1987 (Vijay-Kumar et al., JMB 1987) and is a

textbook example of the “ubiquitin fold” or β-grasp fold: a mixed α/β topology with five β-strands
wrapped around a central α-helix (β-β-α-β-β-α-β). The fold is extraordinarily stable, with a melting temperature above 80°C, which allows ubiquitin to survive the rigors of proteasomal unfolding
and recycling.
Key residues to commit to memory: - C-terminal Gly-Gly (G75-G76) : the absolute C-terminus,
exposed after cleavage of the ubiquitin precursor. G76 forms the isopeptide bond with substrate
lysine ε-amines (or an amide with the substrate N-terminus in rare cases). - Seven internal lysines:
K6, K11, K27, K29, K33, K48, K63. Each is an acceptor for additional ubiquitin G76 C-termini, producing topologically distinct polyubiquitin chains. - M1 (Met1): the α-amino group of the initiator
methionine can accept a ubiquitin G76, producing head-to-tail “linear” (M1) chains assembled by
LUBAC (HOIP/HOIL-1L/SHARPIN). - I44 hydrophobic patch (L8, I44, V70) : the canonical binding surface for ubiquitin-binding domains (UBDs) such as UBA, UIM, CUE, and the Rpn10/Rpn13
proteasome receptors. K48 chains display a compact conformation that masks I44 except at chain
ends, while K63 and M1 chains are extended and expose multiple I44 surfaces, which contributes
to topology-specific recognition.

subsections.title: Ubiquitin gene organization
subsections.body:
Ubiquitin is encoded in humans by four loci: UBA52 and

RPS27A encode fusions of ubiquitin to ribosomal proteins L40 and S27a, while UBB and UBC
encode polyubiquitin precursors. All four are cleaved by DUBs to produce free, functional
monomeric ubiquitin. This organization couples ubiquitin supply to ribosome biogenesis, and
under proteotoxic stress UBB and UBC are transcriptionally upregulated.

subsections.title: E1: ubiquitin activation
subsections.body:
Humans encode two ubiquitin E1 enzymes: UBA1 (the canonical E1, essential, X-linked in part, with somatic mutations underlying VEXAS syndrome; Beck

et al. 2020, NEJM) and UBA6 (a minor E1 that also activates FAT10). The E1 reaction proceeds
in two steps, both ATP-dependent: (1) ubiquitin adenylation at G76, producing a ubiquitin-AMP
mixed anhydride and releasing pyrophosphate; (2) thioester transfer from AMP to the E1 activesite cysteine (UBA1 C632), producing the E1~Ub thioester and releasing AMP . A second ubiquitin
is then adenylated at a separate site, so a fully loaded E1 carries one thioester-linked and one
adenylate-linked ubiquitin (doubly loaded state). This doubly loaded state is kinetically primed

for E2 transthiolation.
Key facts:
• Two ATP are consumed per ubiquitin activated (one for adenylation, and the second arises
from the doubly loaded state; net ATP cost per conjugation event is typically one ATP , with
AMP and PPi as products).
• VEXAS syndrome (Vacuoles, E1, X-linked, Autoinflammatory , Somatic; methionine-41 UBA1
mutations in myeloid progenitors) is a striking example of a UPS disease in hematopoiesis and
is directly relevant to a UPS-focused committee chair.
keyFacts: Two ATP are consumed per ubiquitin activated (one for adenylation, and the second arises from the doubly loaded state; net ATP cost per conjugation event is typically one ATP , with AMP and PPi as products).

VEXAS syndrome (Vacuoles, E1, X-linked, Autoinflammatory , Somatic; methionine-41 UBA1 mutations in myeloid progenitors) is a striking example of a UPS disease in hematopoiesis and is directly relevant to a UPS-focused committee chair.

subsections.title: E2: ubiquitin-conjugating enzymes
subsections.body:
Humans have approximately 40 E2 enzymes (distinct Ubc proteins plus UBE2-family members with additional N- or C-terminal extensions). They

share a conserved ~150 aa UBC fold with a catalytic cysteine that accepts ubiquitin from E1 by
transthiolation, forming an E2~Ub thioester. The E2 is the primary determinant of polyubiquitin chain linkage type in RING-type reactions, because the E3 orients the substrate while the E2
active site positions a particular ubiquitin lysine (or M1) for nucleophilic attack. Classic examples: - UBE2R1/CDC34 builds K48 chains on SCF substrates. - UBE2S elongates K11 chains on
APC/C substrates; UBE2C/UBCH10 initiates. - UBE2N/UBC13 together with the UEV protein
UEV1A/MMS2 builds K63 chains in DNA damage and NF-κB signaling. - UBE2K/HIP2 builds
free K48 chains. - UBE2L3/UBCH7 is the obligate E2 for HECT and RBR E3s; it is chemically incapable of discharging ubiquitin to a lysine in the absence of an acceptor cysteine, which explains
why it cannot productively work with most RING E3s.
Some E3 families are coupled to particular E2s: CRLs mostly use CDC34 (K48) and sometimes
UBE2D (promiscuous); BRCA1/BARD1 uses UBE2N; Parkin (RBR) uses UBE2L3.

subsections.title: E3: ubiquitin ligases E3s confer substrate specificity and are
subsections.body:
the most numerous component of the cascade (~600 to 700 human genes). They fall into three mechanistic classes.

RING E3s (~600 in humans). The RING (Really Interesting New Gene) domain is a zinc-binding
cross-brace motif with two structural Zn2+ ions coordinated by eight cysteines or histidines. RING
E3s do NOT form a covalent intermediate with ubiquitin; they act as scaffolds that bind the E2~Ub
thioester in a closed, catalytically primed conformation in which the ubiquitin C-terminus is held
against the E2 active site (Plechanovová et al. 2012, Nature; Dou et al. 2012, NSMB). The substrate
lysine attacks the thioester directly , so transfer is a one-step reaction from E2 to substrate. U-box
E3s (e.g., CHIP/STUB1) are a RING structural variant that uses salt bridges instead of Zn2+ coordination but work by the same scaffold mechanism. Most RING E3s work as dimers (homo- or
heterodimers) or as multi-subunit complexes (CRLs, APC/C).
HECT E3s (~28 in humans). HECT (Homologous to E6-AP C-Terminus) E3s have a C-terminal
HECT domain with an obligate active-site cysteine. The mechanism is two-step: ubiquitin is first
transferred from the E2 to the HECT cysteine, producing an E3~Ub thioester, and then from the
E3 to the substrate lysine. The HECT E3 itself determines chain topology because the ubiquitinloaded HECT domain positions the acceptor. NEDD4 family HECTs (NEDD4, NEDD4L, ITCH,
WWP1/2, SMURF1/2, HECW1/2) have WW domains that recognize PPxY motifs on substrates.
HERC family HECTs have additional RCC1-like domains. E6AP/UBE3A (the founding member,
named for its role in HPV E6-mediated p53 degradation) and HUWE1 are notable examples.

RBR E3s (~14 in humans). RBR (RING-Between-RING) E3s are a hybrid: they contain a canonical
RING1 that binds E2~Ub as in RING mechanism, but ubiquitin is first transferred to an active-site
cysteine in RING2 (IBR/Rcat domain), making them RING/HECT hybrids mechanistically (Wenzel et al. 2011, Nature). PARKIN, HOIP (in LUBAC), HHARI/ARIH1, and TRIAD1/ARIH2 are
canonical examples. RBRs are typically auto-inhibited and activated by post-translational modifications or cofactor binding (e.g., PINK1-dependent phosphorylation of Parkin and ubiquitin).
ARIH1 is notable because it cooperates with neddylated CRLs to prime substrates with monoubiquitin, which is then extended by CDC34 (Scott et al. 2016, Cell).
Nuance for committee : The historical RING/HECT dichotomy is a simplification.
RBR E3s do not fit cleanly , and recent biochemistry (Buetow and Huang 2016; Deol
et al. 2019) shows that RING E3 catalysis is more than just “scaffolding”: the RING
domain allosterically activates the E2~Ub thioester by positioning ubiquitin in a closed
conformation, a process referred to as “E2 priming.” So “RING = just a scaffold” is not
quite right.

sections.title: Polyubiquitin Chains: A Code, Not Just a T ag

subsections.title: T opologies and their signals
subsections.body:
All seven internal lysines of ubiquitin, plus the M1 α-amine,

can serve as acceptors, producing eight homotypic chain linkages plus mixed, branched, and heterotypic chains. Each linkage has distinct 3D geometry and is read by different UBDs.
• K48: the canonical proteasomal degradation signal. Chains of four or more K48-linked ubiquitins are efficiently recognized by the 19S regulatory particle (Thrower et al. 2000, EMBO J
established the “K48-linked tetraubiquitin” minimum). Compact closed conformation.
• K11: cell cycle. APC/C builds K11 chains (often branched with K48) on cyclin B, securin, and
mitotic regulators during metaphase to anaphase transition. K11/K48 branches are particularly
potent proteasome signals (Meyer and Rape 2014, Cell).
• K63: non-degradative. Signaling scaffolds in NF-κB (TRAF6 on IRAK1, NEMO), DNA damage
response (RNF8/RNF168 on histone H2A at double-strand breaks), and endocytic trafficking.
• K6: DNA damage (BRCA1/BARD1-dependent) and mitophagy (Parkin).
• K27: DNA damage, innate immunity , less understood.
• K29: AMPK signaling, protein quality control.
• K33: T cell receptor signaling, post-Golgi trafficking.
• M1 (linear) : built exclusively by LUBAC (HOIP catalytic, with HOIL-1L and SHARPIN). Essential for canonical NF-κB activation downstream of TNFR, IL-1R, and NOD2; recognized by
NEMO UBAN domain. M1 chains are a central topic in hematopoiesis and inflammation; HOIP
loss causes embryonic lethality and immunodeficiency .
Key facts:
• The “ubiquitin code” was formalized by Komander and Rape (2012, Annu Rev Biochem): topology conveys functional information because different chains adopt different conformations and
bind different readers.
• A monoubiquitin or short oligoubiquitin (≤3 ubiquitins) is usually a non-degradative signal
(e.g., monoUb-H2B in transcription, monoUb-PCNA in translesion synthesis).

• Recent mass spectrometry (Ubiscan/UbiSite) reveals that essentially every chain topology is
present in cells simultaneously , so the “code” is combinatorial.
keyFacts: The “ubiquitin code” was formalized by Komander and Rape (2012, Annu Rev Biochem): topology conveys functional information because different chains adopt different conformations and bind different readers.
A monoubiquitin or short oligoubiquitin (≤3 ubiquitins) is usually a non-degradative signal (e.g., monoUb-H2B in transcription, monoUb-PCNA in translesion synthesis).

subsections.title: Why chain topology matters Ubiquitin chain topology dictates downstream biology because: 1
subsections.body:
Different UBDs recognize different linkages (NEMO UBAN binds M1; Rpn13 Pru binds

K48; TAB2/TAB3 NZF binds K63). 2. Different DUBs are linkage-selective (CYLD cleaves K63
and M1; OTUB1 is K48-specific; OTULIN is M1-exclusive). 3. Different chain geometries expose
or mask the I44 hydrophobic patch differently , tuning affinity for readers. 4. Branched chains
amplify signals by engaging multiple readers simultaneously .

subsections.title: Architectural overview The 26S proteasome is
subsections.body:
a ~2.5 MDa ATP-dependent protease comprising a 20S catalytic core particle (CP) capped on one or both ends by a 19S regulatory particle

(RP). It was reconstructed at near-atomic resolution by cryo-EM (Lander et al. 2012, Nature; Beck
et al. 2012, PNAS; Dong et al. 2019, Nature).
20S CP: a hollow cylinder of four stacked heptameric rings in α7-β7-β7-α7 arrangement. The two
outer α rings gate substrate entry; the N-terminal tails of α subunits form the gate, which is closed
in free 20S and opened when the 19S docks. The two inner β rings contain three catalytic sites
per ring, for a total of six catalytic sites per particle: - β1 (PSMB6): caspase-like (post-acidic) activity; cleaves after D/E. - β2 (PSMB7): trypsin-like activity ; cleaves after K/R. - β5 (PSMB5):
chymotrypsin-like activity ; cleaves after large hydrophobic residues. This is the principal site
targeted by clinical proteasome inhibitors. The catalytic mechanism is N-terminal threonine nucleophile (Thr1 Oγ), unusual among proteases; the free N-terminal amine serves as the general base.
Immune cells (and IFN-γ-stimulated cells, directly relevant to an IFN-focused chair) replace β1, β2,
β5 with β1i (PSMB9/LMP2), β2i (PSMB10/MECL1), β5i (PSMB8/LMP7) to form the immunoproteasome, which has altered cleavage preferences that favor generation of MHC class I antigenic
peptides. A further variant, the thymoproteasome (β5t), is restricted to cortical thymic epithelial
cells and is essential for positive selection of CD8+ T cells.
19S RP: comprises the “base” (six AAA+ ATPases Rpt1-6 plus non-ATPase subunits Rpn1, Rpn2,
Rpn10, Rpn13) and the “lid” (Rpn3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12, 15/Sem1). The base sits on the α ring,
opens the gate, engages substrates, and uses ATP hydrolysis to unfold them and translocate them
into the CP . The lid contains the intrinsic deubiquitinase PSMD14/Rpn11/POH1, a JAMM/MPN+
zinc metalloprotease that cleaves the entire polyubiquitin chain en bloc at the substrate-proximal
isopeptide bond, recycling ubiquitin just as the substrate commits to translocation.
Key facts:
• Ubiquitin receptors on the 19S: Rpn10/PSMD4 (UIM domain) and Rpn13/ADRM1 (Pru domain) recognize K48 chains; Rpn1 T1 site is an additional receptor (Shi et al. 2016, Science).
Shuttle factors (RAD23A/B, DDI1/2, UBQLN1/2) deliver substrates with UBL-UBA architecture.
• The 20S can also function without a cap as an “intrinsically disordered protein degrader,” relevant to oxidized proteins recognized without ubiquitination.
• PSMD14/Rpn11 was shown by Buckley et al. 2012 (Cell Stem Cell) to be essential for ES cell
pluripotency and iPS reprogramming; it is a point of selective vulnerability in stem cells that a

UPS-focused chair will recognize immediately .
keyFacts: Ubiquitin receptors on the 19S: Rpn10/PSMD4 (UIM domain) and Rpn13/ADRM1 (Pru domain) recognize K48 chains; Rpn1 T1 site is an additional receptor (Shi et al. 2016, Science). Shuttle factors (RAD23A/B, DDI1/2, UBQLN1/2) deliver substrates with UBL-UBA architecture.
The 20S can also function without a cap as an “intrinsically disordered protein degrader,” relevant to oxidized proteins recognized without ubiquitination.

subsections.title: Proteasome inhibitors and clinical relevance
subsections.body:
• MG132 (Z-Leu-Leu-Leu-al): reversible peptide aldehyde that inhibits the β5 chymotrypsin-like
and, at higher concentrations, the β1 caspase-like activity . Research tool, not clinical.
• Bortezomib (V elcade): boronic acid dipeptide; reversibly inhibits β5 (and β1 at higher doses).
First FDA-approved (2003) proteasome inhibitor, for multiple myeloma. The therapeutic rationale: plasma cells produce enormous amounts of immunoglobulin, so they are critically dependent on UPS-mediated clearance of misfolded Ig; proteasome inhibition triggers terminal UPR
and apoptosis.
• Carfilzomib (Kyprolis): epoxyketone; irreversibly inhibits β5 by forming a morpholine adduct
with the catalytic Thr1. Approved 2012 for relapsed/refractory MM.
• Ixazomib (Ninlaro): orally bioavailable boronate; approved 2015 for MM.
• Oprozomib, marizomib (salinosporamide A) : investigational irreversible inhibitors.

subsections.title: Deubiquitinases (DUBs) Humans encode ~100 DUBs in six families: - USP (ubiquitinspecific protease): ~55 members, cysteine proteases.
subsections.body:
Largest family (USP7, USP14, CYLD, BAP1).

- UCH (ubiquitin C-terminal hydrolase) : 4 members (UCHL1, UCHL3, UCHL5/UCH37, BAP1);
cysteine proteases, trim small adducts from ubiquitin. - OTU (ovarian tumor) : ~16 members
(OTUB1, OTUB2, OTULIN, A20/TNFAIP3); cysteine proteases, often linkage-specific. - MJD
(Machado-Joseph disease) : 4 members (ATXN3, JOSD1/2, ATXN3L); cysteine proteases. -
JAMM/MPN+: zinc metalloproteases (PSMD14/Rpn11, BRCC36, CSN5, AMSH/STAMBP). -
MINDY and ZUP1: newer families, K48and K63-selective respectively .
DUBs serve four broad functions: (1) process ubiquitin precursors, (2) rescue substrates from degradation by editing chains, (3) recycle ubiquitin at the proteasome, and (4) terminate signaling (CYLD
on K63/M1, A20 on K63, OTULIN on M1).

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
While the UPS handles soluble, short-lived proteins, macroautophagy degrades aggregates, damaged organelles, and long-lived proteins via double-membrane autophagosomes that fuse with
lysosomes. Selective autophagy (aggrephagy , mitophagy , xenophagy) uses ubiquitin as the targeting signal, read by autophagy receptors (p62/SQSTM1, NBR1, OPTN, NDP52, TAX1BP1) that
bridge K63- or M1-ubiquitinated cargo to LC3/GABARAP on the forming autophagosome. The
UPS and autophagy are coupled: proteasome inhibition activates compensatory autophagy via
Nrf1/NFE2L1 transcription and TFEB nuclear translocation. In hematopoiesis, autophagy is required for HSC maintenance (Mortensen et al. 2011, JEM; Ho et al. 2017, Nature) and mitophagy
clears the fetal mitochondrial network during HSC maturation. Tier 3 will expand this.

subsections.title: The Buckley 2012 landscape
subsections.body:
Buckley et al. (Cell Stem Cell 2012, Aifantis lab) provided the

first proteome-wide map of ubiquitination in pluripotent cells. They used SILAC labeling of mouse
ESCs, digested with trypsin (which leaves a di-glycine “K-GG” remnant on ubiquitinated lysines),

enriched with a K-GG-specific antibody , and performed LC-MS/MS. Headline numbers to memorize: - 2578 ubiquitinated peptides on 1168 proteins in ESCs. - Sites were enriched on chromatin
regulators, transcription factors, and pluripotency machinery , implicating UPS as a principal tuner
of the pluripotent state. - They identified FBXW7 (SCF^FBXW7) as the E3 for c-Myc in ESCs, with
degradation dependent on the T58 phospho-degron primed by GSK3β (with prior S62 priming by
CDK or ERK). Loss of FBXW7 elevates Myc and disrupts self-renewal. - Functional RNAi screens
showed that PSMD14/Rpn11 (the lid-associated DUB) is essential for maintaining ES pluripotency
and is required for iPS reprogramming from fibroblasts.
T ake-home for the qual : The UPS is not just a housekeeping system; in stem cells
it actively sculpts fate decisions by degrading pluripotency regulators and lineagedetermining transcription factors on specific timescales. The Aifantis lab’s framing of
the UPS as a pluripotency regulator is the natural conceptual predecessor to their later
Gao 2015 CRL4-DCAF7 paper.

subsections.title: UPS in hematopoiesis: HSC-relevant E3 ligases
subsections.body:
Hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) rely

on UPS to balance quiescence, self-renewal, and differentiation. Key E3s: - FBW7/FBXW7 (SCF) :
Thompson et al. 2008 (JEM) showed that Fbw7 conditional deletion in HSCs causes accumulation
of c-Myc, loss of quiescence, exit from the stem cell pool, and HSC exhaustion. This established
c-Myc degradation as a core quiescence-enforcing mechanism. Matsuoka et al. 2008 and Reavie et
al. 2010 confirmed and extended this. Fbw7 also degrades Notch1 ICD, cyclin E, JUN, MCL1, and
SREBP , so its phenotype integrates multiple regulators. - ITCH (HECT) : NEDD4-family E3; degrades NOTCH1 and JUN, restrains inflammatory signaling in T cells; Itch-deficient mice have autoimmune phenotype. - c-CBL and CBL-B (RING) : degrade receptor tyrosine kinases (FLT3, KIT,
EGFR) and signaling adapters. CBL mutations are recurrent in juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia
(JMML) and cause a Noonan-like RASopathy with myeloproliferation; they are loss-of-function
for ligase activity and dominant-negative. - TRAF6 (RING): builds K63 chains on IRAK1, NEMO,
and itself; central to TLR/IL-1R and RANK signaling. Essential for HSC maintenance and innate
immunity . - MDM2 (RING) : principal E3 for p53. MDM2 loss causes p53-dependent HSC apoptosis; the MDM2-p53 axis sets the threshold for stress-induced HSC attrition (Abbas et al. 2010).
- TRIM33, RNF20/40, HUWE1, UBR4 : additional lineage-specific E3s with reported HSC phenotypes.
Key facts:
• The dominant conceptual model: UPS enforces HSC quiescence by degrading pro-proliferative
(c-Myc, cyclin E) and pro-differentiation (Notch ICD) factors. Perturbing E3s collapses this
balance, typically causing HSC exhaustion rather than expansion.
• VEXAS syndrome (somatic UBA1 M41 mutations in myeloid progenitors; Beck et al. 2020,
NEJM) is a reminder that even the apical E1 has hematopoietic-specific pathology .
keyFacts: The dominant conceptual model: UPS enforces HSC quiescence by degrading pro-proliferative (c-Myc, cyclin E) and pro-differentiation (Notch ICD) factors. Perturbing E3s collapses this balance, typically causing HSC exhaustion rather than expansion.
VEXAS syndrome (somatic UBA1 M41 mutations in myeloid progenitors; Beck et al. 2020, NEJM) is a reminder that even the apical E1 has hematopoietic-specific pathology .

subsections.title: Why CRLs dominate
subsections.body:
the E3 landscape Of ~600 RING E3s, the largest subfamily is the

Cullin-RING Ligase (CRL) superfamily , with ~250 distinct complexes in humans arising from

combinatorial assembly of a small number of modular parts (Petroski and Deshaies 2005, Nat Rev
Mol Cell Biol; Sarikas et al. 2011, Genome Biol). CRLs degrade at least 20% of all UPS substrates and
regulate cell cycle, development, transcription, metabolism, and stress responses. Seven canonical
cullins (CUL1, CUL2, CUL3, CUL4A, CUL4B, CUL5, CUL7) plus the noncanonical CUL9/PARC
comprise the scaffolds.

subsections.title: The modular architecture Every CRL has four functional parts: 1
subsections.body:
Cullin scaffold : an

elongated arc-shaped protein (~700 to 800 aa) with an N-terminal adaptor-binding domain and a
C-terminal cullin homology domain that binds the RING subunit and accepts neddylation on a
conserved C-terminal lysine. 2. RING-box subunit : RBX1/ROC1 for CUL1/2/3/4A/4B/7 and
most function, RBX2/ROC2/SAG for CUL5 (and sometimes CUL4). RBX1 is a small RING protein
that recruits the E2~Ub thioester; it is the catalytic module. 3. Adaptor: small protein that bridges
cullin and substrate receptor. 4. Substrate receptor (SR) : the specificity module that binds the
degron on the substrate.
Each cullin has its own dedicated adaptor and SR family:

CUL1/SCF: The founding CRL. F-box proteins (so named for the first cyclin F box) bind SKP1
via an F-box motif and substrates via C-terminal WD40 (FBXW), leucine-rich (FBXL), or “other”
(FBXO) domains. Famous F-box proteins and their degrons: - SKP2 (FBXL1) : phospho-T187 on
p27^Kip1, with CKS1 co-factor; couples cell cycle exit to proliferation. - FBXW7: CPD (CDC4
phospho-degron), T/S-P-X-X-S/T/E; substrates include c-Myc (T58), cyclin E (T380), Notch1 ICD,
JUN, MCL1, SREBP . - β-T rCP (FBXW11/FBXW1): DSGxxS phospho-degron; substrates include
IκBα (S32/S36 phosphorylated by IKK), β-catenin, CDC25A, EMI1.
CUL2: Uses Elongin B/Elongin C as adaptors (homologous to SKP1). Substrate receptors have a
BC box plus either a VHL box or SOCS box. The archetype is VHL (von Hippel-Lindau) which degrades HIF-1α and HIF-2α after prolyl hydroxylation by PHD1/2/3 under normoxia. Loss of VHL

causes pseudohypoxia and renal clear cell carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, hemangioblastoma.
CUL3: Unique in that the BTB-domain substrate receptor plays a combined adaptor + receptor
role, directly binding the cullin N-terminus through the BTB fold (homologous to SKP1). Humans
have ~180 BTB proteins. The archetype is KEAP1, which sequesters NRF2/NFE2L2 under basal
conditions and flags it for CRL3^KEAP1-mediated degradation. Under oxidative stress, KEAP1
cysteines are modified and the complex loses activity , allowing NRF2 to accumulate and drive
antioxidant response. Other CUL3 substrate receptors: SPOP (androgen receptor, DAXX, BRD4),
KLHL proteins.
CUL5: Elongin B/C + SOCS-box receptors (SOCS1-7, CIS, ASB proteins). SOCS proteins degrade
activated JAKs (SOCS1 on JAK2) and cytokine receptors, so CUL5 is directly coupled to IFN and
cytokine signaling, which is again a conceptual bridge your committee chair will appreciate.
CUL7 and CUL9: specialized, smaller substrate sets; CUL7 is associated with 3M syndrome
(growth failure) and tumorigenesis.
Key facts:
• CRL substrate receptors bind substrates via short linear motifs (degrons), typically requiring a
post-translational mark (phosphorylation, hydroxylation, acetylation) to license binding. This
provides signal-dependent degradation.
• The ~250 CRL number comes from pairwise combinations of ~7 cullins with their compatible adaptors and ~350 total substrate receptors (70 F-box + 180 BTB + 90 DCAF + ~60
SOCS/VHL/other).
• CRLs are tightly regulated at the assembly level by NEDD8 conjugation, COP9 deneddylation,
and CAND1-mediated receptor exchange (see B.4).
keyFacts: CRL substrate receptors bind substrates via short linear motifs (degrons), typically requiring a post-translational mark (phosphorylation, hydroxylation, acetylation) to license binding. This provides signal-dependent degradation.
The ~250 CRL number comes from pairwise combinations of ~7 cullins with their compatible adaptors and ~350 total substrate receptors (70 F-box + 180 BTB + 90 DCAF + ~60 SOCS/VHL/other).
CRLs are tightly regulated at the assembly level by NEDD8 conjugation, COP9 deneddylation, and CAND1-mediated receptor exchange (see B.4).

subsections.title: NEDD8 and its cascade NEDD8 (Neural precursor cell
subsections.body:
Expressed, Developmentally Downregulated 8) is a ubiquitin-like protein (UBL) with 60% sequence identity and essentially identical

fold to ubiquitin, differing only at a handful of surface residues. Like ubiquitin, it has a C-terminal
diglycine and is conjugated to target lysines by a dedicated three-enzyme cascade: - E1 (NAE) :
heterodimer of NAE1/APPBP1 and UBA3. Note that UBA3 is the catalytic subunit; NAE1 is noncatalytic. Uses ATP to adenylate and load NEDD8 as a thioester. - E2: UBE2M/UBC12 (for CUL1,
2, 3, 4A, 4B; uses DCN1-family co-E3s) and UBE2F (for CUL5; uses RBX2/SAG). - Co-E3 / RING :
Cullin RING-box subunit (RBX1/RBX2) acts as the E3 for its own neddylation, together with a
DCN1-like scaffolding factor (DCNL1-5) that positions UBE2M productively .
The acceptor lysine is a conserved C-terminal cullin lysine. In CUL1 it is K720 (conserved position
in each cullin; often called “cullin K720-equivalent”), in CUL3 K712, in CUL4A K705.

subsections.title: What neddylation does biochemically
subsections.body:
Unneddylated cullins adopt a conformation in

which the RING subunit is held against the cullin body and the E2~Ub is far from the substrate
(and CAND1 can bind, see below). Upon neddylation, NEDD8 wedges against the C-terminal
winged-helix B (WHB) subdomain of the cullin and displaces it, releasing the RING subunit into
a flexible tether. This dramatically increases the conformational space sampled by RBX1 E2Ub,
allowing it to reach lysines on substrate held at the N-terminus of the cullin. The result is: - ~4 to

10-fold increase in ubiquitin-transfer rate to substrate. - CAND1 can no longer bind (NEDD8 and
CAND1 are mutually exclusive). - The complex is locked into an active ubiquitination-competent
state.
Cryo-EM structures (Baek et al. 2020 Nature; Baek et al. 2021 Nature; Horn-Ghetko et al. 2021
Nature) show neddylated CRLs in multiple catalytic conformations consistent with this “flexible”
active state.

subsections.title: MLN4924/pevonedistat MLN4924 (pevonedistat) is
subsections.body:
a NAE (UBA3) inhibitor discovered by

Millennium Pharmaceuticals (Soucy et al. 2009, Nature). Mechanism: MLN4924 is an adenosine
sulfamate analog that, after being loaded by NAE onto NEDD8 in place of AMP , forms a covalent
NEDD8-MLN4924 adduct that is trapped in the active site (a “suicide substrate” mechanism). This
blocks further NEDD8 activation, globally depletes cullin neddylation, and inactivates all CRLs.
Consequences: - Rapid stabilization of CRL substrates: CDT1 (CRL4^CDT2 substrate) accumulates, causing rereplication and DNA damage. - Accumulation of p27, HIF-1α, NRF2, and others.
- Apoptosis or senescence in many cancer cell lines.
Clinically , pevonedistat has been in trials (alone and with azacitidine) for AML and higher-risk
MDS; phase 3 data have been mixed but it remains an active modality , and a hematology-focused
chair will expect you to know this. Clinical use underscores that CRLs are core pathway nodes in
hematopoietic malignancy .

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The COP9 signalosome (CSN) is an eight-subunit complex (CSN1-8) structurally homologous to
the 19S proteasome lid and to eIF3. It contains a JAMM/MPN+ zinc isopeptidase in CSN5/JAB1
that cleaves NEDD8 from the cullin. Without CSN, CRLs are constitutively neddylated and become autoubiquitinated on their substrate receptors (since no substrate is present, the SR itself is
a target). Paradoxically , CSN loss inactivates CRLs in vivo by destroying the SR pool. Therefore,
CRL activity cycles between neddylated (active) and deneddylated (inactive) states, with substrate
binding tilting the equilibrium toward sustained neddylation (because substrate binding to the SR
sterically occludes CSN engagement).

sections.title: CAND1 and the CRL Exchange Cycle

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
CAND1 (Cullin-Associated NEDD8-Dissociated protein 1) is a 120 kDa HEAT-repeat protein
that binds unneddylated, substrate-receptor-free cullins and acts as a substrate-receptor exchange
factor (Pierce et al. 2013, Cell; Reitsma et al. 2017, Cell). Mechanism: 1. After CSN deneddylates
a CRL, substrate is released. 2. CAND1 binds the cullin, displacing the current SR (kinetically ,
not by thermodynamic competition). 3. CAND1-bound cullin is now poised to sample a new SR
from the cytosolic pool. 4. When a SR whose cognate substrate is abundant docks, it outcompetes
CAND1; CAND1 is released; the complex can be neddylated; ubiquitination proceeds.
The net effect is a substrate-driven adaptive remodeling of the CRL repertoire: cells use a limited
pool of cullins to assemble whichever complexes are most needed. This model resolved the early
paradox of how ~7 cullins can serve ~350 substrate receptors without requiring each SR to be
abundant. The concept is essential for understanding DCAF exchange on DDB1-CUL4.

Key facts:
• Neddylation activates CRLs; CAND1 and NEDD8 are mutually exclusive.
• CSN deneddylates, licensing CAND1 binding.
• CAND1 is the exchange factor (catalytic), not a simple inhibitor.
• Substrate binding stabilizes the neddylated active state by occluding CSN engagement, creating
a feedforward that focuses CRL activity on abundant substrates.
keyFacts: Neddylation activates CRLs; CAND1 and NEDD8 are mutually exclusive.
CSN deneddylates, licensing CAND1 binding.
CAND1 is the exchange factor (catalytic), not a simple inhibitor.
Substrate binding stabilizes the neddylated active state by occluding CSN engagement, creating a feedforward that focuses CRL activity on abundant substrates.

sections.title: CRL4: Architecture and the DCAF World

subsections.title: CUL4A vs CUL4B
subsections.body:
Humans have two CUL4 paralogs, CUL4A (chromosome 13q34) and

CUL4B (Xq24, X-linked). They share ~82% sequence identity and both use DDB1 as their adaptor. Differences: - CUL4B has an N-terminal extension (~150 aa) absent in CUL4A that contains
a canonical nuclear localization signal (NLS); CUL4B is therefore predominantly nuclear, whereas
CUL4A partitions between nucleus and cytoplasm. - CUL4B mutations (many loss-of-function)
cause X-linked intellectual disability (Tarpey et al. 2007), with phenotypes not fully phenocopied
by CUL4A loss, implying non-redundant nuclear substrates. - Mouse Cul4a knockout is viable
with subtle skin, sperm, and hematopoietic phenotypes; Cul4b knockout is embryonic lethal due
to placental defects (Liu et al. 2012, PLoS Genet). - Substrates overlap substantially (CDT1, p21,
p27, histones) but with paralog-specific preferences.
For the hematopoiesis literature, Gao et al. 2015 (Cell Stem Cell) conditionally deleted DDB1
(which is obligate for both CUL4A and CUL4B function), thereby ablating all CRL4 activity , rather
than deleting a single cullin paralog. This is the cleanest genetic strategy for CRL4 function.

subsections.title: DDB1: the adaptor DDB1 (Damaged DNA
subsections.body:
Binding protein 1) was originally identified

as the large subunit of the UV-damaged DNA binding complex (UV-DDB) with DDB2/XPE. It is
a large ~127 kDa protein composed of three WD40-like β-propellers ( BPA, BPB, BPC ) plus a Cterminal helical domain (CTD). Crystallography (Li et al. 2006, Cell; Angers et al. 2006, Nature;
Scrima et al. 2008 Cell) established: - BPC docks onto the CUL4 N-terminus (the cullin binding
interface). - BPA and BPB together form the double-propeller cleft that binds the substrate receptor. - BPB can rotate relative to BPA/BPC, creating a flexible clamp that accommodates diverse
receptors.
Substrate receptors that dock into the BPA/BPB cleft are called DCAFs (DDB1-CUL4-Associated
Factors).

subsections.title: DCAFs: the substrate receptor family DCAFs were independently identified by three
subsections.body:
groups in 2006-2008 using DDB1 affinity proteomics: - He et al. 2006 (Genes Dev) : identified
the WDR/DWD box as a DDB1-binding motif; reported ~90 WD40 proteins as candidate DCAFs.
- Angers et al. 2006 (Nature) : structural/proteomic characterization. - Higa et al. 2006 (Nat Cell
Biol): functional DCAFs including CDT2. - Jin et al. 2006 (Mol Cell): proteomic identification and
definition of ~50 DCAFs, clarifying the architecture.
Approximately 90 DCAFs are described in humans. Most (~80%) contain WD40 (WDR) repeats,
which fold into seven-bladed β-propellers. The WD40 β-propeller “top face” typically binds the
substrate, while specific motifs on the propeller’s “side” or its Nand C-terminal extensions mediate DDB1 docking.

Four DDB1-binding motifs are described: 1. WDxR (DWD box / WD40) : a conserved WD-D/ER/K motif within a WD40 repeat; the canonical DCAF recruitment motif. 2. H-box: a helical
motif (as in DDB2/XPE). 3. LIS1 homology (LisH) motif : found in some non-WD40 DCAFs. 4.
Helix-loop-helix / unique : CRBN and some others bind DDB1 via non-WD40 N-terminal regions.

subsections.title: Notable DCAFs and their biology
subsections.body:
• CRBN (Cereblon) : by far the most therapeutically important DCAF. Does NOT use a WD40
repeat to bind DDB1; instead, its N-terminal helical region docks into the BPA/BPB cleft. CRBN
is the direct binding target of thalidomide, lenalidomide, and pomalidomide (IMiDs) , which
bind in a C-terminal “thalidomide-binding domain” (TBD). IMiDs act as molecular glues: they
reshape the CRBN substrate-binding surface so that CRBN recruits neosubstrates it does not
normally bind. Key neosubstrates:
– IKZF1 and IKZF3 (Ikaros, Aiolos) : essential transcription factors in multiple myeloma and
certain lymphomas; their degradation is the primary mechanism of lenalidomide and pomalidomide antimyeloma activity (Krönke et al. 2014 Science; Lu et al. 2014 Science).
– CK1α (CSNK1A1) : degraded specifically by lenalidomide; explains clinical activity in
del(5q) MDS, which is haploinsufficient for CSNK1A1 (Krönke et al. 2015 Nature).
– GSPT1: degraded by CC-885 and related compounds; induces translation termination failure and apoptosis in AML (Matyskiela et al. 2016 Nature).
– SALL4: degraded by thalidomide; a neosubstrate implicated in thalidomide’s teratogenicity (Donovan et al. 2018; Matyskiela et al. 2018 Nat Chem Biol).
– CRBN-directed PROTACs (heterobifunctional degraders linking a CRBN ligand to a target
ligand) are a major drug discovery platform (ARV-471 for ER, ARV-110 for AR, etc.).
• DCAF15: target of indisulam and related aryl sulfonamides ; molecular glue that recruits
RBM39 (a splicing factor) for degradation (Han et al. 2017 Science; Uehara et al. 2017 Nat Chem
Biol). Clinical interest in AML and MDS where RBM39 degradation causes splicing catastrophe.
• VprBP/DCAF1: originally identified as the HIV-1 Vpr-binding protein that Vpr hijacks to
degrade unknown substrates (and arrest the cell cycle in G2/M). VprBP regulates TET2 (a
myeloid tumor suppressor) and associates with mTORC complexes, directly linking DCAF1
to hematopoietic biology . Not a straightforward substrate receptor in all contexts; some data
suggest it also has a kinase-like activity on histone H2AT120.
• DDB2 (XPE): DCAF that recognizes UV-induced CPDs and 6-4 photoproducts in DNA, initiating global genome nucleotide excision repair (GG-NER). Mutations cause xeroderma pigmentosum complementation group E. CRL4^DDB2 ubiquitinates XPC to amplify repair, and also
ubiquitinates histones H2A, H3, H4 at damage sites.
• CSA (ERCC8): DCAF in CRL4^CSA that mediates transcription-coupled NER (TC-NER) after
RNA Pol II stalling at lesions. Mutated in Cockayne syndrome.
• CDT2/DTL: DCAF that degrades CDT1 (replication licensing factor), p21, and SET8 specifically
during S phase, by recognizing substrates only when bound to PCNA via a PIP-degron (Havens
and Walter 2011). Couples substrate destruction to active replication.
• DCAF4: reported roles in telomere regulation (via KEAP1-independent effects) and lymphocyte function; its in vivo substrate repertoire is incompletely defined.

• DCAF8: implicated in giant axonal degeneration and cardiac hypertrophy contexts.
• DCAF11: regulates MHC class I and some cell cycle proteins; recruited by some PROTACs.
• DCAF7 (HAN11/WDR68) : the subject of this exam. Central DCAF of the hematopoietic
system (see Gao 2015 below). Unusual among DCAFs in that it has several reported
DDB1-independent functions, including scaffolding DYRK1A/B and HIPK kinases.
Key facts for the CRBN side :
• IMiDs and molecular glues reshape DCAF surfaces to recruit neosubstrates; they are the first
clinically validated example of pharmacological protein degradation.
• This validates the idea that any DCAF could, in principle, be a drug target if a small molecule
reshapes its surface. Thus DCAF7 is a priori a druggable node , which is a significant part of
why the Aifantis lab (and others) pursue it.

subsections.title: DCAFs in development and disease
subsections.body:
Beyond cancer, DCAFs underlie specific genetic diseases: - DDB2 (XPE): xeroderma pigmentosum complementation group E. - CSA (ERCC8): Cockayne syndrome A. - CUL4B: X-linked intellectual disability with cerebral malformation. - CRBN:

rare autosomal recessive nonsyndromic intellectual disability (original identification). - WDR62
(a DDB1-interacting WD40) : microcephaly . - DCAF17: Woodhouse-Sakati syndrome (hypogonadism, alopecia, diabetes, deafness).

sections.title: The Gao 2015 Paper: Foundation for DCAF7 in Hematopoiesis

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This paper (Gao et al., Cell Stem Cell 2015, Aifantis lab) is the cornerstone reference and must be
known in fine detail.

subsections.title: DDB1 interactome in hematopoietic vs. non-hematopoietic cells
subsections.body:
The authors performed

affinity purification-mass spectrometry (AP-MS) of FLAG-tagged DDB1 in three cell contexts: -
HEK293T (epithelial) - mouse embryonic stem cells (ESCs) - HL60 (human acute promyelocytic
leukemia, a surrogate for hematopoietic progenitors)
In 293T and ESCs, DDB1 coprecipitated a canonical DCAF spectrum including DCAF4, DCAF8,
DCAF11, DCAF15, VprBP/DCAF1 , DCAF2/CDT2, and others.
In HL60 cells, the AP-MS produced a striking result: DCAF7 was the sole DCAF reproducibly
detected; the other DCAFs were undetectable or far below stoichiometric levels. This is the cornerstone evidence that the hematopoietic lineage has a restricted, DCAF7-dominant CRL4 receptor
repertoire. This is not merely a transcriptional argument; DCAF7 is the DCAF that actually occupies DDB1 in hematopoietic cells.
Caveat: HL60 is a single leukemia line and AP-MS has sensitivity and stoichiometry
limits. The paper supports this finding with orthogonal approaches (DCAF7 overexpression, functional genetics), but a committee question might probe whether DCAF7
exclusivity is a quantitative or qualitative claim. The fair statement is that DCAF7 is
the dominant DCAF in HL60 and primary hematopoietic cells by AP-MS, though
other DCAFs may also be present at lower levels .

subsections.title: DDB1 loss-of-function phenotypes in hematopoiesis To ablate all CRL4 activity , Gao et
subsections.body:
al. conditionally deleted Ddb1 with two drivers: - V av-Cre(pan-hematopoietic, from E10.5): animals die perinatally by P7 with profound pancytopenia, loss of HSC/MPP populations, and decimated downstream progenitors. Fetal liver hematopoiesis collapses. This places CRL4 at the
top of the hematopoietic hierarchy as essential. - Mx1-Cre (IFN-inducible, pIpC-triggered): adultinduced deletion; animals die within 3 weeks with bone marrow failure, HSC depletion, and a
similar cascade of progenitor loss. This shows the requirement is adult-intrinsic, not only developmental, and directly couples CRL4 to an IFN-responsive Cre (note the IFN connection for a chair
in IFN signaling: Mx1-Cre uses the Mx1 promoter driven by type-I IFN response; pIpC triggers
IFN-α/β).
Importantly , both models produce a phenotype that is hematopoiesis-specific in the sense that
other Vav-Cre-expressing lineages are not primarily affected.

subsections.title: Mechanism: CDKN1A/p21 as a CRL4 substrate in HSPCs
subsections.body: Transcriptomic and proteomic analysis showed that CDKN1A (p21) accumulates in Ddb1-null HSPCs. To establish p21 as a direct CRL4 substrate: - p21 coimmunoprecipitates with CUL4A and DDB1. - p21 levels rise upon MG132 treatment in WT cells; in Ddb1-null cells, p21 is already elevated and MG132 produces no further increase (epistasis). - In vitro reconstitution shows CRL4 ubiquitinates p21.

subsections.title: p53 and p21 rescue experiments and their implications To test whether
subsections.body:
the Ddb1 phenotype is driven entirely by unchecked p21/p53 (the canonical CDKI axis), the authors performed

genetic rescue: - T rp53−/−background partially rescues: ST-HSC (short-term HSC) populations
are restored , but LT-HSC (long-term HSC) and MPP populations are not rescued . Lethality is
delayed but not prevented. - Cdkn1a (p21) −/− background does not rescue the HSPC loss.
Interpretation: the CRL4-DDB1 axis acts on a broader program than p21 or p53 alone. Some targets
are p53-dependent (restoration of ST-HSCs suggests a p53-mediated apoptosis branch), while LTHSC maintenance requires additional CRL4 substrates (likely including DCAF7-specific targets).
This is the opening that makes DCAF7 worth studying: the phenotype implicates substrates beyond the canonical CRL4-p21 axis.

subsections.title: ESC vs
subsections.body:
HSPC contrast In mouse ESCs, Ddb1 deletion causes differentiation without

apoptosis. In HSPCs, Ddb1 deletion causes apoptosis and HSC loss . This is a key conceptual
contrast: the same CRL4 complex executes opposite phenotypes depending on the cellular context
(i.e., the substrate landscape and the downstream network). It underscores that CRL4 is not
a generic housekeeper but a context-dependent fate regulator , which is exactly the niche a
DCAF7-focused thesis occupies.
Key facts:
• HL60 DDB1 AP-MS: DCAF7 is the sole DCAF detected; contrast with DCAF4/8/11/15/VprBP
in ESCs and 293T.
• Vav-Cre Ddb1-cKO: lethal by P7; Mx1-Cre Ddb1-cKO: lethal within 3 weeks.
• p21 is a bona fide CRL4 substrate (coIP , MG132 epistasis).
• Trp53−/− rescues ST-HSCs, not LT-HSCs/MPPs; p21−/− does not rescue.
• ESCs: differentiation without death; HSPCs: death. Context-dependent.
keyFacts: HL60 DDB1 AP-MS: DCAF7 is the sole DCAF detected; contrast with DCAF4/8/11/15/VprBP in ESCs and 293T.
Vav-Cre Ddb1-cKO: lethal by P7; Mx1-Cre Ddb1-cKO: lethal within 3 weeks.
p21 is a bona fide CRL4 substrate (coIP , MG132 epistasis).
Trp53−/− rescues ST-HSCs, not LT-HSCs/MPPs; p21−/− does not rescue.
ESCs: differentiation without death; HSPCs: death. Context-dependent.

sections.title: Integration: Why CRL4 and DCAF7 Matter

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
CRL4 complexes integrate signals across replication, genome integrity , and cell fate. In
hematopoiesis specifically , CRL4 via DCAF7 appears to coordinate the transition between quiescent self-renewal and proliferative stress responses. Given (1) the Gao 2015 evidence that DCAF7
is the dominant DCAF in hematopoietic cells, (2) the fact that DCAFs are pharmacologically
tractable (CRBN/IMiDs, DCAF15/indisulam), and (3) the broader CRL4 literature linking these
ligases to DNA damage and transcription, DCAF7 is positioned as a lineage-restricted substrate
receptor with potential therapeutic relevance in myeloid disease. Sections C and beyond will
dissect DCAF7 biochemistry , substrates, and hematopoietic function.
DCAF7 Biology

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
DCAF7 is a small, ancient, singleton WD40 β-propeller that operates as both a kinase scaffold and
a CRL4 substrate receptor, with a hematopoietic-selective CRL4 role documented by Gao 2015. Its
non-hematopoietic substrate repertoire (MEN1, LIG1, influenza PA, TFEB) establishes the biochemical plausibility of substrate-degradation activity in blood. The student’s proposal places IFIT3 as
the hematopoietic substrate, the loss of which in DCAF7-KO cells amplifies IFN signaling, produces MPN clone-selective vulnerability and synergy with IFN-α (including with the first-in-class
tool compound BC18630, Lear 2025), and in the developmental arm accounts for extramedullary
hematopoiesis and postnatal lethality when the P4 DCAF7 surge is eliminated. The model unifies disease and development under a single biochemistry: DCAF7 as a post-translationally tunable brake on IFN signaling in hematopoietic cells. Open questions — full substrate set, mutantselectivity mechanism, scaffolding/E3 coupling, developmental substrate identity — are tractable
with the experimental plans outlined, and the work sits at the exact intersection of UPS biology ,
IFN signaling, and hematopoiesis that defines the committee’s center of gravity .
Normal Hematopoiesis

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Human DCAF7: locus, structure, and transcript Human DCAF7 (Ensembl ENSG00000136485;
NCBI Gene ID 10238) is located on the long arm of chromosome 17 at band 17q23.2. This cytoband
is a hotspot in hematologic malignancy — 17q amplifications and rearrangements are recurrent in
AML and MDS — though DCAF7 itself has not been positionally implicated as a driver of those
events. The gene spans approximately 40 kb of genomic DNA and is organized into 12 exons
encoding a single well-supported protein-coding transcript of ~3.7 kb. The 5′ UTR is short and relatively GC-rich, consistent with a housekeeping-type promoter, and ChIP-seq data from ENCODE
show constitutive occupancy by general transcription factors along with STAT-family footprints in
cytokine-stimulated blood cells — a detail worth remembering given the IFN angle of the proposal.
Mouse Dcaf7: syntenic locus Mouse Dcaf7 (MGI:1914305) is located on chromosome 11, in a
region of conserved synteny with human 17q. The coding sequence is preserved exon-for-exon;
the mouse protein is identical in length to the human protein and differs by zero amino acids in
the mature ORF. This 100% amino acid conservation across mammals is a striking feature and is a
useful point to raise in committee — it argues strongly for a fundamental, non-redundant cellular
role.

The protein: size, domain content, predicted fold DCAF7 is 342 amino acids long and has a
calculated molecular weight of approximately 37–38 kDa; it runs near that position on SDS-PAGE.
Essentially the entire protein is composed of WD40 repeats. Sequence-based predictors (SMART,
Pfam PF00400) call seven WD40 repeats, each ~40 residues long, and structure predictors uniformly
return a seven-bladed β-propeller as the global fold.
Aliases and their historical origin DCAF7 is one of the more alias-rich E3 adapters in the literature. The student should be comfortable with all of the following names because different subfields
have not converged on a common usage:
• WDR68 (WD40 Repeat Domain 68). The structural-genomics name, emphasizing the fold.
Used by the kinase-biology literature (Miyata & Nishida, Glenewinkel) and in zebrafish developmental biology .
• HAN11 (Human homolog of ANkyrin repeat 11). A historical misnomer. The original cDNA
was cloned as a homolog of the Arabidopsis AN11 gene, which was assumed at the time to
be ankyrin-repeat based on a superficial reading of its sequence. In fact, AN11 is WD40, not
ankyrin. The “HAN11” name persists in the MEKK1/HIPK2 signaling literature (Ritterhoff
2010).
• an11 in Petunia hybrida. This is the original name of the gene. an11 (anthocyanin 11) was identified in the 1990s as a pigment-pathway regulator in petunia flowers, where it acts as a WD40
co-activator of a bHLH–MYB transcriptional complex controlling anthocyanin biosynthesis.
That the mammalian ortholog rescued petunia an11 mutants was the first demonstration of
the gene’s deep evolutionary conservation.
• Crn (Drosophila homolog). Used rarely; reflects the same core fold.
• WDR68 in zebrafish, where it is required for craniofacial development (Nissen 2006; later work
by Wang et al.).
Key facts — nomenclature
• DCAF7 = WDR68 = HAN11 = mammalian AN11 . These are the same gene.
• The “HAN11” name is a misnomer (WD40, not ankyrin).
• When reading papers, always check the alias — kinase biologists use WDR68, plant biologists
use AN11, ubiquitin/CRL biologists use DCAF7.
Evolutionary conservation Across mammals, DCAF7 is 100% conserved at the amino acid level.
Conservation to Drosophila Crn is ~60% identity; to petunia AN11, ~45% over the WD40 core. A
gene with this conservation profile almost always performs a biochemical task that cannot be duplicated by a paralog and that the cell cannot afford to tune away . DCAF7 has no close paralogs
in the mammalian genome — it is essentially a singleton among WD40 DCAFs.
Expression DCAF7 mRNA and protein are broadly expressed (GTEx, Human Protein Atlas),
with notably high expression in hematopoietic lineages: HSPCs, monocytes, and megakaryocytes
cluster high. In mouse bone marrow, DCAF7 protein rises postnatally , with a measurable surge
around P4 (postnatal day 4) in LSK-enriched populations — a point of considerable importance
to the proposal’s developmental arm. Cancer Dependency Map (DepMap) CERES/Chronos data

show that blood-lineage cell lines (AML, MPN, lymphoma) score DCAF7 as a preferential dependency relative to solid-tumor lines, with the signal strongest in JAK2/MPN-relevant models. This
pan-cell-line blood-lineage bias is the independent line of evidence the student can point to when
asked whether DCAF7’s hematopoietic role is just an artifact of a particular model.
Key facts — gene and protein
• 342 aa, ~37 kDa, seven-bladed WD40 β-propeller.
• Human 17q23.2; mouse chr 11; identical protein sequences.
• No paralog; 100% mammalian conservation.
• Blood-enriched expression; DepMap blood-line dependency; P4 postnatal surge in mouse BM.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
WD40 architecture, generalized WD40 repeats are ~40-residue modules, each ending in the
canonical Trp-Asp dipeptide that gives the family its name. Each repeat folds into a four-stranded
antiparallel β-sheet (blade). Seven blades pack around a pseudo-sevenfold symmetry axis to form
a closed β-propeller with a narrow central pore. WD40 propellers are among the most common
protein-interaction folds in eukaryotes; they present three distinct interaction surfaces:
• The top face (near the D-A loops between the outer β-strands of adjacent blades) — the canonical substrate / partner-binding surface.
• The bottom face (near the B-C loops) — less commonly used for interaction, sometimes engaged.
• The circumferential edge — used in several DCAF–DDB1 interactions.
The substrate-binding face For DCAF WD40 propellers, the top face is generally the substratebinding surface. DCAF1 (VprBP), DCAF15, and CRBN all present substrate recognition determinants on or near the top face. For DCAF7 specifically , there is no crystal structure with a bound
substrate, but alanine scanning (from Lear 2025 Sci Adv , in the TFEB context) and AlphaFold modeling both localize predicted substrate contact residues to the top face.
The DDB1-binding face and the WDXR motif question DDB1 engages most DCAFs through a
characteristic “H-box” helix and/or a short WDXR motif embedded in a specific position within
the WD40 repeat. Jin et al. (2006, Mol Cell) classified DCAFs based on the presence of this motif; WDXR-positive DCAFs bind DDB1 through a relatively stereotyped interface, while WDXRnegative DCAFs use alternative contacts — typically an extended H-box region or a distinct surface
patch on the propeller bottom.
DCAF7 lacks a canonical WDXR motif. This is an important structural feature. It implies:
• DCAF7 uses an alternate mode of DDB1 engagement.
• The DCAF7–DDB1 interface is therefore structurally distinct from, e.g., DDB2–DDB1 or CSA–
DDB1.
• A potentially druggable non-canonical interface exists, which is exactly the premise of the
BC18630 small-molecule program.

Predicted DDB1-binding surface and the BC18630 site Lear et al. (2025, Sci Adv) identified a
set of surface residues on DCAF7 that, when mutated to alanine, abolish binding of the small
molecule BC18630 and, critically , also diminish the DCAF7–TFEB and DCAF7–DDB1 interactions
in the coronavirus-infection setting. The residues cluster on one edge of the propeller in a patch
that is neither the canonical WDXR site nor the classical top-face substrate groove. This patch is a
plausible substrate-receptor interface and, importantly , it is the structural basis for tool-compound
development.
Crystal structure status There is no published high-resolution crystal or cryo-EM structure of
full-length DCAF7, either alone or in complex with DDB1 or a substrate. The available structural
information is therefore:
• Sequence-based predictions of seven WD40 repeats.
• AlphaFold2 / AlphaFold3 models of DCAF7 monomer (high confidence for the propeller core,
lower confidence for the Nand C-terminal tails).
• AlphaFold-Multimer models of DCAF7–DDB1, DCAF7–DYRK1A, DCAF7–IFIT3 — useful for
hypothesis generation but not a substitute for experimental structure.
• Indirect experimental constraints from mutational analyses (Lear 2025; Miyata & Nishida 2011
for DYRK1A binding).
This is an area of opportunity: solving a DCAF7–DDB1 structure would be a significant contribution and is technically within reach using the strategies that worked for DCAF1 and DCAF15.
Post-translational modifications PhosphoSitePlus catalogs multiple phosphorylation sites on
DCAF7, most prominently at clustered serines in the N-terminal tail (Ser3, Ser7-region) and at
surface-exposed sites on blades 1 and 7. The functional consequences of most of these sites are
unresolved. Ubiquitination sites (K-GG proteomics) are also cataloged but their functional significance is unknown; notably , DCAF7 as a DCAF is not typically a substrate of its own E3 complex,
though auto-ubiquitination within CRL4 complexes has precedent (e.g., CRBN).
Key facts — structural biology
• Seven-bladed WD40 β-propeller; no published experimental structure of full-length DCAF7.
• Lacks the canonical WDXR DDB1-binding motif (per Jin 2006 classification); uses an alternate
DDB1 interface.
• BC18630 binds a non-canonical surface patch mapped by mutagenesis (Lear 2025).
• Substrate-binding face is likely the top face (D-A loops), as with most DCAFs.

sections.title: Non-hematopoietic functions — the full biochemical repertoire

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A serious treatment of DCAF7 biology requires understanding its well-characterized roles outside blood, because they constrain the mechanistic possibilities in hematopoiesis. DCAF7 has two,
perhaps three, biochemically distinct modes: (a) it is a scaffold for DYRK/HIPK/MEKK-family
kinases, (b) it is a CRL4 substrate receptor for specific protein substrates, and (c) it has been implicated in Wnt/β-catenin signaling through less clear mechanisms.

subsections.title: Kinase scaffolding — the original DCAF7 function
subsections.body: Before DCAF7 was recognized as a CRL4 substrate receptor, it was known as a scaffold for the DYRK family . • Miyata & Nishida (2011, Biochem J) showed that WDR68 binds DYRK1A through a specific interaction with the DYRK1A “DH-box” (DYRK Homology box), a motif N-terminal of the kinase domain. WDR68 binding promotes DYRK1A nuclear localization and has non-catalytic consequences for DYRK1A function. • Ritterhoff et al. (2010, EMBO J) identified HAN11 as a scaffold connecting MEKK1 (MAP3K1) and HIPK2 in a stress-signaling module. HAN11 knockdown diminishes MEKK1-driven HIPK2 activation, establishing a true adapter role. • Glenewinkel et al. (2016, Sci Rep) showed that DCAF7 is the adapter that bridges the adenovirus E1A oncoprotein to DYRK1A and HIPK2. E1A hijacks DCAF7 to bring these kinases into proximity for viral purposes, confirming the scaffolding activity genetically (the interaction is direct and can be mapped biochemically). • Yu et al. (2019) reported that DCAF7 regulates RNA Pol II C-terminal-domain phosphorylation via DYRK1A in myogenic differentiation — linking DCAF7 scaffolding to transcription and lineage commitment. The consequence of this kinase-scaffolding role is that DCAF7 influences an extended signaling network: • HIPK2 — p53 stabilization at Ser46, apoptosis under genotoxic stress. • DYRK1A — cell-cycle entry , tau phosphorylation, chromatin (a candidate for Down syndrome phenotypes given 21q22 location). • DYRK1B — quiescence maintenance, myogenesis. • MEKK1 (MAP3K1) — JNK activation, inflammatory stress responses. An unresolved question — and an excellent committee question — is whether DCAF7’s kinasescaffolding and CRL4-substrate-receptor roles are independent, competitive, or cooperative. Three non-exclusive models exist: • Independent: two separate biochemical activities using different surfaces of the propeller. • Recruitment-for-degradation: DCAF7 brings these kinases to CRL4 for controlled turnover. • Context-switched: the same DCAF7 molecule switches modes depending on post-translational state, binding partners, or localization. The field has not converged. Both scaffolding and CRL4-recruitment evidence exists; contextdependence is the most parsimonious integration.

subsections.title: T umor suppressor reversal in PanNET — Xu et al. 2023 Xu
subsections.body: and colleagues (Cancer Res 2023) reported that a CUL4B–DCAF7 complex ubiquitinates MEN1 (menin), targeting it for proteasomal degradation. This activity is neddylation-dependent (i.e., requires an active cullin) and is elevated in pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (PanNET), where MEN1 is a canonical tumor suppressor whose loss drives tumor progression. Three implications for the MPN story: • DCAF7 is bona fide a CRL4 substrate receptor capable of driving substrate degradation. • DCAF7 can be oncogenic when its substrate is a tumor suppressor. • The same biochemistry (DDB1–DCAF7–substrate ￿ K48 ubiquitination ￿ degradation) plausibly operates in hematopoiesis, with IFIT3 rather than MEN1 as the relevant substrate. This paper is also important because it anchors the CRL4B (cullin 4B) specificity — DCAF7 can work with either CUL4A or CUL4B, and the relevant complex in hematopoiesis is not strictly defined.

subsections.title: DNA replication and damage — DNA
subsections.body: Ligase I (Peng 2016) Peng et al. (JBC 2016) demonstrated that CUL4–DDB1–DCAF7 ubiquitinates DNA Ligase I (LIG1) , the replicative DNA ligase responsible for joining Okazaki fragments and sealing nicks in the lagging strand. LIG1 turnover is cell-cycle-coupled, peaking around S phase entry . DCAF7-mediated LIG1 degradation therefore positions DCAF7 in the replication machinery and, by extension, in the DNA damage response — consistent with broader roles of CRL4 in replication (e.g., CRL4-CDT2 and p21/SET8). This is an underappreciated DCAF7 function but matters because IFN signaling and replication stress cross-talk, particularly in the JAK2V617F context where aberrant cytokine signaling intersects with replication stress.

subsections.title: HCC and Wnt/β-catenin DCAF7 has been implicated in hepatocellular carcinoma through effects on β-catenin
subsections.body:
stability

The mechanism is less crisp than in the PanNET or LIG1 settings —
reports suggest DCAF7 stabilizes β-catenin or modulates its downstream transcriptional output,
but direct biochemical substrate assignment has been more contested. The HCC literature serves
mainly to reinforce that DCAF7 has oncogenic activity in multiple tissue contexts; the mechanistic
details are less firmly established than the PanNET, LIG1, or antiviral arms.

subsections.title: Antiviral function against influenza — Yu 2025 Yu et al. (J
subsections.body: Virol 2025) established that DCAF7 is a substrate receptor in a CRL4B–DDB1–DCAF7 complex that K48-polyubiquitinates the influenza A virus PA polymerase subunit , targeting it for proteasomal degradation. Consequences: • DCAF7 restricts influenza A replication; knockdown enhances viral titer, overexpression restricts it. • The mechanism is classic CRL4-substrate targeting — a viral protein is the substrate, but the biochemistry is the familiar K48 degradation pathway . • DCAF7 is therefore ahost antiviral factor, a role that is conceptually distinct from its scaffolding or developmental functions. Citation caveat (important for the student). This paper is sometimes cited in the proposal and in earlier drafts for “DCAF7 as kinase scaffold.” That is incorrect — Yu 2025 is specifically about DCAF7 acting as a substrate-receptor antiviral factor, targeting a viral protein for degradation. The scaffolding citations are Miyata & Nishida 2011, Ritterhoff 2010, and Glenewinkel 2016. Keep these straight; the committee will notice.

subsections.title: Small-molecule inhibitors — Lear 2025 Sci Adv and BC18630
subsections.body: A major field-level development is the advent of tool compounds targeting DCAF7. Historically , most DCAFs have been undruggable by virtue of the WD40 fold — there are few pockets and no precedent, with two famous exceptions: CRBN (bound by lenalidomide and pomalidomide, and the foundation of molecular glue drug discovery) and DCAF15 (bound by indisulam, the original arylsulfonamide degrader). DCAF7 has been tool-free until recently . • Lear et al. (2025, Sci Adv) , with Dziak and collaborators, described BC18813 and the more advanced analog BC18630 as small-molecule DCAF7 inhibitors. • The compounds were developed in the context of coronavirus infection , where DCAF7 promotes the degradation of TFEB (master regulator of autophagy/lysosomal biogenesis). BC18630 disrupts the DCAF7–TFEB interaction, stabilizing TFEB and restoring autophagy during infection. • Surface mutagenesis — alanine scanning of a specific propeller patch — abolishes BC18630 binding, defining the compound’s site. • In the student’s preliminary data, BC18630 shows an IC50 ~1.80 μM in HEL cells (JAK2V617F MPN model), with synergy with IFN-α (Loewe score ~7.15). The significance is substantial. BC18630 is, along with lenalidomide and indisulam, one of only a handful of small-molecule DCAF modulators, and it is currently the only one relevant to DCAF7. Its existence makes the DCAF7 target chemically actionable in ways that most DCAFs are not. Key facts — non-hematopoietic repertoire • DCAF7 acts as (i) a kinase scaffold (DYRK1A/1B, HIPK2, MEKK1), (ii) a CRL4 substrate receptor (MEN1, LIG1, influenza PA, TFEB), and (iii) a suspected modulator of β-catenin (HCC). • Substrate set spans tumor suppressors, replication machinery , viral proteins, and autophagy regulators — i.e., DCAF7 is not substrate-narrow. • BC18630 (Lear 2025) is the first published DCAF7 tool compound; binds a non-canonical surface patch. • Yu 2025 is about substrate-receptor activity (not kinase scaffolding) — a common misciting to avoid.

subsections.title: Overview
DCAF7 as the hematopoietic-selective DDB1 partner — Gao et al. 2015 Gao et al.
subsections.body: (2015) performed DDB1 immunoprecipitation followed by mass spectrometry in multiple cell types, asking a simple question: which DCAF adapters are bound to DDB1 in each lineage? • In HL60 (promyelocytic/AML line), DCAF7 was the only DCAF detected bound to DDB1 above background. • In HEK293T and mouse ESCs , DCAF7 was not detected; instead, DDB1 pulled down DCAF4, DCAF8, DCAF11, DCAF15, and VprBP (DCAF1). The straightforward interpretation is that CRL4 substrate-receptor repertoires are tissue-specific at the DCAF level , and that DCAF7 is the hematopoietic-dominant adapter. This is the entry point for the entire project: if the DDB1–DCAF7 complex is the functional CRL4 in blood cells, its substrates (whatever they turn out to be) are the hematopoietic CRL4 outputs. Two open questions that the committee will want addressed: • Is DCAF7 protein expressed but not DDB1-bound in non-blood cells , or is non-blood expression genuinely lower? Expression data (GTEx, HPA) support the latter but do not exclude the former. • If multiple DCAFs compete for DDB1 , what determines the blood-lineage shift toward DCAF7 — transcriptional, post-translational, or availability/competition? No clean answer in the literature.

subsections.title: DCAF7 in MPN — the student’s preliminary data in detail
subsections.body: This is the heart of the proposal. The student should be able to describe every experiment, the control, the readout, and the interpretation. mRNA expression in MPN CD34+ cells. Mining of the Norfo 2014 and Rampal 2014 transcriptomic datasets (both are CD34+ MPN patient cohorts) shows DCAF7 mRNA upregulation relative to healthy controls. Important caveat : the main body text of neither paper explicitly highlights DCAF7 as a differentially expressed gene; the signal is visible in the supplementary differentialexpression tables. The student should verify this in the primary supplementary data before relying on the claim in committee — saying “Norfo and Rampal reported DCAF7 upregulation” without the caveat is a trap, because the committee may pull up the papers live. Functional dependency in JAK2V617F HEL cells. • DCAF7 loss (CRISPR knockout or shRNA) in HEL cells (JAK2V617F+) reduces proliferation substantially . • In WT control cells (e.g., CD34+ cord blood, WT cell lines), DCAF7 loss is tolerated — essentially no proliferation defect. • This selective dependency profile — fine in WT, lethal in mutant — mirrors a synthetic-lethal relationship and is the core therapeutic premise. In vivo conditional knockout. • Model: Jak2V617F; Mx1-Cre; Dcaf7fl/fl . • Mx1-Cre is induced with pI:pC, yielding a hematopoietic-restricted DCAF7 deletion on a JAK2V617F background. • Phenotype: reduced WBC, reduced PLT, reduced spleen size (splenomegaly is the MPN phenotype; DCAF7 loss attenuates it). • Lineage analysis: loss of MPs (myeloid progenitors) and MEPs (megakaryocyte-erythroid progenitors); preservation of HSC and MPP compartments . • Interpretation: DCAF7 is required for MPN clone expansion at the committed progenitor level but not for HSC maintenance — a favorable therapeutic window. TMT proteomics on DCAF7-KO HEL cells. • Quantitative tandem-mass-tag proteomics comparing DCAF7-KO vs WT HEL cells. • Top accumulated protein: IFIT3 (Interferon-Induced protein with Tetratricopeptide repeats 3). • Several other ISG-associated proteins also accumulate but IFIT3 is the strongest hit. • Interpretation: IFIT3 is a candidate DCAF7 substrate in MPN cells. Cycloheximide chase. • In DCAF7-KO HEL cells, the IFIT3 half-life is extended relative to WT (post-translational stabilization). • Interpretation: consistent with IFIT3 being degraded by a DCAF7-dependent E3 complex. T ranscriptional control. • RNA-seq shows IFIT3 mRNA is unchanged between DCAF7-KO and WT HEL. • This is a critical control: the IFIT3 accumulation is post-translational, not transcriptional. IFN-α synergy. • Genetic: DCAF7-KO HEL cells and Jak2V617F BM from the Mx1-Cre KO are hypersensitive to IFN-α. • Pharmacological: BC18630 + IFN-α in HEL cells give a Loewe synergy score of ~7.15 (above the conventional threshold of ~5 for called synergy). • Interpretation: DCAF7 inhibition and IFN-α potentiate each other — consistent with a model in which DCAF7 dampens IFN signaling. scRNA-seq on LSKs from the conditional KO. • Mouse LSK cells from Jak2V617F; Mx1-Cre; Dcaf7fl/fl . • Transcriptional signature shows upregulation of ISGs : Ifit3, Isg15, Mx1, Irf7. • Upstream sensors and adapters also upregulated: Ifnar1, Ifngr1, Stat3 . • Hallmark GSEA: IFN Alpha Response signature is significantly enriched. • Interpretation: DCAF7 loss derepresses the IFN-α transcriptional program in HSPCs, consistent with IFIT3 stabilization and a broader enhancement of IFN signaling. Key facts — MPN preliminary data • DCAF7 is upregulated (mRNA) in MPN CD34+ cells per Norfo 2014 / Rampal 2014 supplementary data (not in main text). • DCAF7 loss selectively impairs JAK2V617F cells (HEL) while sparing WT; in vivo attenuates MPN phenotype while preserving HSC/MPP . • IFIT3 is the top accumulated protein in DCAF7-KO HEL proteomics; stabilized posttranslationally (CHX chase); unchanged at mRNA. • DCAF7 loss sensitizes to IFN-α (genetic and pharmacological, Loewe ~7.15). • scRNA-seq shows ISG and receptor upregulation in Jak2V617F; Mx1-Cre; Dcaf7fl/fl LSKs.

subsections.title: DCAF7 in development — the V av-Cre KO phenotype
subsections.body: The developmental arm of the proposal uses a complementary genetic model: Dcaf7fl/fl; Vav-Cre . Vav-Cre drives excision in hematopoietic cells from E10.5–E12.5 onward, yielding a much earlier and constitutively hematopoietic knockout than Mx1-Cre. • ~30–40% postnatal lethality in the Vav-Cre KO. • Normal E14.5 fetal liver hematopoiesis — no HSC frequency defect in fetal liver; no lineage skewing at that timepoint. • Death in the first postnatal week — before weaning, in a narrow window. • The timing of lethality coincides with the P4 surge in DCAF7 protein (from MGD/public proteomic data), suggesting that the P4 surge corresponds to a developmental requirement. • P4 KO analysis : – LSK expansion in spleen and liver — extramedullary hematopoiesis that should not be present at this age. – CD11b+ myeloid expansion in BM — myeloid-biased differentiation. – Consistent with a failure to complete the fetal-to-adult hematopoietic transition on schedule. The mechanistic hypothesis linking this to the IFN arm is the perinatal IFN pulse described by Li et al. 2020 (and consistent with a broader literature on fetal/perinatal IFN signaling). The pulse peaks around E16–E18.5 and extends into the first postnatal days, temporally overlapping the P4 DCAF7 surge. The model is: • In normal development, the perinatal IFN pulse primes HSC emergence and lineage specification. • DCAF7’s P4 surge acts as a molecular timer that clips the IFN pulse by degrading IFIT3 (and potentially other ISG effectors). • In the Vav-Cre KO, the surge does not occur, the IFN pulse is not terminated on time, and the consequence is prolonged or amplified IFN signaling in HSPCs ￿ extramedullary expansion and myeloid bias. • The postnatal lethality reflects the inability to complete the perinatal hematopoietic transition. This is an attractive integrative model because it uses the same molecular mechanism (DCAF7 ￿ IFIT3 degradation ￿ IFN signaling dampening) in development and in disease, with opposite valence — the developmental phenotype reflects loss of a normally protective brake, while the MPN phenotype reflects the clone exploiting that brake to escape IFN-α therapy . Key facts — development • Vav-Cre KO: ~30–40% postnatal lethality; normal fetal liver; death in week 1. • P4 KO phenotype: LSK expansion in spleen/liver; BM myeloid bias. • Coincides with the P4 DCAF7 surge and the tail of the perinatal IFN pulse (Li 2020). • Hypothesis: DCAF7 normally terminates the perinatal IFN pulse via IFIT3 degradation.

subsections.title: DCAF7 in pediatric myeloid disease — DS-AMKL and the L340F mutation
subsections.body: Independent human genetic evidence reinforces the hematopoietic importance of DCAF7. • DS-AMKL (Down syndrome-associated acute megakaryoblastic leukemia) harbors recurrent DCAF7 L340F mutations in roughly 5–10% of cases . • The L340F mutation is located in the C-terminal tail and is reported to disrupt a PDZ-binding motif, potentially altering DCAF7 interactions with a subset of partners. • Kirsammer et al. (2016, Blood) developed mouse models of DS-AMKL biology that are the relevant in vivo context. Citation caveat — the student should verify this before relying on it in committee. The proposal cites “Yoshida 2013 (Nat Genet)” for the L340F recurrence claim. However: • The 2013 Nat Genet paper on megakaryoblastic leukemia genomics is Sakaguchi et al. 2013 , with Yoshida as a middle author. • The main gene list in Sakaguchi 2013 does not foreground DCAF7. • The primary citation for the L340F recurrence is Labuhn et al. 2019 (Cancer Cell) , which reported DS-AMKL exomes and specifically called out DCAF7 mutations. • The student should cross-check the primary sequencing data in Labuhn 2019 supplementary tables and cite Labuhn 2019 as the principal source, mentioning Sakaguchi/Yoshida 2013 only with care. The significance of DS-AMKL evidence is that it provides human clinical validation that DCAF7 function is important for blood malignancy — not just in mouse models or in dependency screens, but in actual patients, recurrently , in a defined subtype.

sections.title: Integrative synthesis — connecting DCAF7 to IFN biology, MPN, and development

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A single mechanistic framework connects the disparate pieces:
Central model. DCAF7 acts as a post-translationally tunable brake on interferon signaling in
hematopoietic cells by serving as the CRL4 substrate receptor that drives K48-polyubiquitination
and proteasomal degradation of IFIT3 (and likely a small set of additional ISG effectors). IFIT3 is an
IFN-induced tetratricopeptide protein that acts as a scaffold amplifying antiviral and antiproliferative outputs (forming complexes with IFIT1/IFIT2, promoting STAT1/STAT2 output). By clipping
IFIT3, DCAF7 shortens the duration of the ISG response and dampens signaling amplitude. This
is a negative feedback loop distinct from the canonical transcriptional negative regulators (USP18,
SOCS1/3).
In MPN. The JAK2V617F clone is under chronic autocrine/paracrine cytokine stress and would be
sensitive to IFN signaling if unbuffered. The clone upregulates DCAF7 mRNA (Norfo/Rampal
supplementary data), presumably to enforce tighter IFIT3 clipping, dampen the basal ISG response,
and escape the effects of IFN-α therapy — which is a mainstay of MPN treatment. DCAF7 inhibition (genetic or BC18630) de-represses IFIT3, restores the IFN response, and re-sensitizes the MPN
clone to IFN-α. The Loewe synergy between BC18630 and IFN-α (~7.15) reflects this mechanism.
In perinatal development. During the E16–P5 window, a physiologic perinatal IFN pulse (Li
2020) primes HSC emergence, lineage specification, and the fetal-to-adult hematopoietic transition.
This pulse must be terminated on time to avoid excessive myeloid priming and extramedullary
hematopoiesis. The P4 surge in DCAF7 expression corresponds to the window in which the pulse
is normally shut off, and the Vav-Cre KO — where the surge cannot occur — shows exactly the
phenotype expected if the pulse persists: extramedullary LSK expansion, BM myeloid bias, and
postnatal lethality .
A unified view. The same biochemistry (DCAF7 ￿ IFIT3 degradation ￿ IFN signaling dampening) is used in two opposite contexts: - In disease, the mechanism is co-opted by the MPN clone

to evade IFN-α therapy . Inhibition is therapeutic. - In development, the mechanism is required
to terminate a physiologic IFN pulse . Loss is pathologic.
This duality is what makes DCAF7 a therapeutic target rather than simply a tumor suppressor or
oncogene. The therapeutic window reflects (i) the selective mutant-cell dependency and (ii) the
HSC sparing in Mx1-Cre, with developmental risk only in the context of perinatal perturbation —
adult patients are outside the window.
Non-IFN substrates almost certainly exist. The proteomics identifies IFIT3 as the top hit,
but the complete substrate set is not defined, and several DCAF7 functions documented elsewhere (MEN1 in PanNET, LIG1 in replication, PA in influenza, TFEB in coronavirus, plus the
