DYRK/HIPK/MEKK kinase scaffolding) argue that DCAF7 has a broader substrate repertoire.
Unbiased substrate discovery (e.g., BioID proximity labeling, TurboID, or neddylation-dependent
K-GG proteomics comparing DCAF7-WT vs DCAF7-KO with MLN4924 parallel) will be needed
to fully enumerate the substrate set in hematopoietic cells.
Key facts — integrative model
• DCAF7 is a tunable post-translational brake on IFN signaling in hematopoietic cells, via IFIT3
degradation.
• MPN: clone upregulates DCAF7 ￿ tighter brake ￿ IFN-α escape. Inhibition restores sensitivity (synergy with IFN-α).
• Development: P4 DCAF7 surge terminates the perinatal IFN pulse; without it, pulse persists
￿ extramedullary hematopoiesis and lethality .
• The IFIT3 model does not exclude additional substrates; the DCAF7 substrate set in
hematopoiesis is likely broader and awaits unbiased discovery .

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A qualifying exam committee will ask explicitly about what is not known. The student should be
prepared for each of the following.
Is IFIT3 the only relevant MPN substrate? Almost certainly not. IFIT3 is the top TMT hit, but
the proteomics is thresholded and dynamic range-limited, and biologically meaningful substrates
often sit outside the top 10. Unbiased substrate discovery — comparing DCAF7-WT vs DCAF7KO cells under MLN4924 (to stabilize CRL substrates), combined with BioID or TurboID to capture
transient substrates, and K-GG proteomics to enrich ubiquitinated species — is the priority . The
student should be able to articulate this experimental plan.
Why is DCAF7 selectively required in JAK2V617F vs WT cells? Two non-exclusive explanations:
• Elevated CRL4-DCAF7 activity in the mutant clone , consistent with DCAF7 mRNA upregulation. A larger E3 complex pool processes a larger ISG load, and the mutant becomes addicted
to the brake.

• Substrate-level stress specific to the mutant, where JAK2V617F drives chronic IFN/JAK-STAT
tone that generates constitutive IFIT3 induction. The mutant has more substrate to clear per
unit time.
Distinguishing these requires measuring CRL4-DCAF7 complex abundance, neddylation state,
and IFIT3 flux in matched WT vs JAK2V617F cells.
Scaffolding vs E3-adapter — competitive, cooperative, or distinct? This is the most structurally
interesting open question. Three models:
• Independent, spatially separated — the propeller has enough surface to accommodate both activities on different faces; scaffolding and CRL4 assembly happen in distinct subcellular pools.
• Mutually exclusive, context-switched — DCAF7 can only do one at a time; regulation (PTMs,
partner availability , localization) picks the mode.
• Coupled — kinase substrates (DYRK1A, HIPK2, MEKK1) are themselves CRL4-DCAF7 substrates in some contexts; scaffolding is the first step in regulated turnover.
The proposal does not resolve this; experiments comparing DCAF7 mutants that disrupt one activity but not the other would.
How does hematopoietic-selective DDB1–DCAF7 binding arise? The Gao 2015 finding implies
a tissue-specific regulator — a cofactor, a post-translational modification, or a competing binder
— that biases DDB1 toward DCAF7 in blood cells and away in others. Candidates:
• Lineage-specific DCAF7 PTMs that enhance DDB1 affinity .
• Lineage-specific competition, e.g., a solid-tissue DCAF that outcompetes DCAF7 and is absent
in blood.
• Differential DCAF7 expression — the simplest hypothesis but insufficient to explain complete
selectivity .
Addressing this requires quantitative, lineage-matched DDB1 IPs with stoichiometric MS.
What is the developmental substrate at the P4 window? IFIT3 is the MPN substrate, and the
perinatal-IFN-pulse model extends IFIT3 to development. But the P4 window might also involve
a developmentally specific factor — a fetal identity regulator that must be cleared for adult
hematopoiesis to proceed. Candidates include HMGA2, LIN28B, or an unknown substrate. Distinguishing “IFIT3 clipping” from “fetal identity factor clearance” requires substrate proteomics
in matched fetal-liver vs P4-BM HSPCs from WT and Vav-Cre KO.
Compensatory ISG induction vs defective clearance In the DCAF7-KO scRNA-seq, ISGs and
IFN receptors are both upregulated. This could reflect (i) genuine de-repression due to IFIT3 stabilization driving a positive feedback loop; (ii) compensatory transcriptional induction responding
to some other defect; or (iii) both. Time-resolved experiments (IFN-α stimulation kinetics in WT
vs KO) would separate these.

The L340F mutation — gain or loss? DS-AMKL L340F disrupts a PDZ-binding motif in the
DCAF7 C-terminus. Whether L340F is loss-of-function, gain-of-function, or a subtle rewiring of
partner selection is not definitively established. Modeling L340F in iPSC-derived megakaryocytes
and in mouse KI lines is the obvious experiment; interpretation should be cautious until substrate/partner profiling is done.
Key facts — open questions
• Unbiased substrate discovery (BioID + K-GG + MLN4924) is the priority for completing the
MPN mechanism.
• Mutant-selective dependency likely reflects elevated CRL4-DCAF7 activity , substrate load, or
both.
• Scaffolding vs E3-adapter coupling is unresolved and structurally fundamental.
• The developmental substrate at P4 may differ from or overlap with IFIT3.
• L340F mechanism (GOF vs LOF) is open.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Hematopoiesis is the developmental and homeostatic process by which a small population of
hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) generates, throughout the lifetime of the organism, the ten or
so mature blood lineages: erythrocytes, megakaryocytes/platelets, granulocytes (neutrophils,
eosinophils, basophils), monocytes/macrophages, dendritic cells, mast cells, B lymphocytes, T
lymphocytes, innate lymphoid cells, and NK cells. In an adult human, approximately 10^11
to 10^12 mature blood cells are produced per day , and this enormous output is sustained by a
numerically rare stem cell compartment that must balance self-renewal, quiescence, multilineage
differentiation, and responsiveness to acute and chronic stress. For a dissertation that examines
DCAF7 function in hematopoiesis, a rigorous understanding of HSC ontogeny , hierarchy , niche
biology , cell-cycle regulation, lineage specification, and stress response is foundational: DCAF7

loss-of-function phenotypes can only be interpreted against the backdrop of the canonical HSC
biology outlined below, and the committee chair’s expertise in the ubiquitin-proteasome system
(UPS) and type I interferon (IFN) signaling in hematopoiesis makes the intersections between
these pathways and HSC biology (Sections D5, D7, D9) particularly important to master.

sections.title: Developmental ontogeny of the hematopoietic system

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Mammalian hematopoiesis occurs in successive anatomic waves that are spatially and temporally
distinct and that generate qualitatively different progenitor and stem cell populations. The canonical division is between primitive hematopoiesis (transient, embryonic, principally erythroid) and
definitive hematopoiesis (adult-type, multilineage, HSC-derived). The mouse is the dominant experimental system and the timeline below refers to mouse embryonic days (E), with corresponding
human stages noted in parentheses where relevant.

subsections.title: Primitive hematopoiesis in the yolk sac (E7.0–E9.0)
subsections.body: The first blood cells appear in the extraembryonic yolk sac blood islands at approximately E7.0–E7.5 in the mouse (equivalent to ~day 16–18 of human gestation). These blood islands are derived from mesodermal precursors that coemerge with vascular endothelium, giving rise to the “hemangioblast” concept (Choi et al. 1998). Primitive erythrocytes are large, nucleated, and express embryonic hemoglobins (εy , βH1, ζ in mouse; ε, ζ in human) rather than adult α/β chains. These cells enter circulation at E8.25 when the heart begins beating and mature in the bloodstream, eventually enucleating around E12–E14 (Kingsley et al. 2004). In parallel, the yolk sac produces erythro-myeloid progenitors (EMPs) from E8.25 onward, which generate BFU-E (burst-forming unit erythroid), CFU-E, macrophages, and megakaryocytes but are not HSCs: they cannot long-term repopulate irradiated adult recipients. EMPs seed the fetal liver and contribute the first wave of definitive (adult-type) erythrocytes and, importantly , give rise to tissue-resident macrophages including microglia, Kupffer cells, and Langerhans cells that persist into adulthood (Gomez Perdiguero et al. 2015 Nature; Hoeffel et al. 2015 Immunity).

subsections.title: Definitive HSC emergence in the AGM (E10.5–E11.5)
subsections.body: The first cells with bona fide longterm multilineage repopulating activity arise in the aorta-gonad-mesonephros (AGM) region, specifically from the ventral wall of the dorsal aorta, between E10.5 and E11.5 in mouse (Medvinsky and Dzierzak 1996 Cell). These HSCs bud from specialized hemogenic endothelium through a process termed endothelial-to-hematopoietic transition (EHT) , which has been visualized live by confocal imaging of transgenic zebrafish and mouse embryos (Bertrand et al. 2010 Nature; Boisset et al. 2010 Nature; Kissa and Herbomel 2010 Nature). During EHT, a flat endothelial cell rounds up, loses tight junctions, upregulates hematopoietic markers, and detaches into the aortic lumen as an intra-aortic hematopoietic cluster (IAHC). The molecular logic of EHT is controlled by a conserved transcription factor network: • RUNX1 (AML1) is absolutely required: Runx1^-/- embryos die at E12.5 with complete absence of definitive hematopoiesis, though primitive hematopoiesis is preserved (Okuda et al. 1996; Wang et al. 1996). Conditional endothelial deletion using VE-cadherin-Cre confirms that RUNX1 is needed in hemogenic endothelium, not later (Chen et al. 2009 Nature). • SCL/TAL1 is required upstream for mesoderm-to-hemangioblast specification (Shivdasani et al. 1995). • GATA2 drives hemogenic competence and maintains emerging HSCs; haploinsufficiency in humans causes MonoMAC/Emberger syndromes with cytopenias and AML predisposition (de Pater et al. 2013). • NOTCH1 signaling from underlying mesenchyme induces RUNX1 and EHT; Notch1^-/- and Jag1^-/- embryos lack AGM HSCs (Kumano et al. 2003). • CDX–HOX axis (CDX2/4 ￿ HOXA cluster) patterns posterior mesoderm and confers definitive hematopoietic identity (Wang et al. 2008; Ng et al. 2016 Nat Biotechnol). • BMP4 from ventral mesoderm and Hedgehog signals cooperate with retinoic acid to polarize the aorta dorso-ventrally . Key facts: • Primitive vs definitive hematopoiesis differ in location (yolk sac vs AGM), output (nucleated embryonic RBCs/EMPs vs multilineage HSC-derived), and self-renewal potential (limited vs long-term). • EHT requires RUNX1, SCL/TAL1, GATA2, and NOTCH1. • EMPs are not HSCs but produce long-lived tissue-resident macrophages.

subsections.title: Placenta as an HSC site
subsections.body:
The mouse placenta contains transplantable HSCs from E10.5–

E11.5, and at its peak harbors more HSCs than the AGM proper (Gekas et al. 2005 Developmental Cell; Ottersbach and Dzierzak 2005). Whether placental HSCs arise de novo from placental
hemogenic endothelium or are seeded from AGM and expanded locally is still debated, but the
placenta is now recognized as a major site of HSC expansion prior to fetal liver colonization.

subsections.title: Fetal liver colonization and expansion (E11.5–E16.5) HSCs migrate through
subsections.body:
the circulation and colonize the fetal liver beginning at ~E11.5. The fetal liver is the dominant site of HSC

expansion during mid-gestation: Morrison et al. (1995 PNAS) demonstrated that the HSC pool expands approximately 38-fold between E12 and E16 . Unlike adult bone marrow HSCs, fetal liver
HSCs are actively cycling (a majority in S/G2/M), express distinct surface markers (e.g., MAC1/CD11b^low, AA4.1/CD93^+), and differ transcriptionally (SOX17, LIN28B, HMGA2 high; Kim
et al. 2007; Copley et al. 2013). Fetal liver is also the site of the hemoglobin switch from embryonic
to fetal forms and, in humans, eventually to adult β-globin.

subsections.title: Bone marrow colonization (~E17.5 onward)
subsections.body:
At approximately E17.5 in mouse (around the

second trimester in humans), HSCs migrate out of the fetal liver and colonize developing bone
marrow cavities , which become the definitive adult HSC site for life. This migration depends
on CXCR4–CXCL12 signaling and on the maturation of marrow niche components (endothelial
sinusoids, osteoblasts, perivascular mesenchymal stromal cells). Around 3–4 weeks postnatally ,
mouse HSCs transition from a fetal-like, cycling, SOX17/LIN28B-high state to the adult, quiescent,
BMI1/GFI1-high state; this switch is regulated by post-transcriptional mechanisms including the
LIN28B–let-7–HMGA2 axis (Copley et al. 2013 Nat Cell Biol).
Key facts:

• Adult HSC ontogeny sequence: yolk sac (primitive, EMPs) ￿ AGM (definitive HSC birth)
￿ placenta ￿ fetal liver (expansion) ￿ bone marrow (lifelong maintenance) .
• Fetal liver HSCs cycle; adult BM HSCs are quiescent — the LIN28B/let-7/HMGA2 axis governs
the switch.
• The placenta is a major HSC reservoir prior to fetal liver colonization.
keyFacts: Adult HSC ontogeny sequence: yolk sac (primitive, EMPs) ￿ AGM (definitive HSC birth) ￿ placenta ￿ fetal liver (expansion) ￿ bone marrow (lifelong maintenance) .
Fetal liver HSCs cycle; adult BM HSCs are quiescent — the LIN28B/let-7/HMGA2 axis governs the switch.
The placenta is a major HSC reservoir prior to fetal liver colonization.

sections.title: The HSC hierarchy — classical and revised models

subsections.title: The classical “tree” model
subsections.body:
The classical model, refined over four decades, posits a strict

hierarchy in which a small apex population of long-term HSCs (LT-HSCs) gives rise sequentially
to short-term HSCs (ST-HSCs / MPPs), then to oligopotent common myeloid (CMP) and common
lymphoid progenitors (CLP) , and finally to committed unilineage progenitors (GMP , MEP , BLP ,
TLP) that generate mature cells (Weissman 2000 Cell; Akashi et al. 2000 Nature; Kondo et al. 1997
Cell). In this model:
• LT-HSCself-renews for the lifetime of the animal; reconstitutes ≥16 weeks in competitive transplant, supports serial transplantation.
• ST-HSC / MPP1 reconstitutes for weeks to a few months but has limited self-renewal.
• CMP generates GMP (granulocyte-macrophage progenitor ￿ neutrophils, monocytes) and
MEP (megakaryocyte-erythroid progenitor ￿ RBCs, platelets).
• CLP generates T, B, NK, and some dendritic cell lineages.

subsections.title: MPP subsets (Pietras framework)
subsections.body:
Adolfsson et al. (2005 Cell) introduced lymphoid-primed

multipotent progenitors (LMPPs: Lin−Sca1+cKit+Flt3+) that lack megakaryocyte-erythroid potential. Pietras et al. (2015 Cell Stem Cell) refined the adult MPP compartment into four lineage-biased
subsets:
• MPP1: CD150+CD48−Flt3−, ST-HSC-like, balanced output.
• MPP2: CD150+CD48+Flt3−, megakaryocyte/erythroid-biased.
• MPP3: CD150−CD48+Flt3−, myeloid-biased (granulocyte/monocyte).
• MPP4: CD150−CD48−/+Flt3+, lymphoid-biased (LMPP-like).
This framework shows that “multipotency” is heterogeneous even at the MPP level, and that stress
hematopoiesis is largely orchestrated by shifting the MPP subset ratios (e.g., infection expands
MPP3/MPP4).

subsections.title: Revised continuum and lineage-priming models
subsections.body:
Single-cell RNA-seq and functional

single-cell assays in the last decade have largely dissolved the sharp boundaries of the classical
tree. Key observations:
• Notta et al. (2016 Science) performed single-cell transplants of human progenitors and found
that unilineage megakaryocytic and erythroid output emerges directly from HSC/MPP-like
cells without passing through a discrete CMP intermediate; the “CMP” gate contains a mixture
of unipotent and oligopotent cells.
• V elten et al. (2017 Nat Cell Biol) used scRNA-seq of human HSPCs and described a “continuum of low-primed undifferentiated cells” (CLOUD-HSPCs) with gradual, rather than step34

wise, lineage commitment.
• Giladi et al. (2018) and Rodriguez-Fraticelli et al. (2018 Nature) likewise support continuous
commitment trajectories.
Together these studies reframe hematopoiesis as a Waddington-like continuous landscape in which
lineage-priming gradients replace discrete oligopotent intermediates, with a small reservoir of
bona fide multipotent LT-HSCs at the apex.

subsections.title: Clonal barcoding In vivo clonal tracking has been pivotal in challenging and refining
subsections.body:
the

hierarchy:
• Naik et al. (2013 Nature) used cellular barcoding (retroviral tags) to show that individual
HSPCs have highly biased, often unilineage, outputs — many HSCs behave as lineage-biased
rather than truly multipotent in any single division.
• Sun et al. (2014 Nature) and Busch et al. (2015 Nature) used transposon and Tie2- / Fgd5-CreER
tracing and inferred that native (unperturbed) hematopoiesis is driven largely by MPPs, not
by HSCs, in steady state, with HSCs contributing modestly over months.
• Pei et al. (2017 Nature) used Polylox barcoding to demonstrate HSC clonal heterogeneity and
stable lineage output preferences across time.
• Rodriguez-Fraticelli et al. (2020 Nature) and Bowling et al. (2020 Cell) (LARRY CRISPR barcodes) tie scRNA-seq state to clonal fate, linking transcriptional priming to functional output.
Key facts:
• Classical model: LT-HSC ￿ ST-HSC/MPP ￿ CMP/CLP ￿ committed progenitors. Revised
view: continuous commitment with HSC/MPP lineage priming.
• MPP2 (Mk/E-biased), MPP3 (myeloid-biased), MPP4 (lymphoid-biased) underlie lineage
adaptation to stress (Pietras 2015).
• Native, unperturbed hematopoiesis is MPP-driven (Busch 2015; Sun 2014); transplantation biology is HSC-driven — the two assays query different compartments.
keyFacts: Classical model: LT-HSC ￿ ST-HSC/MPP ￿ CMP/CLP ￿ committed progenitors. Revised view: continuous commitment with HSC/MPP lineage priming.
MPP2 (Mk/E-biased), MPP3 (myeloid-biased), MPP4 (lymphoid-biased) underlie lineage adaptation to stress (Pietras 2015).
Native, unperturbed hematopoiesis is MPP-driven (Busch 2015; Sun 2014); transplantation biology is HSC-driven — the two assays query different compartments.

sections.title: Surface markers and flow cytometry gating

subsections.title: Mouse LSK and SLAM gating
subsections.body:
Operational purification of mouse HSCs rests on exclusion

of mature lineage markers followed by stem-cell-specific antigens:
• Lineage cocktail (Lin) : biotin-conjugated antibodies against Gr-1 (Ly6G/C), Mac-1 (CD11b;
note: omit in fetal liver , since fetal HSCs express CD11b), B220 (CD45R), CD3e (or CD4 + CD8),
Ter119 (erythroid), and NK1.1. Stained with streptavidin-fluorophore and excluded.
• LSK: Lin− Sca-1+ c-Kit+ . Contains LT-HSC, ST-HSC, MPPs (~0.05–0.1% of BM).
• SLAM markers (Kiel et al. 2005 Cell): CD150 (Slamf1), CD48 (Slamf2), CD244 (Slamf4).
– LT-HSC: Lin− Sca-1+ c-Kit+ CD150+ CD48− (~0.005–0.01% of BM).
– MPP1 / ST-HSC: Lin− Sca-1+ c-Kit+ CD150− CD48−.
– MPP2: CD150+ CD48+.
– MPP3: CD150− CD48+.
– MPP4: CD150− CD48− Flt3+ (CD135+) (lymphoid-biased LMPP).

Alternative schemes use CD34 and Flk2/Flt3 (CD135): LT-HSCs are CD34− Flt3−, ST-HSCs CD34+
Flt3−, MPPs CD34+ Flt3+ (Osawa et al. 1996; Christensen and Weissman 2001).

subsections.title: Myeloid progenitor (MP) gating
subsections.body:
Below the LSK gate, myeloid progenitors are defined

within Lin− Sca-1− c-Kit+ cells (sometimes termed LK) using CD34 and FcγRII/III (CD16/32)
(Akashi et al. 2000):
• CMP: CD34+ CD16/32^low.
• GMP: CD34+ CD16/32^high.
• MEP: CD34− CD16/32^low.
Refined gating (Pronk et al. 2007 Cell Stem Cell) adds CD150 and Endoglin (CD105) to resolve:
• PreMegE: CD150+ CD105−.
• MkP: CD150+ CD41+.
• PreCFU-E: CD150− CD105+.
• CFU-E: CD150− CD105+ Ter119^mid.
CLP is defined as Lin− Sca-1^low c-Kit^low IL7Rα+ Flt3+ (Kondo et al. 1997).

subsections.title: Human HSC markers
subsections.body:
• Bulk HSPC: CD34+ CD38−.
• LT-HSC enriched: CD34+ CD38− CD90+ CD45RA− CD49f+ (Notta et al. 2011 Science) — singlecell transplant of CD49f+ HSCs reconstituted NSG mice, establishing this phenotype as the
purest human LT-HSC population to date.
• MPP: CD34+ CD38− CD90− CD45RA− CD49f−.
• LMPP: CD34+ CD38− CD90− CD45RA+.
• CMP: CD34+ CD38+ CD123+ CD45RA−.
• GMP: CD34+ CD38+ CD123+ CD45RA+.
• MEP: CD34+ CD38+ CD123− CD45RA−.

subsections.title: Practical flow cytometry: gating hierarchy and controls
subsections.body:
A typical analytical gating strategy

for mouse bone marrow:
FSC-A vs SSC-A (exclude debris, define cells)
↓
FSC-H vs FSC-A (exclude doublets)
↓
Live gate (DAPI−, 7AAD−, or Zombie-NIR−)
↓
Lineage− (dump channel)
↓
Sca-1+ c-Kit+ (LSK)
↓
CD150+ CD48− (LT-HSC)

Essential controls:
• FMO (Fluorescence-Minus-One) controls for every positive gate where the boundary is ambiguous (especially SLAM markers, CD34, CD45RA).
• Single-stain compensation controls (UltraComp beads or cells) for every fluorophore.
• Biological controls: wild-type vs knockout tissue, or c-Kit^-/- or Sca-1^-/- cells to verify staining
specificity .
• Viability dye applied before fixation (DAPI is live-only; Zombie/LIVE-DEAD amine dyes are
fixation-compatible).
• CD45.1/CD45.2 discrimination is essential in transplantation to distinguish donor, competitor,
and recipient-derived cells.
• Intracellular staining (e.g., Ki-67, pSTAT, active caspase-3, DCAF7) requires fixation
(2–4% PFA) and permeabilization (saponin or methanol for nuclear/phosphoepitopes;
Foxp3/Transcription Factor Fix/Perm buffers for nuclear TFs like GATA1, PU.1).
Key facts:
• LT-HSC (mouse) = Lin− Sca-1+ c-Kit+ CD150+ CD48− (Kiel 2005).
• LT-HSC (human) = CD34+ CD38− CD90+ CD45RA− CD49f+ (Notta 2011).
• Fetal HSCs express Mac-1/CD11b — omit from the lineage cocktail when gating fetal liver.
keyFacts: LT-HSC (mouse) = Lin− Sca-1+ c-Kit+ CD150+ CD48− (Kiel 2005).
LT-HSC (human) = CD34+ CD38− CD90+ CD45RA− CD49f+ (Notta 2011).
Fetal HSCs express Mac-1/CD11b — omit from the lineage cocktail when gating fetal liver.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The HSC niche was formally proposed by Schofield (1978) as a microanatomic compartment that
maintains stem cell identity . Subsequent work has identified multiple cellular constituents and a
dense cytokine/chemokine milieu.

subsections.title: Cellular components
subsections.body:
• Endosteal niche: adjacent to trabecular bone; includes osteoblasts, osteocytes, and MSCs. Early
work by Calvi et al. (2003 Nature) and Zhang et al. (2003 Nature) implicated osteoblasts in
HSC maintenance; subsequent imaging (Kiel et al. 2005) showed that most HSCs are actually
perivascular, not endosteal. Osteoblasts may be more important for lymphoid progenitors than
for LT-HSCs.
• Perivascular niche: MSCs surrounding sinusoids and arterioles. Key populations:
– Nestin-GFP+ MSCs (Méndez-Ferrer et al. 2010 Nature): rare perivascular cells expressing
high CXCL12, SCF, Angpt1; depletion reduces HSC numbers.
– CAR (CXCL12-Abundant Reticular) cells (Sugiyama et al. 2006): reticular stromal cells
producing CXCL12 throughout the marrow.
– LepR+ MSCs (Ding et al. 2012 Nature; Zhou et al. 2014): essentially the same as
Nestin^low/CAR population; conditional deletion of Scf from LepR+ cells depletes HSCs.
• Arteriolar niche: NG2+ pericytes around small arterioles in the endosteal region; Kunisaki et
al. (2013 Nature) showed quiescent HSCs preferentially localize here.
• Sinusoidal endothelial cells : produce SCF and jagged-1; Ding et al. (2012) showed that deletion of Scf from endothelial cells also depletes HSCs.

• Sympathetic nervous system : tyrosine hydroxylase (TH)+ adrenergic nerve fibers regulate
HSC egress via a circadian β3-adrenergic signal on MSCs (Méndez-Ferrer et al. 2008 Nature).
• Non-myelinating Schwann cells: activate latent TGF-β in the niche (Yamazaki et al. 2011 Cell).
• Megakaryocytes: directly contact LT-HSCs and release CXCL4/PF4 and TGF-β to maintain
quiescence (Bruns et al. 2014; Zhao et al. 2014 Nat Med).
• CD169+ macrophages : support HSC retention and MSC function; clodronate depletion mobilizes HSCs (Chow et al. 2011 J Exp Med).
• Regulatory T cells in the niche (Fujisaki et al. 2011 Nature): create an immunoprivileged site.
• Adipocytes: inhibitory in young marrow (Naveiras et al. 2009 Nature), supportive in irradiated/regenerating marrow — context-dependent.

subsections.title: Key niche factors
subsections.body:
• CXCL12 / SDF-1 ￿ CXCR4 : master retention axis; loss of CXCL12 from MSCs/ECs mobilizes
HSCs. AMD3100/ plerixafor is the clinical CXCR4 antagonist used for PBSC mobilization in
transplantation.
• SCF (Kit-ligand) ￿ c-KIT : essential for HSC maintenance; Steel and W mutant mice are classic
macrocytic anemia / mast cell defect models.
• TPO ￿ MPL : thrombopoietin maintains LT-HSC quiescence; Mpl^-/- and Thpo^-/- mice show
progressive HSC loss.
• Angiopoietin-1 (Angpt1) ￿ TIE2 : supports HSC quiescence at endosteum (Arai et al. 2004
Cell).
• FGF , Wnt (canonical and non-canonical): Wnt tone from niche regulates HSC self-renewal in
a dose-dependent manner (Luis et al. 2011).
• Notch ligands (Jagged-1, DLL4) : contribute to HSC maintenance, though definitive in vivo
requirement is debated.
• TGF-β: activated by Schwann cells and megakaryocytes; enforces quiescence.

subsections.title: Oxygen, metabolism, and niche localization HSCs reside in relatively hypoxic microenvironments (pO2 ~10–20 mmHg
subsections.body:
measured by two-photon phosphorescence lifetime imaging,

Spencer et al. 2014 Nature). This hypoxia correlates with low mitochondrial mass, predominantly
glycolytic metabolism, and HIF-1α stabilization. HSC metabolism is characterized by:
• Low mitochondrial membrane potential and low ROS (Jang and Sharkis 2007).
• PDK2/3-mediated inhibition of pyruvate flux into the TCA cycle (Takubo et al. 2013 Cell Stem
Cell).
• FAO (fatty acid oxidation) and PPAR-δ signaling for asymmetric self-renewal divisions (Ito et
al. 2012 Nat Med).
• FOXO-driven antioxidant program (Tothova et al. 2007 Cell): Foxo1/3/4 triple knockout causes
HSC exhaustion via elevated ROS.
Key facts:
• HSCs sit predominantly in perivascular niches near sinusoids/arterioles, supported by LepR+
MSCs and ECs that supply SCF and CXCL12.
• Plerixafor (AMD3100) is the clinical CXCR4 antagonist for HSC mobilization.

• HSCs are hypoxic, glycolytic, low-ROS — their metabolic state enforces quiescence and protects genome integrity .
keyFacts: HSCs sit predominantly in perivascular niches near sinusoids/arterioles, supported by LepR+ MSCs and ECs that supply SCF and CXCL12.
Plerixafor (AMD3100) is the clinical CXCR4 antagonist for HSC mobilization.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
LT-HSCs are the most quiescent cells in the hematopoietic system. In vivo BrdU/label-retention
experiments and H2B-GFP pulse-chase (Wilson et al. 2008 Cell; Foudi et al. 2009 Nat Biotechnol)
show that dormant LT-HSCs divide approximately once every 145 days in mouse (i.e., ~5 divisions/lifetime), while an “active” HSC subpopulation cycles every ~36 days. At steady state, only
~4–5% of LT-HSCs are in S/G2/M.

subsections.title: Dormant vs active HSCs
subsections.body:
Wilson et al. (2008) labeled H2B-GFP under a doxycyclineregulated tetO and followed label dilution: the most dormant LT-HSC fraction retained label

longest, had the highest serial-transplant capacity , and was selectively activated by injury (5-FU,
IFN-α, G-CSF). This established a two-tier LT-HSC model: a deeply dormant reserve pool and a
more active LT-HSC pool that handles day-to-day demand.

subsections.title: Molecular regulators of quiescence
subsections.body:
• CIP/KIP CDK inhibitors: p21 ( Cdkn1a), p27 (Cdkn1b), and especially p57 (Cdkn1c) — the dominant CDKI in LT-HSCs (Matsumoto et al. 2011; Zou et al. 2011 Cell Stem Cell). Cdkn1a^-/- HSCs
initially expand but exhaust.
• FOXO transcription factors (FOXO1/3/4): redox balance and quiescence. Triple-knockout
HSCs lose quiescence and exhaust (Tothova et al. 2007).
• Gfi1 and Gfi1b : enforce quiescence; loss reduces HSC numbers (Hock et al. 2004).
• Egr1: restrains HSC proliferation and localization.
• Mll (KMT2A), Bmi1: PcG/TrxG balance; Bmi1 represses Ink4a/Arf.
• N-MYC vs c-MYC: c-MYC drives HSC egress from niche and differentiation (Wilson et al. 2004);
N-MYC supports maintenance; balance between them tunes self-renewal vs commitment.
• PTEN/PI3K/AKT/mTOR: PTEN loss drives HSC into cycle and ultimately depletes the HSC
pool while producing leukemia — demonstrating the self-renewal/exhaustion tradeoff (Yilmaz
et al. 2006 Nature).

subsections.title: Mitotic history and functional decline
subsections.body:
Bernitz et al. (2016 Cell) used an H2B-GFP mouse

to show that cumulative division number — not chronological age — determines LT-HSC selfrenewal capacity . After ~4 divisions, HSCs lose serial engraftment potential. This “divisional
memory” concept is central to aging and stress biology and is mechanistically linked to accumulated DNA damage, replication stress, and epigenetic drift.

subsections.title: ROS, replication stress, and genomic integrity
subsections.body:
Quiescence protects HSCs from three major

stresses:
• Replication stress : HSCs have low expression of DNA-repair machinery in G0 and rely on
fidelity of origin firing during rare divisions. Flach et al. (2014 Nature) showed aged HSCs
accumulate replication stress with reduced MCM helicase levels.

• ROS: FOXO, MTH1 (NUDT1, sanitizes oxidized dNTPs), and glutathione pathways control
ROS. p38 MAPK is activated by ROS and limits HSC lifespan (Ito et al. 2006 Nat Med); Nacetylcysteine or p38 inhibition rescues HSC function in aged or stressed settings.
• Genotoxic stress : HSCs preferentially use non-homologous end-joining (NHEJ) in G0, which
is error-prone; conversion to homologous recombination (HR) upon cycling. This trade-off
underlies clonal hematopoiesis accumulation with age.
Key facts:
• LT-HSCs are deeply quiescent: ~5% cycle at a time; dormant subset divides ~every 145 days
(Wilson 2008).
• p57 (Cdkn1c) is the dominant CDK inhibitor in LT-HSCs.
• Divisional history , not age alone, erodes HSC potency (Bernitz 2016).
• Quiescence is genoprotective; forced cycling (PTEN loss, IFN, 5-FU) drives exhaustion.
keyFacts: LT-HSCs are deeply quiescent: ~5% cycle at a time; dormant subset divides ~every 145 days (Wilson 2008).
p57 (Cdkn1c) is the dominant CDK inhibitor in LT-HSCs.
Divisional history , not age alone, erodes HSC potency (Bernitz 2016).
Quiescence is genoprotective; forced cycling (PTEN loss, IFN, 5-FU) drives exhaustion.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body: Lineage commitment is governed by hierarchically organized, cross-antagonistic transcription factor (TF) networks. The principal TFs and their roles:

subsections.title: HSC maintenance and self-renewal
subsections.body:
• HOXA9, HOXA7, MEIS1: HOX cluster factors are highly expressed in HSCs and early progenitors; HOXA9 overexpression immortalizes HSCs in vitro, and MEIS1 is a cofactor. Both are
hijacked in MLL-rearranged leukemia.
• GATA2: essential for HSC emergence and maintenance; haploinsufficiency causes MonoMAC/Emberger (human) with cytopenias and AML predisposition.
• RUNX1 (AML1): EHT plus adult HSC emergency response; heterozygous germline mutations
cause familial platelet disorder with predisposition to AML (FPD/AML).
• MLL (KMT2A): H3K4 methyltransferase required for HSC maintenance.
• BMI1: Polycomb PRC1 component; represses Ink4a/Arf and prevents senescence (Park et
al. 2003).
• EVI1 (MECOM): required for adult HSC self-renewal (Goyama et al. 2008).
• GFI1, GFI1B: restrict HSC cycling.

subsections.title: Myeloid lineage
subsections.body:
• PU.1 (Spi1) : ETS family master TF; low levels ￿ lymphoid, high levels ￿ myeloid. Graded
PU.1 dosage (Dakic et al. 2005) specifies alternate fates.
• C/EBPα ( Cebpa): drives GMP formation and granulocyte maturation; loss causes block at
CMP￿ GMP transition (Zhang et al. 2004).
• C/EBPε: terminal neutrophil maturation; loss causes neutrophil-specific granule deficiency .
• IRF8: monocyte/DC specification.
• GATA1–PU.1 cross-antagonism: GATA1 and PU.1 bind each other’s regulatory elements and
mutually inhibit, operating as a bistable toggle switch that drives erythroid (GATA1-high) vs
myeloid (PU.1-high) fate decisions (Nerlov and Graf 1998; Arinobu et al. 2007).

subsections.title: Erythroid lineage
subsections.body:
• GATA1: master erythroid TF; drives hemoglobin genes, heme biosynthesis, and terminal maturation.
• KLF1 (EKLF): β-globin expression and erythroid cytoskeleton.
• SCL/TAL1: required for erythroid and multilineage specification.
• LDB1, LMO2: cofactors in GATA1/SCL complex.

subsections.title: Megakaryocytic lineage
subsections.body:
• GATA1(again), FLI1, RUNX1: megakaryocyte core trio.
• NF-E2: platelet granule formation and proplatelet release.
• MEIS1: megakaryocyte maturation.

subsections.title: B lymphoid lineage
subsections.body:
• E2A (TCF3): initiates B-lineage program in CLPs.
• EBF1: drives Pax5 expression.
• PAX5: enforces B-lineage identity , represses alternative fates. Pax5^-/- pro-B cells retain multipotency .
• IRF4/IRF8: late B-cell and plasma cell biology .

subsections.title: T lymphoid and NK lineage
subsections.body:
• NOTCH1: T-lineage specification in thymus; sustained Notch signaling from thymic epithelium.
• GATA3, TCF1 (T cf7), BCL11B: T-cell commitment and identity . Bcl11b deletion reprograms T
progenitors to NK/myeloid fates.
• EOMES, T-bet (TBX21), ID2 : NK cell specification and maturation.
Key facts:
• GATA1–PU.1 cross-antagonism is the paradigmatic bistable switch for erythroid-vs-myeloid
fate.
• PAX5 enforces B-cell identity; BCL11B enforces T-cell identity; loss reprograms.
• HOXA9/MEIS1 are dominant HSC self-renewal TFs and are co-opted in MLL leukemia.
keyFacts: GATA1–PU.1 cross-antagonism is the paradigmatic bistable switch for erythroid-vs-myeloid fate.
PAX5 enforces B-cell identity; BCL11B enforces T-cell identity; loss reprograms.
HOXA9/MEIS1 are dominant HSC self-renewal TFs and are co-opted in MLL leukemia.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Hematopoietic cytokine receptors fall into two main superfamilies: type I/II cytokine receptors
(using JAK-STAT) and receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) . Understanding this wiring is critical
because several hematologic malignancies (JAK2 V617F, FLT3-ITD, KIT D816V , MPL mutations)
reflect constitutive activation, and because the UPS and IFN pathways — central to the committee
chair’s expertise — intersect directly with cytokine signaling.

subsections.title: JAK-STAT-coupled cytokine receptors
subsections.body:
Ligand Receptor JAK Primary STAT Main role

(homodimer)

(late; CFU-E ￿
proerythroblast)

(homodimer)

megakaryopoiesis

(CSF3R)

expansion

myelopoiesis,
acute phase

and T)
