Key negative regulators: SOCS family (SOCS1/3 especially), CIS, PTP1B, SHP1/2; SOCS proteins
act in part by recruiting E3 ligases (Cullin5 complexes with Elongin B/C — mechanistically relevant to DCAF/UPS biology).

subsections.title: Receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs)
subsections.body:
• c-KIT (CD117) ￿ SCF : dimerization ￿ autophosphorylation ￿ PI3K/AKT and RAS/MAPK.
Essential for HSC and mast cell biology . W/Wv (KIT) and Sl/Sld (SCF) mutants are classic models.
• FLT3 ￿ FLT3L : early progenitor survival and lymphoid/DC expansion; FLT3-ITD is a common
AML driver.
• CSF1R ￿ M-CSF : macrophage development.
RTK signaling flows through PI3K/AKT/mTOR (survival, metabolism) and RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK
(proliferation), and does not use JAK/STAT.

subsections.title: Other developmentally critical pathways
subsections.body:
• Wnt: Canonical β-catenin signaling tunes HSC self-renewal in a dose-dependent manner —
too little or too much disrupts HSCs (Luis et al. 2011 Cell Stem Cell). Non-canonical Wnt5a
maintains HSC quiescence via planar cell polarity (Sugimura et al. 2012).
• Notch: critical for T-cell specification in thymus; role in adult HSC maintenance is debated but
clearly supports lymphoid vs myeloid choice.
• Hedgehog: dispensable for adult HSC maintenance ( Smo conditional KO has minimal effect)
but important for embryonic hematopoiesis and leukemia stem cells.
• BMP4 and TGF-β: BMPs pattern embryonic mesoderm for hematopoiesis; TGF-β enforces
adult HSC quiescence.
• mTORC1: integrates nutrient and growth factor signals; TSC1/TSC2 deletion activates
mTORC1 and exhausts HSCs (Chen et al. 2008; Gan et al. 2008).
• AMPK and LKB1 : energy-stress sensing; LKB1 loss depletes HSCs (Gurumurthy et al. 2010;
Nakada et al. 2010; Gan et al. 2010 — three back-to-back Nature papers).

subsections.title: Intersections with UPS and IFN (committee-chair relevance)
subsections.body:
• IFN-α/β ￿ ISGs : Many ISGs are ubiquitin-pathway components (ISG15, USP18, TRIMs, IRF
ubiquitination). ISG15 is an IFN-induced ubiquitin-like modifier; USP18 is an ISG15 protease
and a negative regulator of IFNAR signaling.
• SOCS proteins nucleate Cullin5-based E3 ligases that ubiquitinate JAK/cytokine receptor substrates, attenuating STAT signaling.
• CRL4 (Cullin4-DDB1) with DCAF substrate receptors — the family to which DCAF7 belongs
— are key ubiquitin ligases in hematopoiesis, interfacing with replication, differentiation, and
potentially IFN signaling.
Key facts:
• EPO-R, MPL, G-CSF-R, GM-CSF-R, IL-3R, IL-7R are JAK-STAT receptors (mostly JAK2).
• c-KIT, FLT3, CSF1R are RTKs signaling through PI3K/MAPK.
• IFN-α/β signals via JAK1/TYK2 ￿ STAT1/STAT2/IRF9 (ISGF3) to induce ISGs.
• SOCS proteins and CRL E3 ligases link UPS to cytokine signaling shutdown.
keyFacts: EPO-R, MPL, G-CSF-R, GM-CSF-R, IL-3R, IL-7R are JAK-STAT receptors (mostly JAK2).
c-KIT, FLT3, CSF1R are RTKs signaling through PI3K/MAPK.
IFN-α/β signals via JAK1/TYK2 ￿ STAT1/STAT2/IRF9 (ISGF3) to induce ISGs.
SOCS proteins and CRL E3 ligases link UPS to cytokine signaling shutdown.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Because HSCs are defined functionally , not solely by phenotype, assays to measure self-renewal
and multilineage potential are central to the field.

subsections.title: In vitro colony assays
subsections.body:
• Methylcellulose CFU assay : HSPCs plated in semi-solid methylcellulose (e.g., MethoCult
H4435 human, M3434 mouse) with cytokines (IL-3, IL-6, SCF, EPO, GM-CSF). Colony types
scored at day 7–14:
– CFU-GM: granulocyte-macrophage.
– CFU-G, CFU-M: unilineage.
– BFU-E: hemoglobinized red cell bursts (large, multifocal).

– CFU-GEMM: granulocyte/erythroid/monocyte/megakaryocyte — most primitive, representing multipotent progenitor readout.
• LTC-IC (long-term culture-initiating cell) : HSPCs co-cultured on feeder stroma (MS-5, M210B4, or AFT024) for 5 weeks without added cytokines, then replated in methylcellulose. Readout (secondary CFU) measures a more primitive cell than direct CFU. Considered an in vitro
proxy for HSC/primitive progenitor activity .
• Cobblestone area-forming cell (CAFC) assay : related to LTC-IC, scores cobblestone colonies
beneath stroma.

subsections.title: Historical in vivo: CFU-S
subsections.body:
• CFU-S (spleen colony-forming unit, Till and McCulloch 1961) : lethally irradiated mouse injected with BM cells; macroscopic spleen colonies counted at day 8 (CFU-S8, committed progenitors) or day 12 (CFU-S12, more primitive). Seminal in establishing the clonal basis of
hematopoiesis but not a true HSC assay — CFU-S12 enriches for ST-HSCs/MPPs, not LT-HSCs.

subsections.title: T ransplantation — the gold standard
subsections.body:
• Lethal irradiation : mouse conditioning uses a split dose (typically 9.5–10 Gy , 2 × ~4.75–5 Gy ,
3–4 h apart) to ablate recipient hematopoiesis while sparing radioresistant stroma.
• Competitive bone marrow transplant (CBMT) : test cells (CD45.2) co-injected with fixed number of competitor cells (CD45.1, typically 2–3 × 10^5 whole BM). Donor chimerism in peripheral
blood is read by flow cytometry at 4, 8, 12, and 16 weeks. Multilineage output (myeloid Mac-1+,
B cells B220+, T cells CD3+) must be assessed — myeloid chimerism (short-lived cells) is the
most stringent readout for HSC activity .
• Limiting dilution analysis (LDA) : serial dilutions of test cells (e.g., 10, 30, 100, 300 HSCs per
recipient) with 5–10 mice/dose; successful engraftment defined as ≥1% donor chimerism with
multilineage output at ≥16 weeks. Frequency calculated by Poisson statistics using ELDA (Hu
and Smyth 2009).
• Serial transplantation : primary recipient BM harvested at 16 weeks, transplanted into secondary (and tertiary , quaternary) lethally irradiated recipients. Only true LT-HSCs sustain
serial transplants; this is the gold-standard self-renewal assay .
• Single-cell transplantation: Notta et al. (2011) used it to define CD49f+ human HSCs; in mouse,
Osawa (1996), Kiel (2005), and subsequent studies demonstrate single-cell reconstitution of LTHSCs at ~20–50% efficiency .

subsections.title: Humanized mouse models
subsections.body:
• NSG (NOD scid γc null) : lacks T, B, NK; supports human HSC engraftment.
• NSGS / NSG-SGM3 : NSG with transgenic human SCF, GM-CSF, IL-3 — better myeloid engraftment.
• MISTRG (Rongvaux et al. 2014 Nat Biotechnol): knock-in of human M-CSF, IL-3, GM-CSF,
TPO, SIRPα on Rag2/Il2rg-null background — robust human myeloid and HSC engraftment.

subsections.title: In vivo lineage tracing
subsections.body:
Genetic fate-mapping bypasses transplantation artifacts:

• Fgd5-CreER (Gazit et al. 2014; Gazit et al. 2018): HSC-specific in adult BM (~80–90% specificity
for LT-HSC).
• Pdzk1ip1-CreER (Sawai et al. 2016): LT-HSC-selective.
• Ctnnal1-CreER (Acar et al. 2015): dormant HSC labeling.
• Hoxb5-mCherry (Chen et al. 2016): LT-HSC reporter.
• Tie2-CreER, Scl-CreER: broader HSC/MPP labeling.
• VWF-GFP/EGFP (Sanjuan-Pla et al. 2013): platelet-biased HSC subset.

subsections.title: Clonal tracking
subsections.body:
• Retroviral/lentiviral integration site analysis : original barcoding.
• LeGO barcoding (Naik 2013): short barcode library in lentivirus; single-cell-like clonal tracking.
• T ransposon (Sleeping Beauty): Sun 2014, Busch 2015 for native tracing.
• Polylox (Pei 2017 Nature): Cre-recombinase-driven stochastic barcode generation in vivo.
• CRISPR-based barcoding (LARRY, CARLIN, scGESTALT): pair clonal identity with scRNAseq state (Weinreb et al. 2020 Science; Bowling et al. 2020 Cell).
Key facts:
• Gold standard HSC assay = competitive transplantation with >16-week multilineage (including myeloid) chimerism; serial transplantation assesses self-renewal.
• LTC-IC is the best in vitro proxy for primitive HSC/progenitor activity .
• CFU-S is historical — CFU-S12 enriches for ST-HSCs, not LT-HSCs.
• Fgd5-CreER, Pdzk1ip1-CreER, and Ctnnal1-CreER are best-in-class in vivo HSC lineage tracers.
keyFacts: Gold standard HSC assay = competitive transplantation with >16-week multilineage (including myeloid) chimerism; serial transplantation assesses self-renewal.
LTC-IC is the best in vitro proxy for primitive HSC/progenitor activity .
CFU-S is historical — CFU-S12 enriches for ST-HSCs, not LT-HSCs.
Fgd5-CreER, Pdzk1ip1-CreER, and Ctnnal1-CreER are best-in-class in vivo HSC lineage tracers.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
HSCs must respond to acute myeloablation, infection, inflammation, and blood loss. Stress
hematopoiesis is highly relevant to the committee chair’s interests (IFN signaling, UPS) because
type I IFNs are canonical HSC-activating stress signals and because inflammatory stress drives
HSC exhaustion and clonal selection — the biological substrate for clonal hematopoiesis and
leukemogenesis.

subsections.title: Cytotoxic stress: 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) 5-FU (150 mg/kg IP) kills cycling cells via thymidylate synthase
subsections.body:
inhibition and DNA damage. It spares quiescent LT-HSCs, which are then forced

into cycle to regenerate the progenitor compartment. Readouts: HSC cycling peaks at day 6–10,
progenitor recovery at ~day 14. Serial 5-FU challenge (weekly) exhausts HSCs and is used to test
HSC fitness.

subsections.title: Viral-mimetic and IFN-mediated stress
subsections.body:
• Poly(I:C) (polyinosinic-polycytidylic acid) is a dsRNA analog sensed by TLR3 (endosomal) and
MDA5/RIG-I/MA VS (cytosolic), inducing type I IFNs (IFN-α/β). Poly(I:C) is widely used both
as a stress inducer and as the obligate trigger in Mx1-Cre conditional knockout systems (Kühn
et al. 1995) — an important nuance because Mx1-Cre itself activates the HSC compartment.

• IFN-α direct action on HSCs (Essers et al. 2009 Nature): injection of recombinant IFN-α activates dormant LT-HSCs via STAT1 and Sca-1 upregulation, driving them into cycle; chronic
IFN exposure exhausts HSCs. This is the landmark paper connecting innate immunity to HSC
cycling.
• IFN-γ (Baldridge et al. 2010 Nature): drives HSC proliferation in response to infection (e.g.,
Mycobacterium avium).
• IFN-α/IFN-γ and self-renewal trade-off: acute activation is protective for immune defense but
chronic signaling exhausts HSCs via p53, replication stress, and DNA damage (Pietras 2017;
Walter et al. 2015 Nature).
• ISGs and UPS : ISG15, USP18, TRIM family , and RSAD2 are IFN-induced ubiquitin-pathway
members. The interplay between IFN-activated UPS components and HSC cycling is a mechanistic axis of interest, especially if DCAF7 lies in or adjacent to this circuit.

subsections.title: Bacterial and TLR-driven emergency myelopoiesis
subsections.body:
• LPS engages TLR4 ￿ MyD88/TRIF ￿ NF-κB and IRF3. HSCs and progenitors express functional TLRs and directly respond (Nagai et al. 2006 Immunity); chronic LPS drives HSC into
cycle, biases MPP3 expansion, and exhausts the HSC pool (Takizawa et al. 2017).
• IL-1β drives myeloid bias at the HSC level and, chronically , HSC exhaustion via PU.1 upregulation (Pietras et al. 2016 Nat Cell Biol).
• IL-6 supports emergency granulopoiesis with C/EBPβ (Satake et al. 2012).
• TNF-α: pleiotropic; acute TNF-α supports HSC function (Yamashita and Passegué 2019 Cell
Stem Cell), chronic TNF is exhausting.
• G-CSF: therapeutic and physiologic emergency granulopoiesis; also mobilizes HSCs to blood
via CXCL12 and CXCR4 downregulation in the niche.

subsections.title: Infection-driven myeloid bias
subsections.body:
• Chronic Mycobacterium avium infection in mice drives myeloid-biased HSC output via IFN-γ
(Matatall et al. 2014, 2016 Cell Reports).
• Chronic infection biases HSC self-renewal toward exhaustion and selects for clones resistant to
IFN-mediated cycling — a plausible basis for clonal hematopoiesis with age.

subsections.title: Chronic inflammation and HSC exhaustion
subsections.body:
• Pietras 2014 J Exp Med, 2016 Nat Cell Biol: IL-1 drives myeloid bias and eventual HSC exhaustion via sustained PU.1 activation.
• Walter et al. 2015 Nature : IFN-α-induced replication stress causes DNA damage foci (γH2AX)
in HSCs and long-term functional decline.
• Fleischman et al. 2011 Blood; Bhattacharya et al. 2021 : inflammatory cytokines can select
clones with specific genetic lesions (e.g., JAK2 V617F, TET2, DNMT3A) — tying chronic inflammation to clonal hematopoiesis.
• Muto et al. 2022 Nature: TET2-mutant HSCs gain competitive advantage under chronic inflammation via A20/TNFR/IL-6 pathway .

subsections.title: HSC mobilization Clinical HSC mobilization for apheresis collection uses: - G-CSF (filgrastim): 4–5 daily
subsections.body:
doses; induces neutrophil elastase and CXCL12 cleavage in the niche; downregulates CXCR4 retention. - Plerixafor (AMD3100): CXCR4 antagonist, used in combination with

G-CSF or as salvage. - Cyclophosphamide: mobilization post-chemotherapy recovery phase.
Key facts:
• IFN-α activates dormant HSCs via STAT1/Sca-1 (Essers 2009); IFN-γ drives proliferation during infection (Baldridge 2010).
• Chronic inflammation (IL-1, TNF, IFN) causesmyeloid bias and HSC exhaustion (Pietras 2016;
Walter 2015).
• Mx1-Cre requires poly(I:C), which itself perturbs HSCs — include appropriate controls.
• Inflammation selects for TET2, DNMT3A, JAK2 clones, linking stress to clonal hematopoiesis.
keyFacts: IFN-α activates dormant HSCs via STAT1/Sca-1 (Essers 2009); IFN-γ drives proliferation during infection (Baldridge 2010).
Chronic inflammation (IL-1, TNF, IFN) causesmyeloid bias and HSC exhaustion (Pietras 2016; Walter 2015).
Mx1-Cre requires poly(I:C), which itself perturbs HSCs — include appropriate controls.
Inflammation selects for TET2, DNMT3A, JAK2 clones, linking stress to clonal hematopoiesis.

sections.title: Flow cytometry in practice — a worked gating strategy

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body: A complete, reproducible HSC analysis workflow for mouse bone marrow:

subsections.title: Sample preparation
subsections.body:
1. Euthanize mouse (isoflurane/CO2), dissect tibiae, femora, pelvic bones (pool for maximum
yield); for LT-HSC work, include spine for higher yield (~3–4× over tibia/femur only).
2. Crush bones with mortar and pestle in ice-cold PBS + 2% FBS (or flush for femora alone).
3. Filter through 70 μm strainer; lyse red cells with ACK buffer (ammonium-chloride-potassium);
count viable cells.

subsections.title: Staining
subsections.body:
1. Fc block (2.4G2 anti-CD16/32) for 10 minutes on ice.
2. Lineage cocktail (biotinylated anti-Gr-1, Mac-1, B220, CD3, Ter119, CD4, CD8, NK1.1) — omit
Mac-1 for fetal liver.
3. Antibody panel (fluorophores chosen to avoid spillover):
• Streptavidin-APC-Cy7 or BV510 (Lineage)
• c-Kit-APC
• Sca-1-PE-Cy7
• CD150-PE or BV421
• CD48-FITC or BV605
• CD34-AF700
• CD16/32-PerCP-Cy5.5
• Flt3/CD135-PE-CF594 (if MPP subsetting)
4. Viability dye DAPI (add just before acquisition) or Zombie-NIR if planning fixation.
5. Wash, resuspend at ~10^7 cells/mL.

subsections.title: Gating hierarchy A canonical analysis strategy , applied serially:
subsections.body:
Plot 1: FSC-A (x) vs SSC-A (y) — gate on cells (exclude debris bottom-left)
↓
Plot 2: FSC-H (x) vs FSC-A (y) — gate on singlets (diagonal)
↓
Plot 3: SSC-A (x) vs DAPI (y) — gate on live (DAPI-low)
↓
Plot 4: SSC-A (x) vs Lineage (y) — gate on Lin-
↓
Plot 5: c-Kit (x) vs Sca-1 (y) — gate LSK (top-right) and LK (bottom-right)
↓ (LSK branch)
Plot 6: CD48 (x) vs CD150 (y) — quadrants:
CD150+CD48- = LT-HSC
CD150+CD48+ = MPP2
CD150-CD48+ = MPP3
CD150-CD48- = MPP1 (ST-HSC/MPP)
↓ (optional for MPP4)
Plot 7: Flt3 (x) vs CD150 (y) — Flt3+ = MPP4
↓ (LK branch from Plot 5)
Plot 8: CD34 (x) vs CD16/32 (y) within LK:
CD34+CD16/32^lo = CMP
CD34+CD16/32^hi = GMP
CD34-CD16/32^lo = MEP

subsections.title: Controls (non-negotiable)
subsections.body:
• Unstained sample to set autofluorescence.
• Single-color compensation controls (UltraComp beads preferred; cells for tandem dye degradation).
• FMO for CD150, CD48, Flt3, CD34, CD16/32 — each boundary should be drawn at FMOdefined negative.
• Biological reference : include wild-type control alongside knockout tissue; for DCAF7 conditional KO ( DCAF7^fl/fl;Mx1-Cre or Vav-Cre), include DCAF7^fl/fl Cre-negative littermate.
• CD45.1 vs CD45.2 antibodies validated in a 50:50 mix of CD45.1 and CD45.2 BM to confirm no
cross-reactivity .

subsections.title: Downstream applications
subsections.body:
• FACS sorting for:
– Methylcellulose CFU (plate 500–2000 LSK or 50–200 LT-HSC per 1.1 mL MethoCult).
– Competitive BMT (200–500 LT-HSC with 2–3 × 10^5 CD45.1 WBM competitors into lethally
irradiated CD45.1/.2 F1 recipients).
– Bulk or single-cell RNA-seq (LSK or LT-HSC into lysis buffer or Smart-seq/10x).
– Cell cycle analysis (intracellular Ki-67 + DAPI/Hoechst: G0 = Ki-67−DAPI^2N; G1 = Ki67+DAPI^2N; S/G2/M = Ki-67+DAPI>2N).
• Intracellular phosphoflow (pSTAT1, pSTAT5) requires PFA fix + methanol perm; crucial for
IFN/cytokine signaling experiments.

• DCAF7 intracellular staining (if validated antibody exists): fix/perm with Foxp3 buffer set
(eBioscience) or BD Transcription Factor buffer; include isotype and knockout controls.
Key facts:
• LT-HSC = Lin− Sca-1+ c-Kit+ CD150+ CD48− (~0.005–0.01% of BM).
• FMO controls, compensation, and viability exclusion are non-negotiable.
• Cell cycle state is read with Ki-67 + DNA dye; quiescent HSCs are Ki-67−DAPI^2N (G0).
Integrative synthesis
The hematopoietic system is the paradigmatic adult stem cell hierarchy: a quiescent LT-HSC apex,
lineage-primed MPP intermediates, and committed progenitors whose output is tuned by niche
signals, transcription factor networks, and systemic cytokines. Three themes recur across this section and are particularly important to articulate at the qualifying exam:
1. Quiescence is protective but not absolute . Entry into cycle — driven by IFN-α (Essers 2009),
IL-1 (Pietras 2016), 5-FU, TPO, or loss of niche retention signals — triggers both productive
regeneration and cumulative functional damage. The UPS is deeply embedded in this balance:
p21, p27, p57, cyclins, and STAT proteins are all substrates of UPS ligases, and DCAF7 (as a
CRL4 substrate receptor component) sits in the class of proteins whose perturbation could
rewire cell cycle and stress responses.
2. Lineage identity is plastic under stress . Chronic IL-1, TNF, or IFN skew MPP composition
toward MPP3 (myeloid) at the expense of MPP4 (lymphoid), producing the aging and clonalhematopoiesis phenotype over time. This is the biologic substrate in which DNMT3A, TET2,
JAK2 and other clonal hematopoiesis driver mutations gain selective advantage — and it is
also where UPS and IFN signaling intersect most strongly .
3. Assay choice determines biological question . Transplantation queries LT-HSC self-renewal
under extreme stress; native tracing queries steady-state MPP-dominant output; in vitro
CFU/LTC-IC reports progenitor function without niche integration. A complete HSC phenotype in a DCAF7-conditional knockout must integrate all three, framed by appropriate
UPS and IFN stress challenges (5-FU serial challenge, poly(I:C)/IFN-α injection, LPS, serial
transplantation).
With this foundation, the committee can be expected to probe the intersection of DCAF7 biology
with HSC cycling (via CRL4 substrate ubiquitination of cell-cycle or differentiation factors), with
IFN signaling (through STAT or ISG ubiquitination), and with lineage output under stress — each
of which is interpretable only against the canonical framework laid out in this section.
The Fetal-to-Adult Hematopoietic Transition
keyFacts: LT-HSC = Lin− Sca-1+ c-Kit+ CD150+ CD48− (~0.005–0.01% of BM).
FMO controls, compensation, and viability exclusion are non-negotiable.

Cell cycle state is read with Ki-67 + DNA dye; quiescent HSCs are Ki-67−DAPI^2N (G0). Integrative synthesis The hematopoietic system is the paradigmatic adult stem cell hierarchy: a quiescent LT-HSC apex, lineage-primed MPP intermediates, and committed progenitors whose output is tuned by niche signals, transcription factor networks, and systemic cytokines. Three themes recur across this section and are particularly important to articulate at the qualifying exam:

Quiescence is protective but not absolute . Entry into cycle — driven by IFN-α (Essers 2009), IL-1 (Pietras 2016), 5-FU, TPO, or loss of niche retention signals — triggers both productive regeneration and cumulative functional damage. The UPS is deeply embedded in this balance: p21, p27, p57, cyclins, and STAT proteins are all substrates of UPS ligases, and DCAF7 (as a CRL4 substrate receptor component) sits in the class of proteins whose perturbation could rewire cell cycle and stress responses.

Lineage identity is plastic under stress . Chronic IL-1, TNF, or IFN skew MPP composition toward MPP3 (myeloid) at the expense of MPP4 (lymphoid), producing the aging and clonalhematopoiesis phenotype over time. This is the biologic substrate in which DNMT3A, TET2, JAK2 and other clonal hematopoiesis driver mutations gain selective advantage — and it is also where UPS and IFN signaling intersect most strongly .

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The fetal-to-adult hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) transition is one of the most conceptually rich,
mechanistically unresolved, and translationally consequential developmental windows in mam49

malian biology . It is the interval during which a rapidly proliferating, lineage-skewed, nichedependent fetal stem cell population is reprogrammed into the largely quiescent, lineage-balanced,
bone marrow (BM)-resident adult stem cell pool that will sustain hematopoiesis for the organism’s
lifespan. For a thesis centered on DCAF7 and a specific Aim focused on the perinatal window,
this section is load-bearing: the phenotypes observed in the Vav-Cre Dcaf7 conditional knockout
(cKO) — absence of disruption at E14.5, severe disruption by postnatal day 4 (P4) — are only interpretable against the backdrop of this transition and, in particular, the perinatal type I interferon
(IFN-I) pulse recently defined by the Magee laboratory (Li et al. 2020). This section builds from definitions and comparative biology through intrinsic timing, molecular regulators, the IFN-I pulse,
niche maturation, and culminates in mechanistic hypotheses for DCAF7 function at this window.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• The fetal-to-adult HSC transition spans from roughly embryonic day 16.5 (E16.5) through approximately 8 weeks postnatal in the mouse.
• The perinatal window (last week of gestation through first two postnatal weeks) is the critical
reprogramming interval.
• Changes are multidimensional: cell cycle, metabolism, niche, self-renewal, lineage output,
epigenome, transcriptome.
• The switch is not binary (Li et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell): reprogramming is gradual and uncoordinated; individual cells co-express fetal and adult genes during the transition.
In the mouse, definitive HSCs arise in the aorta-gonad-mesonephros (AGM) region around E10.5,
colonize the fetal liver (FL) where they expand dramatically between E12 and E16, begin colonizing the bone marrow around E17.5, and progressively acquire adult identity over the first several
weeks of postnatal life. The end-point of this maturation — a functionally “adult” HSC compartment — is not reached until approximately 4 to 8 weeks postnatal depending on the feature assayed
(Bowie et al. 2006 J Clin Invest; Bowie et al. 2007 PNAS). The perinatal window, operationally defined as the last week of gestation (E15.5 through birth) and the first two postnatal weeks, is the
densest interval of change and is where most of the interesting cell-biological, transcriptional, and
epigenetic reprogramming occurs.
Critically , the transition is not a coordinated, binary switch. Single-cell transcriptomic studies (Li
et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell) have revealed that at any given moment during the transition, individual
HSCs and progenitors co-express fetaland adult-characteristic transcripts in varying proportions;
the population shifts by a gradient rather than a step function. This has two important consequences. First, it means that “fetal” and “adult” are endpoints of a continuum rather than discrete
states, and that developmental timing studies must sample densely across the window. Second,
it means that any perturbation — genetic, pharmacologic, or disease-associated — that biases this
continuum can produce a population that is neither fully fetal nor fully adult. This framing is
directly relevant to interpreting Dcaf7 cKO phenotypes: a P4 cKO is sampling a population in
the midst of reprogramming, and loss of DCAF7 may skew the balance between fetal and adult
transcriptional modules.
The dimensions of change include: cell cycle (rapidly cycling fetal HSCs transition to quiescent
adult long-term HSCs); metabolism (a shift in ATP provenance and reliance on oxidative phos50

phorylation versus glycolysis, discussed below); niche (from fetal liver supportive stroma to bone
marrow endosteal/perivascular niches); self-renewal capacity (robust, expansion-competent fetal self-renewal transitions to the maintenance self-renewal of adult HSCs); lineage output (fetalbiased lineages such as B1a cells and gamma-delta T cells give way to adult B2 and conventional
alpha-beta T cells); and both the chromatin accessibility landscape and the steady-state transcriptome.
keyFacts: The fetal-to-adult HSC transition spans from roughly embryonic day 16.5 (E16.5) through approximately 8 weeks postnatal in the mouse.
The perinatal window (last week of gestation through first two postnatal weeks) is the critical reprogramming interval.
Changes are multidimensional: cell cycle, metabolism, niche, self-renewal, lineage output, epigenome, transcriptome.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• Fetal HSCs self-renew expansively; fetal liver HSC pool expands roughly 38-fold between E12
and E16 (Morrison et al. 1995 PNAS).
• Adult LT-HSCs are approximately 95% quiescent; fetal HSCs are approximately 100% cycling.
• Fetal HSCs engraft faster and outcompete adult HSCs in mixed competitive transplants.
• Fetal HSCs preferentially produce B1a cells, gamma-delta T cells, and tissue-resident
macrophages.
• Fetal-to-adult hemoglobin switching (gamma-to-beta) is erythroid-specific and controlled by
BCL11A (Sankaran et al. 2008 Science).
Self-renewal and expansion. Fetal HSCs are expansion-competent: Morrison and colleagues
(Morrison et al. 1995 PNAS) demonstrated that the fetal liver HSC compartment expands approximately 38-fold between E12 and E16, a feat that has no parallel in the adult BM, where the HSC
pool is maintained at near-steady-state numbers and per-cell division rates are low. Adult HSCs
are quiescent; the majority of long-term repopulating HSCs (LT-HSCs) divide approximately every
145 days or less frequently depending on the clonal analysis method (Wilson et al. 2008 Cell; Foudi
et al. 2009 Nat Biotechnol), although the precise rarity of division in LT-HSCs remains methodologically debated. Fetal HSCs, by contrast, are essentially all in cycle: Ki-67, BrdU, and Fucci reporter
studies converge on the conclusion that fetal liver HSCs cycle approximately every 10 to 14 hours.
Cell cycle state. The transition from continuously cycling to deeply quiescent is progressive, not
abrupt. Bowie et al. 2006 J Clin Invest and subsequent single-cell transcriptomic work indicate that
quiescence entry begins in the late fetal liver and continues over the first several postnatal weeks.
The transition is driven in part by cell-intrinsic clock mechanisms (see E3) and in part by niche
maturation (see E8). By P7-P14, a substantial fraction of HSCs have entered deep quiescence, and
by 8 weeks the quiescent steady-state is established.
Niche. Fetal HSCs reside in the fetal liver, supported by hepatoblasts, stellate cells, and endothelial cells producing IGF2, angiopoietin-like proteins (ANGPTL2, ANGPTL3), thrombopoietin, and
SCF (Zhang et al. 2006 Blood; Chou and Lodish 2010 PNAS). Adult HSCs reside in BM, primarily
in perivascular niches defined by LepR+ mesenchymal stromal cells and endothelial cells producing CXCL12 and SCF (Ding and Morrison 2013 Nature; Greenbaum et al. 2013 Nature). The niche
switch is progressive and overlapping; a transient portal vessel-associated Nestin+ NG2+ pericyte
niche has been described in the perinatal BM (Khan et al. 2016 Science) that supports HSCs during
colonization before the mature LepR+ niche is fully established.
Engraftment kinetics. Fetal HSCs engraft recipients more rapidly and outcompete adult HSCs
when co-transplanted into the same recipient at equal doses (Rebel et al. 1996 Blood; Bowie
et al. 2007 PNAS). This competitive advantage reflects superior homing, proliferation, and

self-renewal capacities of the fetal compartment.
Surface markers. Fetal HSCs and adult HSCs share core phenotypic markers (Lin-, Sca-1+, c-Kit+,
CD150+, CD48-) but differ in several auxiliary markers. Notably , fetal HSCs are often Mac-1positive (CD11b-dim), whereas adult LT-HSCs are Mac-1-negative (Morrison et al. 1995 PNAS;
Kim et al. 2006 Blood). This distinction is historically important because early HSC purification
protocols relied on Mac-1-positivity as a fetal HSC marker.
Lineage output. Fetal HSCs bias toward B1a cells (peritoneal self-renewing innate-like B cells),
gamma-delta T cells, and tissue-resident macrophage precursors that seed peripheral tissues
(microglia-contributing fetal liver monocytes, for example, although the dominant microglial
population is yolk-sac derived — see E9). Adult HSCs produce conventional B2 cells, conventional
alpha-beta T cells, and bone marrow-derived monocytes/macrophages. The capacity to produce
B1a cells is one of the clearest assays of retained fetal identity — an assay directly employed in
the Ifnar1 knockout work discussed in E5.
Fetal-to-adult hemoglobin switching. Erythroid cells undergo a well-characterized hemoglobin
switch from fetal hemoglobin (HbF, gamma-globin tetramers with alpha-globin) to adult
hemoglobin (HbA, beta-globin tetramers with alpha-globin). This switch is erythroid-cellintrinsic and regulated by the transcriptional repressor BCL11A (Sankaran et al. 2008 Science).
Pharmacologic and genetic reactivation of HbF via BCL11A suppression forms the therapeutic
basis of exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy), the CRISPR therapy for sickle cell anemia (Frangoul et al. 2021 NEJM). The hemoglobin switch is distinct from the HSC-intrinsic fetal-to-adult
transition — it occurs in erythroid progeny , not stem cells — but conceptually parallel and often
invoked in the field.
Metabolism. The metabolic profile of fetal versus adult HSCs is nuanced and the literature is not
fully consistent. The predominant framing is that adult LT-HSCs rely heavily on glycolysis (low
mitochondrial activity , low ROS, low oxygen consumption) to preserve quiescence and protect
genomic integrity (Simsek et al. 2010 Cell Stem Cell; Takubo et al. 2013 Cell Stem Cell), whereas
fetal HSCs have higher mitochondrial activity consistent with rapid proliferation demands. However, other work has complicated this binary view, and ongoing studies continue to refine the
picture. The safest statement for a qualifying exam is that adult LT-HSCs are more glycolytic than
committed progenitors and are mitochondrially quieter than proliferating fetal HSCs — with acknowledgment that the field continues to debate the fine details.
Epigenome. Fetal HSCs have a globally more accessible chromatin landscape than adult HSCs,
with broader distribution of active marks (H3K4me3, H3K27ac) and lower levels of H3K27me3 at
lineage-affiliated genes. Adult HSCs acquire more H3K27me3-mediated silencing at lineage genes,
contributing to the constrained, lineage-balanced output of the adult compartment.
keyFacts: Fetal HSCs self-renew expansively; fetal liver HSC pool expands roughly 38-fold between E12 and E16 (Morrison et al. 1995 PNAS).
Adult LT-HSCs are approximately 95% quiescent; fetal HSCs are approximately 100% cycling.
Fetal HSCs engraft faster and outcompete adult HSCs in mixed competitive transplants.
Fetal HSCs preferentially produce B1a cells, gamma-delta T cells, and tissue-resident macrophages.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• Bowie et al. 2007 PNAS transplanted fetal HSCs into adult recipients and observed that they
switch to adult-like behavior at the same intrinsic age, regardless of environment.
• The switch is therefore cell-autonomous and governed by an intrinsic clock, not nicheinstructed.
• The clock is coordinated but non-synchronous across individual HSCs.

A central question in fetal-to-adult biology is whether the switch is driven by extrinsic niche cues
(the HSC migrates from fetal liver to BM and is then instructed by BM signals to become adult) or by
an intrinsic developmental clock (the HSC keeps time cell-autonomously and switches regardless
of niche). Bowie et al. 2007 PNAS addressed this definitively by transplanting fetal liver HSCs into
adult recipients and tracking their proliferative behavior over time. The transplanted fetal HSCs
continued to behave in a fetal-like (cycling) manner for a period corresponding to their remaining developmental age, then switched to adult-like quiescence at the age-appropriate time point,
regardless of the fact that they were residing in an adult BM niche. Reciprocal experiments and
subsequent work have supported this conclusion: the switch is predominantly cell-autonomous
and intrinsic timing is the dominant mode.
This has two important implications. First, the molecular machinery that counts developmental
time and executes the switch is carried within the HSC itself, placing strong emphasis on intrinsic
regulators (LIN28B, SOX17, EZH2, HMGA2, IGF2BP2/1, BMI1 — discussed in E4). Second, while
the clock is coordinated across cells (most cells switch within a similar developmental window),
it is not perfectly synchronous; individual cells switch at slightly different times, which is part of
why the population-level transition appears gradual rather than stepwise.
The intrinsic clock is not absolute; niche cues modulate it. The perinatal IFN-I pulse (E5) is a clear
example of a signal acting during the transition window that shapes the trajectory , even though
it is not strictly required to initiate the switch. For a DCAF7 proposal, the intrinsic nature of the
clock means that DCAF7 function at this window is most plausibly cell-intrinsic: its substrates and
molecular role inside HSCs and progenitors are the most likely locus of its developmental function.
keyFacts: Bowie et al. 2007 PNAS transplanted fetal HSCs into adult recipients and observed that they switch to adult-like behavior at the same intrinsic age, regardless of environment.
The switch is therefore cell-autonomous and governed by an intrinsic clock, not nicheinstructed.
The clock is coordinated but non-synchronous across individual HSCs.

sections.title: Key cell-intrinsic regulators of the switch

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• LIN28B/let-7 axis is the canonical fetal identity module; LIN28B is high in fetal HSCs, represses
let-7, which otherwise targets Hmga2, Igf2bp1/2, Myc.
• SOX17 is required for fetal HSC maintenance but dispensable in adult HSCs (Kim, Saunders,
Morrison 2007 Cell).
• EZH2 is required for fetal self-renewal; adult HSCs can use EZH1 compensatorily (MochizukiKashio et al. 2011 Blood).
• BMI1 (PRC1) is required for adult HSC self-renewal via Ink4a/Arf repression (Park et al. 2003
Nature).
• Many regulators (TET2, MLL1, DNMT3A/B, MYCN, TCF3, ERG) contribute to fine-tuning the
transition.
LIN28B / let-7 axis The LIN28B/let-7 axis is the most mechanistically tractable fetal identity
module. LIN28B is an RNA-binding protein highly expressed in fetal HSCs and developmentally downregulated into adulthood. It represses the biogenesis of let-7 family microRNAs by
binding the terminal loop of pre-let-7 and recruiting terminal uridyltransferases that mark pre-let7 for degradation (Heo et al. 2008 Mol Cell; Viswanathan et al. 2008 Science). The let-7 targets
relevant to fetal identity include HMGA2 (chromatin architectural factor), IGF2BP1 and IGF2BP2
(mRNA-binding proteins stabilizing fetal transcripts), and MYC. When LIN28B is high, let-7 is
low, and HMGA2/IGF2BP1/2/MYC are high — establishing the fetal expression program. When

LIN28B is developmentally downregulated, let-7 accumulates and the fetal program is silenced
post-transcriptionally .
Yuan et al. 2012 Science showed that enforced expression of LIN28B in adult HSPCs reprograms
them toward fetal-like lymphopoiesis, including B1a cell production — a functional restoration
of a fetal lineage output. This establishes LIN28B as a master regulator sufficient to impose fetal
identity . Conversely , loss of LIN28B accelerates adoption of adult identity . The axis is conserved:
LIN28A, a paralog, plays an analogous role in some contexts and is reactivated in some leukemias,
placing LIN28B at the intersection of fetal identity and oncogenic potential.
SOX17 Kim, Saunders, and Morrison 2007 Cell identified SOX17 as the cleanest fetal-specific
regulator: Sox17 is expressed in fetal HSCs and required for their maintenance, but is not expressed
in and not required by adult HSCs. Conditional deletion of Sox17 in fetal but not adult HSCs results
in loss of fetal HSCs while adult HSCs are unperturbed. This phenotype cleanly dissociates fetal
from adult self-renewal programs and places SOX17 at the core of the fetal transcriptional module.
EZH2 and the PRC2/PRC1 switch Mochizuki-Kashio et al. 2011 Blood demonstrated that EZH2,
the canonical PRC2 H3K27 methyltransferase, is required for fetal HSC self-renewal but dispensable for adult HSC maintenance. The mechanism involves functional compensation by the paralog
EZH1: adult HSCs express sufficient EZH1 to maintain PRC2 activity on critical targets, whereas
fetal HSCs rely uniquely on EZH2. This is another example of a clean fetal-adult regulatory switch,
and it implicates chromatin remodeling as a driver of the transition.
On the PRC1 side, BMI1 is required for adult HSC self-renewal through its repression of the
Ink4a/Arf (Cdkn2a) locus (Park et al. 2003 Nature; Lessard and Sauvageau 2003 Nature). Loss of
Bmi1 leads to de-repression of p16(INK4A) and p19(ARF), driving premature senescence of adult
HSCs. Fetal HSCs are less dependent on BMI1-mediated Ink4a/Arf repression because the locus
is less poised to fire in the fetal context.
HMGA2 HMGA2 is a chromatin architectural non-histone protein that is high in fetal HSCs and
downregulated in the adult via let-7-mediated repression of its 3’ UTR (Nishino et al. 2008 Cell).
HMGA2 is required for fetal HSC expansion; loss of HMGA2 impairs fetal self-renewal but has
modest effects on adult HSC maintenance. HMGA2 sits downstream of the LIN28B/let-7 axis and
is one of the key effectors of that pathway in hematopoiesis.
IGF2BP2 and IGF2BP1 IGF2BP1 and IGF2BP2 are mRNA-binding proteins that stabilize a set of
fetal transcripts, are themselves let-7 targets, and are high in fetal HSCs. Li et al. 2020 Cell Stem
Cell addressed their roles in fetal HSC identity and showed that they contribute to maintaining the
fetal transcriptome. These proteins are of particular interest for the Dcaf7 proposal because they
are candidate developmental substrates of a CRL4-DCAF7 ubiquitin ligase complex (see E11).
Other regulators TET2 controls 5-hydroxymethylcytosine accumulation and is involved in the
fetal-to-adult DNA methylation landscape reshaping; DNMT3A/B establish adult methylation
patterns; MLL1 (KMT2A) regulates Hox gene expression and HSC function across the transition;
MYCN is particularly important in neonatal and juvenile hematopoiesis, partly compensating for

MYC; TCF3 (E2A) and ERG are required for HSC maintenance with stage-specific contributions.
The collective effect is a layered regulatory network in which multiple modules contribute to the
gradual, uncoordinated reprogramming observed at single-cell resolution.
keyFacts: LIN28B/let-7 axis is the canonical fetal identity module; LIN28B is high in fetal HSCs, represses let-7, which otherwise targets Hmga2, Igf2bp1/2, Myc.
SOX17 is required for fetal HSC maintenance but dispensable in adult HSCs (Kim, Saunders, Morrison 2007 Cell).
EZH2 is required for fetal self-renewal; adult HSCs can use EZH1 compensatorily (MochizukiKashio et al. 2011 Blood).
BMI1 (PRC1) is required for adult HSC self-renewal via Ink4a/Arf repression (Park et al. 2003 Nature).

sections.title: The perinatal type I interferon pulse (Li et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell — LOAD-BEARING)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• Li et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell 27(5):732-747 (Magee lab) identified a sterile, developmentally programmed type I IFN pulse at E16.5-E18.5 in mouse.
• The IFN source is fetal skin, not hematopoietic cells, not placenta, not microbiome (germ-free
mice still show the pulse).
• Ifnar1 knockout: HSC numbers unchanged, but multipotent progenitor (MPP) and lymphoidbiased HPC-1 expansion fails perinatally .
• Ifnar1-/- HSCs retain fetal-like function (B1a output preserved); MHC-I genes (H2-K1, H2-D1,
B2m) are reduced in progenitors.
• The pulse reshapes the enhancer landscape; 271 enhancers switch H3K27ac from fetal to adult
configuration.
• FLT3-ITD-driven HPC expansion is rescued by Ifnar1 loss, linking the IFN window to leukemogenic permissiveness.
This subsection is load-bearing for Aim 2 and must be understood in detail.
Discovery and timing Li Y et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell volume 27 issue 5 pages 732-747 (Magee laboratory) performed single-cell RNA sequencing on Lin- c-Kit+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor
cells (HSPCs) sampled at E16.5, P0, P7, P14, and 8 weeks postnatal. The analysis revealed a striking, transient, developmentally coordinated induction of interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs) during late fetal and early postnatal life, peaking at E16.5-E18.5. Bulk RNA-seq on sorted HSPCs confirmed the finding: Irf7, Ifit1, Ifi27l2a, Mx1 and other canonical ISGs peak late prenatally . Proteinlevel ELISA for IFN-alpha and IFN-beta in fetal liver extracts showed approximately 3-fold higher
IFN protein at E18.5 compared to E14.5. Maternal serum IFN levels were low, establishing that the
source of the fetal IFN is intrinsic to the fetus, not of maternal origin.
Source of the IFN A central surprise of the study was the tissue origin of the IFN. The Magee
group systematically queried fetal tissues by qPCR for IFN-alpha/beta transcript abundance. Fetal hematopoietic compartments — dendritic cells, macrophages, and thymocytes — were all negative. The placenta, a natural first candidate, expressed IFN transcripts only at low levels. Fetal
skin emerged as the dominant source, with IFN-alpha transcript levels substantially higher than
in any other examined tissue. To address whether the microbiome or commensal colonization
drove the pulse (a plausible prior hypothesis, given the timing of initial microbial exposure), the
group compared IFN induction in conventional and germ-free mice: IFN induction at E18.5 was
unchanged in germ-free animals. The pulse is therefore a sterile, developmentally programmed
event, cell-intrinsic to fetal skin, and independent of microbial stimulation. This was a paradigmshifting result, overturning the expectation that perinatal IFN signaling in HSPCs would be driven
by microbiome exposure.

Functional consequences — Ifnar1 knockout phenotype The functional dissection of the IFN
pulse was performed via global Ifnar1-/- mice (IFN alpha/beta receptor subunit 1, the common
receptor chain for type I IFNs). Key phenotypes:
• HSC numbers are unchanged at E16.5, P0, and P14 between WT and Ifnar1-/- mice. HSCs
themselves do not require IFN signaling for their own maintenance at this window.
• Hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) expansion fails in Ifnar1-/- animals. Wild-type HPCs
expand 3- to 4-fold between E16.5 and P0; Ifnar1-/- HPCs do not expand.
• Multipotent progenitors (MPPs) and FLK2+ HPC-1 (a lymphoid-biased, MPP4-like population)
are depleted from P0 onward in Ifnar1-/- mice.
• HPC-2 is less affected, and pre-granulocyte-macrophage (pGM) and granulocyte-macrophage
progenitors (GMPs) are only marginally affected, indicating that the IFN pulse specifically supports lymphoid-biased progenitor expansion.
• Ifnar1-/- HSCs retain fetal-like function. Peritoneal B1a cell output is preserved, and competitive engraftment at P0 and P14 is normal. BrdU labeling at P7 shows that quiescence entry is not
delayed in Ifnar1-/-. These results argue that the IFN pulse is not required for the cell-intrinsic
fetal-to-adult clock in HSCs.
• MHC class I genes (H2-K1, H2-D1, B2m) are reduced across Ifnar1-/- progenitors. The IFN
pulse normally upregulates MHC-I in HPCs, likely priming them for immune surveillance interactions and possibly contributing to progenitor identity .
The important interpretive summary: the perinatal IFN pulse is not required for HSC maintenance
or the intrinsic fetal-to-adult switch, but is required for proper expansion and output of lymphoidbiased progenitors and for upregulation of MHC-I. It is a signal that tunes the progenitor compartment during the transition, not one that drives the stem cell transition itself.
Epigenetic and transcriptional reprogramming The Magee group performed ATAC-seq and
ChIPmentation for H3K4me1 and H3K27ac at P7, P14, and P21 on sorted HSPCs. They mapped
approximately 1,230 putative enhancers active across this window. Of these, 271 enhancers exhibited a “switch” in H3K27ac marking from a fetal to an adult configuration — active in the fetal
state and becoming inactive, or vice versa, across the transition window. A critical observation:
fetal enhancers that lose H3K27ac remain chromatin-accessible in adult HSPCs. They are not decommissioned at the level of chromatin accessibility; rather, active marks are redistributed. The
adult enhancer landscape is therefore layered onto a retained fetal chromatin template rather than
replacing it.
Motif enrichment analysis at the enhancers that become H3K27ac-marked in the adult state was
enriched for ETS family , RUNX family , AP-1 (FOS/JUN), and IRF (interferon regulatory factor)
binding motifs. The presence of IRF motifs at adult-activated enhancers is mechanistically consistent with IFN signaling acting through IRF transcription factors to imprint the adult enhancer
program. Identity scoring using quadratic programming approaches (Biddy et al. 2018; Kong et
al. 2020) confirmed that the transition is gradual at the single-cell level, consistent with E1.
FLT3-ITD leukemogenesis connection A striking additional finding tied the IFN pulse to oncogenic permissiveness. FLT3-ITD (internal tandem duplication of FLT3, a recurrent AML mutation) expressed on the Ifnar1-/- background still produced HSC depletion but did not produce the
FLT3-ITD-driven HPC expansion that is normally observed. The canonical FLT3-ITD target genes

— Bcl2, Myc, Met, Csf1r, Irf8 — were not induced in the Ifnar1-/- context. The interpretation:
IFN signaling at the perinatal window is permissive for oncogenic transformation by FLT3-ITD.
Removing IFN during this window blocks the transformation competency . This is a direct link
between developmental IFN signaling and leukemogenesis, with clear implications for childhood
leukemia biology . It also provides a conceptual framework for thinking about how perturbations
of IFN signaling during the perinatal window (e.g., via DCAF7 loss destabilizing IFIT3 or other
IFN pathway components) could have outsize effects on hematopoietic trajectory .
keyFacts: Li et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell 27(5):732-747 (Magee lab) identified a sterile, developmentally programmed type I IFN pulse at E16.5-E18.5 in mouse.
The IFN source is fetal skin, not hematopoietic cells, not placenta, not microbiome (germ-free mice still show the pulse).
Ifnar1 knockout: HSC numbers unchanged, but multipotent progenitor (MPP) and lymphoidbiased HPC-1 expansion fails perinatally .
Ifnar1-/- HSCs retain fetal-like function (B1a output preserved); MHC-I genes (H2-K1, H2-D1, B2m) are reduced in progenitors.
The pulse reshapes the enhancer landscape; 271 enhancers switch H3K27ac from fetal to adult configuration.

sections.title: Is basal IFN signaling required for steady-state adult hematopoiesis? (Li et al. 2023 Blood Adv)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• Li et al. 2023 Blood Adv is the Magee lab follow-up examining Ifnar1-/- at P14 and adult stages
more carefully .
• Finding: basal IFN signaling has only modest effects on steady-state adult hematopoietic output.
• The perinatal IFN pulse is a developmental event, not a chronic requirement.
• Aberrantly elevated IFN (as in DCAF7 loss or chronic inflammation) can still reshape
hematopoiesis even though basal IFN is dispensable.
A natural follow-up question after Li et al. 2020 was whether IFN signaling matters in the adult
steady-state, or whether the perinatal pulse is an isolated developmental event. The Magee laboratory’s 2023 Blood Advances paper (Li et al. 2023) addressed this by carefully examining Ifnar1-/-
mice at P14 and in full adulthood. The finding was that basal IFN signaling has only modest effects
on steady-state hematopoietic output. Adult Ifnar1-/- mice have largely normal blood production,
normal HSC and progenitor compartments in the BM, and normal peripheral blood counts, with
only minor deviations. This strongly supports a model in which the perinatal IFN pulse is a discrete developmental event that reshapes the HPC compartment and imprints the adult enhancer
landscape, rather than a chronic signal that must be maintained throughout life.
For the DCAF7 proposal, this framing is important in two directions. First, it legitimizes the focus
on the perinatal window: if IFN signaling matters developmentally but is dispensable thereafter,
then perturbations of the IFN pathway that act specifically during the perinatal window (for example via DCAF7 regulation of IFIT3 stability) are biologically meaningful. Second, and importantly
as a caveat: aberrantly elevated IFN signaling can still reshape hematopoiesis in the adult, even if
basal IFN is dispensable. This is well established from chronic viral infection, interferonopathies,
and disease states (discussed in E7). So “basal IFN is not required” does not mean “IFN cannot
matter in the adult”; it means “the baseline IFN tone is low and loss of it is tolerated, but elevation
above baseline has consequences.” If DCAF7 loss increases IFN signaling (via IFIT3 stabilization
or otherwise), the phenotype could reflect aberrantly elevated IFN in a window or context where
the baseline is low but the system is responsive.
keyFacts: Li et al. 2023 Blood Adv is the Magee lab follow-up examining Ifnar1-/- at P14 and adult stages more carefully .
Finding: basal IFN signaling has only modest effects on steady-state adult hematopoietic output.
The perinatal IFN pulse is a developmental event, not a chronic requirement.

sections.title: Aberrant IFN in disease (Serra et al. 2026 Am J Hematol)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• Chronic type I IFN signaling in sickle cell anemia (SCA) drives myeloid skewing of HSPCs.

• In a cohort of 739 SCA children, neutrophilia rises approximately 2.5-fold in the first 9 months
of life.
• SCA CD34+ scRNA-seq shows expansion of the neutrophil progenitor (NeutroP) cluster and
an elevated ISG signature (MX1, IFI44L, IRF1, ISG15, STAT1, STAT2, IRF9, BST2).
• CSF3R (G-CSFR) is upregulated in SCA HSPCs as an IFN-driven consequence.
• Hydroxyurea reverses STAT1/MX1/IFIT1/ISG15 elevation in SCA neutrophils — a novel immunomodulatory mechanism beyond fetal hemoglobin induction.
Serra et al. 2026 Am J Hematol provides a disease-state parallel to the developmental IFN biology .
In sickle cell anemia, chronic hemolysis and inflammation drive persistent type I IFN signaling,
which in turn reshapes HSPC output toward the myeloid lineage. In a large pediatric cohort (739
SCA children), neutrophilia rises approximately 2.5-fold over the first 9 months of life, establishing
the clinical timing of the skew. Single-cell RNA-seq of SCA CD34+ cells shows that the neutrophil
progenitor (NeutroP) cluster nearly doubles in relative abundance (from 2.1% to 3.8%), accompanied by a strong ISG signature including MX1, IFI44L, IRF1, ISG15, STAT1, STAT2, IRF9, and
BST2. CSF3R (G-CSF receptor) is upregulated in SCA HSPCs, and this upregulation is an IFNdriven consequence, not a primary response to G-CSF elevation. Ex vivo granulopoiesis assays
confirm the skew: SCA HSPCs cultured in granulocyte-favoring conditions yield approximately
20% CD11b+CD36+ monocytic cells at day 19 compared to 6% in healthy donors.
A particularly important finding: hydroxyurea, the standard SCA therapy historically understood
primarily as an inducer of fetal hemoglobin, reverses the STAT1/MX1/IFIT1/ISG15 elevation in
SCA neutrophils. This is a novel immunomodulatory mechanism for hydroxyurea, distinct from
its HbF induction activity , and suggests that the clinical benefit of hydroxyurea in SCA includes
damping chronic IFN signaling.
The parallel with the developmental IFN biology is instructive: in both settings, the magnitude
and duration of type I IFN signaling determines the HSPC lineage trajectory . At the perinatal
pulse, IFN supports lymphoid-biased progenitor expansion and imprints the adult enhancer landscape. In chronic SCA inflammation, sustained IFN drives myeloid skewing and neutrophil overproduction. The mechanistic commonalities (ISG induction via STAT1/STAT2/IRF9, enhancer reprogramming) and the dose/duration differences (pulse versus chronic) frame IFN signaling as a
context-dependent HSPC-instructive signal. For the DCAF7 proposal, this is directly relevant: if
DCAF7 regulates the magnitude of IFN responses (via IFIT3 or other substrates), loss of DCAF7
could produce an aberrant IFN tone with consequences that depend on the window in which it
occurs.
keyFacts: Chronic type I IFN signaling in sickle cell anemia (SCA) drives myeloid skewing of HSPCs.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• HSCs migrate from fetal liver to bone marrow around E17.5 in the mouse.
• Spleen serves as an intermediate hematopoietic site in some species, less prominent in mouse.
• Bone marrow niche establishment requires bone ossification, vascularization, and stromal cell
specification.
• A transient portal vessel-associated Nestin+ NG2+ pericyte niche operates perinatally (Khan
et al. 2016 Science).
• Kikuchi and Kondo 2006 PNAS: the fetal-to-adult identity switch occurs in the BM after birth,

not during migration.
HSC migration from fetal liver to BM occurs approximately at E17.5 in mice, coinciding with bone
ossification, marrow cavity formation, and establishment of a permissive stromal microenvironment. In some species (e.g., humans), the spleen serves as an intermediate hematopoietic site between fetal liver and BM; in mouse, the spleen’s contribution is less prominent but not absent. The
establishment of the BM niche depends on endochondral ossification, vascular invasion, and the
differentiation of mesenchymal stromal cells into the LepR+, CXCL12-abundant reticular (CAR)
cell population that will support adult HSCs (Omatsu et al. 2010 Immunity; Ding and Morrison
2013 Nature; Greenbaum et al. 2013 Nature).
Khan et al. 2016 Science described a transient perinatal niche at portal vessels, populated by
Nestin+ NG2+ pericytes, that supports HSCs during BM colonization. This niche is specific to the
perinatal window and is replaced/supplemented by the mature perivascular niche in adulthood.
The existence of this transient niche underscores that BM maturation is itself a developmental
process overlapping with the fetal-to-adult HSC transition.
Kikuchi and Kondo 2006 PNAS established that the fetal-to-adult identity switch occurs in the BM
after birth, not during migration from fetal liver. This supports the intrinsic clock model (E3) —
migration does not itself trigger the switch — while also emphasizing that the niche environment
during the switch is the BM rather than the liver.
keyFacts: HSCs migrate from fetal liver to bone marrow around E17.5 in the mouse.
Spleen serves as an intermediate hematopoietic site in some species, less prominent in mouse.
Bone marrow niche establishment requires bone ossification, vascularization, and stromal cell specification.
A transient portal vessel-associated Nestin+ NG2+ pericyte niche operates perinatally (Khan et al. 2016 Science).
Kikuchi and Kondo 2006 PNAS: the fetal-to-adult identity switch occurs in the BM after birth,

sections.title: Perinatal HSC origin and identity of tissue-resident macrophages

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• Yolk-sac-derived primitive macrophages seed tissues (microglia, Kupffer cells, alveolar
macrophages).
• Fetal-liver-derived monocyte macrophages also contribute to tissue-resident populations.
• Adult BM-HSC-derived macrophages replace some tissue populations with ongoing turnover.
• Fetal-origin tissue macrophages are primed by the perinatal IFN pulse, which may shape their
long-term properties.
Tissue-resident macrophages are a mosaic population with developmental origins spanning yolksac erythro-myeloid progenitors, fetal liver monocyte precursors, and adult BM HSC-derived
monocytes. The dominant developmental framework (Ginhoux et al. 2010 Science; Schulz et
al. 2012 Science; Hoeffel et al. 2015 Immunity) establishes that microglia are essentially entirely
yolk-sac derived and maintained by local self-renewal with minimal BM-HSC contribution,
whereas other populations (Kupffer cells, alveolar macrophages, intestinal macrophages) have
varying mixtures of yolk-sac, fetal liver, and adult BM contributions, with some populations
actively replaced by BM-derived monocytes throughout life.
For the DCAF7 proposal, the relevance is twofold. First, the Vav-Cre driver turns on in all
hematopoietic cells including fetal liver monocyte precursors, meaning that some tissue-resident
macrophage populations will have been targeted. Second, fetal-origin tissue macrophages are
primed by the perinatal IFN pulse — they develop and seed tissues in the context of elevated IFN
signaling. Perturbations of the IFN response at this window (including, potentially , via DCAF7
loss) could have lasting consequences for tissue macrophage identity and function, even if they
are not the primary phenotypic readout of the cKO.
keyFacts: Yolk-sac-derived primitive macrophages seed tissues (microglia, Kupffer cells, alveolar macrophages).
Fetal-liver-derived monocyte macrophages also contribute to tissue-resident populations.
Adult BM-HSC-derived macrophages replace some tissue populations with ongoing turnover.

sections.title: Why P4 matters for the Vav-Cre Dcaf7 KO

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• Dcaf7 expression peaks at approximately P4 in public mouse hematopoietic datasets.
• P4 coincides with the tail of the perinatal IFN pulse, the fetal-to-adult HSC transition, BM colonization, and the switch from rapid fetal cycling to adult quiescence.
• Vav-Cre turns on at approximately E12.5 (pan-hematopoietic, early).
• E14.5 Vav-Cre Dcaf7 cKO shows no phenotype (DCAF7 not yet rate-limiting at this stage).
• P4 Vav-Cre Dcaf7 cKO shows severe phenotype — temporal specificity strongly supports a
developmental role.
The temporal profile of the Vav-Cre Dcaf7 cKO phenotype is the most direct evidence that DCAF7
plays a role at the fetal-to-adult transition, and specifically at the perinatal window. Vav-Cre drives
Cre recombinase expression in all hematopoietic cells starting at approximately E12.5 (de Boer et
al. 2003 Eur J Immunol; Stadtfeld and Graf 2005 Development). Dcaf7 is therefore deleted from
keyFacts: Dcaf7 expression peaks at approximately P4 in public mouse hematopoietic datasets.
P4 coincides with the tail of the perinatal IFN pulse, the fetal-to-adult HSC transition, BM colonization, and the switch from rapid fetal cycling to adult quiescence.
Vav-Cre turns on at approximately E12.5 (pan-hematopoietic, early).
E14.5 Vav-Cre Dcaf7 cKO shows no phenotype (DCAF7 not yet rate-limiting at this stage).

P4 Vav-Cre Dcaf7 cKO shows severe phenotype — temporal specificity strongly supports a developmental role. The temporal profile of the Vav-Cre Dcaf7 cKO phenotype is the most direct evidence that DCAF7 plays a role at the fetal-to-adult transition, and specifically at the perinatal window. Vav-Cre drives Cre recombinase expression in all hematopoietic cells starting at approximately E12.5 (de Boer et al. 2003 Eur J Immunol; Stadtfeld and Graf 2005 Development). Dcaf7 is therefore deleted from

subsections.title: onward in the hematopoietic compartment
subsections.body:
Yet at E14.5 — two days after deletion, in the fetal

liver — no phenotype is observed. This rules out an acute requirement for DCAF7 in early fetal
HSCs and HPCs. By P4, however, a severe phenotype has emerged. The temporal gap between
deletion (E12.5) and phenotype (P4) indicates that DCAF7 becomes functionally critical only at the
perinatal window.
This timing coincides with four major events:
1. The tail of the perinatal IFN pulse (peaks E18.5, persists postnatally through P7-P14).
2. The cell-intrinsic fetal-to-adult HSC transition (gradual across this window).
3. BM colonization and establishment of the adult niche.
4. The switch from rapid fetal cycling to adult quiescence entry .
The coincidence of P4 peak Dcaf7 expression with these events, combined with the temporal specificity of the cKO phenotype, strongly suggests that DCAF7 has a stage-specific function at this
window. It is not a constitutive HSC requirement (fetal HSCs do not need it); it is not an adult
maintenance factor (adult HSCs are not the primary phenotypic readout in the cKO); it is specifically required at the transition.

sections.title: Speculative mechanisms linking DCAF7 to the transition

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Key facts:
• Mechanism 1: DCAF7 clips IFIT3 during the perinatal IFN pulse to temper amplification.
• Mechanism 2: DCAF7 degrades fetal identity factors (LIN28B, IGF2BP1/2, SOX17) to allow
adult program installation.
• Mechanism 3: DCAF7 scaffolds DYRK1A/HIPK2 to control cell cycle and p53 during rapid
expansion.
• Mechanism 4: DCAF7 has an unknown developmental substrate that Aim 2 proteomics will
identify .
These are the four primary hypotheses that frame Aim 2 and should be articulated clearly at the

committee meeting.
Mechanism 1 — IFIT3 regulation during the IFN pulse. DCAF7 has been reported to regulate
IFIT3 stability via CRL4-mediated ubiquitination (hypothesized based on preliminary data in the
lab). During the perinatal IFN pulse, IFIT3 is induced and contributes to IFN signal amplification.
DCAF7-mediated IFIT3 turnover may serve to temper the amplitude or duration of the pulse, ensuring that HPC expansion proceeds appropriately without excessive IFN-driven stress. Loss of
DCAF7 would then prolong or amplify the pulse, potentially producing HSC exhaustion, altered
lineage output, or myeloid skewing reminiscent of the chronic-IFN SCA phenotype (E7). This
mechanism is testable via IFIT3 levels, ISG magnitude, and epistasis with Ifnar1.
Mechanism 2 — Degradation of fetal identity factors. The LIN28B/IGF2BP1/2/SOX17 fetal identity module must be downregulated for adult identity to be installed. Transcriptional downregulation is part of this (LIN28B mRNA decreases across the transition), but post-translational degradation may also contribute. DCAF7 could be the CRL4 substrate receptor that targets one or more of
these fetal factors for ubiquitin-proteasome degradation at the perinatal window. Loss of DCAF7
would lead to persistence of fetal identity proteins beyond their developmentally appropriate window, delaying or preventing adoption of the adult program. This mechanism would be testable
via stability assays on candidate substrates in the cKO versus WT and by mass spectrometry for
steady-state differences in fetal factor abundance. This mechanism is particularly appealing because it connects DCAF7’s known CRL4 substrate-receptor function with the cell-intrinsic clock
established in E3.
Mechanism 3 — DYRK1A/HIPK2 scaffolding. DCAF7 has established scaffolding interactions
with DYRK1A and HIPK2, kinases involved in cell cycle control, p53 regulation, and apoptosis.
During the perinatal window, HSCs and HPCs undergo a controlled transition from rapid proliferation to quiescence, and this requires precise balancing of cell cycle regulators and apoptotic
thresholds. DCAF7 may scaffold DYRK1A/HIPK2 activity to control this balance; loss would disrupt it, producing either aberrant apoptosis, cell cycle dysregulation, or both. This mechanism
is testable via phospho-proteomics, cell cycle profiling (Fucci, BrdU/EdU), and apoptosis assays
(Annexin V , cleaved caspase-3).
Mechanism 4 — Unknown developmental substrate. The most honest framing is that CRL4DCAF7 may have a developmental substrate that has not yet been identified. Aim 2’s proteomics
(presumably TurboID or DIA-MS substrate trapping in fetal/perinatal HSPCs) is designed to surface such substrates without committing to a prior hypothesis. This is the most open-ended but
also the highest-potential mechanism, because identifying a novel substrate specifically required
at the perinatal window would be a major contribution.
These mechanisms are not mutually exclusive and indeed may operate in parallel. A unifying
framework is that DCAF7 integrates multiple signaling and identity inputs at the perinatal window — IFN magnitude, fetal factor turnover, and kinase-scaffolded cell cycle control — to execute
orderly transition to adult identity . Loss of DCAF7 disrupts this integration, producing the severe
P4 phenotype while sparing earlier and later windows where it is dispensable or compensated.

Interferon Signaling, ISGs, and the IFIT Family
Scope and why this section matters for your defense. Your thesis sits at the intersection of two literatures that your committee chair knows intimately: the ubiquitin–
proteasome system (UPS) in hematopoiesis, and interferon signaling. Aim 1 rests on
the claim that DCAF7, a CRL4 substrate receptor, degrades IFIT3 in JAK2V617F MPN
cells, and that genetic or pharmacologic loss of DCAF7 stabilizes IFIT3 and hypersensitizes MPN clones to IFN-α. To defend this, you must be fluent not only in IFIT3
biology but in the entire type I IFN signaling cascade, its negative regulation, the ISG
landscape, and the clinical context of IFN-α therapy in MPN. Aim 2 (fetal-to-adult transition) additionally demands you understand how developmental IFN pulses shape
HSC ontogeny .
This section is organized as structured prose interleaved with Committee Q: boxes —
the hard questions you should expect and the depth of answer you should have ready .
keyFacts: Mechanism 1: DCAF7 clips IFIT3 during the perinatal IFN pulse to temper amplification.
Mechanism 2: DCAF7 degrades fetal identity factors (LIN28B, IGF2BP1/2, SOX17) to allow adult program installation.
Mechanism 3: DCAF7 scaffolds DYRK1A/HIPK2 to control cell cycle and p53 during rapid expansion.
Mechanism 4: DCAF7 has an unknown developmental substrate that Aim 2 proteomics will identify . These are the four primary hypotheses that frame Aim 2 and should be articulated clearly at the

sections.title: T ype I vs T ype II vs T ype III interferons — molecular distinctions

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Interferons are cytokines defined historically by their ability to “interfere” with viral replication.
They are now classified into three families based on receptor usage, producing cells, and the biology they drive.
T ype I IFNs
The type I family is the largest and most heterogeneous. In humans it comprises 13 IFN-α subtypes
(IFN-α1, α2, α4, α5, α6, α7, α8, α10, α13, α14, α16, α17, α21), plus IFN-β, IFN-ε, IFN-κ, and IFNω. (IFN-δ and IFN-ζ/limitin exist in other species.) Despite their diversity , all type I IFNs signal
through a single heterodimeric receptor: IFNAR1 + IFNAR2 . The subtypes differ in affinity for
the two receptor chains and in their tissue/cell-type distribution of producers, which gives rise to
subtly different ISG signatures — an important point when the student is asked “why IFN-α and
not IFN-β” for MPN therapy .
• IFN-α: produced predominantly by plasmacytoid dendritic cells (pDCs) and, to a lesser extent,
most nucleated cells during viral infection. TLR7/9 sensing in pDCs is the canonical trigger.
• IFN-β: produced by virtually any nucleated cell upon RIG-I/MDA5/TLR3/cGAS-STING activation. Higher affinity for IFNAR1 than most α subtypes, which translates to a more sustained,
“tonic” signal.
• IFN-ε, IFN-κ : constitutively expressed in reproductive tract and keratinocytes respectively;
barrier immunity .
• IFN-ω: closer to IFN-α evolutionarily; expressed by leukocytes.
Crucial correction to carry into the exam: Type I IFNs are fundamentally pro-inflammatory cytokines. Their antiproliferative and antiviral effects are real but operate alongside, not instead
of, a pro-inflammatory program (induction of CXCL10, IP-10, IL-6 amplification, MHC class I upregulation, APC maturation, Th1 polarization). The “antiproliferative/antitumor” framing the

student initially used conflated a downstream consequence in susceptible cell types with the intrinsic identity of the cytokine. In MPN, the therapeutic window depends on the balance between
pro-inflammatory consequences (which the body tolerates for months to years) and the specific
antiproliferative/apoptotic effect on JAK2V617F HSCs.
T ype II IFN
Type II is a family of one: IFN-γ. It signals through IFNGR1 + IFNGR2 . Unlike type I, which is
an innate immune cytokine made by almost any cell, IFN-γ is produced primarily by activated T
cells (Th1, CD8+) and NK cells , with ILC1s and some macrophage populations contributing. Its
receptor complex is structurally distinct, its ISG signature overlaps with but is not identical to type
I, and its physiology is tilted toward adaptive immunity and macrophage activation rather than
antiviral defense per se.
T ype III IFNs
Type III comprises IFN-λ1 (IL-29), IFN-λ2 (IL-28A), IFN-λ3 (IL-28B), and IFN-λ4 . They signal
through a heterodimer of IFNLR1 (IL-28RA) and IL10R2 (shared with IL-10, IL-22, IL-26) . The
defining feature of type III is tissue bias : IFNLR1 is expressed predominantly on epithelial cells
(respiratory , GI, hepatic) and on neutrophils. This gives type III IFNs an “epithelial/mucosal”
flavor — they control barrier viral infections (norovirus, influenza) without driving the systemic
inflammation characteristic of type I. The downstream signaling is essentially identical to type I
(JAK1/TYK2, ISGF3, ISRE), but the restricted receptor distribution limits the biology .
T able F1. IFN family comparison
