Canonical reviews: Platanias 2005 Nat Rev Immunol (PMID 15864272) for JAK-STAT mechanics;
Diamond & Farzan 2013 Nat Rev Immunol (PMID 23237964) for IFIT family in antiviral immunity .

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The JAK-STAT pathway is the core signal-transduction cascade by which all three IFN families act.
Understanding the wiring precisely matters because JAK2V617F disrupts it in specific ways, and
the therapeutic logic of IFN-α in MPN depends on those specifics.
Receptor–JAK pairing (memorize)
• Type I: IFNAR1 constitutively associates with TYK2; IFNAR2 with JAK1.
• Type II: IFNGR1 with JAK1; IFNGR2 with JAK2.
• Type III: IFNLR1 with JAK1; IL10R2 with TYK2.
Signaling sequence
1. Ligand binding. IFN-α/β engages IFNAR2 first (higher-affinity chain) and recruits IFNAR1
to form the ternary complex.
2. Receptor dimerization brings JAKs into proximity. TYK2 and JAK1 trans-phosphorylate
each other on activation loops.
3. Receptor cytoplasmic tail phosphorylation. Activated JAKs phosphorylate tyrosines on IFNAR1 and IFNAR2 cytoplasmic tails, creating SH2-docking sites.
4. STAT recruitment via SH2 domains. STAT2 docks on IFNAR1 pY466; STAT1 then joins. JAKs
phosphorylate STAT1 Y701 and STAT2 Y690.
5. STAT dimerization and IRF9 recruitment. Phosphorylated STAT1–STAT2 heterodimers associate with IRF9 to form ISGF3 (STAT1–STAT2–IRF9).
6. Nuclear translocation. ISGF3 uses importin-α5 to enter the nucleus.
7. DNA binding. ISGF3 binds ISRE (interferon-stimulated response element; consensus
AGTTTCNNTTTC) in promoters of hundreds of ISGs.
8. Type II parallel. IFN-γ drives formation of GAF — a STAT1 homodimer — which binds GAS
(TTCNNNGAA) elements. The two DNA elements are related but distinct, which is why type
I and type II ISG signatures overlap (STAT1 is shared) but are not identical.
[FIGURE: IFN-α signaling schematic showing IFNAR1/2 ￿ TYK2/JAK1 ￿ STAT1/2 ￿ ISGF3
￿ ISRE]

Additional STAT s and cross-talk
While ISGF3/GAF are the canonical outputs, IFN signaling is promiscuous:
• STAT3: activated by many cytokines including IFNs; often pro-proliferative and anti-apoptotic.
Constitutively active downstream of JAK2V617F in MPN.
• STAT5(5A/5B): the dominant constitutively phosphorylated STAT in JAK2V617F MPN. STAT5
drives self-renewal, erythroid/megakaryocytic proliferation, and BCL-xL expression — this is
the pro-proliferative arm.
• STAT4, STAT6: lineage skewing (Th1, Th2) more than direct MPN relevance.
Why this wiring matters for JAK2V617F MPN — the key insight
JAK2V617F is a gain-of-function mutation in the JH2 pseudokinase domain that constitutively
activates JAK2 even without cytokine. Downstream, this preferentially phosphorylates STAT5
(and STAT3) because those are the STATs that sit on homodimeric type I cytokine receptors (EPOR,
TPOR/MPL, G-CSFR) that normally use JAK2. The STAT1/STAT2/ISGF3 branch is relatively
spared from direct constitutive activation because it requires the IFNAR1/2–TYK2/JAK1 complex
specifically , which is not the main substrate of JAK2V617F.
The therapeutic logic : JAK2V617F cells chronically experience STAT5/3 pro-proliferative signaling (which they depend on), but their STAT1/2 branch remains intact and is hyper-responsive
because JAK2V617F HSCs cycle more and their STAT1-dependent antiproliferative program bites
harder when triggered. IFN-α therapy drives ISGF3 maximally; ISG-mediated antiproliferation
/ apoptosis dominates over the dependent STAT5 signal in susceptible clones. The differential
sensitivity window is what gives IFN-α its remarkable ability to achieve molecular (not just hematologic) responses in a subset of PV and ET patients.
This framing (Vainchenker & Kralovics 2017, Blood) is exactly what the chair will probe when she
asks why IFN-α works.

sections.title: Negative regulation of IFN signaling — where the UPS enters

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A signaling pathway that induces hundreds of genes including its own amplifiers (IRF7, STAT1)
needs tight negative regulation to avoid runaway inflammation. The negative regulators fall into
five mechanistic classes, and — importantly for your thesis — several of them are UPS components
or substrates. This is the conceptual slot where DCAF7/IFIT3 fits.
SOCS proteins
• SOCS1 and SOCS3 are the primary IFN-induced negative regulators. They contain an SH2
domain that binds phosphorylated JAKs or receptor tyrosines, a KIR (kinase inhibitory region)
that directly occludes the JAK active site, and a SOCS box that recruits Elongin B/C–Cul5–
Rbx2 to form an E3 ligase (ECS complex). SOCS thus does double duty: steric inhibition plus
ubiquitination of JAKs and receptor components.

• MPN relevance : SOCS3 is transcriptionally downregulated in JAK2V617F MPN (Fourouclas
2008 Haematologica), partly by promoter methylation, partly by loss of negative feedback because the pathway is already uncoupled from ligand. Reduced SOCS3 contributes to constitutive JAK2 activity and also to the hyper-responsive STAT1 phenotype upon IFN-α addition.
PIAS family
PIAS1/3 bind phosphorylated STATs and block DNA binding (PIAS1 for STAT1, PIAS3 for STAT3).
They also have SUMO ligase activity that contributes to STAT silencing. Less directly UPS but
mechanistically parallel.
USP18 — a particularly important node
USP18 (UBP43) is an IFN-induced deubiquitinase that does two things: 1. Removes the ubiquitinlike modifier ISG15 from substrates (deISGylation). 2. Independently of its enzymatic activity ,
USP18 binds IFNAR2 and displaces JAK1, producing strong negative feedback on type I IFN signaling.
USP18 is the major mechanism behind IFN desensitization — the reason chronic IFN-α exposure
eventually blunts ISG induction. ISG15 itself stabilizes USP18 (by preventing its SKP2-mediated
degradation), which is why ISG15-deficient patients paradoxically have hyperactive type I IFN
responses. USP18 is central to interferonopathy biology .
PTPN2, SHP1, SHP2 phosphatases
Tyrosine phosphatases reverse JAK and STAT phosphorylation. SHP1 (PTPN6) is hematopoietic
and particularly important — loss-of-function in motheaten mice causes hyper-cytokine phenotype. PTPN2 dephosphorylates JAK1 and STAT1.
Ubiquitin-mediated receptor turnover — direct UPS node
IFNAR1 ubiquitination by SCF^βT rCP is the dominant mechanism of IFNAR1 downregulation.
Priming phosphorylation on IFNAR1 by CK1α and p38 creates a phosphodegron that βTrCP recognizes; K48-linked ubiquitination drives endocytosis and lysosomal/proteasomal destruction.
Chronic IFN exposure or inflammatory stress accelerates IFNAR1 turnover, shutting down type I
signaling at the receptor level. This is an important source of heterogeneity in patient IFN response
— cells with high CK1α/p38 activity (stressed, inflamed) downregulate IFNAR1 faster.
Where DCAF7/IFIT3 fits conceptually
The emerging theme of UPS regulation of IFN signaling is that nearly every node has a dedicated ubiquitin ligase : - JAKs ￿ SOCS/ECS - IFNAR1 ￿ SCF^βTrCP - STAT1 ￿ SLIM, other -
ISG15 system ￿ HERC5 (ligase), USP18 (DUB) - IFIT3 ￿ UBE2O (Li 2023 Cell Death Dis) and
CRL4^DCAF7 (your work)

The novelty and defensibility of your finding comes from placing IFIT3 — an ISG effector and ISG
amplifier — into this framework as a UPS substrate, with DCAF7 as the substrate receptor that
tunes its abundance in JAK2V617F cells. The pattern is coherent with the rest of the field.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Type I IFN induces hundreds of ISGs (estimates range from ~300 to >2000 depending on cell type
and stringency). The landmark functional screen is Schoggins 2011 Nature , which systematically
overexpressed ~380 human ISGs against a panel of viruses and identified the antiviral effectors.
Your committee will not ask you to memorize 300 ISGs, but it will ask you to know the major
functional categories and the representative members.
Functional categories
Antiviral effectors. - IFIT family (IFIT1/2/3/5) — RNA-binding, translation inhibition, signaling amplification. Your central family . - MX proteins (MX1, MX2) — dynamin-like GTPases that
form rings around viral nucleocapsids. - OAS/RNase L — 2’-5’ oligoadenylate synthetases activate
RNase L to degrade single-stranded RNA nonspecifically . - PKR (EIF2AK2) — phosphorylates
eIF2α to shut down cap-dependent translation globally . - ISG15/ISGylation system — ubiquitinlike modifier (HERC5 ligase); both covalent substrate modification and extracellular cytokine-like
functions. - APOBEC3 family — cytidine deaminases that hypermutate retroviral and some DNA
virus genomes. - T etherin (BST2) — tethers budding viral particles to the plasma membrane. -
IFITMs — block viral fusion.
Antiproliferative effectors. - IFIT1, IFIT3 — translation inhibition and ISG amplification feed antiproliferative output. - p21 (CDKN1A) — induced via STAT1; cell cycle arrest at G1/S. - XAF1
— binds XIAP , derepresses caspases, pro-apoptotic. - PML — nuclear body organizer; tumor suppressor, pro-senescence. - IRF1 — transcription factor with tumor-suppressor activity .
Immunomodulatory. - MHC class I (HLA-A/B/C and associated TAP , LMP2/7) — enhanced
antigen presentation to CD8+ T cells. - CXCL10 (IP-10), CXCL9, CXCL11 — recruit CXCR3+ T cells;
biomarkers of active IFN signaling. - IRF7 — the “master amplifier” of type I IFN; itself an ISG,
creates a positive feedback loop that sustains IFN-β ￿ IFN-α production. - IDO1 — tryptophan
metabolism, immune suppression.
Cell-death / tumor-suppressor. - TRAIL (TNFSF10) — death-receptor ligand. - XAF1 (as above). -
Caspase induction (especially in hematopoietic cells). - Bcl-2 family rebalancing (often via STAT1dependent Bcl-xL decrease and Bim/Bax increase).
Cell-type–specific ISG signatures
A critical point the chair may push on: the set of ISGs induced in a given cell type is not the set of
ISGs that matter. HSCs, granulocyte progenitors, and mature macrophages induce overlapping
but shifted ISG panels, and the functional output (proliferation arrest, differentiation, migration)
depends on which subset dominates. The Schoggins screen used a single cell type; translating to

hematopoiesis requires caution. Your scRNA-seq data in DCAF7-deficient HSPCs should be read
through this lens — do not assume the antiviral ISGs are the effectors; the antiproliferative and
immunomodulatory subsets are more likely to matter for MPN phenotype.

sections.title: IFIT family in depth — the central family for your thesis

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Members and nomenclature
Gene Aliases Size (aa) Key function
IFIT1 ISG56, P56, IFI56 478 Binds 5’-ppp-RNA
and cap0 RNA;
sequesters from
translation
IFIT2 ISG54, P54, IFI54 472 Binds AU-rich RNA;
pro-apoptotic;
partners with IFIT1/3
IFIT3 ISG60, P60, IFI60,
ISG49
490 Scaffold/bridge for
IFIT1-IFIT2-IFIT3
heterotrimer;
signaling amplifier
(MA VS, TBK1); your
DCAF7 substrate
IFIT5 ISG58, P58 482 Monomeric; binds
5’-ppp-RNA and
uncapped tRNAs
(IFIT4 does not exist in
humans; pseudogene)
Structural biology — TPR solenoid
All IFITs are built from tetratricopeptide repeats (TPRs) : ~34-residue helix-turn-helix motifs that
stack to form an α-helical solenoid (a superhelix). IFIT1/2/3/5 each have ~9 TPR motifs arranged
into two subdomains (N-terminal “cap” and C-terminal “extension”) that together generate a positively charged channel for RNA binding (IFIT1/5) or protein–protein interfaces (IFIT3).
Crystal structures (Abbas et al. 2013 for IFIT5, Yang et al. 2012 for IFIT2) revealed that IFIT1/5
clamp onto the 5’ end of RNA: the triphosphate and first transcribed nucleotide are threaded into
a cleft, and the absence of 2’-O methylation at the first nucleotide (cap0 vs cap1) is what distinguishes self from non-self mRNA. Host mRNAs are cap1 (2’-O-methylated by CMTR1/2), which
IFIT1 cannot bind. Viral mRNAs that bypass host capping machinery present as cap0 or 5’-ppp,
which IFIT1 binds and sequesters.

The IFIT1-IFIT2-IFIT3 heterotrimer (Fleith 2018 NAR, PMID 29554348)
Fleith and colleagues demonstrated biochemically that IFIT1, IFIT2, and IFIT3 form a defined heterotrimeric complex via their C-terminal regions, and that the complex has ~10-fold enhanced
translation-inhibitory activity compared to IFIT1 alone.
• IFIT3 is the scaffold. Its C-terminal domain simultaneously contacts IFIT1 and IFIT2. IFIT3
depletion causes loss of IFIT1 stability (IFIT1 becomes degraded when orphan). This is directly
relevant to your thesis: if DCAF7 degrades IFIT3, the downstream consequence is not only loss
of IFIT3 function but destabilization of IFIT1 — so a committee question about “why IFIT3 and
not the complex” has a clean answer.
• IFIT2 contributes AU-rich RNA binding and brings additional pro-apoptotic activity .
• The heterotrimer is the physiological effector during most viral infections; free IFIT1 is minor.
IFIT3 as a signaling amplifier — Chikhalya 2021 mBio (PMID 34724821)
This paper changed how the field thinks about IFIT3. Rather than viewing IFIT3 purely as a passive scaffold for IFIT1-mediated translation arrest, Chikhalya et al. showed that IFIT3 amplifies
upstream IFN signaling :
• IFIT3 binds MA VSat the mitochondrial outer membrane.
• It stabilizes the MA VS–TBK1interaction, enhancing IRF3 phosphorylation and thereby IFN-β
production.
• IFIT3 depletion blunts ISG induction in response to RIG-I agonists — not just loss of IFIT1
scaffolding, but loss of the upstream amplifier.
Why this matters for your thesis framing. The “antiproliferative” language for IFIT3 that you initially used is a mild extrapolation — IFIT3 does not directly inhibit proliferation the way p21 does.
What IFIT3 does is amplify the IFN ￿ ISGF3 ￿ ISG output , and that amplified output (including
p21, XAF1, MHC-I, CXCL10) is what drives antiproliferation and apoptosis of JAK2V617F clones.
The mechanistic claim should be: DCAF7 loss stabilizes IFIT3 ￿ IFIT3 amplifies IFN-α signaling at multiple nodes (MA VS/TBK1 upstream; IFIT1 scaffolding downstream) ￿ ISG output is
supraphysiologic ￿ MPN clone is pushed past the antiproliferative threshold. This framing is
both more mechanistic and more defensible than “IFIT3 is antiproliferative.”
IFIT3 post-translational regulation
Before your work, the only published E3 ligase for IFIT3 was:
• Li 2023 Cell Death Dis (PMID 38129382): UBE2O ubiquitinates IFIT3 on K236 in hepatocellular carcinoma, with K48-linked chains driving proteasomal degradation. UBE2O is a hybrid
E2/E3 (has both an E2 UBC domain and intrinsic substrate-recognition capacity). In HCC,
UBE2O is upregulated and IFIT3 is correspondingly depressed; UBE2O knockdown rescues
IFIT3 and impairs tumor growth.
Your DCAF7 finding extends this to a second ligase in a different disease context (MPN
hematopoiesis). Confirm whether CRL4^DCAF7 uses K236 or a different lysine — if the same

lysine, the two ligases are truly redundant/competitive on the same degron; if different lysines,
they likely represent distinct regulatory contexts. This is a question the chair will ask.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Your thesis touches two distinct IFN-in-hematopoiesis literatures: acute IFN action on HSCs and
chronic IFN / inflammation driving hematopoietic aging and myeloid bias. Both will come up.
Acute IFN activates dormant HSCs — Essers 2009 Nature
Essers and colleagues showed that a single dose of pIpC (a TLR3/MDA5 agonist that elicits type
I IFN) or recombinant IFN-α drives dormant long-term HSCs into cell cycle within 24–48 hours.
The mechanism is STAT1-dependent: STAT1 knockout HSCs fail to enter cycle upon IFN-α. Surface Sca-1 is upregulated as a biomarker. Key phenotypic consequences:
• Acute IFN ￿ HSC cycling ￿ transient proliferative response
• Chronic IFN ￿ HSC exhaustion (loss of self-renewal, eventual cytopenia)
Parallel findings for IFN-γ — Sato 2009 Nat Med, Matatall 2016, Baldridge 2010
• Sato 2009 Nat Med : IFN-γ likewise activates dormant HSCs, with similar exhaustion on
chronic exposure.
• Baldridge 2010 Nature : during infection (M. avium), IFN-γ mobilizes HSCs from their niche
to support emergency myelopoiesis.
• Matatall 2016 Cell Reports: IFN-γ drives myeloid skewing via C/EBPβ upregulation — HSCs
exposed to chronic IFN-γ preferentially produce myeloid progeny at the expense of lymphoid,
a signature of aged and inflamed hematopoiesis.
Chronic inflammation and HSC aging — Pietras
• Pietras 2014 J Exp Med : chronic inflammation (IL-1β, TNF, IFNs) biases HSCs toward myeloid
output.
• Pietras 2016 Nat Cell Biol : defined MPP subsets (MPP2, MPP3, MPP4) with inflammationregulated output balance.
Synthesis for MPN context
MPN clones live in a chronically inflamed bone marrow (elevated TNF-α, IL-6, TGF-β, type I IFN
signatures). The therapeutic paradox is that adding exogenous IFN-α to an already IFN-primed
environment cures some patients. The resolution is: 1. Baseline chronic inflammation is lowamplitude; therapeutic IFN-α is high-amplitude — a different dose-response regime with different downstream biology (chronic STAT1 tonic vs acute STAT1 burst). 2. JAK2V617F HSCs are

cycling more than normal HSCs, which makes them more vulnerable to STAT1-dependent antiproliferative/apoptotic programs that engage cycling cells preferentially . 3. Mullally 2014 and others:
JAK2V617F HSCs have increased cycling (active) populations that are preferentially affected by
IFN. 4. Normal HSCs tolerate the therapeutic IFN pulse with reversible cycling and minimal exhaustion if treatment is not chronic.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Clinical landscape
• Kiladjian 2008 Blood (PMID 18250227) : PEG-IFN-α2a in polycythemia vera — >50% complete hematologic response, ~20% achieving molecular response (measurable reduction in
JAK2V617F allele burden, sometimes to undetectable). First demonstration that IFN-α can
achieve molecular remission in a myeloid neoplasm — suggesting selective pressure on the
mutant clone, not just symptomatic blood-count control.
• Mascarenhas 2022 Blood, MPN-RC 112 (PMID 35007321) : head-to-head PEG-IFN-α2a vs hydroxyurea in high-risk PV and ET. Deeper molecular responses with IFN but not universal;
hematologic responses comparable. Established PEG-IFN as a legitimate first-line option in
high-risk PV/ET, especially for younger patients aiming at disease modification rather than
symptom control.
• MPD-RC / ROPHEUS trials : supportive evidence; ropeginterferon α-2b (Besremi) approved
for PV .
Mechanism — ongoing debate
Four non-mutually-exclusive models for why IFN-α targets JAK2V617F clones:
1. Direct cycling of quiescent mutant HSCs ￿ apoptosis or exhaustion. Mullally 2014 showed
JAK2V617F HSCs cycle more ￿ IFN-α engages them harder.
2. Differential ISG induction — mutant cells induce a stronger antiproliferative ISG program.
Directly relevant to your thesis: if DCAF7 loss further boosts ISG output (via IFIT3 stabilization), the effect is amplified.
3. CALR-mutant–specific vulnerability — Austin 2020 Haematologica showed IFN-α preferentially eliminates CALR-mutant iPSC-derived HSPCs. Mechanism may involve unfoldedprotein-response–ISG cross-talk.
4. Stem cell niche effects — IFN-α may remodel endothelial/mesenchymal niche in ways that
disfavor mutant HSC maintenance.
The most likely answer is a combination of (1) and (2).
Why some patients fail
• Hasan 2013 : patients with high baseline ISG expression (pre-treatment) paradoxically have
lower response rates — suggesting pathway is already desensitized (USP18 chronically in71

duced, IFNAR1 downregulated).
• Accessory mutations: DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1 co-mutations blunt response.
• TNF-α antagonism : MPN clones have been shown to secrete TNF that creates a selective advantage and antagonizes IFN.
• IFNAR1 downregulation / receptor-level desensitization.
• SOCS1/SOCS3 epigenetic heterogeneity.
Rationale for DCAF7 inhibition + IFN combination
If IFIT3 is a rate-limiting ISG amplifier, and DCAF7 tunes IFIT3 abundance, then DCAF7 inhibition should shift the ISG induction curve leftward (lower IFN dose achieves same ISG output)
and upward (higher maximal ISG output at any given IFN dose) . This converts non-responders
(pathway desensitized or underpowered) into responders, and allows dose reduction to mitigate
the inflammatory toxicity of IFN-α.
This is the core translational claim of Aim 1 — make sure you can articulate it crisply .

sections.title: Aberrant IFN in disease — sickle cell as instructive analogue

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Sickle cell disease — Serra 2026 Am J Hematol
Serra et al. demonstrated that sickle cell anemia (SCA) patients display an elevated peripheral ISG
signature (STAT1, MX1, IFIT1, ISG15 all upregulated in PBMCs and CD34+ HSPCs). This chronic
IFN tone drives:
• Myeloid skewing via CSF3R upregulation on progenitors (G-CSF hyperresponsiveness).
• Increased neutrophilia and vaso-occlusive inflammation.
• Hydroxyurea attenuates the ISG signature — part of its therapeutic mechanism in SCA may
be dampening chronic IFN signaling, not just raising HbF.
Parallel with MPN
Both SCA and MPN are chronic inflammatory hematopoietic disorders with elevated ISG tone .
In SCA the inflammation is pathogenic; in MPN it is part of the disease phenotype but is also
exploited therapeutically by IFN-α. The resolution of this apparent paradox rests on: - Chronic
baseline ISG ≠ acute therapeutic ISG (as above). - The cell type at risk differs: in SCA the myeloid
expansion is the problem; in MPN the JAK2V617F HSC is the problem, and it has unique vulnerabilities (cycling, STAT5-dependent but IFN-α-susceptible).
T ype I interferonopathies
• AGS (Aicardi-Goutières syndrome) : loss-of-function in nucleic-acid sensors (TREX1, RNase
H2, SAMHD1, ADAR1, IFIH1) ￿ endogenous nucleic acid accumulation ￿ chronic type I IFN
￿ severe neuroinflammation and vasculopathy .

• SA VI (STING-associated vasculopathy of infancy): gain-of-function STING ￿ chronic type I
IFN.
• SLE (lupus): an “IFN signature” is a cardinal feature; anifrolumab (anti-IFNAR1) is approved.
These conditions teach us that sustained high-amplitude type I IFN is toxic ; therapeutic IFN-α in
MPN works because it is pulsed, modulated, and acts on a specific vulnerable clone.

sections.title: IFN in development — perinatal pulse (briefly; full treatment in Section E)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This connects directly to Aim 2 (fetal-to-adult transition).
• Li 2020 Cell Stem Cell : a perinatal type I IFN pulse (~E16–E18.5) originating from fetal skin
drives hematopoietic progenitor expansion, MHC-I induction on HSCs, and permissiveness to
FLT3-ITD transformation if the mutation is present.
• Li 2023 Blood Adv : basal IFN signaling is dispensable for adult steady-state hematopoiesis
— so the perinatal pulse is a developmentally specific window.
• T emporal coincidence with DCAF7: DCAF7 protein rises at P4, roughly as the perinatal IFN
pulse is concluding. One plausible model: DCAF7 is part of the machinery that resolves the developmental IFN pulse by degrading IFIT3 (and perhaps other ISG effectors), closing the amplifier loop and allowing the transition from fetal/perinatal inflammatory-primed hematopoiesis
to adult steady-state. If DCAF7 fails to rise or is genetically lost, the perinatal ISG program persists inappropriately into adult HSCs, contributing to the fetal-to-adult transition defect you
observe.
This is a testable model and exactly the kind of integrative framing the chair will appreciate. It ties
Aim 1 and Aim 2 together: DCAF7’s function on IFIT3 is to modulate IFN amplification, which is
why DCAF7 loss has phenotypes in both MPN (hypersensitize to IFN-α) and development (fail to
resolve perinatal IFN program).
Integrative summary — how this section supports your defense
When the chair closes the qualifying exam with “So tell me again — why does DCAF7 matter in
MPN?”, you should be able to deliver this three-sentence synthesis without notes:
1. “Type I IFN therapy in MPN works because JAK2V617F clones have a differential sensitivity to the STAT1/STAT2/ISGF3 branch of IFN signaling — they cycle more, their STAT5/3
pro-proliferative program is dominant at baseline, and acute IFN-α pushes the antiproliferative ISG program above a threshold that the mutant clone cannot compensate.”
2. “IFIT3 is a rate-limiting amplifier of IFN signaling — it scaffolds the IFIT1/2/3 heterotrimer
that inhibits translation, and it binds MA VS to amplify upstream IRF3/IRF7 activation and
IFN-β production — so IFIT3 abundance directly sets ISG output.”

3. “DCAF7, via CRL4, degrades IFIT3; losing DCAF7 stabilizes IFIT3, amplifies ISG output
at a given IFN-α dose, and converts the low-amplitude, desensitized IFN response in nonresponder MPN patients into a high-amplitude therapeutic response — providing a mechanistically rational combination therapy that exploits a UPS node to tune a previously validated cytokine pathway.”
Everything in Sections F1–F9 is the mechanistic scaffolding that supports these three sentences.
Know the wiring (F2), the negative regulators (F3), the IFIT family in depth (F5), the MPN clinical
context (F7), and the integrative model (F9), and you will defend this section robustly .
Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
Scope of this section. MPN is the disease context of the thesis. DCAF7 matters because
it is a CRL4 substrate receptor whose knockdown stabilizes IFIT3 and amplifies type I
interferon (IFN-α) output — exactly the pathway that is uniquely effective at achieving
molecular remission in MPN. To defend that thesis, a candidate must be able to move
fluently from the clinical phenotype (a patient with polycythemia, splenomegaly , and
thrombosis) all the way down to the structural mechanism of JAK2V617F and the singlecell dynamics of the mutant stem cell. Section G is engineered to let you do that, with a
depth bias toward JAK-STAT signaling, clonal dynamics, IFN response heterogeneity ,
and MPN inflammation — the four topics most likely to be probed by a committee
whose chair studies UPS and IFN biology .

sections.title: Overview and Classification of the Chronic MPN Spectrum

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Myeloproliferative neoplasms are clonal hematopoietic stem-cell disorders characterized by the
expansion of one or more terminally differentiated myeloid lineages in the absence of dysplasia,
with a constitutive cytokine-independent proliferative drive that almost always funnels through
JAK-STAT. The three classical Philadelphia-chromosome–negative MPNs — polycythemia vera
(PV), essential thrombocythemia (ET), and primary myelofibrosis (PMF) — form a disease spectrum rather than three discrete entities. They share driver mutations (JAK2, CALR, MPL), a common cytokine-rich inflammatory milieu, a tendency to transition into one another, and a shared
terminal risk of transformation to post-MPN acute myeloid leukemia (sAML, often called “blast
phase”). The BCR::ABL1-positive disease chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is historically grouped
with MPN but is a different beast therapeutically and is typically discussed separately .
Epidemiology. There are roughly 30,000 new MPN cases per year in the United States. Annual
incidence is approximately 2–3 per 100,000 for PV , 1–2 per 100,000 for ET, and 0.5–1 per 100,000 for
PMF. Prevalence is higher than incidence would suggest because survival, particularly in PV and
ET, is measured in decades. Median age at diagnosis is 60–70; PV shows a mild male predominance
while ET is slightly enriched in females. A non-trivial fraction of the general population carries
a low-burden JAK2V617F clone without meeting MPN criteria — Nielsen and colleagues (2011,
Haematologica) reported JAK2V617F positivity in roughly 0.1–0.2% of unselected adults, a number
that rises steeply with age and overlaps with the CH/CHIP landscape (see Section C).

Classification frameworks.
Framework Year What it fixed
PVSG (Polycythemia V era
Study Group)
1970s First rigorous PV diagnostic
criteria (RBC mass,
splenomegaly , exclusion of
secondary causes)
WHO 2008 2008 First to incorporate
JAK2V617F as a major
criterion
Tefferi, Thiele & Vardiman
2009 Cancer (PMID 19806641)
2009 Clarified the transition to
molecular-era criteria;
integrated JAK2 exon 12 and
MPL into ET/PMF
classification
WHO 2016 — Arber 2016
Blood (PMID 27069254)
2016 Added CALR; lowered
hemoglobin threshold for PV
(Hb >16.5 g/dL men, >16.0
women) to capture “masked
PV”; formalized pre-PMF vs
overt PMF; made BM
histology a major criterion
WHO 2022 (and ICC 2022) 2022 Reinforced integration of
molecular and histopathologic
criteria; preserved three-entity
structure; adjusted grading
systems
The WHO 2016 update is the single most load-bearing reference for current diagnostic practice,
and the lowered PV hemoglobin threshold is a common source of trick questions: older guidelines
missed up to a third of JAK2V617F-positive patients whose Hb was in the 16–18 g/dL range. PrePMF is a morphologic category — thrombocytosis with overlapping clinical features of ET but
with megakaryocyte atypia and reticulin fibrosis grade 0–1 — and carries a worse prognosis than
true ET. Distinguishing pre-PMF from ET by bone marrow morphology is a recurring topic in
hematopathology conferences and is clinically meaningful, because pre-PMF patients progress to
overt MF faster.
Clinical features. The classic patient presents with one of three things: an incidental CBC abnormality (erythrocytosis, thrombocytosis), a thrombotic event (often venous, often in an unusual
site — splanchnic veins, cerebral venous sinuses, retinal), or a constellation of constitutional symptoms (fatigue, pruritus — especially aquagenic in PV , night sweats, bone pain, early satiety from
splenomegaly , and weight loss). Erythromelalgia (burning, erythema of the distal extremities)
is an ET/PV classic. Hemorrhage paradoxically coexists with thrombotic risk, especially at very
high platelet counts due to acquired von Willebrand syndrome. Splenomegaly is nearly universal
in overt PMF, common in PV , and less common in ET. The MPN Symptom Assessment Form Total
Symptom Score (MPN-SAF TSS) is the standard symptom measure and is driven heavily by the

same inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8) that are elevated in plasma.
Progression. PV and ET convert to post-PV or post-ET myelofibrosis at rates of roughly 10–20% at
15 years. Any MPN can evolve to blast-phase disease (AML or, more rarely , MDS/AML), but the
risk is front-loaded in PMF, where lifetime transformation risk is 5–15% and reaches higher with
accumulating high-risk mutations (ASXL1, SRSF2, EZH2, IDH1/2, TP53). Post-MPN AML has a
notoriously poor prognosis, with median survival measured in months, and is the major driver of
MPN mortality .
[FIGURE: MPN disease spectrum — PV/ET ￿ post-PV/ET MF ￿ blast phase, with driver mutation frequencies and progression rates]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
MPN is the textbook example of a disease with a small number of mutually-exclusive driver mutations that all converge on the same pathway . Understanding this convergence — and why the
pathway is uniquely tractable by IFN-α — is the biological scaffold of the thesis.

subsections.title: JAK2V617F
subsections.body:
JAK2V617F is a somatic gain-of-function mutation in exon 14 that substitutes phenylalanine for
valine at residue 617 within the JH2 pseudokinase domain. Independent discovery by four groups
in 2005 (James Nature, Kralovics NEJM, Baxter Lancet, Levine Cancer Cell) was one of the defining
moments of modern hematology: a single point mutation explained the long-suspected shared
pathophysiology of the MPNs and converted the field from phenotypic to molecular overnight.
Structural mechanism. JAK2 is a four-domain cytoplasmic kinase: FERM and SH2 domains tether
the kinase to cytokine receptors (EPOR, MPL, G-CSFR), the JH2 pseudokinase domain holds the
JH1 catalytic kinase in a cis-autoinhibited conformation, and JH1 is the active kinase. V617 lives on
the surface of JH2 at the JH1–JH2 interface. Substitution to phenylalanine stabilizes an alternative
JH2 conformation that disrupts the autoinhibitory contacts and releases JH1 into a constitutively
active state. Crucially , JAK2V617F still requires a homodimeric cytokine receptor scaffold (EPOR,
MPL, or G-CSFR) to signal — which explains the tissue restriction of the phenotype to erythroid,
megakaryocyte, and granulocyte lineages rather than, for example, lymphoid cells that use heterodimeric JAKs.
[FIGURE: JAK2 domain architecture — FERM–SH2–JH2–JH1; V617F in JH2; cartoon of released
autoinhibition]
Frequency. JAK2V617F is present in ~95% of PV , 50–60% of ET, and 50–60% of PMF. A small residual fraction of PV patients who are V617F-negative harbor JAK2 exon 12 mutations (N542-E543del,
E543-D544del, K539L, and related in-frame lesions) that cause an almost identical pathway output
but a distinctive erythroid-predominant phenotype.
Zygosity and allele burden. JAK2V617F can exist in heterozygous, hemizygous, or homozygous
states. Acquired uniparental disomy (UPD) at 9p — mitotic recombination that duplicates the
mutant allele and deletes the wild-type — is common in PV and correlates with homozygosity .
PV is enriched for homozygous clones; ET is more often heterozygous. Homozygosity shifts the
erythroid bias and is associated with higher hematocrit and higher risk of progression. Quantitative allele burden (measured by allele-specific qPCR or ddPCR on peripheral blood granulocytes)

is used as a pharmacodynamic biomarker, particularly for IFN-α response — deep molecular response (often defined as >50% reduction or <1% residual) is the pharmacologic goal in IFN trials.
Why JAK2V617F produces three different diseases. Multiple non-exclusive factors: (1) allele
dose — higher burden skews toward PV; (2) co-occurring mutations (e.g., TET2 or DNMT3A first
vs JAK2 first); (3) germline modifiers including the GGCC “46/1” haplotype, a predisposing polymorphism that also favors JAK2-mutant clones; (4) host factors including sex, age, and inflammatory milieu; (5) stem cell state at the time of transformation.

subsections.title: MPL mutations
subsections.body:
MPL encodes the thrombopoietin receptor (TPO-R, c-MPL). Recurrent activating mutations in the
transmembrane domain, predominantly W515L and W515K (and less frequently S505N, the latter
often familial), occur in approximately 3–5% of ET and 5–10% of PMF, and are mutually exclusive
with JAK2V617F. W515 is a tryptophan at the junction of the transmembrane and intracellular
domains that normally maintains the receptor in an inactive dimer state; mutation releases the
conformational brake and produces ligand-independent JAK2 activation. Pikman and colleagues
(2006) described the retroviral W515L model that established the phenotype in mice.

subsections.title: CALR mutations
subsections.body:
CALR (calreticulin) mutations were discovered simultaneously by Klampfl and Nangalia, both in
NEJM in 2013, and were the biggest surprise in MPN genetics post-JAK2. They account for roughly
20–25% of ET and 25–35% of PMF, and are the predominant driver in JAK2/MPL-negative disease.
Two classes of frameshift.
Type Lesion Frequency Phenotype bias
Type 1 52-bp deletion,
c.1092_1143del
(p.L367fs*46)
~60% of
CALR-mutant MPN
PMF > ET, deeper
leukocytosis, more
MF progression
Type 2 5-bp insertion,
c.1154_1155insTTGTC
(p.K385fs*47)
~30% ET-biased, higher
platelet counts
Other type 1-like /
type 2-like
Various +1
frameshifts
~10% Grouped by
C-terminal
basic/acidic character
Mechanism. All CALR mutations create +1 frameshifts in exon 9 that replace the wild-type Cterminus with a novel shared sequence and, critically , delete the KDEL ER-retention motif. The
mutant protein escapes the ER, traffics to the Golgi and cell surface, and physically binds MPL
through its novel positively-charged C-terminus engaging the N-glycosylated extracellular MPL
domain. This forces MPL into a constitutively dimerized, TPO-independent active conformation
that activates JAK2-STAT5 — the same pathway output as JAK2V617F, via a completely different
upstream mechanism. Type 1 and type 2 differ in the electrostatic character of the retained novel

C-terminus: type 1 retains more basic residues and engages MPL more efficiently , potentially explaining its more aggressive clinical phenotype. CALR-mutant MPN has lower hemoglobin and
higher platelet counts than JAK2-mutant MPN at diagnosis, is associated with younger age, and
carries a lower thrombotic risk — a rare “favorable” molecular subset within a malignant disease.

subsections.title: “Triple-negative” MPN
subsections.body:
Roughly 10% of PMF and a smaller fraction of ET lack mutations in JAK2, CALR, and MPL. Deep sequencing has revealed non-canonical JAK2 or MPL mutations, alternative activators such as CSF3R
(overlapping with atypical CML), or mutations in signaling regulators (LNK/SH2B3 is a frequent
hit). A subset of “triple-negative” patients is genuinely clonally hematopoietic without an identifiable driver and may reflect the limits of current sequencing or novel mechanisms.
[FIGURE: driver mutation landscape — JAK2V617F / JAK2 exon 12 / CALR type 1 / CALR type
2 / MPL W515 / triple negative; pie charts for PV , ET, PMF]
Unified pathway output. Every canonical MPN driver converges on constitutive homodimeric
cytokine-receptor-scaffolded JAK2 activation, producing dominantly STAT5-driven (and secondarily STAT3, STAT1) transcription in erythroid, megakaryocytic, and granulocytic progenitors.
Vainchenker and Kralovics (2017, Blood) is the canonical comprehensive review.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Driver mutations alone rarely explain the clinical heterogeneity of MPN. Whole-exome and targeted sequencing studies have built a picture in which MPN is a clonal hematopoiesis disorder
with a driver layer and an independently-acquired epigenetic/splicing/tumor-suppressor layer,
and the relative order of those acquisitions shapes disease biology .
Recurrent cooperating mutations (roughly ranked by frequency in PMF).
• TET2 — α-KG-dependent DNA demethylation; loss-of-function; frequently precedes JAK2 in
CH and in MPN; biases toward myeloid and increases HSC self-renewal. Present in 10–25% of
MPN.
• DNMT3A — DNA methyltransferase; loss-of-function; CH-type mutation that often precedes
JAK2.
• ASXL1 — polycomb-associated; loss-of-function truncations are strongly adverse prognostic
factors, particularly in PMF; one of the high-molecular-risk markers in MIPSS70.
• EZH2 — PRC2 catalytic subunit; loss-of-function; adverse, leukemic-transformation associated.
• IDH1/IDH2 — neomorphic gain-of-function producing 2-hydroxyglutarate; strong leukemictransformation association; therapeutically targetable with ivosidenib/enasidenib.
• SRSF2, U2AF1, SF3B1 — splicing factors; SRSF2-P95 mutations are strongly associated with
transformation and are one of the high-molecular-risk markers; SF3B1 is more common in
MDS/MPN overlap (especially MDS/MPN-RS-T) than in classical MPN.
• TP53 — loss-of-function or dominant-negative; near-universal in blast phase; rare at diagnosis.
• NRAS/KRAS, CBL, RUNX1 — occur at transformation.
Order matters. Ortmann and colleagues (2015, NEJM) showed that whether TET2 is acquired be78

fore or after JAK2 V617F changes the biology of the resulting clone. JAK2-first patients are younger
at diagnosis, have a higher PV:ET ratio, a higher thrombotic risk, and their single-cell-derived
colonies show JAK-STAT priming in stem cells that precedes TET2 loss. TET2-first patients have
larger pre-existing CH clones into which JAK2V617F is dropped, often present later, and respond
differently to ruxolitinib. This is a conceptually important result: it shows that the same two mutations can produce different diseases depending on order, and it is one of the pieces of evidence
that the pre-JAK2 epigenetic state of the stem cell determines fitness landscape.
Genomic classification. Grinfeld and colleagues (2018, NEJM, PMID 30304655) integrated mutational and clinical data from over 2,000 MPN patients to produce an eight-class genomic classification that outperforms the WHO phenotypic categories for prognosis. The classes include
CALR-mutant, JAK2/homozygous/chromatin, TP53/aneuploidy , and several others. This paper
is the single most-cited “genomic MPN” reference for modern prognosis and is highly likely to be
probed by a committee.
Prognostic scoring. MIPSS70 and MIPSS70+ v2.0 integrate high-molecular-risk mutations (ASXL1,
SRSF2, EZH2, IDH1/2, U2AF1 Q157) with karyotype, age, blood counts, and constitutional symptoms for PMF prognostication and transplant decisions. IPSS and DIPSS-plus are the older clinicalonly scores. For PV/ET, the relevant scores are IPSS-E (ET) and the newer MIPSS-ET/PV that
incorporate molecular data.
[FIGURE: MPN clonal evolution — CH background (TET2/DNMT3A) ￿ JAK2V617F driver acquisition ￿ subclonal evolution (ASXL1, EZH2, SRSF2, TP53) ￿ blast phase transformation; with
fishplot schematic]

sections.title: JAK-STAT as the Central MPN Driver

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The cytokine–JAK–STAT axis is the organizing principle of MPN biology , and everything from
hydroxyurea response to IFN selectivity to ruxolitinib benefit ultimately relates back to it.
The canonical cascade. Ligand (EPO, TPO, G-CSF) binds a preformed homodimeric receptor.
Receptor conformational change activates the constitutively-associated JAK2, which transphosphorylates its partner JAK2 and then phosphorylates receptor cytoplasmic tyrosines. Phosphotyrosines recruit STAT SH2 domains; JAK2 then phosphorylates STATs on their C-terminal
tyrosine. Phosphorylated STATs dimerize, translocate to the nucleus, and drive transcription of
STAT target genes including BCL-XL, CCND1/2, PIM1, MYC, CISH, and SOCS3.
What JAK2V617F changes. Constitutive JH1 activity means JAK2 no longer waits for ligand. Baseline phospho-STAT5 is elevated; the cell behaves as if EPO, TPO, and G-CSF were perpetually
present. STAT5 is the dominant effector in MPN (erythropoietic and megakaryocytic programs),
STAT3 contributes to proliferation and to the inflammatory secretory program, and STAT1 is paradoxically elevated, producing a basal interferon-gene signature in the absence of exogenous IFN.
This baseline STAT1/ISG skew is important for the thesis: it means MPN cells are “primed” in the
ISG axis and sensitive to perturbations that amplify that axis.
Parallel pathways. JAK2V617F also activates PI3K–AKT–mTOR (via scaffold proteins and STAT5
transcriptional targets) and MAPK (RAF-MEK-ERK). The PI3K axis contributes to survival and
metabolic rewiring; MAPK contributes to proliferation. Pharmacologic combinations that add
PI3K inhibition to JAK inhibition have been tested but have been limited by toxicity .
Hierarchy of pathway dependence. PV stem and progenitor cells are the most “JAK-addicted”

— they collapse rapidly under JAK inhibition. ET cells are intermediate. PMF cells often show
compensatory activation of parallel pathways, explaining the partial clinical benefit of single-agent
ruxolitinib in MF and the rationale for combination strategies (JAK + BET, JAK + BCL-XL with
navitoclax, JAK + MDM2, JAK + IFN).
Feedback loss. Normally , STAT5/STAT3 induce CISH and SOCS1/3, which bind phosphorylated
JAK2/receptor and inhibit further signaling. In MPN, this brake is broken: SOCS3 is epigenetically
silenced by promoter hypermethylation in a subset of JAK2V617F patients (Fourouclas 2008), and
LNK/SH2B3 — a negative regulator that binds phospho-JAK2 — is lost in a small fraction of triplenegative patients, further amplifying the signal. The combination of constitutively active JAK2 and
impaired feedback gives an abnormally sustained signal.
[FIGURE: JAK-STAT signaling in MPN — receptor dimer, JAK2V617F, STAT5/3/1, SOCS3 silencing, LNK loss; ISG baseline elevation]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The second conceptual pillar of modern MPN biology , after JAK-STAT, is that MPN is a chronic
inflammatory disease. Plasma and bone-marrow cytokines are elevated, and the elevation is not
a bystander — it is produced by the mutant clone and feeds back onto stem-cell fitness and niche
remodeling.
Elevated cytokines. TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8 (CXCL8), IL-1β, IP-10 (CXCL10), IL-2R, IL-10, IL-15, and
HGF are consistently elevated in PV , ET, and especially PMF. The cytokine burden correlates with
MPN-SAF TSS, with constitutional symptoms, and with cardiovascular risk independent of thrombosis.
Clone as cytokine source. Fisher and colleagues and others have shown that JAK2V617F-mutant
granulocytes and monocytes themselves produce elevated cytokines — the clone is the major cellular source of IL-8, IL-6, and TNF-α. This matters because it transforms MPN inflammation from
a “reactive” phenomenon to a clonal property , and it explains why JAK inhibition produces rapid
symptom benefit by shutting down cytokine production (even when allele burden is unchanged).
Hasselbalch’s hypothesis. Hans Hasselbalch has argued for over a decade that chronic inflammation is both a consequence and a cause of MPN — that the inflammatory milieu selects for JAK2mutant clones, and that resolving inflammation (with IFN-α or with anti-inflammatory strategies)
may be disease-modifying. The evidence is circumstantial but substantial: JAK2V617F CH is more
common in patients with autoimmune disease, cardiovascular inflammation, and chronic infection; IFN-α produces disease-modifying responses that correlate with reductions in inflammatory
markers; and MPN risk is modifiable by anti-inflammatory strategies in mouse models.
Marrow inflammation and fibrosis. The progression from ET/PV to post-MPN MF is driven by
stromal remodeling: mutant megakaryocytes secrete TGF-β, PDGF, bFGF, and CCL2 that activate
bone-marrow stromal cells, drive reticulin and collagen deposition, and produce the hypocellular,
fibrotic “dry tap” marrow. TGF-β is the dominant pro-fibrotic cytokine; its neutralization (luspatercept and others) is in clinical development.
Niche damage. Arranz and colleagues (2014, Nature) showed that JAK2V617F produces sympathetic nervous system damage in the bone marrow niche, with loss of Schwann-cell support for
the mesenchymal stromal compartment that normally sustains HSCs. This “neuropathy” means
MPN does not just outcompete normal HSCs — it actively degrades the niche that would support

them. β3-adrenergic agonists partially rescue the phenotype in mice. This is a key result because
it reframes MPN as a niche-remodeling disease, not just a cell-autonomous clone.
Systemic consequences. MPN patients have elevated cardiovascular risk even beyond what is
predicted by thrombosis alone. Inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and atherosclerosis acceleration are all plausible contributors. This is part of why control of inflammation is a legitimate
therapeutic goal beyond cytoreduction.
[FIGURE: MPN inflammation — mutant clone as cytokine source, marrow TGF-β/PDGF/bFGF
￿ fibrosis, niche neuropathy , systemic inflammation ￿ thrombosis and cardiovascular risk]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The MPN stem cell is the target of every disease-modifying therapy and the reason IFN-α works
where hydroxyurea and ruxolitinib do not. Understanding its cell-cycle state and its relationship
to the normal HSC pool is load-bearing for the thesis.
Mullally 2010 Cancer Cell. The foundational mouse paper. Conditional Jak2V617F at endogenous
dosage using Vav-Cre or Mx1-Cre produced a PV-like phenotype, and competitive transplantation
showed that Jak2V617F HSCs engraft but do not outcompete wild-type HSCs at low doses — in
fact, at limiting cell numbers they show a competitive disadvantage. This is a foundational insight:
MPN HSC fitness depends on context (cell number, niche, inflammation) and is not intrinsically
superior to normal HSCs. The result has been reproduced in multiple Jak2V617F knock-in alleles.
Functional heterogeneity. Lundberg and colleagues (2014) showed that MPN is sustained by a
rare population of disease-initiating cells within the phenotypic HSC compartment, with the bulk
of JAK2V617F-positive cells being short-term progenitors that cannot reconstitute disease on transplant. This finding explains why deep clinical responses with JAK-allele burden reduction can still
relapse — unless the rare disease-initiating cell is eliminated, the clone can regrow.
Cell-cycle state and IFN selectivity. JAK2V617F HSCs cycle more than wild-type HSCs, losing
some of the deep quiescence that normally protects the HSC pool. This cycling is the mechanistic
basis for IFN-α selectivity: proliferating HSCs are more sensitive to type I IFN-induced apoptosis and to IFN-driven mobilization out of quiescence, whereas wild-type HSCs retreat into quiescence and survive. Prick and colleagues (2014) and subsequent work formalized this framework.
The thesis-relevant extension: anything that amplifies IFN signaling (DCAF7 inhibition, stabilizing IFIT3, blocking ISG turnover) should preferentially eliminate mutant HSCs because it pushes
their already-cycling state into terminal IFN-induced senescence/apoptosis while WT HSCs remain quiescent.
Single-cell temporal dynamics. Three recent landmark studies reconstructed JAK2V617F
acquisition and expansion timelines from single-cell phylogenies. - Psaila 2020 — single-cell
RNA+mutation profiling of PMF patients; identified megakaryocyte-lineage bias and MK-primed
progenitors. - V an Egeren 2021 Cell Stem Cell — somatic phylogenetic reconstruction showing
that JAK2V617F is often acquired in utero or in childhood and expands over decades before
clinical presentation. This reframes MPN as a disease of decades-long clonal drift, not acute
transformation. - Williams 2022 Nature — similar whole-genome phylogenetic analysis in MPN;
confirmed early-life acquisition and measured clonal expansion rates, which are remarkably slow
(doubling times of years).
The implication: by the time a 60-year-old presents with PV , the JAK2V617F clone is often 30–50

years old. Early molecular intervention is a real possibility .
[FIGURE: MPN HSC biology — Mullally competitive disadvantage at low doses; disease-initiating
cell rarity; JAK2V617F HSC cell cycle; IFN selectivity schematic]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
MPN therapy is a layered problem: reduce thrombotic risk, control symptoms, prevent or delay
progression, and — for a growing minority — attempt molecular remission. No single agent accomplishes all four.
Risk-adapted framework. PV and ET are risk-stratified by age (>60) and prior thrombotic event;
high-risk patients receive cytoreduction in addition to aspirin ± phlebotomy (PV only). PMF is riskstratified by IPSS/DIPSS-plus or MIPSS70(+), and high-risk patients are considered for allogeneic
stem cell transplant.
Aspirin and phlebotomy. Low-dose aspirin reduces thrombotic events in PV and ET (ECLAP
trial for PV). Phlebotomy targets hematocrit <45% in PV (Marchioli CYTO-PV trial) and is the
cornerstone of PV management regardless of molecular therapy .
Hydroxyurea. An antimetabolite (ribonucleotide reductase inhibitor) that is the first-line cytoreductive for high-risk PV and ET. It reduces thrombotic risk and controls counts but produces variable molecular response (rarely deep), has long-term myelosuppression and skin-malignancy concerns, and is resistance-prone. Hydroxyurea resistance or intolerance (HU-R/I, per ELN criteria)
is the trigger for second-line cytoreduction.
JAK inhibitors.
Drug Target Indication Key trial
Diseasemodifying?
Ruxolitinib JAK1/JAK2 Intermediate/highrisk MF; HU-R/I
PV
COMFORT-I,
COMFORT-II
(Harrison 2016
Leukemia —
long-term
COMFORT-II)
Spleen +
symptom benefit;
OS signal; no
clonal
eradication
Fedratinib JAK2 (FLT3) MF (2nd line) JAKARTA,
JAKARTA-2
No
Pacritinib JAK2 (IRAK1,
FLT3)
MF with thrombocytopenia
PERSIST-2 No
Momelotinib JAK1/JAK2
(ACVR1)
MF with anemia MOMENTUM,
SIMPLIFY-1/2
No — but helps
anemia via
hepcidin
suppression
Ruxolitinib is the prototype JAK inhibitor and produces rapid spleen reduction and symptom improvement, driven largely by shutting off pathologic cytokine production rather than by eliminating the clone. COMFORT-I and II showed overall survival benefit in MF (largely from reduc82

tion in cachexia/inflammation mortality). However, ruxolitinib does not eliminate the JAK2V617F
clone, does not prevent transformation to blast phase, and produces a “persistence” state in which
MPN stem cells rewire to survive JAK inhibition (Ross 2021, Haematologica, PMID 33472356; earlier Koppikar/Levine 2012 Nature showed heterodimeric rewiring). This is a canonical limitation
of targeted therapy in MPN and is the conceptual gap that IFN-α and its amplifiers (like DCAF7
inhibition) aim to fill.
Interferons — the disease-modifying option.
Agent Format Approval / use
IFN-α2a / α2b Daily SC Historical; off-label for PV/ET
PEG-IFN-α2a (Pegasys) Weekly SC Off-label for MPN; multiple
trials
Ropeginterferon-α2b
(Besremi)
Every 2 weeks,
monopegylated
FDA-approved 2021 for PV
Seminal and recent trials: - Kiladjian 2008 Blood (PMID 18250227) — PEG-IFN-α2a produced
complete hematologic responses in ~80% of PV patients and molecular responses (JAK2V617F
reductions) in ~50%, establishing for the first time that IFN-α can selectively reduce the mutant
clone. - Quintás-Cardama 2009 JCO — PEG-IFN-α2a in PV and ET; durable molecular responses.
- PROUD-PV and CONTINUATION-PV (Gisslinger 2020) — ropeginterferon vs hydroxyurea in
PV; ropeg superior at 3 years for molecular and hematologic response, leading to 2021 FDA approval. - Mascarenhas 2022 Blood (PMID 35007321) — MPN-RC 112 — PEG-IFN-α2a vs hydroxyurea in high-risk PV and ET; similar hematologic response rates but deeper molecular responses
with PEG-IFN. The landmark randomized comparison.
The unique property of IFN-α therapy: it can produce molecular responses including complete
molecular remission (CMR, undetectable JAK2V617F) in a subset of PV and ET patients. The effect is preferentially on the mutant HSC compartment — explained by the cell-cycle selectivity
described in Section G6. The mechanism at the level of IFIT3 and DCAF7 is the subject of the
thesis and is detailed in Section F.
Limitations of IFN-α are substantial: approximately 50% of patients discontinue for tolerability
(flu-like symptoms, depression, hepatitis, autoimmunity), 30–40% do not achieve deep molecular
response, and among responders, a fraction relapse on treatment due to acquired ISG tolerance
or outgrowth of IFN-resistant subclones. These limitations are exactly the opening for a DCAF7targeted combination strategy .
Anagrelide. A PDE3 inhibitor that selectively reduces platelet production; second-line for ET. No
effect on JAK2 burden.
Novel and combination agents. - Imetelstat — telomerase inhibitor; promising in MF in
IMbark/IMpactMF trials; unique mechanism targeting MPN progenitor telomerase. - BET
inhibitors (pelabresib) — MANIFEST-2 trial combining pelabresib with ruxolitinib showed
improved spleen response but recent concerns about death signals. - BCL-XL inhibitors (navitoclax) — TRANSFORM-1/2 trials in combination with ruxolitinib; addresses apoptotic resistance
in MPN stem cells. - MDM2 antagonists (idasanutlin, navtemadlin/KRT-232) — reactivate
p53 in TP53-wild-type MPN cells. - Epigenetic agents — azacitidine (accelerated/blast phase),

decitabine, targeting the epigenetic cooperators. - Hepcidin modulators, luspatercept — for
anemia.
Allogeneic HCT. The only curative modality , but with 20–30% transplant-related mortality , reserved for highand intermediate-2 risk PMF and for blast phase.
[FIGURE: MPN therapy landscape — risk stratification ￿ HU, ruxolitinib, IFN-α, novel combinations, transplant]

sections.title: Unmet Needs and the Therapeutic Rationale for DCAF7

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The clinical gap is specific and quantifiable. JAK inhibitors palliate but do not cure. IFN-α induces molecular remissions but fails in 30–40% of patients for lack of depth of response, and drops
out another 50% for tolerability . The net fraction of MPN patients who achieve durable complete
molecular remission on current therapy is low — perhaps 20–25% of PV , lower in ET, negligible
in overt MF.
The rationale for DCAF7 inhibition + IFN-α. 1. IFN-α is the only drug class that selectively reduces the mutant HSC clone, and its mechanism of action depends critically on ISG protein output
(Section F). 2. DCAF7 is a CRL4 substrate receptor that targets IFIT3 for proteasomal degradation
(the thesis mechanism). In the unperturbed state, IFIT3 is continuously produced by ISGF3 transcription and continuously cleared by CRL4^DCAF7, setting a steady-state IFIT3 level. 3. DCAF7
knockdown or pharmacologic inhibition stabilizes IFIT3, amplifying ISG signaling and pushing
MPN cells past the apoptotic/senescence threshold in IFN-α. 4. Wild-type HSCs, being less IFNprimed and less cycling, have a larger buffer and are relatively spared — the therapeutic window.
5. This is a combination, not a monotherapy: DCAF7 inhibition is intended to convert IFN-α nonresponders into responders and to deepen the response of partial responders.
Competing combination strategies in development. - JAK + IFN (RUXOPEG trial, Kiladjian/Mascarenhas) — rational but additive toxicities. - JAK + BET (pelabresib + ruxolitinib) —
epigenetic reprogramming. - JAK + BCL-XL (navitoclax + ruxolitinib, TRANSFORM trials) —
apoptotic sensitization. - JAK + MDM2 (navtemadlin) — p53 reactivation.
DCAF7 + IFN-α differs from all of these by engaging the IFN pathway directly and preserving the
molecular-response capability of IFN. The thesis makes a mechanism-first argument rather than
an empirical one.
Patient selection. First-in-human rationale would target high-risk PV or ET patients who have
failed or are losing response to PEG-IFN or ropeginterferon — an enriched population where the
mechanism of failure (insufficient ISG output) maps to the proposed mechanism of action (ISG
amplification). Baseline ISG score on whole blood and baseline IFIT3 expression could serve as
pharmacodynamic biomarkers. PMF would be a later-line indication given poorer IFN tolerability
and baseline cytokine storm.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A thesis spanning in vitro biochemistry to clinical implications requires model systems that capture
different layers of MPN biology . The student’s data integrate several; this section canvases them

and calls out pitfalls.
Mouse models.
Model Allele / system Strengths Caveats

(tamoxifen inducible)
