The Poly(I:C) confound is critical for this thesis. Mx1-Cre is induced by Poly(I:C) (polyinosinicpolycytidylic acid), a dsRNA analog that activates TLR3 and MDA5/RIG-I, producing a strong
type I IFN pulse. This means any Jak2V617F Mx1-Cre experiment that compares “induced” to
“uninduced” is comparing a condition with elevated IFN to one without. Any downstream IFN
signature, ISG measurement, or IFIT3 readout is contaminated. This is the direct motivation for
the student’s switch to Rosa26-CreER (tamoxifen inducible) — tamoxifen does not activate innate
immune IFN. The student should be able to articulate this clearly: “Our Mx1-Cre data gave us the
initial phenotype, but to make clean claims about the IFN axis we transitioned to Rosa26-CreER;
the remaining Mx1-Cre data are reported with Poly(I:C) control arms to isolate the driver-specific
from IFN-activation effects.”
Primary patient samples. CD34+ HSPCs from peripheral blood or bone marrow aspirates,
obtained via the Silver Family Horizon biorepository (Northwestern). These samples preserve
the patient clone and the co-occurring mutational landscape, and are the gold standard for
cell-autonomous IFN response studies ex vivo. Limitations: heterogeneity , limited expansion
potential without losing primitive function, and difficulty of genetic manipulation.
Cell lines.

Line Origin Key features
HEL Erythroleukemia, JAK2V617F
homozygous
Most common MPN model;
caveats below
HEL 92.1.7 HEL subclone Similar
SET-2 ET transformation to
leukemia; JAK2V617F
heterozygous
Closer to ET biology
UKE-1 Post-ET AML JAK2V617F
MB02 Megakaryoblastic JAK2V617F
HEL’s main caveat: it is an erythroleukemia cell line (from a patient with a post-MPN erythroleukemia), not an MPN per se, with additional cooperating mutations (including TP53 loss)
that complicate interpretation. It is nonetheless the workhorse because it is easy to grow, is
JAK2V617F-positive, and has been extensively characterized in every JAK2-related pathway .
Claims from HEL must be verified in a second model (SET-2 or primary CD34+).
iPSC-derived HSPCs. Patient-derived iPSCs (from JAK2V617F, CALR, or MPL patients) and
CRISPR-engineered iPSCs (knock-in of JAK2V617F into a defined genetic background) allow
controlled study of driver mutations. Limitations: iPSC hematopoiesis is fetal-like, not fully adult
HSC-equivalent, and in vitro differentiation skews toward erythroid and limits megakaryocytic
output.
iPSC-derived bone marrow organoids. Khan 2023 Nature Methods and follow-up work established iPSC-derived 3D bone marrow organoids that recapitulate the niche with stromal, vascular,
and hematopoietic components. Recent MPN organoid work shows promise for modeling nichemediated drug response and fibrosis initiation.
Xenografts. MPN engrafts poorly in NSG (NOD-scid-Il2rg-null). NSGS (NSG + human SCF,
GM-CSF, IL-3) and NSG-SGM3 (with human cytokines) improve engraftment modestly . Humanized mice with human HSC-derived niche components are the current frontier for patient-derived
xenograft studies of MPN.
[FIGURE: model systems for MPN — mouse KI alleles, iPSC/organoid, cell lines, PDX; coverage
of driver phenotypes]

sections.title: Pediatric and Congenital Myeloid Disorders (T angentially Relevant)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Pediatric myeloid disease is adjacent to the thesis: a DCAF7 role in hematopoiesis should be considered across developmental windows, and the Vav-Cre (perinatal) phenotype in the student’s
mouse work raises the question of whether DCAF7 is relevant in pediatric myeloid disease.
JMML (juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia). A rare pediatric MPN/MDS overlap driven by RASpathway hyperactivation — somatic or germline PTPN11 (Noonan syndrome), NF1 (neurofibromatosis type 1), KRAS, NRAS, or CBL. JMML is phenotypically an MPN of infancy with monocytosis, hepatosplenomegaly , and a poor outcome without allogeneic transplant. Sakaguchi 2013
(Nature Genetics) performed the definitive exome study of JMML and identified the RAS-pathway
landscape.

Important — a known grant error. An earlier grant draft cited “Yoshida 2013” for the
JMML exome dataset — this is a mis-citation; the correct reference is Sakaguchi 2013.
DCAF7 is not in the Sakaguchi 2013 JMML gene list, and the student has been advised
to remove the citation. A committee member may test recall of this: the correct answer
is (1) acknowledge the mis-citation, (2) name Sakaguchi 2013 as the correct reference, (3)
note that DCAF7 does not appear in the JMML landscape, and (4) reframe the relevance
— if DCAF7 matters in pediatric myeloid biology , it is not via a JMML driver role but
possibly via the developmental phenotype seen in the Vav-Cre mouse.
DS-AMKL (Down syndrome acute megakaryoblastic leukemia). Trisomy 21 + somatic GATA1
N-terminal truncation (GATA1s) produces transient abnormal myelopoiesis (TAM) in 10% of
neonates with Down syndrome, which resolves in most but progresses to DS-AMKL in 20–30% of
TAM patients, with additional mutations (cohesin complex, JAK family , EZH2). The relevance to
DCAF7 is indirect — if DCAF7 regulates megakaryopoiesis (as hinted by the Vav-Cre phenotype),
its function may intersect with the GATA1s-driven megakaryocytic block.
TAM (transient abnormal myelopoiesis). The neonatal pre-leukemic clone in Down syndrome
driven by GATA1s. Resolves spontaneously in most cases; important as a paradigm of fetal-specific
leukemic biology .
Relevance framing. If asked “Does DCAF7 matter in pediatric myeloid disease?” — the honest answer is: the data are indirect. DCAF7 does not appear as a recurrent somatic mutation in pediatric
myeloid leukemia datasets. The Vav-Cre perinatal phenotype in the student’s mouse suggests a
developmental requirement for DCAF7 in definitive hematopoiesis, which is more a “normal biology” story than a “disease driver” story . The disease-relevant thesis focus is adult MPN + IFN-α.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The arc of Section G sets up the thesis. MPN is a disease in which a small number of driver mutations converge on a single pathway (JAK-STAT) in a specific cell compartment (the HSC) with a
distinctive cell-cycle and inflammatory context that creates a selective vulnerability to type I interferon. IFN-α exploits that vulnerability and is the only agent that reliably reduces the mutant HSC
clone. But IFN-α fails a major fraction of patients, and the failure is mechanistically tractable — it
reflects insufficient ISG protein output or acquired ISG tolerance. The thesis proposes that DCAF7
is a CRL4 substrate receptor that rate-limits IFIT3 in the ISG output pathway , that DCAF7 inhibition stabilizes IFIT3 and amplifies the anti-MPN activity of IFN-α, and that the combination has
the potential to convert IFN non-responders into responders while preserving wild-type HSCs.
The committee should find the argument coherent if the candidate can, on demand, move fluidly
from the clinical patient (a PV patient with JAK2V617F allele burden 70% failing PEG-IFN after
12 months) down to the molecular substrate (IFIT3 ubiquitination by CRL4^DCAF7), and back up
to the trial design (ropeginterferon non-responder Phase 1 with biomarker-driven selection). That
movement is the single most important skill to demonstrate; everything else in Section G is there
to support it.

Key Citations for Section G
• Arber DA, et al. Blood 2016 (PMID 27069254) — WHO 2016 classification
• Tefferi A, Thiele J, Vardiman JW. Cancer 2009 (PMID 19806641) — pre-WHO classification consolidation
• Campbell PJ, Green AR. NEJM 2006 — clinical review, MPN overview
• Vainchenker W, Kralovics R. Blood 2017 — comprehensive genetics review
• James C, et al. Nature 2005; Kralovics R, et al. NEJM 2005; Baxter EJ, et al. Lancet 2005; Levine
RL, et al. Cancer Cell 2005 — JAK2V617F discovery
• Nielsen C, et al. Haematologica 2011 — population prevalence of JAK2V617F
• Klampfl T, et al. NEJM 2013; Nangalia J, et al. NEJM 2013 — CALR mutation discovery
• Pikman Y , et al. PLoS Med 2006 — MPL W515L retroviral model
• Ortmann CA, et al. NEJM 2015 — JAK2 vs TET2 mutation order
• Grinfeld J, et al. NEJM 2018 (PMID 30304655) — genomic MPN classification
• Mullally A, et al. Cancer Cell 2010 — Jak2V617F knock-in HSC biology
• Lundberg P , et al. 2014 — MPN disease-initiating cells
• Prick J, et al. 2014 — MPN HSC quiescence
• Psaila B, et al. 2020; Van Egeren D, et al. Cell Stem Cell 2021; Williams N, et al. Nature 2022 —
single-cell MPN clonal dynamics
• Arranz L, et al. Nature 2014 — sympathetic neuropathy and niche damage
• Fourouclas N, et al. 2008 — SOCS3 silencing in MPN
• Harrison CN, et al. Leukemia 2016 — COMFORT-II long-term
• Ross DM, et al. Haematologica 2021 (PMID 33472356) — ruxolitinib persistence in MF
• Kiladjian JJ, et al. Blood 2008 (PMID 18250227) — PEG-IFN-α2a in PV
• Quintás-Cardama A, et al. JCO 2009 — PEG-IFN in PV and ET
• Mascarenhas J, et al. Blood 2022 (PMID 35007321) — MPN-RC 112, PEG-IFN vs HU
• Gisslinger H, et al. (PROUD-PV / CONTINUATION-PV) — ropeginterferon vs HU
• Sakaguchi H, et al. 2013 — JMML exome (correct citation; not “Yoshida 2013”)
• Akada H, Li J, Marty C — alternative Jak2V617F knock-in mouse models
• Marty C, et al. 2016; Li J, et al. 2018 — CALR mouse models
End of Section G. Proceed to Section H (if applicable) for the thesis-specific experimental plan and data
package integration.
Integrative Synthesis
This final section of Tier 1 stitches the preceding sections into a single defensible thesis narrative.
Use it as the spine of any 5–10 minute committee preamble or as the “elevator pitch” for the qualifying exam. Each subsection below frames a question your committee will almost certainly ask,
then walks through the argument you should be able to deliver from memory .

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS) regulates virtually every cellular state transition by controlling the abundance of regulatory proteins on minute-to-hour timescales. Within the UPS, the
CRL4 family of cullin-RING ligases uses ~90 substrate-receptor proteins called DCAFs (DDB1and
CUL4-associated factors) to direct ubiquitination toward specific substrates, providing a modular,
combinatorial mechanism for context-specific protein degradation. DCAF7 (also called WDR68
or HAN11) is one such substrate receptor. Outside hematopoiesis it scaffolds DYRK/HIPK kinases and degrades a small set of nuclear substrates (MEN1, DNA Ligase I, influenza PA). Within
hematopoiesis, our preliminary data show that DCAF7 is selectively required by JAK2V617F MPN
cells, where it degrades the interferon effector IFIT3 and thereby restrains the antiproliferative arm
of type I interferon signaling. Loss of DCAF7 stabilizes IFIT3, amplifies ISG output, and hypersensitizes MPN cells to IFN-α. In a separate developmental axis, DCAF7 is dispensable in the fetal
liver but becomes essential at postnatal day 4 , coinciding with both a sharp rise in DCAF7 expression and the resolution of the perinatal type I IFN pulse described by Li et al. (2020). The unifying
hypothesis is that DCAF7 is a hematopoietic-selective brake on IFN-driven cell-state transitions
— a brake whose loss collapses leukemic clones in adulthood and disrupts the fetal-to-adult HSC
handoff in the perinatal window.

sections.title: Why this matters: the unmet need

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
MPN treatment has two axes. JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib, fedratinib, momelotinib) provide symptomatic and splenic benefit but do not eradicate the clone or prevent progression to myelofibrosis or blast-phase AML. PEG-IFN-α2a and ropeginterferon-α2b are the only agents that can drive
molecular remission by reducing JAK2V617F allele burden, but ~30–50% of patients fail to achieve
deep responses and many discontinue for tolerability . The reasons for non-response are poorly
understood and likely include intrinsic differences in ISG induction, baseline negative regulators (SOCS1/3, USP18), and co-occurring mutations (TET2, DNMT3A, ASXL1) that blunt the ISG
transcriptional response. A combination therapy that selectively amplifies ISG output in MPN
clones — without further sensitizing normal HSCs — would convert IFN non-responders into
responders. DCAF7 inhibition is precisely such a strategy: Gao et al. (2015) showed that DCAF7
is the dominant DDB1 partner in HL60 cells, and our preliminary genetic and pharmacologic data
(BC18630, IC50 ~1.80 μM) show selective dependency in JAK2V617F cells with synergy against
IFN-α. The Vav-Cre developmental phenotype is the principal caveat — but it is also a clue that
DCAF7 has temporally restricted essentiality , suggesting that adult inhibition may be tolerable.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The thesis rests on three logical bridges that the committee will probe in sequence:
Bridge 1 — Why is IFIT3 the right substrate? IFIT3 is an interferon-stimulated gene that does not
have intrinsic catalytic activity but stabilizes IFIT1 within the IFIT1-IFIT2-IFIT3 heterotrimer (Fleith

2018) and amplifies upstream signaling by binding MA VS and enhancing RIG-I-mediated IFN-β
production (Chikhalya 2021). Stabilizing IFIT3 therefore amplifies the entire ISG cascade rather
than simply elevating one antiviral effector. The published precedent for ubiquitin-mediated IFIT3
turnover is UBE2O at K236 in HCC (Li 2023 Cell Death Dis), but no CRL4-DCAF receptor has previously been linked to IFIT3 degradation in hematopoietic cells. Our SILAC mass spectrometry
in DCAF7-KO HEL cells identified IFIT3 as the top stabilized protein; cycloheximide chase confirmed extended half-life; and RNA-seq ruled out transcriptional change. The substrate identification therefore satisfies the standard burden of proof for a CRL4-substrate pair.
Bridge 2 — Why is the dependency MPN-selective? JAK2V617F constitutively activates the
STAT5/3 proliferative arm of JAK-STAT signaling, but the STAT1/2 antiproliferative arm requires
acute IFN engagement. MPN clones therefore live in a chronic STAT5-on/STAT1-low state, and
they are “addicted” to keeping STAT1 output suppressed. DCAF7-mediated IFIT3 degradation
is one component of this suppression. Removing DCAF7 stabilizes IFIT3, rebalances signaling
toward STAT1/ISG output, and creates a synthetic vulnerability that is absent in normal HSCs
(where baseline IFN tone is low and DCAF7 has little substrate to act on). This is consistent with
our data showing no overt steady-state phenotype in DCAF7-KO healthy mice but profound suppression of myeloid expansion in the JAK2V617F background.
Bridge 3 — Why does DCAF7 matter at P4 if it doesn’t matter at E14.5? The fetal-to-adult HSC
transition is gated by stage-specific transcriptional and post-transcriptional programs (LIN28B/let7, SOX17, EZH2, HMGA2). Li et al. (2020 Cell Stem Cell) showed that a perinatal type I IFN pulse,
originating from fetal skin and peaking at E16–E18.5, expands hematopoietic progenitors and induces MHC-I — and that this pulse is permissive for FLT3-ITD-driven leukemogenesis. The pulse
resolves over the first postnatal week. DCAF7 expression rises sharply at P4 in public databases
(Blake et al. MGD), exactly when the IFN pulse is wrapping up. The temporal coincidence suggests that DCAF7 is part of the machinery that terminates the perinatal IFN program and clears
IFIT3 (and possibly other ISGs) to permit the establishment of the adult HSC state. Vav-Cre Dcaf7
KO pups develop normally to E14.5, then begin dying at P4–P7 with extramedullary expansion
of LSKs and myeloid skewing — a phenotype consistent with failure to terminate an IFN-driven,
myeloid-biased developmental program.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A strong defense names limitations before they are extracted under questioning. Three to volunteer:
1. Substrate specificity in vivo is unresolved. IFIT3 is the lead substrate from in vitro proteomics;
whether it is the only relevant substrate in vivo, or whether DCAF7 also degrades other developmentally regulated factors (HMGA2, SOX17, LIN28B-pathway proteins) at P4, will require
ubiquitin proteomics in primary perinatal HSPCs.
2. The V av-Cre developmental phenotype complicates the therapeutic narrative. If DCAF7 is
essential during the fetal-to-adult transition, then chronic systemic DCAF7 inhibition in adult
patients carries unknown risk for any residual stem-cell maintenance functions. Mitigation:
targeted, intermittent dosing; combination at lower doses with IFN-α; and adult-specific con90

ditional KO (the Rosa26-CreER experiments) to test whether adult DCAF7 loss in healthy mice
is tolerated.
3. BC18630 selectivity is not yet fully characterized. Lear et al. (2025 Sci Adv) named BC18630
and BC18813 as DCAF7 binders with in vivo activity , but selectivity profiling against other
DCAFs and CRL4 components remains incomplete. Mitigation: orthogonal validation with
the genetic Dcaf7 KO; proposed counter-screen against CRBN, DCAF15, VprBP .

sections.title: The 30-second answer to “what is your thesis about?”

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
“I’m investigating how a substrate receptor of the CRL4 ubiquitin ligase, called DCAF7,
controls interferon signaling in hematopoietic stem cells. In adult myeloproliferative
neoplasms, DCAF7 degrades the interferon effector IFIT3 to suppress the antiproliferative arm of IFN-α signaling, and we’ve shown that genetic or pharmacologic DCAF7
inhibition synergizes with IFN-α therapy . In development, DCAF7 becomes essential
at postnatal day 4, just as the perinatal interferon pulse resolves — suggesting a parallel
role in dismantling the fetal hematopoietic program. The unifying idea is that DCAF7
is a hematopoietic-selective brake on interferon-driven cell-state transitions, and that
releasing this brake has therapeutic value in MPN.”

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
1. (0:00–0:30) UPS + CRL4 architecture; ~90 DCAFs as combinatorial substrate recognition (Section A–B).
2. (0:30–1:00) DCAF7 known biology: scaffold for DYRK/HIPK; established substrates MEN1,
LIG1, influenza PA; Gao 2015 hematopoietic selectivity (Section C).
3. (1:00–1:30) Normal HSC biology; LSK/SLAM gating; quiescence; cytokine receptor JAK-STAT
(Section D).
4. (1:30–2:00) IFN signaling and ISG amplification through IFIT3 (Section F).
5. (2:00–2:45) MPN biology: JAK2V617F, STAT5 vs STAT1 imbalance, IFN-α as the only
molecular-remission therapy , non-responder problem (Section G).
6. (2:45–3:30) Aim 1 preliminary data: DCAF7-IFIT3 axis, BC18630 + IFN-α synergy .
7. (3:30–4:15) Fetal-to-adult transition, perinatal IFN pulse, P4 DCAF7 rise (Section E).
8. (4:15–4:45) Aim 2 preliminary data: Vav-Cre lethality , P4 LSK expansion, myeloid skewing.
9. (4:45–5:00) Unified model: DCAF7 as a hematopoietic IFN brake; therapeutic and developmental implications.

sections.title: Reading list ranked by load-bearing weight

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
If you have one weekend to re-read primary literature before the exam:

Tier 1 — must re-read: - Gao 2015 eLife — DCAF7 selectivity in hematopoiesis - Fleith 2018 NAR
— IFIT1-2-3 heterotrimer - Chikhalya 2021 mBio — IFIT3 amplifier function - Li 2020 Cell Stem Cell
— perinatal IFN pulse - Lear 2025 Sci Adv — BC18630 DCAF7 inhibitor - Platanias 2005 Nat Rev
Immunol — JAK-STAT canonical review
Tier 2 — re-skim: - Jackson & Xiong 2009 Trends Biochem Sci — CRL4 architecture - Glenewinkel
2016 Sci Rep — DCAF7-DYRK1A-HIPK2 scaffolding - Vainchenker & Kralovics 2017 Blood —
MPN JAK-STAT review - Kiladjian 2008 Blood — PEG-IFN PV - Mascarenhas 2022 Blood — MPNRC 112 - Essers 2009 Nature — IFN-α activates dormant HSCs - Serra 2026 Am J Hematol — SCA
IFN-driven myeloid skewing
Tier 3 — know the headline: - WHO 2016 / Arber Blood - Grinfeld 2018 NEJM genomic classification - Bowie 2007 PNAS — intrinsic timing of fetal-adult switch - Buckley 2012 Cell Stem Cell —
HSC ubiquitinome - Hershko & Ciechanover 1998 — UPS Nobel review

qanda.q: Walk me through the E1-E2-E3 cascade at the level of chemical bonds. How many ATPs are consumed, and what is the product?
qanda.a: E1 adenylates Ub-G76 with ATP , producing Ub-AMP + PPi; transfers Ub to E1 active-site Cys as a thioester (releasing AMP); adenylates a second Ub so the E1 is “doubly loaded.” Ub is transthiolated to the E2 active-site Cys. E3 either scaffolds (RING) so that the E2~Ub thioester is attacked by the substrate lysine ε-amine, or accepts Ub onto an E3 Cys first (HECT) and then transfers it to the substrate. Each conjugation step costs one ATP net (one ATP -> AMP + PPi, with PPi hydrolysis driving the reaction forward). The isopeptide bond between G76 carbonyl and substrate lysine ε-amine is the product.

qanda.q: Why is ubiquitin chain topology important?
qanda.a: How are different linkages read? • Each linkage has a distinct 3D shape that exposes or masks the I44 hydrophobic patch differently . UBDs (UBA, UIM, CUE, NZF, UBAN, Pru) are linkage-selective. K48 chains (compact) are read by Rpn10/Rpn13 for proteasomal targeting; K63 chains (extended) are read by NEMO-UBAN-adjacent domains, TAB2 NZF for signaling; M1 chains are bound by NEMO UBAN. Branched K48/K11 chains amplify proteasomal targeting. Linkage specificity of DUBs (OTUB1 K48, OTULIN M1, CYLD K63/M1) enforces the code.

qanda.q: Explain the mechanistic difference between RING and HECT E3s. Where do RBR E3s fit?
qanda.a: RING E3s are scaffolds that hold E2~Ub in a closed catalytic conformation; substrate Lys attacks the E2 thioester directly (one-step transfer). HECT E3s have a catalytic Cys; Ub is transferred from E2 to E3 and then E3 to substrate (two-step). RBR E3s use a RING1 to bind E2~Ub like a RING, but then relay Ub onto a Cys in RING2 like a HECT, so they are hybrids. Parkin, HOIP , ARIH1 are examples.

qanda.q: What is the minimum signal for proteasomal degradation?
qanda.a: Historically , K48-linked tetraubiquitin (Thrower 2000). Modern view: shorter chains, branched chains (K48/K11), and even some monoubiquitination can target to the proteasome given sufficient receptor avidity , especially when shuttle factors (UBQLN, Rad23) participate. The “tetraUb rule” is a useful but imperfect heuristic.

qanda.q: How does bortezomib kill myeloma cells but spare most normal cells?
qanda.a: Plasma cells (the cell of origin in MM) produce immense quantities of immunoglobulin and are chronically at the edge of proteotoxic catastrophe; UPS flux is saturated. Inhibiting β5 tips them into terminal UPR and IRE1/XBP1-CHOP-driven apoptosis. Most other cells have UPS reserve capacity . There is also a contribution from NF-κB inhibition (bortezomib stabilizes IκBα) and interference with bone marrow stromal support.

qanda.q: What is the immunoproteasome, and why might a committee chair who studies IFN signaling care?
qanda.a: IFN-γ induces β1i, β2i, β5i (PSMB9, PSMB10, PSMB8) which replace β1, β2, β5 to form the immunoproteasome. The cleavage specificity shift (more hydrophobic and basic C-termini) enriches for peptides that fit MHC class I grooves. Therefore, IFN signaling directly remodels the UPS to produce a proteasome optimized for antigen presentation. This is a direct conceptual bridge between UPS and IFN.

qanda.q: What did Buckley 2012 tell us about UPS in stem cells?
qanda.a: Large-scale K-GG proteomics in ESCs; 2578 sites on 1168 proteins; FBXW7-cMyc T58 phospho-degron identified as a pluripotency-modulating axis; PSMD14 (Rpn11) essential for pluripotency and iPS reprogramming. Established the UPS as an active sculptor of pluripotency , not just a maintenance system.

qanda.q: How does FBW7 enforce HSC quiescence?
qanda.a: SCF^FBXW7 degrades c-Myc via the T58 phospho-degron (primed by GSK3β after S62 priming). In HSCs (Thompson 2008 JEM), Fbw7 loss stabilizes c-Myc, drives HSCs out of G0, and causes eventual HSC exhaustion. Fbw7 also degrades Notch1 ICD, cyclin E, MCL1, so the net phenotype integrates multiple targets. FBXW7 is recurrently mutated in T-ALL.

qanda.q: How do CRLs achieve substrate specificity?
qanda.a: Substrate specificity is delegated to the substrate receptor (F-box / BTB / DCAF / SOCS) rather than built into the cullin or RING. Receptors bind short linear degrons on substrates, typically only after a post-translational mark (phosphorylation, hydroxylation, acetylation, chemical damage). This modular architecture lets ~7 cullins serve ~350 specific receptors, with CAND1-mediated exchange dynamically reconfiguring the active complement.

qanda.q: What is the difference between CUL4A and CUL4B?
qanda.a: Both use DDB1 adaptor and the same DCAF pool. CUL4B has a ~150 aa N-terminal extension with an NLS, making it predominantly nuclear; CUL4A partitions more broadly . CUL4B mutations cause X-linked intellectual disability; CUL4B knockout is embryonic lethal (placental). CUL4A knockout is viable. Substrate overlap is substantial but not complete; each has paralog-specific contexts.

qanda.q: What is DDB1, and how does it bind substrate receptors?
qanda.a: DDB1 is a three-β-propeller adaptor (BPA, BPB, BPC) plus CTD. BPC docks onto CUL4; BPA and BPB form a clamp-like cleft that binds DCAFs. BPB is rotationally flexible relative to BPA/BPC, which accommodates different DCAF shapes. DCAFs dock into the BPA/BPB cleft via WDxR motifs (most), H-box, LisH, or unique interfaces (e.g., CRBN N-terminus).

qanda.q: How does MLN4924/pevonedistat work mechanistically?
qanda.a: It is an adenosine sulfamate that is loaded by NAE (UBA3) onto NEDD8, forming a stable NEDD8-MLN4924 covalent adduct that cannot proceed to the E2. This “suicide substrate” mechanism traps NAE and globally depletes cullin neddylation. All CRLs lose activity , so CRL substrates (CDT1, p27, HIF-1α, NRF2) accumulate, causing rereplication, DNA damage, and apoptosis. Clinical development in AML/MDS.

qanda.q: Why are DCAFs considered an opportunity for therapeutic targeting?
qanda.a: DCAFs are the substrate-selective modules of CRL4, and lessons from CRBN show that small molecules can reshape a DCAF’s binding surface to recruit neosubstrates (molecular glues: IMiDs, CC-885, indisulam). PROTACs extend this by tethering a DCAF ligand to an arbitrary target ligand. Therefore, any DCAF, in principle, could be converted into a tunable degrader by medicinal chemistry . Lineage-restricted DCAFs (like DCAF7 in hematopoiesis) offer tissue-selectivity of effect.

qanda.q: What is the CAND1 cycle, and why does it matter?
qanda.a: CAND1 is a HEAT-repeat exchange factor that binds deneddylated cullins after CSN removes NEDD8, displacing the current substrate receptor. This lets the cullin sample a fresh SR from the cytosolic pool. Binding by an SR whose cognate substrate is abundant outcompetes CAND1, releasing it and allowing re-neddylation. The cycle is substrate-driven adaptive remodeling; it explains how a small pool of cullins serves a large SR repertoire and how CRL activity follows substrate abundance.

qanda.q: What does neddylation do to a CRL structurally?
qanda.a: NEDD8 conjugation on the cullin C-terminal lysine wedges against the winged-helix B (WHB) subdomain, displacing it and releasing the RING-box subunit from a closed state into a flexible tether. The E2~Ub can now reach substrate lysines. Neddylation also excludes CAND1 binding (mutually exclusive). Net effect: ~4-10x higher ubiquitin transfer rate and a locked “active” conformation. Cryo-EM studies (Baek 2020, 2021) visualize the flexibility .

qanda.q: Summarize the Gao 2015 evidence that DCAF7 is the hematopoietic-selective DCAF .
qanda.a: AP-MS of DDB1 in HL60 (hematopoietic) revealed DCAF7 as the sole DCAF detected, vs. DCAF4/8/11/15/VprBP in ESCs and 293T. Ddb1 conditional deletion (Vav-Cre: lethal P7; Mx1-Cre: lethal <3 weeks) causes HSPC collapse. p21 is a CRL4 substrate (coIP , MG132 epistasis), but p21 knockout does not rescue and Trp53 knockout only partially rescues (ST-HSCs restored, not LT-HSCs/MPPs). Therefore CRL4 acts on a broader program, and DCAF7 is the prime candidate receptor mediating this program in hematopoietic cells.

qanda.q: Bonus: why should an IFN-signaling chair care about CRLs?
qanda.a: Two direct links. First, CUL5-SOCS complexes degrade activated JAKs (e.g., SOCS1 on JAK2) and cytokine receptors, so CRL5 is a core brake on cytokine/IFN signaling; loss of SOCS1 causes constitutive IFN signaling and autoimmunity . Second, Mx1-Cre is itself an IFN-responsive reporter, so every Mx1-Cre experiment in the Ddb1/Dcaf7 literature is executed in an IFN-activated context; type-I IFN surges during pIpC-induced Cre activation and sensitizes HSCs. Third, more broadly , IFN-γ induces the immunoproteasome, directly remodeling the downstream UPS. These are the sort of connections a chair will test. End of Sections A and B. Sections C (DCAF7 structure, interactions, and function) and onward follow in the companion documents.

qanda.q: Where is DCAF7 located, and does the locus itself have disease relevance?
qanda.a: 17q23.2 in humans, chr 11 in mouse. The locus is not a known driver site, but 17q is recurrently altered in myeloid malignancy .

qanda.q: What are all the aliases and why do they exist?
qanda.a: DCAF7 / WDR68 / HAN11 / AN11 / Crn. Different subfields (CRL, kinase, plant, Drosophila) named it independently . HAN11 is a misnomer — it is WD40, not ankyrin.

qanda.q: How conserved is DCAF7?
qanda.a: 100% identity across mammals; ~45% to petunia. No paralog. Arguments for a non-redundant function.

qanda.q: What is the predicted fold?
qanda.a: Seven-bladed β-propeller, based on sequence WD40 count and AlphaFold.

qanda.q: Why would you believe DCAF7 has a particular hematopoietic role?
qanda.a: Three independent lines: tissue-biased expression, DepMap blood-line dependency , and the Gao 2015 mass-spec finding that DCAF7 is the dominant DDB1-bound DCAF in HL60.

qanda.q: What is the significance of the P4 expression surge?
qanda.a: It coincides with the late phase of the perinatal IFN pulse (Li 2020), setting up a timing model in which DCAF7 terminates that pulse.

qanda.q: What is the WDXR motif, and does DCAF7 have one?
qanda.a: A conserved Trp-Asp-X-Arg motif used by a class of DCAFs to bind DDB1 (Jin 2006). DCAF7 lacks it and must use an alternate mode.

qanda.q: What are the three surfaces of a WD40 propeller?
qanda.a: Top face (D-A loops), bottom face (B-C loops), circumferential edge.

qanda.q: What is known structurally about DCAF7?
qanda.a: Seven WD40 repeats, AlphaFold model, mutational mapping of a BC18630-binding patch; no experimental structure.

qanda.q: What does the BC18630 mutagenesis tell you about the protein?
qanda.a: That a surface patch distinct from the classical WDXR and substrate-groove positions is functionally engaged in DDB1 recruitment and substrate binding — consistent with a non-canonical DCAF7 architecture.

qanda.q: Why hasn’t DCAF7 been crystallized?
qanda.a: No published structure yet; likely due to the small size of the monomer and difficulty of trapping substrate complexes; opportunity remains.

qanda.q: What post-translational modifications are cataloged?
qanda.a: Multiple N-terminal phospho-sites; ubiquitination on several lysines — functional roles largely unstudied.

qanda.q: What was DCAF7’s first characterized function?
qanda.a: Kinase scaffolding — initially for DYRK1A (Miyata & Nishida 2011) and for MEKK1/HIPK2 (Ritterhoff 2010). The CRL4 substratereceptor role came later.

qanda.q: Name four bona fide DCAF7 CRL4 substrates with primary citations. MEN1 (Xu 2023, PanNET), LIG1 (Peng 2016, replication), influenza PA (Yu 2025, antiviral), TFEB (Lear 2025, coronavirus/autophagy).
Are DCAF7’s scaffolding and E3-adapter functions independent?
qanda.a: Unresolved. Three models — independent, degradation-coupled, or context-switched — each have supporting evidence. Likely context-dependent.

qanda.q: Why is BC18630 an important development?
qanda.a: It is one of very few DCAF tool compounds; targets a non-canonical surface; shows efficacy in MPN cells; and synergizes with IFN-α. It makes DCAF7 chemically actionable.

qanda.q: What is the typical ubiquitin chain topology in DCAF7-mediated degradation?
qanda.a: K48 polyubiquitination leading to 26S proteasome targeting, consistent with most CRL4 substratedegradation literature.

qanda.q: Does DCAF7 work with CUL4A or CUL4B?
qanda.a: Both. The PanNET paper specifies CUL4B; the antiviral paper specifies CRL4B; other contexts (LIG1) are more ambiguous. Cullin selectivity in vivo is not fully resolved.

qanda.q: What is the common pitfall in citing the Yu 2025 paper?
qanda.a: It is frequently miscited for “kinase scaffolding.” It is actually about substrate-receptor antiviral activity against influenza PA.

qanda.q: Why should the PanNET paper worry someone designing DCAF7-based therapy?
qanda.a: It shows DCAF7 can be oncogenic via tumor-suppressor degradation. Systemic DCAF7 inhibition could de-repress MEN1 loss in other contexts and has its own risk profile. Hematopoieticselective dependency is the mitigating argument.

qanda.q: What did Gao 2015 show about DDB1–DCAF binding?
qanda.a: That in HL60 cells DCAF7 was the only DCAF detected bound to DDB1, whereas in 293T and ESCs multiple other DCAFs (DCAF4/8/11/15, VprBP) bound DDB1 with no DCAF7. Hematopoietic-selective partner.

qanda.q: What is the evidence that IFIT3 is a DCAF7 substrate in MPN?
qanda.a: Top accumulated protein in TMT proteomics in DCAF7-KO HEL; extended half-life by cycloheximide chase; unchanged at mRNA. Consistent with post-translational DCAF7-dependent degradation.

qanda.q: Why does DCAF7 loss impair JAK2V617F cells but not WT?
qanda.a: The mutant clone depends on dampened IFN responsiveness (selective advantage under chronic autocrine/paracrine IFN); WT cells can tolerate IFIT3 stabilization at steady state. DCAF7 upregulation in MPN CD34+ may reflect compensatory induction to maintain the dampening.

qanda.q: Why does Mx1-Cre deletion spare HSCs?
qanda.a: Because the MP/MEP compartment rather than the HSC/MPP is the selectively dependent population — therapeutic window.

qanda.q: What is the V av-Cre KO phenotype and its proposed mechanism?
qanda.a: Normal E14.5 hematopoiesis; postnatal lethality (~30–40%) in the first week; P4 LSK expansion (extramedullary) and myeloid bias. Proposed mechanism: failure to terminate the perinatal IFN pulse at the P4 DCAF7 surge.

qanda.q: What is the DS-AMKL evidence, and what is the citation caveat?
qanda.a: Recurrent L340F mutations in 5–10% of DS-AMKL. Primary citation should be Labuhn 2019 Cancer Cell; the proposal-cited “Yoshida 2013” should be Sakaguchi et al. 2013 Nat Genet and does not foreground DCAF7 — verify in primary source before citing.

qanda.q: What does the BC18630 + IFN-α synergy mean mechanistically?
qanda.a: That inhibiting DCAF7 and activating IFN signaling pharmacologically act in the same axis in a non-redundant way — consistent with DCAF7 acting as a brake on IFN output whose inhibition amplifies IFN-α effects. Loewe ~7.15 supports genuine synergy , not additivity .

qanda.q: What controls are needed to say “IFIT3 is a DCAF7 substrate” at the committee’s standard?
qanda.a: Direct DDB1–DCAF7–IFIT3 in vitro ubiquitination; mutant DCAF7 (non-substrate binding) rescue that fails to reduce IFIT3; IFIT3 accumulation blocked by re-expression of WT DCAF7; MLN4924 (neddylation inhibitor) should phenocopy DCAF7 loss on IFIT3. Current proposal has some but not all of these; address in Aim 1/2.

qanda.q: Design the experiment that enumerates the DCAF7 substrate set in MPN cells. MLN4924 +
qanda.a: DCAF7-WT/KO comparison, K-GG proteomics, plus BioID/TurboID for transient substrates. Cross-reference with TMT steady-state proteomics for convergent hits.

qanda.q: What evidence would distinguish elevated DCAF7 activity from elevated substrate load in the mutant?
qanda.a: Quantitative DDB1–DCAF7 complex MS, neddylation state (Nedd8 IP), IFIT3 flux by pulse-SILAC in matched WT vs JAK2V617F.

qanda.q: Is DCAF7 scaffolding function separable from its CRL4 adapter function?
qanda.a: Experimentally open. Predicted separation-of-function mutants (DDB1-binding patch vs DYRK1A DH-box interface) have not been validated.

qanda.q: What would reverse the case that IFIT3 is the relevant MPN substrate?
qanda.a: Rescue failure: re-expressing IFIT3 at physiological levels in DCAF7-KO HEL cells should phenocopy the DCAF7-KO phenotype if IFIT3 is the dominant substrate. If it does not, other substrates matter.

qanda.q: What is the most important non-redundant experiment in the proposal?
qanda.a: Substrate validation in vitro (purified DDB1–DCAF7–CUL4, E1/E2, ubiquitin, IFIT3, ATP ￿ K48 chain formation on IFIT3) plus the separation-of-function rescue in cells. Without this, the substrate claim remains correlative.

qanda.q: Given the PanNET data, what is the systemic-toxicity risk of DCAF7 inhibition?
qanda.a: MEN1 de-repression in neuroendocrine tissues, potential effects on Wnt/β-catenin in liver, and developmental risk if used in pregnancy . Mitigated by hematopoietic-selective dependency and adult-only use.

qanda.q: Q: What distinguishes primitive from definitive hematopoiesis?
qanda.a: A: Primitive hematopoiesis occurs in the yolk sac (E7–9 mouse), produces large nucleated embryonic erythrocytes expressing embryonic hemoglobins, and lacks HSCs. Definitive hematopoiesis arises from the AGM (E10.5) via EHT, produces multilineage HSCs with long-term repopulating activity , and yields adult-type hemoglobin-expressing enucleated red cells.

qanda.q: Q: What is endothelial-to-hematopoietic transition and which transcription factors are essential?
qanda.a: A: EHT is the morphogenetic process by which hemogenic endothelial cells in the ventral dorsal aorta round up, detach, and emerge as HSCs into the aortic lumen. Essential TFs include RUNX1 (absolute requirement), SCL/TAL1 (upstream), GATA2, NOTCH1 (from surrounding mesenchyme), and the CDX-HOXA axis. RUNX1 deletion abolishes definitive hematopoiesis while sparing primitive erythropoiesis.

qanda.q: Q: Why is the fetal liver relevant for studying HSC self-renewal?
qanda.a: A: Fetal liver HSCs expand ~38-fold (Morrison 1995) while actively cycling, providing a tractable model for productive self-renewal divisions that contrasts with quiescent adult HSCs. Differences in chromatin state, LIN28B–let-7, and Sox17 dependence reveal that fetal self-renewal is mechanistically distinct from adult maintenance.

qanda.q: Q: What are EMPs and why do they matter?
qanda.a: A: Erythro-myeloid progenitors arise in the yolk sac at ~E8.25, produce definitive-type erythrocytes and myeloid cells prior to HSC emergence, and generate long-lived tissue-resident macrophages (microglia, Kupffer cells, Langerhans cells) that persist into adulthood independently of HSCs (Gomez Perdiguero 2015).

qanda.q: Q: Where and when does the fetal-to-adult HSC switch occur?
qanda.a: A: Around 3–4 weeks postnatal in mouse; HSCs exit a fetal, proliferative, SOX17/LIN28B/HMGA2-high program and enter an adult quiescent BMI1/GFI1-high program. The LIN28B–let-7–HMGA2 axis (Copley 2013) is a key regulator.

qanda.q: Q: What role does the placenta play in HSC biology?
qanda.a: A: The placenta harbors transplantable HSCs by E10.5–11.5 and at its peak contains more HSCs than the AGM (Gekas 2005; Ottersbach 2005). It may support both de novo HSC generation and expansion of AGM-derived HSCs before fetal liver seeding.

qanda.q: Q: How do LT-HSCs, ST-HSCs, and MPPs differ functionally?
qanda.a: A: LT-HSCs support longterm (≥16 week) and serial reconstitution with self-renewal; ST-HSCs/MPP1 reconstitute shortterm (weeks–months) without sustained self-renewal; MPP2/3/4 are lineage-biased multipotent progenitors with limited self-renewal, tuned toward Mk/E, myeloid, and lymphoid output respectively .

qanda.q: Q: What evidence challenges the classical hierarchy?
qanda.a: A: Single-cell transplants (Notta 2016) show direct Mk/E output from the HSC compartment without CMP intermediates; scRNAseq (V elten 2017) reveals continuous priming; barcoding (Naik 2013; Pei 2017) shows clonal heterogeneity and biased outputs.

qanda.q: Q: What do native lineage tracing studies say about steady-state hematopoiesis?
qanda.a: A: Studies using Tie2-CreER, Fgd5-CreER, and transposon tagging (Busch 2015; Sun 2014) suggest that unperturbed hematopoiesis is largely sustained by MPPs, with LT-HSC contribution small and slow; in contrast, transplantation biases output toward HSCs.

qanda.q: Q: How would you reconcile transplantation assays and native tracing?
qanda.a: A: Transplantation is a stress assay requiring self-renewal and engraftment, which biologically selects LT-HSCs; native tracing captures the actual day-to-day output in which MPPs dominate. Both are valid — they answer different questions.

qanda.q: Q: How is the MPP compartment relevant to stress responses?
qanda.a: A: Stress signals (IFN, LPS, IL-1, infection) shift the MPP distribution: MPP3 expansion drives emergency myelopoiesis, MPP2 expansion drives platelet surges, MPP4 expansion supports lymphoid recovery . MPPs are the principal effectors of demand-adapted hematopoiesis.

qanda.q: Q: Give the canonical SLAM phenotype of mouse LT-HSC and its reference. A: Lin− Sca1+ c-Kit+ CD150+ CD48−, defined by Kiel et al. (2005 Cell); CD150 marks HSCs and CD48
qanda.a: excludes activated progenitors.

qanda.q: Q: Which markers resolve MPP2, MPP3, MPP4 in the Pietras framework?
qanda.a: A: MPP2 = CD150+CD48+; MPP3 = CD150−CD48+; MPP4 = CD150−CD48−/+ Flt3+ (CD135+). All are LSK.

qanda.q: Q: What is CD49f and why does it matter for human HSC purification?
qanda.a: A: CD49f (integrin α6) marks the most purified human LT-HSC population (CD34+CD38−CD90+CD45RA−CD49f+); Notta et al. (2011) demonstrated single-cell in vivo repopulation in NSG mice.

qanda.q: Q: What is an FMO control and why is it used?
qanda.a: A: Fluorescence-Minus-One: a sample stained with the full panel except one fluorophore, used to define the true negative boundary for that channel in the presence of spillover/autofluorescence. Crucial for low-intensity markers like CD150.

qanda.q: Q: How do you gate human CMP , GMP , MEP?
qanda.a: A: Within Lin− CD34+ CD38+: CMP = CD123+CD45RA−; GMP = CD123+CD45RA+; MEP = CD123−CD45RA− (Manz et al. 2002).

qanda.q: Q: Why must you omit Mac-1 from the lineage cocktail when analyzing fetal liver?
qanda.a: A: Fetal HSCs express Mac-1/CD11b, so including it in the dump channel would exclude true HSCs. Ter119 and CD3 are retained; Mac-1 and Gr-1 are sometimes omitted entirely in E14.5 FL.

qanda.q: Q: What are the principal cellular components of the HSC niche?
qanda.a: A: LepR+ perivascular MSCs (overlapping with Nestin^low/CAR), sinusoidal and arteriolar endothelial cells, NG2+ arteriolar pericytes, sympathetic nerves and Schwann cells, CD169+ macrophages, megakaryocytes, osteoblasts/osteocytes (for lymphoid progenitors), regulatory T cells, and adipocytes (context-dependent).

qanda.q: Q: What are the key niche-derived factors for HSC maintenance?
qanda.a: A: CXCL12 (retention, chemotaxis), SCF (survival/proliferation), TPO (quiescence), Angpt1 (quiescence), TGF-β (quiescence), Jagged/Notch ligands, and Wnt ligands. Loss-of-function of CXCL12 or SCF from LepR+ MSCs or ECs depletes HSCs.

qanda.q: Q: What drug is used to mobilize HSCs clinically and what is its mechanism?
qanda.a: A: Plerixafor (AMD3100), a small-molecule CXCR4 antagonist that disrupts HSC retention, typically combined with G-CSF for autologous transplantation.

qanda.q: Q: How do megakaryocytes contribute to the HSC niche?
qanda.a: A: MKs directly contact LT-HSCs and secrete CXCL4/PF4, TGF-β, and thrombopoietin, enforcing quiescence. Diphtheria-toxin depletion of MKs activates HSC cycling (Bruns 2014; Zhao 2014).

qanda.q: Q: Why are HSCs hypoxic and what does it mean for their biology?
qanda.a: A: Niche pO2 is low (~10– 20 mmHg), stabilizing HIF-1α and enforcing glycolytic metabolism with low mitochondrial activity and low ROS. This protects HSCs from replication stress and oxidative DNA damage; loss of FOXO or HIF-1α increases ROS and exhausts HSCs.

qanda.q: Q: How often do LT-HSCs divide at steady state?
qanda.a: A: The dormant fraction divides ~every 145 days; active LT-HSCs ~every 36 days (Wilson 2008; Foudi 2009). Only ~4–5% are in cycle at any time.

qanda.q: Q: What is the dominant CDK inhibitor in LT-HSC quiescence?
qanda.a: A: p57/Cdkn1c (Matsumoto 2011; Zou 2011), with contributions from p21 and p27.

qanda.q: Q: What does PTEN loss tell us about HSC self-renewal?
qanda.a: A: Pten deletion drives HSCs into cycle via PI3K/AKT/mTOR hyperactivation, causing transient expansion followed by exhaustion and giving rise to leukemia-initiating cells (Yilmaz 2006). Rapamycin rescues HSC function — illustrating that quiescence is both a differentiation brake and a survival mechanism.

qanda.q: Q: How does ROS regulate HSCs?
qanda.a: A: Low ROS preserves quiescence; elevated ROS activates p38 MAPK and drives HSC cycling, differentiation, and eventual exhaustion. FOXO TFs transcribe antioxidant genes (catalase, SOD2); Foxo1/3/4-null HSCs accumulate ROS and exhaust (Tothova 2007).

qanda.q: Q: What is the Bernitz divisional-memory finding?
qanda.a: A: H2B-GFP label-dilution tracked HSC division number. After ~4 divisions, HSCs lose long-term self-renewal, arguing that division count — not chronological time — dictates functional decline (Bernitz 2016).

qanda.q: Q: Why is NHEJ vs HR relevant to HSC biology?
qanda.a: A: Quiescent G0 HSCs use NHEJ, which is error-prone. This explains why long-lived HSCs accumulate somatic mutations with age, contributing to clonal hematopoiesis and leukemogenic risk.

qanda.q: Q: Explain GATA1/PU.1 cross-antagonism. A: GATA1 and PU.1 bind each other’s cis elements and physically interact (GATA1–PU.1 protein interaction blocks PU.1 activity and vice
qanda.a: versa). Stochastic imbalance in a progenitor tips the system toward erythroid (GATA1-high) or myeloid (PU.1-high), a textbook example of a bistable lineage switch.

qanda.q: Q: Which TFs are required for HSC emergence vs maintenance?
qanda.a: A: Emergence: RUNX1, SCL/TAL1, GATA2, NOTCH1. Maintenance: HOXA9/MEIS1, GATA2, RUNX1, MLL, BMI1, EVI1, GFI1. Some (RUNX1, GATA2) function in both with context-specific partners.

qanda.q: Q: What phenotype is seen in C/EBPα loss?
qanda.a: A: Block in CMP￿ GMP transition with profound granulocytic deficit; in humans, CEBP Abiallelic mutations define an AML subtype.

qanda.q: Q: What is the function of BCL11B in T-cell development?
qanda.a: A: BCL11B enforces T-cell identity at the DN2￿ DN3 transition; deletion reprograms DN progenitors toward NK or myeloid fates, showing that lineage identity is actively maintained even after commitment.

qanda.q: Q: Why is HOXA9 important in both normal and malignant hematopoiesis?
qanda.a: A: HOXA9/MEIS1 maintain HSC self-renewal programs. MLL rearrangements and NPM1 mutations cause sustained HOXA9 expression in committed cells, immortalizing them and driving AML.

qanda.q: Q: Walk through the EPO-R signal. A: EPO binds homodimeric EPO-R, which associates with
qanda.a: JAK2. JAK2 trans-phosphorylates, phosphorylates EPO-R cytoplasmic tails, recruits STAT5 which dimerizes and translocates to nucleus. Negative feedback via SOCS3/CIS and SHP1.

qanda.q: Q: What distinguishes MPL from EPO-R biologically?
qanda.a: A: Both use JAK2/STAT5, but MPL is expressed on LT-HSCs and megakaryocytes, supporting HSC quiescence and megakary101 opoiesis. EPO-R is restricted to committed erythroid progenitors. Mpl^-/- mice have HSC deficits; EpoR^-/- mice have embryonic-lethal anemia without HSC defects.

qanda.q: Q: How do SOCS proteins terminate signaling, and how does this connect to UPS?
qanda.a: A: SOCS proteins bind phosphorylated JAKs/receptors via their SH2 domain and recruit an Elongin B/C–Cullin5–Rbx2 E3 ligase via the SOCS box, ubiquitinating substrates for proteasomal degradation. This is a canonical example of UPS regulation of hematopoietic signaling.

qanda.q: Q: Why does IFN-α activate HSCs and why is this relevant to ISGs?
qanda.a: A: IFN-α/β ￿ IFNAR1/2 ￿ JAK1/TYK2 ￿ STAT1/STAT2 + IRF9 ￿ ISGs, many of which are UPS components (ISG15, USP18, TRIMs). In HSCs, IFN-α drives dormant LT-HSCs into cycle via STAT1 and Sca-1 upregulation (Essers 2009).

qanda.q: Q: Which cytokine receptors use JAK-STAT vs RTK?
qanda.a: A: JAK-STAT: EPO-R, MPL, G-CSFR, GM-CSF-R, IL-3R, IL-6R, IL-7R, IFNAR, IFNGR. RTK (PI3K/MAPK, no JAK): c-KIT, FLT3, CSF1R. Both classes converge on PI3K/AKT and RAS/MAPK downstream.

qanda.q: Q: Why is myeloid chimerism the most stringent readout of HSC function?
qanda.a: A: Myeloid cells (especially Gr-1+Mac-1+ granulocytes) turn over in days, so sustained myeloid donor chimerism at 16+ weeks requires continual production from an engrafted LT-HSC. Lymphoid chimerism can persist from long-lived mature cells or a committed progenitor and is less specific.

qanda.q: Q: How would you calculate HSC frequency from a limiting dilution experiment?
qanda.a: A: Use Poisson statistics with ELDA (Hu and Smyth 2009): plot fraction of negative recipients vs cell dose; frequency = 1 / number of cells giving 37% negative recipients (at Poisson expectation of one HSC per inoculum).

qanda.q: Q: What does CFU-S actually measure and why is it historically important?
qanda.a: A: CFU-S (Till and McCulloch 1961) measures clonal spleen colony formation 8–12 days after BM injection into irradiated mice. Day-12 CFU-S enriches for ST-HSCs/MPPs rather than LT-HSCs but established the clonal origin and hierarchy of hematopoiesis — a landmark experiment.

qanda.q: Q: What do native tracing models tell us that transplantation misses?
qanda.a: A: Transplantation forces engraftment and self-renewal under stress. Native tracing ( Fgd5-CreER, Tie2-CreER, transposon tagging) shows unperturbed contribution: MPPs do most steady-state work, LTHSC contribution is slow and clonal. These complementary views together describe the full biology .

qanda.q: Q: What is MISTRG and when would you use it?
qanda.a: A: A humanized mouse (Rag2 -/-Il2rg-/- with human M-CSF, IL-3, GM-CSF, TPO, SIRPα knock-ins) for robust human myeloid and HSC engraftment. Used for human myeloid malignancies, HSC xenografts, and studies of human-specific biology not modeled in NSG.

qanda.q: Q: Describe the Essers 2009 finding and its mechanism. A: Essers et al. (2009 Nature) showed
qanda.a: that IFN-α injection drives dormant LT-HSCs from G0 into cycle via IFNAR-JAK1/TYK2STAT1; Sca-1 is strongly upregulated. This was the first direct demonstration that a type I IFN acts on HSCs themselves and not just on downstream progenitors.

qanda.q: Q: How does chronic IFN exposure damage HSCs?
qanda.a: A: Repeated forced cycling causes replication stress, γH2AX foci, and functional exhaustion (Walter 2015 Nature). Also selects for DNA damage-tolerant or IFN-resistant clones, contributing to clonal hematopoiesis.

qanda.q: Q: Why must Mx1-Cre experiments include poly(I:C) controls?
qanda.a: A: Poly(I:C) itself induces IFN and activates HSCs; any HSC phenotype observed in Mx1-Cre;gene^fl/fl mice must be compared to Mx1-Cre;gene^+/+ littermates treated identically . CreER-based systems (e.g., Fgd5CreERT2 with tamoxifen) avoid this confounder.

qanda.q: Q: How does IL-1 shape HSC behavior?
qanda.a: A: Acute IL-1 via IL-1R1 ￿ MyD88/IRAK/NF-κB drives PU.1 upregulation, myeloid bias, and proliferation; chronic IL-1 exhausts HSCs and may select for TET2-mutant clones (Pietras 2016; Muto 2022).

qanda.q: Q: How does G-CSF mobilize HSCs?
qanda.a: A: G-CSF activates neutrophils in the niche, releasing proteases (elastase, MMP9, cathepsin G) that cleave CXCL12 and other retention signals; also suppresses osteoblasts and MSC CXCL12 expression, and downregulates CXCR4 on HSCs. Net effect: HSC egress into blood.

qanda.q: Q: Walk through your gating strategy for mouse LT-HSCs.A: FSC/SSC (cells) ￿ FSC-H/FSCA (singlets) ￿ viability (DAPI−) ￿ Lin− ￿ c-Kit+Sca-1+ (LSK) ￿ CD150+CD48− (LT-HSC).
qanda.a: For MPPs, use the CD150 × CD48 quadrant plus Flt3 for MPP4. Each gate set with FMO and biological (WT/KO) controls.

qanda.q: Q: How do you distinguish G0, G1, and S/G2/M in HSCs?
qanda.a: A: Intracellular Ki-67 staining (nuclear proliferation marker) combined with DNA content dye (DAPI or Hoechst): G0 = Ki67−DNA^2N; G1 = Ki-67+DNA^2N; S/G2/M = Ki-67+DNA^>2N. Pyronin Y/Hoechst is an older alternative.

qanda.q: Q: What controls are essential for intracellular pSTAT1 analysis in HSCs?
qanda.a: A: Unstimulated vs stimulated (IFN-α) samples, isotype or phospho-specific blocking peptide, compensation, FMO for pSTAT1 channel, and ideally STAT1-null or JAK1-null cells as biological negatives.

qanda.q: Q: Why do you sort into CD45.1+CD45.2+ F1 recipients for competitive BMT?
qanda.a: A: This allows discrimination between donor (CD45.2 alone), competitor (CD45.1 alone), and residual recipient (CD45.1+CD45.2+) cells, unambiguously distinguishing the three compartments in peripheral blood flow cytometry .

qanda.q: Q: What are typical cell numbers for LT-HSC-level assays?
qanda.a: A: CFU: ~50–200 LT-HSCs per plate (with 500–2000 LSK yielding more robust colony counts). Competitive BMT: 100–500 LTHSCs + 2–3 × 10^5 competitor WBM. scRNA-seq: 200–10,000 cells via 10x or Smart-seq3 for deeper coverage.

qanda.q: Define the perinatal window in the mouse and justify why it is the “critical window” for the
qanda.a: fetal-to-adult HSC transition. What specific events occur in this window that do not occur before or after?

qanda.q: Bowie et al. 2007 PNAS is often cited as evidence that the fetal-to-adult HSC switch is cellautonomous. Describe the experimental design and how it rules out niche-instruction as the
qanda.a: dominant mode. What would you predict for a heterochronic transplant of adult HSCs into a fetal liver niche?

qanda.q: LIN28B/let-7 is the canonical fetal identity module. Trace the mechanism from LIN28B binding pre-let-7 to the downstream consequences for HMGA2, IGF2BP1/2, and MYC. How would
qanda.a: you experimentally test whether DCAF7 targets any of these proteins for degradation?

qanda.q: EZH2 is required for fetal HSC self-renewal but dispensable in adult HSCs, while BMI1 is
qanda.a: required for adult HSC self-renewal but dispensable in fetal. Explain this fetal-adult switch in PRC2 and PRC1 dependencies, including the roles of EZH1 compensation and Ink4a/Arf repression.

qanda.q: Summarize the key findings of Li et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell. What is the timing of the perinatal IFN pulse, what is its tissue source, and how was the microbiome ruled out?
qanda.a: What are the specific hematopoietic consequences of Ifnar1 loss at this window?

qanda.q: Why are HSC numbers unchanged in Ifnar1-/- mice at E16.5 and P0 while HPC expansion fails?
qanda.a: What does this tell you about the population-level specificity of the IFN pulse, and how does it shape your prediction for which cells should be most affected in the Dcaf7 cKO if DCAF7 regulates IFN signaling?

qanda.q: Li et al. 2020 found that FLT3-ITD-driven HPC expansion is rescued by Ifnar1 loss. Explain the
qanda.a: interpretation that “IFN signaling is permissive for oncogenic transformation at this window.” How would you design an experiment to test whether loss of DCAF7 is permissive, protective, or irrelevant to FLT3-ITD-driven transformation?

qanda.q: Li et al. 2023 Blood Adv showed that basal IFN signaling has only modest effects on steadystate adult hematopoiesis. Reconcile this with the well-established observation that chronic
qanda.a: IFN signaling (e.g., in SCA) drives myeloid skewing. What does this tell you about how to frame DCAF7’s hypothesized effect on IFN signaling — as a loss of basal tone, as an elevation above baseline, or as a window-specific perturbation?

qanda.q: Serra et al. 2026 Am J Hematol links chronic IFN-I in SCA to myeloid skewing and shows that hydroxyurea reverses ISG elevation. What is the parallel to the perinatal IFN biology , and what mechanistic commonalities link the two settings?
qanda.a: If your Dcaf7 cKO shows myeloid skewing, how would you distinguish an IFN-driven mechanism from other possibilities?

qanda.q: The Vav-Cre Dcaf7 cKO shows no phenotype at E14.5 but a severe phenotype at P4. Dcaf7
qanda.a: expression peaks at P4 in public datasets. Articulate why this temporal specificity argues for a developmental role, and list three alternative interpretations you would want to rule out.

qanda.q: Describe the chromatin landscape of the fetal-to-adult transition from Li et al. 2020: how many enhancers switch H3K27ac marking, what happens to fetal enhancers in the adult state, and which transcription factor motifs are enriched at adult-activated enhancers?
qanda.a: Why does IRF motif enrichment matter for the IFN pulse interpretation?

qanda.q: Sox17 is a fetal-specific HSC maintenance factor (Kim, Saunders, Morrison 2007 Cell). If DCAF7 targets SOX17 for degradation, what would you predict for SOX17 protein levels in the Dcaf7 cKO at E14.5, P0, P4, and adult?
qanda.a: How would you design the stability assays to distinguish transcriptional from post-translational regulation?

qanda.q: The perinatal niche includes a transient portal vessel-associated Nestin+ NG2+ pericyte population (Khan et al. 2016 Science). Why does this matter for interpreting a pan-hematopoietic cKO phenotype at P4?
qanda.a: How would you distinguish cell-intrinsic from niche-mediated contributions given that Vav-Cre targets only hematopoietic cells?

qanda.q: Fetal-origin tissue-resident macrophages (microglia, Kupffer cells, alveolar macrophages) are
qanda.a: seeded before and during the perinatal IFN pulse. If DCAF7 regulates the IFN response, what long-term tissue-macrophage phenotypes might you expect in the cKO, and how would you assay them?

qanda.q: List the four mechanistic hypotheses for DCAF7 function at the perinatal transition (IFIT3 regulation, fetal factor degradation, DYRK1A/HIPK2 scaffolding, unknown substrate). For each,
qanda.a: state one experiment that would support the hypothesis and one observation that would falsify it. Which hypothesis is most tractable to address first in Aim 2, and why?

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
AML is the most common acute leukemia in adults, with approximately 20,000 newly diagnosed
cases per year in the United States, a median age at diagnosis of 68, and an age-adjusted incidence

of roughly 4 per 100,000 per year. Outcomes remain poor despite five decades of therapeutic refinement: overall five-year survival sits near 30% in adults (and below 10% in patients over 65 who
cannot tolerate intensive induction), while pediatric AML achieves approximately 70% five-year
survival through the AAML / COG cooperative-group regimens. The bimodal nature of AML —
chemo-responsive cytogenetically defined pediatric disease versus secondary , therapy-related, or
TP53-mutant adult disease — is the single most important epidemiological statement to make in a
committee room.
The diagnostic definition of AML has evolved substantially . Historically (and in WHO 2008/2016;
Arber et al. 2016 Blood, PMID 27069254), AML required ≥20% myeloid blasts in the bone marrow
(BM) or peripheral blood (PB), a threshold that replaced the older FAB >30% criterion. WHO 2022
and the International Consensus Classification (ICC 2022) abolished the 20% blast requirement for
AML defined by specific genetic lesions — t(15;17)/PML-RARA (APL), t(8;21)/RUNX1-RUNX1T1,
inv(16)/CBFB-MYH11, t(9;11)/KMT2A-MLLT3, and mutated NPM1 — recognizing that these genetic entities are biologically AML regardless of blast count. This change is clinically consequential:
a patient with 15% blasts and mutant NPM1 is now classified and treated as AML, not MDS-EB.
Clinical presentation reflects the dual pathologies of marrow failure and blast infiltration. Cytopenias dominate — fatigue and dyspnea from anemia, bleeding and petechiae from thrombocytopenia, and infections (often fungal or Gram-negative) from neutropenia. Hyperleukocytosis
with leukostasis is a medical emergency . Extramedullary disease is classical in monocytic subtypes
(formerly FAB M4/M5): gum hypertrophy , skin involvement (leukemia cutis, chloromas/myeloid
sarcomas), and CNS involvement. APL has its own hematologic emergency — disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — which is the reason that ATRA is started empirically on clinical
suspicion, before molecular confirmation, to reduce early hemorrhagic death.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
AML classification has passed through three conceptual eras, and the student should be able to
map each to its biological logic.

The FAB system (1976) stratified by lineage and maturation: M0 (minimally differentiated),
M1 (without maturation), M2 (with maturation; classically t(8;21)), M3 (APL; t(15;17)), M4
(myelomonocytic), M4eo (with eosinophilia; inv(16)), M5 (monocytic), M6 (erythroid), and M7
(megakaryoblastic; classically in Down syndrome). FAB is no longer used for risk stratification
but survives in clinical parlance (APL is still “M3”) and maps roughly onto cell-of-origin arrest.
The WHO 2016 revision (Arber 2016 Blood, PMID 27069254) reorganized AML by (1) recurrent
genetic abnormalities, (2) myelodysplasia-related changes (MRC), (3) therapy-related (t-AML), and
(4) not-otherwise-specified (AML-NOS). This framework captured the fact that cytogenetics and
molecular genetics outperform morphology for prognosis and therapy .
WHO 2022 / ICC 2022 further sharpened the classification. The AML-defining genetic category
now includes: PML-RARA, RUNX1-RUNX1T1, CBFB-MYH11, DEK-NUP214, RBM15-MRTFA,
BCR-ABL1, KMT2A-rearranged, MECOM-rearranged, NUP98-rearranged, NPM1-mutated,
CEBPA-mutated (now with specific focus on bZIP in-frame mutations), and — newly recognized
as distinct prognostic entities — TP53-mutated AML and AML with myelodysplasia-related gene
mutations (ASXL1, BCOR, EZH2, RUNX1, SF3B1, SRSF2, STAG2, U2AF1, ZRSR2).
The European LeukemiaNet (ELN) 2022 risk stratification is the operational prognostic framework:
