subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body: AML driver genetics fall into functional classes (signaling; transcription factors; epigenetic regulators; spliceosome; cohesin; tumor suppressors). The student should be able to name class, prototypical mutation, prognostic weight, and therapeutic vulnerability for each of the following.

subsections.title: NPM1
subsections.body:
NPM1 is the single most common adult AML driver, mutated in approximately 30% of adult AML.
Mutations cluster in exon 12 (most commonly the type A c.860_863dupTCTG four-base-pair duplication), introducing a frameshift that creates a new C-terminal nuclear export signal (NES) and
disrupts the tryptophan residues required for nucleolar localization. The result is cytoplasmic
mislocalization of NPM1 (detectable by immunohistochemistry as NPMc+), dysregulation of the
ARF–MDM2–p53 axis, and activation of a HOXA9/MEIS1 transcriptional program that enforces
self-renewal and differentiation block. NPM1mut AML frequently co-occurs with FLT3-ITD (approximately 40% co-occurrence), DNMT3A R882 (approximately 50%), and IDH1/IDH2 or TET2
mutations. In isolation, NPM1mut is favorable; with FLT3-ITD, risk becomes intermediate. Importantly , NPM1mut is a validated MRD target (RT-qPCR) and — because of its HOXA9 addiction —
is sensitive to menin-KMT2A inhibition (revumenib; Issa et al. 2023 Nature).

subsections.title: FLT3
subsections.body:
FLT3 (a type III receptor tyrosine kinase) is mutated in approximately 30% of adult AML. Two
classes dominate: FLT3-ITD (internal tandem duplications in the juxtamembrane autoinhibitory
domain; approximately 25% of AML), which produces constitutive ligand-independent dimerization and STAT5 activation, and FLT3-TKD point mutations (most commonly D835Y in the activation loop; approximately 7%), which produce conformational activation. FLT3-ITD is adverse
(unless offset by NPM1); the allelic ratio and insertion length matter historically though ELN 2022
simplified by dropping the 0.5 threshold. FLT3-TKD is less prognostic as a de novo driver but is
a major resistance mechanism to type I FLT3 inhibitors at relapse. Therapeutic targeting uses midostaurin (Rydapt; type I multikinase, first-line with 7+3 per RATIFY), gilteritinib (Xospata; type I,
relapsed/refractory per ADMIRAL), and quizartinib (Vanflyta; type II, more FLT3-selective, firstline per QuANTUM-First).

subsections.title: DNMT3A
subsections.body:
DNMT3A encodes a de novo DNA methyltransferase. Mutations — predominantly the dominantnegative R882H/C hotspot — occur in approximately 25% of AML. R882 mutants impair tetramerization and exert a dominant-negative effect on wild-type DNMT3A, reducing global CpG methylation and causing focal hypomethylation at enhancers of stemness genes. DNMT3A is a canonical founder lesion: it is the most common clonal hematopoiesis (CHIP) mutation (Jaiswal 2014
NEJM), trackable to HSCs years before transformation, and cooperates with later NPM1 and FLT3
hits. DNMT3A-mut is adverse on its own and persists in remission bone marrow (hence “DTA”
mutations are excluded from MRD quantification).

subsections.title: IDH1 and IDH2
subsections.body:
Isocitrate dehydrogenase mutations (IDH1 R132 and IDH2 R140 / R172, together ~15–20% of AML)
are neomorphic — the mutant enzyme gains the ability to reduce alpha-ketoglutarate (αKG) to the
oncometabolite D-2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG). 2-HG competitively inhibits αKG-dependent dioxygenases: TET2 (DNA 5mC ￿ 5hmC), the KDM4 and KDM6 Jumonji-domain histone demethylases
(H3K9, H3K27, H3K36), and prolyl hydroxylases. The result is a CpG island hypermethylator phenotype (CIMP), H3K9/K27/K36 hypermethylation, stabilization of HIF1α signaling, and a differentiation block at the myeloblast stage. IDH1 and IDH2 are targeted with ivosidenib (Tibsovo) and
enasidenib (Idhifa), respectively , both of which produce “differentiation syndrome” (analogous to
APL DS) — clinical evidence that the differentiation block is released in vivo. Olutasidenib (IDH1)
joined the armamentarium in 2022.

subsections.title: TET2
subsections.body:
TET2 is a dioxygenase that catalyzes 5mC ￿ 5hmC, opposing DNMT3A. Loss-of-function mutations (nonsense, frameshift, or missense affecting the catalytic domain) reduce 5hmC and produce
focal hypermethylation at enhancers. Like DNMT3A, TET2 is a founder CHIP lesion (Jaiswal 2014
NEJM). It cooperates with cooperating late drivers and is part of the DTA trio.

subsections.title: ASXL1 and EZH2
subsections.body:
ASXL1 (additional sex combs-like 1) scaffolds the PR-DUB deubiquitinase complex (with BAP1)
that removes H2AK119ub, and also participates in Polycomb repressive complex 2 (PRC2) recruitment. Loss-of-function truncating mutations (typically in exon 12) are adverse in AML and
common in MDS and MPN. EZH2 (catalytic PRC2 H3K27 methyltransferase) mutations are typically loss-of-function in myeloid disease (the opposite of lymphoma, where EZH2 Y641 is gain-offunction). Both ASXL1 and EZH2 are ELN 2022 adverse secondary-AML-type mutations.

subsections.title: CEBPA
subsections.body:
CEBPA encodes a granulocytic master transcription factor. Classical biallelic mutations — Nterminal frameshift (producing truncated p30 dominant-negative) plus C-terminal bZIP in-frame

indel — were favorable in WHO 2016. ELN 2022 sharpened the favorable designation to any inframe bZIP CEBPA mutation (bi- or monoallelic), reflecting data that the bZIP lesion is the driver
of the favorable phenotype.

subsections.title: RUNX1 and the Core-Binding Factor Complex
subsections.body:
RUNX1 (AML1) and its heterodimer partner CBFB form the core-binding factor (CBF) essential for
definitive hematopoiesis. Translocations involving the CBF — t(8;21)(q22;q22)/RUNX1-RUNX1T1
(AML1-ETO) and inv(16)(p13q22) or t(16;16)/CBFB-MYH11 — produce favorable-risk CBF AMLs
with high cure rates on high-dose cytarabine consolidation. Somatic point mutations in RUNX1
(distinct from the fusion) are adverse. Germline RUNX1 mutations cause familial platelet disorder
with predisposition to AML (FPD-AML).

subsections.title: PML-RARA and APL
subsections.body:
The t(15;17)(q22;q21) PML-RARA fusion defines APL, which has an exceptional biology: the PMLRARA fusion acts as a constitutive super-repressor at RARE-containing promoters via enhanced
NCoR/SMRT/HDAC recruitment, blocking granulocytic differentiation. ATRA (all-trans retinoic
acid) binds the RARA moiety , displaces corepressors, and triggers proteasomal degradation of
PML-RARA; arsenic trioxide (ATO) binds the PML moiety , induces oxidation, SUMOylation, and
RNF4-mediated ubiquitination and proteasomal destruction. The combination is curative in >95%
of non-high-risk APL without cytotoxic chemotherapy (Lo-Coco et al. 2013 NEJM, APL0406 trial).
APL is therefore the paradigmatic UPS-driven differentiation therapy — discussed in detail in I.6
and I.11.

subsections.title: KMT2A (MLL) Rearrangements
subsections.body:
KMT2A (MLL1; 11q23) rearrangements occur in approximately 5–10% of adult AML and up to 70%
of infant AML, fusing KMT2A to >80 partners — most commonly MLLT3 (AF9; t(9;11)), AFDN
(AF6), ELL, MLLT1 (ENL), MLLT10 (AF10), and others. Fusion proteins recruit DOT1L (H3K79
methyltransferase) and SEC/super-elongation complex to HOXA9 / MEIS1 loci, sustaining stemness programs. KMT2Ar AML is a therapy-related classic (topoisomerase II inhibitor exposure:
etoposide, anthracyclines). Menin (MEN1) is an obligate cofactor for KMT2A-r and KMT2A-WT
action at HOXA9, and menin–KMT2A inhibitors (revumenib/SNDX-5613, ziftomenib) produce
differentiation responses in KMT2Ar and NPM1mut AML (Issa et al. 2023 Nature).

subsections.title: TP53
subsections.body:
TP53 mutations (~10% of de novo AML, up to 30–40% of therapy-related and complex-karyotype
AML) are the worst prognostic lesion. They cluster in the DNA-binding domain (R175, R248, R273),
often with 17p loss producing biallelic inactivation. TP53-mut AML is chemo-resistant, has poor
allo-HCT outcomes, and is the indication for experimental agents like eprenetapopt (APR-246; a
mutant-p53 reactivator; FDA-rejected after failed phase III) and was a recent focus of the failed
magrolimab (anti-CD47) program.

subsections.title: Signaling Mutations
subsections.body:
RAS family (NRAS, KRAS), KIT (more common in CBF AMLs), PTPN11, NF1, CBL, and JAK2 are
recurrent signaling drivers. They are typically late, subclonal cooperating hits; their persistence or
expansion can track relapse. JAK2 in de novo AML is rare, but JAK2V617F is the defining driver
of the MPNs that concern this thesis, and JAK2V617F persists into blast-phase AML.

subsections.title: Splicing-Factor Mutations
subsections.body:
SRSF2 (P95), U2AF1 (S34/Q157), SF3B1 (K700E), and ZRSR2 mutations are characteristic of MDS
and MDS-related AML. They are adverse in AML (ELN 2022) and produce aberrant splicing of key
transcripts (e.g., EZH2 in SRSF2-mut; ABCB7 and TMEM14C in SF3B1-mut with ring sideroblasts).
They are secondary-AML-type mutations, a critical category given that a fraction of MPN-BP cases
acquire SRSF2 or U2AF1 at transformation.

subsections.title: Cohesin Complex
subsections.body:
STAG2, RAD21, SMC1A, and SMC3 mutations disrupt sister-chromatid cohesion and chromatin
looping, producing altered CTCF-anchored TAD boundaries and dysregulated enhancer–
promoter contacts at stemness loci. STAG2 is the most common, adverse, and ELN 2022
secondary-AML-type.

subsections.title: The “DTA” Concept
subsections.body:
DNMT3A, TET2, and ASXL1 mutations are collectively referred to as DTA mutations. They are
founder / preleukemic / CHIP mutations that expand the HSC clone in which the cooperating
late AML driver later arises. Clinically , DTA variants persist in remission and do NOT count as
measurable residual disease (MRD); using them as MRD markers would falsely predict relapse in
every DTA patient, because DTA-mutant hematopoiesis is compatible with long-term remission.

sections.title: Clonal Architecture and the Preleukemic Hierarchy

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The modern view of AML pathogenesis is a serial clonal evolution from a preleukemic HSC carrying a founder DTA mutation (most often DNMT3A, TET2, or ASXL1) to the leukemic clone acquiring cooperating late hits in NPM1, FLT3, IDH, RAS, or WT1. Jan & Majeti (2011 Sci Transl Med)
and Jan et al. (2012 Sci Transl Med) used targeted sequencing of sorted HSC/MPP/LMPP/GMP
fractions from AML patients to demonstrate that founder mutations (DNMT3A) were present in
phenotypically normal HSCs, while later cooperating mutations (NPM1) were restricted to the
leukemic bulk. Shlush et al. (2014 Nature) extended this to show that DNMT3A mutations traced
to functionally engrafting HSCs in xenotransplantation assays, establishing a preleukemic HSC as
the cell of origin of many AMLs.
Jaiswal et al. (2014 NEJM) established the population-scale counterpart: clonal hematopoiesis of
indeterminate potential (CHIP) — somatic DTA (and JAK2, SF3B1, etc.) mutations at V AF ≥2% in
otherwise healthy individuals — is present in >10% of people over 70 and confers an approximately

10-fold increased risk of hematologic malignancy (including AML), plus independent cardiovascular risk through inflammasome / macrophage effects. CHIP is the epidemiological link between
aging, inflammation, and the preleukemic state. This is directly relevant to the thesis, because
DCAF7 and CRL4 biology in aged HSCs (post-P4) operates on a substrate of accumulating CHIP
lesions, and IFIT3-mediated IFN signaling is a component of the CHIP-associated inflammatory
phenotype.
[FIGURE: Clonal evolution — preleukemic HSC ￿ MPP expansion ￿ NPM1/FLT3 acquisition
￿ AML bulk ￿ relapse with resistant subclone]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Bonnet & Dick (1997 Nat Med) established the leukemic stem cell (LSC) paradigm by showing that
only the CD34+CD38− fraction of AML blasts could initiate leukemia in NOD/SCID xenografts,
while the CD34+CD38+ and CD34− bulk could not. This was the founding experiment of the cancer stem cell field in any malignancy . Subsequent work (Sarry et al. 2011 JCI; Taussig et al. 2010
Blood) established that LSC activity is not strictly confined to CD34+CD38− phenotypes — particularly in NPM1-mut AML, LSC activity can reside in CD34− fractions, reflecting functional rather
than strictly phenotypic heterogeneity .
LSC surface markers used for detection, prognosis, and therapeutic targeting:
Marker Notes
CD34+CD38− Classical LSC phenotype; Bonnet & Dick 1997
CD47 “Don’t-eat-me” signal; Majeti 2009 Cell;
targeted by magrolimab (failed phase III in
TP53-mut)
TIM3 (HA VCR2) LSC-enriched, not on HSC; sabatolimab
CLL-1 (CLEC12A) LSC-restricted; CAR-T and BiTE candidate
CD123 (IL3RA) LSC-enriched; tagraxofusp (approved in
BPDCN), flotetuzumab CD3xCD123 BiTE
CD33 Pan-myeloid; gemtuzumab ozogamicin (ADC)
CD99 LSC-enriched; antibody-drug conjugate
candidate
GPR56, IL1RAP , CD25 Additional LSC-enriched markers
LSC functional properties : quiescence (G0), high BCL-2 dependency (mechanistic basis for venetoclax+HMA; Lagadinou 2013 Cell Stem Cell), reliance on oxidative phosphorylation and fattyacid oxidation (Ye 2016 Cell Stem Cell), and niche anchoring via CXCL12-CXCR4. Ng et al. (2016
Nature) derived the LSC17 gene-expression score , a 17-gene prognostic signature that outperforms cytogenetics and common mutations for predicting chemo-resistance — it is now in clinical
use.
UPS connections to LSCs (the committee chair’s territory). FBW7 (FBXW7), the substrate receptor
of the SCF^FBW7 CRL1 complex, enforces HSC and LSC quiescence by targeting c-Myc, cyclin
E, Notch ICD, and MCL-1 for degradation; FBW7 loss accelerates LSC cycling and paradoxically

sensitizes to imatinib in CML-LSCs (Reavie 2013 Cancer Cell; Thompson 2008 J Exp Med). More
broadly , CRL substrate receptors regulate the stability of nearly every HSC and LSC fate determinant (HOXA9, MEIS1, C/EBPα, GATA1, p21, p27, cyclins). DCAF7 as a CRL4 substrate receptor
in HSC/LSC biology is an open question directly relevant to the thesis — and precisely the kind
of gap the committee will probe.

sections.title: Differentiation Block as an AML Hallmark

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Myeloid differentiation arrest at the myeloblast stage is the defining cellular phenotype of AML
and is mechanistically converged on by multiple genetic drivers. Understanding this convergence
is essential for the UPS committee angle, because two of the three clinically validated differentiation therapies in AML (ATRA for APL; IDH inhibitors for IDH-mut AML) operate by releasing the
block, and both have UPS-adjacent mechanisms.

subsections.title: The HOXA9/MEIS1 Axis
subsections.body:
HOXA9 and its cofactor MEIS1 are upregulated in approximately 70% of AML and are required
for leukemic self-renewal. They are driven by multiple upstream lesions: KMT2A rearrangements
(through DOT1L recruitment and H3K79me2/3 deposition), NPM1 mutations (through a mechanism that requires menin and KMT2A at the HOXA cluster), NUP98 fusions, and CDX2 dysregulation. The menin–KMT2A–HOXA9 axis is pharmacologically targetable with menin inhibitors
(revumenib / SNDX-5613, ziftomenib, bleximenib; Issa et al. 2023 Nature demonstrated 53% overall response and 30% CR/CRh in relapsed/refractory KMT2Ar and NPM1-mut AML), and menininhibitor therapy phenotypically releases the differentiation block, producing differentiation syndrome.

subsections.title: PML-RARA as the Paradigmatic UPS-Dependent Differentiation Block
subsections.body:
In APL, PML-RARA homodimerizes and binds RARE elements as a super-repressor, recruiting
NCoR/SMRT/HDAC3 complexes at physiological ATRA concentrations that would otherwise release wild-type RARA. Pharmacological ATRA (10^-6 M) overcomes the dominant-negative and
— critically — triggers proteasomal degradation of PML-RARA via caspase cleavage, ligase recruitment (RNF4, SIAH2), and ubiquitination. ATO independently targets the PML moiety by oxidation ￿ SUMOylation ￿ RNF4-mediated poly-ubiquitination ￿ 26S proteasome degradation.
The result is simultaneous de-repression of RARE target genes and destruction of the oncoprotein. The combination achieves >95% cure without cytotoxic chemotherapy (Lo-Coco et al. 2013
NEJM). APL is the only disease in which two UPS-engaging agents produce cure, and it is the
biological proof-of-concept that motivates targeted protein degradation broadly — including the
PROTAC/molecular-glue field and, implicitly , DCAF7-substrate pairings.

subsections.title: IDH Mutations and the 2-HG-Mediated Block
subsections.body:
IDH1/2 mutations produce 2-HG, which inhibits TET2 and Jumonji histone demethylases,
producing a CIMP hypermethylator state that represses myeloid differentiation genes (C/EBPα
targets among them). Ivosidenib (IDH1) and enasidenib (IDH2) reduce 2-HG by >90%, restore

TET2/KDM activity , demethylate target loci over weeks, and produce differentiation syndrome
in ~10–15% of responders — direct clinical proof that the block is released in vivo.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
AML therapy has evolved dramatically since 2017 after three decades of near-stagnation. The
student should be fluent in mechanism, indication, and a key trial for each modality .

subsections.title: Induction Chemotherapy
subsections.body:
7+3 (seven days of continuous-infusion cytarabine 100–200 mg/m²/day + three days of daunorubicin 60–90 mg/m²/day or idarubicin 12 mg/m²/day) has been the induction standard since
the 1970s. It produces approximately 70% complete remission (CR) in medically fit adults
under 60, with induction mortality of 5–10%. Cytarabine is incorporated into DNA as Ara-CTP;
daunorubicin intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II. Consolidation is typically highdose cytarabine (HiDAC, 3 g/m² q12h × 3 days × 3–4 cycles) for favorable-risk, or allo-HCT for
intermediate/adverse-risk disease.

subsections.title: Venetoclax + Hypomethylating Agent (HMA)
subsections.body:
V enetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor) + azacitidine or decitabine (Dinardo et al. 2020 NEJM, VIALE-A)
is the practice-changing regimen for patients unfit for intensive induction (typically ≥75 years or
with significant comorbidities). VIALE-A showed ~65–70% composite CR (CR + CRi) and median
OS 14.7 months vs 9.6 months with azacitidine alone. The mechanistic basis is that LSCs are BCL2 dependent (Lagadinou 2013 Cell Stem Cell) and that HMAs downregulate MCL-1 (the major
BCL-2-family resistance node), converging on mitochondrial priming and apoptosis. V enetoclax +
HMA has largely replaced low-dose cytarabine and single-agent HMA in the unfit population and
is being tested in the fit and in MRD-eradication settings.

subsections.title: T argeted Kinase Inhibitors
subsections.body:
FLT3 inhibitors: midostaurin (RATIFY; added to 7+3 for FLT3-ITD/TKD in first-line), gilteritinib
(ADMIRAL; single-agent, relapsed/refractory FLT3-mut), quizartinib (QuANTUM-First; first-line
with 7+3 for FLT3-ITD only), crenolanib (phase III). Resistance: FLT3-TKD (D835, F691L gatekeeper) at relapse.
IDH inhibitors : ivosidenib (IDH1 R132; single-agent or combined with azacitidine per AGILE
trial), enasidenib (IDH2 R140/R172), olutasidenib (IDH1). All produce differentiation syndrome.
Menin-KMT2A inhibitors: revumenib (SNDX-5613; Issa et al. 2023 Nature), ziftomenib (KOMET001 trial), bleximenib — for KMT2A-r and NPM1-mut AML. Revumenib received FDA accelerated
approval in 2024 for R/R KMT2Ar acute leukemia.

subsections.title: APL-Specific Therapy
subsections.body:
ATRA + ATO (Lo-Coco et al. 2013 NEJM, APL0406): standard for non-high-risk APL, >95% cure
rate, chemotherapy-free. High-risk APL (WBC >10,000) still requires anthracycline addition.

subsections.title: Formulation and Post-Remission Innovations
subsections.body:
CPX-351 (Vyxeos) : liposomal 5:1 molar ratio cytarabine:daunorubicin, approved for therapyrelated AML and AML with MDS-related changes based on improved OS over 7+3 in older
patients (Lancet 2018 J Clin Oncol).
Oral azacitidine (CC-486) (Wei et al. 2020 NEJM, QUAZAR AML-001): maintenance therapy postremission in patients not proceeding to transplant, improved OS (24.7 vs 14.8 months). Pharmacokinetically distinct from injectable azacitidine.
Gemtuzumab ozogamicin (anti-CD33 calicheamicin ADC; Mylotarg): approved for CD33+ AML,
particularly CBF-AML, improves EFS with fractionated dosing.
Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (allo-HCT) : the only potentially curative consolidation for intermediateand adverse-risk AML in fit patients. Relies on graft-versus-leukemia
(GvL) effect plus myeloablative/reduced-intensity conditioning.

subsections.title: UPS-Directed Therapy in AML
subsections.body:
Bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor; approved in myeloma, mantle cell lymphoma) was evaluated
in AML (AAML1031 pediatric COG trial added bortezomib to standard induction; no benefit)
and in adult AML (limited single-agent activity). The failure contrasts with plasma-cell biology ,
where proteasome inhibition triggers catastrophic proteotoxic stress — AML blasts do not share
the extreme immunoglobulin biosynthetic burden that makes plasma cells uniquely proteasomeaddicted.
Pevonedistat (MLN4924): a NEDD8-activating enzyme (NAE, UBA3/NAE1) inhibitor that blocks
NEDD8 conjugation to cullins and thereby globally inactivates all CRL ubiquitin ligases (CRL1–
CRL5, including CRL4A/B). Pevonedistat plus azacitidine was tested in phase III (PEVENAZA
/ Pevonedistat 3001) for higher-risk MDS, CMML, and low-blast AML; the study failed its primary EFS endpoint in 2021 and development was halted. Importantly , despite the clinical failure,
pevonedistat is THE canonical research tool to acutely globally inactivate CRL-mediated ubiquitination — and it is exactly the pharmacological probe the student will use in Aim 1b ubiquitin
proteomics to stabilize CRL4-DCAF7 substrates (including the proposed IFIT3 substrate) for immunoprecipitation and mass-spectrometric identification. Mechanistically , pevonedistat is a prodrug that forms a covalent NEDD8-pevonedistat adduct that is a dead-end substrate for UBA3.
T argeted protein degraders (PROTACs, molecular glues) : IKZF1/3 degradation by lenalidomide/iberdomide via CRL4^CRBN is the flagship molecular-glue example in hematologic
malignancy (approved for myeloma; active in AML trials). CRBN biology is the template
for DCAF substrate-receptor targeting; DCAF7 is, in principle, a candidate for glue-based
reprogramming, and this is an angle the committee may explore.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Pediatric AML is epidemiologically and biologically distinct from adult AML, a distinction that
matters for this thesis because Axis 2 concerns postnatal day 4 — the perinatal / neonatal window.
Feature Pediatric AML Adult AML
Incidence ~500/yr US ~20,000/yr US
Median age varies by subgroup 68
Most common cytogenetics KMT2A rearrangements, CBF
fusions, NUP98 fusions
normal karyotype (~50%),
complex karyotype
Most common mutations KMT2A-r, WT1, KIT (in CBF),
CBFA2T3-GLIS2 (infant
AMKL)
NPM1, DNMT3A, FLT3,
IDH1/2, TET2
NPM1 frequency <10% ~30%
TP53 / complex karyotype rare common
5-year OS ~70% ~30% (lower in elderly)
Down syndrome AML (ML-DS) : trisomy 21 predisposes to transient abnormal myelopoiesis
(TAM), a neonatal pre-leukemic syndrome with megakaryoblastic blasts driven by the combination of trisomy 21 and acquired GATA1s mutations (exon 2 frameshift producing a short GATA1
isoform). TAM resolves spontaneously in ~80% of infants but progresses to ML-DS (predominantly
FAB M7, acute megakaryoblastic leukemia) in ~20%, typically within the first 4 years, with cooperating mutations in cohesin (STAG2, RAD21), CTCF, and the JAK-STAT pathway (JAK2, JAK3,
MPL, SH2B3). ML-DS has excellent outcomes (>90% cure) on reduced-intensity therapy . The
TAM￿ ML-DS progression is a unique window in which a perinatal hematopoietic disturbance
directly produces leukemia — conceptually adjacent to the Li et al. (2020 Cell Stem Cell) perinatal IFN / FLT3-ITD leukemogenesis model and to the student’s own P4 fetal-to-adult transition
biology .
Infant KMT2A AML: neonatal KMT2A-r AML (often MLLT3, MLLT10, AF10, ENL partners) arises
in utero (concordance studies in identical twins show prenatal origin) and is exceptionally aggressive. The menin–KMT2A–HOXA9 axis is the therapeutic vulnerability , and pediatric menininhibitor trials are now active.

sections.title: AML Connections to the Thesis T opics

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This sub-section is the committee centerpiece. Three interlocking connections — IFN, UPS, and
MPN-to-AML transformation — directly link AML to the student’s thesis.

subsections.title: IFN Signaling in AML
subsections.body:
Essers et al. (2009 Nature)showed that IFN-α activates dormant HSCs out of quiescence via STAT1–
SCA1–PKB signaling, with rapid Ki67 upregulation and cell-cycle entry within 24 hours. The biological corollary — that IFN-α could be used to drag LSCs out of chemo-protective quiescence

and sensitize them to cell-cycle-active cytotoxics — spawned clinical efforts (including pegylated
IFN-α in AML and CML-LSC eradication strategies), though routine clinical use in AML has been
limited by heterogeneous response and toxicity . The paper remains conceptually central to any
thesis that invokes IFN in HSC biology , and the committee chair (who studies IFN signaling) will
expect a deep command of it.
Li et al. (2020 Cell Stem Cell) is perhaps the single most important AML-side paper for this thesis.
The authors showed that a perinatal IFN pulse is PERMISSIVE for FLT3-ITD–driven leukemogenesis: FLT3-ITD knock-in mice do not develop leukemia efficiently in steady state, but perinatal
IFN exposure (mimicking neonatal infection or poly(I:C) stimulation) synergizes with FLT3-ITD
to drive AML. Mechanistically , IFN signaling in perinatal HSCs cooperates with FLT3-ITD-driven
STAT5 to reshape the transcriptional output toward a leukemogenic program. This paper creates
a direct bridge between her two thesis axes: Axis 1 (DCAF7-IFIT3 IFN regulation) and Axis 2
(DCAF7 requirement at P4). If DCAF7 restrains IFN signaling (by degrading IFIT3) during the
fetal-to-adult transition, then DCAF7 loss at P4 should phenocopy or amplify the permissive perinatal IFN state of Li 2020 — predicting that DCAF7-null perinatal hematopoiesis would be hypersusceptible to FLT3-ITD (or analogous) transformation. This is the hypothesis to state plainly in
committee.

subsections.title: UPS in AML
subsections.body:
• FBW7 (SCF^FBW7 CRL1): HSC/LSC quiescence via c-Myc, cyclin E, MCL-1, Notch ICD;
committee-chair territory .
• PML-RARA degradation : the curative UPS paradigm (RNF4, SIAH2; ATRA + ATO; Lo-Coco
2013 NEJM).
• CRBN (CRL4^CRBN): lenalidomide-driven IKZF1/3 degradation; molecular glue paradigm.
• HUWE1, ITCH, MDM2 : p53 family regulation.
• USP7, USP9X, USP28 : deubiquitinases active in MYC/p53 axes; drug targets.
• Pevonedistat: global CRL inactivation; research tool and failed drug (discussed I.7.6 and I.11).
DCAF7 is a CRL4 substrate receptor — the same family as CRBN. The student should be able to
state this fluently and draw the obvious analogy: just as CRL4^CRBN can be redirected by molecular glues to degrade neo-substrates (IKZF1, IKZF3, GSPT1, CK1α), CRL4^DCAF7 is conceptually
druggable by molecular glues if its substrate interface is defined. This is the translational horizon
of the thesis.

subsections.title: MPN-to-AML Transformation (Blast-Phase MPN)
subsections.body:
Approximately 5–15% of primary myelofibrosis (PMF) patients transform to blast-phase MPN
(MPN-BP) over 10 years; PV and ET transform at ~5% over the same interval. MPN-BP is biologically and clinically distinct from de novo AML:
• The founding JAK2V617F (or CALR, or MPL) mutation persists and is present in the transformed clone.
• Cooperating late hits at transformation disproportionately include TP53 (most common; ~30%),
IDH1/IDH2, SRSF2, ASXL1, RUNX1, and RAS.
• Complex karyotype is common.

• JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib) do not reverse blast phase and are not curative.
• Outcomes are poor: median OS 3–6 months; allo-HCT is the only potentially curative therapy ,
and MPN-BP patients have historically been excluded from de novo AML trials (Mascarenhas
2018 Blood, Blood 2018).
For this thesis, MPN-BP is the disease-progression destination for the chronic-phase JAK2V617F
MPN cells in which DCAF7 degrades IFIT3. The student should be prepared to speculate (cautiously) on whether DCAF7 / IFIT3 biology changes at transformation — for example, whether
MPN-BP clones lose DCAF7 regulation of IFIT3 and thereby escape IFN homeostasis, or conversely
whether DCAF7 activity is required to maintain chronic-phase stability and its loss precipitates
transformation.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Because the committee chair studies UPS in hematopoiesis, a concentrated UPS lens on AML is
essential.

subsections.title: Bortezomib in AML
subsections.body:
Bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor, approved in multiple myeloma and mantle cell lymphoma) was
tested in AML on the theory that blasts might share plasma-cell proteotoxic-stress sensitivity . COG
AAML1031 added bortezomib to standard induction/consolidation in pediatric AML — no OS
benefit, increased toxicity . Adult AML trials (including Attar 2012 Clin Cancer Res and others)
showed limited single-agent activity . The biological explanation is that AML blasts do not have
the immunoglobulin-biosynthetic burden that makes plasma cells uniquely proteasome-addicted;
AML proteostasis relies more on selective CRL-mediated turnover than on bulk proteasomal flux.

subsections.title: Pevonedistat (MLN4924)
subsections.body:
Pevonedistat is a first-in-class NEDD8-activating enzyme (NAE) inhibitor — it forms a covalent
NEDD8-pevonedistat adduct on UBA3 that is a dead-end substrate, preventing NEDD8 conjugation to cullins. Because neddylation is required for CRL E3 ligase activity , pevonedistat globally
inactivates all CRL1–CRL5 ligases. Soucy et al. (2009 Nature) introduced pevonedistat; early AML
data (Swords 2018 Blood) showed single-agent activity . The PEVENAZA (Pevonedistat 3001)
phase III in higher-risk MDS / CMML / low-blast AML plus azacitidine failed its primary eventfree survival endpoint in 2021, halting development.
Critical point for this thesis : pevonedistat’s clinical failure is irrelevant to its research utility .
Pevonedistat is THE canonical tool to globally stabilize CRL substrates for identification. In Aim
1b the student will treat JAK2V617F MPN cells (and wild-type controls) briefly with pevonedistat
(typically 1 µM × 1–4 hours; enough to deplete neddylated cullins and stabilize CRL substrates, not
long enough to induce adaptive responses), perform diGly-remnant ubiquitin proteomics (antiK-ε-GG IP followed by LC-MS/MS), and identify CRL substrates that accumulate with diminished ubiquitination. Parallel DCAF7 IP-MS with and without pevonedistat identifies DCAF7specific CRL4 substrates. IFIT3 is hypothesized to be among these. The committee chair will test
whether the student can articulate this experimental logic precisely — including appropriate con19

trols (MLN7243/TAK-243 as a pan-E1 ubiquitin inhibitor comparator; CRL4 pathway-specific genetic controls via DDB1 degradation or DCAF7 KO).

subsections.title: APL / ATRA / ATO as UPS-Driven
subsections.body:
Differentiation Therapy

APL is the proof-of-principle that ubiquitin-mediated destruction of a driver oncoprotein produces
clinical cure. RNF4 (SUMO-targeted ubiquitin ligase) and SIAH2 are the key E3s. The PML body
biology — PML nuclear bodies recruit SUMO machinery , p53, CBP , and transcriptional regulators
— is directly relevant to the UPS-in-hematopoiesis field and will appear in committee prompts.

subsections.title: Epigenetic-UPS Adjacencies
subsections.body:
PRMT5 inhibitors (GSK3326595, JNJ-64619178), DOT1L inhibitors (pinometostat; limited clinical
activity in KMT2A-r), EZH2 inhibitors (tazemetostat; active in EZH2-mut lymphomas, under
study in myeloid), menin inhibitors (revumenib) — all operate at the epigenetic/UPS interface.
Menin inhibitors in particular work by disrupting a protein–protein interaction that sustains
KMT2A-chromatin complexes; the downstream consequence includes differentiation-coupled
proteasomal turnover of HOXA9-program components.

sections.title: Integration: AML and the DCAF7 Thesis Proposal

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The AML literature converges on the thesis at three points that the student should articulate as the
closing move of this section — and of any committee discussion of AML.
First, the UPS is already the most successful therapeutic modality in AML (ATRA + ATO in
APL; lenalidomide-adjacent CRBN biology; menin inhibitors acting upstream of UPS-dependent
HOXA9-program dissolution). A thesis proposing that CRL4-DCAF7 is a previously unrecognized
substrate receptor regulating IFIT3 in myeloid disease is squarely in this tradition and carries translational optionality: if CRL4-DCAF7 is druggable as a glue surface, the same logic that gave us
lenalidomide and revumenib applies. The chairman’s UPS interest ensures this framing will resonate.
Second, the IFN literature in AML — Essers 2009 for HSC activation, and especially Li 2020 for
perinatal-IFN-permissive FLT3-ITD leukemogenesis — creates a direct conceptual bridge between Axes 1 and 2 of the thesis . If DCAF7 restrains IFN signaling by degrading IFIT3 during the
fetal-to-adult transition at P4, then the P4 phenotype of Axis 2 is mechanistically consistent with
the perinatal-IFN biology that Li 2020 identified as permissive for FLT3-ITD leukemia. This is a
unifying hypothesis: DCAF7 sets the perinatal IFN tone that gates both normal HSC maturation
and leukemia susceptibility . The committee chair — an IFN-in-hematopoiesis expert — will recognize this immediately and will probe it. The student should state it affirmatively and outline the
experiments (DCAF7 conditional KO × FLT3-ITD; perinatal IFN signature profiling in DCAF7-null
HSCs; IFIT3 stabilization via pevonedistat in perinatal hematopoiesis).
Third, the MPN-to-AML transformation axis (Mascarenhas 2018 Blood) is the diseaseprogression trajectory on which chronic-phase JAK2V617F MPN cells — the cells in which
DCAF7 degrades IFIT3 — ultimately land. The MPN-BP clone retains JAK2V617F and gains TP53,
IDH, SRSF2, or RAS hits. Whether DCAF7-IFIT3 biology is preserved, lost, or flipped at trans20

formation is an empirical question the thesis can address directly by comparing chronic-phase
and blast-phase patient samples, by profiling DCAF7 and IFIT3 in MPN-BP xenograft models,
and by asking whether acute DCAF7 loss in chronic-phase JAK2V617F MPN cells accelerates
transformation.
In sum, AML is not a tangential topic for this committee — it is a primary axis of evaluation. The
UPS contextualizes the mechanism (ATRA, pevonedistat, CRBN, menin), the IFN literature contextualizes the permissive signal (Essers, Li 2020), and the MPN-BP literature contextualizes the
disease trajectory of the chronic-phase cells she studies. A DCAF7 thesis that can speak this language fluently and identify the open questions at each intersection — LSC DCAF7 dependence,
perinatal IFN tone in DCAF7-null HSCs, MPN-BP DCAF7 dynamics, and CRL4-DCAF7 druggability — is a thesis that survives an AML-heavy qualifying exam.
[FIGURE: Integration schematic — CRL4-DCAF7-IFIT3 axis situated among AML UPS paradigms
(PML-RARA, CRBN/IKZF, FBW7/Myc), perinatal IFN leukemogenesis (Li 2020), and MPN-BP
transformation]
Key Citations
• Arber DA et al. 2016 Blood. PMID 27069254. WHO 2016 classification of myeloid neoplasms
and acute leukemia.
• Bonnet D & Dick JE. 1997 Nat Med. Human AML is organized as a hierarchy originating from
a primitive hematopoietic cell.
• DiNardo CD et al. 2020 NEJM. Azacitidine and venetoclax in previously untreated AML
(VIALE-A).
• Essers MAG et al. 2009 Nature. IFN-α activates dormant HSCs in vivo.
• Issa GC et al. 2023 Nature. The menin inhibitor revumenib in KMT2A-rearranged or NPM1mutant AML (AUGMENT-101).
• Jaiswal S et al. 2014 NEJM. Age-related clonal hematopoiesis and adverse outcomes (CHIP).
• Jan M et al. 2012 Sci Transl Med. Clonal evolution of preleukemic HSCs precedes human AML.
• Jan M & Majeti R. 2011 Sci Transl Med. Preleukemic HSCs in AML.
• Li Y et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell. Perinatal IFN is permissive for FLT3-ITD-driven leukemogenesis.
• Lo-Coco F et al. 2013 NEJM. ATRA and ATO for APL (APL0406 trial).
• Majeti R et al. 2009 Cell. CD47 is an LSC marker; anti-CD47 antibody therapy .
• Mascarenhas J et al. 2018 Blood. MPN blast-phase biology and outcomes.
• Ng SWK et al. 2016 Nature. LSC17 stem-cell gene-expression score.
• Shlush LI et al. 2014 Nature. Preleukemic HSCs in human AML carry DNMT3A mutations.
• Soucy TA et al. 2009 Nature. Pevonedistat (MLN4924): NEDD8-activating enzyme inhibitor.
• Wei AH et al. 2020 NEJM. Oral azacitidine maintenance in AML (QUAZAR AML-001).
• Ye H et al. 2016 Cell Stem Cell. Fatty-acid oxidation and LSC metabolism.
• Lagadinou ED et al. 2013 Cell Stem Cell. BCL-2 dependence of LSCs — rationale for venetoclax.
• Reavie L et al. 2013 Cancer Cell; Thompson BJ et al. 2008 J Exp Med. FBW7 in HSC/LSC quiescence.

Clonal Hematopoiesis and CHIP
Preamble: Why this section matters for the DCAF7 thesis
Clonal hematopoiesis (CH) is the conceptual bridge between normal aging hematopoiesis and
frank myeloid neoplasia, and it sits squarely at the intersection of the three pillars of this thesis:
hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) biology , the ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS), and interferon
(IFN) signaling. The committee chair studies UPS and IFN; the thesis proposes that DCAF7, a
CRL4 substrate receptor, promotes proteasomal degradation of IFIT3 in JAK2V617F-mutant MPN
cells and is separately required for the fetal-to-adult HSC transition. Because MPN almost always
arises from a pre-existing CH field, and because the drivers of CH (DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1 —
collectively “DTA”) are selected by the very inflammatory and IFN cues that the thesis invokes, no
committee examining this work will let the student past without probing the CH literature. This
section is designed to make the student fluent in CH nomenclature, epidemiology , mechanism,
and clinical translation, and — critically — to preempt the committee questions that connect CH
to the DCAF7 mechanism.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body: The terminology around pre-malignant clonal states is tightly defined and the committee will expect precise usage.

subsections.title: Clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP)
subsections.body:
CHIP was codified in a 2015 consensus proposal (Steensma 2015Blood). Three simultaneous criteria
must be met:
1. A somatic mutation in a gene recurrently mutated in hematologic neoplasia.
2. Variant allele fraction (V AF) at least 2 percent in peripheral blood (PB).
3. Absence of cytopenia and absence of diagnostic criteria for a hematologic malignancy , paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria, or monoclonal gammopathy .
The 2 percent V AF floor is not a biological threshold; it reflects the lower bound reliably called
by conventional next-generation sequencing at typical coverage depths (~500x). The name “indeterminate potential” deliberately mirrors MGUS (monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined
significance) and emphasizes that CHIP is a laboratory finding rather than a disease.

subsections.title: Clonal hematopoiesis (CH)
subsections.body:
CH is the broader umbrella term. It encompasses CHIP but also includes sub-2 percent V AF clones
detected by error-corrected sequencing (duplex sequencing, unique molecular identifier barcoding) and lineage-based readouts such as X-inactivation skewing or mosaic chromosomal alterations
(mCAs). When error-corrected sequencing is applied to unselected elderly individuals, essentially

everyone over 60 harbors detectable clonal mutations (Zink 2017 Blood; Young 2016 Nat Commun).
The committee may press on this: “Is CHIP really a distinct entity , or is it just the tip of a continuous iceberg?” The defensible answer is that the 2 percent threshold is operationally useful —
clones above it carry measurable clinical risk — but the underlying biology is a continuum.

subsections.title: Clonal cytopenia of undetermined significance (CCUS)
subsections.body:
CCUS = CHIP plus unexplained cytopenia that does not meet MDS diagnostic criteria (absent
definitive dysplasia, below diagnostic blast thresholds, without MDS-defining cytogenetic abnormalities). CCUS carries a substantially higher rate of progression to overt MDS/AML than CHIP
(roughly 10 percent per year for high-risk CCUS versus 0.5 to 1 percent per year for CHIP), and the
current ELN and IWG recommendations increasingly treat it as a pre-MDS state requiring closer
surveillance.

subsections.title: Idiopathic cytopenia of undetermined significance (ICUS) and idiopathic dysplasia of uncertain
subsections.body:
significance (IDUS)
• ICUS: unexplained cytopenia without clonality and without dysplasia.
• IDUS: morphologic dysplasia without cytopenia and without clonality .
These categories existed before CHIP was formalized and help triage patients with borderline findings. Adding mutational data to an ICUS patient can reclassify them as CCUS.

subsections.title: The CH-to-AML continuum
subsections.body:
The canonical schematic is:
Normal HSC compartment -> CH (sub-threshold) -> CHIP (V AF >= 2 percent, no cytopenia) ->
CCUS (adds cytopenia) -> MDS (adds dysplasia or defining cytogenetics) -> sAML (blasts >= 20
percent).
The parallel myeloproliferative arc is: CHIP with a JAK2, CALR, or MPL mutation -> overt MPN
(PV , ET, PMF) -> accelerated / blast phase MPN (MPN-AP/BP).
[FIGURE: J-1 — Continuum schematic from HSC pool through CH, CHIP , CCUS, MDS, and AML,
with a parallel track branching at JAK2/CALR/MPL toward PV/ET/PMF and then MPN-BP; each
transition labeled with approximate annual progression rates and the dominant co-operating hits
that drive each step.]

subsections.title: IPSS-M
subsections.body:
The revised International Prognostic Scoring System — Molecular (IPSS-M; Bernard 2022 NEJM
Evidence) supplanted IPSS-R for MDS risk stratification. IPSS-M integrates 31 gene mutations with
clinical and cytogenetic variables, reclassifying about 46 percent of patients relative to IPSS-R. The
gene weights reflect CH biology: TP53-multihit, FLT3, and RUNX1 are heavily penalized; SF3B1
(in isolated form) is favorable. Committee relevance: IPSS-M operationalizes the insight that CH

genotypes matter at the MDS stage in the same way they matter in the pre-malignant CHIP/CCUS
stage.

subsections.title: The founding age epidemiology
subsections.body:
The two back-to-back New England Journal of Medicine papers from 2014 remain the reference points
every committee expects a student to cite by first author.
• Jaiswal 2014 NEJM (PMID 25426837): exome sequencing of 17,182 people without known hematologic disease. CHIP prevalence was under 1 percent below age 40, roughly 10 percent between 70 and 79, and greater than 18 percent above age 90. CHIP carriers had elevated all-cause
mortality (hazard ratio ~1.4) and an ~11-fold increased risk of hematologic malignancy , though
the absolute rate remained low (~0.5 to 1 percent per year).
• Genovese 2014 NEJM (PMID 25426838): parallel exome study of 12,380 Swedes, with concordant prevalence and progression rates.

subsections.title: Detection depth dictates apparent prevalence
subsections.body:
Exome-level sensitivity (~5 to 10 percent V AF floor) undercalls CH dramatically . Zink 2017 Blood
used deeper sequencing in the deCODE cohort and found that by age 85 more than half of individuals had detectable somatic clones. Error-corrected duplex sequencing can identify clones at V AF
below 0.1 percent, at which point essentially every adult over 60 has measurable CH. The committee may ask: “What is the ‘true’ prevalence of CH?” The correct epistemology is that prevalence is
a function of the assay floor, not a fixed biological number.

subsections.title: Demographic patterns
subsections.body:
• Sex: CHIP is modestly more common in males, independent of age.
• Ancestry: age-adjusted prevalence differs across populations, with some studies reporting
lower CHIP rates in self-reported Black participants and differences in driver-gene spectrum
(Bick 2020 Nature). These differences are partly explained by diet, smoking, and chronic
inflammation exposure.
• Smoking: accelerates CH expansion, particularly for ASXL1.
• Prior cytotoxic therapy: massively enriches for PPM1D and TP53 clones (see J3.5, J4.3).
[FIGURE: J-2 — Prevalence of CH/CHIP versus age across three detection regimes: exome (~5
percent V AF floor), targeted deep sequencing (~1 percent), and error-corrected duplex sequencing
(~0.01 percent); shaded bands illustrate how the apparent prevalence diverges with increasing
assay sensitivity .]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The CH driver spectrum is heavily skewed, with three genes accounting for roughly two-thirds of
all CHIP cases. Memorize the top six and their distinguishing features.

subsections.title: DNMT3A
subsections.body:
• Frequency: 40 to 50 percent of CHIP mutations — by far the most common.
• Function: de novo DNA methyltransferase; writes methylation at CpG sites, especially at enhancers and intergenic regions.
• Hotspot: R882 (most commonly R882H), which produces a dominant-negative protein that
forms non-functional tetramers with wild-type DNMT3A and reduces cellular methyltransferase activity by more than 80 percent even in heterozygous cells.
• Non-hotspot lesions: truncating mutations distributed across the gene are also common and
produce haploinsufficiency .
• CH biology: DNMT3A-mutant HSCs have subtle self-renewal advantage, blunted differentiation bias, and — critically for this thesis — relative resistance to IFN-driven depletion.

subsections.title: TET2
subsections.body:
• Frequency: 15 to 20 percent.
• Function: alpha-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase that converts 5-methylcytosine to
5-hydroxymethylcytosine, driving active DNA demethylation.
• Lesions: predominantly loss-of-function (nonsense, frameshift, splice), distributed across the
gene — no dominant hotspot.
• Cooperativity: TET2 loss cooperates with a wide range of secondary hits (FLT3, NPM1, JAK2,
RUNX1, SRSF2) and is the most promiscuous “first hit” in myeloid neoplasia.

subsections.title: ASXL1
subsections.body:
• Frequency: 5 to 10 percent.
• Function: component of the Polycomb repressive deubiquitinase (PR-DUB) complex with
BAP1; regulates H2AK119 ubiquitination.
• Hotspot: G646W (a recurrent frameshift at a homopolymer tract that was initially controversial
as a sequencing artifact but is now accepted as a bona fide driver).
• Clinical behavior: ASXL1 CH is particularly responsive to the inflammatory bone marrow environment and carries a higher-than-average progression risk for its V AF.

subsections.title: JAK2 V617F
subsections.body:
• Frequency: 2 to 5 percent of CHIP .
• Function: V617F in JH2 pseudokinase domain disinhibits JAK2 kinase activity , producing
cytokine-independent STAT signaling.
• Clinical spectrum: the same JAK2V617F allele causes CHIP , PV , ET, and PMF depending on
V AF, cooperating hits, and host context.

• Direct thesis relevance: the DCAF7 mechanism in MPN is proposed to operate via IFIT3 degradation in JAK2V617F-bearing cells. Nearly all JAK2V617F MPN patients have a pre-existing or
co-resident DTA clone, which is the crux of the translational risk discussed in J7 and J11.

subsections.title: PPM1D
subsections.body:
• Frequency: rises steeply with prior cytotoxic chemotherapy exposure.
• Function: PP2C-family serine/threonine phosphatase that dephosphorylates and thereby inactivates p53, CHK1, CHK2, ATM, and gamma-H2AX — a negative feedback on the DDR.
• Lesions: truncating mutations in the terminal exon 6 that remove a degron but retain and hyperactivate the phosphatase domain.
• Significance: PPM1D-mutant HSCs survive chemotherapy-induced apoptosis and expand in
therapy-related settings. They are a leading driver of therapy-related MDS/AML.

subsections.title: TP53
subsections.body:
• Frequency: low in unselected aging but greatly enriched after radiation and alkylator exposure.
• Function: canonical tumor suppressor; most CH mutations are missense in the DNA-binding
domain.
• Risk: the highest per-V AF progression risk among common CH drivers; often multi-hit in
MDS/AML with complex karyotype.

subsections.title: Spliceosome genes — SF3B1, SRSF2, U2AF1, ZRSR2
subsections.body:
• SF3B1: canonical driver of MDS with ring sideroblasts (MDS-RS) via mis-splicing of mitochondrial iron genes (ABCB7, among others). In isolation SF3B1 carries favorable prognosis; in
combination with other mutations the prognosis darkens.
• SRSF2: associated with older age, a particularly aggressive CH trajectory , and strong
co-occurrence with TET2 in CMML.
• U2AF1 (S34F, Q157): similar trajectory to SRSF2.

subsections.title: Less common but functionally important drivers
subsections.body:
• GNB1: gain-of-function; activates PI3K and MAPK downstream; occasionally seen as a CH
driver with lineage bias.
• CBL: E3 ligase loss-of-function; activates cytokine receptor signaling (a UPS connection the
committee may find appealing).
• NF1: RAS-pathway activation; rare as a CH driver but important in JMML and overlap disorders.
• Cohesin complex (STAG2, RAD21): occasional CH drivers; more common as secondary hits in
MDS/AML.
• IDH1/IDH2: uncommon in CH, common in AML; produce 2-hydroxyglutarate and epigenetic
rewiring.

the three DTA genes shaded as the dominant segment and inset bar charts comparing gene-specific

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A CH clone expands because its mutation confers a positive selection coefficient in a competitive
HSC pool. The selection coefficient is context-dependent, and a central insight of the last five years
is that the context often involves inflammation — the same signaling axis central to this thesis.

subsections.title: Intrinsic fitness (DNMT3A, TET2)
subsections.body:
DNMT3Aand TET2-mutant HSCs show small but real intrinsic advantages per division: blunted
differentiation commitment, modestly enhanced self-renewal, and resistance to exhaustion. Mathematical modeling (Watson 2020Science; Fabre 2022 Nature) fits observed clonal trajectories to selection coefficients of a few percent per year, which compound over decades. Crucially , the selection
coefficient is largely constant across adult life for DNMT3A, producing the characteristic linear-toexponential expansion seen on longitudinal sequencing.

subsections.title: Niche and age-related advantage (ASXL1, SRSF2)
subsections.body:
Some drivers show age-dependent selection coefficients: the ASXL1 or SRSF2 clone that is neutral
at 40 becomes positively selected at 70 because the bone marrow niche itself has changed. This is
the cellular substrate of “inflammaging” — chronic elevation of IL-6, TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma, and
damage-associated molecular patterns in the aged marrow — and the selected mutants are those
best able to tolerate or exploit that milieu.

subsections.title: Therapy-related fitness (PPM1D, TP53, CHEK2)
subsections.body:
Cytotoxic chemotherapy and ionizing radiation impose intense negative selection on normal
HSCs via DDR-triggered apoptosis and senescence. Mutations that disable this axis — PPM1D
truncations, TP53 missense, CHEK2 LOF — allow a few mutant clones to survive and repopulate. Coombs 2017 Cell Stem Cell documented this phenomenon in solid tumor patients
post-chemotherapy and established therapy-related CH as a distinct entity with its own risk
profile.

subsections.title: Infection and inflammation-driven selection (THESIS-ADJACENT)
subsections.body:
This is the mechanism the committee chair will zero in on.
• Hormaechea-Agulla 2021 Cell Stem Cell showed that TET2-mutant HSCs are resistant to IFNgamma-driven proliferative stress and therefore gain a selective advantage under Mycobacterium avium infection. The mechanism involves blunted IFN-gamma-responsive gene expression, which preserves HSC quiescence while wild-type cells are driven into cycle and exhaustion.
• Avagyan 2021 Science used a zebrafish model and human data to show that chronic inflammation selectively expands DNMT3A-mutant HSCs. This is the seminal direct demonstration that
an IFN-driven inflammatory signal is a positive selection pressure for a DTA clone.

• Caiado 2023 Blood extended this to show that DNMT3A-mutant expansion is inflammationdependent in mouse models, and that anti-inflammatory interventions can blunt clonal drift.
• SanMiguel 2022 Cancer Discovery and others have identified TNF-alpha as a parallel selector for
TET2 mutants.
The unifying picture: DTA mutants are IFN/inflammation-tolerant, wild-type HSCs are
IFN/inflammation-sensitive, and chronic elevation of these cytokines converts the bone marrow
into a selection chamber that preferentially expands DTA clones.

subsections.title: Mathematical modeling
subsections.body:
Two frameworks dominate.
• Watson 2020 Science: neutral-drift plus fitness-coefficient models fit to longitudinal V AF trajectories. Estimates a distribution of fitness coefficients across genes; DNMT3A is lower, spliceosome genes higher.
• Fabre 2022 Nature: ultra-deep longitudinal sequencing demonstrates that most trajectories are
deterministic (stable exponential growth), not stochastic, over decades.
The practical implication is that a single V AF measurement contains enough information — given
the gene identity — to forecast expansion with useful precision.

subsections.title: Cardiovascular disease
subsections.body:
The most clinically impactful non-malignant consequence of CHIP is cardiovascular.
• Jaiswal 2017 NEJM: CHIP carriers had a 1.9-fold higher risk of coronary heart disease and a 4fold higher risk of early-onset myocardial infarction (before age 50), independent of traditional
risk factors including LDL, smoking, hypertension, and diabetes.
• Fuster 2017 Science: TET2 loss in hematopoietic cells of Ldlr-knockout mice accelerated
atherosclerosis via NLRP3 inflammasome activation and IL-1beta secretion from TET2-mutant
macrophages.
• Mechanism: TET2-null macrophages infiltrating atherosclerotic plaques show elevated
IL-1beta, IL-6, and chemokine production; the inflammatory amplification drives plaque
progression and instability .
• Therapeutic implication: the CANTOS trial (Ridker 2017 NEJM, post-hoc analysis Svensson
2022 JAMA Cardiol) showed that the IL-1beta-blocking antibody canakinumab reduced cardiovascular events preferentially in TET2-CHIP carriers, providing a human proof-of-concept for
the mechanism.

subsections.title: Heart failure
subsections.body:
CHIP worsens heart failure outcomes, particularly post-myocardial-infarction HF.
• Dorsheimer 2019 JAMA Cardiol : CHIP carriers with ischemic HF had worse progression-free
and overall survival.
• Abplanalp 2021 Circulation: DNMT3A-mutant monocytes in HF patients exhibit a proinflammatory transcriptional signature; single-cell analysis shows preferential expansion of
the DNMT3A clone within circulating monocytes of HF patients.

subsections.title: All-cause mortality
subsections.body:
Across population cohorts, CHIP carries a hazard ratio for all-cause mortality of roughly 1.3 to 1.5,
largely attributable to the cardiovascular increment rather than to hematologic malignancy , which
remains rare in absolute terms.

subsections.title: Hematologic malignancy progression
subsections.body:
• Overall rate: 0.5 to 1 percent per year CHIP-to-MN conversion.
• Risk modifiers: V AF (>10 percent carries higher risk), number of mutations (two or more substantially elevates risk), specific genes (TP53, spliceosome genes, and U2AF1 are high-risk), and
presence of cytopenia (CCUS versus CHIP).
• Stratification tools: the CHRS (section J6) operationalizes these risk modifiers.

subsections.title: Solid tumors
subsections.body: Evidence is mixed. Several studies have associated CHIP with increased incidence of therapyrelated myeloid neoplasms following solid-tumor treatment (especially with platinum or alkylators). Associations with incident solid tumors per se are weaker and confounded by shared exposures (smoking, age).

subsections.title: Autoimmunity and inflammation
subsections.body:
CHIP is enriched in patients with cytopenias of presumed autoimmune etiology , and UBA1 mutations drive the VEXAS syndrome (vacuoles, E1 enzyme, X-linked, autoinflammatory , somatic),
which is a distinct somatic-mosaicism disease whose CH-like architecture has reshaped thinking
about adult-onset autoinflammation (Beck 2020 NEJM). VEXAS is UPS-relevant and worth flagging
for the committee chair: UBA1 is the ubiquitin E1, and its loss in myeloid progenitors produces
systemic inflammation — a direct intersection of UPS biology and CH.

subsections.title: COVID-19 severity
subsections.body:
Bolton 2021 Nat Commun reported that CHIP carriers had worse COVID-19 outcomes, consistent
with the broader picture of CH as a dysregulated immune-compartment state, although subsequent larger cohorts have attenuated the effect size.

[FIGURE: J-4 — Forest plot of hazard ratios for CHIP across outcomes (all-cause mortality , MI,
stroke, HF, incident MDS/AML, COVID-19 severity), with point estimates and 95 percent confidence intervals drawn from the cited primary literature.]

subsections.title: Clonal Hematopoiesis Risk Score (CHRS)
subsections.body:
Weeks 2023NEJM Evidence developed and validated the CHRS to predict CHIP-to-MN progression.
Inputs include:
• Age.
• Presence of cytopenia (red cell distribution width, mean corpuscular volume, cytopenia grade).
• Number of mutations.
• Maximum V AF.
• Gene identity , with high-risk genes (TP53, JAK2, spliceosome genes, RUNX1, IDH1/2, FLT3,
NRAS, RAS-pathway) weighted more heavily .
Output: three risk strata (low, intermediate, high) with 10-year MN incidence ranging from under
1 percent to greater than 50 percent. The CHRS is now the field-standard tool and should be named
by name in any committee discussion of CH risk stratification.

subsections.title: Clinical practice
subsections.body:
CHIP screening is not routine outside of research or specific settings (pre-transplant donor evaluation, post-chemotherapy surveillance, unexplained cytopenia workup). Prospective observational
cohorts (IMPACT, NIH CHIVE, and institution-specific clinics at Dana-Farber, MSKCC, and Washington University) are defining the natural history and the role of interventions.

subsections.title: Interventions under study
subsections.body:
• IL-1beta/IL-6 axis blockade (canakinumab, ziltivekimab) — primary for the cardiovascular indication.
• Anti-inflammatory lifestyle measures — statins, Mediterranean diet, smoking cessation — with
preliminary evidence of slowed clonal expansion.
• Disease-specific therapies for CCUS progressing toward MDS — largely supportive and
clinical-trial-driven.

sections.title: Inflammation, aging, and CHIP — DIRECT THESIS RELEVANCE

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This subsection is the single most likely origin of committee questions, because it connects the three
pillars of the thesis (HSC, UPS, IFN) to the CH literature through a chain the chair studies directly .

subsections.title: Inflammaging
subsections.body:
The aged bone marrow exhibits elevated IL-6, IL-1beta, TNF-alpha, and a Type I and Type II IFN
interferon-stimulated gene (ISG) signature. Cellular sources include senescent stromal cells, clonally expanded myeloid cells, and an expanded regulatory T-cell compartment. The composite
picture is a chronic low-grade inflammatory tone that changes HSC fate decisions.

subsections.title: IFN-gamma selects for DNMT3A and protects TET2
subsections.body:
• Avagyan 2021 Science: in zebrafish and human datasets, chronic inflammation drives expansion of DNMT3A-mutant HSCs via IFN-gamma signaling. DNMT3A loss de-represses
inflammation-responsive genes in HSCs such that mutant cells tolerate IFN-gamma exposure
while wild-type cells are driven into exhaustion.
• Hormaechea-Agulla 2021 Cell Stem Cell: TET2-mutant HSCs are resistant to IFN-gamma-driven
proliferative stress and gain fitness under infection (M. avium model). The mechanism involves
blunted ISG induction in the mutant HSC.
The generalizable principle: DTA mutants are IFN-refractory; wild-type HSCs are IFN-responsive.
Under chronic IFN tone, the mutant outcompetes wild-type.

subsections.title: T ype I IFN in MPN therapy
subsections.body:
IFN-alpha (pegylated IFN-alpha-2a, ropeginterferon alfa-2b) is a mainstay of MPN therapy . Key
observations:
• IFN-alpha preferentially reduces JAK2V617F allele burden, sometimes producing complete
molecular remission.
• IFN-alpha does not reduce — and may transiently expand — DTA clones (DNMT3A, TET2).
• Hasan 2013 Blood and subsequent longitudinal studies of IFN-treated MPN show that patients
with DTA + JAK2V617F clonal architectures are less likely to achieve durable molecular remission, because the DTA reservoir can seed JAK2V617F re-emergence.
• Mechanistically , IFN-alpha drives wild-type and JAK2V617F HSCs out of quiescence into cycle,
where they are selectively depleted; DTA HSCs are IFN-refractory and stay in quiescence.

subsections.title: Explicit connection to the DCAF7 thesis
subsections.body:
If the thesis is correct that DCAF7 degrades IFIT3 in JAK2V617F cells, then pharmacologic
DCAF7 inhibition would stabilize IFIT3 and potentiate IFN-driven antiproliferative and antiviral
programs specifically within the JAK2V617F clone. This creates a therapeutic gradient:

• JAK2V617F clone: DCAF7 inhibition + IFN -> maximal IFIT3 accumulation -> enhanced depletion. Good.
• Co-resident DTA clone: DCAF7 inhibition is mechanistically agnostic here; but the IFN component of any combination regimen is a known positive selector of DNMT3A (Avagyan 2021)
and neutral-to-favorable for TET2 (Hormaechea-Agulla 2021). Bad.
This asymmetry predicts that DCAF7-based therapy will produce deeper JAK2V617F responses
but will not touch — and may worsen — the DTA reservoir, setting up the relapse dynamic welldocumented with IFN-alpha monotherapy . The committee will want to hear this explicitly acknowledged and a proposed experimental or clinical strategy for monitoring it (see J11).

subsections.title: Broader UPS-IFN-CH triangle
subsections.body:
The committee chair’s interest in UPS plus IFN invites a broader framing. Several pieces fit together:
• ISG15 and its conjugation machinery (UBE1L/UBA7, UBC8, HERC5) function as IFN-induced
ubiquitin-like tags. ISGylation is reversed by USP18, which is also IFN-induced and is the
principal negative regulator of Type I IFN signaling.
• IFIT family proteins (IFIT1, 2, 3) are ISGs; IFIT3 scaffolds MA VS-dependent RIG-I signaling
and amplifies Type I IFN. Stabilization of IFIT3 (as proposed in the thesis) would be expected
to raise steady-state IFN signaling.
• CRL4 substrate receptors (DCAFs) as a class regulate multiple ISGs; DCAF1 (VprBP), DCAF11,
and DCAF15 are all established or candidate regulators of innate immunity .
• VEXAS (UBA1 loss) is an acquired somatic UPS lesion that produces CH-like architecture and
systemic IFN-driven inflammation.
A confident student will sketch this triangle unprompted when asked “how does your thesis relate
to the chair’s interests?”
[FIGURE: J-5 — Triangle diagram with vertices UPS (CRL4-DCAF7, VEXAS-UBA1, ISG15/USP18),
IFN signaling (Type I, Type II, ISG signature, IFIT3), and CH drivers (DTA, JAK2V617F), with
arrows annotated by selected citations indicating the direction of causal or correlational evidence.]

subsections.title: Donor-derived CH
subsections.body:
Roughly 1 to 2 percent of allogeneic HSC donors — and up to 5 to 10 percent of older donors
— carry CHIP . A donor clone transplanted into a recipient often expands due to the proliferative
demand of engraftment, producing “donor cell leukemia” in rare but documented cases. Newell
2023 Blood and Gibson 2022 JCO quantified these risks.

subsections.title: CH in autologous stem cell transplantation
subsections.body:
Gibson 2017 JCO showed that CHIP detected in autograft material was associated with an elevated
risk of therapy-related MDS/AML and worse overall survival in lymphoma patients undergoing
autologous HSC transplant. The autograft is a concentrated sample of the patient’s HSC pool postchemotherapy , and TP53 and PPM1D clones within it are particularly high-risk.

subsections.title: Implications for the fetal-to-adult transition arm of the thesis
subsections.body: The second thesis pillar — DCAF7 required for fetal-to-adult HSC transition — has indirect transplant relevance. Any agent that modulates HSC transition programs has to be carefully assessed for effects on transplantable HSC function. Pre-clinical models in the thesis should therefore include a transplant readout with serial dilution, secondary transplant, and lineage-output measurements, because defects in fetal-adult transition might manifest as engraftment or multi-lineagereconstitution deficits.

subsections.title: Order of acquisition
subsections.body:
Ortmann 2015 NEJM showed in MPN that the order in which JAK2 and TET2 are acquired shapes
disease behavior. JAK2-first patients present younger, have higher likelihood of PV phenotype,
and respond differently to therapy compared with TET2-first patients, who skew older and toward
ET. The mechanistic explanation is that the pre-existing clonal substrate (TET2-mutant HSC pool
versus wild-type HSC pool) modulates the JAK2V617F phenotype.

subsections.title: JAK2V617F can be acquired in utero
subsections.body:
• Van Egeren 2021 Cell Stem Cell : single-cell phylogenies constructed from MPN patient HSC
colonies identified JAK2V617F acquisition events decades before clinical diagnosis, with several patients showing acquisition in utero.
• Williams 2022 Nature: extended the approach across MPN patients and confirmed that
JAK2V617F is frequently a childhood or in-utero event that drifts clonally for 30 to 50 years
before producing overt MPN.
• Implication for the fetal-to-adult transition pillar: if DCAF7 is required for normal fetal-adult
transition and is dysregulated or cooperative with JAK2V617F-driven clonal drift, then
JAK2V617F CH in children and young adults is a clinically silent but mechanistically relevant
target population.

subsections.title: MPN genomic classification
subsections.body:
Grinfeld 2018 NEJM applied unsupervised genomic classification to ~2000 MPN patients and defined 8 genomic subgroups that predict prognosis better than the WHO morphologic categories.

Key groupings include:
• TP53 group: highest risk, rapid progression to MPN-BP .
• Chromatin/spliceosome group (ASXL1, SRSF2, U2AF1, EZH2, IDH1/2, SF3B1): intermediate
risk, PMF enrichment.
• JAK2-only or CALR-only groups: favorable.
• DNMT3A/TET2-with-JAK2 group: intermediate; the DTA-plus-JAK2 architecture most common in PV and ET.

subsections.title: The MPN-as-late-CH framing
subsections.body:
The unifying picture is that MPN is not a sudden-onset disease but a late clinical manifestation
of a lifelong CH trajectory . JAK2V617F is the match; the DTA substrate is the kindling; aging
and inflammation are the bellows. This framing is now standard and should be recited to any
committee member who asks about MPN natural history .
[FIGURE: J-6 — Lifetime trajectory diagram: in-utero JAK2V617F acquisition, decades of silent
clonal drift at low V AF, inflection at an aging-associated inflection point, overt MPN diagnosis,
subsequent risk of MPN-BP on acquisition of TP53/RAS/IDH hits.]

sections.title: CHIP to MPN to AML progression

subsections.title: Progression rates
subsections.body:
• CHIP to MN overall: 0.5 to 1 percent per year.
• CCUS to MDS: up to 10 percent per year for high-risk CCUS.
• MPN to MPN-BP: roughly 1 to 3 percent per year cumulative, varying by subtype — lower for
PV and ET (~5 percent lifetime), higher for PMF (10 to 15 percent lifetime).
• MDS to sAML: risk scaled by IPSS-M risk stratum; high-risk MDS has median time to AML of
under 12 months.

subsections.title: Cooperating hits that drive transformation
subsections.body:
The transition from MPN or MDS to overt AML almost always involves acquisition of additional
genetic hits in one or more of:
• TP53 (high-risk; often multi-hit; complex karyotype).
• RAS pathway (NRAS, KRAS, CBL, PTPN11, NF1, RIT1).
• IDH1/IDH2.
• Spliceosome (SRSF2, U2AF1).
• Transcription factors (RUNX1, CEBPA).
• Signaling (FLT3-ITD, FLT3-TKD).
Rampal 2014 Blood characterized the mutational landscape of MPN blast-phase disease and identified TP53 and RAS-pathway acquisition as the dominant transformation routes. Caveat noted

from citation-audit discussion: the Rampal 2014 paper’s full text does not reference DCAF7 specifically; any thesis discussion that invokes Rampal should cite it for the transformation-mutation
landscape only , not for any DCAF7-specific claim.

subsections.title: Clinical risk stratification for progression
subsections.body:
For CHIP and CCUS, the CHRS (J6.1) provides probabilistic guidance. For MPN, the MIPSS70+
v2.0 (for PMF) and MYSEC-PM (for post-PV and post-ET myelofibrosis) incorporate mutational
data. For MDS, IPSS-M (J1.6) is the current standard.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Clonal hematopoiesis is the substrate on which MPN is built, and any mechanism-to-therapy story
for JAK2V617F MPN has to be told against the backdrop of the underlying DTA CH field. The
DCAF7 thesis must account for three realities: (1) the most common clonal architecture in PV and
ET is DTA + JAK2V617F, not JAK2V617F alone; (2) IFN-based interventions target JAK2V617F cells
but select for DTA cells; and (3) any combination regimen that relies on IFN-mediated execution
of the DCAF7-sensitized clone risks expanding the DTA reservoir that seeds relapse. Fluency with
the CH literature, the DTA-IFN selection axis, and the CHRS risk stratification framework is nonnegotiable for the qualifying exam, and explicit acknowledgment of the translational risk — not
avoidance of it — is the mark of a prepared candidate.
Essential citations to recall by first author, year, and journal
• Steensma 2015 Blood — CHIP consensus definition.
• Jaiswal 2014 NEJM (PMID 25426837) — founding CHIP epidemiology .
• Genovese 2014 NEJM (PMID 25426838) — parallel CHIP epidemiology .
• Zink 2017 Blood — deeper-sequencing prevalence in deCODE.
• Jaiswal 2017 NEJM — CHIP and cardiovascular disease.
• Fuster 2017 Science — TET2-macrophage IL-1beta in atherosclerosis.
• Dorsheimer 2019 JAMA Cardiology — CHIP and heart failure.
• Abplanalp 2021 Circulation — DNMT3A monocytes in HF.
• Bolton 2021 Nature Communications — CHIP and COVID-19.
• Coombs 2017 Cell Stem Cell — therapy-related CH (PPM1D, TP53).
• Avagyan 2021 Science — chronic inflammation selects DNMT3A clones.
• Hormaechea-Agulla 2021 Cell Stem Cell — TET2 IFN-gamma resistance.
• Caiado 2023 Blood — DNMT3A inflammation-dependence.
• SanMiguel 2022 Cancer Discovery — TNF-alpha selects TET2.
• Watson 2020 Science — CH fitness-coefficient modeling.
• Fabre 2022 Nature — longitudinal deterministic CH trajectories.
• Weeks 2023 NEJM Evidence — CHRS risk score.
• Bernard 2022 NEJM Evidence — IPSS-M.
• Ortmann 2015 NEJM — JAK2/TET2 order of acquisition in MPN.

• Van Egeren 2021 Cell Stem Cell — single-cell MPN phylogenies; in-utero JAK2V617F.
• Williams 2022 Nature — lifetime MPN phylogenies.
• Grinfeld 2018 NEJM — MPN genomic classification.
• Gibson 2017 JCO — auto-HCT CH and tMDS risk.
• Beck 2020 NEJM — VEXAS syndrome (UBA1).
• Rampal 2014 Blood — MPN blast-phase genomics (cite for transformation-mutation landscape;
does not contain DCAF7-specific content — student should flag this at the exam).
[FIGURE: J-7 — One-page synoptic figure combining the CH-to-MN continuum, the DTA-IFN
selection axis, the UPS-IFN-CH triangle, and the predicted differential response of JAK2V617F
versus DTA clones to DCAF7 inhibition with or without IFN combination, serving as a single-slide
answer to the integrative committee question.]
Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems
Preface
This section is the methodological backbone of the thesis. The central biological claims in the proposal — that DCAF7 regulates hematopoietic stem and progenitor cell (HSPC) function in homeostasis, stress, and the Jak2V617F-driven myeloproliferative neoplasm (MPN) context — rest entirely on the integrity of conditional mouse genetics. The committee will expect the candidate to
have a working mastery of Cre-lox biology , a nuanced appreciation of Cre driver selection, and a
clear articulation of why certain drivers are used and others are explicitly avoided. Given that the
thesis centers on interferon (IFN)-responsive biology , the selection of Cre systems is not a technical
detail but a scientific necessity . This section closes with committee-style probe questions embedded throughout and a consolidated list at the end.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Biochemistry and site recognition
The Cre (cyclization recombinase) enzyme is a 38-kDa tyrosine recombinase derived from the P1
bacteriophage. It recognizes and recombines a 34-base-pair (bp) DNA target termed the loxP (locus of crossover of P1) site. Each loxP contains two 13-bp palindromic arms flanking an 8-bp
asymmetric spacer. The spacer defines the directionality of the site — which is the key feature the
experimentalist exploits.
Depending on the relative orientation of a pair of loxP sites, Cre catalyzes one of three reactions:
1. Excision — when two loxP sites are in parallel (direct repeat) orientation on the same DNA
molecule, Cre excises the intervening DNA as a circular product, leaving a single loxP scar.
This is the basis of conditional knockout (cKO) alleles.
2. Inversion — when two loxP sites are in antiparallel orientation, Cre inverts the intervening
segment. Used in FLEx switches and Brainbow reporters.

3. T ranslocation / integration — when loxP sites are on separate DNA molecules, Cre can catalyze intermolecular recombination; used in some genetic-trap or knock-in strategies and for
reconstruction of reciprocal translocations in leukemia models.
Mutant lox variants
Cre uses complementary spacer recognition, so lox sites with mutant spacers (lox2272, loxN,
lox5171, among others) recombine efficiently with themselves but not with wild-type loxP .
These variants enable multiplexed genetic strategies — the Brainbow/Confetti system (Snippert
2010 Cell) is the canonical application, where stochastic recombination among a cassette of
incompatible lox sites produces four fluorescent outcomes for clonal tracing.
Floxed allele architecture
A standard conditional (floxed) allele places two loxP sites in parallel orientation around a “critical
exon” — an exon whose loss creates a frameshift-induced premature termination codon (PTC) or
deletes an indispensable protein domain. Before Cre exposure, the locus is transcribed and spliced
normally; the loxP sites are placed in introns at a sufficient distance from splice acceptor/donor
sites that they do not perturb splicing. After Cre exposure, the exon is excised, and the resulting
transcript is either nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) substrate or encodes a null protein.
“Floxed” is community shorthand for “flanked by loxP .” In this proposal, “Dcaf7-floxed” refers to
the lab’s conditional Dcaf7 allele in which a critical exon (or set of exons) is flanked by loxP sites;
Cre activity converts it to a null.
Conditional versus constitutive
A floxed allele on its own is typically a near-wild-type allele (assuming the loxP insertions do not
disrupt regulatory elements — a point to genotype and test for). The phenotype is entirely dictated
by the Cre-driver transgene that delivers recombinase activity in a defined tissue and temporal
window. The choice of Cre driver therefore determines what question is being asked.
Committee Q: “Have you confirmed that the floxed, uninduced Dcaf7 allele behaves as a true wild type?”
— The candidate should note: RT-qPCR and western to verify unaltered Dcaf7 levels in floxed
homozygotes without Cre; compare HSC numbers and lineage output to true wild-type C57BL/6J
controls at steady state.

sections.title: Hematopoietic Cre Drivers — A Comparative Catalog

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Cre-driver selection is the single most consequential decision in any conditional mouse study . Below is a comparative catalog of drivers relevant to hematopoiesis, emphasizing why each would
or would not be appropriate for the Dcaf7 proposal.

T able K2.1 — Hematopoietic Cre drivers

germline leak

(Tek-Cre)

subsections.title: Endothelial +
subsections.body:
hematopoietic
Constitutive Pan-endothelial
Csf1r-Cre Fetal onward Myeloid-biased Constitutive Macrophage leak
outside marrow
LysM-Cre Postnatal Monocyte/granulocyte Constitutive Incomplete in
some myeloid
subsets

HSC
Constitutive History-marker;
cells previously
Flt3+
TetO-Cre with
rtTA
Inducible Depends on rtTA Doxycycline Slow washout,
leaky
Vav1-Cre (de Boer 2003 Eur J Immunol)
Vav1-Cre is the workhorse pan-hematopoietic constitutive driver. The transgene uses the human
V A V1 regulatory region to drive Cre expression in hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells starting approximately E11.5–E12.5, coincident with the emergence of definitive HSCs from the aortagonad-mesonephros (AGM) region. It recombines floxed alleles in essentially all hematopoietic
lineages by the perinatal stage.
Known caveats:
• Vav1 regulatory elements drive some expression in endothelium and a subset of germ cells.
Germline transmission of a recombined (null) allele is a documented risk and must be monitored by genotyping the floxed allele in the next generation.

• Endothelial expression means that a phenotype observed in Vav-Cre mice is not strictly
hematopoietic-cell-autonomous; it may include contributions from endothelial cells that give
rise to, or support, HSCs.
Relevance to this proposal: Vav-Cre; Dcaf7-flox mice die at postnatal day 4–7 (P4–P7). This constitutive deletion captures developmental requirements that tamoxifen-induced adult deletion would
miss, but it introduces the interpretive concern that the phenotype could reflect endothelial Dcaf7
loss. See K12 for the detailed committee question on this point.
Vav-iCre (Georgiades 2002 Genesis)
A codon-improved Vav-Cre with similar biology and slightly reduced germline leakage. Used
interchangeably with Vav1-Cre in many laboratories.
Mx1-Cre (Kuhn 1995 Science)
The historical workhorse of inducible hematopoietic genetics. The Mx1 promoter responds to Type
I IFN signaling; Cre is induced by injection of polyinosinic–polycytidylic acid (Poly(I:C)), a synthetic double-stranded RNA analog that potently induces endogenous IFN-alpha/beta. Standard
dosing is 10–25 mg/kg intraperitoneally (IP) every other day for three doses. Recombination efficiency in HSCs is generally high (>90% at optimal dosing) but less complete in lymphoid compartments.
Critical confound for this thesis: Poly(I:C) is a TLR3, MDA5, and RIG-I agonist. It activates the
interferon-stimulated gene (ISG) program directly . Because DCAF7 biology in this proposal intersects the IFN response (IFIT3, ISG outputs, Jak2V617F-driven inflammatory signaling), any phenotype observed in Mx1-Cre; Dcaf7-flox mice after Poly(I:C) is confounded by the activating agent
itself. See K4 for full treatment.
Rosa26-CreER and Rosa26-CreERT2
