A Cre fused to the mutant ligand-binding domain (LBD) of the estrogen receptor is placed under
the ubiquitous Rosa26 promoter. In the absence of ligand, CreER is cytoplasmic, sequestered by
HSP90. Upon tamoxifen (or its active metabolite 4-hydroxytamoxifen, 4-OHT) binding, CreER
translocates to the nucleus and recombines loxP sites.
The original CreER (single G521R substitution) responds to endogenous beta-estradiol, which
leads to baseline leakiness. CreERT2 (G400V , M543A, L544A triple mutant) is >10-fold more
tamoxifen-specific and is the current standard. Modern “Rosa26-CreER” lines are typically
CreERT2.
Standard induction: tamoxifen in corn oil at 100–200 mg/kg IP , five consecutive daily doses. Tamoxifen chow (e.g., 400–500 mg/kg diet) is an alternative for longer-term, lower-peak induction.
Recombination efficiency in hematopoiesis is high (>80% in HSPCs by flow-based reporter) with
a 2–4 week washout for full excision and pharmacologic clearance.
Relevance: This is the core driver for Aim 1a in the proposal, chosen specifically to avoid the
Poly(I:C)/IFN confound.

Scl-CreER (Gothert 2005 Blood)
Tg(Tal1-cre/ERT)42-056Jrg. Provides adult HSC-enriched expression but with known endothelial
and erythroid expression. A good choice when the question is HSC-restricted, but incomplete
coverage of progenitor compartments.
Fgd5-CreER (Gazit 2014 JEM)
Currently the cleanest HSC-selective inducible driver. Fgd5 is expressed in phenotypic long-term
HSCs with minimal expression in committed progenitors. Recombination efficiency is acceptable
but can be lower than Rosa26-CreERT2.
Tie2-Cre / T ek-Cre (Kisanuki 2001)
Drives recombination in endothelium and in hemogenic endothelium, encompassing nearly
all hematopoietic cells. Useful specifically for teasing apart endothelial contributions — e.g., a
Tek-Cre versus Vav-Cre comparison can delineate endothelial-autonomous versus hematopoieticautonomous phenotypes.
Csf1r-Cre (Deng 2010) and LysM-Cre (Clausen 1999)
Myeloid-lineage drivers. Csf1r-Cre captures macrophage and dendritic lineages and has fetal onset. LysM-Cre is more restricted to monocyte/granulocyte lineage with postnatal onset; recombination efficiency varies by subset.
Flt3-Cre
A “history” marker — labels cells that have transited the Flt3+ multipotent progenitor (MPP) stage.
Because Flt3 is expressed downstream of LT-HSC, this driver spares LT-HSCs while recombining in
MPPs and their progeny . Useful to test whether a phenotype requires HSC versus progenitor-level
deletion.
Rosa-rtTA / T etO-Cre
Doxycycline-inducible Cre via a TetO-Cre transgene crossed to an rtTA driver. Now rarely used in
HSC work because of slow washout, leaky expression, and the advent of better CreERT2 systems.
Reporter crosses to measure Cre efficiency
Because Cre efficiency is not a yes/no variable, every conditional experiment should include a
reporter cross to measure per-cell recombination. Common choices:
• R26-LSL-tdT omato (Ai14)(Madisen 2010 Nat Neurosci)

• R26-mT/mG (mTmG) (Muzumdar 2007 Genesis): switches from membrane-tdTomato to
membrane-GFP upon Cre, giving a “before/after” flow panel within a single animal
• R26-LSL-YFP
For this proposal, the candidate should pilot Rosa26-CreERT2; R26-mTmG with the standard tamoxifen regimen and verify >80% GFP-positive LSKs before committing to full Dcaf7 cohorts.

sections.title: T amoxifen Induction: Pharmacology and Pitfalls

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Pharmacokinetics
Tamoxifen is a prodrug. Hepatic CYP450 enzymes (primarily CYP3A4/5 and CYP2D6) convert
tamoxifen to 4-hydroxytamoxifen (4-OHT) and N-desmethyl-tamoxifen, which are further metabolized to endoxifen. 4-OHT is the active Cre-activating species. Peak 4-OHT levels occur
within hours of IP dosing; tissue clearance is complete within 7–14 days but biologic effects on
hematopoiesis can persist longer.
Standard regimens
• Corn oil vehicle: tamoxifen free base is dissolved in corn oil (10 mg/mL typical) at 37°C.
Ethanol (10%) is sometimes added to aid solubilization, then diluted in corn oil.
• IP dosing: 100–200 mg/kg × 5 consecutive days is the modal regimen for Rosa26-CreERT2 in
adult hematopoiesis.
• Oral gavage: equivalent doses are feasible; useful when IP is contraindicated.
• T am chow (T am Diet, ~400 mg/kg chow):provides continuous low-level induction; useful for
long-term deletion studies and avoids injection stress.
• 4-OHT directly: can be dosed to bypass hepatic conversion; useful in vitro and for specific
pharmacologic questions, but expensive and not standard in vivo.
Washout
A 2–4 week washout between last tamoxifen dose and phenotypic assay is standard. This serves
two purposes: (1) allows clearance of tamoxifen and 4-OHT from tissues to remove direct pharmacologic effects, and (2) ensures excision is complete before readout.
Direct effects of tamoxifen on HSCs
This is a critical pitfall. Tamoxifen has bona fide direct effects on HSCs independent of any Cre
activity:
• Sanchez-Aguilera 2014 Cell Stem Cell demonstrated that tamoxifen (via estrogen-receptor signaling) activates dormant HSCs, expands HSCs, and produces a transient decrease in LT-HSC
numbers with recovery at later time points.

• Kim 2013 Stem Cells showed similar tamoxifen effects on HSPC populations.
These effects are not a Cre artifact; they are pharmacologic. The implication is that any HSC phenotype in a tamoxifen-treated CreERT2; flox experiment must be compared against tamoxifen-treated
Cre-negative littermates, not untreated controls. The experimental design should include a minimum of four arms:
1. Tamoxifen + Cre-positive + flox/flox (experimental)
2. Tamoxifen + Cre-negative + flox/flox (vehicle-matched genotype control)
3. V ehicle (corn oil) + Cre-positive + flox/flox (assess Cre leakiness)
4. V ehicle + Cre-negative + flox/flox (baseline)
Figure callout (Figure K3.1): A schematic four-arm factorial design distinguishing tamoxifen effect, Cre leakiness, and the genetic deletion effect in a 2x2 layout.
Cre toxicity
Cre itself, at high expression levels, has been reported to produce DNA damage via illegitimate
recombination at cryptic loxP-like sites (pseudo-loxP). This is more a concern with retroviral or
transgenic Cre lines driven by strong promoters; Rosa26-driven CreERT2 is generally considered
low-toxicity . Nonetheless, Cre-only controls (tamoxifen + Cre-positive; no floxed allele) can be
added when Cre toxicity is in question.
Committee Q: “T amoxifen itself affects HSCs — how do you control for this?” — Answer: tamoxifentreated Cre-negative littermate controls, 2–4 week washout before assay , steady-state baseline before disease induction, and, where feasible, consistency checks against tamoxifen-chow induction
to distinguish bolus pharmacologic effects from steady induction.

sections.title: The Mx1-Cre / Poly(I:C) Confound — A Structural Problem for the Thesis

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This is arguably the most important methodological issue for the proposal, and the committee will
probe it.
The pharmacology of Poly(I:C)
Poly(I:C) is a synthetic dsRNA analog that engages three pattern-recognition receptors:
• TLR3 (endosomal, in dendritic cells and macrophages)
• MDA5 (IFIH1) (cytosolic, broadly expressed)
• RIG-I (DDX58) (cytosolic; prefers 5’-triphosphate short dsRNA but also senses Poly(I:C))
Engagement of these receptors activates IRF3/IRF7 and NF-kB, producing a burst of Type I IFN
(IFN-alpha, IFN-beta) within hours. Secreted IFN signals autocrine and paracrine through IFNAR1/2, inducing the ISG program in essentially all hematopoietic cells — including HSCs, which
upregulate Sca-1, exit dormancy , and enter cycle.

Essers 2009 Nature
This landmark paper demonstrated that IFN-alpha (whether by direct injection or by Poly(I:C)-
induced endogenous production) activates dormant HSCs and drives them into proliferation. This
established that Poly(I:C) is itself a biologically active intervention on HSCs , not merely a tool
for inducing Cre.
Why this is not fixable by Cre-negative controls
In a typical Mx1-Cre experiment, the Cre-negative Poly(I:C)-treated animal controls for the IFNinduced background. This is adequate when the biology of interest is orthogonal to IFN. It fails
when the biology of interest is IFN itself. In this thesis, DCAF7 intersects the IFN response (IFIT3
as a putative DCAF7 substrate; Jak2V617F as an IFN-inflamed genotype; ISG program modulation).
Both the Cre-positive and Cre-negative arms are in an IFN-activated state. The experiment cannot
isolate a Dcaf7-dependent effect on baseline HSC biology because there is no IFN-naive arm.
A second, subtler problem: the magnitude of ISG induction by Poly(I:C) may differ between Crenegative and Dcaf7-null cells if DCAF7 modulates IFN responses. Thus the “IFN-matched control”
is not actually IFN-matched.
Preliminary Mx1-Cre data in this proposal
The preliminary Mx1-Cre data in the proposal should therefore be framed as hypothesisgenerating, not definitive . The existence of a phenotype warrants further study; the magnitude
and interpretation require the Rosa26-CreERT2 (IFN-clean) experiment to be rigorous.
The move to Rosa26-CreERT2
Rosa26-CreERT2 + tamoxifen induction avoids dsRNA, avoids TLR3/MDA5/RIG-I engagement,
and does not induce Type I IFN. Tamoxifen has its own HSC effects (K3), but these are not IFNmediated and can be controlled by tamoxifen-treated Cre-negative littermates. The ISG baseline in
tamoxifen-treated animals is indistinguishable from vehicle controls, which is the essential point
for this thesis.
Committee Q: “Why switch from Mx1-Cre to Rosa26-CreER for your Aim 1a?” — The candidate
should answer, without hedging: “Poly(I:C) induces Type I IFN directly , which activates the same
ISG program my thesis is trying to study . Mx1-Cre cannot disentangle Cre-dependent deletion
effects from Poly(I:C)-induced IFN effects in an IFN-centered phenotype. Rosa26-CreERT2 with
tamoxifen induces deletion without engaging TLR3/MDA5/RIG-I and without producing Type
I IFN; it is therefore the appropriate driver for any DCAF7 experiment that reads out on the IFN
response. The preliminary Mx1-Cre data motivated the question but cannot answer it.”

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Single-color reporters
• R26-LSL-tdT omato (Ai14) (Madisen 2010 Nat Neurosci): the modern standard. Bright,
broadly expressed from the Rosa26 locus after Cre-mediated removal of a lox-STOP-lox (LSL)
cassette.
• R26-LSL-YFP: older but still in use.
Dual-color reporters
• R26-mT/mG (mTmG) (Muzumdar 2007 Genesis): constitutively expresses membranetdTomato; Cre switches to membrane-GFP . Permits same-animal internal control and clear
identification of recombined versus unrecombined cells by flow or imaging.
Multicolor clonal reporters
• Confetti (R26-Brainbow-2.1) (Snippert 2010 Cell): stochastic recombination among four fluorescent outcomes (nGFP , YFP , RFP , mCFP) permits clonal tracing of progeny .
• Rainbow (R26-Rainbow) (Rinkevich 2011 Nature): related approach; applied to hematopoietic clonal tracing.
High-complexity clonal tracing
• Polylox barcoding (Pei 2017 Nature): Cre-induced stochastic recombination of a concatenated
lox cassette generates ~10^5 unique DNA barcodes. Read out by PCR/sequencing. Enables
native, in vivo HSC clone-level tracking.
• Sleeping Beauty transposons (Sun 2014 Nature): Dox-induced transposition generates unique
integration sites as clonal tags; unbiased and high-diversity .
• LARRY (Weinreb 2020 Science): lentiviral barcoding combined with scRNA-seq; current stateof-the-art for coupling fate and transcriptome.
• CRISPR lineage tracing: CRISPR-induced indels at a defined array (e.g., GESTALT, ScarTrace)
serve as accumulating lineage barcodes.
Relevance to proposal
For scRNA-seq on LSK-gated marrow (Aim 1/2), a lineage tracer is not strictly required for the
primary phenotyping experiments. However, the candidate should be prepared to discuss how
LARRY-like approaches would be needed to resolve whether Dcaf7 loss acts at the clonal level
versus the population level, and whether apparent expansion reflects clonal dominance or uniform
hyperproliferation. This is a reasonable committee follow-up.
Figure callout (Figure K5.1): Schematic showing the flow of information from Cre-activated reporter (e.g., mTmG) through sorted LSK populations into scRNA-seq, with lineage barcoding as
an optional add-on.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Conditional knockout allele design principles
• Critical exon selection: the floxed exon(s) should, when removed, produce a frameshift that
introduces an early PTC subject to NMD. A clean null is preferred over a hypomorph.
• Preserve splice sites: loxP insertions must be placed in introns at least ~100 bp from splice
donor/acceptor sites, avoiding branchpoints and polypyrimidine tracts.
• Preserve regulatory elements: where possible, avoid disrupting known enhancers within introns.
• Allele validation: verify by RT-PCR, western, and — critically — by an independent allele or
rescue experiment.
Dcaf7-floxed (this lab)
The lab’s Dcaf7-floxed allele flanks a critical exon with loxP sites; Cre excision generates a
frameshifted, NMD-targeted null transcript. The allele has been validated by the lab; genotyping
strategies are covered in K7.
Committee Q: “How do you know your Dcaf7-floxed allele gives a true null and not a hypomorph?” —
Answer: RT-qPCR across the excised exon junction, western blot with validated antibody , and a rescue experiment (e.g., Dcaf7 cDNA re-expression reversing the phenotype in CreERT2-excised cells)
constitute the gold standard. A second, independent allele (e.g., a CRISPR-generated frameshift)
or comparison to published Dcaf7-deficient data strengthens the claim.
Knock-in allele design
Knock-ins insert defined coding changes at the endogenous locus. For oncogenic alleles such as
Jak2V617F, the standard approach is a lox-STOP-lox (LSL) cassette upstream of the mutant allele,
preventing expression until Cre removes the STOP .
Jak2V617F conditional models
Four commonly cited Jak2V617F knock-in or conditional alleles exist, each with distinct phenotypes:
• Mullally 2010 Cancer Cell: Jak2-V617F floxed allele, typically paired with Mx1-Cre. Produces
a polycythemia vera (PV)-like phenotype with erythrocytosis, leukocytosis, thrombocytosis,
and splenomegaly . Widely adopted.
• Akada 2010: bacterial artificial chromosome (BAC)-based conditional model with endogenous
Jak2 promoter control. Heterozygous animals develop PV-like disease.
• Li 2010: Rosa26-JAK2V617F-floxed-STOP design. Human JAK2V617F driven from Rosa26 after Cre; allows tissue/stage-specific expression for cell-of-origin studies.
• Marty 2010: conditional knock-in with LSL cassette; activated by Mx1-Cre.
The four alleles differ in promoter (endogenous vs. Rosa26), copy number, and resulting phenotype

severity (PV vs. ET-like vs. MPN).
Committee Q: “Y our Jak2V617F is the Mullally allele. Why not Akada or Li?” — Answer: Mullally’s
allele is the most widely used, gives a robust PV phenotype with reproducible kinetics, and is compatible with both Mx1-Cre and Rosa26-CreERT2. Akada and Li produce milder or cell-of-originspecific phenotypes; these are valuable for specific questions but provide a less standardized background for the Dcaf7-interaction studies. The candidate should note that a future complementary
experiment in the Akada background could test allele-independence of the Dcaf7 effect.
CRISPR and modern allele generation
Traditional conditional allele generation required gene targeting in ES cells via homologous recombination (weeks to years). CRISPR-Cas9 HDR (Wang 2013 Cell) enables one-step generation
of floxed alleles by zygote electroporation or microinjection, though efficiency for long insertions
(a pair of loxP sites flanking an exon, a few kb apart) remains a challenge and often requires two
sequential rounds or long ssDNA donors.
Current refinements:
• i-GONAD (Gurumurthy 2019): intraoviductal electroporation in situ — avoids zygote isolation.
• CRISPR-Cas12a (Cpf1): alternative nuclease with staggered cuts; some groups report
improved HDR.
• Easi-CRISPR: long ssDNA donors for small cassette insertions.
• PITCh, HITI: homology-independent targeted integration approaches.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Floxed allele genotyping (pre-Cre)
A standard two-primer PCR across one loxP site distinguishes wild-type (smaller amplicon) from
floxed (slightly larger, owing to the ~34 bp loxP insert plus any linker). For ambiguous gels, restriction digestion of the amplicon (loxP often introduces a unique restriction site) provides orthogonal
confirmation.
Recombined (excision) allele genotyping
Outer primers flanking the entire floxed region generate:
• No band or very large band in the unrecombined state (too long to amplify efficiently under
standard conditions).
• A defined smaller band in the recombined state, where the intervening sequence is excised
and the remaining single loxP scar is sandwiched between the primers.

This “recombination PCR” is the functional readout of Cre activity . Paired with the floxed-specific
PCR, one can distinguish floxed/floxed, floxed/recombined, and recombined/recombined.
qPCR for excision efficiency
Bulk excision efficiency is quantified by qPCR with primers inside the floxed region (excisionsensitive) normalized to a primer pair outside (excision-insensitive). Complete excision gives zero
signal in the inside pair. This assay is useful for comparing drivers and doses.
Single-cell genotyping
For definitive per-cell excision measurement, FACS-sort the population (e.g., LSK), then run the
recombination PCR on single-cell or low-input lysates. More modern: use mTmG reporter (K2),
where GFP-positive cells by flow are by definition recombined — obviates per-cell PCR.
Relevance
For Dcaf7-flox; Rosa26-CreERT2 mice, the candidate should establish before committing to downstream experiments: (i) floxed genotype by PCR, (ii) tamoxifen-induced excision efficiency by
qPCR on sorted LSK, and (iii) protein-level loss by western on sorted HSPCs.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Core assay
The competitive bone marrow transplant (cBMT) is the gold-standard functional assay for HSC
fitness. The logic: HSCs are defined by long-term multilineage reconstitution of a lethally irradiated recipient. Two donor sources — the experimental genotype (typically CD45.2) and a wildtype competitor (typically CD45.1) — are mixed at a defined ratio and co-injected. Over weeks
to months, the relative contribution of each donor to circulating blood lineages is monitored by
flow cytometry using CD45.1 vs. CD45.2 congenic markers, plus lineage markers (CD3, B220, Gr1/Mac-1).
Canonical protocol
1. Recipient preparation: CD45.1 (B6.SJL-Ptprc^a) recipients irradiated with 950–1050 cGy , typically split into two doses 3–4 hours apart to minimize GI toxicity .
2. Donor mix: 1 × 10^6 whole BM cells from test donor (CD45.2) mixed with 1 × 10^6 from
competitor (CD45.1). Alternative ratios (1:2, 1:5 test:competitor) used when the test genotype
is suspected to have a fitness advantage.
3. Injection: retro-orbital or tail-vein IV within 4–24 hours of irradiation.

4. Peripheral blood sampling: tail or submandibular bleeds at 4, 8, 12, and 16 weeks posttransplant. Flow for CD45.1/CD45.2 chimerism in total leukocytes and in B, T, and myeloid
compartments.
5. Long-term definition: ≥16 weeks chimerism is the consensus long-term readout. Persistent
multilineage CD45.2 contribution at 16 weeks is evidence of LT-HSC function.
Secondary transplant
To assay self-renewal specifically: at 16–20 weeks, harvest BM from primary recipients and retransplant into lethally irradiated secondary recipients (often 1–5 × 10^6 BM cells per secondary ,
sometimes with fresh competitor). Serial transplant-induced exhaustion or persistent chimerism
is a direct measure of self-renewal capacity .
Limiting-dilution analysis (LDA)
To quantify HSC frequency , transplant decreasing numbers of test donor BM (e.g., 10^6, 10^5,
10^4 cells) with fixed competitor. Score each recipient as “reconstituted” (>1% test chimerism in
all lineages at 16 wks) or “not.” Poisson statistics yield HSC frequency . L-Calc or ELDA software
is standard.
Non-competitive transplant
Transplanting test BM alone, without competitor, measures rescue capacity (whether the graft can
support life) but is uninformative about fitness relative to wild type. Useful for engraftment kinetics or when competitor confounds are undesired; rarely sufficient as a primary HSC assay .
CD45.1 fitness caveat (Mercier 2019 Exp Hematol)
The traditional B6.SJL CD45.1 competitor has been shown to have modestly reduced fitness relative
to C57BL/6J CD45.2 even in wild-type-vs-wild-type transplants. This is not a neutral congenic
marker. Mitigations:
• Use CD45.1/CD45.2 double-positive competitor (F1 of B6.SJL × C57BL/6J), which is intermediate in fitness and allows simultaneous distinction of three populations.
• Reverse the experimental arm — perform a parallel transplant with test in CD45.1 and competitor in CD45.2 — to confirm the effect is not congenic-marker-driven.
• Use CD45.1 controls matched to the same colony .
Committee Q: “CD45.1 competitors have been shown to have their own fitness phenotype — how do
you control?” — The candidate should name Mercier 2019 and propose either CD45.1/CD45.2 F1
competitor or arm-reversal to rule out a congenic contribution.

Relevance to proposal
Competitive transplant will be the definitive HSC-autonomous readout for Dcaf7 loss — both at
steady state and in the Jak2V617F background. The experimental arms must include tamoxifentreated Cre-negative and Cre-positive floxed donors, with induction performed either in the donor
prior to transplant or in the recipient after reconstitution, depending on whether the question is
developmental or maintenance.
Figure callout (Figure K8.1): Timeline for a competitive BMT with induction either pre- or posttransplant, with decision tree for cell-autonomy .

sections.title: Mouse Models of MPN and AML

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A working knowledge of the broader MPN/AML model landscape is expected; the committee
may probe alternatives or combinations.
MPN
• Jak2V617F (see K6): PV-like. The driver is this thesis.
• CALR del52 / ins5 (Marty 2016, Li 2018): conditional knock-in CALR frameshift mutants;
ET/PMF phenotype with thrombocytosis and eventual fibrosis.
• MPL W515L (Pikman 2006): originally retroviral BMT; conditional knock-in versions exist;
PMF-like.
• TET2-KO, DNMT3A-KO: clonal hematopoiesis (CH) models; alone produce subtle HSC expansion; combinatorial with JAK2V617F models aggressive MPN.
AML
• Flt3-ITD knock-in (Lee 2007): alone produces MPD-like disease; combined with NPM1c knockin (Vassiliou 2011 Nat Genet) produces AML.
• MLL-AF9 retroviral (Somervaille 2006 Cancer Cell): retrovirally introduced fusion in HSPCs;
robust AML.
• PML-RARA knock-in (He 1998 PNAS): APL model; MRP8-driven transgenic versions target
myeloid lineage.
• T rp53-/-:background for MDS/AML; combinatorial with other hits.
Xenograft and humanized systems
• NSG (NOD/SCID/IL2Rg null): gold-standard immunodeficient host for human cell engraftment.
• NSGS: NSG expressing human SCF, GM-CSF, IL3 — improves myeloid engraftment.
• MISTRG: humanized cytokine knock-ins in the Rag-/- IL2Rg-/- background; supports
myeloid lineages.

• PDX models: primary patient MPN/AML cells engrafted in NSG/NSGS/MISTRG; enable
drug response studies.
Relevance to proposal
Jak2V617F-Mullally is the planned MPN background. A stretch aim could propose crossing into
a CALR or MPL background to test whether the DCAF7 effect is MPN-general or JAK2V617Fspecific — this is a reasonable committee-suggested extension.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Developmental staging and hematopoietic sites
Hematopoiesis shifts sites through development:
• E7.5 yolk sac: primitive hematopoiesis.
• E10.5 AGM: first definitive HSCs emerge from hemogenic endothelium.
• E12.5–E16.5 fetal liver: major site of definitive hematopoietic expansion; HSCs highly proliferative.
• E17.5–birth: transition to bone marrow begins.
• P0–P14 neonatal marrow and spleen: fetal liver wanes; BM becomes dominant by ~P14,
though splenic hematopoiesis persists longer in mouse than in human.
• P14–P28: adult-like BM hematopoiesis established.
Developmental sampling strategy
For a Vav-Cre Dcaf7 phenotype that lethally manifests at P4–P7, a staged sampling strategy is
essential:
• E10.5: AGM dissections; quantify HSC emergence by flow (VE-cadherin+, CD45+, cKit+) and
by transplant.
• E12.5–E14.5 fetal liver: count HSPCs by immunophenotype (LSK, LSK SLAM) and function
(colony-forming assays, fetal liver transplants into lethally irradiated adult recipients).
• P0–P4 neonatal BM + spleen + liver: flow for HSPC compartments; histology for marrow
cellularity and extramedullary hematopoiesis.
• P4–P7 moribund animals: complete necropsy , blood counts, histopathology .
Neonatal flow caveats
Neonatal tissue volumes are small. BM from a P4 pup is ~10 5–106 cells per animal; pooling by
genotype is sometimes required. Spleen and liver still contain active hematopoiesis at this stage
and must be included for a complete picture. Fixation and staining protocols for neonatal samples
often require downscaling.

Developmental versus adult requirement
The Vav-Cre P4 lethality tells us Dcaf7 is required at some point between E11.5 (Cre onset) and P7. It
does not tell us whether that requirement is embryonic (e.g., HSC emergence, fetal liver expansion)
or perinatal (e.g., marrow colonization, early postnatal expansion). Discriminating requires either:
1. Staged Rosa26-CreERT2 induction at different developmental windows (tamoxifen to the
pregnant dam at E10.5 vs. E14.5 vs. P1).
2. Lineage- or site-specific Cre drivers (e.g., Tek-Cre for hemogenic endothelium; Fgd5-CreER
postnatally).
3. Foster-mothering and nutritional rescue to rule out secondary lethality from failure to thrive.
Relevance to proposal
The Vav-Cre arm provides developmental insight that adult-only conditional approaches would
miss. The Rosa26-CreERT2 arm provides adult, IFN-clean insight. Both are needed. The committee will expect the candidate to articulate this complementarity .
Committee Q: “Y our Vav-Cre DCAF7 KO has a P4 phenotype — could that be off-target Cre toxicity in
endothelium?” — Answer: Vav-Cre does express in endothelium, so this is a legitimate concern.
Mitigations: (i) Tek-Cre (endothelial-restricted) + Dcaf7-flox to isolate endothelial contribution;
(ii) compare Vav-Cre phenotype to tamoxifen-induced adult Rosa26-CreERT2 deletion to separate
developmental from maintenance requirements; (iii) reconstitution of irradiated wild-type recipients with Vav-Cre; Dcaf7-flox fetal liver or neonatal BM to test hematopoietic-cell-autonomy; (iv)
histopathology of vasculature in moribund Vav-Cre pups to directly assess endothelial defects.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
IACUC and protocols
All vertebrate animal work requires an approved Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee
(IACUC) protocol specifying species, numbers, procedures, endpoints, and justification. Every
procedure in this proposal — tamoxifen injection, Poly(I:C) injection, transplantation, irradiation,
tissue harvest — must be pre-approved.
Humane endpoints and body condition scoring
• Body condition score (BCS): 1 (emaciated) to 5 (obese); 3 is ideal. BCS <2 is a common humane
endpoint trigger.
• T umor burden:subcutaneous tumors typically limited to ≤2.0 cm in any dimension.
• Spleen palpation: in MPN studies, massive splenomegaly may be an endpoint trigger independent of BCS.
• General distress: hunched posture, ruffled fur, lethargy , labored breathing — any is grounds
for euthanasia.

Husbandry and microbiome
• Co-housing effects: littermates raised together share microbiome; mixing genotypes from different litters can change the phenotype. Best practice: co-housing from weaning, or generation
of experimental cohorts from heterozygous intercrosses so that all genotypes share cage/litter.
• V endor effects:Jackson vs. Charles River colonies can have different microbiomes and different
baseline HSC phenotypes.
• Facility transfers: moving colonies between facilities can shift the microbiome and alter
hematopoietic phenotypes; rederivation may be required.
• Bedding, water, diet: all should be standardized within an experiment.
Germline contamination monitoring
Constitutive Cre drivers (Vav-Cre, Vav-iCre) can produce germline recombination at low frequency .
Every breeding cross should include genotyping for both the floxed and the recombined allele; any
animal carrying a germline-recombined allele (i.e., the “null” allele in germline) must be either
excluded or used as a null control (not a flox control).
Summary and Integration
The mouse genetics framework of this proposal rests on a deliberate, thesis-driven selection of
Cre drivers. The Vav-Cre arm provides a constitutive, pan-hematopoietic developmental window
that has already revealed a P4–P7 lethal phenotype, establishing that DCAF7 is essential in perinatal hematopoiesis. The Mx1-Cre preliminary data generated the initial hypothesis but are confounded by the IFN-inducing properties of Poly(I:C) — a confound that is particularly severe for
a thesis centered on IFN-responsive biology . The Rosa26-CreERT2 arm, with tamoxifen induction and appropriate tamoxifen-treated Cre-negative controls, will be the definitive experiment.
Competitive BMT with careful attention to the CD45.1 fitness caveat (Mercier 2019) will provide
HSC-autonomous readouts. The Jak2V617F-Mullally allele provides a standardized MPN background for the disease-state experiments. Throughout, reporter crosses (R26-mTmG or Ai14) will
be used to verify recombination efficiency , and stage-specific sampling (E12.5 fetal liver through
P7 neonatal BM) will resolve developmental versus adult requirements.
The candidate’s fluency with these systems — and with the trade-offs among them — is what a
committee will probe. The answers above are drawn from the primary literature and should be
deliverable without hesitation.
References
• de Boer J, et al. Eur J Immunol, 2003. (Vav1-Cre)
• Georgiades P , et al. Genesis, 2002. (Vav-iCre)
• Kuhn R, et al. Science, 1995. (Mx1-Cre)

• Gothert JR, et al. Blood, 2005. (Scl-CreER)
• Gazit R, et al. JEM, 2014. (Fgd5-CreER)
• Kisanuki YY , et al. Dev Biol, 2001. (Tek/Tie2-Cre)
• Deng L, et al. Blood, 2010. (Csf1r-Cre)
• Clausen BE, et al. Transgenic Res, 1999. (LysM-Cre)
• Madisen L, et al. Nat Neurosci, 2010. (Ai14)
• Muzumdar MD, et al. Genesis, 2007. (mTmG)
• Snippert HJ, et al. Cell, 2010. (Confetti)
• Rinkevich Y , et al. Nature, 2011. (Rainbow)
• Pei W, et al. Nature, 2017. (Polylox)
• Sun J, et al. Nature, 2014. (Sleeping Beauty HSC tracing)
• Weinreb C, et al. Science, 2020. (LARRY)
• Essers MAG, et al. Nature, 2009. (IFN-alpha and HSCs)
• Sanchez-Aguilera A, et al. Cell Stem Cell, 2014. (Tamoxifen effects on HSCs)
• Kim IK, et al. Stem Cells, 2013. (Tamoxifen and HSPCs)
• Mullally A, et al. Cancer Cell, 2010. (Jak2V617F conditional)
• Akada H, et al. Blood, 2010. (Jak2V617F BAC)
• Li J, et al. Blood, 2010. (Rosa26-JAK2V617F)
• Marty C, et al. Leukemia, 2010. (Jak2V617F conditional)
• Marty C, et al. Blood, 2016. (CALR del52)
• Li J, et al. 2018. (CALR mutant models)
• Pikman Y , et al. PLoS Med, 2006. (MPL W515L)
• Lee BH, et al. Cancer Cell, 2007. (Flt3-ITD)
• Vassiliou GS, et al. Nat Genet, 2011. (NPM1c + Flt3-ITD AML)
• Somervaille TCP , et al. Cancer Cell, 2006. (MLL-AF9)
• He LZ, et al. PNAS, 1998. (PML-RARA)
• Wang H, et al. Cell, 2013. (CRISPR knock-in)
• Gurumurthy CB, et al. Nat Protoc, 2019. (i-GONAD)
• Mercier FE, et al. Exp Hematol, 2019. (CD45.1 fitness)
Flow Cytometry and Cell Sorting in Hematopoiesis
Tier 2 Qualifying Exam Study Guide — DCAF7 in Hematopoiesis
Flow cytometry is the single most important technical competency for a hematopoiesis thesis. Every claim about HSCs, MPPs, myeloid progenitors, and lineage output ultimately rests on gating
decisions, panel design, and the physics of fluorescence detection. This section covers the instrumentation, reagent chemistry , panel logic, gating hierarchies (mouse and human), controls, sorting
strategies, and common pitfalls, with committee-question boxes emphasizing the judgment calls
that arise in the thesis work: LSK sorting on the MACSQuant Tyto for scRNA-seq, SLAM-based
MPP subsetting, iPSC-derived organoid engraftment readouts, and parallel CFU assays.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
A flow cytometer measures optical properties of individual particles (typically cells) as they pass
single-file through one or more laser intercepts. Four sequential events define every measurement.
Hydrodynamic focusing. The sample core stream is injected into a faster-moving sheath fluid
(typically PBS-based). Laminar flow compresses the sample into a thin central column so cells
traverse the laser intercept one at a time. The diameter of this core stream is tunable on most sorters;
a narrower core gives tighter CVs (better resolution) but reduces throughput. On the MACSQuant
Tyto the “flow” is generated by a pneumatic pump pushing sample through a disposable cartridge
rather than a sheath-driven stream, which is a fundamentally different architecture (see L8).
Laser interrogation. Each laser line (commonly 405 nm violet, 488 nm blue, 561 nm yellow-green,
633/640 nm red, sometimes 355 nm UV) excites a defined set of fluorophores. When a cell crosses
the laser it scatters light and excited fluorophores emit photons at longer (Stokes-shifted) wavelengths.
Scatter. Forward scatter (FSC) is collected in line with the laser beam and scales roughly with
cell size/refractive index. Side scatter (SSC) is collected at 90 degrees and scales with internal complexity (granularity , nuclear lobation). FSC-A x FSC-H is the universal doublet discriminator: true
singlets lie on a diagonal because area and height scale together, whereas doublets show elevated
area relative to height.
Fluorescence. Emitted photons are routed through a stack of dichroic mirrors and bandpass filters
to photomultiplier tubes (PMTs). Each PMT sees a narrow emission window corresponding to one
fluorophore. Photons are converted to photoelectrons, amplified by the PMT’s dynode chain, and
digitized.
PMT detectors and optical path. In conventional cytometers, each detector sits behind a filter/mirror cube that defines which wavelengths reach it. For example, on a BD LSRFortessa the
“blue-B” detector typically sits behind a 695/40 filter with a 685 longpass mirror in front, capturing
the PerCP-Cy5.5 or 7-AAD emission window. Reconfiguring the optical path (swapping filters)
is possible but requires re-setting voltages and compensation. Avalanche photodiodes (APDs)
are used on some instruments (Cytek Aurora) and have higher quantum efficiency in the red,
improving APC-family detection.
Digital thresholding vs photon counting. Modern cytometers digitize every pulse and apply a
software threshold (typically on FSC-H) to discard noise/debris below a set level. Older analog
systems used a hardware trigger. Photon counting is a distinct detection regime, used more in
microscopy than cytometry; for flow, what matters is that the signal is integrated over the pulse
width and represented as area (-A), height (-H), and width (-W).
Spectral vs conventional flow. This is the most important architectural distinction for panel planning.
• Conventional cytometry uses filters and mirrors to pre-assign a spectral window to each PMT.
If two fluorophores have overlapping emission, their signals bleed into multiple detectors; this
is corrected by a compensation matrix (L5). Practical ceiling: roughly 12-18 colors with careful
design.
• Spectral cytometry (Cytek Aurora, Sony ID7000, BD FACSymphony A5 SE) disperses the full
emission spectrum across a dense array of detectors (the Aurora has 64 detectors across 5 lasers).
Every fluorophore has a characteristic full-spectrum signature, and an unmixing algorithm

(analogous to least-squares deconvolution) solves for the contribution of each dye at every cell.
Benefits: (1) autofluorescence can be extracted as its own “color” and subtracted, (2) dyes with
similar peaks but different shoulders (BB515 vs FITC vs AF488) can be resolved, (3) 25-40+ color
panels are feasible. Drawback: unmixing is only as good as the reference controls, and spread
(see L5) still exists.
For this thesis, the student currently runs 10-12 color conventional panels on the Fortessa and sorts
on the Tyto; a move to spectral would permit simultaneous SLAM + myeloid progenitor + lineage
gating in one tube, which is useful for rare-event analysis in DCAF7-perturbed marrow.
[FIGURE: Conventional vs spectral optical path — filter cube stack vs dispersion array]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Classical small-molecule conjugates.
• FITC (fluorescein isothiocyanate): 495/519 nm ex/em; bright but pH-sensitive and prone to
photobleaching; high spillover into PE channel.
• PE (R-phycoerythrin): 565/578 nm; brightest common fluorophore, ~20x FITC; large protein
(~240 kDa) so some steric issues; excited well by 488 and even better by 561 nm lasers.
• APC (allophycocyanine): 650/660 nm; bright, red-laser excited, low autofluorescence background.
T andem dyes. A donor fluorophore (PE or APC) is covalently linked to an acceptor (Cy5, Cy5.5,
Cy7, Dazzle 594). Laser excites the donor, FRET transfers energy , the acceptor emits at its longer
wavelength.
• PE-Cy5, PE-Cy5.5, PE-Cy7, PE-Dazzle 594
• APC-Cy5.5, APC-Cy7/Fire 750
T andem instability is the single biggest batch-to-batch source of error. Under heat, fixation (especially methanol or long PFA), or light exposure, the FRET pair dissociates. A degraded PE-Cy7
shows increased PE channel signal and decreased Cy7 signal. Always store tandems at 4 C in the
dark, avoid freeze-thaw, and freshly compensate each experiment. If the student fixes her samples for pSTAT5 intracellular staining (L9), she should avoid PE-Cy7 surface markers or plan for
re-optimization.
Brilliant Violet / Brilliant UV polymer dyes.
Dye Ex (nm) Em (nm) Notes
BV421 405 421 Bright; Pacific Blue replacement
BV510 405 510 Dump channel favorite
BV605 405 605 Bright mid-violet
BV650 405 645

Dye Ex (nm) Em (nm) Notes
BV711 405 711
BV785 405 785 Brightest BV
BUV395 355 395 UV laser required
BUV737 355 737
Brilliant Stain Buffer is mandatory when staining with two or more Brilliant polymers in the same
tube. Without it, the polymers interact non-specifically and create artifactual signals that are not
removable by compensation.
Super Bright (Invitrogen) and NovaFluor (Thermo) are alternative polymer families, useful when
designing panels that need additional violet or UV channels without crowding BD’s BV catalog.
Live/dead discrimination.
Reagent Use on Mechanism
DAPI Unfixed or post-fix DNA Cell-impermeant in live cells;
stains exposed nuclear DNA
in dead/permeabilized cells
7-AAD Unfixed Similar exclusion dye; emits in
PerCP channel
Propidium iodide Unfixed Similar; spectrally noisy , spills
into PE-Cy5
LIVE/DEAD Fixable
(Aqua/Blue/NIR)
Surface-stained, then fix Amine-reactive; labels
cytosolic amines of dead cells »
surface amines of live cells;
survives fixation
Zombie dyes (BioLegend) Same as L/D Fixable Amine-reactive; multiple
colors
DNA dyes. Hoechst 33342 is live-cell permeant and is used for the classic SP (side population)
assay of HSC efflux activity , as well as for cell cycle analysis in live sorts. DAPI is used post-fixation
for DNA content (cell cycle, ploidy , megakaryocyte polyploidy).

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Good panels are not assembled by picking available antibodies from the freezer. They are designed
around the biology of marker expression and the optics of the instrument.
1. Match marker brightness to fluorophore brightness (inverted). Dim markers (low copy number, continuous, or biologically variable) need bright fluorophores. Bright markers (abundant, binary) can tolerate dim fluorophores. Pairing dim with dim (e.g., Flt3 on FITC) is the most common
rookie error.

Relative brightness Fluorophores
V ery bright PE, APC, BV421, PE-Cy7, BV605, BV650
Bright BV711, BV785, AF647, PerCP-Cy5.5
Moderate FITC, AF488, BV510, APC-Cy7
Dim BUV395 (sometimes), Pacific Blue, AF700
For the student’s mouse HSC panel: Flt3/CD135 is expressed at very low copy on MPP4 and must
go on PE, BV421, or a similarly bright channel. CD48 and CD150 are brighter and more binary;
they tolerate BV711, BV605, or APC. c-Kit is bright on LSK; APC-Cy7 or PE-Cy7 is standard.
2. Spillover and compensation burden. Fluorophores that spill strongly into one another should
not be used together on markers that need to be resolved independently . Classic conflicts:
• FITC into PE — biological co-expression in LSK (Sca-1 on one, c-Kit on the other) means heavy
compensation; use bright channels for both.
• PE-Cy5 and PerCP-Cy5.5 — nearly identical emission; never use both in one panel.
• BV605 and BV650 — close neighbors; one should be bright+binary , the other can be
bright+continuous but do not stack them for co-expression markers with continuous gradients.
3. Dump channel for the lineage cocktail. All lineage antibodies (CD3e, B220, Ter119, Gr-1, CD11b,
sometimes CD5) go on a single fluorophore. The Lin+ population is gated out in one step. Most
groups use FITC (cheap, many conjugates available) or APC-Cy7 (keeps the high-brightness bluelaser channels open for SLAM markers). Putting lineage on a separate channel per antigen wastes
colors and adds no information.
4. Dead cell exclusion channel. Always dedicated. DAPI (UV or violet laser depending on instrument configuration) or LIVE/DEAD Fixable Aqua (BV510-adjacent) are standard. Do not co-locate
any other marker in that channel.
5. Spillover Spreading Matrix (SSM). Spillover is a mean correction; spread is the stochastic uncertainty that compensation adds to downstream populations. The SSM quantifies how much noise
channel X adds to channel Y after compensation. Reference: Nguyen et al., 2013, Cytometry A .
Use the SSM to flag channel pairs where the “dim” fluorophore’s resolution will collapse because
a bright neighbor floods it with spread. Rule of thumb: never gate a dim-positive population in a
channel receiving high spread from a bright neighbor.
6. Titrate every antibody. Stain a serial dilution (typically 1:25, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200, 1:400) on a positive and negative sample and compute the stain index: SI = (MFI_pos - MFI_neg) / (2 * SD_neg).
Use the titer at peak SI, not the vendor’s recommendation, which is often 2-4x over-concentrated.
This saves money and reduces non-specific background.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Controls are not optional. They define what “positive” and “negative” mean in a given experiment.

Unstained. A sample of the cells of interest with no antibody , treated identically (Fc block, washes,
fixation if applicable). Defines autofluorescence baseline per channel. Granulocytes are notoriously autofluorescent in FITC and PE; bone marrow unstained will look very different from splenic
unstained.
Single-stain controls. One positive-staining reagent per fluorophore, used to build the compensation matrix (L5). Options:
• Compensation beads (UltraComp, AbC Total, OneComp): uniform positive signal; fast; not
valid for tandems that behave differently on cells.
• Cells stained with one antibody each : biologically valid; required when on-cell and on-bead
spectra differ (tandems, some polymer dyes).
• Positive and negative populations must both be present in the single stain, or the algorithm
cannot compute spillover.
FMO (Fluorescence Minus One). The sample is stained with every antibody in the panel except the
one you intend to gate on. The resulting signal in that channel defines the true negative threshold
given all the cumulative spillover and spread from every other fluorophore. FMO is essential
for dim or continuous markers : CD34, Flt3, CD150 borderline events, Ki-67 MFI-based gating.
Without FMO, the gate for “Flt3+ MPP4” is arbitrary .
Isotype controls. An antibody of the same isotype, fluorophore, and concentration but with irrelevant specificity . Largely deprecated for definitive gating because isotype binding does not recapitulate spillover. They remain useful for (a) confirming that a novel antibody clone is binding
specifically to its antigen rather than via Fc or non-specific interaction and (b) publication requirements in some journals for cytokine/intracellular staining.
Biological negative controls. For HSC gating, Lin+ cells serve as a “definitely not HSC” reference:
they should be CD150-/CD48+ or at least not in the LT-HSC quadrant. For CD150 gating specifically , platelets (CD41+) are CD150-high and make an excellent on-panel positive reference. For
CD34 in human, PB lymphocytes (CD34-) give a negative reference.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Compensation subtracts fluorescence spillover between channels so that signal in channel B is not
counted as positivity on antigen A.
Process. For each single-stain control, the software computes the percentage of fluorophore A’s
signal that appears in each other detector. This builds an N x N spillover matrix. Compensation is
applied by solving a linear inverse: observed = spillover * true, so true = spillover^(-1) * observed.
Requirements.
1. Each single stain must have a clearly positive and a clearly negative population.
2. Positive signal must be at least as bright as it will be in the experimental sample; ideally
brighter (but not saturating).
3. Same lot of fluorophore as the experimental panel (especially for tandems).
4. Same instrument settings (voltages, laser powers).

Auto-compensation. FlowJo (FJ10 and later), BD DIV A, and Cytek SpectroFlo all compute the
matrix automatically from labeled single stains. Always review manually: check N vs N+1 biexponential plots for each pair to confirm the population is flat (not slanting up/down after compensation).
Spillover vs spread.
• Spillover is the expected-value correction: on average, 15% of PE signal appears in PE-Cy5;
subtract 0.15 * PE_signal from PE-Cy5.
• Spread is the stochastic uncertainty around that mean: the variance injected into PE-Cy5 by PEpositive events even after compensation. Spread increases with sqrt(signal). A PE-bright population makes the PE-Cy5 axis dim-negative population noisier than if PE were absent. Compensation cannot remove spread; only panel redesign can.
Common pitfalls.
• Over-compensation. Setting the spillover correction too high; population goes negative (falls
below the axis on biexponential scale). Obvious on N vs N+1 plots as a down-slanting smear.
• Under-compensation. Correction too low; apparent positive population in a channel that
should be negative. Seen as an up-slanting smear.
• T andem mismatch. Compensating with bead single-stains when cells show a different (typically more degraded) spectrum. Fix: compensate on cells, or use ComPensation-on-cells tandem controls.
• Autofluorescence not accounted for. If granulocytes autofluoresce strongly in PE-Cy5 and
the compensation was on lymphocytes, the granulocyte “positive” in PE-Cy5 is actually AF.
Spectral flow solves this by treating AF as its own unmixing component.

sections.title: Mouse HSC and Progenitor Gating — Step by Step

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The canonical gating tree for mouse bone marrow:
FSC/SSC (lymphocyte-ish gate, loose) -> FSC-A x FSC-H singlets -> DAPIlive -> Lin- -> Sca-1+
c-Kit+ (LSK) -> SLAM quadrants (CD150 x CD48) -> optional CD34/Flt3 refinement.
[FIGURE: LSK gating hierarchy — FSC/SSC, singlets, live, Lin-, Sca-1 vs c-Kit, CD150 vs CD48
with MPP1-4 quadrants]

subsections.title: Lineage Exclusion Cocktail
subsections.body:
Standard mouse Lin cocktail.
Marker Lineage Clone (common)
CD3 or CD3e T cells 145-2C11, 17A2
CD4 T helper (optional) RM4-5
CD8a T cytotoxic (optional) 53-6.7

Marker Lineage Clone (common)
B220 / CD45R B cells RA3-6B2
Ter119 Erythroid TER-119
Gr-1 / Ly6G/C Granulocytes RB6-8C5
CD11b / Mac1 Myeloid/monocytes M1/70
V ariations and caveats.
• CD5 substitution for CD3. CD5 covers T cells and B1a B cells; some labs prefer CD5 for broader
coverage. For progenitor purification the difference is minor.
• CD11b and Gr-1 on LSKs. Mature CD11b is low on HSCs, but a subset of committed myeloid
progenitors express intermediate CD11b. Including CD11b in the Lin cocktail ensures clean
mature myeloid exclusion but can trim a fraction of CMP-adjacent progenitors. For LSK sorting
this is usually acceptable. For CMP/GMP/MEP purification, omit CD11b and Gr-1 from the
dump to preserve these populations.
• All lineage antibodies on one channel (FITC or APC-Cy7 commonly) — gives one “Lin-” axis
to gate on, freeing channels for informative markers.

subsections.title: LSK Gate
subsections.body:
After Lingating:
• Sca-1 (Ly6A/E) — stem/progenitor marker, but with major caveats.
• c-Kit (CD117) — receptor tyrosine kinase for SCF; marks HSCs through MPPs and myeloid
progenitors.
Sca-1 caveats.
1. Strain dependence. C57BL/6 is Sca-1 high; 129 strains carry null alleles of Ly6A and show
no or minimal Sca-1 expression. LSK gating cannot be used in 129 backgrounds — Kit-only
gating or alternative strategies (ESAM, EPCR) are required.
2. Interferon upregulation. Sca-1 is an interferon-stimulated gene (ISG). Poly(I:C), viral infection, chronic inflammation, and Mx1-Cre induction itself all upregulate Sca-1 on non-HSC
populations, artifactually inflating the “LSK” gate. Reference: Essers et al., 2009, Nature — IFNalpha drives HSCs from quiescence, and Sca-1 is induced broadly . For the student’s DCAF7
project, this is the reason to avoid Mx1-Cre (poly(I:C)-inducible) and favor Rosa26-CreERT2
(tamoxifen-inducible, no inflammatory upregulation). If Mx1-Cre is unavoidable, parallel noninduced littermates and careful interpretation of LSK percentages are critical.
c-Kit. Positivity is a cutoff judgment. HSCs are c-Kit high; downstream progenitors gradually lose
c-Kit. A conservative gate (only the brightest c-Kit) enriches for stem/MPP; a loose gate captures
more progenitors and gives a bigger sortable population for scRNA-seq.

subsections.title: SLAM HSC Resolution
subsections.body:
SLAM = Signaling Lymphocyte Activation Molecule family . CD150 (SLAMF1) and CD48
(SLAMF2) were introduced by Kiel et al., 2005, Cell as a simpler, functionally validated HSC
gate.
Population Phenotype
LT-HSC LinSca-1+ c-Kit+ CD150+ CD48- (+/- CD34-,
Flt3-)
ST-HSC / MPP1 LSK CD150+ CD48CD34+ (Wilson 2008;
debated)
MPP2 (Mk/E-biased) LSK CD150+ CD48+
MPP3 (myeloid-biased) LSK CD150CD48+
MPP4 (lymphoid-primed, LMPP-like) LSK CD150CD48-/low Flt3+
The MPP1-4 framework is from Pietras et al., 2015, Cell Stem Cell . It operationalizes the CD150 x
CD48 quadrant biologically:
• CD150+CD48- = stem compartment (HSC +/- MPP1).
• CD150+CD48+ = Mk/E-primed progenitors (MPP2) — important in stress erythropoiesis.
• CD150-CD48+ = myeloid-primed (MPP3).
• CD150-CD48Flt3+ = lymphoid-primed (MPP4/LMPP).
CD34 subdivision of the LT-HSC gate is where the field is unsettled. The Morrison lab treats
LT-HSC as CD34- (within LSK CD150+CD48-); Wilson et al., 2008, Cell and others argue CD34+
LT-HSC contains dormant HSCs. For the student’s thesis, the safe framing: define LT-HSC as LSK
CD150+CD48and note whether CD34 is included. MPP1 is the CD34+ subset of that gate and is
sometimes treated as ST-HSC.
Flt3/CD135 gating for MPP4 is the classic LMPP definition (Adolfsson 2005). Flt3 is dim and continuous; a good FMO is essential.

subsections.title: Myeloid Progenitors (LK gate)
subsections.body:
LinSca-1- c-Kit+ (the LK fraction) contains committed myeloid progenitors. Akashi 2000 and
Kondo 1997 define:
Population Phenotype
CMP (common myeloid) LinSca-1- c-Kit+ CD34+ CD16/32 low
GMP (granulocyte-monocyte) LK CD34+ CD16/32 high
MEP (megakaryocyte-erythroid) LK CD34CD16/32 low
The Akashi/Kondo framework has been complicated by continuum models ( Notta 2016, Science ;
Paul 2015, Cell) that show these are not clean populations but a gradient of biased progenitors. For

the student’s work, CMP/GMP/MEP frequencies remain the standard flow readout for myeloid
skewing in DCAF7-KO marrow, with the caveat that scRNA-seq trajectory is the more refined view.

subsections.title: Lymphoid Progenitors
subsections.body:
CLP (common lymphoid progenitor): LinSca-1 low c-Kit low IL7R+ Flt3+ . Kondo 1997 definition. The Sca-1 low / c-Kit low gates distinguish CLP from LSK; IL7R (CD127) is the positive
selector.
Further subsets: B-biased, T-biased, NK-biased progenitors use additional markers (Ly6D for Bbiased, CCR9 for T-biased).

subsections.title: Mature Lineages
subsections.body:
Lineage Markers
Neutrophils CD11b+ Gr-1 high (Ly6G high)
Monocytes CD11b+ Gr-1 mid (Ly6C+)
Erythroid progenitors (R1-R5) Ter119 x CD71; Socolovsky 2001 staging: R1
proerythroblast (Ter119 med, CD71 high),
R2-R4 progressive maturation, R5 mature RBC
(Ter119+ CD71-)
B cells, pro/pre B220+ CD43+/-, IgM-, IgD- (Hardy fractions
A-F)
B cells, mature B220+ IgM+ IgD+; follicular (CD21 mid
CD23+) vs marginal zone (CD21 high CD23-)
T cells, peripheral CD3+ CD4+ or CD8+
Thymocytes DN1-4 via CD44/CD25 on CD4-CD8gate;
then DP , SP
Megakaryocytes CD41+ CD42d+; large FSC — need expanded
FSC gate to include polyploid cells
NK cells NK1.1+ (B6-only allotype), NKp46+ (universal)

sections.title: Human HSC and Progenitor Gating (iPSC Organoid Work)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
For the student’s iPSC-derived hematopoietic organoid and engraftment experiments, the human
HSC phenotype applies.
The Notta 2011 benchmark (Notta et al., 2011, Science): the most primitive human HSC is
CD34+ CD38CD45RACD90+ CD49f+
At single-cell resolution this population shows long-term multilineage engraftment in NSG mice.

Marker Meaning in human
CD34 Pan-stem/progenitor marker; in human
includes HSC through committed progenitors
CD38 Negative on HSC/MPP; positive on committed
progenitors
CD45RA Negative on HSC/MPP; positive on
lymphoid-committed
CD90 (Thy1) Positive on HSC; negative on MPP
CD49f (ITGA6) Positive on most primitive HSC; bright staining
on LT-HSC
CD133 (PROM1) Supports LTC-IC activity; useful for cord-blood
HSC enrichment
ALDH high Functional (Aldefluor assay); enriches HSPC
Critical species difference. CD34 is an HSC marker in human but a committed-progenitor
marker in mouse — the inverse. A CD34+ mouse LSK cell is less primitive than a CD34LSK
cell. A CD34+ human cell is more primitive than a CD34human cell (in the lin-negative context).
Committee members love this question.
Student’s organoid engraftment protocol. iPSC-derived hematopoietic organoids are generated,
the CD34+ HSPC fraction is isolated, electroporated with Cas9-RNP targeting DCAF7, and either
(a) re-aggregated into organoid or (b) transplanted into NSG/NBSGW mice. Engraftment is measured as %CD45+CD34+ (human) in recipient marrow/PB at 8-16 weeks. Additional markers
(CD38, CD90, CD49f) can refine which fraction of engrafted cells retain HSC phenotype.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Analyzers. No physical separation; read samples fast (10-50k events/s on modern instruments).
Examples: BD LSRFortessa, BD FACSymphony A3/A5, Cytek Aurora (spectral analyzer), Beckman CytoFLEX.
Sorters. Physically divert individual cells into collection tubes or plates based on gated criteria.
Sorter Technology Key features
BD FACSAria /
FACSymphony S6
Jet-in-air droplet Fast (10-30k cells/s sort),
aerosol-generating, BSL2+
required for primary human
Sony MA900 / SH800 Microfluidic chip-based or jet Semi-automated; good for
routine sorts
Bigfoot (Thermo) Droplet, high-speed spectral Up to 6-way sort, spectral
capable

Sorter Technology Key features
MoFlo (Beckman) Traditional droplet Classic workhorse; flexible
MACSQuant Tyto Microfluidic closed-cartridge No aerosol, GMP-compatible,
disposable cartridge
MACSQuant Tyto — key for the student’s workflow.
The Tyto is architecturally different from droplet sorters. Sample is loaded into a disposable singleuse cartridge containing a microfluidic channel with a valve. Cells flow past the laser; gated events
trigger the valve to divert them into a “positive” chamber while non-target events flow to “negative.” No aerosols are generated because the system is fully closed. No sheath fluid — the cartridge
contains its own buffer. This has consequences:
• Advantages. Sterile/GMP-compatible (important for clinical HSPC products); no aerosol containment required (BSL2 work is easier); gentle on cells (low pressure, no droplet shearing);
excellent for rare populations because the entire sample is recoverable if unsorted cells are collected too.
• Disadvantages. Slower than droplet sorters (typically ~3-10k events/s processed, lower sort
rate for rare events); limited to 2-way sorting (positive/negative); cartridges are consumables
and add cost per sort.
• Why use it for scRNA-seq. Cleaner (no sheath-fluid contamination of 10X lanes with droplet
break-off debris); higher post-sort viability (important for the >80% viability threshold for 10X
loading); closed system reduces contamination risk for downstream library prep.
Sort purity vs yield. Every sorter has a purity-yield trade-off set by the droplet anti-coincidence
logic (on droplet sorters) or by the valve timing window (on Tyto). “Purity mode” rejects any event
where a contaminating cell is within the coincidence window; “yield mode” accepts. For scRNAseq input, purity matters more than yield because contaminating cells get sequenced and confuse
trajectory analysis.
Post-sort purity check. Re-analyze 2-3% of sorted product through the same instrument (or a quick
analyzer) to confirm target population enrichment (typically >90-95% purity expected). Report this
in publications.
For scRNA-seq specifically: - Sort into PBS + 0.04% BSA (10X recommendation) or PBS + 0.5%
BSA with low RNase, kept on ice. - Process within 30 minutes of sort completion to minimize
stress-response gene induction (JUN, FOS, HSP — these appear in any “dissociation signature” and
confound analysis; Machado et al., 2021; van den Brink 2017). - Viability >80% before loading 10X;
below this, ambient RNA/empty-droplet calls distort the dataset. - Count on a hemocytometer or
Countess after sort; target concentration per 10X v3.1 loading table.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Surface markers are accessible to antibodies in intact cells. Intracellular antigens (transcription
factors, phospho-epitopes, cytokines, cell cycle markers) require fixation (to immobilize antigens)

and permeabilization (to let antibody in).
General workflow.
1. Surface stain (if including surface markers) on live cells in the standard buffer.
2. Fix with 2-4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 10-20 min at RT or on ice.
3. Permeabilize:
• Saponin (0.1-0.5%) — reversible, requires saponin in all subsequent buffers; good for cytokines.
• T riton X-100(0.1%) — harsher; good for some TFs.
• eBioscience FOXP3/T ranscription Factor Buffer set — standard for nuclear TFs (FOXP3,
Tbet, GATA3).
• Methanol (90% ice-cold, 30 min) — required for phospho-epitopes; permeabilizes and denatures simultaneously .
4. Intracellular antibody stain in the same permeabilization buffer.
5. Wash and acquire.
Phospho-flow. For pSTAT1, pSTAT3, pSTAT5, pERK, pAKT. Reference: Krutzik and Nolan, 2003,
Cytometry A. Cells are typically stimulated briefly (e.g., IFN-alpha for pSTAT1, EPO for pSTAT5),
then immediately fixed with PFA to freeze the phospho-state, then methanol-permeabilized. Critical details: methanol must be ice-cold; many surface markers and most tandem dyes do not survive
methanol (plan surface panel accordingly — BV421, BV510, AF647 are relatively methanol-robust;
PE-Cy7 is not).
Ki-67 is nuclear; pairs with DAPI for cell cycle (G0 = Ki-67DAPI 2n, G1 = Ki-67+ DAPI 2n,
S/G2/M = Ki-67+ DAPI >2n). The Tbet/FOXP3 buffer works well for Ki-67.
Lineage TFs. FOXP3 (Tregs), GATA3 (Th2, MEP , mast cells), TBET (Th1), PU.1 (myeloid) are all
nuclear and require TF buffer permeabilization.
IFN-stimulated proteins (IFIT3, MX1, ISG15) are cytosolic and stain well with the same TF buffer
or with saponin-based protocols. For DCAF7 work, if the student wants to measure ISG induction
at the protein level post-perturbation, this is the protocol.

sections.title: Single-Cell Sorting for scRNA-seq and Functional Assays

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Index sort. The sorter records the full FACS parameter vector for every single cell placed into a
plate well (or, in some workflows, links to droplet-based identifiers). After sequencing, the student
can retrospectively link each cell’s transcriptome to its surface phenotype — this is the “best of both
worlds” approach for trajectory stratification.
Plate-based scRNA-seq (SmartSeq2, SmartSeq3). Sort one cell per well of a 96- or 384-well plate
pre-loaded with lysis buffer. Full-length transcript coverage. Lower throughput (~384 cells/plate)
but higher per-cell depth and better for low-expression transcript detection and isoform analysis.
Expensive per cell.
Droplet scRNA-seq (10X Genomics Chromium). Bulk-sort the population (LSKs, CD34+, etc.)
into PBS + 0.04% BSA, load onto the Chromium controller, which encapsulates individual cells

with gel beads carrying unique barcodes in oil droplets (GEMs). Reverse transcription happens
in-droplet; barcode tags every transcript with a cell-of-origin ID. Higher throughput (5,000-10,000
cells per lane); 3’- or 5’-end sequencing only (shorter reads, no isoform info without add-ons).
Student’s workflow.
1. Harvest bone marrow from 8-12 week Rosa26-CreERT2; DCAF7^fl/fl (tamoxifen-induced) or
WT.
2. ACK-lyse RBCs, Fc-block.
3. Stain Lin cocktail (FITC), c-Kit (APC), Sca-1 (PE-Cy7), CD150 (BV605), CD48 (BV711), DAPI.
4. Sort LSKs on MACSQuant Tyto directly into PBS + 0.04% BSA on ice.
5. Count; assess viability; adjust to 1000 cells/uL.
6. Load 10X Chromium v3.1 (GEX only) targeting 10,000 cells per lane.
7. Library prep per 10X protocol; sequence on Illumina NovaSeq (50k reads/cell target).
8. Analyze in Seurat: QC (nFeature 200-6000, %mito <10), normalize, integrate (Harmony by
batch), cluster, annotate (reference-map to Tabula Muris or Dahlin 2018 LK-LSK reference).
9. Trajectory: Monocle 3 or Slingshot from LT-HSC anchor through MPP subsets to committed
progenitors.
Quality metrics.
Metric Target
nFeature per cell 1500-6000 (LSK)
nCount per cell 5000-30000
% mitochondrial reads <10% (<5% preferred)
Doublet rate <5% post-DoubletFinder/Scrublet
Cells passing QC >70% of loaded

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Flow phenotype without functional validation is not enough. CFU assays quantify clonogenic
progenitor activity .
Methylcellulose CFU. Sort LSK or Lincells (typically 500-2000 per plate) into cytokinesupplemented methylcellulose medium.
Medium Use
MethoCult M3434 Mouse, full cytokine cocktail (SCF, IL-3, IL-6,
EPO) — supports CFU-GM, BFU-E,
CFU-GEMM, CFU-E
MethoCult M3630 Mouse, pre-B CFU
MethoCult H4435 Human, similar full cocktail

Medium Use
MethoCult H4034 Optimum Human, optimized
Colony types (scored at day 7-14).
Colony Morphology Cytokine dependence
CFU-E Small, tight, red EPO-dependent, day 2-3
BFU-E Large multi-cluster, red SCF + EPO; day 7-14
CFU-GM Granulocyte/macrophage mixed SCF, IL-3, IL-6
CFU-M Macrophage only M-CSF / IL-3
CFU-G Granulocyte only G-CSF / IL-3
CFU-GEMM Mixed (most primitive) Full cocktail
Serial replating. Pick colonies (or pool plates), re-plate into fresh methylcellulose. Normal
HSPCs replate 2-3 times and lose capacity . Cells with enhanced self-renewal (MLL-AF9,
HoxA9-overexpressing, leukemic transformation) replate 5+ times. Serial replating is therefore a
self-renewal proxy .
Student’s use. Parallel CFU assays on WT vs DCAF7-KO (genetic) and WT +/- BC18630 (pharmacologic DCAF7 inhibitor) LSK or Lincells. Expected readouts:
• Total CFU count (colony-forming efficiency).
• Colony type distribution (myeloid vs erythroid skewing).
• Colony size (proliferation capacity).
• Replating capacity (self-renewal proxy).
This complements flow and scRNA-seq by providing a functional, lineage-output readout of the
same perturbation.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Debris and doublets.
• Debris: excluded on FSC/SSC via a loose live-lymphocyte-ish gate (but keep wide enough to
capture large megakaryocytes if relevant).
• Doublets: FSC-A x FSC-H (primary); supplement with SSC-A x SSC-H for stricter exclusion.
True singlets are diagonal; doublets deviate upward in area.
Dead cells. Always include a viability dye and gate dead cells out early (right after singlets). Dead
cells non-specifically bind antibodies and cause false-positive signals in every channel. For fixed
samples, use amine-reactive LIVE/DEAD Fixable (stained before fixation).

Autofluorescence. Granulocytes are highly autofluorescent in FITC/PE channels and moderately
so in PerCP-Cy5.5. Always check the unstained granulocyte gate. For granulocyte-heavy samples
(peripheral blood, inflamed marrow), consider moving key markers away from FITC/PE, or use
spectral flow (which can unmix AF explicitly).
RBC contamination. Mouse bone marrow and human PB have large RBC fractions that dominate
events, slow the cytometer, and increase debris.
• ACK lysis: ammonium chloride buffer, 3-5 min RT, quench with PBS. Standard for BM and PB.
• Ficoll density gradient : for human PB/cord blood; gives mononuclear cell fraction.
• No ACK needed for spleen if mashed gently; for liver (fetal or adult), ACK is recommended.
Fc receptor non-specific binding. Mouse myeloid cells, B cells, and progenitors express Fc receptors (CD16, CD32, CD64) that bind antibody Fc regions non-specifically . Always pre-incubate
with anti-CD16/32 (clone 2.4G2) “Fc block” for 10 min before adding the stain cocktail. Human
equivalent: TruStain FcX or pooled human IgG.
Spillover spread. Use the SSM (Nguyen 2013) to identify problem channel pairs pre-acquisition;
redesign rather than compensate heroically .
Batch effects.
• Run all samples on the same day if possible.
• Same instrument, same voltages, same antibody lots.
• Acquire controls (unstained, FMOs) at the start and end of the run.
• If multi-day acquisition is unavoidable, include bridge samples (same biological reference in
every batch) and normalize in silico (e.g., CytoNorm).
Sort-specific issues.
• Nozzle size: 70 um for lymphocytes; 100 um for larger cells (megakaryocytes, some progenitors
on swollen-cell days).
• Pressure: lower pressure = gentler but slower; higher pressure = more shear.
• Temperature: 4 C sample cooler to preserve viability .
• Collection tube pre-coating: 0.5-1% BSA or FBS in the collection tube prevents cells from sticking to plastic.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Building on L8 and L10:
• Sort into 10X-compatible buffer. PBS + 0.04% BSA (Ca2+/Mg2+-free). No FBS (complement
activity). No EDTA if downstream enzymes care.
• Viability >80% required. Ambient RNA from dead cells contaminates all droplets; SoupX or
CellBender can partially correct but prevention is better.
• T arget ~10,000 cells loaded on a 10X v3.1 lane to capture ~7,000-8,000 high-quality cells after
QC and doublet removal.

• Chromium v3.1 vs GEM-X. GEM-X is the newer generation with higher capture efficiency and
better sensitivity; if available, prefer it.
• Demultiplexing strategies for multiplexed samples.
– CITE-seq HTOs (hashtag antibodies) : each sample is labeled with a unique oligo-tagged
antibody against CD45 or other pan-hematopoietic marker; pool samples pre-loading; demultiplex computationally (HTODemux, demuxmix). Reduces batch effect within a lane.
– Genetic demultiplexing : for CD45.1/CD45.2 congenic mixes or cross-strain samples, use
souporcell, vireo, or demuxlet with SNP information.
• Integration. Seurat CCA (anchoring), Harmony (iterative clustering-based correction), scVI
(deep-learning-based); Harmony is the current mid-range default for modest batch effects.
• T rajectory inference. Monocle 3 (DDRTree/UMAP-based pseudotime), Slingshot (principal
curves through clusters), PAGA (abstract graph). For LSK hematopoietic hierarchy , Monocle 3
with explicit root node at the LT-HSC cluster is standard; validate with RNA velocity (scV elo)
for direction.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
• Kiel et al., 2005, Cell — SLAM markers (CD150/CD48) for mouse HSC identification.
• Notta et al., 2011, Science — CD34+CD38-CD45RA-CD90+CD49f+ as the human HSC benchmark.
• Pietras et al., 2015, Cell Stem Cell — MPP1-4 framework based on CD150/CD48/Flt3 within
LSK.
• Wilson et al., 2008, Cell — CD34 and dormant HSC biology .
• Essers et al., 2009, Nature — IFNalpha activates HSCs; Sca-1 as an ISG.
• Akashi et al., 2000, Nature and Kondo et al., 1997, Cell — CMP/GMP/MEP and CLP frameworks.
• Socolovsky et al., 2001, Cell — R1-R5 erythroid staging via Ter119/CD71.
• Krutzik and Nolan, 2003, Cytometry A — phospho-flow methodology .
• Nguyen et al., 2013, Cytometry A — Spillover Spreading Matrix for panel design.
• Notta et al., 2016, Science — revised continuum model of human myeloid progenitors.
• Adolfsson et al., 2005, Cell — Flt3+ LMPP definition.
• Dahlin et al., 2018, Blood — single-cell reference map of mouse LK/LSK compartment.
• Machado et al., 2021 and van den Brink et al., 2017 — dissociation-induced stress signatures
in scRNA-seq.
End of Section L. Cross-reference: Section F (mouse genetic models, Cre systems) for the Rosa26-CreERT2
vs Mx1-Cre decision; Section K (scRNA-seq analysis) for downstream Seurat/Monocle workflow; Section
M (functional assays, transplantation) for CFU and in vivo engraftment quantitation.
Ubiquitinomics Methodology

Preamble and Scope
This section is designed as the technical backbone for Aim 1b of the thesis, which proposes to use
ubiquitin proteomics to define the biochemical mechanism of DCAF7-mediated IFIT3 degradation and to map the specific ubiquitination sites on IFIT3. Because the committee chair studies the
ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS) and interferon biology , he will expect a near-immediate, fluent
command of mass-spectrometry-based proteomics, the K-epsilon-GG (K-εGG) diGly workflow,
the conceptual logic that allows one to call something a “CRL substrate,” and the set of orthogonal
validation experiments that differentiate a real CRL-DCAF7-IFIT3 axis from indirect or pleiotropic
effects. This section therefore walks from the ground up through bottom-up proteomics, quantitative labeling, the diGly method and its confounders (especially the NEDD8/ISG15 remnant
problem, which is directly relevant to an ISG-centric thesis), the Buckley 2012 paradigm paper, the
orthogonal toolkit for CRL substrate discovery , the validation burden of proof, mass-spec concepts
likely to be probed during questioning, and finally practical reagents, software and anticipated
committee questions.
The student should read this alongside Section L (CRL4 biochemistry) and Section N (IFN/ISG
biology); the three form an integrated unit for the Aim 1b defense.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Bottom-up (or “shotgun”) proteomics is the dominant paradigm for unbiased protein identification and quantification. A cell or tissue lysate is denatured and reduced/alkylated, proteins are
digested proteolytically (most commonly with trypsin, which cleaves C-terminal to lysine and arginine except when followed by proline), and the resulting peptide mixture is separated by reversephase liquid chromatography (LC, usually C18) coupled online to a tandem mass spectrometer
(MS/MS). The instrument measures precursor peptide masses (MS1) and fragments selected precursors into sequence-informative band y-ions (MS2). Software then matches the fragmentation
spectra to a theoretical digest of a proteome database (e.g., UniProt human) and infers proteins
from peptide-spectrum matches (PSMs).
Instrumentation
The workhorses relevant to the student’s current proteomics infrastructure and to contemporary
literature are:
Instrument Manufacturer Class Relevant strengths
Q Exactive HF-X Thermo Quadrupole-Orbitrap High-resolution DDA;
mature TMT and
SILAC workflows
Orbitrap Exploris 480 Thermo Quadrupole-Orbitrap Higher sensitivity ,
FAIMS Pro
compatible; improved
duty cycle

Instrument Manufacturer Class Relevant strengths
Orbitrap Astral Thermo Q-Orbitrap-Astral
hybrid
State-of-the-art DIA;
very high speed and
sensitivity; short
gradients
timsTOF Pro / SCP Bruker Trapped-ion-mobility
TOF
PASEF acquisition;
single-cell proteomics;
fast duty cycle
For a K-εGG experiment on 10-20 mg of input at typical scale with TMT multiplexing, Exploris 480
or Orbitrap Astral are current best choices; timsTOF Pro with diaPASEF is an increasingly common
alternative.
Acquisition Modes
Data-dependent acquisition (DDA) selects the top N most intense precursors in each MS1 scan
for fragmentation. This produces sparse, stochastic sampling of the peptide population and has
been the standard for SILAC and TMT experiments.
Data-independent acquisition (DIA / SWATH) fragments all precursors within fixed isolation
windows across the full m/z range. This yields fully digital, reproducible peptide maps at the cost
of more complex deconvolution. DIA-NN and Spectronaut are the reference analysis tools. DIA
is increasingly used for label-free K-εGG and is attractive for the student’s future work because it
tolerates low-input and primary samples better than TMT.
Software Landscape
Tool Origin Best fit
SEQUEST / Proteome
Discoverer
Yates / Thermo Commercial, TMT workflows
MaxQuant Cox & Mann 2008 Nat
Biotechnol
Free; SILAC and LFQ gold
standard
FragPipe / MSFragger Nesvizhskii V ery fast; DDA and DIA;
open-search for PTMs
Spectronaut Biognosys Commercial DIA
DIA-NN Demichev Free DIA; neural network
Perseus Max Planck Downstream statistics, t-tests,
clustering, GSEA

Quantification Strategies at a Glance
Quantification can be label-free (LFQ, iBAQ, APEX for absolute approximations) or label-based
(SILAC metabolic labeling, TMT/iTRAQ chemical labeling). The next section enumerates these in
the depth the committee will expect.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
SILAC — Stable Isotope Labeling by Amino acids in Cell culture
SILAC (Mann lab, Ong & Mann 2002) metabolically incorporates heavy isotope-labeled arginine
(13C6 or 13C6-15N4) and lysine (13C6 or 13C6-15N2) into cellular proteins over five to seven doublings. Binary (heavy vs light) or triplex (heavy/medium/light) formats are standard. Quantification is inferred directly at MS1 from paired precursor peaks separated by a defined mass shift (e.g.,
+8 Da for 13C6-15N2-Lys, +10 Da for 13C6-15N4-Arg).
Strengths: near-perfect quantitative precision for pairwise comparisons because labels are mixed
before any biochemistry; uniquely well-matched to K-εGG when sample is a HEL-cell line pair
of DCAF7-WT vs DCAF7-KO. Limitations: requires dividing cells in defined media, cannot label
primary tissue, is capped at three channels (or five with neutron-encoded SILAC), and full labeling
consumes expensive dialyzed serum and specialized media.
The student’s preliminary global proteomics “SILAC-like” comparison of DCAF7-KO vs WT HEL
cells is a canonical, strong design for discovery; IFIT3 emerging as a top hit in that screen sets up
Aim 1b.
TMT — T andem Mass T ags
TMT (and its relatives iTRAQ and the newer TMTpro) are isobaric chemical tags that label peptide
N-termini and lysine ε-amines via NHS-ester chemistry after digestion. All tagged peptides from a
sample pool share the same precursor mass; upon fragmentation, low-mass reporter ions (126–134
Th in TMT-10plex, extended in TMTpro to 16and 18-plex) resolve the channel of origin. Samples are combined pre-MS, which is a major efficiency gain and eliminates run-to-run variation,
enabling up to 18 samples per acquisition.
TMT -SPS-MS3
Because standard MS2-based TMT suffers from precursor co-isolation (multiple peptides cofragmented in the same isolation window), reporter ratios are systematically compressed toward
unity (ratio compression). Synchronous Precursor Selection MS3 (SPS-MS3) , introduced by
Ting, McAlister and Gygi (Ting 2011 Nat Methods), selects multiple MS2 fragment ions, co-isolates
and HCD-fragments them together in an MS3 step, and measures reporters only from validated
substrate fragments. SPS-MS3 is the gold standard for accurate TMT quantification and is the
acquisition mode the student should propose for Aim 1b K-εGG TMT experiments, with the cost
of ~2× longer duty cycle.

Label-Free Quantification (LFQ)
MaxLFQ (Cox 2014) performs MS1-level intensity-based quantification across LC-MS runs with delayed normalization and composite intensity estimates; iBAQ (intensity-based absolute quantification) divides summed peptide intensity by the number of theoretically observable tryptic peptides
for approximate absolute abundance.
Strengths: works for any input, including primary MPN samples the student may use in later aims;
flexible; no label cost. Limitations: requires highly reproducible LC, more MS time per sample, and
more careful batch design.
Trade-off Summary
Strategy Best for Drawback
SILAC Binary cell-line comparisons
(the student’s WT vs
DCAF7-KO HELs)
Not compatible with primary
cells
TMT / TMTpro Many-condition,
multi-replicate experiments
(e.g., time-course on
dTAG-DCAF7)
Expensive labels; ratio
compression without SPS-MS3
LFQ Primary samples, flexible N Reproducibility burden
iBAQ / APEX Absolute abundance estimate Approximate

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Ubiquitinomics is methodologically cousin to phosphoproteomics: both rely on post-digestion
enrichment of modified peptides. The student should be able to speak to the broader PTM-MS
ecosystem.
• Phosphopeptide enrichment: titanium dioxide (TiO2) chromatography and iron-based immobilized metal affinity chromatography (Fe-IMAC) bind the acidic phosphate; SIMAC (sequential IMAC then TiO2) and MOAC (metal-oxide affinity chromatography) are refinements;
EasyPhos (Humphrey 2018) is a streamlined high-throughput hybrid.
• ETD / EThcD fragmentation: electron-transfer dissociation preserves labile PTMs (phospho-,
glyco-) better than HCD because it cleaves the N–Cα bond without ejecting the modification;
EThcD (Frese 2012) adds supplemental HCD energy to improve sequence coverage. Rarely
needed for K-εGG because the diGly remnant is a stable amide bond, but important for
phospho-degron mapping on DCAF7 substrates.
This matters for Aim 1b if IFIT3 turns out to be phospho-primed for DCAF7 recognition, analogous
to FBXW7-cMyc (see M5).

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This is the central technical platform for Aim 1b and must be understood in full.
Principle
Ubiquitin is a 76-amino-acid protein ending in Leu-Arg-Gly-Gly (LRGG). It is covalently attached
to substrate lysines through an isopeptide bond between its C-terminal Gly76 and the ε-amine of
a substrate lysine. When a ubiquitinated protein is digested by trypsin, cleavage occurs at Arg74
of ubiquitin, leaving a Gly-Gly (GG) remnant of 114.04 Da covalently attached to the modified
lysine ε-amine on the substrate peptide. This remnant is a mass-spectrometric signature that unambiguously identifies (with the caveats below) the modified residue.
Enrichment
Because ubiquitinated peptides are a small fraction of the tryptic digest, enrichment is essential.
