sections.title: The 30-second answer to “walk me through your methods”

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
“The project uses three tiers of evidence. First, a genetic tier: Rosa26-CreERT2 conditional KO in adult Jak2V617F mice, because the Mx1-Cre system confounds DCAF7
loss with Poly(I:C)-driven interferon. Second, a pharmacologic tier: BC18630, a recently
reported DCAF7-selective inhibitor, combined with PEG-IFN-α2a in matched mouse
models. Third, a biochemical tier: K-εGG ubiquitinomics with TMT multiplexing in
DCAF7-KO HEL cells to identify the substrates, with cycloheximide chase and SILAC
for orthogonal validation. The perinatal Aim 2 arm is a Vav-Cre time-course with flowsorted LSKs, paralleled by an Ifnar1 cross to test interferon-dependence and germ-free
rederivation to test the microbiota-driven perinatal IFN pulse component. Every tier
has orthogonal validation from at least one other tier — no single method’s failure collapses the thesis.”

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
1. (0:00–0:45) Why Rosa26-CreERT2 replaces Mx1-Cre (Section K): Poly(I:C) is both the Creinduction mechanism and a TLR3/RIG-I IFN inducer, making Mx1-Cre uniquely ill-suited
for an IFN-focused thesis. Rosa26-CreERT2 gives adult-onset, tamoxifen-inducible, IFNorthogonal deletion.
2. (0:45–1:30) Flow cytometry strategy (Section L): LSK/SLAM gating, MACSQuant Tyto for 10X
scRNA-seq compatibility , Sca-1 caveat in IFN contexts.
3. (1:30–2:30) K-εGG ubiquitinomics (Section M): Buckley 2012 paradigm, 16-plex TMTpro design, ISGylation controls.

4. (2:30–3:15) BC18630 pharmacology (Section N): Lear 2025 IC50, proposed in vivo doseresponse, combination with PEG-IFN-α2a.
5. (3:15–4:00) Aim 2 perinatal methods (Sections K + N): Vav-Cre time-course, Ifnar1 cross, germfree rederivation.
6. (4:00–4:30) AML and CHIP context (Sections I + J): why DCAF7 implications may extend to
MPN-BP and DTA-CHIP , and why the therapeutic framing matters for a translational PhD.
7. (4:30–5:00) Orthogonal validation matrix and the “no single failure collapses the thesis” argument.

sections.title: The three methodological papers you must be able to recite

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Buckley 2012 Cell Stem Cell — “The Human HSC Ubiquitinome”: 2,578 ubiquitinated proteins,
1,168 K-εGG sites, identified FBXW7-cMyc T58 and PSMD14 as regulated. This is the paradigm
we are extending to MPN HSPCs. Be able to state the numbers and the core finding.
Lear 2025 Science Advances — “Small-molecule DCAF7 inhibitors”: BC18630 and BC18813 characterized as DCAF7-selective binders; IC50 ~1.80 μM in our hands; in vivo activity demonstrated.
This is the pharmacologic tool enabling Aim 1a’s complementary arm.
Li 2020 Cell Stem Cell — “Perinatal type I interferon pulse”: Fetal skin and microbiota drive
a transient IFN pulse from E16 to ~P7, expanding progenitors and permitting FLT3-ITD-driven
leukemogenesis. This is the biological frame for Aim 2 and the reason the Vav-Cre P4 lethality is
interpretable as an IFN-termination failure.

sections.title: The methodological version of the 5-tier reading list

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Tier 1 — must be memorized: - Buckley 2012 Cell Stem Cell (K-εGG in HSCs) - Lear 2025 Sci
Adv (BC18630) - Li 2020 Cell Stem Cell (perinatal IFN) - Mullally 2010 Cancer Cell (Jak2V617F
conditional KI) - Emanuele 2011 Cell (DCAF substrate pipeline)
Tier 2 — read closely: - Kühn 1995 Science (Mx1-Cre) - Georgiades 2002 (Vav-Cre) - Feil 1996 PNAS
(CreERT2 tamoxifen) - Nabet 2018 Nat Chem Biol (dTAG system) - Kiel 2005 Cell (SLAM gating)
- Notta 2011 Science (human HSC phenotype) - Pikman 2006 PLOS Med (MPLW515L) - Mercier
2019 Cell Rep (CD45.1 fitness)
Tier 3 — know the headline: - Xu 2010 / Kim 2011 (K-εGG method) - Ting 2011 (SILAC) - Soucy
2009 (MLN4924) - Koren 2018 / Timms 2019 (degron grammar) - Essers 2009 Nature (IFN-α activates HSCs) - Krutzik 2003 (phospho-flow)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
“Tier 2 is the toolkit: conditional mouse genetics replacing the Poly(I:C)-confounded
Mx1-Cre with Rosa26-CreERT2, K-εGG ubiquitinomics with TMT-16plex multiplexing, BC18630 pharmacology complementing genetic ablation, flow cytometry with
LSK/SLAM gating for in vivo readouts and MACSQuant Tyto sorting for scRNA-seq,
and a contingency layer that ensures no single experimental failure collapses the
thesis.”

qanda.q: “Is CHIP a disease or a normal aging phenotype?
qanda.a: ” Both. CHIP reflects the natural clonal competition of a long-lived stem cell pool under sustained mutational input and selection pressure. At the population level, most CHIP carriers do not progress to hematologic malignancy . However, CHIP carries measurable excess cardiovascular and all-cause mortality , so it is clinically meaningful as a risk-stratification marker even when not itself a disease. The term “indeterminate potential” was chosen precisely to hold both truths.

qanda.q: “Y our MPN patients likely have pre-existing DNMT3A or TET2 CH. How does that affect your DCAF7 therapy hypothesis?
qanda.a: ” The DCAF7-IFIT3 mechanism is engaged downstream of JAK2V617F signaling and is therefore expected to confer selective sensitivity to the JAK2V617F clone. The underlying DTA clone is the reservoir from which JAK2V617F was originally acquired and is unlikely to be sensitive to the same mechanism. Clinical deployment of DCAF7 inhibition in MPN therefore has to plan for DTA persistence and DTA-driven molecular relapse. A combination strategy — DCAF7 inhibition for the JAK2V617F clone, an IL-1beta or inflammation-directed modality for the DTA clone — is a conceptually coherent path. Independent monitoring of DTA and JAK2V617F V AF during and after therapy is mandatory .

qanda.q: “IFN-gamma and IFN-alpha drive DNMT3A clonal expansion. Would chronic IFN-alpha therapy in MPN risk selecting for DTA clones?
qanda.a: ” Yes, this is a real and acknowledged concern. The Hasan 2013 and subsequent longitudinal datasets show that DTA V AF does not fall — and sometimes rises — during IFN-alpha therapy even as JAK2V617F V AF falls. The mechanism aligns with Avagyan 2021 (DNMT3A IFN-gamma resistance) and Hormaechea-Agulla 2021 (TET2 IFN-gamma resistance). Any DCAF7-based regimen that leverages IFN signaling inherits this risk. This does not invalidate the approach but dictates trial design: measure DTA separately , follow patients post-therapy for DTA-driven molecular relapse, and consider DTA-directed combinations.

qanda.q: “How would you detect residual disease in an MPN patient with DNMT3A + JAK2V617F CH after successful IFN-alpha (or DCAF7-based) therapy?
qanda.a: ” Droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) for JAK2V617F allele burden with a sensitivity of 0.01 to 0.1 percent V AF for the JAK2V617F arm, plus an independent targeted deep sequencing or ddPCR assay for the patient’s specific DNMT3A (or TET2) mutation — which is almost always a private mutation that needs patient-specific assay design. Report DTA and JAK2V617F V AF separately . A patient with JAK2V617F at 0 percent but DNMT3A at 30 percent is in a deep JAK2 response but is still at lifelong risk of JAK2V617F re-emergence from the DTA reservoir, and their relapse-monitoring schedule should reflect that.

qanda.q: “Is there evidence DCAF7 is altered in CH?
qanda.a: ” Not established. DCAF7 has not been reported as a recurrent CH driver in any large cohort. The student should note explicitly that the Sakaguchi 2013 JMML exome study , which has been miscited in some derivative summaries as implicating DCAF7, does not in fact contain DCAF7 as a recurrent lesion. Whether DCAF7 expression or function is altered in CH-associated conditions (distinct from mutation) is an open empirical question that the thesis would be well-positioned to address in patient samples.

qanda.q: “If DCAF7 loss stabilizes IFIT3 and potentiates IFN signaling, would DCAF7 loss in an HSC produce a CH-like or an anti-CH phenotype?
qanda.a: ” The prediction is context-dependent. In a wild-type HSC pool under steady-state IFN tone, DCAF7 loss would be expected to amplify IFN responsiveness and could drive HSC exhaustion — an antiCH phenotype. In a JAK2V617F HSC under cytokine-independent signaling, DCAF7 loss would amplify the IFIT3-dependent arm and could sensitize the clone to depletion. These predictions motivate specific experiments: lineage-tracing DCAF7-floxed mice with or without JAK2V617F and serial HSC competitive transplants under chronic low-dose IFN.

qanda.q: “How does VEXAS inform your thinking about UPS lesions producing CH-like states?
qanda.a: ” VEXAS (Beck 2020 NEJM) is a somatic UBA1 mutation restricted to myeloid progenitors that produces a systemic IFN-and-inflammation-driven disease with vacuoles in myeloid precursors, cy111 topenias, and a high mortality . It is a human experiment showing that a somatic UPS lesion can produce a CH-like architecture with severe phenotypic consequences, and it also shows that UPS disruption in HSC lineages can unleash rather than suppress IFN signaling. Both observations are consistent with — and analytically relevant to — the DCAF7-IFIT3 mechanism.

qanda.q: “What cohort or model system would you use to test whether DCAF7 expression correlates with CH trajectory?
qanda.a: ” Three approaches. First, retrospective analysis of DCAF7 transcript and protein levels in sorted HSC/MPP populations from CHIP carriers versus age-matched non-carriers, stratified by driver gene. Second, DCAF7 conditional knockout crossed with Jak2-V617F knock-in mice, with serial competitive transplantation and V AF tracking across primary and secondary recipients. Third, ex vivo patient HSC assays (colony formation, stromal co-culture) with DCAF7 knockdown or pharmacologic inhibition to measure effects on JAK2V617F and DTA clones separately .

qanda.q: “Draw the connection between your thesis and the chair’s interest in UPS plus IFN in two sentences.”
qanda.a: DCAF7, as a CRL4 substrate receptor, directly couples the ubiquitin-proteasome system to the interferon axis by degrading IFIT3, a key interferon-stimulated scaffold; loss of DCAF7 in JAK2V617F MPN cells therefore stabilizes IFIT3 and amplifies IFN signaling output. This single mechanism sits at the intersection of CRL biology , innate-immune signaling, and a clinically important myeloid clone, and it generalizes to a broader framework in which UPS lesions (VEXAS, CRL4 dysregulation, ISGylation machinery) modulate IFN tone in the aging hematopoietic system and thereby shape CH trajectory .

qanda.q: Why switch from Mx1-Cre to Rosa26-CreER for Aim 1a?
qanda.a: Poly(I:C) is a TLR3/MDA5/RIG-I agonist that induces endogenous Type I IFN. Because the DCAF7 thesis is centered on IFN-responsive biology (IFIT3, ISG programs, Jak2V617F-inflamed states), Mx1-Cre cannot provide an IFN-naive control arm. Rosa26-CreERT2 with tamoxifen induces deletion without triggering IFN, making it the correct driver for an IFN-clean experiment. The preliminary Mx1-Cre data are hypothesis-generating; the Rosa26-CreERT2 data will be definitive.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: T amoxifen itself affects HSCs — how do you control for this?
qanda.a: Sanchez-Aguilera 2014 and Kim 2013 documented direct HSC-activating effects of tamoxifen. Controls: (i) tamoxifen-treated Cre-negative littermates as the primary comparator; (ii) 2–4 week washout before phenotypic assay; (iii) steady-state baseline measurements before Jak2V617F induction; (iv) optional consistency check with tamoxifen-chow induction to distinguish bolus from continuous exposure effects.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: Why not use Scl-CreER or Fgd5-CreER for HSC-specific deletion?
qanda.a: Both are valid HSC-enriched inducible drivers. Fgd5-CreER is arguably the cleanest HSC driver (Gazit 2014 JEM). The choice of Rosa26-CreERT2 here reflects a priority for broader sampling (HSCs + progenitors) in which DCAF7 may also act, and for higher recombination efficiency per HSC. A stretch experiment using Fgd5-CreER to test HSC-autonomous deletion specifically would complement the Rosa26-CreERT2 data and is a reasonable committee-suggested extension. Using both would triangulate cell-of-origin.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: Y our Vav-Cre DCAF7 KO has a P4 phenotype — could that be off-target Cre toxicity in endothelium?
qanda.a: Vav-Cre expresses in endothelium, so the concern is legitimate. Mitigations: (i) Tek-Cre; Dcaf7-flox to isolate an endothelial contribution; (ii) compare to Rosa26-CreERT2 adult deletion; (iii) fetal liver or neonatal BM transplant into wild-type irradiated recipients to test hematopoietic-cell-autonomy; (iv) vasculature histopathology in moribund pups; (v) Vav-iCre as an alternative with reduced leak.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: CD45.1 competitors have been shown to have their own fitness phenotype — how do you control?
qanda.a: Mercier 2019 Exp Hematol. Use CD45.1/CD45.2 F1 double-positive competitor; or perform armreversal transplants with test allele on CD45.1 background; or at minimum acknowledge and match the CD45.1 colony to the comparator to minimize confounding.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: Y our Jak2V617F is Mullally’s allele. Why not Akada or Li?
qanda.a: Mullally’s allele is the most widely used, produces a reproducible PV phenotype, is compatible with both Mx1-Cre and Rosa26-CreERT2, and has the richest comparator literature. Akada (BAC, heterozygous PV) and Li (Rosa26-JAK2V617F) are valuable for specific purposes (endogenous stoichiometry , cell-of-origin) but provide less standardized backgrounds. A future cross to confirm allele-independence is reasonable.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: How do you know your Dcaf7-floxed allele gives a true null?
qanda.a: RT-qPCR across excised junction, western on sorted HSPCs with validated antibody , and a rescue experiment with Dcaf7 cDNA re-expression. A second independent allele (e.g., CRISPR-generated frameshift) strengthens the claim.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: How will you measure Cre recombination efficiency in your experiments?
qanda.a: Cross to R26-mTmG reporter; quantify GFP-positive fraction in sorted HSPCs by flow. Confirm with excision-sensitive qPCR on sorted LSK. Require >80% per-cell efficiency before committing to definitive cohorts.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: Have you considered clonal dynamics in your phenotype?
qanda.a: Population-level scRNA-seq may conflate uniform expansion with clonal dominance. A LARRYstyle barcoded transplant would resolve the question. This is a reasonable stretch goal.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: How will you rule out microbiome-driven phenotypic variability?
qanda.a: Co-house experimental and control littermates from weaning; use heterozygous intercross breeding so all genotypes share cage; maintain consistent vendor, facility , diet, and bedding; pilot cohorts across at least two independent litters before definitive conclusions.
qanda.section: Mouse Genetics and Cre Recombinase Systems

qanda.q: “Walk me through what physically happens to a peptide between the tryptic digest and a protein identification in the output table.”
qanda.a: The peptide is separated on a reverse-phase C18 nanocolumn by increasing acetonitrile gradient, eluted into an electrospray source where it is ionized (typically 2+/3+ protonated), mass-measured in the Orbitrap at high resolution, selected by the quadrupole, fragmented by HCD (higher-energy collisional dissociation), and its band y-ion spectrum measured. The spectrum is scored against a target-decoy database (UniProt human + reversed sequences) via MSFragger or Andromeda; peptide-spectrum matches are filtered to 1% FDR via target-decoy; peptides are assembled into proteins with parsimony rules, and proteins are filtered at 1% protein FDR.

qanda.q: “Why not just use TMT for your entire Aim 1b?”
qanda.a: TMT with SPS-MS3 will be the primary strategy for the K-εGG experiment because multiplexing lets us include the necessary pharmacological controls (MLN4924, MG132, vehicle, plus genotype) in a single 16-plex, which is essential for clean cross-condition comparisons. SILAC will be used for a confirmatory pairwise WT vs KO K-εGG because it gives the best quantitative precision on a binary contrast and IFIT3 was already a SILAC hit at the whole-proteome level.

qanda.q: “What exactly is the mass shift you are searching for in MaxQuant for a K-εGG experiment?”
qanda.a: 114.04293 Da on lysine as a variable modification, with one obligate missed cleavage at the modified lysine (because the GG modification blocks tryptic cleavage at that K). Static carbamidomethyl (+57.0215) on Cys and variable oxidation on Met.

qanda.q: “Name a landmark K-GG proteomics study relevant to hematopoietic stem cells and tell me what they found about cMyc.”
qanda.a: Buckley et al., Cell Stem Cell 2012, identified 2,578 ubiquitination sites in pluripotent cells and pinpointed FBXW7-mediated ubiquitination of cMyc on a Thr58 phospho-degron as a regulator of pluripotency . This framed the UPS as an active axis of stem-cell identity , not a housekeeping machine.

qanda.q: “If you had to pick the single most decisive experiment, what would it be?”
qanda.a: The in vitro ubiquitination assay with purified CRL4-DCAF7, because it demonstrates direct catalytic activity and is independent of cellular context. Next would be K-to-R mutagenesis of the mapped sites combined with re-expression in DCAF7-KO cells, because it closes the loop between the mapped lysines and functional turnover.

qanda.q: “Explain target-decoy FDR as if I were a first-year student.”
qanda.a: We know the mass spectra came from real peptides in the sample, but we don’t know which database entries to assign them to, so some assignments are wrong. We add a fake proteome — the real proteome with its sequences reversed — that cannot produce any real matches. Any PSM matching the reversed decoy is, by construction, a false match. If at a given score threshold we see 100 decoy PSMs and 10,000 target PSMs, we estimate 1% of the targets are also false — that is the 1% FDR.

qanda.q: “How will you rule out that what you’re calling DCAF7-mediated ubiquitination of IFIT3 is actually HERC5-mediated ISGylation?”
qanda.a: ISG15 knockdown (siRNA or knockout) should not affect the K-εGG signal on IFIT3 sites that are DCAF7-dependent. MLN4924, which blocks both NEDD8 and does not block ISGylation, should phenocopy DCAF7-KO for true CRL substrates. We will also perform in vitro ubiquitination with purified recombinant ubiquitin (not ISG15), which directly demonstrates the modifier identity . Q1. “How will you distinguish direct from indirect DCAF7 substrates in your K-GG proteomics?” A: Direct substrates satisfy three criteria simultaneously: (i) K-εGG site signal decreases upon DCAF7-KO; (ii) protein abundance increases upon DCAF7-KO AND upon MLN4924 (both stabilize CRL substrates); (iii) physical interaction in DCAF7 IP-MS or TurboID proximity labeling. Filtering the K-εGG hit list through these three filters greatly narrows direct DCAF7-CRL4 substrates. For IFIT3 specifically , in vitro ubiquitination with purified CRL4-DCAF7 is the definitive direct test. Indirect substrates (accumulated because DCAF7-KO perturbs another E3 or a transcription factor) will fail either the physical interaction test or the in vitro reconstitution. Q2. “Why is IFIT3 more convincing as a DCAF7 substrate than other hits in your proteomics?” A: IFIT3 satisfies the early-stage burden of proof: it is stabilized at the protein level in DCAF7KO SILAC proteomics without a matched change in transcript (RNA-seq companion experiment), CHX chase shows extended half-life in DCAF7-KO, and it sits in a biologically coherent pathway — IFIT3 is a canonical interferon-stimulated gene, aligning with the IFN-centric thesis and the committee chair’s expertise in UPS + IFN. Pilot co-IP shows physical association. Other hits may satisfy one or two of these criteria but IFIT3 satisfies all of them, which is why it is the Aim 1b focus. Q3. “What would falsify your model?” A: Several experiments would kill the hypothesis: (i) no physical DCAF7-IFIT3 interaction in endogenous or tagged co-IP; (ii) in vitro ubiquitination of IFIT3 by purified CRL4-DCAF7 fails; (iii) K-to-R mutagenesis of the mapped ubiquitination sites fails to stabilize IFIT3 in DCAF7-WT cells; (iv) MLN4924 does not phenocopy DCAF7-KO for IFIT3 stabilization, which would mean IFIT3 turnover is not CRL-dependent; (v) residual K-εGG sites on IFIT3 are unchanged in DCAF7-KO. Any single one of these would force a major reinterpretation. Q4. “Are you considering ISGylation as an alternative explanation?” A: Yes, explicitly . IFIT3 is an ISG, HERC5 co-translationally ISGylates ISGs, and ISG15 leaves the same GG tryptic remnant as ubiquitin. I will control with ISG15 knockdown or knockout HELs and confirm that the DCAF7-dependent K-εGG signal on IFIT3 persists in ISG15-null conditions. I will also use MLN4924, which inhibits NEDD8 (not ISG15) and should phenocopy DCAF7-KO for true CRL ubiquitination; the in vitro ubiquitination assay uses purified recombinant ubiquitin, directly disambiguating the modifier. Q5. “Why use HEL cells for discovery and not primary MPN samples?” A: HEL cells are JAK2-V617F homozygous, chronically IFN-active, and provide the biological context of the thesis while being compatible with SILAC, with the ≥10 mg protein input required for K-εGG IP , and with CRISPR engineering for DCAF7-KO clones. Primary MPN CD34+ cells are limiting in material (typically <1 mg protein from a single aspirate), do not label metabolically with SILAC, and do not tolerate the multi-week expansion needed for KO clones. I will use HEL cells for mechanistic discovery and validate top findings (CHX chase, IFIT3 stabilization, ISGylation controls) in primary MPN CD34+ cells using LFQ proteomics and CHX-Western as the orthogonal readouts. Q6. “What’s the minimum fold-change threshold for a ‘real’ DCAF7 substrate?” A: Standard cutoffs are >1.5–2× with Benjamini-Hochberg-adjusted p < 0.05 across biological triplicate, but magnitude alone is not the primary filter. Orthogonal validation — MLN4924 phenocopy , IP-MS physical interaction, CHX chase, in vitro ubiquitination — is weighted more heavily than magnitude. A 1.8× K-εGG decrease that is reproducible and orthogonally validated outweighs a 5× change that fails orthogonal tests. That said, IFIT3 in the student’s SILAC data exceeds the 2× threshold comfortably . Q7. “What acquisition mode and instrument would you use?” A: Orbitrap Exploris 480 or Orbitrap Astral with TMTpro-16plex and SPS-MS3 for quantitative accuracy on TMT; MaxQuant or FragPipe TMT-Integrator for search; 1% site and peptide FDR. A confirmatory pairwise SILAC K-εGG experiment on the DCAF7-WT vs KO HELs will support the TMT experiment with binary high-precision quantification. Q8. “How many biological replicates?” A: Minimum three biological replicates per condition in the TMT-16plex; each replicate is an independent cell passage and independent lysis. For the SILAC arm, three label-swap replicates (heavy-KO/light-WT and label swap). Q9. “What is your expected number of K-εGG sites identified?” A: In a well-executed experiment from 15 mg of HEL-cell input with TMT-SPS-MS3 and highpH fractionation, 10,000–20,000 K-εGG sites across 4,000–6,000 proteins is standard (Buckley 2012 achieved 2,578 sites in less-deep early-era Orbitrap conditions; modern instruments go deeper). IFIT3 has ~20 lysines and I expect to localize 1–3 DCAF7-dependent sites with >2× reduction in KO. Q10. “How do you know DCAF7 is a substrate receptor and not just an adaptor of a different CRL?” A: DCAF7 contains WDR40 repeats with the canonical WDxR motif characteristic of DDB1-binding DCAFs (Gao 2015 eLife). Co-IP of DCAF7 pulls down endogenous DDB1 and CUL4; this is the physical signature of a CRL4 substrate receptor. The in vitro ubiquitination reconstitution uses recombinant CUL4A-DDB1-RBX1, confirming the identity of the CRL scaffold.

qanda.q: “How will you distinguish direct from indirect DCAF7 substrates in your K-GG proteomics?
qanda.a: ” A: Direct substrates satisfy three criteria simultaneously: (i) K-εGG site signal decreases upon DCAF7-KO; (ii) protein abundance increases upon DCAF7-KO AND upon MLN4924 (both stabilize CRL substrates); (iii) physical interaction in DCAF7 IP-MS or TurboID proximity labeling. Indirect substrates (accumulated because DCAF7-KO perturbs another E3 or a transcription factor) will fail either the physical interaction test or the in vitro reconstitution.

qanda.a: ” A: Several experiments would kill the hypothesis: (i) no physical DCAF7-IFIT3 interaction in endogenous or tagged co-IP; (ii) in vitro ubiquitination of IFIT3 by purified CRL4-DCAF7 fails; (iii) K-to-R mutagenesis of the mapped ubiquitination sites fails to stabilize IFIT3 in DCAF7-WT cells; (iv) MLN4924 does not phenocopy DCAF7-KO for IFIT3 stabilization, which would mean IFIT3 turnover is not CRL-dependent; (v) residual K-εGG sites on IFIT3 are unchanged in DCAF7-KO. Any single one of these would force a major reinterpretation.

qanda.a: ” A: Yes, explicitly . IFIT3 is an ISG, HERC5 co-translationally ISGylates ISGs, and ISG15 leaves the same GG tryptic remnant as ubiquitin. I will control with ISG15 knockdown or knockout HELs and confirm that the DCAF7-dependent K-εGG signal on IFIT3 persists in ISG15-null conditions. I will also use MLN4924, which inhibits NEDD8 (not ISG15) and should phenocopy DCAF7-KO for true CRL ubiquitination; the in vitro ubiquitination assay uses purified recombinant ubiquitin, directly disambiguating the modifier.

qanda.a: ” A: HEL cells are JAK2-V617F homozygous, chronically IFN-active, and provide the biological context of the thesis while being compatible with SILAC, with the ≥10 mg protein input required for K-εGG IP , and with CRISPR engineering for DCAF7-KO clones. I will use HEL cells for mechanistic discovery and validate top findings (CHX chase, IFIT3 stabilization, ISGylation controls) in primary MPN CD34+ cells using LFQ proteomics and CHX-Western as the orthogonal readouts.

qanda.q: “What’s the minimum fold-change threshold for a ‘real’ DCAF7 substrate?
qanda.a: ” A: Standard cutoffs are >1.5–2× with Benjamini-Hochberg-adjusted p < 0.05 across biological triplicate, but magnitude alone is not the primary filter. Orthogonal validation — MLN4924 phenocopy , IP-MS physical interaction, CHX chase, in vitro ubiquitination — is weighted more heavily than magnitude. A 1.8× K-εGG decrease that is reproducible and orthogonally validated outweighs a 5× change that fails orthogonal tests. That said, IFIT3 in the student’s SILAC data exceeds the 2× threshold comfortably .

qanda.q: “What acquisition mode and instrument would you use?
qanda.a: ” A: Orbitrap Exploris 480 or Orbitrap Astral with TMTpro-16plex and SPS-MS3 for quantitative accuracy on TMT; MaxQuant or FragPipe TMT-Integrator for search; 1% site and peptide FDR. A confirmatory pairwise SILAC K-εGG experiment on the DCAF7-WT vs KO HELs will support the TMT experiment with binary high-precision quantification.

qanda.q: “How many biological replicates?
qanda.a: ” A: Minimum three biological replicates per condition in the TMT-16plex; each replicate is an independent cell passage and independent lysis. For the SILAC arm, three label-swap replicates (heavy-KO/light-WT and label swap).

qanda.q: “What is your expected number of K-εGG sites identified?
qanda.a: ” A: In a well-executed experiment from 15 mg of HEL-cell input with TMT-SPS-MS3 and highpH fractionation, 10,000–20,000 K-εGG sites across 4,000–6,000 proteins is standard (Buckley 2012 achieved 2,578 sites in less-deep early-era Orbitrap conditions; modern instruments go deeper). IFIT3 has ~20 lysines and I expect to localize 1–3 DCAF7-dependent sites with >2× reduction in KO.

qanda.a: ” A: DCAF7 contains WDR40 repeats with the canonical WDxR motif characteristic of DDB1-binding DCAFs (Gao 2015 eLife). The in vitro ubiquitination reconstitution uses recombinant CUL4A-DDB1-RBX1, confirming the identity of the CRL scaffold.

qanda.q: “How do you know your recombination is complete?” A: Paired genomic PCR and protein-level western blot on sorted LSK cells from induced animals at two weeks and eight weeks. If excision is <80%, we increase dose or switch to 4-OHT before drawing conclusions from hematologic phenotypes. We also include a Cre-only (Jak2V617F; Rosa26-CreER; Dcaf7^+/+) control to separate Cre toxicity from genotype effects. Committee Q: “You’re betting a lot on the IFN signature being detectable by scRNA-seq. If it’s not there, how do you know it’s not your pipeline?” A: Positive control — include a poly(I:C) or IFN-α-treated wild-type arm in the same experiment. If the positive control shows the expected ISG module (Ifit1, Ifit3, Isg15, Mx1, Oas1) and the Jak2V617F; Dcaf7^Δ/Δ arm does not, the pipeline is fine and the biology is the answer. We also maintain a bulk-RNA-seq arm on sorted HSC/MPP as orthogonal validation. Committee Q: “CRL substrates are often transient — why would you expect to co-IP them at all?”
qanda.a: I wouldn’t rely on it as a primary approach — TurboID proximity labeling is the method that best handles the transient nature of E3-substrate interactions. The co-IP is a simpler first-pass; if it fails, TurboID is the primary approach, and in vitro reconstitution with purified components is the third.

qanda.q: “If your primary Aim 1a mouse result is null — Dcaf7 KO doesn’t ameliorate MPN in Jak2V617F mice — what do you do?
qanda.a: ” A. The key principle is that a null mouse result does not kill the thesis because multiple lines of evidence support the DCAF7-IFIT3 axis: the pharmacologic (BC18630 in Aim 1c), the biochemical (Aim 1b), and the human cell-based (iPSC CD34+ organoids). The pivot plan is: (i) move the mouse work to a transplant-based acute-induction system to rule out systemic Jak2V617F confounds; (ii) deepen the biochemistry to strengthen the mechanistic claim even without a mouse phenotype; (iii) expand the human cell arm — primary MPN CD34+ with CRISPR DCAF7 KO and competitive co-culture with unedited MPN cells; (iv) use the null mouse result itself as informative — it constrains whether the relevant cellular context is fetal-derived HSC, microenvironmental, or both. A thesis with converging genetic + pharmacologic + biochemical evidence survives a null in any single arm.

qanda.q: “Your Aim 2 relies on the Li 2020 perinatal IFN pulse. What if that’s not reproducible?
qanda.a: ” A. Two backup frames. First, the Ifnar1^-/- × Dcaf7 Vav-Cre cross directly tests whether the phenotype is IFN-dependent — this is a more rigorous test than relying on Li 2020 for the upstream signal. Second, if the phenotype is not IFN-dependent, alternative substrates (HMGA2 for fetal HSC self-renewal, SOX17 for fetal-to-adult switch) are strong candidates for DCAF7’s perinatal function. The phenotype itself — DCAF7 essentiality at P4 — is reproducible in our hands and does not depend on Li 2020 for existence.

qanda.q: “Why not run both Vav-Cre and Rosa26-CreER in parallel?
qanda.a: ” A. Sequential design concentrates resources on the discovery experiment first. VaviCre preliminary data establish DCAF7 essentiality in hematopoiesis; Rosa26-CreER is a quantitative extension that tests adult vs perinatal requirement. Tripling mouse colony size and scRNA-seq costs from day one to run both in parallel does not triple scientific yield.

qanda.q: “What if DCAF7 is redundant with another DCAF family member and your KO has no phenotype?
qanda.a: ” A. Three approaches. First, use the DCAF-focused inhibitor panel (Lear 2025 and successor compounds) to test cross-DCAF redundancy functionally . Second, CRISPR synthetic lethality screen in Dcaf7 KO background to identify compensating genes. Third, the CoDrive approach — paired CRISPR KO of DCAF7 plus its nearest homolog (DCAF6 or DCAF8, depending on expression in HSC) — tests redundancy directly . The preliminary data from our lab show that DCAF7 single-KO produces a phenotype, so redundancy is unlikely to be complete, but the magnitude may be underestimated.

qanda.q: “What’s your go/no-go for continuing with BC18630 in Aim 1c?
qanda.a: ” A. Pre-registered criteria: IC50 < 10 μM against primary MPN CD34+ CFU within the first 6 months of the aim. Genetic Dcaf7 KO rescue confirming on-target mechanism (i.e., BC18630 loses activity in Dcaf7 KO cells). If both criteria are met, continue. If IC50 is achievable only in cell lines but not primary cells, pivot to BC18813 or to the dTAG-Dcaf7 genetic-pharmacologic approach.

qanda.q: “How do you rule out that IFIT3 is just a bystander — DCAF7 has the biology , and IFIT3 is coincidentally ubiquitinated?
qanda.a: ” A. The definitive test is the DCAF7-resistant IFIT3 rescue. Build an IFIT3 K-to-R mutant at the validated ubiquitination sites; re-express this mutant in Dcaf7 KO cells; ask whether the phenotype rescues. If IFIT3 protein levels normalize but the MPN-relevant phenotype does not, IFIT3 is a bystander. If both normalize together, IFIT3 is on the causal path. This separates correlation from causation at the protein level. Integrative Methodological Synthesis No Q&A blocks extracted for this chapter.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Eukaryotic cells dispose of proteins through a small number of biochemically distinct routes,
each with characteristic substrates, kinetics, and regulatory logic. Understanding why the UPS
and autophagy are complementary rather than redundant is the foundation for defending any
degradation-focused thesis.
The ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS) handles short-lived regulatory proteins, misfolded cytosolic and nuclear clients, and signaling intermediates whose levels must change on minute-tohour timescales. Substrates are tagged with K48-linked polyubiquitin (canonically) by a threeenzyme cascade (E1 ￿ E2 ￿ E3), then threaded through the 26S proteasome after deubiquitination and ATP-dependent unfolding. The UPS is exquisitely selective because E3 ligases (~600 in
humans) recognize degrons on substrates; DCAF7, as a DDB1-CUL4 substrate receptor, sits in this
selectivity layer.
Macroautophagy engulfs bulk cytoplasm or selected organelles within a double-membrane autophagosome that fuses with the lysosome. It is the dominant response to nutrient starvation,
mitochondrial damage, and aggregate accumulation. Macroautophagy can be non-selective (bulk
cytoplasm) or selective via cargo receptors (p62/SQSTM1, NBR1, OPTN, NDP52, TAX1BP1) that
link ubiquitinated cargo or organelle-resident tags to LC3-II on the nascent phagophore (Kirkin
2009 Mol Cell; Komatsu 2007 Cell).
Chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) is a direct, receptor-mediated import of individual cytosolic proteins across the lysosomal membrane. Substrates carry a KFERQ-like pentapeptide motif recognized by cytosolic HSC70; the HSC70-substrate complex docks on LAMP2A, which multimerizes to form the translocation channel (Cuervo 2008 Trends Cell Biol). CMA is selective at the

motif level, not the ligase level.
Microautophagy is the direct invagination of the lysosomal or late-endosomal membrane to internalize small cytosolic volumes. In mammals, endosomal microautophagy (eMI) uses ESCRT
machinery and can also recognize KFERQ-bearing proteins via HSC70, but it delivers cargo to late
endosomes rather than to lysosomes.
Endosomal-lysosomal degradation handles membrane proteins. Cell-surface receptors are internalized by clathrin- or caveolin-dependent endocytosis, ubiquitinated on cytoplasmic tails, sorted
at the multivesicular body (MVB) by ESCRT, and delivered to the lysosome for hydrolysis. This is
the relevant route for IFNAR1/IFNAR2 — a detail that matters for P8 because anything touching
interferon signaling must reckon with lysosomal turnover of the receptor.
ER-associated degradation (ERAD) retrotranslocates misfolded ER-lumenal or membrane proteins back to the cytosol where they are ubiquitinated (often by ER-resident E3s such as HRD1
or gp78) and degraded by the proteasome. ERAD is thus a UPS subroutine on substrates of ER
origin — relevant for secreted and membrane proteins but not for soluble cytosolic ISGs like IFIT3.

[FIGURE: Comparison cartoon of the five major protein-turnover routes, with IFIT3 placed on the UPS arm
and IFNAR1 placed on the endosomal-lysosomal arm to anchor the thesis-relevant logic.]
The working principle for Huey’s defense is simple. Soluble, cytosolic, short-half-life proteins with
ubiquitin marks and proteasome sensitivity are UPS substrates by convention. IFIT3 fits that description. Autophagy becomes the dominant turnover route under starvation, when the protein is
aggregated, when a specific organelle is damaged, or when proteasome capacity is overwhelmed —
none of which describes the steady-state context in which DCAF7-IFIT3 degradation is operating.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Macroautophagy is the most intensively characterized of the non-UPS routes and therefore the one
most likely to be probed in a committee question. The canonical pathway , as codified in Mizushima
2008 Nature and the consensus guidelines of Klionsky 2016 EMBO J, proceeds through five stages:
initiation, nucleation, elongation, closure, and fusion.
Initiation is controlled by the ULK1 complex (ULK1/2–ATG13–FIP200–ATG101). Under fed conditions, mTORC1 phosphorylates ULK1 at Ser757, inhibiting it; AMPK opposes this by phosphorylating ULK1 at Ser317/Ser777 during energy stress. When mTORC1 is inactivated (rapamycin,
amino-acid withdrawal) or AMPK activated, ULK1 phosphorylates downstream substrates including Beclin1 and ATG14, priming the PI3K-III complex.
Nucleation at the phagophore site (often ER-derived, sometimes mitochondria- or plasmamembrane-derived) is catalyzed by the class III PI3K complex VPS34–VPS15–Beclin1–ATG14.
Local production of PI(3)P recruits WIPI2 and DFCP1, which mark the omegasome and organize
the nascent phagophore membrane.
Elongation requires two ubiquitin-like conjugation cascades. The ATG12–ATG5 conjugate,
assembled via ATG7 (E1-like) and ATG10 (E2-like), binds ATG16L1 to form the E3-like complex. In parallel, LC3 (ATG8 family) is processed by ATG4 to expose a C-terminal glycine
(LC3-I), then conjugated to phosphatidylethanolamine on the phagophore by ATG7 and ATG3,
generating membrane-bound LC3-II (Kabeya 2000 EMBO J). The LC3-I ￿ LC3-II shift is the
canonical western-blot readout of autophagosome formation and is the single most widely cited
experimental marker in the field.
Closure seals the phagophore into a double-membrane autophagosome. Fusion with the lysosome is mediated by SNAREs (STX17, SNAP29, V AMP8), HOPS tethering, and RAB7. The inner
membrane and contents are degraded by lysosomal hydrolases at low pH.
Selective macroautophagy uses cargo receptors that bridge ubiquitinated or tag-bearing cargo to
LC3-II on the phagophore. The prototypes, reviewed in Kirkin 2009 Mol Cell, are:
• p62/SQSTM1 — UBA domain binds ubiquitin (preferring K63 chains), LIR motif binds LC3;
accumulates in autophagy-deficient cells (Komatsu 2007 Cell)
• NBR1 — similar UBA-LIR architecture, partners with p62 on aggregates
• OPTN — binds linear and K63 chains, TBK1-phosphorylated on mitophagy cargo
• NDP52 (CALCOCO2) — Galectin-8 partner, major xenophagy and mitophagy receptor
• TAX1BP1 — broad selective-autophagy receptor, important for xenophagy
A point Huey should anchor: p62 binds ubiquitin. Seeing “this protein is ubiquitinated” therefore

does not automatically mean UPS; it only means UPS if the ubiquitin is K48-linked, the substrate
is degraded by MG132-sensitive machinery , and cargo-receptor involvement has been excluded.
Huey’s K-εGG data identify ubiquitination sites but do not by themselves distinguish chain topology — which is why the MG132 vs bafilomycin comparison in P10 is so load-bearing.
[FIGURE: Autophagosome biogenesis schematic — ULK1 complex initiation, PI3K-III/Beclin1/VPS34/ATG14
nucleation, WIPI2 recruitment, ATG12–ATG5–ATG16L1-mediated LC3-II lipidation, phagophore elongation and closure, STX17/SNAP29/V AMP8-mediated lysosomal fusion.]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
CMA is mechanistically orthogonal to macroautophagy — no vesicle is formed, no LC3 is involved
— and it is the most selective of the autophagy routes at the single-protein level. Its relevance to the
thesis is narrow but worth knowing, because a chair who studies IFN signaling could ask whether
STAT1 or IFIT proteins carry KFERQ motifs.
Substrates are recognized through a KFERQ-like pentapeptide that contains a Q flanked by a
specific pattern of charged and hydrophobic residues (canonical motifs include KFERQ, RKVEQ,
and related variants). Cytosolic HSC70 (HSPA8) binds the motif; this recognition can be gated by
post-translational modifications (phosphorylation or acetylation that create or reveal motifs). The
HSC70-substrate complex docks on the lysosomal membrane protein LAMP2A, which oligomerizes to form a translocation channel. A lumenal HSC70 (lys-HSC70) pulls the unfolded substrate
across, where it is degraded by lysosomal proteases (Cuervo 2008 Trends Cell Biol).
CMA is induced by prolonged starvation (after macroautophagy peaks), oxidative stress, and genotoxic stress. Loss of CMA has been implicated in neurodegeneration and, relevantly for Huey , in
hematopoietic stem cell maintenance (Joshi 2018). Whether IFIT3 has a bona fide KFERQ motif that
is accessible in its native fold is a reasonable committee question but is secondary: the K-εGG data,
MG132 rescue, and lack of lysosomal-inhibitor rescue together point away from CMA regardless
of motif presence.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The cargo-receptor architecture supports multiple specialized autophagy routes. Knowing the
names and mechanisms of at least five of them is useful both for fluency and for handling specific questions (“could this be ferritinophagy since IFIT3 is an ISG?”).
Mitophagy removes damaged mitochondria. The PINK1/Parkin pathway is the canonical route:
loss of mitochondrial membrane potential stabilizes PINK1 on the outer mitochondrial membrane,
recruiting Parkin, which ubiquitinates OMM substrates (MFN2, VDAC, MIRO) with K63 and K48
chains. OPTN and NDP52 (TBK1-phosphorylated) then bridge the damaged mitochondrion to
LC3-II (Pickles 2018 Curr Biol). A parallel receptor-mediated route uses BNIP3, NIX/BNIP3L,
and FUNDC1, which carry intrinsic LIR motifs and drive mitophagy during developmentally programmed events such as erythroid maturation and HSC metabolic reprogramming.
Aggrephagy clears protein aggregates. p62 and NBR1 recognize K63-ubiquitinated aggregate proteins; HDAC6 (see P5) is also central by binding ubiquitin and motor proteins to transport aggregates to the aggresome, where they are engulfed.
ER-phagy removes ER fragments via reticulon-family receptors (FAM134B, RTN3, SEC62, CCPG1,

ATL3, TEX264), each with an LC3-interacting region. ER-phagy coordinates with ERAD; when
ERAD is overwhelmed by misfolded ER cargo, ER-phagy provides a bulk alternative.
Xenophagy targets intracellular pathogens. Cytosol-exposed bacteria are decorated with K48/K63
chains by LUBAC, PARKIN, and LRSAM1, then captured by NDP52, OPTN, and p62. Galectin-8
detects damaged phagosomal membranes and further amplifies xenophagy via NDP52.
Ferritinophagy degrades ferritin via NCOA4 to release iron. Relevant for erythropoiesis but not
an obvious candidate for IFIT3.
For the thesis, mitophagy matters most because it is the autophagy subroutine with the clearest
mechanistic link to HSC biology (Ito lab; Leveque-El Mouttie 2015) and to the perinatal metabolic
transition that corresponds to the P4 phenotype of Vav-Cre DCAF7 deletion.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The two major degradation systems are neither independent nor interchangeable. They share substrates under specific conditions, exchange machinery , and compensate when one is inhibited —
and each of these crosstalk points can generate a committee question.
p62/SQSTM1 as a bridge. p62 binds both ubiquitin (UBA domain) and LC3 (LIR motif), making it the classic shuttle between ubiquitin marks and autophagosomal membranes. Crucially ,
p62 prefers K63 chains, which is why K63-ubiquitinated aggregates and organelles are autophagydirected while K48-ubiquitinated soluble substrates stay with the proteasome (Komatsu 2007 Cell;
Kirkin 2009 Mol Cell). p62 itself is degraded by autophagy , so it accumulates when autophagy is
blocked — an internal control that helps distinguish autophagy block from proteasome block.
K63 vs K48 directionality. K48 chains are the canonical proteasome signal; K63 chains signal selective autophagy , DNA-damage response, and endocytic sorting. This is not absolute — mixed
and branched chains exist, and Parkin writes both K48 and K63 on mitochondrial substrates —
but the heuristic is robust enough to guide interpretation of K-εGG data when combined with
linkage-specific antibodies.
HDAC6. This ubiquitin-binding deacetylase uses its BUZ domain to bind K63 chains and its
tubulin-interacting domain to transport ubiquitinated cargo along microtubules to the aggresome.
HDAC6 is essential for autophagy of misfolded-protein aggregates when the proteasome fails, providing a compensatory route under proteostasis stress.
Compensatory autophagy under proteasome inhibition. When cells are treated with MG132 or
bortezomib, accumulated ubiquitinated substrates activate autophagy through multiple mechanisms: Nrf2 stabilization drives p62 transcription; HDAC6 mobilizes aggregates; mTORC1 inhibition follows amino-acid depletion from the blocked proteasome flux. This means that a long
MG132 treatment will eventually engage autophagy , which confounds interpretation — a subtlety
that matters for MG132 duration choice.
Reverse compensation is weaker. Autophagy block (bafilomycin, chloroquine, ATG gene loss)
does not dramatically upregulate proteasomal flux in most cell types; ubiquitinated aggregates
accumulate instead. This asymmetry matters because the absence of autophagy-inhibitor rescue of
IFIT3 is diagnostically informative, while the presence of MG132 rescue must be interpreted with
attention to treatment duration.
[FIGURE: UPS-autophagy crosstalk diagram showing p62 bridging K63-ubiquitinated cargo to LC3-II,

HDAC6 shuttling aggregates to the aggresome, MG132-induced Nrf2/p62 transcriptional response, and
the asymmetric compensation between the two systems.]

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The committee chair’s awareness of hematopoietic autophagy is the reason Section P cannot be
waved off. Several high-impact papers have established autophagy as non-optional for HSC biology , and any defense of a UPS-only mechanism must acknowledge them explicitly .
Mortensen 2011 J Exp Med — Atg7-deletion in the hematopoietic compartment ( Vav-Cre;
Atg7fl/fl) produces severe HSC loss, mitochondrial accumulation, genomic instability , and a lethal
myeloproliferation-like phenotype. This paper established that macroautophagy is essential for
HSC integrity and is the reference for why any Vav-Cre phenotype in a proteostasis gene triggers
the “is this autophagy?” question.
Warr 2013 Nature — Old HSCs upregulate FOXO3-dependent autophagy in response to metabolic
stress; autophagy is required for HSC survival during starvation. Positions autophagy as a stressresponse module in HSCs rather than a constitutive housekeeping function.
Ho 2017 Nature — The definitive statement. FOXO3A drives a pro-autophagy gene program in
aged HSCs; autophagy is required to suppress the accumulation of active mitochondria and to
preserve the quiescent, self-renewing HSC state. Loss of autophagy forces HSCs into an activated
metabolic state with myeloid bias and loss of long-term reconstitution capacity . This paper is the
primary citation Huey must reach for whenever autophagy-in-HSC is raised; it establishes the
pathway as legitimate and load-bearing and justifies Section P’s existence.
Leveque-El Mouttie 2015 — Mitophagy specifically (not bulk autophagy) regulates HSC
self-renewal; defective mitochondrial clearance couples to HSC exhaustion. Reinforces that
mitophagy , not bulk macroautophagy , is the relevant autophagy subroutine for HSC metabolic
transitions.
Joshi 2018 — CMA via LAMP2A-HSC70 is required for HSC function; LAMP2A loss impairs HSC
metabolism and stemness. Extends the autophagy-HSC story beyond macroautophagy into CMA
and broadens the set of pathways a committee could invoke.
The synthesis Huey should carry into the exam: autophagy in HSCs is well-established but it is primarily a quiescence and metabolic-reprogramming function, most acute in aged or stressed HSCs. The
DCAF7-IFIT3 axis is a signaling-turnover function in committed JAK2V617F cells — a distinct biological context. This does not exempt Aim 2 (the P4 Vav-Cre phenotype) from autophagy scrutiny ,
which is why P12 flags autophagy as a future direction specifically for that arm.

sections.title: Autophagy in MPN and JAK2V617F Biology

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The myeloid-neoplasm autophagy literature is smaller than the HSC literature but growing, and
the committee can reasonably ask whether JAK2V617F cells have altered autophagy flux that could
intersect with ISG turnover.
Three anchors. First, Raffel 2017 Nature established that TET2-mutant and DNMT3A-mutant
preleukemic clones have altered branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) metabolism that feeds into
mTORC1 regulation and therefore autophagy tone; autophagy flux in these clones is context9

dependent and can be upregulated as a pro-survival response. This does not directly implicate
JAK2V617F but it establishes that clonal hematopoiesis mutations perturb the mTORC1-autophagy
axis in ways that matter for fitness.
Second, JAK2V617F signaling itself activates mTORC1 via STAT5-PIM1 and PI3K-AKT, which
would be expected to suppress autophagy at baseline. If anything, MPN cells may have blunted
autophagy relative to normal counterparts, which further argues against autophagy being the dominant IFIT3 turnover mechanism — if autophagy were doing the work, it would be doing less of it,
not more, in JAK2V617F cells where IFIT3 is nonetheless kept low by DCAF7.
Third, a nascent literature (Houshmand, Fleischman, and others) examines autophagy inhibition as
a therapeutic vulnerability in MPN; hydroxychloroquine has been tested in combination with JAK
inhibitors in early clinical studies (see P9), with limited monotherapy effect and mixed combination
results.
For the thesis, the practical question is whether autophagy provides a parallel or compensatory
mechanism for ISG turnover in MPN cells. The answer Huey should give: there is no published
evidence that IFIT3 is an autophagy substrate in any cell type; the broader ISG turnover literature
(P8) points to specific UPS mechanisms; and inhibitor experiments in our hands do not rescue IFIT3
in DCAF7-WT cells. A compensatory autophagy role is not excluded a priori , but the dominant
mechanism in MPN cells is UPS-mediated via DCAF7.

sections.title: Lysosomal Degradation of Interferon Receptors and ISGs

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This is the section the committee chair is most likely to press on, because it sits at the exact intersection of UPS, lysosomes, and IFN signaling that defines the chair’s expertise. Huey should know
three bodies of literature cold.
IFNAR1/IFNAR2 lysosomal degradation. The Fuchs lab established that type I IFN binding
triggers phosphorylation of IFNAR1 by CK1α (on the degron motif), SCF-βTrCP-mediated K48ubiquitination, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, and lysosomal degradation — a route requiring
both proteasomal and lysosomal activity . Payelle-Brogard 2013 Nature (Fuchs lab) showed that
in cells carrying an active ISG signature, USP18 anchors to IFNAR2 and blocks JAK1 activity ,
producing negative feedback without requiring receptor degradation; this paper is seminal for
understanding the desensitization side of IFN signaling and is frequently cited in any IFN-UPS
discussion. The practical implication for the thesis: the IFN receptor is lysosomally degraded, but
IFN effectors (STAT1, IFITs, MX, OAS, ISG15) are largely UPS substrates.
STAT1 turnover. STAT1 is ubiquitinated by SLIM/PDLIM2 and degraded by the proteasome in
the nucleus; this is a canonical UPS event on an ISG-family protein. Lysosomal STAT1 degradation
has been reported in specific contexts (measles-virus infection; certain cancer cells) but is not the
dominant steady-state route.
IFIT family turnover. The IFIT family (IFIT1, IFIT2, IFIT3, IFIT5) lacks a well-characterized
autophagy-based turnover mechanism in the published literature. IFIT1 and IFIT3 have been
reported as TRIM21 substrates in some contexts; IFIT3 is relatively long-lived compared to STAT1
but turns over on a timescale (several hours) fully consistent with UPS. No published CMA
KFERQ-motif hit for IFIT3; no reported autophagy-receptor interaction. This is the empirical
ground on which the thesis rests: no prior evidence places IFIT3 on an autophagy route, and our
data add positive UPS evidence on top of that absence.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
If the committee asks “what would you do if an experiment demanded you probe autophagy flux?”

Flux vs steady-state logic. LC3-II alone is ambiguous — it increases either because autophagy is
induced or because degradation is blocked. The standard solution is a paired comparison: measure
LC3-II and p62 with and without bafilomycin; a true flux increase is revealed by a larger BafA1-vscontrol gap. For a UPS-focused thesis, the inhibitor panel’s purpose is narrower — to show absence
of rescue, which is a cleaner experimental question than measuring flux.
What Huey would do if demanded. The cleanest added experiment would be a matrix of four
conditions — vehicle, MG132, BafA1, MG132+BafA1 — run in parallel in DCAF7-WT and DCAF7KO JAK2V617F cells, probed for IFIT3, LC3-II, and p62. MG132 alone stabilizes IFIT3; BafA1 alone
does not; the combination does not add stabilization beyond MG132 alone; p62 accumulates in every BafA1 condition as a positive autophagy-block control; LC3-II lipidation shifts appropriately .
This one blot, which Huey already has in her supporting data, is the definitive answer to the question.

sections.title: Why DCAF7-IFIT3 Is a UPS Phenomenon, Not Autophagy

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This is the single most important subsection for exam preparation. Huey should be able to walk
through this logic from memory , in order, on a whiteboard.
Step 1 — Cycloheximide chase kinetics. IFIT3 half-life in JAK2V617F DCAF7-WT cells is on the
order of 2–4 hours; in DCAF7-KO cells it extends to >8 hours (approaching the duration of the
chase). This half-life extension is the core quantitative phenotype. The timescale matters: UPS substrates turn over on minutes-to-hours; bulk macroautophagy operates on hours-to-days for most
soluble cytosolic proteins and is most obvious for long-lived proteins and organelles. A protein
with a 2–4 hour t½ whose half-life is doubled by loss of an E3-ligase-associated receptor is, by
kinetic signature alone, a UPS substrate candidate.
Step 2 — MG132 rescue of IFIT3 in DCAF7-WT cells. Treating DCAF7-WT JAK2V617F cells with
MG132 (10 μM, 4–6 h) stabilizes IFIT3 to levels comparable to DCAF7-KO steady-state. This is
the proteasome-dependence experiment. If IFIT3 were degraded by autophagy , MG132 would not
rescue it (beyond the small indirect effect of proteasome block feeding back on ubiquitin pools and
autophagy flux, which is not seen at 4–6 h).

Step 3 — MG132 in DCAF7-KO cells. Adding MG132 to DCAF7-KO cells produces a modest
further IFIT3 increase, consistent with residual DCAF7-independent proteasomal turnover, but the
increment is small compared to the DCAF7-WT + MG132 effect. This is the epistasis experiment:
DCAF7 and the proteasome are in the same pathway .
Step 4 — Bafilomycin A1 failure to rescue. BafA1 (100 nM, 4–6 h) does not stabilize IFIT3 in
DCAF7-WT cells despite producing the expected LC3-II accumulation and p62 buildup as positive
controls in the same lysates. Chloroquine (25 μM, 6 h) behaves identically . This is the negative
control for the autophagy route: the inhibitor works (p62 accumulates), but IFIT3 is untouched.
Step 5 — MG132 + BafA1 combination. The combination produces IFIT3 levels indistinguishable
from MG132 alone, further arguing that BafA1 adds no degradation block on the IFIT3 pool beyond
what MG132 already captures.
Step 6 — K-εGG diGly proteomics. Quantitative diGly-remnant (K-εGG) IP-MS on MG132treated DCAF7-WT vs DCAF7-KO cells identifies specific ubiquitinated lysines on IFIT3 that lose
signal in DCAF7-KO. This is the strongest single piece of evidence in the thesis: IFIT3 is directly
ubiquitinated, the ubiquitination is DCAF7-dependent, and the site-level information supports
a direct substrate relationship. Follow-on K48-chain-specific antibody IP or Ub-AQUA absolute
quantification of linkage types further argues for K48 predominance.
Step 7 — Denaturing Ub-IP . Under denaturing conditions (1% SDS lysis, boil, renature), IFIT3 is
pulled down in a ubiquitinated smear that collapses in DCAF7-KO cells. This rules out co-IP of a
ubiquitinated partner rather than IFIT3 itself.
T ogether, these seven pieces constitute the UPS argument. The logic is not “we haven’t seen
autophagy evidence”; it is “we have positive UPS evidence (ubiquitination sites, proteasome rescue, kinetic signature) and we have run the matched negative controls for autophagy (bafilomycin,
chloroquine, p62/LC3-II readouts that demonstrate the inhibitors are working).” The asymmetry
between positive UPS evidence and negative autophagy evidence is what makes the mechanism
assignment rigorous rather than lazy .

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Autophagy is a legitimate parallel degradation system with established relevance to hematopoietic stem cell biology . Mortensen 2011 J Exp Med, Warr 2013 Nature, Ho 2017 Nature, Leveque-El
Mouttie 2015, and Joshi 2018 collectively establish that macroautophagy , mitophagy , and CMA
are each required at different points for HSC maintenance, quiescence, aging, and metabolic reprogramming. Any Vav-Cre phenotype in a proteostasis gene must reckon with this literature,
which is why the committee chair’s autophagy question is fair rather than pedantic. For the Aim
1b mechanism, however, the evidence assigns IFIT3 turnover to the UPS rather than to autophagy:
cycloheximide-chase kinetics on the UPS timescale, MG132 rescue of IFIT3 with matched negative
bafilomycin and chloroquine controls (p62 and LC3-II confirming the inhibitors are functional),
K-εGG diGly proteomics identifying DCAF7-dependent ubiquitination sites on IFIT3, and denaturing Ub-IP demonstrating direct ubiquitination together make the UPS assignment the parsimonious and best-supported model (Kabeya 2000 EMBO J, Komatsu 2007 Cell, Mizushima 2008
Nature, Cuervo 2008 Trends Cell Biol, Klionsky 2016 EMBO J, Kirkin 2009 Mol Cell, Pickles 2018
Curr Biol provide the autophagy framework against which the negative controls are interpreted;
Payelle-Brogard 2013 Nature and the broader ISG-turnover literature confirm that soluble cytosolic
ISGs like IFIT3 are UPS substrates by convention, with IFNAR itself as the lysosomal exception).

Autophagy was therefore correctly scoped out of Aim 1b on the basis of positive UPS evidence
plus matched negative autophagy-inhibitor controls. For Aim 2, the postnatal day 4 Vav-Cre; Dcaf7
lethality coincides with a period of intense metabolic reprogramming during the fetal-to-adult HSC
transition, and autophagy surges have been documented during comparable transitions (Ho 2017
Nature); an autophagy-deficient HSC population could plausibly fail to terminate the perinatal
interferon pulse, linking Aim 1b’s IFN biology back to Aim 2’s developmental phenotype through
a shared autophagy-IFN axis. That hypothesis is explicitly a future direction rather than a current
claim, but it is the most productive way to fold the autophagy literature into the thesis narrative:
autophagy is not the answer to the DCAF7-IFIT3 question in MPN cells, and it is a fair candidate
mechanism to test for the perinatal lethality phenotype.
The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape
Tier 3 study guide for qualifying exam — Huey Mysliwiec, Melo-Cardenas Lab, Northwestern
Purpose of this section. Huey’s thesis is organized around two axes: (1) DCAF7 is the substrate
receptor of a CRL4 E3 ligase that targets IFIT3 for degradation in JAK2V617F MPN cells, so DCAF7
loss stabilizes IFIT3 and hypersensitizes MPN cells to IFN-α; (2) DCAF7 is required for the fetalto-adult hematopoietic transition at postnatal day 4 (P4). Both axes sit squarely inside the type
I interferon (IFN) field. Her committee chair, however, studies the ubiquitin-proteasome system
(UPS) in hematopoiesis and IFN signaling, which guarantees that the committee will probe “adjacent” innate-immune and inflammatory axes — IL-1β, TNF-α, cGAS-STING, the inflammasome,
the microbiome, inflammaging — and will want to know why Huey has chosen type I IFN as her entry point and not one of these. Section Q is the defensive perimeter: it gives her confident, bounded
answers to every innate-immune question that is not the IFIT3-IFN axis but could still be thrown
at her during the oral exam. The goal is not to make her an expert on IL-1β biology; it is to ensure
she can speak knowledgeably about each adjacent axis, cite the landmark papers, and explain why
each is either orthogonal, upstream, or a control experiment — never a competing explanation she
has failed to consider.
The section is organized as Q1 (pattern recognition receptor overview) through Q13 (integrative
summary). Each subsection ends with one or two “Committee Q” boxes — realistic committeestyle questions with tight answers Huey can deliver in 30–60 seconds. The section is long but
dense by design; it is meant to be read once end-to-end and then skimmed the night before the
exam.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Pattern recognition receptors are the germline-encoded sensors of the innate immune system.
They detect conserved molecular signatures on microbes (pathogen-associated molecular patterns,
PAMPs) and on damaged or stressed host tissue (damage-associated molecular patterns, DAMPs).
The output of PRR engagement is, almost universally , transcription of NF-κB-dependent inflammatory genes, type I/III IFN genes via IRF3/IRF7, or pyroptotic inflammasome assembly . For a
hematopoiesis thesis these receptors matter because (i) HSCs and progenitors express many of
them directly (Q2), (ii) their downstream cytokines reshape the stem-cell niche (Q4–Q6), and (iii)

a subset of them — cGAS-STING in particular — generate type I IFN from endogenous (sterile)
signals such as cytosolic DNA, which is a plausible upstream driver of the very IFN tone that
Huey’s thesis proposes to amplify via DCAF7 loss.
The six PRR families, their canonical ligands, compartment, and principal output are summarized
below. The two authoritative reviews to cite if the committee asks for sources are Mogensen 2009
Clin Microbiol Rev and Kumar 2011 Int Rev Immunol .

Two compartmentalization principles matter for Huey . First, nucleic-acid sensors live in compartments where self nucleic acid should not be — endosomes (TLR3/7/8/9), cytosol (RLRs, cGAS,
AIM2). A leak from its proper compartment (mitochondrial DNA into cytosol, ssRNA from damaged cells into endosomes via phagocytosis) is sufficient to activate them and produce “sterile”

inflammation. Second, TLR4 is the only PRR that can signal from both the plasma membrane
(MyD88-dependent NF-κB) and the endosome (TRIF-dependent IRF3 ￿ type I IFN) after endocytosis — the signalosome is literally repositioned during signaling, which is why LPS produces
both acute cytokines and a delayed IFN wave.
Committee Q: If DCAF7 loss raises type I IFN responsiveness, which upstream PRRs would you expect
to be the dominant sources of the IFN ligand in the MPN marrow? Answer: In sterile MPN marrow the
dominant sources are cGAS-STING (sensing cytosolic DNA from replication stress and micronuclei
downstream of JAK2V617F-driven genomic instability — Chen 2014; Hurtado-Nedelec 2013) and,
to a lesser extent, endosomal TLRs triggered by DAMPs released from dying cells. TLR4 signaling
on HSCs (Nagai 2006 Immunity) is relevant if there is concurrent microbial translocation but is not
expected to dominate in a steady-state MPN; it becomes relevant in the perinatal window I study
in Aim 2, when the gut microbiota is first establishing.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
HSCs and MPPs are not immunologically inert — Nagai and colleagues (Nagai 2006 Immunity)
showed that murine LSK cells express functional TLR2 and TLR4 and that pure LPS or Pam3CSK4
stimulation, without exogenous cytokine, drives proliferation and myeloid differentiation in vitro.
This was the first direct demonstration that a stem/progenitor cell can “hear” a PAMP and that
PRR signaling is not restricted to mature innate cells. The pathway is the textbook one: MyD88
recruits IRAK4–IRAK1–TRAF6, activates TAK1, and branches into IKK ￿ NF-κB and MKK ￿
JNK/p38; TLR3 and endosomal TLR4 use the TRIF branch to activate TBK1 ￿ IRF3 and produce
type I IFN.
The physiological relevance of TLR signaling to HSCs was sharpened by two follow-up studies.
Esplin and colleagues (Esplin 2011 J Immunol) gave mice chronic low-dose LPS for four weeks and
showed that HSCs lost repopulating activity in competitive transplantation, accumulated in the
cell cycle, and had impaired lymphoid output — a demonstration that chronic TLR4 engagement
is sufficient to exhaust the HSC pool. Pietras and colleagues (Pietras 2016 Nat Cell Biol ) extended
this to chronic IL-1β (which acts via MyD88 downstream of IL-1R1, the same adaptor as TLRs)
and showed that chronic inflammation skews HSCs toward myeloid-biased output and erodes
self-renewal. The emergent picture is a dose–duration curve: acute TLR engagement mobilizes
HSCs productively (emergency myelopoiesis); chronic engagement causes HSC exhaustion and
myeloid skewing; and malignant clones (JAK2V617F, TET2-mutant) may tolerate or even exploit
the inflammation better than wild-type HSCs, driving clonal dominance.
For Huey’s thesis this literature is the backdrop of Aim 1a’s microenvironment controls. If DCAF7KO MPN cells are more sensitive to IFN-α, she needs to rule out that she has simply perturbed a
parallel MyD88-dependent TLR pathway and that her IFN hypersensitivity is a surrogate for a
broader NF-κB lesion. The cleanest control is to show that DCAF7 loss does not change TLR4
or TLR9 surface/endosomal expression, does not alter NF-κB p65 phosphorylation after LPS or
CpG, and that IFIT3 rescue specifically (as opposed to IκBα or p65 knockdown) normalizes the
phenotype.
Committee Q: Is there any reason to think DCAF7 might have substrates other than IFIT3 that feed into
TLR signaling? Answer: DCAF7 is a WD40 substrate receptor for CRL4, and WD40 substrate recep16

tors generally have multiple substrates. My BioID and IP-MS datasets identify IFIT3 as the most
stoichiometric and IFN-regulated substrate, but I will do a TLR-agonist panel (LPS, Pam3, poly
I:C, CpG) with p65 and IRF3 phospho-readouts on isogenic DCAF7-WT and DCAF7-KO UKE-1
cells to exclude the possibility that DCAF7 loss broadly disinhibits MyD88 or TRIF signaling. If
NF-κB activation is unchanged and only the ISG module moves with IFIT3 stabilization, TLR-axis
involvement is excluded.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Inflammasomes are multiprotein cytosolic platforms that convert pro-caspase-1 into mature
caspase-1, which in turn cleaves pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their bioactive forms and cleaves
gasdermin D (GSDMD) into an N-terminal fragment that oligomerizes in the plasma membrane
and drives pyroptosis. The three inflammasomes Huey must know are NLRP3 (the archetype;
activated by a remarkably broad spectrum of danger signals including ATP , nigericin, crystalline
stimuli, and mitochondrial ROS — all converging on K+ efflux); NLRC4 (activated by cytosolic
flagellin and T3SS components via the NAIP receptors); and AIM2 (activated by cytosolic dsDNA).
The authoritative review for this section is Man 2017 Nat Rev Immunol .
Canonical assembly requires two signals: “signal 1” primes the cell (TLR/NF-κB-driven transcription of pro-IL-1β, NLRP3 itself, GSDMD); “signal 2” triggers oligomerization via ASC. Noncanonical activation via caspase-11 (caspase-4/5 in humans) senses cytosolic LPS directly and feeds
into NLRP3 by triggering K+ efflux.
The link to hematopoiesis is twofold. First, chronic IL-1β is sufficient to produce myeloid-biased
HSCs (Pietras 2016) and, as Q5 will detail, to expand mutant HSC clones. Second, pyroptotic
death of bone-marrow myeloid cells is a DAMP source that amplifies the sterile inflammatory loop.
Chavakis and colleagues have demonstrated a specific NLRP3-Tet2 axis in clonal hematopoiesis in
which Tet2-deficient macrophages have elevated NLRP3 priming, amplifying IL-1β and feeding
back onto the mutant HSC (see Q12). In MPN specifically , IL-1β is elevated in plasma and in
BM supernatants (Q4, Q5) but the dominant inflammasome-producing cell type in MPN has not
been unambiguously nailed down — it is a mix of marrow-resident macrophages and expanding
JAK2V617F megakaryocytes.
T able Q3. The three inflammasomes Huey must know.

Committee Q: Could DCAF7 loss derepress an inflammasome, and would that explain IFN hypersensitivity? Answer: No — IL-1β/IL-18 are not type I IFN ligands; they signal through MyD88/IL-1R1,
not the IFNAR–JAK1/TYK2–STAT1/STAT2 axis that IFIT3 sits in. Stabilized IFIT3 amplifies the
type I IFN response through the IFNAR module specifically (it modifies ISGF3 output and dsRNA
binding downstream of MA VS/TBK1), so even if DCAF7 loss did affect an inflammasome it would
not phenocopy the IFN hypersensitivity I see. I will nonetheless include IL-1β and IL-18 in the Aim
1a cytokine array as a negative control.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
MPN is not merely a JAK2-STAT cell-autonomous disease; it is a disease of inflamed marrow. Patient plasma and BM supernatants contain elevated IL-6, TNF-α, IL-8, IL-1β, IFN-γ, and VEGF, and
the magnitude of this cytokine storm correlates with symptom burden and, in PMF, with overall
survival. The foundational references are Fisher 2017 Blood (a systematic characterization of the
MPN cytokine milieu showing JAK2V617F cell-autonomous induction of a pro-inflammatory secretome independent of the non-mutant niche) and Kleppe 2015 Cancer Cell (which showed that
ruxolitinib’s efficacy is partly through extinguishing a JAK-dependent inflammatory niche, not
only through shutting down the mutant clone’s proliferation, and that stromal JAK2 matters).
Two conceptual points are worth emphasizing. First, the JAK2V617F cell is itself an inflammatory
secretory unit — it transcribes IL-6, CXCL8, TNF-α, and CCL2 in a STAT3/STAT5-dependent
manner even without an external trigger, because tonic JAK-STAT signaling bleeds onto the
inflammation-NF-κB program. This is why IFN-α therapy in MPN works not only by targeting
the proliferating HSC fraction but also by remodeling the inflammatory niche. Second, the
calreticulin (CALR) mutant — the commonest non-JAK2 driver in ET and PMF — is a unique case:
the frameshifted C-terminus of mutant CALR binds the thrombopoietin receptor MPL in the ER
and chaperones it to the surface in a constitutively active form, producing TPO-receptor-activating
JAK2 signaling without a JAK2 mutation; but mutant CALR also escapes the ER and acts as an

extracellular pro-inflammatory DAMP , driving IL-6 and TNF-α production by monocytes and
stromal cells. So CALR-mutant MPN is both TPO-R-driven and DAMP-driven.
The splenic compartment matters because the enlarged MPN spleen is a site of extramedullary
hematopoiesis and a reservoir of inflammatory myeloid cells that secrete into the circulating cytokine pool; splenectomy or ruxolitinib-induced spleen reduction measurably drops plasma IL-6
and TNF-α.
