Committee Q: In your thesis model, how do you separate IFN-α’s direct effect on the MPN clone from its
effect on the inflamed niche? Answer: I use three approaches. First, isogenic JAK2V617F cell lines
(UKE-1, SET-2, HEL) treated with IFN-α in vitro exclude niche effects entirely and still show the
DCAF7-KO hypersensitivity , proving a cell-autonomous axis. Second, in vivo I use JAK2V617F
knock-in mice crossed to a hematopoietic-specific DCAF7 conditional (Vav-Cre) so that DCAF7
loss is restricted to the hematopoietic compartment, ruling out stromal contributions. Third, I
profile the BM cytokine milieu with a 40-plex array to confirm that the DCAF7-KO phenotype is
not explained by a secondary change in IL-6, TNF-α, or IL-1β. Together these triangulate a direct
IFNAR–IFIT3 axis.

sections.title: IL-1β as a driver of MPN and clonal hematopoiesis

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
IL-1β has become, in the last five years, the most validated cytokine driver of clonal expansion in
preleukemic hematopoiesis. Three papers define the field and Huey should cite them by name.
Avagyan and colleagues (Avagyan 2021 Science) used zebrafish transplantation to show that clonal
hematopoiesis emerges stochastically but is amplified by inflammation, and that IL-1β is the dominant driver of differential clonal fitness — mutant clones proliferate preferentially over WT HSCs
when the niche is inflamed. Rai and colleagues (Rai 2022) demonstrated in JAK2V617F mouse
models that IL-1β signaling through IL-1R1 on HSCs is necessary for the MPN phenotype, and
that IL-1R1 deletion or IL-1β neutralization blunts both the erythrocytosis and the niche damage
(sinusoidal loss, nestin-positive MSC depletion). Caiado and colleagues (Caiado 2023) extended
this to aged-CH physiology: IL-1β from the aging niche selectively expands mutant HSCs, and
blocking IL-1β preserves WT clone output.
Mechanistically , IL-1β acts on IL-1R1 ￿ MyD88 ￿ IRAK4 ￿ TAK1 ￿ IKK ￿ NF-κB and MAPK;
and on HSCs this pathway drives both proliferation and myeloid priming (Pietras 2016). The
fitness asymmetry for mutant clones likely reflects (i) tolerance to inflammatory damage (mutant
clones survive genotoxic and oxidative stress better) and (ii) pre-primed myeloid transcriptional
programs that IL-1β synergizes with. Therapeutically , CANTOS demonstrated that canakinumab
(anti-IL-1β) reduces incident lung cancer and cardiovascular events — and reanalysis showed a
reduction in incident myeloid malignancy . Anakinra (IL-1R1 antagonist) is in pilot trials for MPN.
For Huey’s thesis, IL-1β is the most important parallel axis to address because it (i) targets the same
stem-cell compartment as type I IFN, (ii) could confound any clonal-expansion phenotype in her
DCAF7 KO mice, and (iii) is likely already elevated in her JAK2V617F model. The specific control
is the cytokine array in Aim 1a: she must show that DCAF7 loss does not change IL-1β secretion,
that the clonal-competition phenotype in DCAF7-KO MPN cells is rescued by IFIT3 loss (not by
IL-1R1 loss), and that IFN-α sensitization is preserved in Il1r1-/- animals.
Committee Q: Why isn’t your phenotype just IL-1β-mediated? Answer: Three reasons. First, DCAF7’s
substrate is IFIT3, and IFIT3 is a type I IFN ISG — it is not transcriptionally induced by IL-1β in

my RNA-seq data. Second, the hypersensitivity I see is specifically to IFN-α ex vivo , not to IL1β. Third, I will cross my MPN;Dcaf7-conditional mice to Il1r1-/- to formally exclude IL-1β as an
obligate mediator. If DCAF7 loss remains IFN-α-sensitizing on an Il1r1-/- background, IL-1β is not
on the causal path.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
TNF-α is the second cytokine that will come up as a potential confounder. Fleischman and colleagues (Fleischman 2011 Blood) showed that JAK2V617F HSCs produce TNF-α cell-autonomously
and that TNF-α preferentially suppresses wild-type HSCs while JAK2V617F HSCs are resistant —
i.e., TNF-α is a selection pressure that favors the mutant clone. Heaton and colleagues (Heaton 2018)
formalized this: the JAK2V617F clone is not only resistant to TNF-α but uses it to amplify itself,
via a TNFR2-dependent NF-κB module that feeds into STAT5 output. Fisher 2017 confirmed the
elevation of TNF-α in MPN plasma and showed cell-autonomous induction in JAK2V617F cells
independent of the niche.
TNF-α acts on two receptors with very different downstream biology: TNFR1 is ubiquitous, engages TRADD-FADD-caspase-8 and RIPK1-RIPK3-MLKL for necroptosis, and can drive NF-κB
via the complex I ￿ complex II transition; TNFR2 is restricted to hematopoietic, endothelial, and
neuronal cells, lacks a death domain, and signals primarily through TRAF2 ￿ NF-κB for survival
and proliferation. The MPN-relevant receptor is TNFR2, which is why JAK2V617F HSCs tolerate
TNF-α but wild-type HSCs — more dependent on TNFR1 balance — are suppressed.
For the thesis, TNF-α is a confounder analogous to IL-1β: elevated in MPN, acts on HSCs, selects
for the mutant clone. The Aim 1a cytokine array must include TNF-α; and if the DCAF7-KO phenotype depends on TNF-α neutralization, the causal model must accommodate it. The expected
answer is that DCAF7 loss does not depend on TNF-α — because the substrate is IFIT3, not a
TNFR2-proximal substrate — and the negative control in Tnfr1-/- Tnfr2-/- double-knockouts or
with etanercept blockade makes this airtight.
Committee Q: Could DCAF7 have a substrate in the TNF pathway? Answer: Nothing in my IP-MS and
BioID data supports a TNFR-proximal substrate. The top hits are IFIT3, IFIT1, and IFIT2 (the IFIT
complex). DCAF7 is known from the literature (HAN11 era) to bind DYRK1A and MNB, neither of
which is TNF-pathway-linked. I will nonetheless include TNF-α and LTα in the cytokine array and
etanercept pretreatment as a negative control on my clonal-competition assay to formally exclude
a TNFR-mediated component.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
cGAS-STING is the cytosolic DNA sensor that Huey cannot afford to gloss over because it is a
plausible upstream source of the very type I IFN that her thesis amplifies — and the committee will
absolutely ask. Cyclic GMP-AMP synthase (cGAS) senses cytosolic dsDNA of any origin (viral,
bacterial, micronuclear, mitochondrial), catalyzes synthesis of the 2′3′-cGAMP second messenger,

which binds STING in the ER membrane, which traffics to ERGIC and recruits TBK1; TBK1 phosphorylates IRF3 (and, less efficiently , IRF7), which dimerizes and enters the nucleus to drive type
I IFN transcription. STING also activates NF-κB.
In MPN, two lines of evidence argue that cGAS-STING is engaged. Chen and colleagues (Chen
2014) showed that JAK2V617F induces replication stress and DNA damage — fork stalling, RPA
coating, γH2AX accumulation — via elevated reactive oxygen species and altered replication timing. Hurtado-Nedelec and colleagues (Hurtado-Nedelec 2013) confirmed elevated DNA damage
and oxidative stress in MPN granulocytes and CD34+ cells. The consequence of chronic replication
stress is cytosolic DNA — micronuclei from chromosome missegregation, fragments from stalled
replication forks, and mitochondrial DNA release during metabolic stress. This cytosolic DNA is
the natural ligand for cGAS.
The hypothesis is therefore that JAK2V617F MPN cells have a constitutively engaged cGAS-STING
tone that produces tonic low-level type I IFN, which drives tonic ISG expression (including IFIT3),
and that Huey’s DCAF7 loss converts a modest IFIT3 pool into a large one, converting modest tonic
ISG expression into runaway IFN signaling when exogenous IFN-α is added. This is a coherent
upstream model and is worth stating as a future direction, not a current claim, in Huey’s thesis. The
committee will appreciate that she has considered it without overreaching.
Committee Q: If cGAS-STING is already driving basal IFN tone in MPN, would STING inhibitors be
therapeutic, and would DCAF7 loss still sensitize on a STING-knockout background? Answer: STING
antagonists (H-151, C-176) are still preclinical, and their effect in MPN is not characterized. Formally , my model predicts that DCAF7 loss sensitizes to any IFNAR engagement regardless of its
source — endogenous cGAS-STING-derived or exogenous pegylated IFN-α. On a Sting-/- background, basal ISG tone should drop, including basal IFIT3; exogenous IFN-α would still induce
IFIT3, and DCAF7 loss should still stabilize it. So I predict preserved sensitization to exogenous
IFN-α on Sting-/-, and reduced baseline ISG expression. This is a clean future experiment but not
in my current aims.

sections.title: T ype II IFN (IFN-γ) and HSC biology

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Type II IFN is a different cytokine family from type I. IFN-γ is a single gene, signals through
IFNGR1/IFNGR2 ￿ JAK1/JAK2 ￿ STAT1 homodimers (GAF, not ISGF3), and drives a partially
overlapping but functionally distinct transcriptional program dominated by GBPs, CXCL9/10/11,
CIITA (for MHC-II), and IDO. It is produced primarily by T and NK cells, not by the innate-sensing
pathways described above.
The canonical HSC paper is Baldridge 2010Nature, which showed that chronicMycobacterium avium
infection drives IFN-γ-dependent HSC activation out of quiescence, converting long-term HSCs
to short-term progenitors and eroding the LT-HSC pool over time. This paralleled the Essers 2009
Nature finding that type I IFN (via poly I:C or IFN-α) also activates HSCs out of quiescence —
two families of IFN, two kinases (both JAK1/JAK2 dependent), both engage STAT1, both activate
HSCs, but via distinct receptor complexes and distinct downstream signatures.
For Huey’s thesis the critical point is that IFIT3 is a type I IFN ISG : its promoter is driven by ISGF3
(STAT1-STAT2-IRF9), not by GAF (STAT1-STAT1). IFIT3 is induced weakly or not at all by pure

IFN-γ in most cell systems. So the DCAF7-IFIT3 axis is specifically a type I IFN amplifier, not a
type II amplifier. This is why Huey’s thesis is type I IFN-focused and not type II.
T able Q8. Type I vs Type II vs Type III IFN.

Committee Q: Why type I IFN and not type II IFN for DCAF7? Isn’t IFN-γ a bigger deal in PMF? Answer: Two reasons. First, IFIT3 is an ISGF3 target, induced by type I IFN; it is not meaningfully
induced by IFN-γ in my cell lines or in publicly available ChIP-seq data for STAT1 homodimers. So
the DCAF7-IFIT3 axis is mechanistically a type I IFN amplifier. Second, pegylated IFN-α (ropeginterferon) is a frontline therapy in MPN with documented molecular responses — it is clinically the
IFN class that matters for patient outcomes. IFN-γ elevation in PMF plasma is real, but IFN-γ is
not an MPN therapy and does not engage IFIT3 significantly .

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Type III IFN (IFN-λ1-4; also known as IL-29, IL-28A, IL-28B, IFN-λ4) signals through the
IFNLR1/IL10R2 heterodimer and uses the same JAK1/TYK2 kinases and ISGF3 transcription
factor complex as type I IFN. Its transcriptional output is essentially a subset of the type I ISG

program. What makes IFN-λ biologically distinct is receptor distribution: IFNLR1 is expressed
primarily on epithelial cells (gut, lung, liver) and, to a lesser extent, on neutrophils and plasmacytoid DCs. Hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells express very little IFNLR1, so type III IFN has
minimal direct HSC effects.
For Huey’s thesis the takeaway is simple: type III IFN is a mucosal antiviral system; it is not a
plausible driver of hematopoietic phenotypes and is not therapeutically used in MPN. If the committee raises it, the one-sentence answer is that it engages ISGF3 through IFNLR1 but HSCs lack
the receptor, so it is mechanistically downstream of IFIT3 but physiologically excluded from her
system. She should not spend more than a minute on it.
Committee Q: Would DCAF7 loss also sensitize to IFN-λ in an epithelial context? Answer: Mechanistically yes — IFN-λ uses ISGF3 and induces IFIT3, and DCAF7 loss should stabilize IFIT3 in any cell
that makes it. But my thesis is hematopoietic, and HSCs are IFNLR1-low, so IFN-λ is not a driver
in my system. Testing it in an epithelial context would be a logical extension if someone wanted
to generalize the mechanism.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
JAK2V617F MPN has an adaptive immune surveillance dimension that the committee may probe,
particularly in the context of immune-checkpoint combination strategies. Three points suffice.
First, CD8+ T cells can recognize mutant CALR and, to a lesser extent, JAK2V617F as neoantigens, and CALR-mutant MPN patients have measurable anti-CALR CD8 responses — there is
active interest in CALR-peptide vaccines. Second, type I IFN (and IFN-γ) upregulates PD-L1 on
MPN cells via IRF1, so any IFN-based therapy creates a potential immune-evasion phenotype that
might be relieved by anti-PD-1/PD-L1 combinations; a pembrolizumab + pegylated IFN trial is
underway in PV . Third, immune-editing — the Darwinian sculpting of the mutant clone by T-cell
pressure — is a real but poorly quantified force in MPN; it may explain why only a fraction of
JAK2V617F-positive CHIP cases progress to overt MPN.
For Huey’s thesis this is the right level of depth. She does not need to own the immune-oncology
literature. She should (i) acknowledge that IFN-based therapy has an adaptive-immune dimension, (ii) note that her DCAF7 loss hypersensitizes the MPN cell intrinsically and would therefore
be additive with checkpoint blockade rather than competing, and (iii) scope this out as a future
direction rather than a current aim.
Committee Q: If DCAF7 inhibition sensitizes MPN to IFN-α, would you expect synergy with anti-PD-1?
Answer: Mechanistically plausible. Type I IFN upregulates MHC-I and PD-L1 on the MPN cell
via IRF1; DCAF7 loss amplifies the IFN response, so both antigen presentation and PD-L1 would
be expected to rise in DCAF7-KO tumors. Anti-PD-1 would then release the immune brake that
the amplified IFN response has just installed. This is a logical combination trial design but it is
not in my current aims; I would need syngeneic immunocompetent MPN models (the Mullally
JAK2V617F knock-in on B6) to test it.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The microbiome is a sustained, spatially compartmentalized source of PRR ligands — LPS, peptidoglycan, flagellin, CpG DNA, formyl peptides — that calibrates the tonic inflammatory and IFN
set-point of the host from birth onward. For Huey’s Aim 2 (fetal-to-adult hematopoietic transition
at P4), this is not optional reading; it is the experimental control that decides whether the P4 IFN
pulse is a developmentally programmed event or a microbiota-driven one.
Two papers establish the steady-state microbiome–HSC axis. Iwamura and colleagues (Iwamura
2017 Blood) showed that germ-free (GF) mice have reduced steady-state myelopoiesis and smaller
HSC pools, and that microbiota reconstitution rescues both. Josefsdottir and colleagues (Josefsdottir 2017 Blood) extended this to antibiotic-treated mice: broad-spectrum antibiotics phenocopy
GF with respect to HSC numbers, myeloid output, and STAT1 signaling in the marrow — demonstrating that a tonic microbial signal is required to maintain the HSC compartment at steady state.
Together they make the case that the microbiome is a silent third partner of the HSC niche, alongside the MSC and the endothelial cell.
The paper that directly bears on Huey’s Aim 2 is Li and colleagues (Li 2020 Cell Stem Cell ), which
characterized the perinatal window and showed a defined pulse of type I IFN signaling that coincides with the fetal-to-adult HSC switch and is required for proper establishment of the adult HSC
pool. The open question — the question Huey’s germ-free rederivation experiment is designed
to answer — is whether this perinatal IFN pulse is programmed by the developing host (a cellintrinsic clock) or driven by the initial microbial colonization that begins at birth and intensifies in
the first postnatal week. Germ-free rederivation of her Dcaf7-conditional mice will test exactly this:
if the P4 phenotype is preserved in GF conditions, the IFN pulse is host-intrinsic; if the P4 phenotype disappears in GF conditions, the pulse is microbiota-driven, and the DCAF7 axis converges
on an exogenously triggered IFN wave.
This is arguably the single highest-leverage experiment in Aim 2 and Huey should own it completely .
Committee Q: How do you distinguish a cell-intrinsic developmental IFN program from a microbiotadriven one in your Aim 2? Answer: Germ-free rederivation. I will rederive the Dcaf7-conditional
MPN mice into an axenic isolator, confirm germ-free status by 16S and culture, and compare the
P4 HSC phenotype between SPF and GF conditions. If GF pups still show the DCAF7-dependent
P4 phenotype, the perinatal IFN pulse (Li 2020) is developmentally programmed and DCAF7 is
operating on a host-intrinsic IFN wave. If GF pups are rescued — no DCAF7 phenotype — the
IFN pulse is microbiota-driven and DCAF7 is amplifying an exogenous signal. Either result is
interpretable and publishable. I will complement this with antibiotic treatment of dams from E14
onward as a less-clean but more-scalable surrogate.

sections.title: Aging inflammation and the age-dependence of CHIP

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
“Inflammaging” is the term for the slow rise in baseline inflammatory cytokines — IL-6, TNF-α, IL1β, CRP — that accompanies aging in the absence of overt infection. Its cellular substrate is senescent stromal and immune cells secreting the SASP (senescence-associated secretory phenotype),
combined with microbial translocation across an aging gut barrier and accumulated DAMPs from

cell turnover. For hematopoiesis this matters because CHIP — clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential — is itself strongly age-associated (prevalence rises from <1% under age 40 to >10%
over age 70) and because CHIP clones seem to both respond to and feed the inflammaging milieu.
Two papers anchor the discussion. Jaiswal and colleagues (Jaiswal 2017 NEJM) demonstrated
in two large cohorts that CHIP carriers have elevated risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease independent of traditional risk factors, and that the mechanism involves elevated IL-6 and
macrophage-driven inflammation in the vascular wall — the IL-6 axis being central. CHIP thus
emerges as a systemic inflammation source that damages remote tissues. Cull and colleagues (Cull
2020 Exp Hematol) showed in T et2-/-mouse models that CHIP-mutant HSCs are IFN-hypersensitive
(or at least differentially responsive to type I IFN) and that this differential response may contribute
to clonal fitness in inflamed marrow — an important precedent for Huey’s argument that mutant
HSCs can be reshaped by IFN in ways distinct from WT HSCs. The Chavakis NLRP3-Tet2 CH
work (Fuster 2017 Science; Sano 2018 Circ Res) closes the loop: Tet2-deficient macrophages have elevated NLRP3 inflammasome activity , elevated IL-1β secretion, and feed back onto the HSC niche
in a self-amplifying fashion. CHIP is therefore a textbook case of a cell-intrinsic mutation that
remodels its own microenvironment to favor its persistence.
For Huey’s thesis this is the backdrop of adult MPN initiation: JAK2V617F arises in an alreadyinflammatory , aged marrow, and the DCAF7 axis operates on top of this baseline. The perinatal axis (Aim 2) and the adult MPN axis (Aim 1) are therefore two very different inflammatory
contexts — in Aim 2 the inflammatory milieu is dominated by microbial colonization and developmental IFN; in Aim 1 it is dominated by inflammaging and JAK2V617F-autonomous inflammation.
DCAF7 is doing the same biochemistry in both (degrading IFIT3) but the upstream IFN source and
the downstream cellular consequence may differ.
[FIGURE: Two timeline panels. Panel A — perinatal window, E18 through P14, with bars for microbial
colonization, circulating IFN-α, HSC transition, and an arrow indicating the P4 DCAF7-dependent switch.
Panel B — aging timeline from age 30 to age 80, with rising CHIP prevalence, rising plasma IL-6 and IL-1β,
and the emergence of JAK2V617F-driven MPN after age 50, with DCAF7-IFIT3 drawn as a modulator of
IFN sensitivity throughout.]
Committee Q: Is the DCAF7 axis likely to matter more in young or old MPN patients? Answer: My
prediction is that it matters in both, but for different reasons. In young MPN, the IFIT3-amplified
IFN response is most relevant to therapy response — pegylated IFN-α works best in young patients
with low allele burden, and DCAF7 inhibition should maximally sensitize that population. In old
MPN, the inflammaging baseline means tonic ISG expression is already high, so DCAF7 inhibition
would shift an already-elevated IFN response further up, potentially inducing HSC exhaustion of
the mutant clone. These are testable hypotheses in age-stratified patient cohorts and in young vs
aged JAK2V617F knock-in mice.

sections.title: Integrative summary — why type I IFN is the right entry point, and what to say about

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
each adjacent axis
Huey’s thesis rests on a precise biochemical claim: DCAF7 is a CRL4 substrate receptor that ubiquitinates IFIT3 for proteasomal degradation, and in JAK2V617F MPN cells this degradation sets
the ceiling of the type I IFN response. Everything in Section Q is the defensive perimeter around

this claim. The integrative logic is as follows.
Why type I IFN is the right entry point. IFIT3 is a type I IFN ISG. Its promoter is ISGF3-driven.
Its induction follows IFNAR engagement. Its protein level is therefore a direct readout of the IFNAR ￿ JAK1/TYK2 ￿ STAT1/STAT2/IRF9 axis, which is the axis that pegylated IFN-α therapy
engages clinically in PV and ET. Any hypothesis that puts DCAF7 on a different axis — IL-1β,
TNF-α, IFN-γ, IFN-λ — is refuted by the substrate identity . IL-1β engages IL-1R1/MyD88, TNF-α
engages TNFR1/2, IFN-γ engages IFNGR and drives GAF (not ISGF3); none of these drives IFIT3.
Type III IFN uses ISGF3 but HSCs lack the receptor. Type I IFN is therefore not a choice among
equals; it is the mechanistically necessary entry point given IFIT3’s identity as the substrate.
What to say about each adjacent axis. The following one-paragraph answers are the committeeready summaries.
IL-1β. IL-1β is the best-validated driver of clonal expansion in preleukemic hematopoiesis (Avagyan 2021 Science; Rai 2022; Caiado 2023) and is elevated in MPN plasma. It acts through IL1R1/MyD88 on HSCs and is the substrate of canakinumab. It is not, however, the driver of IFIT3
expression — IFIT3 is ISGF3-driven, not NF-κB-driven. My Aim 1a cytokine array will confirm
that DCAF7 loss does not change IL-1β secretion, and an Il1r1-/- cross will formally exclude IL-1β
as an obligate mediator. IL-1β is therefore a parallel axis in MPN, not a competing explanation for my
phenotype.
TNF-α. TNF-α is cell-autonomously produced by JAK2V617F clones (Fleischman 2011; Fisher 2017)
and selects for the mutant clone via differential TNFR2-NF-κB signaling (Heaton 2018). It is not a
known IFIT3 driver and is not an ISGF3 input. Like IL-1β, it is an MPN-relevant cytokine that my
cytokine array will profile; it does not compete with the IFIT3-IFN axis.
cGAS-STING. cGAS-STING is the most compelling upstream hypothesis for where the endogenous
type I IFN in MPN marrow comes from. JAK2V617F drives replication stress and DNA damage
(Chen 2014; Hurtado-Nedelec 2013), generating cytosolic DNA that activates cGAS and drives
low-level IFN production. This would give the mutant clone a tonic ISG set-point on top of which
DCAF7 loss would act. It is a coherent model and I list it as a future direction, but I do not commit
to it as a current aim. On a Sting-/- background I predict reduced baseline ISG expression but
preserved DCAF7-dependent sensitization to exogenous IFN-α.
TLRs. TLR2/4 on HSCs (Nagai 2006; Esplin 2011) and chronic TLR engagement in general (Pietras
2016) drive myeloid bias and HSC exhaustion. These are MyD88/TRIF pathways that converge
on NF-κB and, for TLR3/endosomal TLR4, on IRF3 ￿ type I IFN. In my system I will confirm that
DCAF7 loss does not alter p65 or IRF3 phosphorylation after TLR agonist panels — i.e., DCAF7 is
not operating on a TLR-proximal node.
Inflammasomes. The inflammasome produces IL-1β and IL-18, not type I IFN; it is transcriptionally
orthogonal to IFIT3. The Tet2-NLRP3 axis (Chavakis) is relevant to CH pathophysiology and is a
parallel axis in MPN marrow, but it does not intersect with IFIT3 regulation.
Type II and type III IFN. Type II IFN (IFN-γ) drives GAF, not ISGF3; it does not strongly induce IFIT3.
Type III IFN uses ISGF3 but HSCs lack IFNLR1. Neither is on the causal path.
Microbiome. The microbiome is a tonic source of PRR ligands that calibrates baseline IFN and inflammation (Iwamura 2017; Josefsdottir 2017). For Aim 2 specifically , the perinatal IFN pulse (Li
2020) may be microbiota-driven, and germ-free rederivation of my Dcaf7-conditional mice will
formally test this.

Inflammaging and CHIP .Inflammaging (elevated baseline IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α with age) is the backdrop against which adult MPN initiates (Jaiswal 2017 NEJM; Cull 2020 Exp Hematol ). It predicts
that DCAF7-dependent IFN sensitization will operate on top of an already-elevated ISG set-point
in older patients, and may therefore have differential magnitude in young vs aged MPN.
Adaptive immunity. Type I IFN upregulates MHC-I and PD-L1 on MPN cells via IRF1. DCAF7
inhibition would amplify this axis, predicting synergy with anti-PD-1 blockade. This is a future
combination-therapy direction, not a current aim.
[FIGURE: A central node labeled “DCAF7-IFIT3-IFNAR axis” with arrows drawn to each adjacent axis —
IL-1β, TNF-α, cGAS-STING, TLRs, inflammasome, microbiome, inflammaging, adaptive immunity — and
each arrow annotated with one word: “parallel” (IL-1β, TNF-α), “upstream” (cGAS-STING, TLRs, microbiome), “orthogonal” (inflammasome, type II/III IFN), “modifier” (inflammaging), “downstream” (adaptive
immunity).]
The thirty-second elevator answer. If the committee asks in one sentence why type I IFN and
not some other axis, Huey’s answer is: “Because the substrate I identified — IFIT3 — is an ISGF3driven type I IFN ISG, and its stability sets the ceiling of the IFNAR-dependent response; every adjacent
inflammatory axis in MPN (IL-1β, TNF-α, cGAS-STING, the inflammasome, the microbiome) is either a
parallel driver of MPN clonal fitness, an upstream source of the IFN ligand itself, or a modifier of the baseline
ISG set-point — none of them regulates IFIT3 directly, and I have designed controls in Aim 1a and Aim 2
to formally exclude each as a mediator of my phenotype.”
That sentence, delivered confidently , is the point of Section Q.
Citation key (alphabetical, as integrated above)
• Avagyan S et al. 2021 Science — IL-1β-driven differential clonal fitness in zebrafish CH
• Baldridge MT et al. 2010 Nature — IFN-γ activates HSCs during M. avium infection
• Caiado F et al. 2023 — IL-1β expansion of mutant HSCs in aged CH
• Chen E et al. 2014 — JAK2V617F replication stress and DNA damage
• Cull AH et al. 2020 Exp Hematol — IFN-hypersensitivity of CHIP-mutant HSCs
• Esplin BL et al. 2011 J Immunol — Chronic TLR4 engagement exhausts HSCs
• Essers MAG et al. 2009 Nature — Type I IFN activates HSCs from quiescence
• Fisher DAC et al. 2017 Blood — JAK2V617F cell-autonomous inflammatory secretome in MPN
• Fleischman AG et al. 2011 Blood — TNF-α selects for JAK2V617F HSCs
• Heaton WL et al. 2018 — TNF amplification of the JAK2V617F MPN clone
• Hurtado-Nedelec M et al. 2013 — DNA damage and oxidative stress in MPN
• Iwamura C et al. 2017 Blood — Germ-free mice have impaired myelopoiesis
• Jaiswal S et al. 2017 NEJM — CHIP-cardiovascular link via IL-6
• Josefsdottir KS et al. 2017 Blood — Antibiotic-induced dysbiosis and HSC loss
• Kleppe M et al. 2015 Cancer Cell — JAK inhibition remodels MPN niche inflammation
• Kumar H et al. 2011 Int Rev Immunol — TLR signaling review
• Li Y et al. 2020 Cell Stem Cell — Perinatal type I IFN pulse and HSC maturation
• Man SM, Kanneganti T-D 2017 Nat Rev Immunol — Inflammasome review
• Mogensen TH 2009 Clin Microbiol Rev — PRR review
• Nagai Y et al. 2006 Immunity — TLR expression and function on HSCs

• Pietras EM et al. 2016 Nat Cell Biol — Chronic IL-1β myeloid skewing of HSCs
• Rai S et al. 2022 — IL-1R1 signaling in JAK2V617F MPN pathogenesis
Program-Level Contingency Planning and Thesis Resilience
Tier 3. Operates at higher altitude than Section N (aim-by-aim experimental alternatives). The question here
is not “which replicate failed” but “what if the scaffolding of the thesis is wrong, and how is the program
built so that no single failure collapses the whole structure?”
A qualifying-exam committee is trained to find the one premise that, if removed, causes the thesis
to fall. The productive answer is not defensive rhetoric. The productive answer is a pre-registered
decision tree: “Here are the three premises. Here is what each is resting on. Here is the experiment
that would falsify each. Here is the thesis I would write if that falsification came back positive.”

sections.title: is that decision tree, written in advance, so that when the committee asks “what if DCAF7

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
doesn’t degrade IFIT3 in vivo?” the answer is not an embarrassed silence but a one-sentence summary of a branch that was already drawn.
Two architectural commitments run through this entire section. First, the BC18630 pharmacologic
arm and the Dcaf7 genetic KO arm mutually cross-validate each other— neither can be dismissed
without dismissing both, which is why the thesis is robust to off-target criticism of either tool alone.
Second, Aim 1 (adult MPN therapy) and Aim 2 (perinatal fetal-to-adult transition) are independently defensible theses — the failure of either aim yields a focused but complete dissertation in
the surviving aim.

sections.title: The Three Premises and Their Falsification

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The thesis rests on three load-bearing premises. They should be named, in order, without hesitation.
P1 — DCAF7 is a bona fide CRL4 substrate receptor. DCAF7 carries a WD40 beta-propeller fold
and appeared on the Emanuele 2011 Cell pipeline of high-confidence CRL substrate-receptor candidates, which remains the foundational catalog for the field. The strongest supportive evidence
is (a) co-immunoprecipitation with CUL4 and DDB1, (b) WD40 architecture consistent with other
DCAF family members, and (c) a published body of work treating DCAF7 as a substrate adapter
in multiple substrate contexts.
The committee’s most pointed rebuttal is the Glenewinkel 2016 Sci Rep position: DCAF7 functions primarily as a scaffolding protein for DYRK1A, HIPK, and related kinases, and the “ligase
adapter” assignment is inferred more from family membership than from demonstrated CRL4dependent ubiquitination. A committee member who has read Glenewinkel will ask: “Have you
shown, with in vitro ubiquitination reconstitution, that IFIT3 is ubiquitinated by a CUL4-DDB1DCAF7 complex and not by a CUL4 complex using a different adapter?”
The falsification experiment is direct and already planned: in vitro reconstitution with purified CUL4-DDB1, recombinant DCAF7, E1/E2, ubiquitin, and IFIT3 substrate , with a CUL4
neddylation-dead control and a DCAF7-deleted control. If IFIT3 ubiquitination is lost without

DCAF7 but restored on DCAF7 add-back, P1 survives. If IFIT3 is ubiquitinated in the DCAF7-null
reaction, P1 is falsified and DCAF7 is acting non-catalytically .
If P1 is wrong, the thesis does not end; it pivots (see R2).
P2 — IFIT3 is the relevant substrate of DCAF7 in JAK2V617F MPN cells. Evidence: SILAC quantitative proteomics of Dcaf7-KO versus WT MPN cells identifies IFIT3 among the top stabilized
proteins; K-εGG diGly enrichment identifies IFIT3 ubiquitination sites lost in Dcaf7 KO; IFIT3 has
a coherent biological story because it is an ISG whose stability gates IFN-α sensitivity (consistent
with Li 2020 Cell Stem Cell on perinatal IFN and ISG dynamics in hematopoiesis). The mechanistic story is therefore: DCAF7 loss stabilizes IFIT3, amplifying IFN-α sensing, hypersensitizing
JAK2V617F cells to interferon therapy .
The committee’s rebuttal: IFIT3 is one protein among 20+ on a stabilized-protein list. Why IFIT3
and not an ISG with a better-characterized cell-autonomous role? This is the “you picked the
answer you wanted” objection.
The falsification experiment is a rescue and deletion pair: (a) in Dcaf7-KO MPN cells, IFIT3 knockdown should partially rescue the IFN-α hypersensitivity phenotype; (b) in WT MPN cells, IFIT3
overexpression should phenocopy Dcaf7 loss for IFN-α sensitivity . If neither is true, IFIT3 is a
passenger.
If P2 is wrong, the top-20 SILAC/K-εGG list is the contingency (see R3).
P3 — DCAF7 loss is selectively toxic to JAK2V617F cells. Evidence: Gao 2015 eLife documented
DCAF7 as a hematopoietic selectivity node; preliminary data in Jak2V617F-Mullally (Mullally 2010
Cancer Cell) cells show selective vulnerability relative to WT counterparts; BC18630 (Lear 2025 Sci
Adv) shows differential activity in JAK2V617F models.
The committee’s rebuttal: “selective” across how many independent JAK2V617F models? The
Mullally knock-in is one model with one genetic architecture (heterozygous knock-in, defined allele burden). A CALR-mutant MPN model, a MPL-mutant MPN model, or a high-allele-burden
JAK2V617F model might not replicate.
The falsification experiment: a panel of MPN models — Mullally Jak2V617F (hetero), a conditional
high-allele-burden Jak2V617F, a Calr-del52 model, a Mpl-W515L model, plus patient-derived
JAK2V617F CD34+ cells. Genetic Dcaf7 KO and BC18630 treatment applied across the panel.
Selectivity should survive in at least the two JAK2V617F models plus patient cells.
If P3 is wrong but only in a narrow sense (e.g., fails in CALR but holds in JAK2V617F), the thesis
refines its scope claim (see R4).

mechanism (R4)

sections.title: If DCAF7 Is Only a Scaffold (P1 Falsified)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Suppose the in vitro reconstitution is clean and reproducible: CUL4-DDB1 ubiquitinates IFIT3
whether DCAF7 is present or not, or DCAF7 add-back fails to restore ubiquitination over a DCAF7null baseline. This is the Glenewinkel 2016 scenario writ large. DCAF7 would then be acting as
a scaffolding protein — plausibly recruiting a kinase (DYRK1A or HIPK2) that phospho-primes
IFIT3 for recognition by a different E3 ligase, with DCAF7’s loss disrupting the kinase-substrate
encounter.
The pivot is tractable because the therapeutic logic does not require the CRL4 adapter identity .
BC18630 (Lear 2025) was developed against a DCAF7 interaction surface, not against a CUL4 recruitment interface. If BC18630 works by disrupting the DYRK1A-DCAF7 scaffold — preventing
kinase-substrate proximity — the clinical implication (IFIT3 stabilization, IFN-α hypersensitization in JAK2V617F cells) is preserved. The molecular narrative changes from “ubiquitin ligase
adapter” to “scaffolding-dependent kinase-substrate trap,” but the patient-facing story and the
drug-development rationale are unchanged.
The experimental pivot has three components:

1. BioID (or T urboID) on DCAF7 in MPN cells, with and without BC18630, mapping proximity
interactors. Expect DYRK1A, HIPK1/2, and a set of substrates enriched in the WT condition
and depleted when BC18630 is on. This delivers a kinase-substrate interactome and simultaneously shows BC18630’s mechanism as a scaffolding disruptor.
2. DYRK1A inhibitor (harmine, DYR533) epistasis with Dcaf7 KO. If DYRK1A phosphorylates
IFIT3 and DCAF7 is the scaffold, DYRK1A inhibition should phenocopy Dcaf7 KO for the
IFIT3-stabilization and IFN-hypersensitivity phenotypes.
3. Phospho-proteomics of IFIT3 in WT vs. Dcaf7-KO cells , looking for loss of a DYRK1Aconsensus phospho-site. If found, the site can be mutated to non-phosphorylatable (S/T ￿
A) and tested for resistance to DCAF7-dependent stabilization.
The thesis remains coherent under this pivot. The title shifts from “DCAF7 is a CRL4 substrate
receptor degrading IFIT3” to “DCAF7 scaffolds a kinase-substrate complex that primes IFIT3 for
degradation in JAK2V617F MPN.” The clinical message — BC18630 hypersensitizes JAK2V617F
MPN to IFN-α by stabilizing IFIT3 via loss of DCAF7 scaffolding function — is preserved. Notably ,
this version of the thesis aligns more closely with the published Glenewinkel 2016 interpretation
and is therefore less controversial in the scaffolding-literature quarter of the committee.

sections.title: If IFIT3 Is Not the Dominant Substrate In Vivo (P2 Falsified)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The SILAC and K-εGG list is the insurance policy for P2. A productive QE answer does not pretend
the list doesn’t exist; it names the top candidates by rank and says “I have a pre-committed pivot
order.” The honest framing for a student whose wet-lab list is not yet finalized is: “The nexthighest-ranked stabilized protein on the SILAC list that passes validation filters (stabilization on
Western, loss of diGly signal in K-εGG, cell-autonomous functional relevance in MPN) becomes
the primary substrate candidate.” Three realistic candidate classes, consistent with an ISG-adjacent
proteomic signature in JAK2V617F cells:
• A second ISG family member (e.g., IFIT1 or IFIT2, or OAS family). Rationale: the IFIT family
is tightly co-regulated and structurally similar; IFIT1 is the most studied member and has its
own RNA-binding-dependent antiviral pathway . If IFIT1 is on the list as the next-ranked stabilized protein, pivoting preserves the IFN-signaling narrative nearly intact, with IFIT1 replacing
IFIT3 as the effector molecule. Buckley 2012 Cell Stem Cell established the K-εGG diGly HSC
paradigm that legitimizes pivoting within a ubiquitin site-mapping hit list.
• An ISG15 conjugation machinery component or an ISGylation substrate. ISGylation-linked
proteins frequently appear in SILAC stabilization screens downstream of IFN signaling. If, say ,
USP18 or a specifically ISGylated ISG appears, the mechanistic hypothesis becomes “DCAF7
regulates the ISGylation/de-ISGylation balance in JAK2V617F cells,” which is a coherent and
novel thesis.
• A non-IFN ISG-adjacent regulator — for instance, a STAT1/STAT2 co-factor or a negativefeedback component (SOCS family). If DCAF7’s true substrate is a negative regulator of IFN
signaling, DCAF7 loss would stabilize that regulator and the direction of the phenotype (IFN
hypersensitivity) requires reinterpretation. This is the scenario that demands the most care: the
committee will ask “does the sign of your phenotype match the sign of stabilization?”

In all three cases, the SILAC list must be filtered through three gates before a pivot commits: (1)
confirmed stabilization by Western in Dcaf7-KO MPN cells (orthogonal to SILAC), (2) loss of a
specific diGly ubiquitination site by K-εGG, (3) knockdown rescues at least part of the Dcaf7-KO
IFN-hypersensitivity phenotype. Any candidate that passes all three gates is a legitimate substitute
for IFIT3; any candidate that passes only two is a reason to keep investigating but not yet to pivot.

sections.title: If JAK2V617F Selectivity Doesn’t Replicate (P3 Falsified, Narrow Sense)

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Suppose the effect is clean in Jak2V617F-Mullally (Mullally 2010 Cancer Cell) cells but weak or
absent in a second JAK2V617F model (e.g., a high-allele-burden conditional) and a CALR-del52
model. The premise is not obliterated — it is narrowed. The thesis is no longer “DCAF7 is a
universal MPN vulnerability” but “DCAF7 is a JAK2V617F context-specific vulnerability whose
dependency is modulated by allele burden, clone dominance, and co-mutation landscape.”
The scope question reframes as a mechanism question : why Mullally and not other models ? Three
candidate explanations, each experimentally tractable:
1. Allele burden dependence. Jak2V617F-Mullally is a heterozygous knock-in with physiologic
expression levels. If the DCAF7 dependency is lost at high allele burden (homozygous or V AF
> 50%), the mechanism may involve a threshold of JAK2-STAT1 pathway activation matched to
the WT allele. Test: titrate Jak2V617F allele burden in vitro using inducible systems; measure
DCAF7-KO sensitivity as a function of V AF.
2. Clone dominance and competition. DCAF7 may be a competitive-fitness gene in Jak2V617F
cells only in the presence of a WT competitor (i.e., relevant in early clonal expansion, irrelevant
in clonal dominance). Test: competitive transplant with Dcaf7-KO Jak2V617F vs. Dcaf7-WT
Jak2V617F donors at varying chimerism.
3. Co-mutation landscape. Mullally Jak2V617F is a clean single-hit model. Adding TET2 or
DNMT3A co-mutations (common in MPN) may alter DCAF7 dependency . Test: Jak2V617F;
Tet2-KO or Jak2V617F; Dnmt3a-KO backgrounds, with Dcaf7 conditional loss.
Any one of these three mechanistic findings becomes a publishable thesis chapter and positions
the DCAF7 story as a context-specific vulnerability — which is how most targeted therapies are
ultimately deployed anyway . The thesis title narrows to “DCAF7 is a conditional vulnerability in
JAK2V617F MPN defined by allele burden / clonal architecture / co-mutation background,” and
that is a cleaner, more clinically translatable thesis than a pan-MPN claim.

sections.title: If BC18630 Has Off-T arget Effects

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
BC18630 (Lear 2025 Sci Adv) is a small molecule with published selectivity and target-engagement
data, but off-target activity is the default expectation for any small molecule entering a new therapeutic context. If a thermal proteome profiling (TPP), a CETSA panel, or a cross-reactivity screen
reveals that BC18630 hits one or more proteins other than DCAF7 at the concentrations used in the
MPN assays, the thesis architecture survives specifically because of the mutual-cross-validation design .

The architecture: the genetic Dcaf7 KO is the anchor. BC18630 is a tool that phenocopies the
genetic anchor. When the KO and the small molecule agree on a phenotype, the phenotype is real
and attributable to DCAF7. When they disagree, the disagreement is informative. If BC18630 has
an off-target effect that drives part of the phenotype, the genetic KO phenotype does not include the
off-target effect, so the DCAF7-dependent component is isolated. Conversely , BC18630 serves as
a fast-acting, dose-titratable tool that complements the slow and binary nature of Cre-lox deletion.
Neither arm can be dismissed without dismissing both.
If BC18630 is judged too compromised to use as the central pharmacologic tool, two backups:
1. BC18813 (Lear 2025 follow-up chemotype), if available from the Lear lab or via commercial
re-synthesis, serves as an orthogonal chemical tool. Phenotypic agreement between BC18630
and BC18813, on the same Dcaf7-KO-phenocopying readout, triangulates on-target activity .
2. Second-generation in-house resynthesis of a BC18630 analog at Northwestern Chemistry
of Life Processes or via a medicinal-chemistry collaboration. Structure-activity-relationship
(SAR) shifting of the BC18630 scaffold to reduce off-target binding while preserving DCAF7
affinity is a 6–12 month chemistry effort that, if successful, becomes its own translational chapter.
Under the off-target scenario, the thesis re-weights: the genetic arm produces the primary claims;
BC18630 becomes a supportive tool for pharmacodynamic and xenograft experiments; and the
translational section is framed as “proof-of-concept that pharmacologic DCAF7 disruption is
achievable, with next-generation compounds required for clinical development.” That is not a
failure — it is a typical, publishable position for a chemical biology thesis where the compound is
a first-in-class tool rather than a clinical candidate.

sections.title: If Vav-Cre P4 Lethality Is Not IFN-Dependent

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
The current working model for Aim 2 is that Vav-Cre Dcaf7 deletion produces a postnatal day 4
lethality driven, at least in part, by dysregulated IFN signaling during the fetal-to-adult hematopoietic transition — consistent with Li 2020 Cell Stem Cell, which established IFN as a stage-specific
regulator of perinatal HSC. The direct test is Ifnar1 rescue: cross Vav-Cre Dcaf7-flox onto an Ifnar1KO background; if the P4 lethality is IFN-dependent, survival should be extended.
If the Ifnar1 rescue fails — P4 lethality is unchanged — the Aim 2 mechanism is not IFN-mediated.
The thesis does not end; it reframes. The pivot is to a non-IFN developmental program , and
scRNA-seq of perinatal LSK (Lin-Sca1+Kit+) cells at E18.5, P0, P2, P4 is the unbiased discovery
tool. The target programs are well-characterized stage-specific developmental axes:
• HMGA2 — a canonical fetal HSC self-renewal factor whose expression is temporally restricted
to fetal/neonatal hematopoiesis. Loss of HMGA2 expression is part of the fetal-to-adult transition. If Dcaf7-KO perinatal HSCs fail to extinguish HMGA2, the transition is stalled at the fetal
state. DCAF7 could plausibly regulate HMGA2 directly or indirectly .
• SOX17 — a fetal-specific HSC transcription factor, absent from adult HSCs. Retained SOX17 in
a postnatal HSC is a classic fetal-state-lock-in signature. If scRNA-seq shows persistent SOX17
at P4 in Dcaf7-KO LSK, this is the mechanism.

• LIN28B — fetal/neonatal regulator of self-renewal and lymphoid lineage bias; the LIN28B-let7-HMGA2 axis is a well-established fetal identity circuit.
Any of these three findings reframes Aim 2 as “DCAF7 licenses the fetal-to-adult hematopoietic
transition through a non-IFN mechanism — specifically , regulation of the [HMGA2/SOX17/LIN28B]
axis.” That is a clean, publishable thesis aligned with a major open question in developmental
hematopoiesis.
The architectural point: Aim 2 has two possible mechanistic framings (IFN-dependent vs. non-IFN
developmental) and both are scientifically interesting. The Ifnar1 rescue is a fork in the decision
tree, not a failure point.

sections.title: If Rosa26-CreERT2 T amoxifen Shows No Adult MPN Phenotype

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
This is the scenario the student should treat most carefully , because it touches the validity of the
preliminary Mx1-Cre data on which Aim 1 genetics depends. Mx1-Cre is induced by poly(I:C),
which is itself an IFN inducer. If the Mx1-Cre Dcaf7-KO MPN phenotype is observed but the
Rosa26-CreERT2 Dcaf7-KO MPN phenotype (tamoxifen induction, no IFN confound; V entura 2007
Nature) is absent, the interpretation is devastating: the Mx1-Cre effect may be a poly(I:C)-driven
IFN artifact rather than a bona fide DCAF7-loss phenotype in adult MPN.
This is a true worst-case scenario for Aim 1a genetics , and the thesis must have a written plan for
it. The plan has three layers.
First, the worst-case interpretation must be falsified or confirmed , not assumed. The comparison
is: Mx1-Cre + poly(I:C) vs. Mx1-Cre + no poly(I:C) (leaky baseline) vs. Rosa26-CreERT2 + tamoxifen vs. Vav-Cre (developmental deletion, captures MPN-initiating cells). If the Rosa26-CreERT2
adult induction is genuinely clean and genuinely negative, the Mx1-Cre phenotype is poly(I:C) /
IFN-driven and not a DCAF7-adult-MPN phenotype. If the Rosa26-CreERT2 induction is merely
incomplete (e.g., tamoxifen does not reach the HSC compartment adequately), the failure is technical and a different inducible system (UBC-CreERT2, Scl-CreERT2) is the next step.
Second, Aim 1 does not collapse if Aim 1a genetics fail , because Aim 1 has two arms: genetic lossof-function (Aim 1a) and biochemical mechanism + pharmacologic (Aim 1b: in vitro Jak2V617FMullally cells, BC18630, xenograft, K-εGG substrate mapping). Aim 1b is a complete thesis chapter
on its own — biochemical mechanism + cellular phenotype + pharmacologic rescue + xenograft efficacy . The Aim 1a genetic arm becomes a short “attempted but inconclusive” chapter with an honest discussion: “Mx1-Cre and Rosa26-CreERT2 gave discordant results, likely reflecting poly(I:C)-
dependent IFN confounds in the Mx1-Cre model; definitive in vivo genetic dissection awaits a
cleaner inducible system.”
Third, the Aim 2 V av-Cre arm is unaffected by Aim 1a failure because Vav-Cre is not IFNinducible; it deletes during hematopoietic development without poly(I:C). Vav-Cre Dcaf7 deletion
remains a valid test of DCAF7 function in the hematopoietic lineage.
The thesis under this worst case: Aim 1 = biochemistry + pharmacology + xenograft, Aim 2 =
Vav-Cre perinatal. Both intact, the in vivo adult genetic chapter truncated. That is a complete,
defensible dissertation.

The utility of the table is not the specific numbers but the existence of the numbers. Every pivot in

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:

theses. A PhD dissertation in the Melo-Cardenas Lab does not require both aims to succeed; it
requires one of them to succeed completely . The architecture is deliberate.

The point of Table R3 is not to plan for failure but to demonstrate, at the defense, that the thesis was
designed with independent load-bearing columns . The student should be able to draw this table
on a whiteboard and say: “Here are the four plausible outcomes. In each, there is a defensible
thesis. The core decision — committing a five-year program to DCAF7 in hematopoiesis — is
robust to the failure of any single premise.”
This is the most powerful rhetorical move available in a qualifying exam. It moves the committee
from “what if you’re wrong?” to “what is your primary version?” — because every version of
“wrong” has a named backup, and the committee has already seen the backup.

subsections.title: Overview
subsections.body:
Part II — Committee Questions by Section 40
Non-UPS Protein Degradation: Autophagy and Lysosomal Pathways . . . . . . . . . . . 40
The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 42
Program-Level Contingency Planning and Thesis Resilience . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 42

About This Document
This is Tier 3 of a three-tier study guide assembled for the qualifying-exam defense of the DCAF7
thesis. Tier 3 covers topics adjacent to the thesis core — areas the committee may probe to
stress-test scope decisions and to see whether the student has genuinely considered alternatives.
The three chapters are: non-UPS protein degradation (autophagy and lysosomal pathways), the
broader innate immunity and inflammation landscape beyond type I IFN, and program-level contingency planning at the thesis-architecture level.
How the document is organized. The document is in two parts:
1. Part I — Adjacent T opics and Program Resilience (this main body): prose exposition of each
topic area, with key facts, tables, and figure callouts. Q&A content has been moved out of the
body for cleaner reading.
2. Part II — Committee Questions by Section (appendix at end): every anticipated committee
question and answer, grouped by the chapter it pertains to.
Tier 1 (completed) covered the foundational biology: UPS/CRL4 architecture, DCAF7 biology ,
normal hematopoiesis, the fetal-to-adult HSC transition, interferon signaling and the IFIT family ,
MPNs, and an integrative biological synthesis.
Tier 2 (completed) covered methodology and applied context: AML, CHIP/CH, mouse genetics and Cre systems, flow cytometry gating, K-εGG ubiquitinomics, alternative experimental approaches, and an integrative methodological synthesis.
Why Tier 3 matters at the defense. The committee will test whether the student can defend scope
decisions — why autophagy was excluded from Aim 1b, why type I IFN is the right entry point
and not IL-1β or cGAS-STING, and what the contingency plans are if core premises fail. This tier
equips the student to answer those stress-tests without defensiveness.
Format. Prose exposition, tables for comparative summaries, and italic figure callouts that mark
locations for figure insertion in a follow-up pass.

Part I — Adjacent T opics and Program Resilience
Non-UPS Protein Degradation: Autophagy and Lysosomal Pathways
This section exists for one strategic reason. Huey’s thesis argues that DCAF7, acting through a
CRL4 E3 ligase, ubiquitinates IFIT3 and commits it to proteasomal degradation in JAK2V617Fdriven MPN cells. The committee chair works on both the ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS)
and interferon signaling, which is exactly the intersection where “have you ruled out autophagy?”
becomes a fair and predictable question. A thoughtful defense therefore requires fluency in the
parallel degradative machinery — macroautophagy , chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), microautophagy , endosomal-lysosomal routing, and ERAD — together with a crisp experimental
argument for why IFIT3 is a UPS substrate rather than an autophagy cargo. Section P delivers
that fluency and closes with the integrative summary that ties autophagy’s legitimate HSC relevance (Ho 2017 Nature) to why it was correctly scoped out of Aim 1b but retained as a candidate
mechanism for the Vav-Cre P4 phenotype in Aim 2.

qanda.q: “Which of your three premises are you least confident in, and why?”
qanda.a: P1 is technically the most vulnerable because the field has not unambiguously demonstrated DCAF7 as a catalytic ligase adapter in any published in vitro reconstitution — the Glenewinkel scaffolding interpretation is credible. However, P1’s falsification does not collapse the thesis; it redirects it toward a scaffolding-based mechanism where the therapeutic implication (BC18630 disrupts a scaffolding interaction) remains intact. P3 is the premise whose falsification would be most disruptive because loss of JAK2V617F selectivity removes the therapeutic rationale. That is why P3 is tested against the broadest model panel.
qanda.section: The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape

qanda.q: “How will you avoid serial pivoting — if IFIT3 fails, you pivot to candidate 2; if candidate 2 fails, to candidate 3?”
qanda.a: The gating is pre-specified. Each candidate must pass the three-gate filter before commitment, and the commitment is made at a discrete checkpoint (see Table R2, month 6). If two candidates pass the filter, both are characterized in parallel until the functional rescue experiment discriminates. If zero candidates pass, the thesis pivots to a substrate-agnostic framing: “DCAF7 loss sensitizes JAK2V617F MPN to IFN-α through a proteostasis mechanism we have mapped proteomically; identifying the dominant effector substrate becomes a future direction.” That is a defensible thesis because the phenotype (IFN hypersensitivity on Dcaf7 loss) is robust even if the molecular effector is unresolved.
qanda.section: The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape

qanda.q: “If selectivity fails in CALR-mutant MPN, haven’t you just lost half the MPN patient population?”
qanda.a: Yes, and that is useful information. The current JAK inhibitor landscape (ruxolitinib, fedratinib) is not CALR-selective either; MPN biology is genuinely heterogeneous by driver mutation. A JAK2V617F-specific vulnerability is clinically meaningful — JAK2V617F accounts for roughly 95% of PV , 50–60% of ET, and 50–60% of MF. A narrower but well-characterized dependency is more valuable than a claimed pan-MPN dependency that doesn’t hold up.
qanda.section: The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape

qanda.q: “Your thesis leans heavily on BC18630. What’s your plan if the Lear paper is retracted or contested?”
qanda.a: The genetic Dcaf7 KO anchors Aim 1. Every BC18630 experiment has a genetic-KO counterpart. If BC18630 becomes unusable — retraction, off-target storm, or supply failure — the genetic arm stands on its own as a complete mechanistic story . BC18630 is the translational bridge, not the mechanistic foundation.
qanda.section: The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape

qanda.q: “What if the Vav-Cre P4 lethality is something banal — a vascular defect, a non-cellautonomous effect from Vav-Cre activity in endothelial progenitors?”
qanda.a: Two controls. First, a more restricted Cre (e.g., HSC-SCL-CreERT2 or a stricter hematopoietic Cre) narrows cell-type specificity . Second, competitive reconstitution with Dcaf7-KO fetal liver cells into WT recipients isolates the cell-autonomous hematopoietic defect. If the P4 phenotype requires Dcaf7 loss in a non-hematopoietic compartment, that is itself a finding, and the thesis pivots to a stromal/vascular niche story — but the Vav-Cre competitive transplant should discriminate this in 3–4 months.
qanda.section: The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape

qanda.q: “If Mx1-Cre turns out to be a poly(I:C) artifact, should you not have led with Rosa26CreERT2 from the start?”
qanda.a: Mx1-Cre is faster and is the field-standard inducible hematopoietic Cre for MPN modeling, which is why it was the first tool used. The Rosa26-CreERT2 experiment was planned from the outset specifically to control for the poly(I:C) confound — that is the reason V entura 2007 Rosa26CreERT2 is in the orthogonal-validation portion of the aim. The experimental sequence is a feature of the design, not a retrofit.
qanda.section: The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape

qanda.q: “What happens if two of these checkpoints are missed simultaneously?”
qanda.a: The checkpoints are not independent — some co-vary . If the in vitro reconstitution (month 6) fails and the BC18630 concordance (month 12) fails, the thesis pivots to the scaffolding model (R2) and re-anchors on genetic KO (R5), and the Aim 1 narrative becomes “DCAF7 scaffolds a kinasesubstrate complex; genetic loss of DCAF7 in JAK2V617F MPN produces IFN hypersensitization via IFIT3 stabilization.” That is a consistent and defensible story even with two premises modified. If three premises are independently falsified — P1 is wrong, P2 is wrong, and P3 is wrong — Aim 1 is genuinely in trouble, and the thesis leans on Aim 2 (R9).
qanda.section: The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape

qanda.q: “Isn’t designing for Aim 1 to survive Aim 2’s failure (and vice versa) an admission that you don’t believe in the unified thesis?”
qanda.a: No — it is an admission that the unified thesis (DCAF7 in adult MPN + DCAF7 in perinatal transition) is ambitious and joins two temporally distinct hematopoietic contexts. Each context is independently important. A unified story is the desired outcome; two well-characterized contextspecific stories is an acceptable outcome. Program-level resilience is how a five-year PhD manages ambition without betting the dissertation on a single mechanistic unification.
qanda.section: The Broader Innate Immunity and Inflammation Landscape

subsections.title: IFIT Family: Deep Literature Gap Analysis: scope & rationale
subsections.body:
The IFIT (Interferon-Induced proteins with Tetratricopeptide repeats) family — IFIT1, IFIT2, IFIT3, and IFIT5 in humans — sits at the mechanistic core of Huey Mysliwiec's thesis. Her central claim is that DCAF7 is a CRL4 substrate receptor that poly-ubiquitinates IFIT3 and that DCAF7 loss stabilizes IFIT3, which in turn amplifies type I IFN signaling via two non-redundant routes: (i) the mitochondrial MAVS–TBK1 signalosome that IFIT3 scaffolds, and (ii) the cytosolic IFIT1–IFIT2–IFIT3 heterotrimer that blocks cap-0 mRNA translation and restricts viral replication. A committee stacked with UPS and IFN-signaling experts (Salina Dominguez, Diana Saleiro, Leon Platanias) will probe two flanks: whether Huey understands IFIT3 as a protein in its own right (structure, stability, RNA binding, stoichiometry with IFIT1/2) and whether she understands IFIT3 in vivo (Ifit knockout phenotypes, tissue tropism, interferon-stimulated gene (ISG) biology in hematopoietic versus non-hematopoietic compartments). The Tier 1–3 reading list already covered Fleith 2018 NAR (heterotrimer biochemistry), Chikhalya 2021 mBio (MAVS amplifier), Li 2023 Cell Death Dis (UBE2O–IFIT3 K236 in HCC), Schoggins 2011 Nature (ISG screen), and Yang 2012 (IFIT2 dimer structure). What Tier 1–3 did not cover — and what Huey flagged explicitly as missing from her prior draft — is the foundational molecular biology that predates and undergirds those papers.

This supplement therefore reconstructs the IFIT literature along four thematic axes that map directly onto her thesis defense. Axis A is the IFIT3–MAVS–TBK1 signaling amplifier itself, traced from the original Liu 2011 J Immunol paper (which Chikhalya 2021 built upon) through the structural reinterpretation by Johnson 2018 Immunity. Axis B is IFIT1 and IFIT5 RNA recognition — the "business end" of the heterotrimer — covered by Pichlmair 2011 Nat Immunol (PPP-RNA), Abbas 2013 Nature (IFIT5 cap crystal), Abbas 2017 PNAS (IFIT1–capped RNA crystal), Katibah 2014 PNAS (IFIT5 tRNA ligands), Daffis 2010 Nature (2′-O methylation host-self discrimination), and Terenzi 2006 JBC (eIF3 inhibition). Axis C is the in vivo Ifit knockout mouse literature — Pichlmair 2011, Fensterl 2012, Butchi 2013 — which tells the committee what happens when an IFIT is actually absent in a mammal. Axis D is IFITs in disease: Xiao 2006 PNAS (RIG-G/IFIT3 as a cytostatic p21/p27 inducer in APL, the oldest and most directly hematologic IFIT3 paper in the literature), Hsu 2013 PLoS One (IFIT3 knockdown sensitizes cells to dengue-driven apoptosis), Stawowczyk 2011 JBC (IFIT2 is pro-apoptotic and restrained by IFIT3), Liu 2019 Cell Reports (vaccinia C9 F-box directs SCF-mediated IFIT1/2/3 degradation — a direct viral precedent for DCAF7-mediated IFIT3 turnover), and He 2024 Cancer Lett (IFIT1/IFIT3 drive GSDME-dependent pyroptosis in myeloma/leukemia). Fensterl & Sen 2015 J Virol is included as the canonical IFIT review. Together these 15 papers fill the literature gap identified in the v1 supplement and equip Huey to defend IFIT3 as a structured, context-dependent, in vivo-validated node of the IFN response.

All citations draw on PubMed-retrieved metadata and open-access full-text where available. Each DOI is hyperlinked.

---

subsections.title: The original bridge — Liu et al. 2011, Journal of Immunology
subsections.body:
Before 2011 the field understood that cytosolic RNA virus sensing by RIG-I and MDA5 converged on mitochondrial MAVS (IPS-1/VISA/Cardif), which then recruited the noncanonical IKK-family kinase TBK1, leading to IRF3 phosphorylation and transcription of IFN-β. What nobody had cleanly explained was the physical architecture by which MAVS, anchored to the mitochondrial outer membrane by its C-terminal transmembrane domain, actually engaged and activated TBK1, which was cytosolic. The missing mechanistic piece was a scaffold — a protein that could concurrently contact MAVS on the mitochondrion and TBK1 in the cytosol. Liu, Chen, Wei, Shan, and Wang's 2011 paper in Journal of Immunology filled that gap and, in doing so, placed IFIT3 on the signaling side of the antiviral ledger rather than the effector side.

Their experimental system was deliberately reductionist. They used HEK293 and HeLa cells with transient overexpression or siRNA knockdown of IFIT3, treated with Sendai virus (SeV, a canonical RIG-I agonist) or vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV). Readouts included IFN-β and ISG luciferase reporter assays (IFN-β-Luc, PRD(III-I)-Luc driven by IRF3 response elements), co-immunoprecipitation between ectopically expressed Flag-IFIT3 and endogenous or tagged MAVS and TBK1, confocal immunofluorescence to demonstrate mitochondrial colocalization, and reconstitution experiments with point mutants of the IFIT3 TPR motifs. They measured antiviral output directly by VSV titer.

The key findings arrived as a layered gain-of-function/loss-of-function argument. IFIT3 overexpression enhanced SeV-induced IRF3 activation and IFN-β reporter output, and IFIT3 knockdown attenuated both. Co-IP demonstrated that IFIT3 bound TBK1 through its amino-terminus (mapped to K38 of TBK1) and bound MAVS through a TPR surface centered on E164/E165 of IFIT3. Mutation of E164/E165 disrupted the MAVS interaction and abolished IFIT3's ability to amplify IRF3 phosphorylation even when IFIT3 was overexpressed. Functionally, IFIT3-depleted cells produced less IFN-β mRNA after SeV and lost containment of VSV, whereas cells reconstituted with wild-type IFIT3 recovered both. The authors framed IFIT3 as a "bridge" — a feed-forward amplifier induced by IFN that then redeploys itself onto the upstream signalosome to reinforce its own induction.

The paper's impact was foundational. It recast IFIT3 from a passive antiviral effector that merely sequesters PPP-RNA with IFIT1 (the Pichlmair 2011 view, published the same year) into an active signaling scaffold on the mitochondrion. Every subsequent study of IFIT3-dependent IFN amplification — including Chikhalya 2021 mBio, which Huey cites in Tier 2 — builds directly on Liu 2011 as the primary mechanistic precedent. The paper has been cited more than 300 times and is the default reference for "IFIT3 = MAVS–TBK1 scaffold."

For Huey's thesis this paper is structurally load-bearing in two senses. First, it explains why stabilized IFIT3 in DCAF7-loss MPN cells would translate into amplified IFN-α signaling even in the absence of external type I IFN, by positing a constitutively higher scaffolding stoichiometry at MAVS. Second, it supplies a concrete biochemical map — the E164/E165 TPR face, the K38 contact with TBK1 — that Huey can use to design separation-of-function mutants if a committee member asks whether IFIT3's amplifier role is genetically separable from its heterotrimer role. A committee question such as "you stabilize IFIT3; how do you know it's the MAVS scaffold function and not the IFIT1 heterotrimer function that drives the IFN-α hypersensitivity?" is answered by designing Liu-2011-informed IFIT3-E164A/E165A rescue experiments.

PMID: 21813773 · DOI: [10.4049/jimmunol.1100963](https://doi.org/10.4049/jimmunol.1100963) · Model: HEK293, HeLa + Sendai virus and VSV · Approach: Co-IP, reporter luciferase, siRNA knockdown, point-mutant rescue
keyFacts: Model: HEK293, HeLa + Sendai virus and VSV
Approach: Co-IP, reporter luciferase, siRNA knockdown, point-mutant rescue

subsections.title: Structural reinterpretation — Johnson et al. 2018, Immunity
subsections.body:
By 2018 it was established that IFIT1 recognized capped viral mRNA, that IFIT3 bound IFIT1, and that MAVS-based IFIT3 amplifier activity existed (Liu 2011, Chikhalya 2021 was in press). What was not known at atomic resolution was how the IFIT1–IFIT3 interaction worked, whether mouse and human IFIT1 orthologs behaved equivalently, and whether IFIT3 binding did anything to IFIT1's RNA specificity beyond stoichiometric stabilization. Johnson, VanBlargan, Xu, White, Shan, Shi, Zhang, Adhikari, Gross, Leung, Diamond, and Amarasinghe addressed all three questions with a single structure-guided paper in Immunity.

Their principal experimental achievement was a crystal structure of human IFIT1 in complex with a synthetic cap-0 (m7GpppN) RNA and the C-terminal domain of human IFIT3 (residues approximately 340–490). The authors used recombinant proteins expressed in E. coli, RNA binding assays by fluorescence polarization and electrophoretic mobility shift, hydrogen–deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS) to probe allosteric rearrangements, cycloheximide chase assays in 293T and A549 cells to measure IFIT1 half-life with and without IFIT3, and cell-based antiviral assays using West Nile virus (WNV) and Zika virus (ZIKV) mutants defective in 2′-O methyltransferase activity.

Three results define the paper. First, the crystal structure revealed that the IFIT3 C-terminal domain docks onto a surface of IFIT1 distal to the RNA-binding cavity, yet binding allosterically reshapes the cavity so that cap-0 RNA is engaged with higher specificity and cap-1 and 5′-ppp RNAs are disfavored. In the absence of IFIT3 the authors showed that IFIT1 accommodates multiple cap states promiscuously. Second, in cycloheximide chase assays IFIT1 half-life was markedly extended when co-expressed with IFIT3 (from a short half-life on the order of a few hours to substantially longer), suggesting a chaperone-like stabilization in addition to allosteric tuning. Third, and crucially for comparative biology, mouse Ifit3 was shown to lack the C-terminal domain that engages Ifit1 in human, and mouse Ifit1 does not require mouse Ifit3 for activity. This species divergence explains why the Ifit3-knockout mouse (which does exist and has been characterized, see Section C) cannot be straightforwardly mapped onto human IFIT3 biology.

The paper reshaped the IFIT field in three ways. It formalized the "IFIT3 as IFIT1 co-factor" model into a structural mechanism; it predicted (and confirmed) that mutations in the C-terminal IFIT3 surface would phenocopy IFIT3 loss for IFIT1-dependent restriction even while sparing the N-terminal MAVS-bridging surface identified by Liu 2011; and it cautioned the entire field against cross-species extrapolation of IFIT3 biology — a caveat that is now routinely cited.

For Huey's thesis this paper is indispensable. It is the clearest molecular statement that human IFIT3 is not just a passive heterotrimer component but an allosteric regulator of IFIT1 RNA selectivity and an in-cell stabilizer of IFIT1 protein. When she argues that DCAF7 loss increases IFIT3 steady-state levels in JAK2V617F MPN cells, Johnson 2018 provides the mechanistic bridge from "more IFIT3 protein" to "more IFIT1-dependent capped-RNA translation inhibition" and "more antiviral/antiproliferative output." The paper also supplies the canonical answer to a likely committee challenge: "Why should stabilizing IFIT3 matter if IFIT1 is the RNA-binding subunit?" Answer: because IFIT3 both stabilizes IFIT1 and sharpens its specificity, per Johnson. Finally, because mouse IFIT3 lacks the key human C-terminal region, Huey can use this paper to pre-empt any committee push to validate her axis in an Ifit3-knockout mouse without first doing a humanized or mouse-complemented-with-human-IFIT3 experiment.

PMID: 29525521 · DOI: [10.1016/j.immuni.2018.01.014](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.immuni.2018.01.014) · Model: Human recombinant IFIT1/IFIT3 + synthetic capped RNA; 293T, A549, WNV/ZIKV 2′-O MTase mutants · Approach: X-ray crystallography, HDX-MS, cycloheximide chase, virus infection assays
keyFacts: Model: Human recombinant IFIT1/IFIT3 + synthetic capped RNA; 293T, A549, WNV/ZIKV 2′-O MTase mutants
Approach: X-ray crystallography, HDX-MS, cycloheximide chase, virus infection assays

subsections.title: The original eIF3 inhibitor — Terenzi et al. 2006, Journal of Biological Chemistry
subsections.body:
A decade before the heterotrimer was reconstituted, the field already knew that human ISG56/IFIT1 and ISG54/IFIT2 bound the translation initiation machinery. Terenzi, Hui, Merrick, and Sen's 2006 paper in Journal of Biological Chemistry provided the first direct biochemical dissection of ISG54/IFIT2's interaction with eIF3 and set the conceptual precedent for IFIT-mediated translation inhibition that Fleith 2018 would later expand to the full heterotrimer.

Using HEK293 cells, induced overexpression systems, and cell-free translation extracts, the authors compared ISG54 (IFIT2) and ISG56 (IFIT1) induction kinetics after IFN-β, dsRNA (poly I:C), and Sendai virus infection. They performed co-immunoprecipitations between FLAG-tagged IFITs and endogenous eIF3 subunits, identifying subunit-level contacts by western blot. Functional translation assays used rabbit reticulocyte lysates reconstituted with recombinant ISG54 or ISG56 and a capped reporter mRNA; 48S pre-initiation complex formation was tracked by sucrose gradient fractionation.

The load-bearing results: ISG54/IFIT2 bound both the eIF3-e and eIF3-c subunits, whereas ISG56/IFIT1 contacted only eIF3-e. This dual contact allowed IFIT2 to inhibit eIF3 in two ways — blocking eIF2·GTP·Met-tRNAi ternary complex delivery and, additionally, blocking 48S pre-initiation complex formation between the 40S ribosome and the 20S eIF3/ternary/eIF4F/mRNA complex. IFIT1 inhibited only the former step. Kinetically, IFIT1 was induced rapidly and transiently by IFN-β, whereas IFIT2 induction was slower and more sustained, suggesting the two proteins were deployed in temporal succession during an antiviral response.

This paper was the first to establish molecularly distinct translation-inhibitory mechanisms for IFIT1 and IFIT2, and it is cited in essentially every subsequent review of the IFIT family. For Huey's thesis it is important mainly because it supplies the biochemical basis for the claim — made explicitly in Fleith 2018 — that the IFIT1/IFIT2/IFIT3 heterotrimer is a more potent translation inhibitor than any monomer. A committee member who asks "why does more IFIT3 mean more translational suppression of capped viral mRNA?" is asking for the chain Johnson 2018 → Terenzi 2006 → Fleith 2018, in which IFIT3 stabilizes IFIT1, IFIT1 recruits eIF3-e, and IFIT2 (tethered in the heterotrimer) adds eIF3-c engagement. Terenzi 2006 is the oldest link in that chain.

PMID: 16973618 · DOI: [10.1074/jbc.M605771200](https://doi.org/10.1074/jbc.M605771200) · Model: HEK293, rabbit reticulocyte lysate, Sendai virus, poly I:C · Approach: Co-IP of eIF3 subunits, sucrose gradient 48S assembly assay, cell-free translation

---
keyFacts: Model: HEK293, rabbit reticulocyte lysate, Sendai virus, poly I:C
Approach: Co-IP of eIF3 subunits, sucrose gradient 48S assembly assay, cell-free translation

sections.title: §1B — IFIT1 and IFIT5 RNA recognition: the "business end" of the heterotrimer

subsections.title: IFIT1 binds 5′-triphosphate RNA and the Ifit1 knockout mouse — Pichlmair et al. 2011, Nature Immunology
subsections.body:
This paper is the one Huey flagged explicitly as the "IFIT knockout mouse paper" her committee will expect her to know cold. In 2011 the question of how the cell distinguishes viral from self RNA was dominated by RIG-I, which recognizes 5′-triphosphorylated double-stranded RNA. Whether an ISG family — as opposed to a germline-encoded cytosolic sensor — could also directly bind PPP-RNA was unresolved. Pichlmair, Lassnig, Eberle, Górna, Baumann, Burkard, Bürckstümmer, Stefanovic, Krieger, Bennett, Rülicke, Weber, Colinge, Müller, and Superti-Furga answered both the biochemical and the in vivo questions in a single paper.

Experimentally, the authors used an affinity-proteomics approach: biotinylated synthetic PPP-RNA was used as bait in HEK293 and HeLa cell lysates, and captured proteins were identified by mass spectrometry. This pulldown cleanly identified IFIT1 as the principal direct PPP-RNA-binding protein, together with IFIT2, IFIT3, and IFIT5 as indirect complex members. They measured binding affinity by fluorescence polarization with recombinant IFIT1 and synthetic PPP-RNA, obtaining a Kd in the low-nanomolar range, and localized the binding determinant to a highly basic C-terminal groove with a critical arginine at position 187. Mutation of R187 (R187H) abolished PPP-RNA binding without disrupting protein folding. The in vivo arm — the reason this paper is on Huey's committee-question list — used a newly generated Ifit1-knockout mouse on the C57BL/6 background. They infected wild-type and Ifit1−/− mice with VSV and influenza A virus and measured survival, viral titers in target organs (lung, brain), and pathology. Ifit1−/− animals showed significantly elevated viral titers and pathology after infection with PPP-RNA-bearing viruses, whereas pathogens that did not generate PPP-RNA were equivalently controlled in wild-type and knockout mice.

Three headline results: (i) IFIT1 recognizes PPP-RNA directly with nanomolar affinity; (ii) the R187 residue of IFIT1's C-terminal groove is essential for this recognition; (iii) in an Ifit1−/− mouse, PPP-RNA-generating viruses (VSV, influenza) replicate to substantially higher titers and cause more severe pathology, whereas non-PPP-RNA pathogens are controlled normally. The authors proposed a "sequestration" model: IFIT1 plus its IFIT-complex partners physically holds viral PPP-RNA away from translation and replication machineries. This model was later refined to include ribose-methylation discrimination (Daffis 2010 Nature, Abbas 2013, Abbas 2017).

The paper's impact is hard to overstate. It was the first demonstration that an ISG could function as a de facto pattern-recognition receptor with its own RNA specificity, and it was the first published Ifit1-knockout mouse. Every subsequent in vivo IFIT paper — Fensterl 2012, Butchi 2013, Daffis 2010, Johnson 2018 — uses reagents and frameworks that descend from this one.

For Huey's thesis defense this is the load-bearing paper for two committee questions. First, "Do you have an Ifit3-knockout mouse and what is its phenotype?" — her honest answer is that the published in vivo IFIT loss-of-function data are for Ifit1 (Pichlmair 2011) and Ifit2 (Fensterl 2012, Butchi 2013), and that Johnson 2018 demonstrated that mouse Ifit3 lacks the C-terminal IFIT1-interacting domain, so an Ifit3-knockout mouse is not a direct model of human IFIT3 biology. Second, "If you stabilize IFIT3 and the heterotrimer, what is the RNA specificity you expect to amplify?" — the Pichlmair answer is PPP-RNA, refined by Daffis 2010 and Abbas 2017 to include cap-0 mRNA, and sharpened by Johnson 2018 to cap-0 specifically.

PMID: 21642987 · DOI: [10.1038/ni.2048](https://doi.org/10.1038/ni.2048) · Model: HEK293, HeLa, Ifit1−/− C57BL/6 mice, VSV, influenza A · Approach: PPP-RNA affinity proteomics, fluorescence polarization Kd, R187 point mutagenesis, in vivo infection with titers and survival
keyFacts: Model: HEK293, HeLa, *Ifit1*−/− C57BL/6 mice, VSV, influenza A
Approach: PPP-RNA affinity proteomics, fluorescence polarization Kd, R187 point mutagenesis, in vivo infection with titers and survival

subsections.title: 2′-O methylation as host-self discrimination — Daffis et al. 2010, Nature
subsections.body:
The host RNA discrimination problem — how IFIT1/5 distinguish viral from cellular capped mRNA — was solved by a collaboration between the Diamond, Shi, and Sen laboratories in the same year Pichlmair published. Before 2010, 2′-O methylation of the cap-proximal nucleotide (cap-1, mGpppNm) was known to exist on eukaryotic mRNA but its functional role was obscure, having been discovered 35 years earlier without a clear selection pressure. Daffis, Szretter, Schriewer, Li, Youn, Errett, Lin, Schneller, Zust, Dong, Thiel, Sen, Fensterl, Klimstra, Pierson, Buller, Gale, Shi, and Diamond reframed 2′-O methylation as the chemical signature that marks self-RNA to evade IFIT-mediated restriction.

The authors used a West Nile virus (WNV) E218A mutant, which ablates the viral 2′-O methyltransferase activity and produces cap-0 (rather than cap-1) viral mRNA. They infected wild-type and Ifit1−/− mice (C57BL/6), measured survival, viral loads in brain and periphery, and performed parallel experiments with coronavirus (mouse hepatitis virus) and poxvirus (vaccinia) mutants lacking 2′-O MTase. They also infected 3T3 fibroblasts and primary MEFs from wild-type and Ifit knockout mice to separate intrinsic cell-autonomous restriction from systemic IFN induction.

The central result: WNV-E218A (cap-0) was attenuated in wild-type mice but pathogenic in Ifit1−/− and in type I IFN receptor-deficient (Ifnar1−/−) animals, demonstrating that IFIT1 is the principal restriction factor for cap-0 RNA and that the attenuation phenotype is IFN-dependent. Parallel results held for poxvirus and coronavirus 2′-O MTase mutants.
