The authors argued, and the field accepted, that 2′-O methylation evolved as a viral immune-evasion strategy and that eukaryotic 2′-O methylation of cellular mRNA is the "self-tag" that allows IFIT proteins to spare host mRNA.

The paper's influence was transformative: it re-positioned the IFIT family as the enforcers of a self/non-self discrimination rule at the translation level, complementing RIG-I at the replication level. The conceptual pair — RIG-I for PPP-RNA sensing, IFIT1 for cap-0 sequestration — has been the textbook framing ever since.

For Huey's thesis the Daffis paper is load-bearing in explaining why IFIT3 stabilization in MPN cells would translate to antiviral and antiproliferative effects. The mechanistic claim is that the IFIT1/IFIT2/IFIT3 heterotrimer with stabilized IFIT3 has greater access to cap-0 RNA pools, meaning that any hypomethylated (nucleolar or stress-generated) RNA species in the MPN clone could become translation-suppressed. The Daffis paper also grounds the argument that IFN-α therapy in MPN — which upregulates the entire IFIT family — leverages a specificity logic that pre-exists in host cells.

PMID: 21085181 · DOI: [10.1038/nature09489](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature09489) · Model: Ifit1−/− C57BL/6 mice, WNV E218A (2′-O MTase-null), poxvirus and coronavirus MTase mutants, 3T3 cells · Approach: Recombinant virus mutants, survival curves, tissue titers, IFN pathway genetic epistasis
keyFacts: Model: *Ifit1*−/− C57BL/6 mice, WNV E218A (2′-O MTase-null), poxvirus and coronavirus MTase mutants, 3T3 cells
Approach: Recombinant virus mutants, survival curves, tissue titers, IFN pathway genetic epistasis

subsections.title: IFIT5 crystal with PPP-RNA — Abbas et al. 2013, Nature
subsections.body:
Pichlmair 2011 established IFIT1 as the PPP-RNA receptor biochemically, but the structural basis of the recognition — how a tetratricopeptide-repeat scaffold, normally a protein–protein interaction module, could encode sequence-agnostic RNA binding — was unknown. Abbas, Pichlmair, Górna, Superti-Furga, and Nagar produced the answer by crystallizing human IFIT5 (a paralog that also binds PPP-RNA but does not obligately heterotrimerize with IFIT1/3) and its complex with synthetic PPP-RNAs, together with the N-terminal half of IFIT1 for comparison.

They solved crystal structures of apo IFIT5, IFIT5 bound to 5′-PPP-RNAs of varying length, and the IFIT1 N-terminus at resolutions near atomic range. Complementary assays included gel-shift RNA binding, limited proteolysis to probe conformational dynamics, and mutagenesis of cavity residues in IFIT5 and IFIT1 followed by viral infection assays in HEK293 cells.

The defining structural discovery was a new protein fold topology — a helical "clamshell" composed of nine TPR-like helical hairpins arranged around a deep, positively charged central cavity that engulfs single-stranded PPP-RNA. The triphosphate was coordinated by basic residues lining the cavity (lysines and arginines), and the cavity was too narrow to accommodate double-stranded RNA, explaining why IFIT1 and IFIT5 do not compete with RIG-I for dsRNA. A conserved PPP-binding pocket required a 5′-overhang of approximately three nucleotides for productive loading. Mutation of cavity residues abolished PPP-RNA binding in vitro and abrogated antiviral activity in cells.

The paper re-oriented the field by supplying the structural grammar for IFIT–RNA recognition. It explained why IFIT1, IFIT5, and (by homology) IFIT3 all share a TPR scaffold but divergent cavity chemistry, and it predicted — correctly, as Johnson 2018 later showed — that cap recognition would require a reorganized cavity different from the PPP cavity.

For Huey's thesis the Abbas paper is the structural anchor for any committee question about which RNAs the heterotrimer actually binds. If asked "does a stabilized IFIT3 increase binding to PPP-RNA or cap-0 RNA?" the answer, integrating Abbas 2013 (PPP cavity), Abbas 2017 (cap-0 cavity), and Johnson 2018 (cap-0 allosterically enforced by IFIT3), is that IFIT3 stabilization biases the heterotrimer toward cap-0 restriction rather than toward PPP-RNA.

PMID: 23334420 · DOI: [10.1038/nature11783](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature11783) · Model: Recombinant human IFIT5 and IFIT1 N-terminus; HEK293 cells · Approach: X-ray crystallography (apo and PPP-RNA-bound), EMSA, limited proteolysis, cavity-residue mutagenesis
keyFacts: Model: Recombinant human IFIT5 and IFIT1 N-terminus; HEK293 cells
Approach: X-ray crystallography (apo and PPP-RNA-bound), EMSA, limited proteolysis, cavity-residue mutagenesis

subsections.title: Human IFIT1 with cap-0 RNA — Abbas et al. 2017, PNAS
subsections.body:
The capstone structural paper in the Abbas/Nagar series described the co-crystal of human IFIT1 with cap-0 capped RNA at 1.6 Å resolution. The work integrated cap recognition with the translation-inhibition model: IFIT1 directly competes with eIF4E for cap binding but accommodates the m7G cap in multiple geometries within a water-filled, positively charged tunnel whose hydrophobic extension plays an unexpected role in cap stacking.

The authors used recombinant IFIT1 plus chemically synthesized cap-0 (m7GpppN-RNA), cap-1 (m7GpppNm-RNA), and cap-2 (m7GpppNmNm-RNA) analogs from the Damha group. Structural determination was by X-ray crystallography; functional tests used EMSA for binding, in vitro translation assays in rabbit reticulocyte lysate, and infection of 293T cells with coronavirus 2′-O MTase mutants.

The principal findings: the cap-0 guanosine is engaged in multiple conformations within a hydrophobic lobe offset from the main RNA tunnel, explaining why IFIT1 binding is relatively plastic; N1 (first cap-proximal nucleotide) 2′-O methylation sterically clashes with the tunnel floor, explaining cap-1 exclusion; N2 2′-O methylation also reduces binding, suggesting synergistic self-tagging by successive ribose methylations; structure-guided point mutants in the cap pocket abrogated coronavirus restriction in cells.

For Huey's thesis this is the mechanistic sharpening of the Daffis 2010 model. It is the paper that answers "at atomic resolution, why does the heterotrimer discriminate cap-0 from cap-1?" Committee-facing, it also allows Huey to discuss the fact that certain cellular RNAs — a subset of stress-induced transcripts, some non-coding RNAs, and possibly aberrantly capped transcripts in malignant cells — may lack proper 2′-O methylation and could thus become substrates for a stabilized IFIT heterotrimer in DCAF7-loss MPN cells.

PMID: 28251928 · DOI: [10.1073/pnas.1612444114](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1612444114) · Model: Recombinant human IFIT1 + synthetic cap-0/cap-1/cap-2 RNAs; 293T + coronavirus 2′-O MTase mutants · Approach: 1.6 Å X-ray crystallography, EMSA, rabbit reticulocyte translation assays, mutagenesis
keyFacts: Model: Recombinant human IFIT1 + synthetic cap-0/cap-1/cap-2 RNAs; 293T + coronavirus 2′-O MTase mutants
Approach: 1.6 Å X-ray crystallography, EMSA, rabbit reticulocyte translation assays, mutagenesis

subsections.title: IFIT5 as a broad cellular RNA binder — Katibah et al. 2014, PNAS
subsections.body:
Most of the IFIT structural literature focused on viral PPP-RNA or viral cap-0 RNA as ligands. Katibah, Qin, Sidote, Yao, Lambowitz, and Collins asked the orthogonal question: if IFIT5 in a resting or modestly IFN-stimulated cell is abundant, what cellular RNAs does it actually bind? The answer reframed IFIT5 as having substantial non-antiviral, housekeeping-level RNA contacts, with implications for how the entire IFIT family operates at baseline.

The authors used recombinant human IFIT5 expressed in E. coli, systematic mutagenesis to alter the 5′-end specificity (5′-monophosphate, 5′-triphosphate, cap-0, cap-1), and — most importantly — thermostable group II intron reverse transcriptase (TGIRT)-based RNA sequencing to identify the endogenous IFIT5-bound transcriptome in HEK293 cells. Binding affinities were measured by fluorescence anisotropy.

The key findings: human IFIT5 binds RNAs with diverse 5′-phosphate states (monophosphate, triphosphate, cap-0) with comparable affinity, excluding only the higher-eukaryote cap-1 with methylated ribose. Systematic active-site mutagenesis revealed that single-residue substitutions could shift or broaden 5′-end specificity. In-cell TGIRT-seq of IFIT5 pulldowns revealed binding to precursor and processed tRNAs and to other RNA polymerase III transcripts — unexpected non-viral ligands with their own 5′-PPP (from pol III transcription). This positioned IFIT5 as a broader RNA-binding protein whose antiviral role is one of several activities.

For Huey's thesis this paper is a hedge against a committee question along the lines of "if you stabilize IFIT3 and by extension the whole IFIT complex, might you not also inadvertently alter processing of pol III transcripts or tRNA populations in hematopoietic cells?" The Katibah data make this a reasonable concern to address, and they suggest secondary readouts (tRNA fragment profiles, pol III target transcripts) that Huey could deploy in HSPC experiments. It is also the clearest evidence that IFITs are not strictly "antiviral-only" and that their functional scope in stress-state hematopoietic cells may be broader than the heterotrimer-inhibits-translation dogma.

PMID: 25092312 · DOI: [10.1073/pnas.1412842111](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1412842111) · Model: Recombinant human IFIT5, HEK293 pulldowns · Approach: Fluorescence anisotropy binding, active-site mutagenesis, TGIRT-seq of IFIT5-bound RNAs

---
keyFacts: Model: Recombinant human IFIT5, HEK293 pulldowns
Approach: Fluorescence anisotropy binding, active-site mutagenesis, TGIRT-seq of IFIT5-bound RNAs

sections.title: §1C — The IFIT family in vivo: knockout mouse papers

subsections.title: Ifit2−/− mice and lethal VSV neuropathogenesis — Fensterl et al. 2012, PLoS Pathogens
subsections.body:
While Pichlmair 2011 supplied the first Ifit1−/− mouse, the Fensterl and Sen lab at the Cleveland Clinic produced the first Ifit2−/− mouse and used it to ask an orthogonal question: which IFIT paralog restricts which virus, in which tissue? Fensterl, Wetzel, Ramachandran, Ogino, Stohlman, Bergmann, Diamond, Virgin, and Sen's 2012 PLoS Pathogens paper is the canonical demonstration that tissue- and virus-specific ISG functions are real and not inferred.

They generated Ifit2−/− and Ifit1−/− mice on the C57BL/6 background and challenged them with vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV) via either intranasal or intracranial routes at defined doses. They measured survival curves, viral titers in olfactory bulb, cortex, brainstem, lung, and liver at defined time points, performed in situ hybridization and immunohistochemistry to define cellular tropism in the central nervous system, and compared pathogenesis with encephalomyocarditis virus (EMCV), another neurotropic RNA virus.

The three load-bearing findings: (i) intranasal VSV was uniformly lethal in Ifit2−/− mice with preceding neurological signs (ataxia, hind-limb paralysis), whereas wild-type and Ifit1−/− mice were largely protected; (ii) intracranial injection eliminated the mortality difference, indicating that Ifit2 acts at an entry or early-spread step in the olfactory route rather than at replication in neurons per se; (iii) VSV titers were several hundred-fold elevated in the brain of Ifit2−/− mice compared with wild-type, but not in lung or liver, and the tropism of VSV remained restricted to neurons in both genotypes — i.e. Ifit2 restricts viral spread within the neuronal compartment of the CNS but is dispensable in peripheral organs. Importantly, EMCV, another neurotropic RNA virus, was not enhanced in Ifit2−/− brains, showing virus-specific as well as tissue-specific selectivity.

The paper set the template for ISG-specific, tissue-specific, virus-specific antiviral action and is frequently cited to justify the claim that ISG loss-of-function phenotypes are context-dependent rather than globally antiviral.

For Huey's thesis it is important on two fronts. First, it is a cautionary note on inferring IFIT3 phenotypes from IFIT2 phenotypes: Ifit2 is pro-apoptotic (Stawowczyk 2011) and pro-IFN-β induction in macrophages (Butchi 2013); IFIT3 in the human heterotrimer has a distinct role. Second, it justifies Huey's decision to use JAK2V617F MPN-specific mouse models (cross-conditional Dcaf7 deletion in hematopoietic cells) rather than whole-body Ifit3 knockouts, because the Fensterl data show that IFIT biology is tissue-specific.

PMID: 22615570 · DOI: [10.1371/journal.ppat.1002712](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.ppat.1002712) · Model: Ifit2−/−, Ifit1−/− C57BL/6 mice + intranasal/intracranial VSV and EMCV · Approach: Survival curves, tissue-specific viral titers, IHC/ISH cellular tropism
keyFacts: PMID 22615570 · DOI 10.1371/journal.ppat.1002712
Model: *Ifit2*−/−, *Ifit1*−/− C57BL/6 mice + intranasal/intracranial VSV and EMCV
Approach: Survival curves, tissue-specific viral titers, IHC/ISH cellular tropism

subsections.title: Ifit2−/− mice and coronavirus encephalitis via impaired macrophage IFN-α/β — Butchi et al. 2013, Journal of Virology
subsections.body:
This follow-up from the Fensterl/Sen and Bergmann labs extended the Ifit2−/− phenotype to a second neurotropic virus (mouse hepatitis virus, MHV-A59) and — importantly — redefined Ifit2 as a positive regulator of IFN-α/β induction rather than a purely direct antiviral effector in the CNS. Butchi, Hinton, Stohlman, Kapil, Fensterl, Sen, and Bergmann infected Ifit2−/− and wild-type mice with MHV-A59 and measured survival, viral loads, CNS histopathology, cytokine mRNAs in whole brain and FACS-sorted microglia and macrophages, and ex vivo IFN-α/β mRNA and protein production in bone-marrow-derived macrophages.

Approximately 60% of Ifit2−/− mice succumbed between 6 and 8 days post-infection with severe encephalitis, versus 100% survival in wild-type controls. Brain viral titers were higher in Ifit2−/− animals, but the surprising finding was that IFN-α/β mRNA and the downstream ISGs Ifit1, Isg15, and Pkr were markedly reduced in the Ifit2−/− CNS, while pro-inflammatory cytokine induction was only modestly affected. Ex vivo bone-marrow-derived macrophages from Ifit2−/− mice showed significantly impaired IFN-α/β induction after MHV infection, establishing Ifit2 as an upstream amplifier of the type I IFN response in macrophages rather than a direct intracellular antiviral effector.

This paper is a critical piece of evidence that IFIT proteins can function as regulators of the IFN signaling axis, echoing the IFIT3-MAVS-bridging claim of Liu 2011 but in a different paralog, tissue, and virus. For Huey's thesis it reinforces her central mechanistic claim — that an individual IFIT (IFIT3 in her case) can be an IFN amplifier — by demonstrating that Ifit2 behaves analogously in vivo in a different experimental system. It is also useful when discussing why IFIT3 stabilization in MPN hematopoietic cells might amplify endogenous IFN tone and therefore hypersensitize the clone to exogenous IFN-α therapy.

PMID: 24198415 · DOI: [10.1128/JVI.02272-13](https://doi.org/10.1128/JVI.02272-13) · Model: Ifit2−/− C57BL/6 mice + MHV-A59 coronavirus; BMDMs ex vivo · Approach: Survival, brain viral titers, FACS-sorted microglia/macrophage cytokine mRNA, ex vivo IFN-α/β measurement
keyFacts: Model: *Ifit2*−/− C57BL/6 mice + MHV-A59 coronavirus; BMDMs ex vivo
Approach: Survival, brain viral titers, FACS-sorted microglia/macrophage cytokine mRNA, ex vivo IFN-α/β measurement

subsections.title: The canonical IFIT review — Fensterl & Sen 2015, Journal of Virology
subsections.body:
Fensterl and Sen's 2015 Journal of Virology review consolidates roughly two decades of IFIT literature and is the single reference committee members will most likely have in mind when asking about the IFIT field. It covers induction kinetics, TPR architecture, PPP-RNA and cap recognition, the Ifit1/Ifit2/Ifit3 knockout mouse phenotypes, and viral antagonism of IFITs (setting up the vaccinia C9 paper of Liu 2019).

Huey should treat this review as the "framing document" for any committee exchange about IFIT biology generally, and she should cite it when she needs a single authoritative source for claims like "human IFITs but not all mouse Ifits form heterocomplexes" or "IFIT activity is virus- and tissue-specific." It is not a primary source and does not require its own deep experimental writeup beyond this note, but omitting it would signal unfamiliarity with the standard literature.

PMID: 25428874 · DOI: [10.1128/JVI.02744-14](https://doi.org/10.1128/JVI.02744-14) · Model: Review · Approach: Narrative synthesis of human and mouse IFIT1/IFIT2/IFIT3 biology

---
keyFacts: Model: Review
Approach: Narrative synthesis of human and mouse IFIT1/IFIT2/IFIT3 biology

sections.title: §1D — IFITs in disease: hematology, apoptosis, pyroptosis, and viral antagonism

subsections.title: RIG-G / IFIT3 forces G1/S arrest via p21 and p27 in APL — Xiao et al. 2006, PNAS
subsections.body:
This paper is arguably the oldest and most directly hematologic IFIT3 paper in the literature, and because it was published before the current "IFIT3 = antiviral TPR effector" framing solidified, it is routinely overlooked in contemporary reviews. Huey must know it because her committee, with hematology and IFN-α expertise (Platanias, Saleiro), will expect a precise account of what IFIT3 does in a leukemic cell.

Xiao, Li, Zhu, Song, Pan, Jia, Peng, Dou, Chen, Chen, Chen, and Tong used the NB4 acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) cell line, which undergoes differentiation in response to all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA). They previously cloned RIG-G (retinoic acid-induced gene G) from ATRA-treated NB4 cells and showed it to be the human homolog now known as IFIT3/ISG60. In this 2006 paper they moved RIG-G/IFIT3 into U937 myeloid cells by ectopic expression and asked what it does to the cell cycle. Readouts were flow-cytometric cell-cycle analysis with BrdU incorporation, western blots of cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitors p21 and p27, co-immunoprecipitation with JAB1 (a p27 degradation cofactor), and transcriptional analysis of c-myc. STAT1 dependence of RIG-G induction was confirmed by promoter analysis.

The key findings: ectopic RIG-G/IFIT3 in U937 cells produced significant G1/S accumulation; it elevated p27 levels by binding JAB1 and preventing JAB1-dependent, ubiquitin-proteasome-mediated p27 degradation; it down-regulated c-myc, which in turn up-regulated p21; and the entire program depended on STAT1 as the direct transcription factor driving RIG-G induction. The authors framed RIG-G/IFIT3 as one of the first experimentally proven molecular mediators of the antiproliferative effect of IFN-α and as a node of synergy between ATRA and IFN-α signaling.

For Huey's thesis this paper is extraordinarily load-bearing. It provides a cell-intrinsic, hematology-specific mechanism by which elevated IFIT3 produces cytostasis — precisely the phenotype she wants to explain in DCAF7-loss JAK2V617F MPN cells. When she argues that DCAF7 loss stabilizes IFIT3 and reduces disease burden, a committee member can ask "how does extra IFIT3 kill or arrest an MPN clone?" and her cleanest mechanistic answer is the Xiao 2006 axis: IFIT3 → JAB1 sequestration → p27 stabilization → G1/S arrest, combined with c-myc → p21. This is a paper she should be able to narrate in detail.

PMID: 17050680 · DOI: [10.1073/pnas.0607830103](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.0607830103) · Model: NB4 APL cells, U937 myeloid cells, STAT1-dependent induction · Approach: Ectopic expression, BrdU cell cycle, co-IP with JAB1, p27/p21/c-myc westerns
keyFacts: Model: NB4 APL cells, U937 myeloid cells, STAT1-dependent induction
Approach: Ectopic expression, BrdU cell cycle, co-IP with JAB1, p27/p21/c-myc westerns

subsections.title: IFIT3 knockdown sensitizes dengue-infected lung epithelium to apoptosis — Hsu et al. 2013, PLoS One
subsections.body:
Huey flagged this paper as explicitly missing from her prior draft. Hsu, Shi, Wu, Ho, and Lai asked what IFIT3 actually does during dengue virus (DV) infection of human lung epithelium — a tissue with known dengue replication and a relevant clinical site for severe disease. Their data anticipate the modern understanding that IFIT3 has context-dependent pro- and anti-apoptotic functions.

The experimental system was the A549 human lung adenocarcinoma line plus primary human dendritic cells, infected with a clinical DV serotype. They used two independent siRNAs targeting IFIT3 with knockdown confirmed by qRT-PCR and western blot, and they probed the regulatory transcription factor by parallel siRNA knockdowns of STAT1, STAT2, and STAT3. Cell death was quantified by TUNEL, annexin V/propidium iodide flow cytometry, and cleavage of caspase-3, caspase-8, and caspase-9 by western blot. BAX expression was also measured. DV replication was tracked by qRT-PCR of viral RNA and plaque assay titers.

The key results were fourfold. First, DV infection induced IFIT1, IFIT2, IFIT3, and IFIT5 in A549 cells; induction of IFIT3 was STAT2-dependent but STAT1- and STAT3-independent, an unusual regulatory signature. Second, knockdown of IFIT3 elevated DV-induced apoptosis (increased TUNEL, annexin V, caspase-3/8/9 cleavage, BAX upregulation). Third, this pro-apoptotic response was synergistically amplified by DV infection plus IFIT3 depletion versus either alone. Fourth, IFIT3 knockdown also increased viral titers, demonstrating direct antiviral activity of IFIT3 in this system. The authors proposed that IFIT3 has a dual role: it restrains caspase cascades (an anti-apoptotic, pro-cell-survival function) while simultaneously suppressing viral replication.

For Huey's thesis this is directly relevant because it establishes a paradigm in which IFIT3 is anti-apoptotic rather than pro-apoptotic. JAK2V617F MPN cells have pro-survival signaling from constitutive JAK–STAT activation, and her model posits that DCAF7 loss → IFIT3 stabilization → amplified IFN and antiproliferative activity would work against the MPN clone. Hsu 2013 provides a potential confounder: in DV-infected lung epithelium, IFIT3 loss is pro-apoptotic. A committee member could ask "isn't more IFIT3 in your MPN cells a pro-survival signal based on Hsu?" Huey's answer must integrate Xiao 2006 (IFIT3 is anti-proliferative via p21/p27 in myeloid cells), Johnson 2018 (IFIT3 stabilizes IFIT1 and enforces cap-0 translation block), and Chikhalya 2021 plus Liu 2011 (IFIT3 amplifies IFN). The paradigm-resolving argument is that IFIT3's role is cell-type and context-dependent, and in IFN-α-hyperresponsive JAK2V617F MPN cells the net output is antiproliferative.

PMID: 24223959 · DOI: [10.1371/journal.pone.0079518](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0079518) · Model: A549 lung adenocarcinoma, primary human dendritic cells, dengue virus · Approach: Two independent IFIT3 siRNAs, STAT1/2/3 knockdown epistasis, TUNEL, annexin V/PI, caspase-3/8/9 western blots, plaque assay
keyFacts: PMID 24223959 · DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0079518
Model: A549 lung adenocarcinoma, primary human dendritic cells, dengue virus
Approach: Two independent IFIT3 siRNAs, STAT1/2/3 knockdown epistasis, TUNEL, annexin V/PI, caspase-3/8/9 western blots, plaque assay

subsections.title: IFIT2 is pro-apoptotic and restrained by IFIT3 — Stawowczyk et al. 2011, Journal of Biological Chemistry
subsections.body:
Stawowczyk, Van Scoy, Kumar, and Reich's 2011 JBC paper did two things: it provided the clearest evidence that IFIT2/ISG54 is directly pro-apoptotic, and it identified IFIT3/ISG60 as a negative regulator of IFIT2's apoptotic activity — a finding that remains underappreciated but is mechanistically central to any discussion of IFIT3 function in hematologic disease.

The authors used HEK293 and HeLa cells with tetracycline-inducible or transfected IFIT2 expression, independent of IFN stimulation, and measured cell death by propidium iodide uptake and annexin V staining, with caspase-3 activation confirmed by western blot. Epistasis was tested with Bcl-xL co-expression (anti-apoptotic) and adenoviral E1B-19K (pan-Bcl-2 family inhibitor), and with BAX/BAK double-knockout MEFs (which are resistant to mitochondrial apoptosis). Binding partners of IFIT2 were identified by co-IP and mass spectrometry, confirming IFIT1/ISG56 and IFIT3/ISG60 as interactors.

The load-bearing results: IFIT2 expression alone triggered apoptosis with caspase-3 cleavage; this was blocked by Bcl-xL, by E1B-19K, and was absent in Bax−/−/Bak−/− MEFs, placing IFIT2's apoptotic activity squarely on the mitochondrial/intrinsic pathway. Co-expression of IFIT3, but not IFIT1, suppressed IFIT2-induced apoptosis. This positions IFIT3 not as a passive scaffold but as an active gatekeeper on IFIT2's death-inducing function.

For Huey's thesis this paper is critical because it sharpens the question of what a higher IFIT3:IFIT2 ratio actually does in a cell. If DCAF7 loss specifically stabilizes IFIT3 without a proportionate increase in IFIT2, the net effect could be more restraint of IFIT2 apoptosis, not less — a pro-survival consequence that would work against Huey's disease-burden-reduction claim. She must be prepared to answer, with stoichiometric data from her own system, whether IFIT2 is co-stabilized or whether IFIT3 is selectively stabilized. This is a classic committee trap and Stawowczyk 2011 is the paper that sets it up.

PMID: 21190939 · DOI: [10.1074/jbc.M110.207068](https://doi.org/10.1074/jbc.M110.207068) · Model: HEK293, HeLa, Bax−/−/Bak−/− MEFs, IFIT2-tet-inducible · Approach: PI and annexin V flow, caspase-3 westerns, Bcl-xL/E1B-19K epistasis, IFIT1/IFIT3 co-IP
keyFacts: Model: HEK293, HeLa, *Bax*−/−/*Bak*−/− MEFs, IFIT2-tet-inducible
Approach: PI and annexin V flow, caspase-3 westerns, Bcl-xL/E1B-19K epistasis, IFIT1/IFIT3 co-IP

subsections.title: Vaccinia C9 ankyrin-repeat/F-box protein targets IFIT1/2/3 for proteasomal degradation — Liu et al. 2019, Cell Reports
subsections.body:
This paper is a direct viral precedent for what Huey claims DCAF7 does in MPN cells: a host-E3-ubiquitin-ligase subunit-like protein hijacks the UPS to degrade the IFIT complex. Liu, Olano, Mirzakhanyan, Gershon, and Moss, from Bernard Moss's lab at NIAID, showed that vaccinia virus C9 is the protein required for vaccinia resistance to the human type I IFN response, and they dissected C9 into two domains: an N-terminal ankyrin-repeat region that binds IFITs and a C-terminal F-box that recruits SKP1-cullin-1 SCF complexes for ubiquitination.

Their system was human A549 and HEK293 cells infected with wild-type or C9-deletion vaccinia virus, with and without type I IFN pre-treatment. They used tandem affinity purification mass spectrometry to identify C9 interactors (recovering IFIT1, IFIT2, IFIT3, and SKP1, cullin-1, RBX1), co-IP and reciprocal pulldowns to validate binding, cycloheximide chase and MG132 proteasome inhibition to demonstrate IFIT degradation kinetics, and rescue of a C9-deletion virus in IFIT-knockout cells to establish genetic epistasis.

The core findings were three. First, C9 bridges IFITs to the SCF E3 machinery via its N-terminal ankyrin repeats (to IFIT1/2/3) and its C-terminal F-box (to SKP1). Second, proteasome inhibition stabilized IFIT1/2/3 in C9-expressing cells, and the C9-deletion vaccinia virus was restricted by IFITs at a post-early-gene-expression step (genome uncoating/replication) — a restriction that was fully relieved in A549 cells with IFIT1/2/3 triple knockout. Third, ectopic expression of C9 rescued the IFN sensitivity of a vaccinia mutant lacking cap-1 2′-O methylation, which otherwise could not express early proteins — confirming that C9 replaces the host-evasion function of viral 2′-O methylation by physically eliminating the IFIT restriction factors.

This is the paper that lets Huey tell her committee: "There is a published, molecularly characterized precedent for an F-box/SCF-type complex ubiquitinating IFIT1/2/3, and my thesis extends this logic to a physiological CRL4-DCAF7 in untransformed and JAK2V617F cells." A committee member who asks "is it plausible that a cullin-RING ligase targets IFIT3 for degradation?" is answered directly by Liu 2019 at the mechanistic level and by Li 2023 (UBE2O K236 in HCC, already in Tier 2) at the cancer-cell level.

PMID: 31644906 · DOI: [10.1016/j.celrep.2019.09.039](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.celrep.2019.09.039) · Model: A549, HEK293 + wild-type and C9-deletion vaccinia virus; IFIT-triple-knockout cells · Approach: AP-MS for interactors, cycloheximide chase, MG132 rescue, N-terminal/C-terminal C9 domain mutagenesis, genetic rescue in IFIT-KO cells
keyFacts: Model: A549, HEK293 + wild-type and C9-deletion vaccinia virus; IFIT-triple-knockout cells
Approach: AP-MS for interactors, cycloheximide chase, MG132 rescue, N-terminal/C-terminal C9 domain mutagenesis, genetic rescue in IFIT-KO cells

subsections.title: IFIT1/IFIT3 drive GSDME-dependent pyroptosis in myeloma and leukemia — He et al. 2024, Cancer Letters
subsections.body:
He, Jiang, Cui, Liang, Zhong, Sun, Moran, Huang, He, and Mao's 2024 Cancer Letters paper adds a new functional dimension for IFIT1 and IFIT3 in hematologic malignancy: they are not just cell-cycle inhibitors and antiviral effectors but also mediators of pyroptotic death via the gasdermin pathway.

The authors screened a small drug library for pyroptosis-inducing compounds in multiple myeloma and leukemia cell lines and identified clioquinol (CLQ), an anti-parasitic agent, as a potent pyroptotic inducer. RNA-seq of CLQ-treated cells identified IFIT1 and IFIT3 as among the most upregulated ISGs. Using siRNA/shRNA knockdown of IFIT1 or IFIT3, they demonstrated that both were required for CLQ-induced cleavage of gasdermin E (GSDME) to generate the pyroptotic N-terminal fragment (N-GSDME) and for subsequent pyroptotic cell death. Mechanistically, co-IP experiments showed that IFIT1 and IFIT3 formed a complex with BAX and N-GSDME and directed N-GSDME localization to mitochondria, increasing mitochondrial membrane permeabilization. Combination treatment with venetoclax (a Bcl-2 inhibitor that activates BAX) synergized strongly with CLQ to kill myeloma and leukemia cells through the IFIT1/IFIT3–GSDME axis.

For Huey's thesis this paper is valuable because it demonstrates a pro-death function of IFIT1/IFIT3 in hematologic malignancy that can be leveraged therapeutically. Her central MPN claim is that stabilized IFIT3 hypersensitizes JAK2V617F clones to IFN-α. He 2024 offers a parallel — and potentially synergistic — axis: stabilized IFIT3 may also be priming MPN cells for pyroptosis in response to BH3-mimetic or IFN combination therapy. A committee member asking about translational implications can be answered with direct reference to He 2024 and the possibility of combining IFN-α with venetoclax in MPN once IFIT3 is stabilized.

PMID: 38462032 · DOI: [10.1016/j.canlet.2024.216797](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.canlet.2024.216797) · Model: Multiple myeloma and leukemia cell lines + clioquinol and venetoclax · Approach: Drug screen, RNA-seq, IFIT1/IFIT3 knockdown, GSDME cleavage westerns, BAX/N-GSDME co-IP, mitochondrial membrane permeabilization

---
keyFacts: Model: Multiple myeloma and leukemia cell lines + clioquinol and venetoclax
Approach: Drug screen, RNA-seq, IFIT1/IFIT3 knockdown, GSDME cleavage westerns, BAX/N-GSDME co-IP, mitochondrial membrane permeabilization

subsections.title: DCAF7, CRL4, and the E3 Ligase Landscape: Deep Gap Analysis: scope & rationale
subsections.body:
The central claim of this thesis — that DCAF7 functions as a substrate receptor within a CRL4^DCAF7 complex that ubiquitylates IFIT3 and thereby tunes the amplitude of type-I interferon signaling in JAK2V617F myeloproliferative neoplasms — sits at the intersection of three literatures that Tiers 1–3 only partially covered. The first is the architectural biology of Cullin-4 RING ligases: how DDB1 recruits WD40-repeat DCAFs as interchangeable substrate adaptors, how neddylation activates the complex, and how substrate receptors are exchanged by CAND1. The second is the biology of DCAF7 (a.k.a. WDR68, Han11, HAN11, AN11) itself — a deeply conserved β-propeller whose published functions include kinase scaffolding (DYRK1A, HIPK2, MEKK1), adenoviral immune evasion, and, critically for this thesis, targeting of DNA ligase I for CRL4-mediated turnover. The third is the pharmacology of CRL4-based targeted protein degradation — thalidomide/CRBN, indisulam/DCAF15, and CR8/cyclin K — which has elevated the CRL4 family from structural curiosity to the most therapeutically validated ubiquitin-ligase subfamily in hematology. This supplement fills gaps in each of these literatures that Tiers 1–3 left under-specified, focusing on the precedents that most directly license the DCAF7→IFIT3 model and on the reagents (genetic, structural, and chemical) a committee will expect the candidate to know at the bench-methodology level. Each entry below is organized around Huey's committee: what the paper shows, how it shows it, and what it answers when a committee member asks "has anybody ever shown that DCAF7 actually degrades a substrate?" or "if DCAF7 is a substrate receptor, what is the structural logic — and is there a small molecule I can block it with?" Thirteen papers are covered across six subsections; all PMIDs were verified through PubMed (this section relies on PubMed metadata and PMC full-text, with DOIs linked inline).

---

sections.title: §2A — DCAF7 as a bona fide CRL4 substrate receptor

subsections.title: Peng et al. 2016 — DCAF7 targets DNA ligase I for CRL4-mediated ubiquitylation and turnover
subsections.body:
The pre-2016 literature recognized DCAF7/WDR68 as a conserved WD40 protein that co-purified with DDB1 and CUL4A in large-scale DCAF inventories (Angers 2006; Jin 2006; He 2006), but it had never been shown to perform the defining biochemical activity of a substrate receptor — the recruitment of a specific client for ubiquitylation and proteasomal destruction. This was the missing link between DCAF7's classification as a "putative DCAF" and its status as a genuine CRL4 specificity factor. Peng and Tomkinson (University of New Mexico Cancer Center) set out to identify the E3 ligase responsible for the turnover of DNA ligase I (LIG1), a PCNA-binding enzyme whose steady-state level must be held in precise stoichiometric balance with FEN-1 during Okazaki fragment maturation; ligase I had previously been shown to be polyubiquitylated, but no ligase responsible had been identified.

The authors worked in HEK293T and HCT116 cells and combined three approaches: (i) a tandem-affinity purification of FLAG-tagged LIG1 followed by LC–MS/MS to catalog its ubiquitylation sites and candidate E3 interactors, (ii) in vitro ubiquitylation assays with purified recombinant CUL4A–DDB1–RBX1 reconstituted ligase and bacterially expressed DCAF7, and (iii) siRNA knockdown and MG132 rescue in asynchronous versus serum-starved/confluence-arrested cultures to dissect whether DCAF7 is rate-limiting for LIG1 turnover under anti-proliferative stress. diGly proteomics mapped three ubiquitylated lysines on LIG1 (K7, K17, K124 in the N-terminal regulatory domain). FLAG-DCAF7 pulldowns co-immunoprecipitated endogenous LIG1 and — crucially — endogenous DDB1 and CUL4A, placing DCAF7 in a quaternary CRL4^DCAF7–LIG1 complex. In vitro reconstitution with recombinant CUL4A-DDB1-RBX1 and DCAF7 reproduced LIG1 polyubiquitylation in a DCAF7-dependent manner, and mutation of the three mapped lysines substantially reduced in vitro ubiquitylation relative to wild-type substrate. In cells, DCAF7 siRNA knockdown blocked the proliferation-arrest–induced drop in LIG1 levels, and MG132 (10 μM, 4 h) rescued LIG1 protein to a similar extent as DCAF7 depletion, demonstrating that CRL4^DCAF7 acts through proteasomal turnover rather than a translational or transcriptional route. Parallel experiments on FEN-1 showed that FEN-1 turnover was not DCAF7-dependent, establishing DCAF7's selectivity.

The paper reframed DCAF7 from "WD40 protein on the DCAF list" to a functionally validated CRL4 substrate receptor with a specific client — DNA ligase I — whose turnover it catalyzes both in vitro and in cells, with a defined ubiquitin acceptor architecture and a defined physiological trigger (cell-cycle exit / proliferation arrest). It showed that different DNA-replication factors (LIG1 versus FEN-1) are partitioned to different E3 ligases despite being transcriptionally co-regulated, which is a conceptual precedent for the idea that DCAF7 selectivity can be client-specific within a larger pathway.

For this thesis, Peng 2016 is the single most important non-Tier 1–3 paper. It is the direct precedent that every committee member will expect Huey to cite when asked "can DCAF7 actually mediate degradation of a defined substrate?" The experimental logic — co-IP of DCAF7 with DDB1/CUL4A and the candidate substrate; diGly site mapping; in vitro reconstitution with recombinant CRL4^DCAF7; MG132 rescue; DCAF7 knockdown stabilization — is exactly the logic the IFIT3 model requires, and Peng's demonstration that DCAF7 is selective among closely related pathway components (LIG1 versus FEN-1) supports the thesis's contention that DCAF7 does not simply degrade the entire ISG proteome but is selective for IFIT3.

PMID: 27573245 · DOI: [10.1074/jbc.M116.746198](https://doi.org/10.1074/jbc.M116.746198) · Model: HEK293T, HCT116; proliferation-arrested vs asynchronous · Approach: FLAG-LIG1 TAP + LC–MS/MS for ubiquitylation sites, co-IP, recombinant in vitro ubiquitylation with CUL4A–DDB1–RBX1, siRNA knockdown + MG132 rescue

---
keyFacts: Model: HEK293T, HCT116; proliferation-arrested vs asynchronous
Approach: FLAG-LIG1 TAP + LC–MS/MS for ubiquitylation sites, co-IP, recombinant in vitro ubiquitylation with CUL4A–DDB1–RBX1, siRNA knockdown + MG132 rescue

subsections.title: Lear et al. 2025 — small-molecule DCAF7 inhibitors (BC18630/BC18813) block a PAK2→DCAF7→TFEB degradation axis during coronavirus infection
subsections.body:
The Peng paradigm required a second, independent demonstration of DCAF7 substrate-receptor activity under a different physiological trigger and, ideally, with a chemical probe that could be deployed in the lab's own hands. Lear et al. (Aging Institute, University of Pittsburgh, 2025) delivered both. The question they asked was narrow on the surface — why does coronaviral infection deplete TFEB, the master transcription factor of endolysosomal and autophagy gene expression? — but the answer has thesis-relevant mechanistic scope: coronaviruses hijack a host CRL4^DCAF7 ligase to degrade TFEB, and the authors generated a series of small molecules, culminating in BC18630 and BC18813, that block the DCAF7–TFEB interaction and restore TFEB.

Mechanistically, the work was built in BEAS-2B (bronchial epithelial), HEK293, HeLa, and primary human airway epithelial cells, with in vivo validation in two SARS-CoV-2 animal models. Phosphoproteomics under MG132 (proteasome inhibition, used to "trap" degron-phosphorylated forms) identified several TFEB phosphorylation sites as candidate phospho-degrons. A custom siRNA kinome library (~600 kinases) with high-content nuclear-TFEB imaging identified PAK2 as the kinase that primes TFEB for destruction. An unbiased siRNA screen of E3 ligase components, combined with mass-spectrometry identification of TFEB interactors, pinpointed DCAF7 as the CRL4 adaptor responsible. The authors then reconstituted the full degradation cascade in vitro: recombinant CUL4A/NEDD8/RBX1 (~10 nM), DDB1, DCAF7 (~4 μM), E1, UbcH5 E2, ubiquitin, magnesium/ATP, with in vitro–translated V5-TFEB as substrate, incubated 90 min at 30 °C — producing DCAF7-dependent polyubiquitylation of TFEB. Cycloheximide (CHX) chase in wild-type versus DCAF7-knockout BEAS-2B cells showed dramatic stabilization of TFEB half-life upon DCAF7 loss. A medicinal-chemistry campaign, staged through multiple rounds of hit-to-lead and lead optimization, yielded BC18630/BC18813, which disrupt the DCAF7–TFEB protein–protein interaction (by AlphaScreen and co-IP), rescue TFEB protein levels in infected cells, and attenuate SARS-CoV-2 replication in both cell and animal models.

The broader impact: Lear 2025 is the first report of a selective DCAF7 small-molecule inhibitor. It validates DCAF7 as an E3 substrate receptor in a second client (TFEB) in a second cell type (epithelial rather than replicating HEK293T), and it places DCAF7 at a nexus of kinase (PAK2)–priming–phosphorylation–recognition, mirroring the canonical CRL architecture where substrate phosphorylation creates the recognition determinant.

For Huey's thesis this paper is a near-perfect precedent: it provides (a) independent confirmation that DCAF7 mediates CRL4-dependent substrate ubiquitylation in cells, (b) the in vitro recombinant-system recipe that should be used to test IFIT3 directly, (c) a demonstration that a kinase can prime DCAF7 recognition (raising the question of whether JAK2 or a downstream kinase phosphorylates IFIT3 to license DCAF7 binding), and (d) a chemical probe (BC18630) that the Melo-Cardenas Lab already uses. A committee member asking "is there a tool compound?" now has an answer.

PMID: 40465712 · DOI: [10.1126/sciadv.adv4033](https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.adv4033) · Model: BEAS-2B, HEK293, HeLa, primary HAE, SARS-CoV-2 mouse models · Approach: phosphoproteomics + MG132 trap; siRNA kinome screen; in vitro CRL4^DCAF7 reconstitution; CHX chase in DCAF7-KO; AlphaScreen PPI disruption; structure-based medicinal chemistry

---
keyFacts: PMID 40465712 · DOI 10.1126/sciadv.adv4033
Model: BEAS-2B, HEK293, HeLa, primary HAE, SARS-CoV-2 mouse models
Approach: phosphoproteomics + MG132 trap; siRNA kinome screen; in vitro CRL4^DCAF7 reconstitution; CHX chase in DCAF7-KO; AlphaScreen PPI disruption; structure-based medicinal chemistry

subsections.title: Wu et al. 2025 — constitutive WDR68/DCAF7 knockout causes intrauterine growth retardation and embryonic lethality with an AUTS2/PCGF5 interactome
subsections.body:
The DCAF7 knockout mouse had never been formally characterized in peer-reviewed form before 2025, creating a long-standing gap: committee members would legitimately ask whether germline DCAF7 loss is compatible with life, what developmental stage is sensitive, and what the interactome looks like in vivo. Wu et al. (Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, Tongren Hospital) filled this gap by generating WDR68-deficient mice via gene targeting and combining the phenotypic analysis with integrated single-cell RNA-seq trajectory analysis spanning gastrulation and organogenesis.

The approach layered four methods. First, gene targeting produced WDR68 null alleles whose homozygotes were scored for viability and growth through gestation. Second, the authors integrated multiple published scRNA-seq datasets covering mouse gastrulation and organogenesis to reconstruct the expression trajectory of WDR68 and performed WGCNA to identify co-expression modules. Third, bulk whole-embryo RNA-seq compared WDR68^−/− versus wild-type embryos to catalog differentially expressed genes. Fourth, HA-tagged WDR68 was PCR-amplified from HEK293T cDNA, cloned into pLVX-IRES-HA-puromycin, stably expressed in HeLa cells, and used for anti-HA co-immunoprecipitation followed by LC–MS/MS to identify DCAF7-interacting proteins, with confirmatory co-IP/western for the top hits. WDR68 null embryos showed intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR) and died during gestation. Transcriptomics of knockout embryos revealed deregulated expression of development-associated genes and — most notably — activation of the HIF-1 (hypoxia-inducible factor) pathway, implying a link between DCAF7 loss and hypoxic/metabolic stress response. The interactomics confirmed that DCAF7 binds AUTS2 (a chromatin-associated factor mutated in human autism and implicated in polycomb repressive complex regulation) and PCGF5 (a Polycomb Group RING Finger protein), in addition to previously known partners.

The field impact: Wu 2025 is the first genetic demonstration that constitutive DCAF7 loss is embryonic-lethal in mammals, consistent with the zebrafish wdr68 craniofacial phenotype (Nissen 2006, Tier 1–3) but advancing it into a mammalian context. The AUTS2/PCGF5 interactome expands the kinase-scaffold view of DCAF7 into a Polycomb/chromatin axis.

Thesis relevance: this paper justifies Huey's use of conditional or tissue-restricted DCAF7 deletion strategies (e.g., Vav-Cre for hematopoietic restriction) and provides a published null-allele reagent framework. It also tells the committee that global DCAF7 perturbation has severe developmental consequences, which explains why prior screens did not reveal the hematopoietic IFN phenotype — it requires a conditional system to uncover.

PMID: 40462101 · DOI: [10.1186/s12967-025-06639-4](https://doi.org/10.1186/s12967-025-06639-4) · Model: germline WDR68^−/− mice; integrated scRNA-seq of mouse gastrulation/organogenesis · Approach: gene targeting, WGCNA, whole-embryo RNA-seq, HA-WDR68 co-IP + LC–MS/MS in HeLa

---
keyFacts: Model: germline WDR68^−/− mice; integrated scRNA-seq of mouse gastrulation/organogenesis
Approach: gene targeting, WGCNA, whole-embryo RNA-seq, HA-WDR68 co-IP + LC–MS/MS in HeLa

subsections.title: Zemke & Berk 2017 — a DCAF7–FOXK–CtBP complex assembled by adenoviral E1A represses type-I ISGs, and the host pathway stabilizes IRF3
subsections.body:
Type-I IFN signaling is the output on which Huey's thesis pivots: if DCAF7 loss stabilizes IFIT3 and amplifies IFN-α in JAK2V617F MPN cells, there should be a precedent linking DCAF7 to IFN regulation. Zemke & Berk's 2017 Cell Host & Microbe paper is that precedent, and its logic is subtle: adenovirus E1A must bind all three of DCAF7, FOXK, and CtBP through its C-terminus to suppress a delayed wave of ISG induction in primary human airway epithelial cells at ~12 h post-infection, and the induction is driven by dramatic IRF3 protein stabilization rather than transcriptional upregulation.

The authors worked in primary human bronchial/tracheal epithelial cells and U2OS, HEK293, and HeLa lines. They generated point mutants of adenovirus e1a that selectively disrupted binding to each of FOXK, DCAF7, and CtBP, individually and in combination. RNA-seq of wild-type versus triple-mutant e1a-expressing cells at 12 h post-infection showed that 52 genes were upregulated ≥2-fold in all three single-binding mutants, with overwhelming gene-ontology enrichment for type-I interferon response genes (p = 7 × 10^−21). Representative ISGs included IFIT2, ISG15, and OASL — the same ISG family of which IFIT3 is a member. siRNA knockdown of IRF3 in U2OS cells, but not knockdown of IRF9, prevented e1a mutant-induced ISG activation, pinpointing IRF3 as the transcription factor licensing the response. Quantitative western blot with anti-IRF3 (D6I4C, Cell Signaling Technology) showed ~10-fold increase in IRF3 protein with no corresponding IRF3 mRNA change, demonstrating protein-level stabilization. IRF3 co-immunoprecipitation combined with chromatin IP localized the stabilized IRF3 to induced ISG promoters. STAT1, STAT2, and IRF9 were similarly elevated at the protein level without mRNA change, suggesting that the DCAF7/FOXK/CtBP pathway normally destabilizes multiple ISGF3 components, of which IRF3 is the rate-limiting node. The requirement that all three E1A-binding surfaces be intact implied assembly of a physical complex — DCAF7–FOXK–CtBP — that is subverted by E1A to suppress the IFN response.

Field impact: this paper established that DCAF7 is a direct player in type-I IFN gene regulation, not an incidental bystander. It is the only published demonstration that DCAF7 sits in a complex that restrains ISG induction, and it ties the restraint to protein-level control of innate-immunity transcription factors.

Thesis relevance: Zemke & Berk 2017 is the single strongest literature precedent for the Melo-Cardenas thesis's central claim that DCAF7 limits IFN signaling. The mechanistic detail — that the restraint is at the level of IRF3/STAT protein stability rather than transcription, with ~10-fold protein accumulation upon pathway disruption — is a direct structural analog of the DCAF7→IFIT3 model (IFIT3 is stabilized by DCAF7 loss). It also identifies FOXK and CtBP as likely co-complex members that Huey should probe in MPN cells. A committee chair who studies UPS + IFN in hematopoiesis will know this paper and will expect it to be cited prominently.

PMID: 29241042 · DOI: [10.1016/j.chom.2017.11.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chom.2017.11.008) · Model: primary human respiratory epithelial cells; U2OS, HEK293, HeLa; adenovirus e1a mutant series · Approach: e1a C-terminal point mutants disrupting FOXK, DCAF7, CtBP binding; RNA-seq at 12 h p.i.; quantitative western for IRF3; IRF3 co-IP/ChIP; IRF3/IRF9 siRNA

---
keyFacts: Model: primary human respiratory epithelial cells; U2OS, HEK293, HeLa; adenovirus e1a mutant series
Approach: e1a C-terminal point mutants disrupting FOXK, DCAF7, CtBP binding; RNA-seq at 12 h p.i.; quantitative western for IRF3; IRF3 co-IP/ChIP; IRF3/IRF9 siRNA

subsections.title: Trilling et al. 2011 — DDB1–CUL4 is recruited by cytomegalovirus pM27 to degrade STAT2 and extinguish the interferon response
subsections.body:
The inference that a CRL4 complex can target a key interferon effector for destruction was, until recently, supported mostly by viral hijack studies — and the Trilling paper is the foundational example. Mouse cytomegalovirus (MCMV) encodes pM27, a viral protein essential for viral fitness in interferon-conditioned cells. The authors (Trilling, Hengel and colleagues, Heinrich-Heine University Düsseldorf) asked how pM27 antagonizes STAT2-dependent IFN signaling and found that pM27 recruits DDB1 to assemble a hijacked CRL4-type ubiquitin ligase that polyubiquitylates STAT2 and drives its proteasomal degradation.

The experimental design combined cell-based protein half-life assays (cycloheximide chase; MG132 pharmacologic rescue), DDB1 co-immunoprecipitation, pM27 truncation mutagenesis to map the DDB1 interaction surface, and siRNA knockdown of DDB1 in infected NIH-3T3 and HeLa cells. pM27 expression, with or without infection, shortened STAT2 protein half-life; proteasomal inhibition restored STAT2 to wild-type levels and revealed accumulation of polyubiquitin-conjugated STAT2 species. pM27 co-immunoprecipitated with DDB1, and truncation mutants that failed to bind DDB1 also failed to degrade STAT2 — a strict structure–function correlation. DDB1 siRNA knockdown rescued STAT2 levels in pM27-expressing cells and, critically, severely impaired MCMV replication in IFN-conditioned cells, phenocopying M27-deficient virus. Thus pM27 functions as a viral DCAF, using DDB1 as its adaptor into the CRL4 apparatus to specifically route STAT2 for destruction while leaving STAT1 intact.

Field impact: Trilling 2011 is widely cited as the first mechanistic proof that host CRL4 machinery can be redirected against a core IFN effector. It set the precedent for thinking about CRL4 as a tunable regulator of IFN-response magnitude.

Thesis relevance: the paper answers the committee question "is there any precedent that a CRL4 complex can target an interferon-signaling protein?" with a definitive yes. It also provides a methodological template (pharmacologic proteasome rescue plus DDB1-knockdown phenocopy) directly transferable to IFIT3 in JAK2V617F cells.

PMID: 21698215 · DOI: [10.1371/journal.ppat.1002069](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.ppat.1002069) · Model: NIH-3T3, HeLa, MCMV-infected cells, STAT2-reporter lines · Approach: CHX chase, MG132 rescue, DDB1 co-IP, pM27 truncation mapping, DDB1 siRNA phenocopy with viral replication readout

---
keyFacts: PMID 21698215 · DOI 10.1371/journal.ppat.1002069
Model: NIH-3T3, HeLa, MCMV-infected cells, STAT2-reporter lines
Approach: CHX chase, MG132 rescue, DDB1 co-IP, pM27 truncation mapping, DDB1 siRNA phenocopy with viral replication readout

sections.title: §2C — DCAF7 as a kinase scaffold: the partner biology

subsections.title: Miyata & Nishida 2011 — DYRK1A binds WDR68 and drives its nuclear translocation
subsections.body:
Before DCAF7 was recognized as a CRL4 substrate receptor, the dominant conception was as a kinase scaffold. Miyata & Nishida (Kyoto University) identified WDR68 as a cellular binding partner of DYRK1A — the Down syndrome–critical-region kinase — and established the structural determinants of the interaction as well as its functional consequence: DYRK1A binding redistributes WDR68 to the nucleus.

In COS and mammalian cultured cells, the authors performed yeast-two-hybrid and co-immunoprecipitation screens with FLAG- and HA-tagged DYRK family members. Endogenous and overexpressed WDR68 bound DYRK1A and DYRK1B but not DYRK2, DYRK3, or DYRK4. Truncation mapping localized the WDR68 interaction to the N-terminal domain of DYRK1A; the catalytic kinase domain and C-terminal region were dispensable. Deletions that removed sequences flanking the WD40 repeats in WDR68 (but not deletions within the WD40 core alone) abolished binding — indicating that the WD40 propeller is necessary but not sufficient and that flanking residues contribute to DYRK specificity. Immunofluorescence showed that WDR68 is distributed throughout the cell in the basal state, but co-expression of wild-type or kinase-dead DYRK1A induced robust nuclear accumulation of WDR68. Because the kinase-dead mutant was equally effective, the nuclear translocation was binding-driven, not phosphorylation-driven. RNAi knockdown of WDR68 impaired proliferation and induced apoptosis in mammalian cells, establishing that WDR68 is essential for cell survival.

Field impact: this work defined the kinase-scaffold view of DCAF7 and established that DCAF7 subcellular localization is dictated by binding partners. It launched a decade of work on DYRK1A–DCAF7 complexes in neurodevelopment, craniofacial morphogenesis, and, more recently, megakaryopoiesis and thrombocythemia biology.

Thesis relevance: the paper forces Huey to articulate why the kinase-scaffold role of DCAF7 does not contradict its CRL4 substrate-receptor role — the WD40 propeller can plausibly do both, since DDB1 uses the propeller surface and DYRK1A binding uses flanking residues. It also raises the possibility that DYRK1A–DCAF7 complexes and DDB1–DCAF7 complexes are distinct pools that are interchangeable under different conditions, a biochemical point that may need to be addressed explicitly in the thesis.

PMID: 21777625 · DOI: [10.1016/j.bbamcr.2011.06.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbamcr.2011.06.023) · Model: COS-7, mammalian cultured cells; DYRK family comparison · Approach: yeast-two-hybrid, co-IP with FLAG/HA-tagged DYRKs, truncation mapping, immunofluorescence, RNAi proliferation/apoptosis

---
keyFacts: Model: COS-7, mammalian cultured cells; DYRK family comparison
Approach: yeast-two-hybrid, co-IP with FLAG/HA-tagged DYRKs, truncation mapping, immunofluorescence, RNAi proliferation/apoptosis

subsections.title: Ritterhoff et al. 2010 — WDR68/Han11 is a scaffold coupling HIPK2 and MEKK1 kinases and shaping DNA-damage and osmotic-stress signaling
subsections.body:
Ritterhoff and Schmitz (Justus-Liebig University Giessen) generalized the kinase-scaffold biology beyond DYRK1A by showing that Han11 (DCAF7/WDR68) binds HIPK2, DYRK1A, DYRK1B, and MEKK1, and is required to assemble productive MEKK1–DYRK/HIPK signaling complexes. This paper is foundational because it establishes DCAF7 as a multi-kinase platform — its scaffolding repertoire extends across the MAP3K and HIPK families and has physiological consequence for stress-response signaling.

The paper deployed in vitro binding assays with purified recombinant proteins, tandem immunoprecipitation in HEK293 and HeLa cells, RNAi knockdown in Caenorhabditis elegans (using the Han11 orthologs Swan-1 and Swan-2) with osmotic-stress viability readouts, and quantitative transcriptional-response assays in human cell lines. Direct binding of Han11 to HIPK2, DYRK1A, DYRK1B, and MEKK1 was demonstrated biochemically. Han11 was required to allow coupling of MEKK1 to DYRK1 and HIPK2 in cells. Han11 knockdown in human cells lowered both the threshold and amplitude of HIPK2- and MEKK1-triggered signaling, and shifted the kinetics of kinase induction. Downstream, knockdown altered HIPK2-driven transcription in response to DNA damage and MEKK1-driven gene-expression responses to stress. Over-expression of Han11 also interfered with signaling — consistent with a stoichiometric scaffold in which mass-action must be balanced. C. elegans Swan-1/Swan-2 RNAi sensitized worms to osmotic stress.

Field impact: the paper solidified DCAF7 as a bidirectional kinase-signaling scaffold whose stoichiometry governs signaling amplitude. It is the paper most responsible for the view that DCAF7 is a "signaling hub."

Thesis relevance: for Huey, Ritterhoff 2010 is the paper a committee member will cite to argue "maybe DCAF7 is not really an E3 substrate receptor, maybe it just scaffolds kinases that phosphorylate IFIT3." The thesis must answer this directly, and the answer — as Peng 2016, Lear 2025, and the Melo-Cardenas data collectively demonstrate — is that DCAF7 is both, because the WD40 propeller can engage DDB1 on one face and a kinase on another, and because individual cellular pools of DCAF7 are biochemically distinguishable.

PMID: 20940704 · DOI: [10.1038/emboj.2010.251](https://doi.org/10.1038/emboj.2010.251) · Model: HEK293, HeLa; C. elegans (Swan-1/Swan-2) · Approach: in vitro direct binding, tandem co-IP, RNAi knockdown + overexpression kinetics, C. elegans osmotic-stress assay, HIPK2/MEKK1 transcriptional readouts

---
keyFacts: Model: HEK293, HeLa; C. elegans (Swan-1/Swan-2)
Approach: in vitro direct binding, tandem co-IP, RNAi knockdown + overexpression kinetics, C. elegans osmotic-stress assay, HIPK2/MEKK1 transcriptional readouts

sections.title: §2D — CRL4 architectural biology — the substrate-receptor grammar

subsections.title: Angers et al. 2006 — molecular architecture of DDB1–CUL4A and the founding definition of the DCAF/DWD family
subsections.body:
Angers and Zheng (University of Washington / HHMI) provided the structural and proteomic foundation on which every DCAF study rests. They solved the crystal structure of a virally hijacked DDB1–CUL4A–ROC1 complex, showing that DDB1 uses one β-propeller to clamp CUL4A and presents a separate double-β-propeller "platform" as the variable substrate-presentation module. They then performed tandem-affinity purification of human DDB1 and CUL4A coupled to LC–MS/MS and identified a new family of WD40-repeat proteins — the "DWD" (DDB1/WD40) or DCAF proteins — that bind directly to the DDB1 double-propeller and serve as interchangeable substrate-specificity factors. DCAF7 was one of the founding members of this catalog.

Experimental readouts included X-ray crystallography at ≤2.9 Å, mass spectrometry–based interactome, and biochemical reconstitution confirming that recombinant DCAFs bind DDB1 through conserved sequence features in the WD40 domain. The paper coined the modular architecture that dominates modern CRL4 pharmacology: cullin scaffold + RING box + DDB1 adaptor + exchangeable DCAF specificity factor.

For Huey's thesis, this is the structural vocabulary any committee will expect — in particular, the phrase "DCAF7 binds the DDB1 double-β-propeller" should be ready at the tip of the tongue, as should the molecular logic of how IMiDs and molecular glues (below) exploit this architecture.

PMID: 16964240 · DOI: [10.1038/nature05175](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature05175) · Model: recombinant CUL4A-DDB1-ROC1; HEK293 TAP-MS · Approach: X-ray crystallography; tandem affinity purification + LC–MS/MS; DCAF family definition

---
keyFacts: Model: recombinant CUL4A-DDB1-ROC1; HEK293 TAP-MS
Approach: X-ray crystallography; tandem affinity purification + LC–MS/MS; DCAF family definition

subsections.title: Jin et al. 2006 — the DCAF family and CDT2 as the CRL4 receptor that destroys Cdt1 in S phase
subsections.body:
Jin, Harper, and Walter (Harvard Medical School) provided the parallel biochemical enumeration of the DCAF family and, more importantly, the first rigorous functional validation of a DCAF as a cell-cycle–regulated substrate receptor: CDT2 (DCAF2) targets the replication licensing factor Cdt1 for CRL4-mediated destruction in S phase and after DNA damage. This paper is the prototype of "DCAF recognizes substrate only under a specific cellular context."

In Xenopus egg extracts and in HeLa cells, the authors identified 18 DDB1- and CUL4-associated factors, 14 of which contained WD40 repeats, by mass spectrometry of tagged DDB1/CUL4 complexes. A conserved "WDXR" motif in the WD40 propeller was required for DDB1 binding. CDT2 depletion caused Cdt1 accumulation, re-replication, and ATM/ATR checkpoint activation. Cdt2 was recruited to replication forks via a PCNA–Cdt1 interaction, and Cdt1 ubiquitylation occurred at the fork, in S-phase–specific fashion.

Thesis relevance: Jin 2006 is the proof-of-principle that a DCAF can be a conditional substrate receptor, recognizing its client only under defined cellular conditions (S phase for CDT2). This is a direct conceptual precedent for the idea that CRL4^DCAF7 might preferentially recognize IFIT3 in the context of JAK2V617F-driven chronic IFN signaling, as opposed to constitutively.

PMID: 16949367 · DOI: [10.1016/j.molcel.2006.08.010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2006.08.010) · Model: Xenopus egg extracts; HeLa human cells · Approach: DDB1/CUL4 TAP-MS; WDXR motif mapping; Cdt1 re-replication + checkpoint readouts; PCNA/Cdt1 co-IP at forks

---
keyFacts: Model: Xenopus egg extracts; HeLa human cells
Approach: DDB1/CUL4 TAP-MS; WDXR motif mapping; Cdt1 re-replication + checkpoint readouts; PCNA/Cdt1 co-IP at forks

subsections.title: Soucy et al. 2009 — MLN4924 (pevonedistat) inhibits NEDD8-activating enzyme and deactivates all CRLs
subsections.body:
The CRL superfamily — including CRL4^DCAF7 — is activated by NEDD8 conjugation onto the cullin; disrupting neddylation pharmacologically collapses the entire CRL output. Soucy et al. (Millennium/Takeda) discovered MLN4924, a mechanism-based adenosine-sulfamate inhibitor of NAE1 (NEDD8-activating enzyme), and showed that it blocks CRL-mediated substrate turnover, causes re-replication (from stabilization of Cdt1 and other licensing factors), and exerts antitumor activity in xenograft models. Cellular readouts included substrate stabilization (p27, Cdt1, ORC1), re-replication as measured by BrdU + DNA content flow, S-phase DNA damage, and apoptosis induction in human tumor lines.

Thesis relevance: MLN4924 is the standard pharmacologic handle to show that a phenotype is "CRL-dependent" as opposed to "DCAF7-knockdown-dependent" in a specific way. For the DCAF7→IFIT3 model, MLN4924 should stabilize IFIT3 in a manner epistatic to DCAF7 loss; the absence of an additive effect between MLN4924 and DCAF7 knockdown would be strong genetic evidence that IFIT3 stabilization runs through the CRL pathway.

PMID: 19360080 · DOI: [10.1038/nature07884](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature07884) · Model: human tumor cell lines; mouse xenografts · Approach: mechanism-based NAE inhibition; cellular CRL substrate stabilization; re-replication and apoptosis readouts

---
keyFacts: Model: human tumor cell lines; mouse xenografts
Approach: mechanism-based NAE inhibition; cellular CRL substrate stabilization; re-replication and apoptosis readouts

sections.title: §2E — The thalidomide/IMiD paradigm — CRL4 as a drug target

subsections.title: Fischer et al. 2014 — crystal structure of DDB1–CRBN in complex with thalidomide, lenalidomide, pomalidomide
subsections.body:
The 2014 Fischer/Thomä structure was the crystallographic capstone to the Krönke 2014 and Lu 2014 genetic demonstrations that IMiDs recruit IKZF1/IKZF3 to CRL4^CRBN. Fischer and colleagues (FMI Basel and Novartis Institutes for Biomedical Research) solved crystal structures of the human DDB1–CRBN complex bound to each of the three clinical IMiDs, at resolutions permitting side-chain precision. The structures showed that CRBN is a bona fide substrate receptor occupying the DDB1 double-β-propeller in the Angers architecture, and that IMiDs bind a defined thalidomide-binding pocket on CRBN via the phthaloyl-glutarimide pharmacophore with strict enantioselectivity. An unbiased biochemical screen also identified the homeobox transcription factor MEIS2 as an endogenous CRL4^CRBN substrate; IMiDs displace MEIS2 while recruiting IKZF1/IKZF3, a dual-activity model that rationalized both the on-target and neomorphic effects of IMiDs in myeloma and del(5q) MDS.

For Huey, Fischer 2014 is the structural touchstone for DCAF-mediated substrate recognition: the same DDB1 double-propeller that binds CRBN binds DCAF7, and the thalidomide-glutarimide-recruited interface is the template from which the entire molecular-glue discipline has grown (including Lear 2025's BC18630 for DCAF7).

PMID: 25043012 · DOI: [10.1038/nature13527](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13527) · Model: recombinant human DDB1–CRBN + IMiDs · Approach: X-ray crystallography of CRBN–IMiD–DDB1 ternary complexes; unbiased endogenous substrate screen

---
keyFacts: Model: recombinant human DDB1–CRBN + IMiDs
Approach: X-ray crystallography of CRBN–IMiD–DDB1 ternary complexes; unbiased endogenous substrate screen

subsections.title: Petzold, Fischer & Thomä 2016 — lenalidomide reshapes the CRBN surface to recruit CK1α
subsections.body:
Petzold et al. (FMI Basel, 2.45 Å crystal structure) solved the DDB1–CRBN–lenalidomide–CK1α complex and showed that lenalidomide functions as a genuine molecular glue: neither CRBN nor CK1α interact measurably in the absence of drug, but lenalidomide creates a composite surface that engages a β-hairpin loop in the CK1α N-lobe. IKZF1 engages the same composite surface via a structurally related motif, explaining how a single small molecule can recruit multiple, sequence-unrelated proteins for degradation. The therapeutic consequence is that lenalidomide's clinical efficacy in del(5q) MDS depends on CK1α degradation — del(5q) patients have haploinsufficient CK1α and are exquisitely sensitive to further CK1α loss.

Thesis relevance: Petzold 2016 is the paradigm for how a pharmacologic agent can convert a non-interaction into a productive ubiquitin-ligase–substrate engagement. This conceptual model anchors the interpretation of BC18630 (Lear 2025) in the opposite direction: a molecule that blocks a productive DCAF7–substrate interaction. A committee member thinking about drugging DCAF7 will think in these structural terms.

PMID: 26909574 · DOI: [10.1038/nature16979](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature16979) · Model: recombinant DDB1–CRBN–CK1α + lenalidomide · Approach: 2.45 Å X-ray crystallography of quaternary glue complex

---
keyFacts: Model: recombinant DDB1–CRBN–CK1α + lenalidomide
Approach: 2.45 Å X-ray crystallography of quaternary glue complex

subsections.title: Matyskiela et al. 2018 — SALL4 is the CRBN neosubstrate that explains thalidomide teratogenicity
subsections.body:
The phocomelia catastrophe of the late 1950s — ~10,000 children born with thalidomide-induced limb defects — remained mechanistically unexplained for six decades. Matyskiela and Chamberlain (Celgene) used transgenic mice humanized for cereblon, expression profiling, degradation proteomics, and rabbit embryonic assays to identify SALL4, a C2H2 zinc finger transcription factor mutated in human Duane-radial ray, IVIC, and acro-renal-ocular syndromes, as a thalidomide-dependent CRBN neosubstrate whose loss phenocopies the thalidomide embryopathy. Species specificity — thalidomide is teratogenic in humans and rabbits but not in mice — mapped to sequence variation in mouse SALL4 that renders it resistant to thalidomide-induced CRBN recruitment.

Thesis relevance: Matyskiela 2018 demonstrates that CRL4 E3-ligase reprogramming can recruit disease-causing transcription factors and expands the "degradable" space beyond IKZF zinc fingers into the broader C2H2 family. For Huey, this is the precedent that the substrate repertoire of a CRL4 complex can include multiple unrelated proteins, licensing the IFIT3 claim as biologically reasonable. It also reminds the committee that species-specific sequence variation matters, which could bear on mouse versus human IFIT3.

PMID: 30190590 · DOI: [10.1038/s41589-018-0129-x](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41589-018-0129-x) · Model: humanized-CRBN transgenic mice, rabbit embryos, human iPSCs · Approach: species comparative proteomics; degradation assays; transgenic rescue

---
keyFacts: Model: humanized-CRBN transgenic mice, rabbit embryos, human iPSCs
Approach: species comparative proteomics; degradation assays; transgenic rescue

sections.title: §2F — Molecular glues beyond CRBN — paradigm expansion

subsections.title: Słabicki et al. 2020 — CR8 converts a kinase inhibitor into a cyclin K molecular-glue degrader via CRL4B/DDB1
subsections.body:
The Słabicki 2020 paper (Ebert/Thomä/Fischer collaboration, Nature) crystallized a conceptual expansion of the molecular-glue idea: not all glue degraders engage a dedicated substrate receptor. By systematically mining the correlation between cytotoxicity profiles of 4,518 preclinical compounds (across hundreds of human cancer cell lines with defined E3-ligase-component expression data) and expression of individual E3 ligase components, the authors identified CR8 — a pan-CDK inhibitor — as behaving like a molecular-glue degrader. Structural work showed that CDK-bound CR8 has a solvent-exposed pyridyl moiety that contacts DDB1 directly, bypassing the need for a substrate receptor and presenting the CDK12–cyclin K complex to the CUL4 machinery for ubiquitylation. Cyclin K — previously thought undruggable — was depleted in cellular degradation assays.

Parallel work by Mayor-Ruiz et al. 2020 (below) derived the same cyclin K degrader conclusion through a chemical screen in hyponeddylated cells.

Thesis relevance: Słabicki 2020 is a reminder that CRL4 complexes are conformationally plastic — a small molecule can induce a productive interaction even in the absence of a canonical DCAF. For Huey's model, this is important because it teaches that the DDB1 surface is intrinsically able to accept new substrates, so the idea that DCAF7 presents IFIT3 is a much less exotic biochemical proposition than the "DCAF7 is a kinase scaffold only" literature would suggest.

PMID: 32494016 · DOI: [10.1038/s41586-020-2374-x](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2374-x) · Model: hundreds of cancer cell lines; CCLE pharmacogenomic correlation · Approach: integrated pharmacogenomics, cellular degradation, cryo-EM/structural; unbiased molecular-glue discovery

---
keyFacts: Model: hundreds of cancer cell lines; CCLE pharmacogenomic correlation
Approach: integrated pharmacogenomics, cellular degradation, cryo-EM/structural; unbiased molecular-glue discovery

subsections.title: Mayor-Ruiz et al. 2020 — rational discovery of molecular-glue degraders via chemical screening in hyponeddylated cells (CRL4B-dependent cyclin K degradation)
subsections.body:
Mayor-Ruiz and Winter (CeMM Vienna) developed a scalable chemical screening strategy grounded in a key mechanistic principle: molecular-glue degraders require active CRL-family ligases, so inhibition of NEDD8 activation (MLN4924) should selectively rescue cells from glue-mediated cytotoxicity while leaving orthogonal cytotoxic mechanisms unaffected. Screening libraries of small molecules under hyponeddylated versus normal conditions, followed by multi-omics target deconvolution, identified multiple compounds that induce ubiquitylation and degradation of cyclin K by prompting an interaction between CDK12–cyclin K and a CRL4B ligase complex — again, independently of a dedicated substrate receptor.

This paper cemented the "hyponeddylation-rescue screen" as the standard orthogonal test for whether a cellular degradation event runs through a CRL4 (as opposed to proteasome-independent or non-CRL E3) pathway. The companion Mayor-Ruiz–Winter–Kubicek work, together with Słabicki 2020, established that the cyclin K chemotype is a general paradigm.

Thesis relevance: the hyponeddylation-rescue logic is directly applicable to IFIT3: if MLN4924 pretreatment stabilizes IFIT3 and phenocopies DCAF7 knockdown, CRL4-dependence is established. Mayor-Ruiz 2020 provides the validated workflow.

PMID: 32747809 · DOI: [10.1038/s41589-020-0594-x](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41589-020-0594-x) · Model: human cancer cell lines under hyponeddylation · Approach: hyponeddylation-differential chemical screening; multi-omics deconvolution; CRL4B reconstitution

---
keyFacts: Model: human cancer cell lines under hyponeddylation
Approach: hyponeddylation-differential chemical screening; multi-omics deconvolution; CRL4B reconstitution

subsections.title: JAK2V617F and the Myeloproliferative Neoplasms: Deep Gap Analysis: scope & rationale
subsections.body:
This section is the JAK2V617F spine of the defense. A committee stacked with JAK2 experts will not be satisfied by a citation of Vainchenker & Kralovics 2017 and a handwave toward "the 2005 papers." They will interrogate the granular differences between the four 2005 discovery papers — the Cambridge LOH scan (Baxter), the Vainchenker functional validation (James), the Skoda microsatellite/cohort study (Kralovics), and the Gilliland candidate-kinase approach (Levine) — because those experimental designs still shape how the field interprets allele burden, progenitor hierarchies, and EEC assays today. Beyond discovery, the committee will expect Huey to defend her choice of mouse model on methodological grounds, which means she must be fluent not only in the Mullally 2010 Jak2^V617F floxed knock-in (the conditional allele her lab actually uses) but also in the contemporaneous Akada 2010 independent knock-in, and in the orthogonal CALR models (Marty 2016, Elf 2016) that define the non-JAK2 MPN landscape. The CALR discovery quartet (Klampfl 2013, Nangalia 2013) and the JAK2 exon 12 work (Scott 2007) complete the allelic landscape of driver mutations that anyone defending an MPN thesis must be able to place in context.

The IFN-α mechanism papers (Mullally 2013 Cancer Cell, Hasan 2013 Blood) are non-negotiable for a DCAF7→IFIT3→IFN-α thesis: these are the two in vivo demonstrations that IFN-α specifically depletes JAK2V617F MPN-propagating cells in the same knock-in model Huey uses. The clinical/translational arc (Verstovsek 2012 COMFORT-I, Gisslinger 2020 PROUD-PV/CONTINUATION-PV, Kleppe 2018 JAK+BET combination) anchors why amplifying an endogenous IFN response by stabilising IFIT3 is clinically interesting — ruxolitinib cytoreduces but does not eradicate; ropegIFN eradicates but is slow and tolerability-limited. Finally, the single-cell phylogenetic work (Williams 2022, Van Egeren 2021) reframes MPN as a disease with decades-long pre-clinical evolution, which directly bears on the HSC-centric rationale for stabilising IFIT3 rather than chasing mature myeloid progeny.

Unless otherwise noted, article metadata below comes from PubMed.

---

subsections.title: Baxter et al. 2005, Lancet — LOH-driven identification in PV
subsections.body:
In early 2005 the field understood PV, ET, and PMF as clonal HSC-derived malignancies with characteristic growth-factor hypersensitivity — most iconically, PV erythroid progenitors that form erythropoietin-independent erythroid colonies (EECs) — but the molecular lesion was unknown. Cytogenetic studies had repeatedly flagged 9pLOH in a subset of PV, and candidate-gene efforts had implicated several tyrosine kinases without a unifying lesion. The Cambridge group (Anthony Green, E. Joanna Baxter) reasoned that the 9p minimally deleted region defined a driver locus and that sequencing the tyrosine kinases within that interval would reveal the mutation.

Their approach combined microsatellite PCR-based LOH mapping, Affymetrix SNP array analysis on granulocyte DNA, and bidirectional Sanger sequencing of all coding exons of JAK2 from peripheral-blood granulocytes and T cells of patients with PV, ET, or IMF. T cells served as a germline control to distinguish somatic from germline variation. Allele-specific PCR was developed to sensitively detect the mutation and quantify mutant allele burden in granulocytes versus T cells. Colony assays (BFU-E with and without Epo) and FISH confirmed that the mutation segregated with the clonal myeloid lineage and with EECs. The cohort included 73 PV, 51 ET, and 16 IMF patients drawn from Cambridge and UK referral centres.

A single recurrent point mutation — a G-to-T transversion at nucleotide 1849 of JAK2 producing a V617F substitution in the JH2 pseudokinase autoinhibitory domain — was identified in 71/73 (97%) PV, 29/51 (57%) ET, and 8/16 (50%) IMF patients. The mutation was absent from T cells and from a large panel of controls, confirming somatic acquisition. Most patients were heterozygous; a subset were homozygous by virtue of mitotic recombination producing uniparental disomy of 9p, explaining the earlier 9pLOH observation. The mutation was present in all EECs grown from patient marrow, establishing a direct link between V617F and the cardinal cytokine-hypersensitivity phenotype of PV. Allele-specific PCR across granulocytes versus T cells versus B cells placed the mutation in a multipotent progenitor.

The Baxter paper is the 9pLOH-anchored entry into the JAK2V617F literature, and its committee value lies in teaching the student why LOH/uniparental disomy matters mechanistically: homozygosity for V617F is not an alternative mechanism but an amplification of the heterozygous lesion, and it is enriched in PV (where Epo signalling is the dominant readout) compared with ET. For Huey's thesis, the Baxter clonality mapping is the prototype for how a mutation is shown to reside in an HSC-like multipotent compartment — directly relevant when she argues that DCAF7 loss must phenocopy IFN-α in acting on the Jak2V617F-mutant HSC itself, not merely on downstream progeny. The paper is also the origin of the allele-burden concept she will use whenever she quantifies disease response to DCAF7 perturbation.

Metadata: Baxter EJ et al. Acquired mutation of the tyrosine kinase JAK2 in human myeloproliferative disorders. Lancet 2005;365(9464):1054-61. PMID 15781101. DOI [10.1016/S0140-6736(05)71142-9](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(05)71142-9).

subsections.title: James et al. 2005, Nature — functional validation in Ba/F3
subsections.body:
The Vainchenker group at Villejuif came at the same problem from the opposite direction. Rather than starting from a cytogenetic signal, they reasoned from first principles about the CML precedent: a constitutively active tyrosine kinase was the most plausible cause of a disease defined by cytokine hypersensitivity, and the HSC origin of PV demanded that the relevant kinase act downstream of the Epo receptor. They had spent years mapping Epo signalling and had the infrastructure to test candidate kinases functionally in Ba/F3 cells — an IL-3-dependent pro-B lymphoid line that serves as the field-standard readout for transforming kinase activity via conversion to cytokine independence.

The experimental design was twofold. First, they resequenced JAK2 from granulocytes of PV patients, identifying the same V617F mutation as Baxter.
