Second, and more influentially, they performed functional validation: the V617F mutant was expressed in Ba/F3 cells co-expressing EpoR, MPL, or G-CSFR, and in HEL and UT-7 erythroid lines. In parallel, they used siRNA knockdown of JAK2 in primary PV progenitors to test requirement. A murine bone-marrow transduction/transplantation (BMT) assay expressed V617F retrovirally in lethally irradiated recipients. Biochemistry — phospho-JAK2, phospho-STAT5, phospho-ERK, phospho-AKT — read out constitutive signalling.

The headline findings: V617F was present in >80% of their PV cohort. In Ba/F3-EpoR, V617F conferred Epo-hypersensitivity and eventually IL-3-independent growth; in Ba/F3 alone, V617F was insufficient, establishing that a type I cytokine receptor scaffold is required to productively display the mutant kinase — a mechanistic detail that foreshadowed the MPL-dependence of CALR mutants a decade later. V617F drove constitutive phospho-STAT5 in the absence of ligand. In the murine BMT model, V617F recipients developed erythrocytosis within weeks, formally demonstrating that V617F alone is sufficient to initiate a PV-like phenotype in vivo. The paper established the JH2→JH1 autoinhibitory release model as the mechanistic basis for V617F gain-of-function.

The James paper is the functional companion to Baxter and is disproportionately cited because it delivers the mechanistic proof — constitutive kinase, erythrocytosis in mice, hypersensitivity to Epo through a type I cytokine receptor scaffold. For Huey's thesis this matters in two ways. First, the Ba/F3-EpoR/MPL readout remains the workhorse for testing whether a new regulator of the JAK2 pathway (including a ubiquitin ligase like DCAF7) alters the signalling amplitude of V617F in a cell-autonomous manner. Second, the BMT retroviral model defined what disease the field expects Jak2V617F to initiate in vivo — a benchmark against which all subsequent knock-in models, including the Mullally 2010 conditional allele, are measured.

Metadata: James C et al. A unique clonal JAK2 mutation leading to constitutive signalling causes polycythaemia vera. Nature 2005;434(7037):1144-8. PMID 15793561. DOI [10.1038/nature03546](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03546).

subsections.title: Kralovics et al. 2005, NEJM — microarray LOH + cohort
subsections.body:
The Skoda group in Basel took a hybrid approach that produced the most clinically annotated of the four 2005 papers. Their hypothesis was identical to Baxter's — that 9pLOH harboured the driver — but their toolkit and, critically, their cohort design differed. They microsatellite-mapped the 9pLOH minimal region in a larger cohort (244 MPN patients: 128 PV, 93 ET, 23 IMF) and sequenced the shortlist of candidate genes from this interval. Because their cohort was large, longitudinally sampled, and clinically annotated, they could correlate V617F status with disease duration, fibrotic progression, thrombotic/haemorrhagic events, and cytoreductive treatment exposure.

They identified V617F in all 51 patients with 9pLOH, 66/193 of those without 9pLOH (heterozygous), and none of the V617F-wild-type set. Frequencies were 65% in PV, 57% in IMF, and 23% in ET. They explicitly demonstrated that 9pLOH was generated by mitotic recombination converting the heterozygous V617F mutation to homozygosity — the same conclusion as Baxter but from a larger and more statistically tractable series. Crucially, they then performed clinical outcome analyses: patients with V617F had significantly longer disease duration, higher rates of fibrotic transformation, more thrombotic/haemorrhagic complications, and greater cytoreductive treatment exposure than V617F-negative patients. In vitro colony assays and genetic analyses supported a proliferative and survival advantage conferred by V617F, consistent with clonal expansion.

What separates Kralovics in the committee's mind is the disease-burden correlate. By showing that homozygous patients had a more aggressive clinical course, Kralovics operationalised the dose-dependence of V617F signalling — a concept that Akada 2010 would later validate genetically in a mouse knock-in by directly comparing heterozygous and homozygous Jak2V617F alleles. For Huey, the Kralovics cohort is the reference for how allele burden maps onto disease phenotype in humans; she will have to reconcile any DCAF7-dependent change in allele burden (or in apparent allele burden due to IFIT3-mediated ISG activation masking the mutant clone) against this clinical reference dataset.

Metadata: Kralovics R et al. A gain-of-function mutation of JAK2 in myeloproliferative disorders. N Engl J Med 2005;352(17):1779-90. PMID 15858187. DOI [10.1056/NEJMoa051113](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa051113).

subsections.title: Levine et al. 2005, Cancer Cell — candidate-gene approach + mechanism
subsections.body:
The Gilliland group (with Ross Levine as first author, now Sloan Kettering) reached the same endpoint through a candidate-gene high-throughput resequencing effort. Rather than cytogenetically triangulating on 9p, they directly resequenced a panel of tyrosine kinases in a large web-recruited MPN cohort assembled through an internet-based MPD Consortium protocol — a methodological novelty at the time. The cohort sizes were substantial (164 PV, 115 ET, 46 MMM) and a control set of 269 normal individuals was sequenced in parallel, giving the paper the cleanest somatic-vs-germline discrimination of the quartet.

They identified JAK2V617F in 121/164 PV (of which 41 were homozygous and 80 heterozygous), 37/115 ET, and 16/46 MMM, and in 0/269 controls. Molecular cytogenetics plus SNP genotyping established that homozygosity arose through duplication of the mutant allele, i.e., uniparental disomy — echoing the Baxter and Kralovics conclusions independently. The Levine paper's mechanistic contribution was the most biochemical of the four: they expressed V617F in Ba/F3 cells and in primary haematopoietic progenitors, demonstrated constitutive tyrosine kinase activity, and (most importantly for later drug-development work) mapped V617F-driven activation of the downstream STAT5/PI3K/ERK cascade. They also linked mutant allele burden to disease phenotype and provided early evidence for JAK2 inhibitor sensitivity in V617F-expressing cells — a thread that Gilliland's group would follow into the ruxolitinib era.

Levine is the fourth leg of the stool and is the one most likely to be invoked by a committee member of the Sloan Kettering/Broad lineage; its experimental novelty (candidate-kinase resequencing in a crowdsourced cohort) is historically important because it foreshadowed how the field would pivot to exome-scale sequencing to discover CALR in 2013. For Huey's thesis, Levine anchors two claims she will make: first, that V617F sufficiency in primary progenitors is well-established and does not need to be rebuilt; second, that downstream STAT5/PI3K/ERK output is the canonical signalling axis through which any modifier of JAK2V617F (including an IFIT3-stabilising perturbation like DCAF7 loss) must be read. The paper also codified the JAK2 inhibitor sensitivity paradigm she will implicitly rely on when she justifies the clinical translatability of a DCAF7-directed strategy.

Metadata: Levine RL et al. Activating mutation in the tyrosine kinase JAK2 in polycythemia vera, essential thrombocythemia, and myeloid metaplasia with myelofibrosis. Cancer Cell 2005;7(4):387-97. PMID 15837627. DOI [10.1016/j.ccr.2005.03.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccr.2005.03.023).

---

sections.title: §3B — CALR and MPL — the second wave of drivers

subsections.title: Klampfl et al. 2013, NEJM — whole-exome sequencing
subsections.body:
Between 2005 and 2013, JAK2V617F and MPL W515L/K mutations collectively accounted for ~55-60% of ET and ~55-65% of PMF, leaving a substantial "triple-negative" population without a driver. The Kralovics group (Vienna CeMM, with Klampfl as first author) performed whole-exome sequencing on six patients with JAK2/MPL-wild-type PMF and extended hits into a very large resequencing cohort (1107 MPN samples plus a broad spectrum of other myeloid neoplasms and controls).

Exome sequencing identified a recurrent class of small insertions and deletions in exon 9 of CALR, the gene encoding calreticulin, an ER chaperone. Targeted resequencing revealed 36 distinct insertion/deletion variants, all sharing one feature: a +1-bp frameshift to an alternative reading frame producing a novel, positively charged C-terminal peptide that replaces the normal KDEL-like ER-retention sequence. CALR mutations were entirely absent from PV, mutually exclusive with JAK2 and MPL mutations, and present in 67% of JAK2/MPL-wild-type ET and 88% of JAK2/MPL-wild-type PMF. Functional experiments showed that overexpression of the most frequent CALR deletion (a 52-bp deletion, "type 1") produced cytokine-independent growth of Ba/F3 cells by an unknown mechanism involving STAT5 activation. Clinically, CALR-mutant patients had a lower risk of thrombosis and longer overall survival than JAK2V617F-mutant patients.

Klampfl redrew the MPN driver map overnight: ~60% JAK2, ~25-30% CALR, ~3-5% MPL, ~10% triple-negative. For Huey's thesis, CALR is important in three ways. First, because CALR mutations drive ET/PMF but not PV, any thesis built around DCAF7/IFIT3 dynamics in JAK2V617F should either acknowledge that CALR-driven disease may use a parallel but non-identical signalling wiring, or explicitly test both drivers. Second, CALR-mutant disease also responds (partially) to IFN-α, making IFN-α the rare therapy with activity across driver genotypes — this argues that the IFIT3-centred mechanism Huey studies may generalise beyond V617F. Third, the STAT5-centric downstream signature of CALR-mutants overlaps with the JAK2V617F signature she will use to call "MPN cells" in her scRNA-seq.

Metadata: Klampfl T et al. Somatic mutations of calreticulin in myeloproliferative neoplasms. N Engl J Med 2013;369(25):2379-90. PMID 24325356. DOI [10.1056/NEJMoa1311347](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1311347).

subsections.title: Nangalia et al. 2013, NEJM — independent discovery + phylogenetics
subsections.body:
In the same NEJM issue, the Tony Green lab at Cambridge (with Jyoti Nangalia as first author) reported independent discovery of CALR mutations from a completely different study design. They exome-sequenced 151 MPN samples spanning PV, ET, and PMF, then screened 1345 hematologic cancers, 1517 non-hematologic cancers, and 550 normal controls by targeted resequencing. Mutation burdens were 6.5/6.5/13 per PV/ET/PMF patient, consistent with PMF carrying a heavier co-mutational load. They identified 148 CALR mutations across 19 distinct variants in ~70-84% of JAK2/MPL-wild-type MPN and in 8% of MDS samples, with no mutations in non-hematologic cancers.

The conceptual contribution distinct from Klampfl is the phylogenetic reconstruction. Nangalia and colleagues grew clonal hematopoietic colonies from CALR-mutant patients, performed targeted sequencing of known MPN driver and co-mutated genes across colonies, and built phylogenetic trees. They established that CALR mutations appeared at the earliest phylogenetic node in multiple patients — i.e., CALR is an initiating lesion rather than a secondary event — and that CALR mutations were detectable in HSC/MPP-immunophenotyped cells. They also performed immunofluorescence and flow for calreticulin subcellular localisation, showing mutant CALR retained in the ER without increased surface or Golgi accumulation — prefiguring the Elf 2016/Araki mechanistic work showing that mutant CALR binds MPL intracellularly during its transit through the secretory pathway.

Nangalia is the paper that imports the single-cell/single-colony phylogenetic method into the MPN driver-discovery toolkit; Williams 2022 and Van Egeren 2021 are the natural descendants, applying whole-genome phylogenetics to JAK2V617F-mutant colonies. For Huey, Nangalia is the pedagogical template for how one proves a mutation is an HSC-level initiating lesion — exactly the argument she would need to make about any secondary lesion (including DCAF7 loss) in a composite mutation scenario. It also codifies the concept that driver mutations are acquired early and expand over years to decades, a frame she will return to when defending the long-term stability of any IFN-α-driven HSC depletion.

Metadata: Nangalia J et al. Somatic CALR mutations in myeloproliferative neoplasms with nonmutated JAK2. N Engl J Med 2013;369(25):2391-405. PMID 24325359. DOI [10.1056/NEJMoa1312542](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1312542).

subsections.title: Scott et al. 2007, NEJM — JAK2 exon 12 mutations
subsections.body:
A clinical puzzle persisted after the 2005 papers: ~3-5% of PV patients were V617F-negative but phenotypically indistinguishable from V617F-positive PV, with EECs and suppressed serum Epo. Scott and colleagues (Green lab again) searched for additional activating mutations in JAK and STAT family members by Sanger sequencing in V617F-negative PV and idiopathic erythrocytosis patients. They identified four distinct somatic gain-of-function mutations clustered in exon 12 of JAK2 in 10 V617F-negative patients, the most common being K539L. Biochemistry in Ba/F3-EpoR cells demonstrated cytokine-independent proliferation with increased phospho-JAK2 and phospho-ERK1/2 versus wild-type or V617F JAK2; a murine BMT model of the K539L mutation recapitulated erythrocytosis.

Clinically, patients with exon 12 mutations presented with isolated erythrocytosis (no thrombocytosis or leucocytosis), distinctive bone marrow morphology with prominent erythroid hyperplasia, and often profoundly suppressed Epo. Erythroid colonies grew Epo-independently, and unlike V617F-PV, all exon 12 colonies were heterozygous — homozygosity (uniparental disomy) was never observed, suggesting the mutation provides such a strong kinase-activating signal that homozygosity is incompatible with normal haematopoiesis. This is a rare human-genetic instance of a "too-hot" kinase mutation that cannot tolerate gene-dose doubling.

For Huey's defense, exon 12 matters because it is the canonical counterexample to "V617F explains all PV." A committee member asking about exon 12 is probing whether she understands that PV is a single phenotype with at least two mutational solutions both funnelling through JAK2, and that her DCAF7/IFIT3 mechanism — if truly targeting the JAK2-STAT1/IFN-α axis — should in principle engage exon 12 disease identically. Whether her model includes exon 12 (very few patient samples will carry this) is a reasonable question, and the honest answer is that her mouse model is V617F and exon 12 is a pure human-clinical aside, but she should know it exists.

Metadata: Scott LM et al. JAK2 exon 12 mutations in polycythemia vera and idiopathic erythrocytosis. N Engl J Med 2007;356(5):459-68. PMID 17267906. DOI [10.1056/NEJMoa065202](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa065202).

---

sections.title: §3C — Canonical mouse models of JAK2V617F and CALR

subsections.title: Mullally et al. 2010, Cancer Cell — the floxed JAK2V617F knock-in
subsections.body:
Pre-2010, the field's murine JAK2V617F models were retroviral BMT models (James 2005, Wernig 2006, Lacout 2006) and a few bacterial-artificial-chromosome transgenics (Tiedt 2008, Xing 2008). All suffered from supraphysiological expression, integration artefacts, or mosaic expression. The Ann Mullally paper established the first conditional knock-in in which a floxed minigene cassette in the endogenous murine Jak2 locus is excised by Cre to produce physiological heterozygous Jak2V617F expression from the native promoter — the model that Huey's lab (Melo-Cardenas) and most of the JAK2 field now use.

The allele design places a loxP-flanked stop cassette (or in the Mullally variant, an inverted mutant exon 14 that becomes the expressed copy after Cre recombination) into the endogenous Jak2 locus. Crossed to Mx1-Cre, pIpC injection induces Cre in IFN-responsive compartments including HSCs, converting ~80-95% of haematopoietic cells to heterozygous Jak2V617F expression. The cardinal phenotype: by 4 weeks post-pIpC, mice developed erythrocytosis (Hct ~70%, vs ~45-50% WT), leucocytosis, thrombocytosis, splenomegaly (spleen weights 3-5× WT), Epo-independent BFU-E colonies, and histological features of human PV with progressive reticulin fibrosis. The MPN was lethal within months in a subset.

The mechanistically critical experiments were the competitive transplant and JAK2 inhibitor studies. In 1:1 competitive BMT (CD45.2 Jak2V617F vs CD45.1 WT) into lethally irradiated recipients, Jak2V617F HSCs did not outcompete WT HSCs at the LT-HSC compartment level — strikingly, the disease-initiating capacity resided within the HSC fraction but without a significant selective competitive advantage at the stem cell level. Myeloid progenitors (MEPs, especially erythroid-biased progenitors) were expanded and lineage-skewed, but could not transplant the disease. This dissociation — disease-initiating activity in HSCs, disease-maintaining amplification in progenitors — defined the modern MPN hierarchy. Treatment with a JAK2 kinase inhibitor (TG101348, fedratinib precursor) ameliorated the peripheral blood phenotype and reduced spleen weight but did NOT eliminate the disease-initiating HSC population on secondary transplant.

Mullally 2010 is the single most important paper for defending Huey's experimental system. The model is her model: Mx1-Cre;Jak2V617F/+ mice with pIpC induction. Every phenotypic baseline she quotes (Hct, spleen weight, WBC, reticulin) traces to this paper, and every argument she makes about IFIT3 stabilisation acting on disease-initiating HSCs rather than on mature myeloid cells rests on the hierarchy the Mullally paper established. It is also the paper that taught the field that JAK2 inhibition is insufficient for cure — motivating the combination and eradication strategies (IFN-α, JAK+BET) that frame her thesis.

Metadata: Mullally A et al. Physiological Jak2V617F expression causes a lethal myeloproliferative neoplasm with differential effects on hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells. Cancer Cell 2010;17(6):584-96. PMID 20541703. DOI [10.1016/j.ccr.2010.05.015](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccr.2010.05.015).

subsections.title: Akada et al. 2010, Blood — an alternative knock-in with allele-dose comparison
subsections.body:
Published roughly contemporaneously with Mullally, the Mohi lab (Akada as first author) generated an independent conditional knock-in using a different targeting strategy (a FLEx-inverted mutant exon design) in the endogenous Jak2 locus, crossed to Mx1-Cre. The key feature distinguishing this model from Mullally 2010 is that Akada explicitly compared heterozygous and homozygous Jak2V617F mice head-to-head, resolving the gene-dose question that had been unanswerable in heterozygous-only models.

Heterozygous Jak2V617F animals developed a classical PV-like disease: haemoglobin and haematocrit elevated above WT, leucocytosis, thrombocytosis, splenomegaly, suppressed Epo, and Epo-independent BFU-E. Homozygous Jak2V617F animals developed a more severe disease with significantly greater reticulocytosis, leucocytosis, neutrophilia, thrombocytosis, marked expansion of erythroid progenitors, larger spleens, and accelerated reticulin fibrosis. Biochemically, STAT5, AKT, and ERK phosphorylation scaled with Jak2V617F gene dose, establishing that signal output is dose-dependent and that homozygous V617F drives a phenotype closer to PMF-like fibrotic progression while heterozygous V617F drives a PV-like phenotype. This genetic demonstration in mice directly validates the clinical observation from Kralovics 2005 that homozygous patients have more aggressive disease.

For Huey, Akada is the dose-response reference. If DCAF7 loss amplifies IFIT3 and dampens MPN signalling, the magnitude of that dampening should be interpretable against this signal-dose benchmark — does DCAF7 loss shift a homozygous phenotype toward heterozygous? Toward WT? She will also want to know, and cite, that the Akada model is an alternative allele that some labs use, because a reasonable committee question is "why use Mullally and not Akada?" — the honest answer is historical (the lab had Mullally mice) and practical (broader published comparison set).

Metadata: Akada H et al. Conditional expression of heterozygous or homozygous Jak2V617F from its endogenous promoter induces a polycythemia vera-like disease. Blood 2010;115(17):3589-97. PMID 20197548. DOI [10.1182/blood-2009-04-215848](https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2009-04-215848).

subsections.title: Marty et al. 2016, Blood — CALR knock-in / retroviral model
subsections.body:
Between the 2013 CALR discovery and 2016, the field needed a CALR mouse model. Marty and the Villejuif group (Vainchenker) took the retroviral BMT approach: they transduced lethally irradiated recipient mouse BM with retroviruses expressing either CALRdel52 (type 1), CALRins5 (type 2), wild-type CALR, or a CALR with an in-frame deletion of the entire exon 9 (CALRdelex9) as a control for the mutant C-terminal peptide.

CALRdel52 transduced animals developed thrombocytosis within weeks driven by megakaryocyte hyperplasia, progressing to myelofibrosis, splenomegaly, and osteosclerosis at ~6 months. Disease was serially transplantable into secondary recipients. CALRins5 produced a milder thrombocytosis with less frequent progression to fibrosis, mirroring the clinical observation that type 2 CALR disease is more ET-restricted. CALRdelex9 (which preserves frameshift removal but lacks the novel C-terminus) produced no disease, establishing that the mutant C-terminal peptide is necessary for oncogenicity. Crucially, in c-mpl-null mice (Mpl-KO), the CALR mutants failed to induce thrombocytosis, proving that MPL expression is required in vivo — and in tpo-null mice, thrombocytosis was reduced but not abolished, indicating that TPO contributes to but is not strictly required for disease.

For Huey's thesis, the Marty paper frames the CALR half of MPN biology: the mechanism funnels through MPL, then through JAK2/STAT5/STAT3/STAT1, generating a signalling output that overlaps with V617F at the STAT-family node but differs upstream. The relevance is that any therapy acting distal to MPL/JAK2 — including an IFN-α-amplifying strategy — has a plausible path to efficacy in CALR-mutant MPN. If DCAF7 loss works in V617F through an IFN axis, extending the model to CALR-mutant disease is an obvious aim 3.

Metadata: Marty C et al. Calreticulin mutants in mice induce an MPL-dependent thrombocytosis with frequent progression to myelofibrosis. Blood 2016;127(10):1317-24. PMID 26608331. DOI [10.1182/blood-2015-11-679571](https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2015-11-679571).

subsections.title: Elf et al. 2016, Cancer Discovery — CALR-MPL physical interaction
subsections.body:
The Hobbs/Mullally lab paper from the same period delivered the biochemical mechanism. Elf expressed mutant CALR (del52) in hematopoietic cells and in reconstituted signalling systems, demonstrating that (1) mutant CALR expression alone was sufficient to engender an MPN phenotype in mice; (2) MPL expression was absolutely required — CALR mutants could not transform Mpl-null cells; (3) the oncogenic activity of mutant CALR depended on the positive electrostatic charge of its mutant C-terminal peptide, which had been generated by all known CALR frameshifts via conversion of the acidic KDEL-like native tail into an arginine/lysine-rich novel sequence; and (4) mutant CALR and MPL physically interact during their co-transit through the secretory pathway, with mutant CALR acting as a rogue chaperone that drives constitutive MPL dimerisation and JAK2 activation.

Specific mechanistic details: co-immunoprecipitation showed mutant CALR-MPL association that was absent for wild-type CALR. Mutating the positively charged residues in the mutant C-terminus abolished binding and transformation. JAK-STAT pathway activation (STAT5, STAT3, STAT1 phosphorylation) was ligand- and TPO-independent in CALR-mutant cells, and JAK2 inhibitor treatment abolished the downstream readout — directly supporting the clinical use of ruxolitinib in CALR-mutant myelofibrosis.

For Huey, Elf is the mechanistic endpoint of the CALR story: it closes the loop from Klampfl/Nangalia (CALR mutated → MPN) to Marty (CALR → MPL-dependent in vivo) to Elf (mutant C-term physically engages MPL in the ER). Her DCAF7-IFIT3 work operates downstream of JAK2, so the relevance of the CALR papers is to establish that the JAK2/STAT signalling node — where IFN-α signalling and JAK2V617F signalling intersect — is a shared chokepoint for both driver-mutation classes. This is what makes her mechanism a generalisable MPN strategy rather than a V617F-only oddity.

Metadata: Elf S et al. Mutant Calreticulin Requires Both Its Mutant C-terminus and the Thrombopoietin Receptor for Oncogenic Transformation. Cancer Discov 2016;6(4):368-81. PMID 26951227. DOI [10.1158/2159-8290.CD-15-1434](https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.CD-15-1434).

---

subsections.title: Mullally et al. 2013, Cancer Cell — IFN-α depletes mutant HSCs
subsections.body:
This is the in vivo paper that undergirds the entire IFN-α clinical rationale in MPN at the molecular level. Building on Kiladjian's clinical demonstration that PEG-IFN produces molecular responses in PV patients, Mullally used the same Jak2V617F knock-in model she had published in 2010 to ask whether IFN-α actually clears disease-initiating HSCs in vivo, not just mature progeny.

Mx1-Cre;Jak2V617F/+ mice with established PV-like disease received IFN-α (recombinant pegylated murine IFN-α) at clinically relevant doses for defined treatment durations. Readouts included peripheral blood counts, LSK/LT-HSC immunophenotype and BrdU/Ki67 cell-cycle state by flow, allele burden in LT-HSC versus downstream progenitors by single-cell or sorted-population qPCR, and — most critically — secondary transplant of bone marrow from treated versus untreated donors into lethally irradiated recipients to functionally test disease-propagating activity.

IFN-α treatment induced haematological responses (normalisation of Hct, WBC, platelets) and progressively reduced the Jak2V617F allele burden within the HSC compartment over time. The defining experiment: secondary BMT from IFN-α-treated donors showed impaired ability to transplant MPN disease, demonstrating that mutant MPN-propagating HSCs had been functionally depleted rather than simply silenced. Mechanistically, IFN-α drove mutant LT-HSCs out of quiescence and into the cell cycle, biasing them toward a predetermined erythroid-lineage differentiation programme that depletes the stem pool. This linked IFN-α action to the Essers/Baldridge/Sato axis of IFN-driven HSC cycling, which in the mutant context translates cycling into exhaustion rather than homeostatic self-renewal.

This paper is load-bearing for Huey's thesis. The mechanism she proposes for DCAF7 loss — IFIT3 stabilisation, amplified IFN-α signalling, reduced MPN burden — is effectively a genetic version of the Mullally 2013 pharmacological result. The experimental template (Mx1-Cre;Jak2V617F induction, IFN treatment, serial BMT to test HSC depletion) is exactly the template she must adapt to DCAF7 conditional knockout. A committee member will certainly ask: "How do you distinguish DCAF7 loss from simply being an IFN-α agonist?" — and the honest answer is that her model formally predicts a DCAF7-loss phenocopy of Mullally 2013, which is a feature rather than a bug.

Metadata: Mullally A et al. Depletion of Jak2V617F myeloproliferative neoplasm-propagating stem cells by interferon-α in a murine model of polycythemia vera. Blood 2013;121(18):3692-702. PMID 23487027. DOI [10.1182/blood-2012-05-432989](https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2012-05-432989). (Note: this paper appeared in Blood, not Cancer Cell — the original workflow spec listed it as Cancer Cell; the PubMed record confirms Blood.)

subsections.title: Hasan et al. 2013, Blood — IFN-α eradicates disease-initiating cells
subsections.body:
The Vainchenker/Villeval parallel paper, with Salma Hasan as first author, arrived at a complementary conclusion using an independent knock-in allele (the Villeval lab's Jak2V617F conditional). Rather than emphasising HSC depletion as the endpoint (Mullally), Hasan emphasised the expansion of early haematopoietic compartments (Lin-, LSK, SLAM LSK/CD48-/CD150+) as a function of age in Jak2V617F/+ animals, establishing that V617F amplifies not only mature myeloid cells but also the disease-initiating stem pool — a contribution to the long-running debate about whether V617F confers an HSC-level competitive advantage.

Competitive repopulation from limiting numbers of V617F SLAM cells demonstrated progressive outgrowth of mutant myeloid, Lin-, LSK, and SLAM populations (but not lymphocytes), consistent with an HSC-intrinsic advantage. IFN-α treatment then prevented disease development by specifically inhibiting JAK2V617F-mutant cells at early stages of differentiation and eradicating disease-initiating cells. Cell-cycle analysis showed that IFN-α specifically drove mutant LT-HSCs into cycle (high BrdU incorporation, low apoptosis in untreated mutants that reversed under IFN) while sparing WT HSCs — the same differential vulnerability Mullally 2013 described.

Hasan's interpretation layered in additional mechanistic inference: IFN-α not only depletes mutant HSCs (Mullally) but does so by selectively mobilising them from a proliferative/anti-apoptotic state that V617F establishes, making IFN-α a specific "anti-stem-cell" agent in the V617F context. The two papers together — Mullally in Blood and Hasan in Blood in 2013 — are the diptych every committee member expects Huey to cite together.

For Huey, Hasan adds the predictive angle: baseline ISG/IFN-pathway activity in patients correlates with IFN-α response, and a DCAF7-loss strategy that pre-activates ISGs (including IFIT3) should be testable as a pre-sensitisation strategy. A committee member may ask whether DCAF7 loss would make a patient refractory to further IFN-α treatment (ceiling effect) or synergistic (potentiation) — Hasan provides the framework for that question.

Metadata: Hasan S et al. JAK2V617F expression in mice amplifies early hematopoietic cells and gives them a competitive advantage that is hampered by IFNα. Blood 2013;122(8):1464-77. PMID 23863895. DOI [10.1182/blood-2013-04-498956](https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2013-04-498956).

---

subsections.title: Verstovsek et al. 2012, NEJM — COMFORT-I
subsections.body:
Ruxolitinib (INCB018424), a selective JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor, entered clinical development on the strength of the 2005-2008 JAK2V617F discovery arc. COMFORT-I was the pivotal double-blind, placebo-controlled phase III trial in intermediate-2 and high-risk myelofibrosis, randomising 309 patients 1:1 to twice-daily oral ruxolitinib (155 pts) or placebo (154 pts).

The primary endpoint was ≥35% reduction in spleen volume by MRI at 24 weeks. Ruxolitinib achieved this in 41.9% of patients versus 0.7% on placebo (p<0.001). 67% of responders maintained response for ≥48 weeks. The total symptom score (TSS) improved by ≥50% at 24 weeks in 45.9% of ruxolitinib-treated patients versus 5.3% on placebo (p<0.001), driven by reduction in constitutional symptoms (fatigue, night sweats, pruritus, abdominal discomfort). An overall survival benefit was observed: 13 deaths on ruxolitinib versus 24 on placebo (HR 0.50, 95% CI 0.25-0.98, p=0.04). Adverse events were predominantly haematological: anaemia and thrombocytopenia were common but rarely required discontinuation (one patient each).

The critical caveat, made explicit in COMFORT-I and its companion COMFORT-II (Harrison 2012), is that ruxolitinib does NOT produce meaningful molecular responses — allele burden typically declines only modestly or not at all — and fibrosis does not reliably regress. The drug is cytoreductive and anti-inflammatory rather than disease-eradicating. This gap between symptomatic benefit and biological cure is the negative space Huey's thesis is designed to fill: a DCAF7-loss strategy aims for molecular response (allele burden decline, HSC depletion) rather than spleen-volume control.

Metadata: Verstovsek S et al. A double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of ruxolitinib for myelofibrosis. N Engl J Med 2012;366(9):799-807. PMID 22375971. DOI [10.1056/NEJMoa1110557](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1110557).

subsections.title: Kleppe et al. 2018, Cancer Cell — JAK+BET combination
subsections.body:
(Note: the original workflow spec cited this as Cancer Discovery; PubMed confirms Cancer Cell 2018, Levine lab.) This paper asked why ruxolitinib monotherapy is insufficient, hypothesising that enhancer-level NF-κB-driven inflammatory transcription (distinct from the JAK2-STAT5 axis) is a parallel driver of MPN progenitor survival and bone marrow fibrosis. Using ChIP-seq for H3K27ac across MPN progenitor populations, Kleppe et al. showed that MPN progenitors have differential enhancer utilisation versus WT, with NF-κB targets marked by hyperactive enhancers. JQ1 (BET bromodomain inhibitor) attenuated NF-κB signalling and reduced cytokine production in vivo.

Combination therapy — ruxolitinib plus JQ1 — in Jak2V617F knock-in MPN mice produced a marked reduction in serum inflammatory cytokines, reduced disease burden (spleen, blood counts), and — uniquely — reversed bone marrow reticulin fibrosis, something ruxolitinib alone does not achieve. The mechanism couples JAK2-STAT5 inhibition (disease-cell-autonomous) to BET-inhibition-mediated NF-κB suppression (both malignant and non-malignant niche cells), with the niche contribution being a major inflammatory-cytokine driver that ruxolitinib cannot address alone.

Kleppe 2018 is directly analogous to Huey's thesis: both propose that ruxolitinib's disease-modifying ceiling can be broken by layering on a second, mechanistically distinct therapy. Huey's distinction is that DCAF7 loss provides the "second therapy" without requiring a separate small molecule, and that the second mechanism is cell-intrinsic IFN/ISG amplification rather than NF-κB suppression. A committee member will ask whether DCAF7 loss plus ruxolitinib is synergistic, additive, or redundant — Kleppe 2018 is the methodological template for that combination study.

Metadata: Kleppe M et al. Dual Targeting of Oncogenic Activation and Inflammatory Signaling Increases Therapeutic Efficacy in Myeloproliferative Neoplasms. Cancer Cell 2018;33(1):29-43.e7. PMID 29249691. DOI [10.1016/j.ccell.2017.11.009](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccell.2017.11.009).

subsections.title: Gisslinger et al. 2020, Lancet Haematology — ropegIFN PROUD-PV/CONTINUATION-PV
subsections.body:
PROUD-PV and its extension CONTINUATION-PV were the phase III randomised non-inferiority trials that led to European (2019) and FDA (2021) approval of ropeginterferon alfa-2b (BESREMi) for PV — the first PV-specific, disease-modifying therapy. Ropegifn is a novel mono-pegylated interferon with extended half-life allowing every-2-week subcutaneous dosing (versus weekly PEG-IFN-α-2a).

PROUD-PV randomised 257 early-stage PV patients (no prior cytoreduction, or <3 years hydroxyurea) 1:1 to ropegIFN (starting 100 μg q2w) versus hydroxyurea (starting 500 mg/day). Primary endpoint in PROUD-PV was complete hematological response (CHR) with normal spleen size at 12 months — not met for non-inferiority: 21% (26/122) ropegIFN versus 28% (34/123) hydroxyurea. CONTINUATION-PV followed 171 patients into year 2-3. At 36 months, CHR with improved disease burden was achieved in 53% (50/95) ropegIFN versus 38% (28/74) hydroxyurea (p=0.044). CHR without the spleen criterion was 71% versus 51% at 36 months (p=0.012). Grade 3/4 adverse events were uncommon in both arms; serious treatment-related AEs occurred in 3/127 ropegIFN versus 5/127 hydroxyurea, with one treatment-related death (acute leukaemia) in the hydroxyurea arm. Importantly, ropegIFN produced progressive JAK2V617F molecular response over time (not tabulated here in detail but reported in the paper) — something hydroxyurea does not achieve.

For Huey, Gisslinger 2020 is the clinical proof-of-concept that IFN-α — the pathway her thesis aims to amplify through DCAF7 loss — is a validated disease-modifying therapy in PV. The slow kinetics of ropegIFN response (benefit becomes visible only at 36 months) directly motivates her interest in a mechanism that might amplify IFN signalling cell-intrinsically and accelerate the molecular response. A committee member may ask whether a DCAF7-loss strategy would be additive with ropegIFN or redundant; the answer depends on whether IFIT3 is the rate-limiting ISG in the context of exogenous IFN-α, which is an open testable question in her thesis.

Metadata: Gisslinger H et al. Ropeginterferon alfa-2b versus standard therapy for polycythaemia vera (PROUD-PV and CONTINUATION-PV): a randomised, non-inferiority, phase 3 trial and its extension study. Lancet Haematol 2020;7(3):e196-e208. PMID 32014125. DOI [10.1016/S2352-3026(19)30236-4](https://doi.org/10.1016/S2352-3026(19)30236-4).

---

subsections.title: Williams et al. 2022, Nature — life histories from phylogenies
subsections.body:
Williams and colleagues (Green/Campbell, Sanger/Cambridge) transformed the temporal understanding of MPN by whole-genome sequencing 1,013 clonal hematopoietic colonies from 12 MPN patients, cataloguing 580,133 somatic mutations, and using the mutational-clock burden per colony (somatic mutations accumulate at roughly 17/year per HSC) to build calibrated phylogenetic trees. This allowed them to infer when each driver mutation was acquired in each patient's life history.

Headline numbers: in 5 patients where JAK2V617F was the first driver, the mutation was acquired between 33 weeks of gestation (in utero) and 10.8 years of age. DNMT3A mutations (where present as first driver) were acquired between 8 weeks of gestation and 7.6 years. A PPM1D mutation was acquired by 5.8 years. The mean latency between JAK2V617F acquisition and MPN diagnosis was 30 years (range 11-54 years). Historical clonal expansion rates varied from 3% to 190% per year; rates increased with acquisition of each additional driver mutation and predicted time to diagnosis. Sequential driver acquisition was often separated by decades, with later-acquired drivers outcompeting ancestral clones.

This paper reframes MPN as a disease with a decades-long pre-clinical evolutionary history, often initiated in utero or childhood. The therapeutic implication is that "early intervention" in MPN might begin decades before symptomatic disease, potentially in CHIP-like JAK2V617F clones detected incidentally. For Huey's thesis, this matters because it establishes that the target of therapy is a long-lived HSC clone that has had decades to adapt — so any intervention (including DCAF7 loss + IFN amplification) must be able to reach the quiescent ancestral HSC, not just its differentiating progeny. This is the strongest argument for why cell-intrinsic ISG amplification (IFIT3 stabilisation) rather than systemic IFN-α might be superior: it engages the mutant HSC continuously.

Metadata: Williams N et al. Life histories of myeloproliferative neoplasms inferred from phylogenies. Nature 2022;602(7895):162-168. PMID 35058638. DOI [10.1038/s41586-021-04312-6](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-04312-6).

subsections.title: Van Egeren et al. 2021, Cell Stem Cell — JAK2V617F HSC allele-specific dynamics
subsections.body:
Van Egeren (Camargo/Mehrle/Mullally, Harvard) reconstructed single-HSC lineage histories and differentiation trajectories in treatment-naive MPN patients using somatic mutation patterns in individual hematopoietic colonies. Whole-genome sequencing of hundreds of colonies per patient, combined with mathematical modelling of clonal expansion, quantified the self-renewal and differentiation dynamics of JAK2V617F-mutant HSCs relative to WT HSCs in the same bone marrow.

Key findings in the two deeply studied individuals: the JAK2V617F mutation was acquired decades before diagnosis — at age 9 ± 2 years in a 34-year-old patient and at age 19 ± 3 years in a 63-year-old patient. In both individuals, mutant HSCs exhibited a selective advantage over WT HSCs — a fitness coefficient that the authors quantified — which reframed the Mullally 2010 finding of "no competitive advantage in murine HSC transplants" as a model-system limitation rather than a feature of human disease. Lineage tracing confirmed that differentiation trajectories from mutant HSCs were skewed toward the myeloid/erythroid/megakaryocytic lineage at the expense of lymphoid.

For Huey, Van Egeren plus Williams together establish two critical points for her defense: (1) JAK2V617F is acquired at a single HSC, typically in childhood, and (2) mutant HSCs have a human-confirmed competitive advantage that operates over decades. Any DCAF7-loss strategy must therefore engage the mutant HSC compartment specifically (Mullally 2013 IFN-α template) and must be durable on the decadal timescale over which mutant HSCs normally expand. A committee member will ask how DCAF7 loss engages the HSC versus progenitor pool — the honest, thesis-aligned answer is that IFIT3 stabilisation should act wherever IFN-α signalling matters, which per Mullally 2013 is disproportionately the HSC.

Metadata: Van Egeren D et al. Reconstructing the Lineage Histories and Differentiation Trajectories of Individual Cancer Cells in Myeloproliferative Neoplasms. Cell Stem Cell 2021;28(3):514-523.e9. PMID 33621486. DOI [10.1016/j.stem.2021.02.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.stem.2021.02.001).

---

subsections.title: Interferon Signaling, ISGs, and Hematopoiesis: Deep Gap Analysis: scope & rationale
subsections.body:
Tiers 1–3 of the Study Bible anchored Huey's thesis in the canonical pillars: Platanias's schematic of type I interferon signaling, Schoggins's ISG screens, Diamond's antiviral ISG review, the Essers–Baldridge–Sato–Pietras–Matatall arc on IFN and hematopoietic stem cells, Li's perinatal IFN work, and Payelle-Brogard's USP18/IFNAR2 scaffold finding. Those entries cover the axis at the level a committee expects from a first-year read-down. What they miss — and what a qualifying committee specializing in IFN signaling and the ubiquitin–proteasome system will probe — are the mechanistic and loss-of-function details that turn an axis into a thesis. This supplement fills five specific gaps. First, the IFIT knockout mouse literature: without Pichlmair 2011 and Fensterl 2012, Huey cannot defend the claim that IFIT3 elevation is a meaningful antiviral output of his DCAF7 axis. Second, the βTrCP/SCF destruction complex that terminates IFNAR1 — the UPS limb every IFN-committee chair will ask about, since DCAF7 is itself a substrate-receptor-adjacent WD40 protein. Third, the ISG15/USP18 paradox, which is the best-characterized example in human biology of an ISG whose absence causes more IFN signaling, directly relevant to interpreting DCAF7 loss-of-function experiments. Fourth, the fetal-to-adult HSC switch that Huey's Axis 2 pins to P4 DCAF7 requirement — Bowie 2007 and Copley 2013 define when and how this switch happens. Fifth, the cGAS-STING literature on micronuclei, because JAK2V617F drives replication stress and chromosomal instability, and any modern IFN-in-MPN story must explain the nucleic-acid trigger. Two additional entries — Stark & Darnell's JAK-STAT retrospective and Daffis 2010 on 2′-O-methylation — close the host-pathogen loop that IFIT1 sits inside. Every paper below is outside Tiers 1–3, every PMID is verified against PubMed metadata, and each writeup runs ~500–700 words of dense prose with specific doses, strains, readouts, and numeric findings.

---

subsections.title: Pichlmair et al. 2011, Nature Immunology — Ifit1-/- mouse and 5′-ppp RNA recognition
subsections.body:
Type I interferon induces hundreds of ISGs, but in 2011 the molecular function of the IFIT family — the most strongly induced cytosolic ISGs by fold-change in almost every transcriptome — remained circumstantial. The Superti-Furga lab had shown in 2010 that IFIT proteins assemble a multi-protein complex scaffolding antiviral translation control, but no direct ligand had been assigned and no knockout had been challenged with live virus. The field needed a biochemical assignment plus a genetic loss-of-function that tied IFIT1 to a specific RNA species produced by specific viruses. Pichlmair et al. 2011 is that paper, and it is the paper Huey was explicitly told to read.

The experimental approach combined three orthogonal methods. First, affinity purification from IFN-β-treated HEK293 cells followed by RNA-bound mass spectrometry identified IFIT1 as the dominant cytosolic protein pulled down by 5′-triphosphate (5′-ppp) single-stranded RNA, the uncapped signature generated by the replication intermediates of negative-strand RNA viruses. Competition with 5′-OH or 5′-capped RNA abolished the interaction. Crystallography, added in companion follow-up work and referenced in the paper, showed IFIT1 contains a tetratricopeptide-repeat (TPR) basket that directly contacts the triphosphate moiety. Second, the authors generated Ifit1-/- mice on the C57BL/6 background by targeted disruption of exon 1. Third, they challenged Ifit1-/- animals and wild-type littermates with three viruses chosen to probe 5′-ppp dependence: vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV, Indiana strain, 10⁴ PFU intravenously), Rift Valley Fever virus (RVFV clone 13, a live attenuated segmented negative-strand bunyavirus), and influenza A virus (PR8, 10³ PFU intranasally).

The key findings were strain-specific. Ifit1-/- mice were markedly more susceptible to VSV, with viral titers in brain and spleen elevated 10- to 100-fold by day 3 post-infection and accelerated mortality (median survival shortened from >10 days to ~5 days at 10⁴ PFU). Susceptibility to RVFV clone 13 was similarly increased, with higher hepatic titers and elevated serum ALT. By contrast, influenza A replication was essentially unaltered in Ifit1-/- animals — consistent with the fact that influenza caps its mRNAs by cap-snatching and therefore does not expose a 5′-ppp ligand for IFIT1 recognition. The authors complemented the in vivo data with Ifit1-/- MEFs, which showed a 5- to 20-fold increase in VSV replication at MOI 0.1 measured by plaque assay at 24 h. Biochemically, they showed that recombinant IFIT1 binds 5′-ppp RNA with low-nanomolar affinity and sequesters it from the translation machinery, explaining the antiviral mechanism as translational restriction of uncapped viral transcripts.

The field impact was threefold. Pichlmair 2011 converted IFIT1 from "most-upregulated ISG" to a defined pattern-recognition effector, completed the innate immune logic in which RIG-I senses 5′-ppp to trigger IFN and IFIT1 then sequesters 5′-ppp inside the IFN response, and established the species-selectivity principle: which virus you restrict depends on which IFIT family member you have and whether the virus exposes a 5′-ppp. It also provided the first IFIT-family knockout phenotype in a mammal, making it the reference study any subsequent IFIT paper (including any future Ifit3-/- work) must cite.

For Huey's thesis, this paper is load-bearing. His DCAF7 → IFIT3 axis predicts that loss of DCAF7 raises IFIT3 protein levels and therefore phenocopies, in part, an "IFN-primed" state at the post-translational layer without requiring ligand. The committee will ask what IFIT3 actually does downstream — Pichlmair's framework says IFIT family members bind specific RNA species and restrict specific viruses, and IFIT3 is the scaffold that recruits IFIT1 to MAVS and amplifies type I IFN induction. The logical consequence — that DCAF7 loss in JAK2V617F cells should elevate IFIT3 scaffolding activity and thereby amplify downstream IFN-α output — is the direct prediction Huey's thesis makes. Without Pichlmair 2011 in the defense, there is no mechanistic floor under the word "IFIT3."

Metadata: Pichlmair A, Lassnig C, Eberle C-A, Górna MW, Baumann CL, Burkard TR, Búrckstummer T, Stefanovic A, Krieger S, Bennett KL, Rülicke T, Weber F, Colinge J, Müller M, Superti-Furga G. IFIT1 is an antiviral protein that recognizes 5′-triphosphate RNA. Nat Immunol. 2011 Jul;12(7):624–630. PMID: 21642987.

---

subsections.title: Fensterl et al. 2012, PLoS Pathogens — Ifit2-/- and VSV neurotropism
subsections.body:
Where Pichlmair 2011 established IFIT1 as a 5′-ppp sensor in systemic antiviral immunity, the Sen laboratory at Cleveland Clinic focused on tissue-specific IFIT function, particularly in the central nervous system where IFN responses are tightly compartmentalized and neurons are exceptionally IFIT-responsive. The gap that Fensterl 2012 filled was whether Ifit2 — the closest paralog of Ifit1 in the mouse cluster on chromosome 19 — had a non-redundant in vivo role, and specifically whether IFIT2 controlled viral neuroinvasion rather than peripheral replication. This distinction matters because several ISGs are redundant systemically but non-redundant in specific organs, and for the DCAF7 → IFIT3 axis the tissue specificity of IFIT family phenotypes is a direct analog.

The approach used Ifit2-/- mice generated by targeted gene replacement at the Sen lab, backcrossed to C57BL/6. The authors infected 5- to 6-week-old Ifit2-/- and wild-type littermates intranasally with VSV Indiana (10⁶ PFU in 25 μL), a dose that produces olfactory-tract-limited infection in wild-type mice and allows measurement of neuroinvasion and CNS spread. Readouts included survival curves over 21 days, brain viral titer by plaque assay on Vero cells, viral antigen immunohistochemistry in serial sagittal brain sections, and quantitative RT-PCR for VSV-N, IFN-β, and a panel of ISGs in olfactory bulb, cortex, brainstem, and spinal cord. Primary neuronal cultures from Ifit2-/- newborns were infected at MOI 0.01 and 0.1 to dissect whether the phenotype was neuron-intrinsic.

The numerical findings were stark. Wild-type mice uniformly survived intranasal VSV at 10⁶ PFU; Ifit2-/- mice showed ~80% mortality by day 8 with severe hindlimb paralysis preceding death. Brain viral titers in Ifit2-/- animals were elevated 100- to 1000-fold by day 5 post-infection, concentrated in brainstem and spinal cord — regions only minimally infected in wild-type animals. Olfactory bulb titers were comparable between genotypes, establishing that IFIT2 was dispensable for primary viral entry and replication at the portal of infection but essential for restricting caudal CNS spread. In primary cortical neuron cultures, Ifit2-/- cells supported 10- to 50-fold higher VSV yield at 24 h compared with wild-type at MOI 0.1, and this difference was abolished by reconstitution with a Flag-tagged IFIT2 transgene. The authors further showed that IFN-β pretreatment failed to protect Ifit2-/- neurons from VSV, while it fully protected wild-type neurons, establishing IFIT2 as a rate-limiting antiviral effector within the neuronal IFN program.

The mechanistic model proposed was that IFIT2, like IFIT1, binds viral RNA species bearing non-self 5′ ends and sequesters them from translation, but does so in a cell-type-restricted manner — neurons express IFIT2 at particularly high levels after IFN stimulation, and their relatively muted expression of redundant antiviral factors leaves them dependent on IFIT2 for cap-methylation-independent restriction. Field impact: Fensterl 2012 established the principle that IFIT paralogs are non-redundant in tissue-specific contexts, anticipated that Ifit3-/- animals would likely have their own restricted phenotype, and provided the second mammalian IFIT-family knockout after Pichlmair. It is cited in every subsequent IFIT-mouse paper.

A dedicated Ifit3-/- mouse phenotype paper from the Sen lab could not be located in PubMed as of 2026; the Sen group's Ifit3 work has been biochemical (IFIT3-IFIT1 heterodimer stabilizing 5′-ppp recognition, MAVS scaffolding). This is a real gap in the literature and one Huey should name explicitly in the defense: the Ifit3-/- mouse phenotype has not been formally published, which makes his planned Ifit3 manipulations in JAK2V617F mice an opportunity to establish that phenotype in a hematopoietic rather than viral context.

For the thesis, Fensterl 2012 supplies the tissue-specificity logic: loss of a single IFIT paralog can produce a lethal phenotype in one compartment (CNS) while being invisible in others. If DCAF7 loss elevates IFIT3 in MPN progenitors, the consequence may be strikingly compartment-specific — visible in stem/progenitor compartments but invisible in mature neutrophils — and this paper is the template for how to argue that phenotype.

Metadata: Fensterl V, Wetzel JL, Ramachandran S, Ogino T, Stohlman SA, Bergmann CC, Diamond MS, Virgin HW, Sen GC. Interferon-induced Ifit2/ISG54 protects mice from lethal VSV neuropathogenesis. PLoS Pathog. 2012;8(5):e1002712. PMID: 22615570.

subsections.title: Note on an Ifit3-/- paper
subsections.body:
Despite careful searching through the Sen laboratory publication record (PMIDs 22615570, 24991014, 25231314 Sendai-Ifit2, and several biochemical IFIT3 papers), no dedicated Ifit3-/- mouse-phenotype primary paper appears in PubMed through the current cutoff. The closest is Wetzel et al. 2014 (J Virol, PMID 25231314) which examined Ifit2-/- in Sendai virus pathogenesis. IFIT3's in vivo role has been inferred from biochemistry (heterodimerization with IFIT1 to stabilize 5′-ppp binding; scaffolding MAVS-TBK1) rather than from a knockout challenge study. Huey should list this explicitly as an unmet literature need and a motivation for his planned hematopoietic Ifit3 manipulations.

---

subsections.title: Kumar et al. 2007, Journal of Cell Biology — ubiquitin-primed endocytic motif for IFNAR1
subsections.body:
The Fuchs laboratory at the University of Pennsylvania has spent two decades dissecting how IFNAR1 is degraded, because IFNAR1 turnover is the single dominant mechanism by which type I IFN signaling is shut off. Before Kumar 2007 the broad outline was known: IFN engagement triggers phosphorylation of IFNAR1 on a serine-rich degron (Ser535 in human, Ser526 in mouse), recruitment of the SCF^βTrCP E3 ubiquitin ligase, polyubiquitination, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, and lysosomal degradation. What was missing was a mechanistic answer to how ubiquitin actually drove endocytosis — whether ubiquitin was just a timer or whether it actively exposed or created the endocytic signal.

Kumar et al. 2007 (J Cell Biol, PMID 18056411) addressed this with a series of mutagenesis, pull-down, and endocytosis kinetic experiments in human cell lines reconstituted with IFNAR1 variants. They identified a linear tyrosine-based endocytic motif in the IFNAR1 intracellular tail that was normally masked in the unstimulated receptor. Upon IFN-α stimulation and SCF^βTrCP-dependent K48 polyubiquitination at Lys501 and two neighboring lysines, a conformational rearrangement exposed the linear motif for μ2-AP2 adaptor binding, nucleating clathrin coat assembly. Alanine substitution of the tyrosine motif abolished endocytosis despite intact ubiquitination; lysine-to-arginine substitutions at the ubiquitination sites preserved the motif but abolished its exposure. The kinetic readout used surface biotinylation followed by pulse-chase stripping: wild-type IFNAR1 had a t½ on the surface of ~20 minutes after IFN-α addition, motif mutants had t½ >3 hours, and ubiquitination-site mutants had t½ >4 hours — establishing that ubiquitin was necessary but not sufficient, and that the linear motif was the actual endocytic signal.

The key numerical findings included: a 4- to 6-fold increase in steady-state IFNAR1 levels in βTrCP-knockdown cells, a 3-fold prolongation of STAT1 phosphorylation following IFN-α2b (1000 U/mL) in motif-mutant reconstituted cells, and a ~10-fold increase in antiviral activity (as measured by VSV protection assay in U5A cells reconstituted with IFNAR1 variants) for the endocytosis-defective mutant. The authors further showed that priming phosphorylation at Ser535 by casein kinase 2 preceded the βTrCP recruitment, integrating the UPS step into a kinase cascade.

Field impact: Kumar 2007 is the paper that established the "ubiquitin-unmasks-endocytic-signal" model for a cytokine receptor, a mechanism subsequently found to apply to other receptor tyrosine and cytokine receptors. It made IFNAR1 degradation the textbook case of how the UPS directly sculpts cytokine signaling duration, not merely amplitude, and made βTrCP the canonical target for pharmacologic IFN potentiation.

For Huey's thesis this paper is the UPS anchor. The committee will ask, "how does the ubiquitin system regulate type I IFN signaling?" — Kumar 2007 is the cleanest single-paper answer for the receptor limb. More importantly, the DCAF7 hypothesis is a parallel UPS mechanism: where βTrCP/SCF terminates IFNAR1, DCAF7/CUL4 is proposed to terminate IFIT3. Both are F-box/DCAF adaptor-based Cullin-RING ligase pairs. Huey should be able to draw the mechanistic analogy cold on the whiteboard: substrate receptor → Cullin scaffold → RING → E2 → K48 polyubiquitin → proteasomal (IFIT3) or lysosomal (IFNAR1) degradation. If the committee asks, "is your system just a re-run of βTrCP–IFNAR1?", Huey's answer is that DCAF7–IFIT3 targets the ISG output rather than the receptor input, and therefore tunes the transcriptional amplitude rather than the signaling duration.

Metadata: Kumar KGS, Barriere H, Carbone CJ, Liu J, Swaminathan G, Xu P, Li Y, Baker DP, Peng J, Lukacs GL, Fuchs SY. Site-specific ubiquitination exposes a linear motif to promote interferon-α receptor endocytosis. J Cell Biol. 2007 Dec 3;179(5):935–950. PMID: 18056411.

---

subsections.title: Liu, Fuchs et al. 2009 — casein kinase 1α and ligand-independent IFNAR1 turnover
subsections.body:
Kumar 2007 established the ligand-dependent βTrCP destruction pathway for IFNAR1, but clinicians and biochemists had long noted that IFNAR1 levels also decline under cellular stress — oxidative stress, ER stress, unfolded protein response, viral infection with agents that do not themselves trigger IFN — in a way that blunts subsequent IFN responsiveness. The question was whether this stress-induced IFNAR1 decline used the same βTrCP machinery or a separate kinase. Liu, Fuchs and colleagues (Mol Cell Biol 2009, PMID 19805514) answered this by identifying casein kinase 1α (CK1α) as the ligand-independent priming kinase that drives IFNAR1 phosphorylation and βTrCP recruitment in the absence of IFN, during cellular stress.

The experimental setup used human NHDF fibroblasts and U5A cells reconstituted with IFNAR1, treated with a panel of stressors (thapsigargin 1 μM for ER stress, hydrogen peroxide 200 μM for oxidative stress, hepatitis C virus replicon induction) and compared with IFN-α2b at 1000 U/mL. siRNA knockdown of CK1α (three independent siRNAs, 70–90% knockdown efficiency) versus CK2 and casein kinase 1ε allowed dissection of which kinase drove which trigger. Readouts were IFNAR1 surface biotinylation decay, phospho-Ser535 specific immunoblot, ubiquitination by βTrCP immunoprecipitation, and functional downstream STAT1 phosphorylation in response to a subsequent IFN pulse.

The findings defined two kinase modalities. CK2 drove ligand-dependent IFNAR1 phosphorylation (IFN-α triggers CK2 via TYK2-dependent mechanisms described earlier in the Fuchs papers), while CK1α drove ligand-independent, stress-triggered phosphorylation. Thapsigargin produced a 60–70% decline in surface IFNAR1 at 4 hours in wild-type cells, entirely blocked by CK1α knockdown; IFN-α triggered the same magnitude of decline unaltered by CK1α knockdown. Functionally, prior thapsigargin exposure desensitized cells to subsequent IFN-α (50% reduction in STAT1 phosphorylation and 70% reduction in ISG induction at matched doses), and this desensitization was rescued by CK1α knockdown. The authors further showed that leishmanial CK1α ortholog (L-CK1α) expression during Leishmania major infection phenocopied stress-induced IFNAR1 loss, providing a pathogen-driven precedent.

Field impact: This paper established a two-kinase model for IFNAR1 turnover (CK2 = ligand, CK1α = stress), both converging on βTrCP, and explained why chronic inflammation or viral infection often leaves cells refractory to therapeutic IFN — CK1α-driven IFNAR1 loss precedes the IFN challenge. It is the foundational reference for the "IFN desensitization" phenomenon clinicians encounter in chronic hepatitis and cancer immunotherapy.

For Huey's thesis, this paper is critical for two reasons. First, MPN is a chronic inflammatory state, and JAK2V617F cells experience sustained oxidative and replication stress — a setting in which CK1α-driven IFNAR1 loss would be expected to blunt endogenous IFN responses. Second, the Melo-Cardenas lab observation that DCAF7 loss elevates IFIT3 and amplifies IFN-α output has to be interpreted against this desensitization background: if DCAF7-depleted cells simultaneously experience ER/oxidative stress, the amplitude of the IFN boost depends on how much IFNAR1 survives. This paper anticipates and predicts the experimental controls Huey will need.

Metadata: Liu J, HuangFu W-C, Kumar KGS, Qian J, Casey JP, Hamanaka RB, Grigoriadou C, Aldabe R, Diehl JA, Fuchs SY. Mammalian casein kinase 1α and its leishmanial ortholog regulate stability of IFNAR1 and type I interferon signaling. Mol Cell Biol. 2009 Dec;29(24):6401–6412. PMID: 19805514.

---

subsections.title: Ivashkiv & Donlin 2014, Nature Reviews Immunology — comprehensive tuning of type I IFN
subsections.body:
Type I IFN is one of the most context-dependent cytokines in mammalian biology: the same ligand produces antiviral immunity at one dose, autoimmunity at another, HSC exhaustion in one compartment, and tolerance in another. By 2014 this heterogeneity had reached a point where the field needed a single authoritative synthesis of the regulatory layers — receptor turnover, negative feedback by USP18 and SOCS, STAT heterodimer composition, chromatin state, crosstalk with other cytokine pathways — that together determine the sign and magnitude of an IFN response in any given cell. Ivashkiv and Donlin 2014 (Nat Rev Immunol, PMID 24362405) is that synthesis, and it is the review a committee expects the candidate to have internalized.

The review organizes IFN tuning into five layers. Layer 1, ligand diversity: 17 human type I IFN genes (13 IFN-α subtypes, IFN-β, IFN-ε, IFN-κ, IFN-ω) differ in IFNAR2-binding affinity by >100-fold and in IFNAR1 recruitment kinetics, which determines whether a ternary complex dwell-time is sufficient to trigger STAT1-STAT2 (antiviral) or STAT1-STAT1 homodimer (pro-inflammatory) signaling. Layer 2, receptor-proximal regulation: IFNAR1 βTrCP-mediated turnover (Kumar 2007), CK1α-driven stress desensitization (Liu 2009), and USP18 competitive displacement of JAK1 from IFNAR2 (Malakhov 2003, Francois-Newton 2011). Layer 3, transcription factor usage: ISGF3 (STAT1-STAT2-IRF9) drives ISRE-containing antiviral genes, STAT1 homodimers drive GAS-containing pro-inflammatory genes, and STAT3 activation biases toward anti-inflammatory/wound-healing outputs. Layer 4, chromatin state: pre-existing H3K4me1 at ISG enhancers allows rapid IFN response, while repressive H3K27me3 at GAS elements blocks pro-inflammatory conversion. Layer 5, cell-type- and context-specific crosstalk: TGF-β, IL-10, glucocorticoids, and TNF all modify ISG induction magnitude and duration, sometimes by regulating USP18.

The review also codifies the "tonic IFN" concept — the idea that low baseline IFN signaling (from commensal microbiota, endogenous retroelements, or micronuclei) sets the STAT1 and ISGF3 levels required for rapid pathogen responses. Loss of tonic IFN desensitizes cells (IFNAR1-/- phenotypes in homeostasis), while excess tonic IFN produces HSC exhaustion, autoimmunity, and interferonopathy. The authors integrate Essers 2009, Baldridge 2010, Sato 2009, and early Matatall data into this tonic-IFN framework.

Field impact: This is the single most-cited modern review of type I IFN regulation. It is the background the committee will assume the candidate has read. For Huey's thesis it is essential for three reasons. First, it provides the vocabulary (tonic IFN, ISGF3 vs STAT1 homodimer bias, receptor-proximal vs transcriptional tuning) to describe where DCAF7 → IFIT3 sits in the regulatory hierarchy. Second, it makes the argument that the interesting therapeutic question is not "more or less IFN" but "which flavor of IFN output" — and IFIT3 is an ISGF3-output ISG, so DCAF7-mediated tuning of IFIT3 selectively amplifies the antiviral/ISGF3 branch without necessarily driving STAT1-homodimer inflammation. Third, it anticipates objections: when a committee member asks "isn't this just redundant with USP18?", Huey's answer is that USP18 tunes at the receptor-proximal layer (2) while DCAF7 tunes at the effector-protein layer — a different regulatory tier with different kinetics and selectivity.

Metadata: Ivashkiv LB, Donlin LT. Regulation of type I interferon responses. Nat Rev Immunol. 2014 Jan;14(1):36–49. PMID: 24362405.

---

subsections.title: Bogunovic et al. 2012, Science — inherited human ISG15 deficiency
subsections.body:
ISG15 is one of the most strongly induced ISGs and had been characterized biochemically as a ubiquitin-like modifier conjugated to hundreds of substrates through an E1 (UBE1L) – E2 (UBCH8) – E3 (HERC5) cascade. In mouse knockouts, Isg15-/- animals showed modest susceptibility to certain viruses (influenza, Sindbis) and had been interpreted as a dispensable antiviral effector. Then, between 2011 and 2012, the Casanova laboratory at Rockefeller identified two consanguineous families with homozygous loss-of-function mutations in ISG15, and the human phenotype was not what anyone expected.

Bogunovic et al. 2012 (Science, PMID 22859821) reported three patients from two unrelated families with homozygous ISG15 null alleles (c.163C>T/p.R55* and a frameshift allele), identified by whole-exome sequencing of children with severe mycobacterial disease — disseminated BCG-osis after vaccination and atypical mycobacterial infections. The clinical phenotype resembled Mendelian susceptibility to mycobacterial disease (MSMD), traditionally caused by IFN-γ pathway defects. Cellular analysis showed absent free and conjugated ISG15 by immunoblot, and functional assay of patient PBMCs revealed blunted IFN-γ production by NK and T cells in response to mycobacterial antigens — explaining the mycobacterial susceptibility. Simultaneously, however, the patients had elevated baseline expression of a large panel of ISGs and elevated STAT1 phosphorylation in response to IFN-α2b (2-fold and 3-fold respectively compared with healthy controls at matched doses). This was the paradox: loss of an ISG produced more IFN signaling, not less.

The authors performed co-immunoprecipitation and cycloheximide chase experiments in patient fibroblasts reconstituted with wild-type or catalytically dead ISG15. USP18 — the type I IFN-specific negative feedback regulator that competitively displaces JAK1 from IFNAR2 — was destabilized in ISG15-null cells, with a protein half-life reduced from >8 hours to ~2 hours by cycloheximide chase. Reconstitution with conjugation-incompetent ISG15 (LRGG→LRAA mutant) still rescued USP18 stabilization, establishing that free ISG15, not ISGylation, was the critical modality. Mechanistically, free ISG15 binds USP18 non-covalently and protects it from SKP2-mediated proteasomal degradation. The functional consequence: ISG15-/- cells have no USP18 brake, hyperactivate type I IFN signaling, and produce the clinical picture of autoinflammation with paradoxical ISG elevation.

Field impact: this paper recast ISG15 from "ubiquitin-like antiviral modifier" to "stabilizer of the type I IFN brake," explained why Isg15-/- mice had a mild phenotype (species differences in USP18 regulation), and opened a new category of interferonopathy driven by loss of a negative feedback regulator. It also established human patient immunology as a source of unexpected mechanistic insight that mouse knockouts had missed.

For Huey's thesis this paper is a direct analog and a direct teaching case. The DCAF7 → IFIT3 axis predicts that loss of DCAF7 elevates IFIT3 protein and amplifies downstream IFN-α — a mechanism in which a UPS component (DCAF7) normally dampens the IFN response, and loss produces hyper-IFN. The Bogunovic 2012 paradigm shows that this is not merely possible but is precisely how a clinically important interferonopathy can be organized. If a committee member asks, "what is the precedent for a ubiquitin-system component being a negative regulator of IFN whose loss causes hyper-IFN?", the answer is USP18/ISG15, and Huey should be able to draw the parallel on demand.

Metadata: Bogunovic D, Byun M, Durfee LA, Abhyankar A, Sanal O, Mansouri D, Salem S, Radovanovic I, Grant AV, Adimi P, Mansouri N, Okada S, Bryant VL, Kong X-F, Kreins A, Velez MM, Boisson B, Khalilzadeh S, Ozcelik U, Darazam IA, Schoggins JW, Rice CM, Al-Muhsen S, Behr M, Vogt G, Puel A, Bustamante J, Gros P, Huibregtse JM, Abel L, Boisson-Dupuis S, Casanova J-L. Mycobacterial disease and impaired IFN-γ immunity in humans with inherited ISG15 deficiency. Science. 2012 Sep 28;337(6102):1684–1688. PMID: 22859821.

---

subsections.title: Zhang, Bogunovic et al. 2015, Nature — mechanism of ISG15-mediated USP18 stabilization
subsections.body:
Three years after the clinical description, the Casanova group and collaborators returned to dissect the mechanistic basis of ISG15 stabilization of USP18 and to define the molecular identity of the auto-inflammatory interferonopathy seen in ISG15-null patients. The question was no longer "does ISG15 regulate USP18?" but "how, and what are the therapeutic implications?"

Zhang et al. 2015 (Nature, PMID 25307056) used a combination of patient-derived fibroblasts (the original Bogunovic families plus two additional pedigrees identified through the Casanova consortium, total five patients), shRNA-mediated ISG15 knockdown in HEK293 and U5A cells, and structural co-crystallization of human ISG15 with USP18 residues 1–368. The biochemical readouts were: USP18 half-life by cycloheximide chase, ubiquitination of USP18 by in vivo TUBE pulldown, STAT2 phosphorylation kinetics after IFN-α2b (1000 U/mL) pulse and withdrawal, and ISG transcription by RNA-seq at 6 and 24 h post-IFN.

The findings established a quantitative model. Free ISG15 binds USP18 through hydrophobic contacts between ISG15 residues 23–42 and USP18 residues 312–332, with a Kd of ~500 nM by isothermal titration calorimetry. This interaction prevents SKP2 (the F-box protein of the SCF^SKP2 complex) from engaging USP18, which would otherwise mediate K48-polyubiquitination on USP18 K101, K141, K270. In ISG15-null cells, USP18 protein was 10- to 20-fold lower despite unaltered USP18 mRNA, STAT2 phosphorylation persisted for >48 h after a 2-h IFN-α pulse (versus return to baseline by 12 h in wild-type), and RNA-seq showed chronic elevation of ISGs including IFIT1, IFIT2, IFIT3, OAS1, MX1, ISG15 itself (a feedforward loop — IFN induces ISG15 which stabilizes USP18 which then brakes IFN), with a 5- to 15-fold elevation in steady state. Patients exhibited elevated type I IFN signatures in whole blood transcriptomes, cerebral calcifications on CT imaging, and autoinflammatory manifestations — clinically overlapping with type I interferonopathies such as Aicardi-Goutières syndrome.

Functionally, reconstitution of patient fibroblasts with conjugation-defective ISG15 (LRGG→LRAA) fully rescued USP18 stabilization and normalized IFN signaling duration, confirming that non-covalent free ISG15, not ISGylation, is the relevant activity. The authors further showed that treatment of patient cells with JAK1/2 inhibitor ruxolitinib normalized ISG expression, suggesting a therapeutic path.

Field impact: Zhang 2015 closed the ISG15–USP18 loop structurally and placed the axis as the mechanistic basis of a novel human interferonopathy. It also made the therapeutic point that JAK inhibition — the MPN-defining class — can treat loss-of-negative-feedback IFN disease, which ties directly to Huey's JAK2V617F system.

For Huey's thesis this paper is the mechanistic deep-dive that pairs with the Bogunovic 2012 clinical description. The committee will ask, "how does a non-enzymatic protein-protein interaction stabilize a UPS substrate?" The Zhang 2015 structure and binding assays are the reference answer. More importantly, the DCAF7/IFIT3 hypothesis implies a similar logic at the effector-protein layer: DCAF7 destabilizes IFIT3, loss of DCAF7 elevates IFIT3, elevated IFIT3 amplifies IFN output. Huey should be able to articulate how his axis is mechanistically analogous (UPS adaptor controls ISG abundance) but functionally inverse (DCAF7 loss augments IFN rather than sustains it through loss of a brake) to the ISG15/USP18 precedent.

Metadata: Zhang X, Bogunovic D, Payelle-Brogard B, Francois-Newton V, Speer SD, Yuan C, Volpi S, Li Z, Sanal O, Mansouri D, Tezcan I, Rice GI, Chen C, Mansouri N, Mahdaviani SA, Itan Y, Boisson B, Okada S, Zeng L, Wang X, Jiang H, Liu W, Han T, Liu D, Ma T, Wang B, Liu M, Liu J-Y, Wang QK, Yalnizoglu D, Radoshevich L, Uzé G, Gros P, Rozenberg F, Zhang S-Y, Jouanguy E, Bustamante J, García-Sastre A, Abel L, Lebon P, Notarangelo LD, Crow YJ, Boisson-Dupuis S, Casanova J-L, Pellegrini S. Human intracellular ISG15 prevents interferon-α/β over-amplification and auto-inflammation. Nature. 2015 Jan 1;517(7532):89–93. PMID: 25307056.

---

subsections.title: François-Newton et al. 2011, PLoS One — USP18 catalytic vs scaffold function
subsections.body:
Payelle-Brogard 2013 (Tier 2) established that USP18 antagonizes IFNAR2–JAK1 interaction at the receptor-proximal step. François-Newton et al. 2011 (PLoS One, PMID 21779393) is the earlier biochemical paper that first separated the catalytic (isopeptidase, deISGylating) activity of USP18 from its signaling-antagonist activity, and showed that type I and type III IFNs induce USP18 with different kinetics and subtype-specificity.

The approach used Huh7.5 and A549 cells treated with type I IFNs at matched antiviral units — IFN-α2, IFN-α8, IFN-β, IFN-ω — and type III IFN-λ1, IFN-λ3 at saturating doses (1000 pM or equivalent). Readouts included STAT1 and STAT2 phosphorylation time courses (0, 15 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 24 h post-IFN), ISG transcriptome by microarray, and USP18 protein induction. A catalytically dead USP18 (C64S, which abolishes isopeptidase activity) was introduced into USP18-knockdown cells.

The findings dissociated function. IFN-β induced STAT1/STAT2 phosphorylation that peaked at 30 min and declined by 4 h; IFN-α2 induced a similar peak but faster decline with stronger USP18 feedback; IFN-α8 produced intermediate kinetics. IFN-λ induced USP18 only weakly and therefore sustained STAT phosphorylation for >24 h — explaining why type III IFN has different in vivo kinetics. Reconstitution with C64S USP18 fully rescued the antagonism of type I IFN signaling, showing that the desensitization was isopeptidase-independent and mediated by USP18 binding to IFNAR2 (anticipating the Payelle-Brogard 2013 structural finding). Quantitatively, USP18-knockdown cells showed 3- to 5-fold elevated antiviral protection against VSV-GFP at low IFN-α doses and persistent STAT2 phosphorylation at 24 h.

Field impact: this paper established the "USP18 as scaffold, not protease" model, the subtype-selective feedback logic among type I IFNs, and the type-I-vs-type-III kinetic distinction. It is the biochemical basis for the therapeutic claim that type III IFN may be preferable for chronic antiviral therapy.

For Huey's thesis the François-Newton 2011 paper supplies a second UPS-adjacent precedent where a "DUB" acts through protein-protein interaction rather than catalysis — the exact non-enzymatic regulatory mechanism invoked in the ISG15/USP18/IFNAR2 triangle and relevant by analogy to whether DCAF7 acts catalytically (through its CUL4 scaffold) or primarily as a non-enzymatic IFIT3 chaperone. This is a likely committee question and worth being prepared for.

Metadata: François-Newton V, Magno de Freitas Almeida G, Payelle-Brogard B, Monneron D, Pichard-Garcia L, Piehler J, Pellegrini S, Uzé G. USP18-based negative feedback control is induced by type I and type III interferons and specifically inactivates interferon α response. PLoS One. 2011;6(7):e22200. PMID: 21779393.

---

subsections.title: Bowie et al. 2007, PNAS — an intrinsically timed developmental checkpoint at 3–4 weeks postnatally
subsections.body:
Huey's Axis 2 pins DCAF7's non-redundant requirement to P4 HSCs and argues that DCAF7 is essential for the fetal-to-adult HSC transition. This claim rests on the developmental-biology finding that HSCs undergo a programmed switch in self-renewal, cycling, and transcriptional state between approximately 3 and 4 weeks of age in the mouse. Bowie et al. 2007 (PNAS, PMID 17379664; note that the original source in the task prompt cited this as JEM but it is in fact PNAS) is the foundational paper defining this checkpoint.

The Eaves laboratory at the Terry Fox Research Institute used limiting-dilution competitive transplantation of purified HSCs (CD45⁺ EPCR⁺ CD48⁻ CD150⁺) from mice at E14.5 fetal liver, postnatal days 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, and adult (8–12 weeks). Every age was assayed by transplantation into lethally irradiated recipients at limiting doses to measure long-term repopulating frequency, by BrdU pulse-chase to measure cycling, and by expression microarray to define transcriptional state. Secondary transplantation at 16 weeks measured self-renewal capacity.

The findings defined a sharp developmental transition. Before 3 weeks of age, HSCs showed a fetal phenotype: high frequency of cycling (>75% BrdU+ in a 24-h pulse), high self-renewal per division (secondary transplant engraftment from 1 HSC ~50%), and a distinctive transcriptional signature with elevated Sox17, Hmga2, Lin28b, and Igf2bp family members. Between 3 and 4 weeks, HSCs shifted abruptly — within a single week — to an adult phenotype: <5% cycling, lower per-division self-renewal (~20% secondary engraftment), and loss of the fetal transcriptional program. Critically, the timing was cell-intrinsic: fetal HSCs transplanted into adult hosts still underwent the switch at the equivalent developmental time (4 weeks post-transplant from fetal source), and adult HSCs transplanted into young recipients did not revert. This established the switch as a programmed, intrinsically timed checkpoint rather than a niche-driven response.

The paper also showed that the switch affected downstream biology: fetal HSCs had greater radioresistance, different cytokine-response profiles (more IL-6-dependent, less SCF-dependent), and produced different progeny ratios (more myeloid bias in fetal, more lymphoid in adult).

Field impact: Bowie 2007 defined the fetal-to-adult HSC switch as a distinct developmental event, motivated two decades of follow-up work on the molecular drivers (Lin28b, Hmga2, let-7, Sox17), and established the competitive-transplant-at-limiting-dose readout that is now standard for HSC functional assays. It placed the switch at 3–4 weeks, which maps to P21–P28 in the mouse.

For Huey's thesis this is the developmental skeleton his Axis 2 hangs on.
