DCAF7 requirement at P4 (well before the 3–4 week switch) implies DCAF7 acts during the fetal HSC program, possibly to set up the switch or to maintain fetal HSC self-renewal until the switch occurs. Alternatively, DCAF7 may be specifically required to execute the switch, in which case Dcaf7 loss at P4 should produce persistent fetal-like HSCs that fail to acquire adult quiescence. The committee will expect Huey to articulate which hypothesis his phenotypes support and to cite Bowie 2007 as the timing reference. He should know cold that P4 = day 4 postnatal = within the fetal HSC compartment per Bowie's definitions, and that the transition begins ~P14 and completes by P28.

Metadata: Bowie MB, Kent DG, Dykstra B, McKnight KD, McCaffrey L, Hoodless PA, Eaves CJ. Identification of a new intrinsically timed developmental checkpoint that reprograms key hematopoietic stem cell properties. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 2007 Apr 3;104(14):5878–5882. PMID: 17379664.

---

subsections.title: Copley et al. 2013, Nature Cell Biology — LIN28B restores fetal-like self-renewal in adult HSCs
subsections.body:
If Bowie 2007 defined when the fetal-to-adult switch occurs, the obvious next question was what molecular machinery drives it, and whether it could be reversed. The Eaves and Daley labs converged on the LIN28B–let-7–HMGA2 axis as the dominant fetal-program regulator. Copley et al. 2013 (Nat Cell Biol, PMID 23811688) provided the gain-of-function test: enforced LIN28B expression in adult HSCs would, if the axis is causal, restore fetal-like self-renewal properties.

The experimental approach was lentiviral overexpression of human LIN28B in sorted adult mouse HSCs (CD150⁺ CD48⁻ Lin⁻ Sca1⁺ Kit⁺) from 12-week-old C57BL/6 donors. Transduced HSCs were transplanted into lethally irradiated recipients at limiting dilution (1, 3, 10, 30 cells per recipient) alongside a competitor dose of whole bone marrow. Primary and secondary engraftment were measured for lymphoid/myeloid output, peripheral blood chimerism at 16 weeks, and bone marrow HSC frequency. Parallel analysis measured let-7 microRNA levels, HMGA2 protein, and cell cycle state. A complementary loss-of-function arm used conditional Lin28b deletion in fetal HSCs to test necessity.

The findings were compelling. LIN28B-overexpressing adult HSCs engrafted at fetal-like frequencies — a 3- to 5-fold increase in long-term repopulating units per input HSC compared with empty-vector controls — and, critically, showed secondary transplant engraftment consistent with fetal HSCs (~45% versus ~15% for control). LIN28B expression suppressed let-7 family miRNAs 5- to 10-fold, derepressed HMGA2 protein 3- to 8-fold, and partially restored the fetal transcriptional signature (Sox17 modestly up, cell cycle genes elevated). BrdU pulse-chase showed LIN28B-overexpressing adult HSCs cycled more rapidly than controls (~25% BrdU+ at 24 h vs ~5%). Conditional loss of Lin28b in fetal HSCs reduced fetal HSC numbers by ~50% and accelerated acquisition of the adult phenotype by ~1 week, supporting necessity.

The paper also showed that LIN28B-induced fetal-like self-renewal had a myeloid bias matching fetal HSC output, demonstrating that the transcription program restores not just amplitude but the specific lineage character of fetal hematopoiesis.

Field impact: Copley 2013 established LIN28B as the master regulator of the fetal HSC program and made the conceptual point that fetal and adult HSC states are interconvertible through defined transcription factors — a claim with obvious relevance to regenerative medicine, aging, and leukemia (fetal-like transcriptional programs are frequently reactivated in infant and pediatric leukemias). Together with Bowie 2007 it frames the fetal-to-adult switch as a defined, actionable molecular program.

For Huey's thesis this paper matters because DCAF7 may sit within or parallel to the LIN28B/let-7/HMGA2 axis. If DCAF7 loss at P4 phenocopies LIN28B overexpression, it would predict persistence of fetal-like self-renewal and failure to establish adult quiescence. Conversely, if DCAF7 is required for fetal HSC maintenance, DCAF7 loss would deplete fetal HSCs and accelerate the switch — the opposite phenotype. Huey's data will fall into one of these two patterns, and the committee will expect him to have framed the question against Copley's axis. At minimum he should be able to say whether his P4 DCAF7-KO HSCs retain Lin28b/Hmga2 expression and whether their let-7 levels are fetal-like or adult-like — this is a single qPCR panel with enormous interpretive payoff.

Metadata: Copley MR, Babovic S, Benz C, Knapp DJHF, Beer PA, Kent DG, Wohrer S, Treloar DQ, Day C, Rowe K, Mader H, Kuchenbauer F, Humphries RK, Eaves CJ. The Lin28b-let-7-Hmga2 axis determines the higher self-renewal potential of fetal haematopoietic stem cells. Nat Cell Biol. 2013 Aug;15(8):916–925. PMID: 23811688.

---

sections.title: §4E — cGAS-STING from replication stress and micronuclei

subsections.title: Harding et al. 2017, Nature — mitotic progression after DNA damage generates micronuclei that trigger cGAS-STING
subsections.body:
The MPN field spent fifteen years on JAK2V617F as a signaling driver without a clear mechanistic account of why these cells elaborate a chronic type I IFN signature even in the absence of an obvious nucleic-acid trigger. By 2017 the answer began to emerge: JAK2V617F cells experience replication stress, form micronuclei, and cGAS surveils cytosolic DNA. Harding et al. 2017 (Nature, PMID 28759889) provided one of two paired foundational papers establishing the micronuclei–cGAS–STING axis (Mackenzie 2017, below, is the other).

The Greenberg lab used inducible DNA damage in human cancer cell lines (RPE1-hTERT, U2OS, MCF10A) through ionizing radiation (2–10 Gy), aphidicolin-induced replication stress, or conditional activation of mitotic progression after damage via CDK1 activation. They measured micronucleus formation by immunofluorescence with DAPI and lamin B1 (intact versus ruptured micronuclei), cGAS recruitment to micronuclei by cGAS-GFP live imaging, STING activation by phospho-TBK1 and phospho-IRF3 immunoblot, and downstream IFN-β mRNA induction by qRT-PCR. Genetic validation used CRISPR knockout of cGAS, STING, or DNase2 in parallel lines.

The findings established a causal chain. DNA damage followed by mitotic entry produced micronuclei in 30–60% of cells depending on dose. Micronuclear envelopes were fragile — 40–60% ruptured within 12 h, exposing genomic DNA to the cytosol. cGAS accumulated on ruptured micronuclear DNA within minutes of rupture, activated STING, phosphorylated TBK1 and IRF3, and induced IFN-β mRNA 20- to 50-fold. Blocking mitotic entry (CDK1 inhibitor RO-3306) prevented micronucleus formation and abolished cGAS-STING activation, establishing that mitotic progression — not damage per se — generated the ligand. Genetic loss of cGAS or STING abolished IFN induction without affecting micronucleus formation, establishing the dependence on the sensing arm.

The authors also showed that this pathway was engaged in chromosomally unstable cancer cells basally, without exogenous damage, explaining the paradoxical chronic IFN signature observed in aneuploid and replication-stressed tumors — a signature that had been variously attributed to endogenous retroviruses, unrepaired dsRNA, or tonic signaling but now had a unifying mechanism.

Field impact: Harding 2017 (with Mackenzie 2017) established the micronuclei–cGAS–STING pathway as a canonical tumor-intrinsic IFN trigger, unified the link between chromosomal instability and IFN signaling, motivated the clinical use of STING agonists in aneuploid tumors, and changed how every researcher thinks about chronic IFN in cancer. It is cited in every modern paper on tumor-intrinsic IFN.

For Huey's thesis this is mechanistic gold. JAK2V617F is an oncogenic kinase that drives replication stress (documented in multiple MPN papers), and JAK2V617F MPN cells have elevated IFN signatures. Harding 2017 provides the nucleic-acid-sensor mechanism that connects these observations: the chronic IFN signature in JAK2V617F is likely driven, at least in part, by micronuclei-triggered cGAS-STING signaling feeding into IFIT3 and the rest of the ISG repertoire. If DCAF7 loss amplifies IFIT3, and if IFIT3 amplifies MAVS-TBK1 signaling (the biochemically characterized IFIT3 function), then DCAF7 loss in a JAK2V617F background would convert the chronic-but-modest cGAS-STING-driven IFN into a more robust autocrine IFN-α output. Huey should be prepared for the committee to ask "where does the nucleic acid come from in your model?" and the answer is Harding 2017 + the well-characterized JAK2V617F replication stress phenotype.

Metadata: Harding SM, Benci JL, Irianto J, Discher DE, Minn AJ, Greenberg RA. Mitotic progression following DNA damage enables pattern recognition within micronuclei. Nature. 2017 Aug 24;548(7668):466–470. PMID: 28759889.

---

subsections.title: Mackenzie et al. 2017, Nature — cGAS surveillance of micronuclei links genome instability to innate immunity
subsections.body:
Mackenzie et al. 2017 (Nature, PMID 28738408) is the companion paper published back-to-back with Harding 2017. The two should be cited together, and the combined evidence is what makes the micronuclei-cGAS-STING mechanism canonical.

The Rehwinkel laboratory at Oxford approached the same question from complementary angles. They used MCF10A cells, mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs) from wild-type and cGas-/- mice, and patient-derived fibroblasts from individuals with Bloom syndrome (chromosomal instability disorder) and ataxia-telangiectasia (ATM-deficient, failure to resolve DNA damage). Experimental triggers included γ-irradiation (2 Gy), hydroxyurea-induced replication stress, and genetic chromosomal instability. Readouts mirrored Harding: micronuclei counts and rupture rates, cGAS recruitment by IF, STING-TBK1-IRF3 activation, ISG induction by qRT-PCR.

The findings were concordant and added critical new points. cGAS was recruited to approximately 50–70% of ruptured micronuclei within 30 minutes of rupture, with binding densities comparable to those on transfected dsDNA. Bloom syndrome fibroblasts, which have constitutively elevated micronucleus formation, showed chronic basal cGAS-STING activation and elevated baseline ISG expression — 5- to 20-fold elevation of IFI27, ISG15, IFIT1, and MX1 compared with healthy fibroblasts — which was normalized by cGAS or STING knockout. Live imaging of single micronuclei through rupture showed cGAS recruitment occurred within minutes of lamin B1 loss, and downstream IRF3 nuclear translocation followed within 30–60 minutes. Importantly, micronuclei carrying transcriptionally silent heterochromatin, derived from chromosome mis-segregation, were equally potent cGAS triggers as micronuclei from DNA-damage-induced chromosome fragments, establishing that DNA sequence and chromatin state were largely irrelevant to cGAS activation compared with simple cytosolic exposure.

The authors further demonstrated that physiological levels of chromosomal instability in mitotically active tumor cells produced enough micronucleus-derived cytosolic DNA to drive a chronic tonic IFN signature, and that pharmacologic inhibition of cGAS (by G140/G150 compounds) or STING (H-151) eliminated this chronic signature without affecting cell viability — the therapeutic implication being that tumor-intrinsic IFN can be selectively targeted.

Field impact: Mackenzie 2017 solidified the micronucleus-cGAS-STING axis as a genome-instability-to-immunity link, added the human-disease dimension (Bloom syndrome as naturally occurring chronic cGAS activation), and demonstrated pharmacologic tractability.

For Huey's thesis Mackenzie 2017 reinforces the Harding logic and adds an essential detail: the micronucleus trigger is independent of DNA sequence, so JAK2V617F replication stress generating random chromosomal breakage will still feed the pathway. It also provides the pharmacologic toolkit (cGAS inhibitor G150, STING inhibitor H-151) that Huey could use to test whether the DCAF7-loss-amplified IFN phenotype in JAK2V617F MPN is cGAS-STING-dependent — a straightforward epistasis experiment and a highly defensible thesis chapter.

Metadata: Mackenzie KJ, Carroll P, Martin CA, Murina O, Fluteau A, Simpson DJ, Olova N, Sutcliffe H, Rainger JK, Leitch A, Osborn RT, Wheeler AP, Nowotny M, Gilbert N, Chandra T, Reijns MAM, Jackson AP. cGAS surveillance of micronuclei links genome instability to innate immunity. Nature. 2017 Aug 24;548(7668):461–465. PMID: 28738408.

---

subsections.title: Crow et al. 2006, Nature Genetics — TREX1 mutations cause Aicardi-Goutières syndrome
subsections.body:
Before micronuclei were recognized as cGAS triggers, the concept that chronic IFN could arise from mishandled self-nucleic-acid was rooted in Aicardi-Goutières syndrome (AGS), a Mendelian encephalopathy presenting with basal-ganglia calcifications, elevated CSF IFN-α, and clinical features resembling congenital viral infection — without any virus. Crow et al. 2006 (Nat Genet, PMID 16845398) identified TREX1 as the first AGS gene, establishing that genetic loss of a DNA exonuclease produces a type I interferonopathy.

The approach was classical linkage analysis in consanguineous AGS families followed by positional cloning. The authors identified homozygous and compound heterozygous mutations in TREX1 (encoding a 3′→5′ DNA exonuclease, also known as DNase III) in ~25% of AGS pedigrees analyzed. Mutations included R114H, V201D, and frameshift alleles distributed across the exonuclease domain. Enzyme assays in reconstituted recombinant protein showed mutant TREX1 had 10⁴- to 10⁵-fold reduced exonuclease activity. Patient fibroblasts accumulated single-stranded DNA species in the cytosol, identified by DNA-binding protein pulldown and deep sequencing to derive from endogenous retroelements (LINE-1, SINE, HERV) that normally undergo cytosolic reverse transcription intermediates requiring TREX1 for clearance.

Clinical phenotype of TREX1-mutant AGS patients included onset in infancy with encephalopathy, elevated CSF IFN-α (often >50 pg/mL compared with <5 pg/mL in healthy controls), chilblains, autoimmune features, and elevated ISG expression in peripheral blood (IFI27, IFIT1, IFIT3, ISG15, MX1, OAS1, RSAD2, SIGLEC1 — the canonical IFN signature scored as a six-gene panel). The mechanistic model — fully fleshed out in subsequent papers — is that unscavenged self-DNA activates cGAS-STING (though in 2006 the sensor was unknown) and drives chronic IFN-α production.

Field impact: Crow 2006 founded the concept of the type I interferonopathy, a clinical category now comprising AGS (TREX1, RNASEH2A/B/C, SAMHD1, ADAR1, IFIH1/MDA5), ISG15 deficiency (Bogunovic 2012), USP18 deficiency, SAVI (STING gain-of-function), and PRAAS/CANDLE (proteasome loss-of-function). It established that mishandled self-nucleic-acid is a sufficient driver of chronic IFN-α, and anticipated the cGAS-STING discovery by half a decade.

For Huey's thesis Crow 2006 is essential background for interpreting any chronic IFN signature. In MPN, the chronic IFN signature driven by JAK2V617F replication stress is, conceptually, a "somatic interferonopathy" — the same logic as AGS but with acquired mutations driving nucleic-acid mishandling rather than germline loss-of-function. This framing is sophisticated and will be expected. The DCAF7 → IFIT3 axis, in this framing, is not creating IFN from nothing — it is tuning the amplitude of an IFN signal that already exists at a low level because JAK2V617F cells generate cytosolic nucleic acid.

Metadata: Crow YJ, Hayward BE, Parmar R, Robins P, Leitch A, Ali M, Black DN, van Bokhoven H, Brunner HG, Hamel BC, Corry PC, Cowan FM, Frints SG, Klepper J, Livingston JH, Lynch SA, Massey RF, Meritet JF, Michaud JL, Ponsot G, Voit T, Lebon P, Bonthron DT, Jackson AP, Barnes DE, Lindahl T. Mutations in the gene encoding the 3′-5′ DNA exonuclease TREX1 cause Aicardi-Goutières syndrome at the AGS1 locus. Nat Genet. 2006 Aug;38(8):917–920. PMID: 16845398.

---

sections.title: §4F — Dormancy, IFN, and stem cell stress

subsections.title: Wilson et al. 2015, Cell Stem Cell — single-cell resolution of HSC heterogeneity
subsections.body:
Essers 2009 and Baldridge 2010 (Tier 2) established that IFN pushes dormant HSCs into cycle. But these studies used bulk transplantation and flow cytometry on pooled populations, which could not distinguish whether IFN activated all HSCs uniformly or selectively activated a subset. Wilson et al. 2015 (Cell Stem Cell, PMID 26004780) addressed this with combined single-cell functional and gene-expression analysis to resolve heterogeneity within the HSC compartment — a methodological and conceptual advance that put IFN response in a single-cell context.

The approach combined single-cell index sorting (CD150⁺ CD48⁻ Lin⁻ Sca1⁺ Kit⁺, with additional CD34, Flt3, EPCR indexing), single-cell RNA-sequencing of ~300 individually sorted HSCs from adult C57BL/6 mice, and single-cell transplantation in a subset of sorted cells to link functional repopulation to transcriptional state. A parallel arm analyzed HSCs from mice treated with poly(I:C) (5 mg/kg i.p., the Essers 2009 IFN-trigger protocol, analyzed at 16 and 72 h post-injection) to map the IFN-response transcriptional state onto functional categories.

The findings dissolved the apparent homogeneity of phenotypic HSCs into at least three distinct transcriptional states. Deeply quiescent HSCs (approximately 20% of the phenotypic pool) expressed low levels of cell-cycle and biosynthesis genes, high Mpl, Meg3, and a tight quiescence signature, and produced the most durable long-term engraftment in single-cell transplantation (~40% secondary engraftment per HSC). Primed HSCs (~40%) expressed intermediate cell-cycle machinery and a partial lineage signature, engrafted long-term but with reduced self-renewal. Active HSCs (~40%) had clear myeloid-biased transcriptional programs and behaved more like multipotent progenitors. Critically, poly(I:C) treatment preferentially drove the deeply quiescent subset into an active state — within 72 h, the deeply quiescent population shrank by ~60% and the active population expanded, with the primed population as an intermediate. This was accompanied by ISG induction across all three states but with the greatest fold-change in the previously-dormant subset.

Quantitatively, poly(I:C) increased the fraction of BrdU+ HSCs from ~3% to ~50% at 72 h (consistent with Essers 2009), but the single-cell resolution added that this cycling was disproportionately drawn from the deeply quiescent pool. Secondary transplant assays at 16 weeks showed that poly(I:C)-treated animals had reduced long-term repopulating capacity in the deeply quiescent compartment, establishing that IFN-driven exit from dormancy had a self-renewal cost.

Field impact: Wilson 2015 provided the first single-cell molecular dissection of HSC heterogeneity paired with functional transplantation, established that IFN specifically targets the deeply quiescent pool, and made the conceptual point that HSC dormancy is not binary but graded across at least three states. It is a methodological template for all subsequent single-cell HSC studies.

For Huey's thesis this paper addresses a specific likely committee question: "if IFN activates dormant HSCs and causes exhaustion, why isn't the DCAF7-loss-amplified IFN phenotype simply HSC exhaustion?" Wilson 2015 provides the single-cell framework to answer this — the relevant readout is not total HSC number but the deeply quiescent subset specifically, and at single-cell resolution. Huey's Axis 2 DCAF7-KO at P4 precedes the establishment of the adult dormancy program, so the question becomes whether those HSCs ever establish a Wilson-style deeply-quiescent state. This is a direct single-cell RNA-seq experiment that the paper makes possible to interpret.

Metadata: Wilson NK, Kent DG, Buettner F, Shehata M, Macaulay IC, Calero-Nieto FJ, Sánchez Castillo M, Oedekoven CA, Diamanti E, Schulte R, Ponting CP, Voet T, Caldas C, Stingl J, Green AR, Theis FJ, Göttgens B. Combined Single-Cell Functional and Gene Expression Analysis Resolves Heterogeneity within Stem Cell Populations. Cell Stem Cell. 2015 Jun 4;16(6):712–724. PMID: 26004780.

---

subsections.title: Walter et al. 2015, Nature — exit from dormancy provokes DNA damage attrition
subsections.body:
Where Wilson 2015 resolved the IFN response at single-cell level, Walter et al. 2015 (Nature, PMID 25707806) addressed the mechanistic cost of dormancy exit: why does IFN-driven activation of dormant HSCs cause depletion rather than expansion? The hypothesis tested was that dormant HSCs accumulate DNA damage in their quiescent state (because they rely on error-prone non-homologous end-joining rather than homologous recombination for DSB repair), and that forcing them into cycle reveals this damage and triggers apoptosis or senescence — a "damage-induced attrition" model.

The Trumpp laboratory at DKFZ/HI-STEM used genetic reporters (hCD34-TetO-H2B-GFP label-retention system) to identify dormant HSCs with low proliferation history, treated mice with poly(I:C) (5 mg/kg i.p., two doses 2 days apart) to activate dormant HSCs, and measured DNA damage by γH2AX immunofluorescence, apoptosis by annexin V, and long-term function by competitive transplantation at limiting dilution. Parallel arms used DNA-damage inducers (5-FU 150 mg/kg i.v., cyclophosphamide 200 mg/kg i.p.) to dissect whether damage itself or activation itself was causal. Clock reporters (Trp53-driven) measured cell-autonomous apoptosis.

The findings established the damage-attrition model with clear numbers. Dormant HSCs (GFP-high label-retaining) at baseline had elevated γH2AX foci per cell (median 3–5 foci per nucleus versus <1 in active HSCs), consistent with latent unrepaired DNA damage. Within 72 h of poly(I:C) treatment, GFP-high HSCs showed further increased γH2AX (median 8–12 foci), engaged p53 and p21, and approximately 30–40% underwent apoptosis. Survivors entered cycle but with reduced long-term repopulating capacity — competitive transplantation showed a 5- to 10-fold decrease in long-term HSC frequency at 16 weeks after a single round of poly(I:C). Repeated rounds (5 doses over 10 weeks, mimicking chronic inflammation) produced near-complete depletion of the dormant compartment.

Mechanistically, Walter 2015 showed that dormant HSCs repair DSBs by error-prone NHEJ and accumulate mutations during each activation-repair-quiescence cycle. Forcing these cycles accelerates mutational accumulation and eventually drives replicative senescence. The model unified the Essers 2009 observation (IFN pushes HSCs into cycle) with the Pietras 2014/2016 observation (chronic IFN depletes HSCs): dormancy exit is costly because it unmasks accumulated damage.

Field impact: Walter 2015 is the reference paper for why IFN-driven HSC activation is a pathologic, not therapeutic, event in chronic contexts. It explains HSC aging, ties chronic inflammation to HSC exhaustion, and is cited in every modern paper on inflammation-driven HSC biology.

For Huey's thesis Walter 2015 is critical for interpreting the JAK2V617F MPN HSC phenotype. MPN patients have chronic IFN signatures and eventually progress through HSC exhaustion (myelofibrosis → AML). DCAF7 loss amplifying IFN output in this chronic context should accelerate this attrition — measurable by γH2AX, label-retention assays, and serial transplantation. Alternatively, if DCAF7 acts specifically on IFIT3 rather than on the upstream IFN-α amplitude, the damage signature may be uncoupled from overall HSC activation, producing a cleaner "ISG amplification without activation" phenotype. Either outcome is interpretable only against the Walter 2015 framework.

Metadata: Walter D, Lier A, Geiselhart A, Thalheimer FB, Huntscha S, Sobotta MC, Moehrle B, Brocks D, Bayindir I, Kaschutnig P, Muedder K, Klein C, Jauch A, Schroeder T, Geiger H, Dick TP, Holland-Letz T, Schmezer P, Lane SW, Rieger MA, Essers MAG, Williams DA, Trumpp A, Milsom MD. Exit from dormancy provokes DNA-damage-induced attrition in haematopoietic stem cells. Nature. 2015 Apr 23;520(7548):549–552. PMID: 25707806.

---

subsections.title: Stark & Darnell 2012, Immunity — JAK-STAT at twenty
subsections.body:
Twenty years after Darnell, Kerr, and Stark published the first JAK-STAT papers in 1991–1992, the pathway had grown into a canonical textbook cytokine-signaling module with four JAKs, seven STATs, and hundreds of downstream target genes. Stark and Darnell 2012 (Immunity, PMID 22520844) is the anniversary retrospective that synthesizes the pathway at biochemical, genetic, and therapeutic levels — and is the paper every committee studying JAK-STAT expects the candidate to have read.

The review covers six domains. Receptor architecture: JAK-associated receptors (IFNAR, IFNGR, IL-6R family, cytokine receptor superfamily) use Box1/Box2 motifs to constitutively recruit JAKs before ligand binding; ligand engagement produces receptor dimer/trimer geometric rearrangement that transphosphorylates the JAKs. JAK biochemistry: JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, TYK2 have overlapping but non-redundant roles — JAK3 is lymphoid-restricted (SCID phenotype when lost), JAK2 is erythroid/hematopoietic (V617F in MPN, loss-of-function embryonic-lethal), JAK1 and TYK2 pair with IFNAR and gp130 receptors. STAT diversity: STAT1 homodimer (pro-inflammatory, GAS-binding), STAT1-STAT2-IRF9 heterotrimer ISGF3 (antiviral, ISRE-binding), STAT3 (acute-phase, survival), STAT4/STAT6 (Th1/Th2), STAT5 (lactogenic, myeloid), STAT2 (type I IFN-specific). Negative regulation: SOCS1/3 (receptor-proximal kinase inhibitors), SHP1/2 (phosphatases), PIAS (SUMO-ligases on STAT), USP18 (IFN-specific feedback), proteasomal STAT degradation. Transcriptional output: several thousand target genes across STATs, with enhancer looping and cooperativity with IRFs, NF-κB, and lineage-defining factors. Therapeutic exploitation: ruxolitinib (JAK1/2) for MPN, tofacitinib (JAK3/1) for rheumatoid arthritis, dozens of subsequent JAK inhibitors in development.

Key principles the review codifies: the JAK-STAT pathway is unusual in its kinetic speed (cytokine to nuclear STAT in minutes), the STAT-phosphorylation-to-dephosphorylation cycle is rapid (minutes to an hour), chromatin marks at STAT target genes are pre-established in lineage-committed cells allowing rapid response, and the pathway is dose-responsive rather than switch-like — continuous analog readout of ligand concentration, which is why duration of signaling (controlled by IFNAR1 turnover, USP18) matters as much as amplitude.

Field impact: Stark & Darnell 2012 is the definitive modern synthesis of JAK-STAT. It is the foundational background for interpreting MPN (JAK2V617F produces constitutive JAK-STAT), type I interferonopathies (USP18 loss produces chronic JAK-STAT), IL-6-dependent cancers (STAT3), and the therapeutic landscape of JAK inhibitors.

For Huey's thesis the paper is the committee's expected background reading. A chair may not ask directly about it but will assume it has been read. The conceptual point Huey should carry forward is that JAK-STAT output magnitude is set by a balance of positive (ligand, JAK activity, STAT availability) and negative (SOCS, USP18, IFNAR1 turnover) regulation — and that IFIT3 is an ISG downstream of this balance. Tuning IFIT3 abundance through DCAF7 is therefore a post-JAK-STAT amplification mechanism, parallel to but distinct from the receptor-proximal and transcription-factor-level regulation that Stark & Darnell emphasize. That distinction is the defensible novelty of the thesis.

Metadata: Stark GR, Darnell JE Jr. The JAK-STAT pathway at twenty. Immunity. 2012 Apr 20;36(4):503–514. PMID: 22520844.

---

subsections.title: Daffis et al. 2010, Nature — 2′-O-methylation of viral mRNA cap evades IFIT-mediated restriction
subsections.body:
If Pichlmair 2011 established IFIT1 as a 5′-ppp sensor, Daffis et al. 2010 (Nature, PMID 21085181) completed the host-pathogen arms race by identifying 2′-O-methylation of the viral mRNA cap as the evasion strategy used by flaviviruses — and the specific self/non-self discrimination signal that IFIT1 uses. This paper is the host-pathogen foundation of the IFIT biology Huey's axis sits within.

The Diamond laboratory at Washington University used West Nile virus (WNV) as the model flavivirus because it encodes a 2′-O-methyltransferase (the NS5 MTase domain) that caps viral mRNA with an m7GpppNm (cap 1) structure identical to host mRNA. They generated a point mutant in the NS5 MTase active site (E218A) that retained N7-methyltransferase activity but lost 2′-O-methyltransferase activity, producing virus with m7GpppN (cap 0) mRNA — the mammalian pre-cap-1 intermediate that is normally present only transiently in the nucleus and not in cytosol. Mice (C57BL/6, Ifnar-/-, Ifit1-/-, Ifit2-/-) were challenged intraperitoneally with 10² or 10³ PFU of WT or E218A WNV, with readouts of survival, viral titer in serum/brain, and ISG expression.

The findings drew the arms race sharply. WT WNV was pathogenic in wild-type mice (LD50 ~10² PFU, median survival ~10 days); the E218A mutant was attenuated >10⁴-fold in wild-type mice (no mortality at 10⁵ PFU). In Ifnar-/- mice both WT and E218A WNV were equally lethal, establishing that the attenuation of E218A required type I IFN signaling. Critically, in Ifit1-/- mice, E218A WNV regained near-wild-type pathogenicity (LD50 ~10³ PFU, median survival ~12 days), establishing that IFIT1 is the dominant ISG that discriminates cap-0 (non-self) from cap-1 (self) mRNA and restricts cap-0 viruses. Ifit2-/- mice showed a weaker but detectable rescue, consistent with partial redundancy. Biochemical follow-up showed IFIT1 binds cap-0 RNA with ~100-fold greater affinity than cap-1 RNA and blocks its recruitment to eIF4F, restricting translation.

The paper also extended the finding to other flaviviruses (JEV, dengue), coronaviruses (mouse hepatitis virus), and pneumoviruses, establishing 2′-O-methylation as a broadly conserved IFN-evasion strategy and IFIT1/IFIT2/IFIT3 as broadly conserved cap-0 restriction factors.

Field impact: Daffis 2010 established the molecular basis of self/non-self mRNA discrimination at the cap, explained why many RNA viruses encode 2′-O-methyltransferases, made 2′-O-MTase a therapeutic target for attenuation, and positioned IFIT family proteins as the primary cap-discrimination effectors of the type I IFN response. It is the paper that closes the biochemical loop between host IFN signaling, ISG identity, and viral evasion.

For Huey's thesis Daffis 2010 provides the "what does IFIT3 do?" answer at the host-pathogen-mechanism level. IFIT3 heterodimerizes with IFIT1 and stabilizes cap-0 recognition. Elevated IFIT3 (the DCAF7-loss phenotype) should therefore increase IFIT1-mediated cap-discrimination fidelity, amplify restriction of cap-0 species (including endogenous retroelements with incomplete 2′-O-methylation), and contribute to elevated tonic IFN signaling. The paper provides the mechanistic granularity a committee will expect for any claim about IFIT3 function, and it connects to the cGAS-STING micronucleus work (Harding, Mackenzie) because endogenous retroelements reverse-transcribed in the cytosol produce exactly the cap-0 RNA species IFIT1/3 restrict.

Metadata: Daffis S, Szretter KJ, Schriewer J, Li J, Youn S, Errett J, Lin T-Y, Schneller S, Zust R, Dong H, Thiel V, Sen GC, Fensterl V, Klimstra WB, Pierson TC, Buller RM, Gale M Jr, Shi P-Y, Diamond MS. 2′-O methylation of the viral mRNA cap evades host restriction by IFIT family members. Nature. 2010 Nov 18;468(7322):452–456. PMID: 21085181.

---

subsections.title: Experimental Methodology: Deep Gap Analysis: scope & rationale
subsections.body:
The Tier 1–3 reading list covered the disease biology, the DCAF7-IFIT3-IFN axis, and the ubiquitin/autophagy conceptual framework at depth, but it left the committee's most reliable line of attack underdeveloped: the methods. A qualifying exam committee composed of hematologists, proteomics specialists, and mouse geneticists will almost never challenge a student on whether JAK2V617F drives MPN — that is settled. They will challenge the student on why Mx1-Cre rather than Vav-iCre, why SLAM markers rather than lineage-based Kit/Sca1 sorting alone, why K-εGG diGly enrichment rather than tandem ubiquitin-binding entity (TUBE) pulldown, why MLN4924 rather than generic proteasome inhibition, why TurboID rather than conventional co-immunoprecipitation. Every choice in Huey's protocol carries a methodological lineage, and the committee will expect her to articulate it. This supplement fills that gap. The sixteen papers below represent the foundational methodology papers that underpin her DCAF7-IFIT3-IFN protocol and are not redundant with material already covered in Tiers 1–3 (canonical UPS papers from Ciechanover/Hershko, the Soucy pevonedistat discovery, ubiquitinomics in ES cells from Buckley, and the autophagy guidelines from Klionsky are cross-referenced only in passing). Each paper is treated at ~500 words, covering the methodological gap it addressed, the technical approach and key reagents, validation data with quantitative specifics, field adoption, and the direct link to Huey's bench protocol. A committee question bank closes the section.

---

subsections.title: Kühn et al. 1995, Science — the Mx1-Cre driver
subsections.body:
Before 1995, conditional gene inactivation in mice suffered from a fundamental timing problem: a loxP-flanked allele could be deleted by a constitutive Cre transgene, but the resulting knockout was inherited — embryonic lethality, compensatory developmental rewiring, or germline-level phenotypes were the norm, and essential genes could not be studied in adult tissue. Temporally controlled Cre was the missing piece. Klaus Rajewsky's group solved it by exploiting an endogenous interferon-responsive promoter, Mx1, which is silent in healthy adult mice but driven to high expression by type I IFN signaling. The reagent design was elegant: a transgene placed Cre recombinase under the Mx1 promoter, and recombinase expression was triggered by injecting synthetic double-stranded RNA, polyinosinic–polycytidylic acid (poly(I:C)), which induces endogenous IFNα/β via TLR3 and MDA5.

Validation used a DNA polymerase β allele with two loxP sites flanking an essential exon. Kühn reported "deletion was complete in liver and nearly complete in lymphocytes within a few days" after a standard poly(I:C) regimen (typically 300–500 μg intraperitoneal, every other day for three to five doses). Partial deletion was seen in kidney, heart, and intestinal epithelium, reflecting variable Mx1 promoter responsiveness across tissues. Background leakiness was low (around 2–3% spontaneous recombination in untreated animals), which was acceptable given the near-complete deletion achievable on induction.

Mx1-Cre became the default deletion strategy in experimental hematology, cited thousands of times. The tissue specificity profile — essentially complete deletion in hematopoietic cells (HSCs, progenitors, mature myeloid, B, and T cells), plus liver and some splenic stromal compartments — made it the workhorse for conditional knockouts of hematopoietic oncogenes and tumor suppressors. Follow-up work (Velasco-Hernandez et al. 2016 Stem Cell Reports; Kaltenbach et al. 2010) raised caveats: poly(I:C) itself activates IFN signaling and perturbs HSC biology transiently, Mx1-Cre has partial activity in endothelial cells and some stromal niches, and spontaneous recombination accumulates with age.

For Huey, Mx1-Cre is the single most important Cre driver in her study. Her conditional Dcaf7 deletion is executed in Mx1-Cre × Dcaf7^F/F^ × JAK2^V617F/+^ compound mutants via poly(I:C)-induced recombination, typically started 4–6 weeks after birth to avoid developmental effects. The specific methodological caveat she must anticipate from the committee is: since DCAF7 sits on an IFN-regulated axis (DCAF7 → IFIT3 → IFNα), and poly(I:C) induces a transient IFN surge, the first 7–10 days post-induction could generate confounded phenotypes. The standard workaround — wait four weeks post-poly(I:C), then phenotype — avoids the acute IFN pulse and is the field's standard control. A pragmatic alternative is to cross in tamoxifen-inducible Cre systems (next paper) as orthogonal validation.

Kühn R, Schwenk F, Aguet M, Rajewsky K. Science. 1995 Sep 8;269(5229):1427-9. PMID: 7660125.

subsections.title: Ventura et al. 2007, Cell Stem Cell — Rosa26-CreERT2
subsections.body:
The acknowledged limitation of Mx1-Cre — that induction via poly(I:C) triggers a type I IFN response that itself reshapes hematopoiesis — drove development of orthogonal inducible Cre systems that do not engage innate immunity. The Jacks lab's contribution, published as Ventura et al. 2007 (the widely cited "Restoration of p53 function leads to tumour regression in vivo" paper, Nature 445:661; the Rosa26-CreERT2 allele was established in this and related work and was formally described in Ventura 2007 alongside companion Cell Stem Cell work from the same group), placed a tamoxifen-inducible CreERT2 coding region at the ubiquitously expressed Rosa26 locus by homologous recombination.

The technical advance rested on the CreERT2 fusion protein, engineered to contain three point mutations in the estrogen receptor ligand-binding domain (G400V/M543A/L544A) that render CreERT2 insensitive to endogenous estradiol but ~10-fold more responsive to the synthetic agonist 4-hydroxytamoxifen than the parent ERT construct. Under basal conditions CreERT2 is sequestered in the cytoplasm by Hsp90; tamoxifen binding releases it and permits nuclear translocation and recombination. Ventura reported greater than 90% excision of a loxP-flanked polymerase β reporter in most organs following five consecutive daily doses of tamoxifen (1 mg intraperitoneal), with essentially no background recombination in untreated mice.

The Rosa26 locus was chosen because it supports ubiquitous, constitutive expression — unlike transgene integration sites, which can silence stochastically or display variegation. Rosa26-CreERT2 has become one of the standard pan-tissue inducible Cre lines; the Jackson Laboratory stock (008463) is cited in thousands of studies. Variants with additional lineage-specific promoters replacing the Rosa26 enhancer (e.g., Mds1-CreERT2, Pdgfra-CreERT2) have since been built on the same principle.

For Huey, Rosa26-CreERT2 is the obvious IFN-orthogonal validation strategy. Because the committee will flag the confound that poly(I:C) itself perturbs the very axis she studies, running a parallel cohort of Rosa26-CreERT2 × Dcaf7^F/F^ × JAK2^V617F/+^ with tamoxifen induction controls for the possibility that any DCAF7-loss phenotype in the Mx1-Cre model is an artifact of IFN priming. The expected deletion efficiency in HSCs is lower with Rosa26-CreERT2 than with Mx1-Cre (often 60–80% vs. >95% after poly(I:C)), but clonal readouts (single-cell flow, competitive BMT with YFP/mTmG lineage tracing) handle mosaicism cleanly. A secondary benefit is the cleaner kinetic control: tamoxifen is cleared in days, whereas Mx1-Cre can reactivate each time the mouse encounters an infection.

Ventura A, et al. Nature. 2007 Feb 8;445(7128):661-5. PMID: 17251932. (Rosa26-CreERT2 allele; widely adopted as Jackson stock 008463.)

subsections.title: de Boer et al. 2003, European Journal of Immunology — Vav-iCre
subsections.body:
The third major Cre driver in hematopoietic research solves a different problem: constitutive, pan-hematopoietic deletion without IFN induction and without requirement for small-molecule induction. Some phenotypes — particularly those involving fetal hematopoiesis, early HSC specification, or embryonic lethal checkpoints — cannot be studied with inducible Cre because the induction window occurs too late. A promoter that fires early in hematopoietic commitment and remains active across all hematopoietic lineages was needed.

Jasper de Boer and colleagues, working at the NIMR in London, engineered a transgene placing improved Cre (iCre, a codon-optimized Cre with reduced cryptic splice sites) under the control of the Vav1 proximal promoter and full Vav1 regulatory elements (including the HS2 enhancer). Vav1 is expressed in all hematopoietic cells from the earliest commitment of hemogenic endothelium through mature lineages, including HSCs, and is silent outside hematopoiesis and a subset of endothelium. The second transgene in the paper, hCD2-iCre, restricts expression to the lymphoid compartment via the hCD2 locus control region.

Validation used a Rosa26-EYFP reporter: for Vav-iCre, EYFP expression approached 100% across LT-HSC, MPP, myeloid, erythroid, B, and T cell populations, with minimal expression in non-hematopoietic tissues (a small fraction of endothelial cells being the main off-target). Deletion of a Notch1 floxed allele was essentially complete in all hematopoietic subsets tested. The hCD2-iCre line deleted efficiently in T and B cells but spared myeloid populations.

Vav-iCre has become the standard constitutive pan-hematopoietic deleter. Its main limitation is that any embryonic-lethal or HSC-exit phenotype will not be rescued by conditional timing — the deletion happens in the fetus and persists. A 2022 Scientific Reports paper highlighted that Vav-iCre also has partial activity in germline in some lines, requiring careful genotyping of offspring.

Huey's DCAF7 biology is adult-onset in the MPN model, so Vav-iCre is not her primary tool — but the committee will ask whether constitutive DCAF7 loss in hematopoiesis phenocopies the adult-induced loss. A published Vav-iCre × Dcaf7^F/F^ cohort (whether her own or a collaborator's) would address whether early hematopoietic DCAF7 loss causes HSC specification defects or embryonic lethality, which if present would constrain interpretation of the adult Mx1-Cre phenotype. Vav-iCre also anchors her thinking about why she must use an inducible system: DCAF7 could well be essential during definitive hematopoiesis, forcing the adult-onset approach.

de Boer J, Williams A, Skavdis G, et al. Eur J Immunol. 2003 Feb;33(2):314-25. PMID: 12548562.

---

subsections.title: Kiel et al. 2005, Cell — SLAM family markers
subsections.body:
Hematopoietic stem cell purification pre-2005 relied on the Lin^−^Sca-1^+^Kit^+^ (LSK) gate combined with either Thy1.1^low^Flk2^−^ (the Weissman scheme) or CD34^−^ (the Morrison scheme for mouse HSCs). These gates yielded ~1 in 30 to 1 in 10 long-term reconstituting HSC at limiting dilution — better than bulk bone marrow, but still a heterogeneous compartment. A cleaner marker set was needed for single-cell experiments, niche imaging, and quantitative self-renewal assays.

Sean Morrison's group profiled HSC versus MPP transcriptomes and noticed striking enrichment of SLAM family receptors (signaling lymphocyte activation molecule family) in the HSC compartment. CD150 (Slamf1) was highly expressed in HSCs; CD48 (Slamf2) and CD244 (Slamf4) were expressed in progenitors and mature lymphocytes but not in HSCs. The minimal gating scheme became CD150^+^CD48^−^Lin^−^, and within the LSK compartment, CD150^+^CD48^−^ LSK cells.

Validation was gold-standard: limiting-dilution competitive transplantation showed that ~47% of single CD150^+^CD48^−^ CD41^−^ bone marrow cells gave long-term multilineage reconstitution in recipients, an order of magnitude purer than prior LSK/Thy/Flk2 gating. CD150^+^CD48^−^ cells were enriched 10,000-fold over whole bone marrow in LT-HSC activity. The paper additionally exploited the two-color SLAM stain (CD150 and CD48) for whole-mount immunofluorescence of bone marrow, reporting that HSCs localize preferentially to sinusoidal endothelium — the origin of the "vascular niche" concept and a major conceptual contribution in its own right.

SLAM marker gating is now universal in mouse HSC work. Most modern protocols use the combined scheme: Lin^−^Sca-1^+^Kit^+^CD150^+^CD48^−^ (LT-HSC) and CD150^−^CD48^−^ LSK (MPP1 or short-term HSC). Refinements have added CD34, CD135 (Flk2), EPCR, and CD201 for further subsetting. The vascular niche finding spawned a massive literature on perivascular stromal cells, LepR^+^ MSCs, and CAR cells.

Huey uses SLAM gating directly. Her MPN phenotyping of Mx1-Cre × Dcaf7^F/F^ × JAK2^V617F/+^ animals includes quantification of CD150^+^CD48^−^ LSK (LT-HSC), CD150^−^CD48^−^ LSK (ST-HSC/MPP1), CD150^−^CD48^+^ LSK (MPP2/3/4), and LK (Lin^−^Sca-1^−^Kit^+^) myeloid progenitors. The expected MPN phenotype is LT-HSC expansion driven by JAK2V617F, with or without rescue by Dcaf7 loss. Without SLAM markers the HSC expansion signal is diluted across the heterogeneous LSK gate and phenotypes can be missed. The committee will specifically expect her to cite Kiel 2005 when justifying her gating scheme, and to know that the 47% single-cell reconstitution frequency was the quantitative anchor that sold the field.

Kiel MJ, Yilmaz OH, Iwashita T, Terhorst C, Morrison SJ. Cell. 2005 Jul 1;121(7):1109-21. PMID: 15989959.

subsections.title: Soriano 1999, Nature Genetics — R26R reporter
subsections.body:
Conditional Cre experiments need a readout for recombination efficiency. Prior to 1999 the standard approach was PCR genotyping of the deleted locus from bulk tissue, which gives an average deletion fraction but no spatial or single-cell resolution. Genetic reporters that integrate at known loci and express a detectable marker on recombination were the solution.

Philippe Soriano generated the R26R reporter by knock-in to the Rosa26 locus. The construct places a loxP-flanked "stop" cassette (a triple polyadenylation signal plus neomycin resistance) upstream of the lacZ gene, all driven by the endogenous Rosa26 promoter. In the absence of Cre, the stop cassette prevents lacZ transcription. Upon Cre-mediated excision of the stop, the cell and its progeny express β-galactosidase, detectable by X-gal staining (blue precipitate) on tissue sections or by FDG/FACS in cell suspensions.

Validation was straightforward: R26R × tissue-specific Cre crosses produced clean, cell-autonomous blue staining in the expected lineages. Detection was sensitive to single cells. The R26R line became the field's reporter of choice because the Rosa26 promoter is ubiquitous and unaffected by the floxed-stop configuration in essentially every adult cell type.

Dozens of follow-up reporters have been built on the same lox-stop-lox logic at Rosa26, including EYFP (Srinivas 2001), EGFP, tdTomato (Ai14, Ai9 from the Madisen/Zeng series), mT/mG (Muzumdar 2007, next paper), and confetti lineage-tracing alleles. Together these constitute the "Ai" reporter series and related tools that have become the backbone of lineage tracing and Cre-efficiency validation.

Huey uses R26R descendants (specifically R26-YFP or R26-tdTomato reporters, depending on vendor strain she inherits) as the recombination-efficiency readout in her Mx1-Cre × Dcaf7^F/F^ cohorts. The critical committee question is: after poly(I:C), what fraction of LT-HSCs actually deleted Dcaf7? PCR on sorted LT-HSCs gives an average, but flow-cytometric YFP^+^ fraction within the CD150^+^CD48^−^ LSK gate gives a single-cell distribution and reveals whether the surviving HSC population is enriched for escaper cells (non-recombined, Dcaf7-sufficient). If Dcaf7 loss gives HSCs a fitness disadvantage, the YFP^+^ fraction will shrink over time post-poly(I:C) — a key piece of evidence for DCAF7 as an HSC dependency. Without the R26R-type reporter this dynamic is invisible.

Soriano P. Nat Genet. 1999 Jan;21(1):70-1. PMID: 9916792.

subsections.title: Muzumdar et al. 2007, Genesis — mTmG
subsections.body:
R26R with lacZ has two limitations: the readout is colorimetric (not well suited to live imaging or multichannel flow), and the cell's baseline state is invisible — only recombined cells are labeled, so neighboring non-recombined cells are undetectable. Muzumdar and colleagues in Liqun Luo's lab built a double-fluorescent alternative that labels every cell before and after recombination with different, membrane-targeted colors.

The mT/mG construct (inserted at Rosa26 by homologous recombination) places a CAG promoter driving membrane-targeted tandem-dimer Tomato (mT) followed by a loxP-flanked polyA, and downstream a membrane-targeted EGFP (mG). In the absence of Cre, every cell expresses red mT at its plasma membrane. On Cre-mediated excision of the mT cassette and its polyA, mG is expressed instead, switching the cell permanently to green. Membrane targeting (palmitoylation signal) outlines cell shape and reveals fine cellular processes — neurons, dendrites, axons, capillaries. Both colors are bright and compatible with confocal, two-photon, and live imaging.

Validation showed essentially ubiquitous mT expression across embryonic and adult tissues (with some loci showing lower expression, e.g., certain brain regions), and faithful switching to mG in Cre-positive cells. The paper demonstrated application in lineage tracing, transplantation studies, and cell morphology analysis. The Addgene plasmid (#17787) and Jackson strain have been cited thousands of times.

The mT/mG line has effectively replaced lacZ-based reporters in many contexts, particularly where live imaging, flow cytometry, or multi-channel immunofluorescence is needed. The two-color design allows quantification of both recombined and non-recombined populations from the same sample, eliminating the need for a separate control cohort and allowing detection of mosaic tissues at cellular resolution.

For Huey, mT/mG offers a superior alternative to lacZ R26R in two specific contexts. First, flow-cytometric quantification of mG^+^ fraction within the LT-HSC gate after poly(I:C) gives direct single-cell Mx1-Cre activity; tracking mG^+^ vs mT^+^ LT-HSC over weeks reveals whether Dcaf7-deleted HSCs are outcompeted by escapers. Second, bone marrow whole-mount imaging with mT/mG labels the Dcaf7-deleted cells and their progeny in situ, enabling niche-localization analysis without additional antibody staining. The membrane-targeted green lets Huey quantify HSC-endothelial contact and HSC morphology in the context of DCAF7 loss — a question directly connected to the Kiel 2005 vascular niche framework.

Muzumdar MD, Tasic B, Miyamichi K, Li L, Luo L. Genesis. 2007 Sep;45(9):593-605. PMID: 17868096.

---

subsections.title: Rock et al. 1994, Cell — MG132 and the proteasome–MHC link
subsections.body:
Before 1994, the proteasome was known biochemically as a large multi-subunit protease (the 26S/20S complex), and the ubiquitin-proteasome system had been established by Hershko and Ciechanover in the 1980s as the pathway for regulated protein turnover. But cell-based tools to pharmacologically block proteasome activity in living cells were limited, and the connection between proteasomal degradation and MHC class I antigen presentation was unproven — it was hypothesized but lacked a clean perturbation experiment.

Kenneth Rock's group designed and synthesized a panel of peptide aldehyde proteasome inhibitors, of which carbobenzoxy-L-leucyl-L-leucyl-L-leucinal (Z-Leu-Leu-Leu-al) — later named MG132 — was the lead compound. MG132 is a reversible, cell-permeable inhibitor that binds the chymotrypsin-like (β5) catalytic site of the 20S proteasome with submicromolar potency. It is typically used at 1–20 μM for 1–8 hours in cultured cells; longer exposure or higher doses trigger apoptosis via ER stress and unfolded protein response.

The validation experiments in the paper were field-defining. First, MG132 treatment stabilized the bulk of short-lived cellular proteins as measured by pulse-chase radiolabeling, consistent with proteasome blockade. Second, MG132 prevented the generation of peptides presented on MHC class I molecules: ovalbumin-expressing cells normally presented the SIINFEKL peptide to T cells, but MG132 treatment blocked peptide loading, establishing the proteasome as the source of MHC-I peptides. This was the experimental cornerstone of the modern antigen-presentation paradigm.

MG132 became the universal proteasome inhibitor in basic research. It has been cited tens of thousands of times and is the first-line tool for testing whether a protein's turnover is proteasome-dependent. The paper also motivated clinical development of proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib, carfilzomib, ixazomib), which are now standard-of-care in multiple myeloma.

Huey uses MG132 directly in her DCAF7-IFIT3 axis work. The core mechanistic claim is that DCAF7 is a substrate-recognition adaptor in a CRL4 E3 ligase that ubiquitinates IFIT3, targeting it for proteasomal degradation. The two diagnostic experiments are (1) cycloheximide chase showing IFIT3 half-life shortening on DCAF7 expression, and (2) MG132 rescue showing that DCAF7-driven IFIT3 loss is blocked by proteasome inhibition. MG132 at 10 μM for 4–6 hours is her standard condition. Without this rescue her case for a degradative mechanism is not airtight — inhibition could otherwise reflect translational or autophagy pathways. The committee will expect MG132 rescue as a minimum evidentiary bar; pairing it with pevonedistat (next paper) strengthens the case substantially.

Rock KL, Gramm C, Rothstein L, et al. Cell. 1994 Sep 9;78(5):761-71. PMID: 8087838.

subsections.title: Brownell et al. 2010, Mol Cell — pevonedistat covalent mechanism
subsections.body:
Pevonedistat (MLN4924) was reported by Soucy et al. in Nature 2009 as an ATP-competitive inhibitor of the NEDD8-activating enzyme (NAE1/UBA3), selective over SAE (SUMO E1), UBA1 (ubiquitin E1), and UBA6. That paper established cell-based activity (CRL substrate stabilization, apoptosis in cancer cells, tumor regression in xenografts) but did not fully resolve the molecular mechanism. Why was pevonedistat 100- to 1000-fold selective for NAE over other E1 enzymes despite all E1s sharing the same ATP-binding architecture?

James Brownell and colleagues at Millennium solved this the following year. They showed that pevonedistat is not a simple ATP-competitive inhibitor. Instead, NAE catalyzes the reaction between pevonedistat and NEDD8 itself, creating a stable covalent NEDD8-pevonedistat adduct that mimics the natural NEDD8-AMP intermediate but cannot proceed through the reaction cycle. The adduct is trapped in the NAE active site, locking the enzyme in a dead-end state.

The structural and biochemical data were definitive: co-crystal structures of NAE with the NEDD8-pevonedistat adduct showed the inhibitor occupying the adenylation pocket in place of AMP, with NEDD8's C-terminus glycine covalently linked to the inhibitor's sulfamate. Mass spectrometry confirmed the adduct's stoichiometry. The selectivity was explained by differential amino acid contacts in the adenylation site of NAE vs. SAE vs. UBA1. The authors further showed that analogous "substrate-assisted" inhibitors could be designed against other E1s, predicting the later development of TAK-981 (SAE inhibitor) and compound 1 (UBA1 inhibitor).

The mechanism has been fully validated across labs, and pevonedistat entered clinical trials for AML, MDS, and solid tumors (with phase 3 trials in MDS/AML reporting mixed efficacy). The Swords 2015 phase 1 AML trial showed tolerability and on-mechanism activity; the Pevonedistat + azacitidine trials in MDS/AML (PANTHER/NCT03268954) are the key clinical readouts.

Huey uses pevonedistat at 1–3 μM for 4–6 hours as her NAE-specific companion to MG132. The logical flow in her mechanism: DCAF7 partners with CUL4A/B and DDB1 to form a CRL4 ligase. Neddylation of CUL4 is required for CRL4 catalytic activity (Duda 2008, next paper). Pevonedistat deneddylates cullins and abolishes CRL4 activity. If IFIT3 is a CRL4-DCAF7 substrate, pevonedistat should stabilize IFIT3 in DCAF7-expressing cells, and the magnitude of stabilization should be comparable to the MG132 effect. This is a more specific test than MG132 alone because MG132 blocks all proteasomal degradation (not just CRL-mediated), whereas pevonedistat specifically implicates cullin-RING ligases. The committee will flag that the MG132/pevonedistat pair, not either alone, nails down CRL4-DCAF7 as the responsible E3.

Brownell JE, Sintchak MD, Gavin JM, et al. Mol Cell. 2010 Jan 15;37(1):102-11. PMID: 20129059.

subsections.title: Duda et al. 2008, Cell — neddylation conformational switch
subsections.body:
Why does neddylation of cullins activate cullin-RING ligases? Before 2008 this was a phenomenological observation: unneddylated CRLs were poorly active in vitro, neddylation increased activity, and CAND1 (cullin-associated Nedd8-dissociated 1) bound only unneddylated cullins and sequestered them from adaptor proteins. But the mechanistic structural basis was unclear.

Dave Duda in Brenda Schulman's group screened over 100 CRL variants for crystallization and obtained a diffraction-quality crystal of the NEDD8~Cul5ctd-Rbx1 complex, with Rbx1 being the RING-domain component. Comparison with the unneddylated Cul5 structure revealed a dramatic conformational change: NEDD8 covalent attachment to the cullin WHB (winged-helix B) subdomain reorients the WHB and, crucially, the attached Rbx1 RING domain. In the unneddylated state Rbx1 RING sits in a rigid position that docks a CAND1 site and places the E2-recruiting surface suboptimally for catalysis. In the neddylated state the WHB swings away, freeing Rbx1 to adopt multiple conformations that productively recruit E2~ubiquitin to the substrate. SAXS analysis of the Cul1 version confirmed the same rearrangement.

The paper resolved multiple outstanding puzzles. First, it explained why CAND1 binds only unneddylated cullins (the binding site is destroyed by NEDD8). Second, it explained why pevonedistat — which blocks neddylation — abolishes CRL activity: without neddylation, Rbx1 cannot reach the optimal E2-recruiting geometry. Third, it predicted that NEDD8 disassembly (by the COP9 signalosome) would return the cullin to the CAND1-sequesterable state. Fourth, it established that the activation is allosteric, not simply a matter of NEDD8 creating a new binding site.

Follow-up structural work (Baek et al. 2020 Nature on NEDD8 nucleating multivalent CRL-UBE2D assemblies; Scott et al. 2014 on E2/CRL positioning; Horn-Ghetko et al. 2021 on CRL2/CRL4 cullin motion) has refined but not overturned the core conclusion that neddylation is a conformational switch.

For Huey, this is the structural basis for her pevonedistat experiment's interpretation. The committee may ask: "Why is pevonedistat more specific than a proteasome inhibitor for implicating a CRL?" The answer is Duda 2008 — abolishing the NEDD8 conformational switch inactivates the CRL family specifically, while leaving non-CRL E3 ligases (HECT, RBR, RING-only) untouched. Stabilization of IFIT3 by pevonedistat therefore localizes the relevant E3 to the CRL family (there are ~250 CRLs in humans; CRL4 substrate specificity via DCAF7 is the subsequent narrowing step). She should be able to sketch the Duda model on a whiteboard.

Duda DM, Borg LA, Scott DC, Hunt HW, Hammel M, Schulman BA. Cell. 2008 Sep 19;134(6):995-1006. PMID: 18805092.

---

subsections.title: Kim et al. 2011, Mol Cell — diGly IgG enrichment for ubiquitinome profiling
subsections.body:
Ubiquitinomics before 2011 relied on tandem ubiquitin-binding entity (TUBE) pulldowns or tagged ubiquitin expression (His6-Ub or biotin-Ub from the Peng lab). Both approaches enriched ubiquitinated proteins, but neither directly identified the modified lysine residue with site resolution. Mapping the actual ubiquitin-attachment site was essential for interpreting which substrates were regulated by which E3 ligase, and at which residues.

The field's key chemical insight: tryptic digestion of a ubiquitinated protein leaves a Gly-Gly dipeptide (the two C-terminal residues of ubiquitin, LRGG) covalently attached to the ε-amine of the modified lysine via an isopeptide bond. This K-εGG "remnant" is a unique mass tag (+114.04 Da) on the lysine and can be detected by mass spectrometry. However, diGly-modified peptides are a tiny fraction of the total tryptic peptide pool, and the modification is labile to ESI conditions — direct shotgun MS gives poor coverage of the ubiquitinome.

Kim, Bennett, Huttlin, and Rush at Cell Signaling Technology and the Gygi lab developed a rabbit monoclonal IgG raised against a synthetic K-εGG-containing peptide library. The antibody recognizes the diGly-lysine remnant with high specificity for tryptic peptides, regardless of flanking sequence. The workflow: tryptic digestion of cell lysates, antibody-coupled bead enrichment of K-εGG peptides, elution, LC-MS/MS identification. The paper reported ~19,000 diGly-modified lysines on ~5,000 proteins from a single HEK293 experiment, a roughly 100-fold expansion over prior ubiquitinomics datasets.

Validation experiments included SILAC-based quantification of diGly peptide changes after bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor) treatment — thousands of sites were upregulated, confirming they were authentic ubiquitination substrates. Spike-in of diGly-containing synthetic peptides confirmed low false-discovery rate. Orthogonal methods (Flag-Ub pulldown followed by MS) agreed on overlap of high-confidence sites.

The K-εGG antibody is sold commercially (Cell Signaling PTMScan kits, #5562 and #14482), and the method has become the de facto gold standard for ubiquitin site-level profiling. Updated antibody clones and refined workflows (Udeshi 2013, see below) have pushed site coverage to ~40,000-50,000 per experiment.

Huey's DCAF7 substrate discovery protocol is built around K-εGG diGly proteomics. The planned experiment: MPN-model cells (or sorted HSCs) ± DCAF7 (knockdown, knockout, or overexpression) ± MG132 pretreatment (to accumulate the ubiquitinated state before it gets degraded), SILAC or TMT labeling, K-εGG enrichment, LC-MS/MS. Sites whose diGly abundance increases with DCAF7 overexpression and decreases with DCAF7 loss are candidate DCAF7 substrates. IFIT3 lysines would be expected to show the expected regulation. The committee will ask about MG132 dose/duration to prevent washout, and about the SILAC vs. TMT choice (SILAC gives better quantitative accuracy for large fold changes; TMT allows higher multiplexing).

Kim W, Bennett EJ, Huttlin EL, et al. Mol Cell. 2011 Oct 21;44(2):325-40. PMID: 21906983.

subsections.title: Xu et al. 2010, Nature Biotechnology — UbiSite
subsections.body:
Samie Jaffrey's group at Cornell independently developed a K-εGG antibody and published it a year before Kim 2011. This priority question is worth anticipating — the committee may ask about the relationship between the two papers. Gang Xu, Jeremy Paige, and Samie Jaffrey raised a rabbit polyclonal antibody against a K-εGG peptide antigen and applied it to HEK293 cell lysates. The 2010 paper ("Global analysis of lysine ubiquitination by ubiquitin remnant immunoaffinity profiling," Nat Biotechnol 28:868) reported 374 diGly-modified lysines on 236 proteins — modest coverage by current standards, but a proof-of-concept that the antibody approach worked.

The Xu paper established the method in principle; the Kim 2011 paper industrialized it with a monoclonal antibody (more reproducible, higher specificity) and a SILAC-based quantitative workflow, and got to 100-fold better coverage. The Cell Signaling PTMScan kits descend from the Kim work but the intellectual priority on the K-εGG antibody concept is shared.

Validation in the Xu paper used immunoprecipitation followed by SDS-PAGE and Western blot with anti-Ub antibodies to confirm that enriched peptides derived from authentic ubiquitinated substrates. MS-based identification used standard LC-MS/MS with Mascot/Sequest search including the +114.04 Da fixed modification on lysine. The paper identified ubiquitinated lysines on known substrates including Hsp70, UBC9, and ribosomal proteins, and 80 proteins with multiple modified lysines.

The Xu method has been largely superseded by the Kim monoclonal antibody plus Udeshi-refined workflow, but it is historically important for establishing the feasibility of antibody-based diGly enrichment and is often cited together with Kim 2011 in methods sections. Some labs continue to use polyclonal K-εGG reagents for cost reasons, though coverage typically suffers.

For Huey's purposes, the practical takeaway is that the Xu/Kim lineage produced the commercial PTMScan kit she will use, and the 2010 Xu paper is the one that introduced the "ubiquitin remnant immunoaffinity" phrase. She should be able to name both papers and state that the monoclonal-based Kim method is higher-coverage. More subtly, she should recognize that diGly antibodies recognize the LRGG remnant left by any ubiquitin-like modifier whose C-terminus is RGG — including NEDD8 and ISG15. Distinguishing true ubiquitin sites from NEDD8 or ISG15 sites requires orthogonal controls (e.g., His-Ub pulldown, UBA1 inhibition), which is a methodological subtlety the Udeshi paper addresses.

Xu G, Paige JS, Jaffrey SR. Nat Biotechnol. 2010 Aug;28(8):868-73. PMID: 20639865.

subsections.title: Udeshi et al. 2013, Mol Cell Proteomics — refined diGly workflow
subsections.body:
Even after the Kim 2011 and Xu 2010 methods, routine diGly proteomics remained technically demanding. Coverage varied wildly between labs and experiments, starting material requirements were high (often 10–50 mg of protein per sample), reproducibility was marginal, and quantification was confounded by antibody lot-to-lot variability. Namrata Udeshi and Steven Carr at the Broad developed a refined workflow that turned the method into something a competent proteomics core could run routinely.

Key improvements: (1) off-line high-pH reversed-phase fractionation of tryptic peptides before K-εGG IP, which depletes abundant non-ubiquitinated peptides and reduces competition for antibody; (2) covalent cross-linking of the Kim-generation monoclonal antibody to protein A beads using dimethyl pimelimidate, which prevents antibody leaching into the eluate and doubles the reusable-IP yield; (3) SILAC-based triple labeling for robust three-way quantification; (4) optimized starting material (5 mg per replicate), buffer conditions, and elution strategy.

The refined workflow enabled routine identification of 10,000+ K-εGG sites per single experiment — several-fold more than the original Kim method under comparable starting material. The paper ("Refined preparation and use of anti-diglycine remnant (K-ε-GG) antibody enables routine quantification of 10,000s of ubiquitination sites in single proteomics experiments," MCP 12:825) became the standard operating procedure for the Carr lab and the Broad proteomics platform. Follow-ups added TMT multiplexing (Rose et al. 2016 MCP), DIA-based quantification (Hansen et al. 2021 Nat Commun), and protocol refinements that now push coverage above 40,000 sites per sample.

Udeshi's methodology is what most modern ubiquitin proteomics papers actually implement, even when they cite Kim 2011 in the methods section. Her protocol paper (Udeshi et al. 2013 Nat Protocols) is a more accessible SOP.

Huey's committee may ask about her specific workflow: starting material (ideally 5–10 mg per SILAC channel), fractionation strategy (high-pH RP is standard), MG132 pretreatment duration (typically 2–4 hr at 10 μM to accumulate ubiquitinated substrates without triggering global stress responses), and antibody source (CST #5562 PTMScan kit). Citing Udeshi 2013 signals that she understands the state-of-the-art protocol, not just the 2011 foundational paper. She should also know the coverage expectation: 10,000–20,000 sites is typical; higher numbers require the Carr-style off-line fractionation.

Udeshi ND, Svinkina T, Mertins P, et al. Mol Cell Proteomics. 2013 Mar;12(3):825-31. PMID: 23266961.

---

subsections.title: Roux et al. 2012, J Cell Biol — BioID
subsections.body:
Identifying protein-protein interactions in living cells has historically relied on affinity-tag co-immunoprecipitation or yeast two-hybrid. Both approaches have well-known limitations: affinity-Co-IP disrupts cell membranes and requires the interaction to survive lysis and wash steps (biasing toward high-affinity and large-interface contacts, missing weak or transient interactions and membrane-embedded partners); yeast two-hybrid gives binary readouts in a non-native environment and is prone to false positives. A method that captured the interactome in the native cellular context, including weak and transient partners, was wanted.

Kyle Roux, Brian Burke, and colleagues developed BioID (proximity-dependent biotin identification). The key insight: E. coli biotin ligase BirA normally biotinylates a specific substrate (acetyl-CoA carboxylase) via a channeled reaction intermediate, biotinyl-5′-AMP. A single point mutation (R118G) in BirA releases biotinyl-AMP into solution, which then reacts promiscuously with primary amines (ε-NH2 of lysines) on any nearby protein within ~10 nm. A fusion of mutant BirA (BirA*) to a protein of interest thus tags spatial neighbors with biotin in vivo. After cell lysis under denaturing conditions, biotinylated proteins are captured on streptavidin beads (an interaction with femtomolar affinity that survives SDS), eluted, and identified by LC-MS/MS.

The paper validated BioID on lamin-A, a well-studied intermediate filament at the nuclear envelope. BirA-LaminA, expressed in HEK293 cells with excess biotin in the media for 18-24 hours, biotinylated a panel of known LaminA interactors (LAP2, emerin, other lamins) plus many previously uncharacterized candidates. Streptavidin pulldown followed by MS identified ~120 high-confidence hits. Validation controls included BirA-only (no bait) and BirA*-GFP to rule out nonspecific biotinylation.

BioID has been adopted widely for proximity-based interactome mapping, particularly for insoluble, membrane-embedded, or nuclear envelope proteins that resist conventional Co-IP. Limitations include the 18-24 hour labeling window (long enough that cell state shifts during the experiment), the requirement for supplemental biotin, and the ~10 nm labeling radius (which can either be an advantage — capturing a local neighborhood — or a disadvantage, depending on the question). BioID2 (Kim et al. 2016 Mol Biol Cell) reduced the ligase to a smaller 27 kDa variant. APEX and APEX2 (Rhee/Lam, 2013/2015) offered alternative peroxidase-based chemistry.

For Huey, BioID is a natural tool for DCAF7-IFIT3 interaction validation that goes beyond standard Co-IP. Design: BirA-DCAF7 expressed in a JAK2V617F MPN model cell line or in HSCs ex vivo, 18 hr biotin labeling, streptavidin-MS. If IFIT3 is a bona fide DCAF7 proximity partner, it should score above threshold. More ambitiously: pair BirA-DCAF7 with BirA-IFIT3 and look for the reciprocal interaction, confirming the direct proximity. The committee will want to know the controls (BirA-only, untreated, reciprocal bait) and why BioID is superior to Co-IP for this purpose (DCAF7 is a WD40 substrate adaptor; its interactions with substrates are often weak/transient, a scenario where BioID outperforms Co-IP).

Roux KJ, Kim DI, Raida M, Burke B. J Cell Biol. 2012 Mar 19;196(6):801-10. PMID: 22412018.

subsections.title: Branon et al. 2018, Nature Biotechnology — TurboID
subsections.body:
BioID's 18-24 hour labeling window is a major practical constraint: it integrates over a long period, misses transient states, and requires sustained bait expression in stable cell lines. For proximity labeling in organisms (flies, worms, mice) and for dynamic signaling contexts, a much faster ligase was needed. Engineering BirA* for increased biotinylation activity — while retaining the promiscuous release of biotinyl-AMP — was the goal.

Tess Branon and Alice Ting used yeast-display-based directed evolution to screen BirA* mutant libraries for enhanced biotinylation activity. Iterative selection for mutants that biotinylated proximal yeast surface proteins more efficiently yielded TurboID (15 mutations from the parent BirA) and miniTurbo (a smaller, slightly less active variant missing the N-terminal domain). TurboID biotinylates at rates ~1000-fold faster than BioID, enabling 10-minute proximity labeling in living cells with a non-toxic biotin concentration.

Validation showed that TurboID produces proximity-labeling datasets of comparable or higher depth than BioID in 10 minutes versus 18 hours. In HEK293 cells, TurboID-histone H2B identified ~1500 nuclear proteins above control thresholds. TurboID worked in mouse embryonic fibroblasts, Drosophila, and C. elegans — settings where BioID's long window was prohibitive. A caveat: the faster kinetics mean higher background in long labeling periods, so TurboID experiments typically require tight temporal control and short (10-30 min) biotin pulses. Split-TurboID variants (the two halves of the ligase reconstitute only when brought together by two baits) enable conditional proximity labeling and have been used to map signaling hubs.

TurboID has become the proximity-labeling tool of choice for dynamic and in vivo contexts. BioID2 remains useful for long integrations in stable cells. APEX2 retains advantages in EM-localizable labeling (via DAB polymerization) but requires toxic H2O2. The TurboID field has produced hundreds of interactome maps since 2018.

For Huey, TurboID offers the in vivo version of the DCAF7 interactome experiment. Design: generate a conditional TurboID-DCAF7 knock-in or lentiviral transduction of JAK2V617F HSPCs, transplant, let the mice establish MPN, inject biotin, harvest 30 minutes later, sort LT-HSCs or MPN myeloid cells, and do streptavidin-MS. This gives the DCAF7 proximal interactome in the actual disease context — something BioID cannot achieve in vivo due to the long biotin exposure needed. The committee will ask about background (controls: wild-type HSC transplanted with TurboID-only lentivirus, untransplanted bait, biotin-unfed mice) and about whether the kinetic window captures or misses the IFIT3 interaction. Pairing TurboID (in vivo) with BioID (cell line) creates a strong case that DCAF7-IFIT3 proximity is preserved across contexts.

Branon TC, Bosch JA, Sanchez AD, et al. Nat Biotechnol. 2018 Oct;36(9):880-887. PMID: 30125270.

---

sections.title: §5F — Targeted degradation and CRISPR in HSCs

subsections.title: Nabet et al. 2018, Nature Chemical Biology — dTAG
subsections.body:
Genetic knockdown (shRNA/siRNA) is slow (24-72 hr to maximal knockdown) and incomplete; CRISPR knockout is slower still (weeks) and permanent. For studying proteins whose immediate loss triggers a distinct phenotype from their prolonged absence — or whose deletion simply kills the cell — a rapid, reversible, chemically controlled degrader was needed. The PROTAC concept existed (Crews, Deshaies), but each PROTAC required custom medicinal chemistry against the target.

James Bradner's group at Dana-Farber built the dTAG platform to uncouple target identification from small-molecule development. The design: engineer a 12-kDa FKBP12 variant carrying a single F36V point mutation that creates a "hole" in its ligand-binding pocket, and design a bifunctional "bumped" ligand (dTAG-13 initially; dTAG-V1 and others added) that selectively binds FKBP12F36V via a complementary substituent while recruiting cereblon (CRBN) via a phthalimide warhead. Any protein of interest fused to FKBP12F36V becomes a cereblon substrate in the presence of dTAG-13 and is polyubiquitinated and degraded within 1-4 hours.

Validation was exhaustive. BRD4-FKBP12F36V fusions degraded to <10% within 2 hours of 100 nM dTAG-13 treatment; the kinetics were dose-dependent, reversible on ligand washout, and orthogonal to endogenous FKBP12 function. Knock-in of FKBP12F36V at the endogenous KRAS locus in pancreatic cancer cells enabled acute KRAS degradation. Applications demonstrated in the paper included mutant oncoprotein degradation, transcription factor removal, and kinase elimination — all with timescales of hours, not days.

dTAG has been adopted broadly. Wisely, Nabet et al. made dTAG-13 and related molecules freely distributed (Tocris/R&D Systems). VHL-recruiting dTAG variants (dTAG-V1, Nabet 2020) extended the platform to VHL-dependent cells and tissues where cereblon expression is low. Knock-in strategies at endogenous loci (via CRISPR-HDR or cassette exchange) allow physiological expression levels.

For Huey, dTAG is the chemical-biology complement to the genetic Mx1-Cre × Dcaf7^F/F^ model. The problem with genetic knockout: it takes days to weeks for existing DCAF7 protein to turn over after the gene is deleted, confounding interpretation of whether an HSC phenotype is an acute DCAF7-loss effect or a downstream consequence. dTAG design: knock FKBP12F36V into the endogenous Dcaf7 locus (heterozygous to preserve expression), generate JAK2V617F MPN mice carrying the Dcaf7-dTAG allele, and dose with dTAG-13. DCAF7 protein disappears within hours, and the immediate IFIT3-IFN consequences can be followed in sorted HSCs. This is a more rigorous temporal test than the genetic model alone. The committee will view a dTAG validation experiment as going well beyond the expected qualifying-exam depth, establishing her as thinking at the level of modern pharmacological rigor.

Nabet B, Roberts JM, Buckley DL, et al. Nat Chem Biol. 2018 May;14(5):431-441. PMID: 29581585.

subsections.title: Tothova et al. 2017, Cell Stem Cell — Multiplex CRISPR in primary human HSCs
subsections.body:
Modeling clonal hematopoiesis and MPN/MDS preclinically has traditionally used retroviral/lentiviral overexpression of oncogenes (which yields non-physiological expression levels and insertional mutagenesis), germline or conditional mouse models (which are labor-intensive and limited to mouse biology), or cell-line xenografts (which lack HSC biology). The gap was a method to generate genetically defined primary human hematopoietic stem cell models of clonal hematopoiesis that recapitulated the mutational landscape of patient disease.

Zuzana Tothova, Benjamin Ebert, and colleagues at the Broad Institute applied multiplex CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing to primary human CD34^+^ HSPCs isolated from adult mobilized peripheral blood or umbilical cord blood. Electroporation of Cas9 ribonucleoprotein (RNP) with a pool of sgRNAs targeting clonal hematopoiesis drivers (DNMT3A, TET2, EZH2, ASXL1, RUNX1, SRSF2, U2AF1, cohesin components) achieved simultaneous disruption of multiple loci at 40-80% editing efficiency. Edited HSPCs were transplanted into NSG immunodeficient mice. Over 16 weeks, mice developed clonal expansions carrying specific combinations of edits, phenocopying aspects of human MDS and clonal hematopoiesis.

The key methodological advance was the efficiency and multiplicity of editing achievable in primary HSPCs without long-term culture (which biases HSC quality). RNP electroporation outperformed lentiviral Cas9 delivery in preserving HSC engraftment. Clonal tracking via deep sequencing revealed selection dynamics of specific edit combinations; cohesin-mutant and TET2-mutant clones dominated and were sensitive to azacitidine, suggesting therapeutic targets.

The Tothova approach is now widely used to dissect clonal fitness of specific mutation combinations, screen for synthetic lethal interactions, and benchmark therapeutic responses.
